Over the complex numbers, every polynomial factors completely into linear factors. These are progressively larger universes of factorability. The factoring decision tree \text{1. GCF?} \to \text{pull it out first, always} \\ \text{2. How many terms?} \\ \quad 2 \text{ terms} \to \text{difference of squares? sum/diff of cubes?} \\ \quad 3 \text{ terms} \to \text{trinomial (}ac\text{ method or inspection)} \\ \quad 4 \text{ terms} \to \text{grouping} The area model of factoring x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x+2)(x+3) \\ \text{Visually: a rectangle with sides }(x+2)\text{ and }(x+3)\text{ has area }x^2+5x+6. \\ \text{The four tile regions are: }x^2,\; 3x,\; 2x,\; 6. Sum of squares does NOT factor over the reals a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b) \qquad \checkmark \\ a^2 + b^2 = \text{prime over } \mathbb{R} \qquad \times \\ \text{(It factors over } \mathbb{C}\text{: }(a+bi)(a-bi)\text{ — covered in L6.)} Special factoring forms — both directions a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b) \qquad \text{(difference of squares)} \\ a^3 - b^3 = (a-b)(a^2+ab+b^2) \qquad \text{(difference of cubes)} \\ a^3 + b^3 = (a+b)(a^2-ab+b^2) \qquad \text{(sum of cubes)} The ac method — step by step \text{Factor } ax^2+bx+c: \\ 1.\; \text{compute } ac. \quad 2.\; \text{find } p,q \text{ with } pq=ac \text{ and } p+q=b. \\ 3.\; \text{rewrite: } ax^2+px+qx+c. \quad 4.\; \text{factor by grouping.} Sum/difference of cubes memory: SOAP (a \pm b)(a^2 \mp ab + b^2) \\ \text{S: Same sign as original. O: Opposite sign. A: Always positive.} \\ a^3 + b^3 = (a+b)(a^2 - ab + b^2) \quad \text{same, opposite, always}+ Factoring as Rectangle Area Each cell of the grid is one term from the multiplication. Factoring recovers the dimensions. GCF first, then trinomial \text{Factor completely: } 6x^3 - 24x^2 + 18x Never skip the GCF step. It reduces the degree of the remaining factor, making the next step easier. = 6x(x^2 - 4x + 3) GCF is $6x$. Always pull this out first — it simplifies everything inside. = 6x(x-1)(x-3) Factor the trinomial $x^2 - 4x + 3$. Need two numbers that multiply to $3$ and add to $-4$: those are $-1$ and $-3$. The ac method on a hard trinomial \text{Factor: } 6x^2 + 7x - 3 The ac method removes all guesswork. It turns a hard trinomial into a grouping problem. a=6,\; c=-3 \Rightarrow ac = -18. \text{ Need } p+q=7,\; pq=-18. Compute $ac$. Find two numbers with that product and sum equal to $b = 7$. p = 9,\; q = -2 \quad (9 \times -2 = -18,\; 9 + (-2) = 7) List factor pairs of $-18$: try $(9,-2)$. It works. 6x^2 + 9x - 2x - 3 Rewrite middle term using the two numbers found. = 3x(2x+3) - 1(2x+3) = (3x-1)(2x+3) Factor by grouping. The common factor $(2x+3)$ appears in both groups. Sum of cubes — recognising the pattern \text{Factor: } 8x^3 + 27 The quadratic factor from a sum/difference of cubes is usually irreducible over the reals. Always check by computing the discriminant. 8x^3 + 27 = (2x)^3 + 3^3 Recognise as sum of cubes with $a = 2x$ and $b = 3$. = (2x + 3)\bigl((2x)^2 - (2x)(3) + 3^2\bigr) Apply the sum of cubes formula: $(a+b)(a^2-ab+b^2)$. = (2x+3)(4x^2 - 6x + 9) Simplify. Note: $4x^2 - 6x + 9$ has discriminant $36 - 144 < 0$ — it does not factor over the reals. Factoring by grouping — four terms \text{Factor: } x^3 + 2x^2 - 9x - 18 Grouping works when the four terms split into two pairs that share a common factor. If the first grouping does not work, try a different pairing. = x^2(x+2) - 9(x+2) Group first two and last two terms. Factor GCF from each pair: $x^2$ from the first, $-9$ from the second. = (x^2 - 9)(x + 2) The common factor $(x+2)$ appears — pull it out. = (x-3)(x+3)(x+2) $x^2 - 9$ is a difference of squares. Factor again. Completely factored. \text{Factor completely: } x^4 - 16 Treat $x^4$ as $(x^2)^2$ — this is a difference of squares. Then check if the factors continue to factor. (x-2)(x+2)(x^2+4) x^4 - 16 = (x^2)^2 - 4^2 = (x^2-4)(x^2+4) Difference of squares with $a = x^2$, $b = 4$. (x^2-4) = (x-2)(x+2) Difference of squares again. (x^2+4) \text{ is irreducible over } \mathbb{R} Sum of squares never factors over the reals. x^4 - 16 = (x-2)(x+2)(x^2+4) Fully factored over the reals. Over $\mathbb{C}$: $(x-2)(x+2)(x-2i)(x+2i)$. \text{Factor } x^6 - 1 \text{ completely over the reals.} First treat as difference of squares $(x^3)^2 - 1^2$, then as difference/sum of cubes. (x-1)(x+1)(x^2+x+1)(x^2-x+1) x^6-1 = (x^3-1)(x^3+1) Difference of squares with $a=x^3$, $b=1$. x^3-1 = (x-1)(x^2+x+1) Difference of cubes. x^3+1 = (x+1)(x^2-x+1) Sum of cubes. x^6-1 = (x-1)(x+1)(x^2+x+1)(x^2-x+1) Both quadratic factors have negative discriminants — irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$. This is the complete factorisation. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Quadratics & Completing the Square
documents.subtitle: The quadratic formula is not magic — it is completing the square, done once for all time
documents.tags: quadratic completing the square quadratic formula discriminant vertex form parabola
documents.assignment: Converting to vertex form \text{Write } f(x) = 2x^2 - 12x + 7 \text{ in vertex form.}   The vertex $(h,k) = (3,-11)$ gives the minimum value, axis of symmetry $x = 3$, and tells you the parabola opens upward. All this information is invisible in standard form. = 2(x^2 - 6x) + 7 Factor the coefficient of $x^2$ out of the first two terms only. = 2(x^2 - 6x + 9) + 7 - 18 Add $(6/2)^2 = 9$ inside the bracket. Because of the factor of 2 outside, we added $2 \times 9 = 18$ to the expression — subtract 18 outside to compensate. = 2(x-3)^2 - 11 Vertex form. Vertex is at $(3, -11)$, which is the minimum (since $a = 2 > 0$). Using the discriminant before solving \text{Without solving, determine the nature of the roots of } 3x^2 - 5x + 4 = 0.   The discriminant is a preliminary test — compute it before solving. It tells you how many solutions to expect and whether complex numbers are needed. \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 25 - 4(3)(4) = 25 - 48 = -23 Compute $\Delta$ directly. \Delta = -23 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{no real roots} Negative discriminant means the parabola does not intersect the $x$-axis. The roots exist but are complex conjugates. Constructing a quadratic from its roots — Vieta's formulas backwards \text{Find a monic quadratic with roots } r_1 = 3 + \sqrt{2} \text{ and } r_2 = 3 - \sqrt{2}.   Irrational roots always come in conjugate pairs $p \pm \sqrt{q}$ when the coefficients are rational. Vieta's formulas let you reconstruct the quadratic directly from the roots. r_1 + r_2 = 6 \qquad r_1 r_2 = (3+\sqrt{2})(3-\sqrt{2}) = 9 - 2 = 7 Compute sum and product of roots — no need to use the full values until the product step. x^2 - (r_1+r_2)x + r_1 r_2 = x^2 - 6x + 7 The monic quadratic with these roots is $x^2 - (\text{sum})x + (\text{product})$.  \text{Find the value of } k \text{ so that } x^2 + kx + 25 = 0 \text{ has exactly one real solution.} Exactly one solution means the discriminant equals zero. k = \pm 10  \Delta = k^2 - 4(1)(25) = 0 Set $\Delta = 0$ for a repeated root. k^2 = 100 \Rightarrow k = \pm 10 Two values of $k$ work: the quadratic $x^2 + 10x + 25 = (x+5)^2$ and $x^2 - 10x + 25 = (x-5)^2$.  \text{A parabola has vertex } (2, -3) \text{ and passes through } (5, 6). \text{ Find its equation in standard form.} Start from vertex form $f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3$, then use the given point to find $a$. f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 1  f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3 Vertex form with vertex $(2, -3)$. 6 = a(5-2)^2 - 3 = 9a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 1 Substitute $(5, 6)$ and solve for $a$. f(x) = (x-2)^2 - 3 = x^2 - 4x + 1 Expand to standard form.
documents.applications: Quadratic equations model projectile motion, profit maximisation, lens optics, and electrical circuit resonance. The discriminant ($b^2 - 4ac$) tells engineers whether a system oscillates, is critically damped, or overdamped — before solving anything. In calculus, completing the square is the technique that makes certain integrals solvable by converting a quadratic into a perfect square form. Completing the square — geometric picture x^2 + bx = \left(x + \frac{b}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 \\ \text{You add the missing corner square, then subtract it: net change = 0.} The discriminant and its meaning \Delta = b^2 - 4ac \\ \Delta > 0: \text{ two distinct real roots} \\ \Delta = 0: \text{ one repeated real root (vertex on }x\text{-axis)} \\ \Delta < 0: \text{ no real roots (complex conjugate pair)} The three forms of a quadratic — when to use each \text{Standard: } ax^2+bx+c \quad \text{(easy to find }y\text{-intercept: it's }c\text{)} \\ \text{Vertex: } a(x-h)^2+k \quad \text{(easy to read vertex }(h,k)\text{ and axis of symmetry)} \\ \text{Factored: } a(x-r_1)(x-r_2) \quad \text{(easy to read roots }r_1, r_2\text{)}
documents.content: Where does the quadratic formula come from? Most students are handed it without explanation. It is actually a geometric argument — and seeing it makes the formula unforgettable. Quadratic equations model projectile motion, profit maximisation, lens optics, and electrical circuit resonance. The discriminant ($b^2 - 4ac$) tells engineers whether a system oscillates, is critically damped, or overdamped — before solving anything. In calculus, completing the square is the technique that makes certain integrals solvable by converting a quadratic into a perfect square form. A quadratic $ax^2 + bx + c$ is a parabola. Its vertex — the minimum or maximum point — is the geometric heart of the function. Vertex form $a(x-h)^2 + k$ makes the vertex $(h, k)$ immediately readable. Completing the square is the algebraic procedure that converts standard form into vertex form. The geometric intuition: $x^2 + bx$ is a square of side $x$ plus a rectangle of width $b$ and height $x$. To complete it into a perfect square, split the rectangle in half and attach one piece to each side of the square. The missing corner is a square of side $b/2$, area $(b/2)^2$. Add it in — and subtract it to keep the value the same. Now you have $(x + b/2)^2 - (b/2)^2$. The discriminant $b^2 - 4ac$ is not just a formula component — it is the quantity under the square root, telling you how far the vertex is from the $x$-axis. Positive: two real roots (vertex and $x$-axis are on opposite sides). Zero: one repeated root (vertex touches $x$-axis). Negative: no real roots (vertex does not reach the $x$-axis). The completing-the-square procedure for $ax^2 + bx + c$: first divide everything by $a$ to get a leading coefficient of 1. Then move $c/a$ to the other side. Add $(b/2a)^2$ to both sides. Factor the left side as a perfect square. Take the square root of both sides (both $\pm$). Solve for $x$. This procedure, done in full generality, produces the quadratic formula. The vertex formula $h = -b/(2a)$ comes directly from completing the square: the vertex is at the $x$-value that makes $(x - h) = 0$, which is $x = h = -b/(2a)$. You do not need to memorise it separately from the quadratic formula — it is just the middle term of the formula without the $\pm\sqrt{\Delta}$ part. Vieta's formulas connect the roots to the coefficients without solving: for $ax^2+bx+c = 0$ with roots $r_1, r_2$: $r_1 + r_2 = -b/a$ and $r_1 r_2 = c/a$. These are useful for checking solutions and for constructing quadratics with given roots. Completing the square — geometric picture x^2 + bx = \left(x + \frac{b}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{b}{2}\right)^2 \\ \text{You add the missing corner square, then subtract it: net change = 0.} The discriminant and its meaning \Delta = b^2 - 4ac \\ \Delta > 0: \text{ two distinct real roots} \\ \Delta = 0: \text{ one repeated real root (vertex on }x\text{-axis)} \\ \Delta < 0: \text{ no real roots (complex conjugate pair)} The three forms of a quadratic — when to use each \text{Standard: } ax^2+bx+c \quad \text{(easy to find }y\text{-intercept: it's }c\text{)} \\ \text{Vertex: } a(x-h)^2+k \quad \text{(easy to read vertex }(h,k)\text{ and axis of symmetry)} \\ \text{Factored: } a(x-r_1)(x-r_2) \quad \text{(easy to read roots }r_1, r_2\text{)} The quadratic formula ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \implies x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} Vieta's formulas for quadratics r_1 + r_2 = -\frac{b}{a} \qquad r_1 r_2 = \frac{c}{a} \\ \text{Example: roots of } x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 \text{ sum to } 5 \text{ and multiply to } 6 \Rightarrow r_1=2, r_2=3. The leading coefficient matters when completing the square 2x^2 + 8x + 3: \text{ factor out 2 first.} \\ 2(x^2 + 4x) + 3 = 2(x^2 + 4x + 4) + 3 - 8 = 2(x+2)^2 - 5 \\ \text{Note: adding 4 inside the bracket adds } 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ to the expression — subtract 8 outside.} Completing the Square — Watch the Geometry Adding and subtracting $(b/2)^2$ is adding zero in disguise — it changes the form but not the value. Converting to vertex form \text{Write } f(x) = 2x^2 - 12x + 7 \text{ in vertex form.} The vertex $(h,k) = (3,-11)$ gives the minimum value, axis of symmetry $x = 3$, and tells you the parabola opens upward. All this information is invisible in standard form. = 2(x^2 - 6x) + 7 Factor the coefficient of $x^2$ out of the first two terms only. = 2(x^2 - 6x + 9) + 7 - 18 Add $(6/2)^2 = 9$ inside the bracket. Because of the factor of 2 outside, we added $2 \times 9 = 18$ to the expression — subtract 18 outside to compensate. = 2(x-3)^2 - 11 Vertex form. Vertex is at $(3, -11)$, which is the minimum (since $a = 2 > 0$). Using the discriminant before solving \text{Without solving, determine the nature of the roots of } 3x^2 - 5x + 4 = 0. The discriminant is a preliminary test — compute it before solving. It tells you how many solutions to expect and whether complex numbers are needed. \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 25 - 4(3)(4) = 25 - 48 = -23 Compute $\Delta$ directly. \Delta = -23 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{no real roots} Negative discriminant means the parabola does not intersect the $x$-axis. The roots exist but are complex conjugates. Constructing a quadratic from its roots — Vieta's formulas backwards \text{Find a monic quadratic with roots } r_1 = 3 + \sqrt{2} \text{ and } r_2 = 3 - \sqrt{2}. Irrational roots always come in conjugate pairs $p \pm \sqrt{q}$ when the coefficients are rational. Vieta's formulas let you reconstruct the quadratic directly from the roots. r_1 + r_2 = 6 \qquad r_1 r_2 = (3+\sqrt{2})(3-\sqrt{2}) = 9 - 2 = 7 Compute sum and product of roots — no need to use the full values until the product step. x^2 - (r_1+r_2)x + r_1 r_2 = x^2 - 6x + 7 The monic quadratic with these roots is $x^2 - (\text{sum})x + (\text{product})$. \text{Find the value of } k \text{ so that } x^2 + kx + 25 = 0 \text{ has exactly one real solution.} Exactly one solution means the discriminant equals zero. k = \pm 10 \Delta = k^2 - 4(1)(25) = 0 Set $\Delta = 0$ for a repeated root. k^2 = 100 \Rightarrow k = \pm 10 Two values of $k$ work: the quadratic $x^2 + 10x + 25 = (x+5)^2$ and $x^2 - 10x + 25 = (x-5)^2$. \text{A parabola has vertex } (2, -3) \text{ and passes through } (5, 6). \text{ Find its equation in standard form.} Start from vertex form $f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3$, then use the given point to find $a$. f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 1 f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3 Vertex form with vertex $(2, -3)$. 6 = a(5-2)^2 - 3 = 9a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 1 Substitute $(5, 6)$ and solve for $a$. f(x) = (x-2)^2 - 3 = x^2 - 4x + 1 Expand to standard form. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Rational Expressions & Partial Fractions
documents.subtitle: Simplifying, operating, and decomposing — the algebra calculus uses to integrate
documents.tags: rational expressions partial fractions simplify add fractions decomposition
documents.assignment:
Simplifying a rational expression 	ext{Simplify: } \dfrac{x^2-9}{x^2+x-6}   The restriction $x 
eq -3$ remains even after cancellation — there is a hole in the graph at $x = -3$. = \dfrac{(x-3)(x+3)}{(x-2)(x+3)} Factor numerator (difference of squares) and denominator (trinomial). = \dfrac{x-3}{x-2} \quad (x 
eq -3) Cancel the common factor $(x+3)$. Domain restriction: $x 
eq -3$ and $x 
eq 2$. Adding rational expressions with different denominators 	ext{Simplify: } \dfrac{3}{x-1} + \dfrac{2x}{x^2-1}   LCD approach: factor all denominators, identify the LCD, convert, then add numerators. x^2-1 = (x-1)(x+1) Factor the second denominator. The LCD is $(x-1)(x+1)$. = \dfrac{3(x+1)}{(x-1)(x+1)} + \dfrac{2x}{(x-1)(x+1)} Convert first fraction to have LCD. = \dfrac{3x+3+2x}{(x-1)(x+1)} = \dfrac{5x+3}{x^2-1} Add numerators. Cannot simplify further (check: $5x+3$ has no common factor with $x^2-1$).  	ext{Decompose: } \dfrac{x^2+2x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+1)} The $x^2+1$ factor is an irreducible quadratic — its partial fraction form is $(Ax+B)/(x^2+1)$, not $A/(x^2+1)$. \dfrac{1}{x-1} + \dfrac{x}{x^2+1}  = \dfrac{C}{x-1} + \dfrac{Ax+B}{x^2+1} Template: linear factor gets a constant numerator; irreducible quadratic gets a linear numerator. x=1: \; 2 = 2C \Rightarrow C=1 Cover-up for the linear factor. x=0: \; -1 = -C + B \Rightarrow B=0. \quad x=-1: \; -2 = -C/2 + (-A+B)/2 \Rightarrow A=1 Substitute convenient values to find $A$ and $B$. = \dfrac{1}{x-1} + \dfrac{x}{x^2+1} Verify by recombining. The second term integrates to $	frac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)$ in calculus.
documents.applications: Partial fractions are used in control systems engineering to analyse the step response of a transfer function — breaking a complex rational function into simple poles that each contribute a decaying exponential. In signals processing, every filter response is a rational function that gets decomposed this way. Partial fraction forms — matched to denominator factors \frac{\text{numerator}}{(x-a)(x-b)} = \frac{A}{x-a} + \frac{B}{x-b} \\ \frac{\text{num}}{(x-a)^2} = \frac{A}{x-a} + \frac{B}{(x-a)^2} \\ \frac{\text{num}}{(x^2+bx+c)} = \frac{Ax+B}{x^2+bx+c} \quad (\text{irreducible quadratic}) Must be proper first \text{If } \deg(\text{numerator}) \geq \deg(\text{denominator}): \text{ do polynomial long division first.} \\ \text{Partial fractions only work on proper fractions (top degree < bottom degree).} The Heaviside cover-up method (for distinct linear factors) \frac{f(x)}{(x-a)(x-b)} = \frac{A}{x-a} + \frac{B}{x-b} \\ A = \lim_{x\to a}(x-a)\cdot f(x)/\text{denom} = f(a)/[(a-b)] \\ \text{Cover the }(x-a)\text{ factor, plug in }x=a\text{ — that's }A.
documents.content:
How do you integrate $\dfrac{1}{x^2 - 1}$? You cannot directly. But if you split it into $\dfrac{1/2}{x-1} - \dfrac{1/2}{x+1}$, each piece integrates to a $\ln$. That splitting is partial fractions — and it is pure algebra. Partial fractions are used in control systems engineering to analyse the step response of a transfer function — breaking a complex rational function into simple poles that each contribute a decaying exponential. In signals processing, every filter response is a rational function that gets decomposed this way. A rational expression is a fraction where numerator and denominator are polynomials. They behave exactly like numeric fractions — same rules for multiplying, dividing, adding, subtracting. The only new complication is that you must track which values make the denominator zero (the domain restrictions). Partial fraction decomposition is the reverse of adding fractions. When you add $ rac{A}{x-1} +  rac{B}{x+1}$, you get a single fraction over $(x-1)(x+1)$. Partial fractions asks: given that combined fraction, recover the $A$ and $B$. The technique: multiply both sides by the denominator, then solve for the coefficients. The key insight: a fraction with a linear factor in the denominator integrates to a logarithm. A fraction with a repeated or quadratic factor needs different forms. Decomposing into these simple pieces is exactly what makes integration possible. The procedure for partial fractions: (1) ensure the fraction is proper; (2) factor the denominator completely; (3) set up the partial fraction template matching each factor type; (4) multiply through by the denominator to clear all fractions; (5) solve the resulting system for the unknown coefficients. Operations on rational expressions follow the same rules as numeric fractions. For multiplication: multiply numerators and denominators, then cancel common factors. For division: multiply by the reciprocal. For addition: find a common denominator, convert, then add numerators. Always state domain restrictions. A rational expression is undefined wherever the denominator is zero. After simplification (cancellation), a hole appears where the cancelled factor was zero — the domain restriction remains even though the expression looks defined there. Partial fraction forms — matched to denominator factors  rac{	ext{numerator}}{(x-a)(x-b)} =  rac{A}{x-a} +  rac{B}{x-b} \  rac{	ext{num}}{(x-a)^2} =  rac{A}{x-a} +  rac{B}{(x-a)^2} \  rac{	ext{num}}{(x^2+bx+c)} =  rac{Ax+B}{x^2+bx+c} \quad (	ext{irreducible quadratic}) Must be proper first 	ext{If } \deg(	ext{numerator}) \geq \deg(	ext{denominator}): 	ext{ do polynomial long division first.} \ 	ext{Partial fractions only work on proper fractions (top degree < bottom degree).} The Heaviside cover-up method (for distinct linear factors)  rac{f(x)}{(x-a)(x-b)} =  rac{A}{x-a} +  rac{B}{x-b} \ A = \lim_{x	o a}(x-a)\cdot f(x)/	ext{denom} = f(a)/[(a-b)] \ 	ext{Cover the }(x-a)	ext{ factor, plug in }x=a	ext{ — that's }A. Operations on rational expressions  rac{a}{b} \cdot  rac{c}{d} =  rac{ac}{bd} \qquad  rac{a}{b} \div  rac{c}{d} =  rac{a}{b} \cdot  rac{d}{c} \  rac{a}{b} +  rac{c}{d} =  rac{ad + bc}{bd} \quad (	ext{then simplify}) The cover-up method — worked  rac{3x+1}{(x-2)(x+3)} =  rac{A}{x-2} +  rac{B}{x+3} \ A =  rac{3(2)+1}{(2+3)} =  rac{7}{5} \quad B =  rac{3(-3)+1}{(-3-2)} =  rac{-8}{-5} =  rac{8}{5} Decomposing a Fraction — Forward and Backward Partial fractions is fraction addition in reverse — we're finding the addends, not the sum. Simplifying a rational expression 	ext{Simplify: } \dfrac{x^2-9}{x^2+x-6} The restriction $x 
eq -3$ remains even after cancellation — there is a hole in the graph at $x = -3$. = \dfrac{(x-3)(x+3)}{(x-2)(x+3)} Factor numerator (difference of squares) and denominator (trinomial). = \dfrac{x-3}{x-2} \quad (x 
eq -3) Cancel the common factor $(x+3)$. Domain restriction: $x 
eq -3$ and $x 
eq 2$. Adding rational expressions with different denominators 	ext{Simplify: } \dfrac{3}{x-1} + \dfrac{2x}{x^2-1} LCD approach: factor all denominators, identify the LCD, convert, then add numerators. x^2-1 = (x-1)(x+1) Factor the second denominator. The LCD is $(x-1)(x+1)$. = \dfrac{3(x+1)}{(x-1)(x+1)} + \dfrac{2x}{(x-1)(x+1)} Convert first fraction to have LCD. = \dfrac{3x+3+2x}{(x-1)(x+1)} = \dfrac{5x+3}{x^2-1} Add numerators. Cannot simplify further (check: $5x+3$ has no common factor with $x^2-1$). 	ext{Decompose: } \dfrac{x^2+2x-1}{(x-1)(x^2+1)} The $x^2+1$ factor is an irreducible quadratic — its partial fraction form is $(Ax+B)/(x^2+1)$, not $A/(x^2+1)$. \dfrac{1}{x-1} + \dfrac{x}{x^2+1} = \dfrac{C}{x-1} + \dfrac{Ax+B}{x^2+1} Template: linear factor gets a constant numerator; irreducible quadratic gets a linear numerator. x=1: \; 2 = 2C \Rightarrow C=1 Cover-up for the linear factor. x=0: \; -1 = -C + B \Rightarrow B=0. \quad x=-1: \; -2 = -C/2 + (-A+B)/2 \Rightarrow A=1 Substitute convenient values to find $A$ and $B$. = \dfrac{1}{x-1} + \dfrac{x}{x^2+1} Verify by recombining. The second term integrates to $	frac{1}{2}\ln(x^2+1)$ in calculus. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Systems of Equations & the Geometry of Solutions
documents.subtitle: Every method — substitution, elimination, matrices — is the same idea from a different angle
documents.tags: systems linear systems substitution elimination matrix Gaussian elimination geometric interpretation
documents.assignment: Elimination on a 2×2 system \text{Solve: } 3x - 2y = 8, \quad 5x + y = 11   Solution: $(30/13, -7/13)$. This is the intersection point of the two lines. 3x - 2y = 8 \quad \text{and} \quad 10x + 2y = 22 Multiply the second equation by 2 to make $y$ coefficients opposites. 13x = 30 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{30}{13} Add equations. $y$ eliminated. y = 11 - 5(\tfrac{30}{13}) = \tfrac{143-150}{13} = -\tfrac{7}{13} Back-substitute. Recognising a dependent system \text{Solve: } 2x - 4y = 6, \quad -x + 2y = -3   Infinitely many solutions: every point on the line $2x - 4y = 6$. Geometrically, the two equations represent the same line. \text{Multiply equation 2 by 2: } -2x + 4y = -6 Prepare for elimination. \text{Add: } 0 = 0 All variables cancel. The statement $0=0$ is always true — infinitely many solutions. y = \frac{2x-6}{4} = \frac{x-3}{2} \quad \text{(parameterise: let } x = t\text{)} The equations are multiples of each other. Express solutions in terms of a free parameter.  \text{Solve the 3×3 system: } x+y+z=6, \; 2x-y+z=3, \; x+2y-z=2 Set up the augmented matrix and use row operations to reach row echelon form. x=1, \; y=2, \; z=3  \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\2&-1&1&|&3\\1&2&-1&|&2\end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_2-2R_1,\;R_3-R_1} \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\0&-3&-1&|&-9\\0&1&-2&|&-4\end{pmatrix} Eliminate $x$ from rows 2 and 3. \xrightarrow{R_3+\tfrac{1}{3}R_2} \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\0&-3&-1&|&-9\\0&0&-\tfrac{7}{3}&|&-7\end{pmatrix} Eliminate $y$ from row 3. z = 3, \quad y = \tfrac{-9+1(3)}{-3} = 2, \quad x = 6-2-3=1 Back-substitute from bottom up.
documents.applications: Systems of equations are everywhere in engineering: structural analysis (force balance at every joint), electrical networks (Kirchhoff's laws), economics (supply-demand equilibrium), and computer graphics (ray-surface intersection). Every finite element analysis solves a system of thousands of equations simultaneously using the matrix methods introduced here. The three solution types — geometrically \text{Unique: lines intersect at one point} \quad \det(A) \neq 0 \\ \text{None: parallel lines, same slope different intercept} \quad \text{(inconsistent)} \\ \text{Infinite: same line} \quad \text{(dependent — one equation is a multiple of the other)} Why elimination works \text{Adding a multiple of equation 1 to equation 2 does not change the solution set.} \\ \text{If } (x_0, y_0) \text{ satisfies both equations, it satisfies any linear combination of them.} Matrix row operations ↔ elimination moves R_i \leftrightarrow R_j \quad \text{swap rows (reorder equations)} \\ kR_i \to R_i \quad \text{scale a row (multiply equation by constant)} \\ R_i + kR_j \to R_i \quad \text{eliminate a variable}
documents.content: Two equations in two unknowns: geometrically, each is a line. The solution is where they cross. What if they are parallel? What if they are the same line? Systems of equations are everywhere in engineering: structural analysis (force balance at every joint), electrical networks (Kirchhoff's laws), economics (supply-demand equilibrium), and computer graphics (ray-surface intersection). Every finite element analysis solves a system of thousands of equations simultaneously using the matrix methods introduced here. A linear system is a set of constraints that must all be satisfied simultaneously. Each equation in two variables is a line — its solution set is a whole line of points. The system's solution is the set of points satisfying all equations at once. For two lines in a plane, there are exactly three cases: one intersection point (unique solution), parallel lines (no solution), or the same line (infinitely many solutions). Three methods — substitution, elimination, and matrix row reduction — all find this intersection. They differ in what they manipulate: substitution replaces one variable; elimination adds equations to cancel a variable; matrix methods automate elimination systematically. The Gaussian elimination algorithm is just elimination applied by an organised procedure. Matrices are a compact notation for systems. The augmented matrix $[A|\mathbf{b}]$ stores the coefficients and constants. Row operations (swap rows, scale a row, add a multiple of one row to another) correspond exactly to valid moves in elimination — they do not change the solution set. Substitution: solve one equation for one variable, substitute into the other. Best when one equation is already solved or easily solved for a variable. Elimination: multiply equations by constants so that one variable has equal (or opposite) coefficients, then add/subtract to eliminate it. Best for balanced-looking systems. Gaussian elimination to row echelon form: use row operations to create zeros below the leading entry in each column (the pivot). Back-substitute from the bottom row up. This always works and scales to any number of variables. The three solution types — geometrically \text{Unique: lines intersect at one point} \quad \det(A) \neq 0 \\ \text{None: parallel lines, same slope different intercept} \quad \text{(inconsistent)} \\ \text{Infinite: same line} \quad \text{(dependent — one equation is a multiple of the other)} Why elimination works \text{Adding a multiple of equation 1 to equation 2 does not change the solution set.} \\ \text{If } (x_0, y_0) \text{ satisfies both equations, it satisfies any linear combination of them.} Matrix row operations ↔ elimination moves R_i \leftrightarrow R_j \quad \text{swap rows (reorder equations)} \\ kR_i \to R_i \quad \text{scale a row (multiply equation by constant)} \\ R_i + kR_j \to R_i \quad \text{eliminate a variable} Cramer's rule (2×2) ax + by = e, \; cx + dy = f \\ x = \frac{ed-bf}{ad-bc}, \quad y = \frac{af-ec}{ad-bc} \quad (ad-bc \neq 0) Augmented matrix and row echelon form \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 & | & 7 \\ 1 & -1 & | & 1 \end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_1 - 2R_2} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 & | & 7 \\ 0 & 5 & | & 5 \end{pmatrix} \\ \text{Row echelon: zeros below each pivot. Back-substitute: } y=1, x=2. Systems as Intersecting Lines The algebra finds a number; the geometry explains what that number means. Elimination on a 2×2 system \text{Solve: } 3x - 2y = 8, \quad 5x + y = 11 Solution: $(30/13, -7/13)$. This is the intersection point of the two lines. 3x - 2y = 8 \quad \text{and} \quad 10x + 2y = 22 Multiply the second equation by 2 to make $y$ coefficients opposites. 13x = 30 \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{30}{13} Add equations. $y$ eliminated. y = 11 - 5(\tfrac{30}{13}) = \tfrac{143-150}{13} = -\tfrac{7}{13} Back-substitute. Recognising a dependent system \text{Solve: } 2x - 4y = 6, \quad -x + 2y = -3 Infinitely many solutions: every point on the line $2x - 4y = 6$. Geometrically, the two equations represent the same line. \text{Multiply equation 2 by 2: } -2x + 4y = -6 Prepare for elimination. \text{Add: } 0 = 0 All variables cancel. The statement $0=0$ is always true — infinitely many solutions. y = \frac{2x-6}{4} = \frac{x-3}{2} \quad \text{(parameterise: let } x = t\text{)} The equations are multiples of each other. Express solutions in terms of a free parameter. \text{Solve the 3×3 system: } x+y+z=6, \; 2x-y+z=3, \; x+2y-z=2 Set up the augmented matrix and use row operations to reach row echelon form. x=1, \; y=2, \; z=3 \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\2&-1&1&|&3\\1&2&-1&|&2\end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_2-2R_1,\;R_3-R_1} \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\0&-3&-1&|&-9\\0&1&-2&|&-4\end{pmatrix} Eliminate $x$ from rows 2 and 3. \xrightarrow{R_3+\tfrac{1}{3}R_2} \begin{pmatrix}1&1&1&|&6\\0&-3&-1&|&-9\\0&0&-\tfrac{7}{3}&|&-7\end{pmatrix} Eliminate $y$ from row 3. z = 3, \quad y = \tfrac{-9+1(3)}{-3} = 2, \quad x = 6-2-3=1 Back-substitute from bottom up. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Complex Numbers & the Complex Plane
documents.subtitle: Inventing $i$ was not arbitrary — it was the only way to make algebra complete
documents.tags: complex numbers imaginary unit complex plane modulus argument Euler formula conjugate
documents.assignment: Dividing complex numbers \text{Compute } \dfrac{3+4i}{1-2i}.   Always multiply by the conjugate to divide. The denominator becomes real (a sum of squares), and the result is in standard form. = \frac{(3+4i)(1+2i)}{(1-2i)(1+2i)} Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator. = \frac{3+6i+4i+8i^2}{1+4} = \frac{3+10i-8}{5} Expand. Use $i^2 = -1$. = \frac{-5+10i}{5} = -1+2i Simplify. The answer is a complex number in standard form. Finding all cube roots of 1 \text{Find all solutions to } z^3 = 1 \text{ in } \mathbb{C}.   The $n$ nth-roots of any complex number are equally spaced on a circle in the complex plane. This geometric regularity is only visible through the complex plane. 1 = e^{i \cdot 0} = e^{i \cdot 2\pi} = e^{i \cdot 4\pi} Write 1 in polar form with all equivalent angles. z = e^{i \cdot 2\pi k/3}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2 Apply De Moivre for cube roots: divide angles by 3. z_0=1, \quad z_1=e^{2\pi i/3}=-\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i, \quad z_2=e^{4\pi i/3}=-\tfrac{1}{2}-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i Three roots equally spaced at 120° on the unit circle.  \text{Use Euler's formula to prove: } \cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta. Expand $e^{i \cdot 2\theta} = (e^{i\theta})^2$ using Euler's formula on both sides. \cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta  e^{i \cdot 2\theta} = \cos 2\theta + i\sin 2\theta Euler on the left. (e^{i\theta})^2 = (\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)^2 = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta + 2i\sin\theta\cos\theta Expand the square. \text{Equate real parts: } \cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \qquad \blacksquare Imaginary parts give $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ as a bonus.
documents.applications: Complex numbers are not a mathematical curiosity — they are the language of electrical engineering (impedance $Z = R + jX$), signal processing (Fourier transforms), and quantum mechanics (wavefunctions are complex-valued). Every AC circuit analysis uses complex exponentials. Every digital filter is designed in the complex plane. Complex number anatomy z = \underbrace{a}_{\text{real part}} + \underbrace{b}_{\text{imaginary part}}i \qquad a, b \in \mathbb{R} \\ |z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \text{ (modulus = distance from origin)} \\ \arg(z) = \theta = \arctan(b/a) \text{ (argument = angle from positive real axis)} Multiplication = rotation + scaling |z_1 z_2| = |z_1||z_2| \qquad \arg(z_1 z_2) = \arg(z_1) + \arg(z_2) \\ \text{Multiplying complex numbers multiplies magnitudes and adds angles.} Euler's formula — the most beautiful equation e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta \\ \text{At } \theta = \pi: \quad e^{i\pi} = -1 \implies e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 \\ \text{This connects }e, i, \pi, 1, 0 \text{ — the five fundamental constants.}
documents.content: What does it mean to rotate a number? Multiplying by $i$ does exactly that — rotates 90° in the complex plane. This is not a coincidence. It is the key to Euler's formula $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$. Complex numbers are not a mathematical curiosity — they are the language of electrical engineering (impedance $Z = R + jX$), signal processing (Fourier transforms), and quantum mechanics (wavefunctions are complex-valued). Every AC circuit analysis uses complex exponentials. Every digital filter is designed in the complex plane. The real numbers have a gap: $x^2 = -1$ has no solution. Rather than accepting this as impossible, mathematicians defined $i$ as the number satisfying $i^2 = -1$. This is not magic — it is the same move made when extending naturals to integers (to solve $x + 5 = 3$) and integers to rationals (to solve $2x = 3$). Each extension filled a gap. A complex number $a + bi$ is a point $(a, b)$ in the complex plane. The real axis is the $x$-axis; the imaginary axis is the $y$-axis. Addition of complex numbers is vector addition — add the real parts, add the imaginary parts. Multiplication is more interesting: it rotates and scales simultaneously. Multiplying by $i$ rotates 90° counterclockwise: $1 \to i \to -1 \to -i \to 1$. Four multiplications by $i$ bring you full circle. This is where the mysterious pattern $i^1=i, i^2=-1, i^3=-i, i^4=1$ comes from — it is rotation, not arithmetic magic. The fundamental theorem of algebra — every polynomial of degree $n$ with complex coefficients has exactly $n$ roots in $\mathbb{C}$ (counted with multiplicity) — means the complex numbers are algebraically closed. No further extensions are needed. Every polynomial factors completely there. Complex number anatomy z = \underbrace{a}_{\text{real part}} + \underbrace{b}_{\text{imaginary part}}i \qquad a, b \in \mathbb{R} \\ |z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \text{ (modulus = distance from origin)} \\ \arg(z) = \theta = \arctan(b/a) \text{ (argument = angle from positive real axis)} Multiplication = rotation + scaling |z_1 z_2| = |z_1||z_2| \qquad \arg(z_1 z_2) = \arg(z_1) + \arg(z_2) \\ \text{Multiplying complex numbers multiplies magnitudes and adds angles.} Euler's formula — the most beautiful equation e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta \\ \text{At } \theta = \pi: \quad e^{i\pi} = -1 \implies e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0 \\ \text{This connects }e, i, \pi, 1, 0 \text{ — the five fundamental constants.} Complex arithmetic (a+bi)+(c+di) = (a+c)+(b+d)i \\ (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd)+(ad+bc)i \\ \overline{a+bi} = a-bi \quad (\text{conjugate}) \\ \frac{a+bi}{c+di} = \frac{(a+bi)\overline{(c+di)}}{|c+di|^2} \quad (\text{multiply by conjugate over conjugate}) De Moivre's theorem (r\,e^{i\theta})^n = r^n e^{in\theta} = r^n(\cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta) \\ \text{Used to find }n\text{th roots: each root is } r^{1/n}e^{i(\theta+2\pi k)/n}, \; k=0,1,\ldots,n-1 The Complex Plane — Multiplication as Rotation Multiplying by $e^{i\theta}$ is a pure rotation by $\theta$ — no scaling. This is the geometric heart of Euler's formula. Dividing complex numbers \text{Compute } \dfrac{3+4i}{1-2i}. Always multiply by the conjugate to divide. The denominator becomes real (a sum of squares), and the result is in standard form. = \frac{(3+4i)(1+2i)}{(1-2i)(1+2i)} Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator. = \frac{3+6i+4i+8i^2}{1+4} = \frac{3+10i-8}{5} Expand. Use $i^2 = -1$. = \frac{-5+10i}{5} = -1+2i Simplify. The answer is a complex number in standard form. Finding all cube roots of 1 \text{Find all solutions to } z^3 = 1 \text{ in } \mathbb{C}. The $n$ nth-roots of any complex number are equally spaced on a circle in the complex plane. This geometric regularity is only visible through the complex plane. 1 = e^{i \cdot 0} = e^{i \cdot 2\pi} = e^{i \cdot 4\pi} Write 1 in polar form with all equivalent angles. z = e^{i \cdot 2\pi k/3}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2 Apply De Moivre for cube roots: divide angles by 3. z_0=1, \quad z_1=e^{2\pi i/3}=-\tfrac{1}{2}+\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i, \quad z_2=e^{4\pi i/3}=-\tfrac{1}{2}-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i Three roots equally spaced at 120° on the unit circle. \text{Use Euler's formula to prove: } \cos(2\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta. Expand $e^{i \cdot 2\theta} = (e^{i\theta})^2$ using Euler's formula on both sides. \cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta e^{i \cdot 2\theta} = \cos 2\theta + i\sin 2\theta Euler on the left. (e^{i\theta})^2 = (\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)^2 = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta + 2i\sin\theta\cos\theta Expand the square. \text{Equate real parts: } \cos 2\theta = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \qquad \blacksquare Imaginary parts give $\sin 2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$ as a bonus. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Inequalities, Absolute Value & Sign Analysis
documents.subtitle: The number line is your workspace — and sign charts do the heavy lifting
documents.tags: inequalities absolute value sign chart interval notation compound inequalities
documents.assignment: Absolute value inequality — distance interpretation \text{Solve: } |x - 4| \leq 3.   Geometric interpretation: $x$ is within distance 3 of 4. The solution is the interval $[1,7]$, centred at 4 with radius 3. -3 \leq x - 4 \leq 3 $|A| \leq 3$ means $-3 \leq A \leq 3$. 1 \leq x \leq 7 Add 4 throughout. Rational inequality using sign chart \text{Solve: } \dfrac{x+1}{x-2} \geq 0   For rational inequalities, always identify BOTH zeros (numerator) and undefined points (denominator). Zeros can be included or excluded based on the inequality sign; undefined points are always excluded. \text{Critical points: } x = -1 \text{ (zero)} \text{ and } x=2 \text{ (undefined)} The sign can only change at zeros of numerator or zeros of denominator. x=-2: \frac{-}{-}=+\geq 0 \checkmark \quad x=0: \frac{+}{-}=-<0 \times \quad x=3: \frac{+}{+}=+\geq 0 \checkmark Test each interval. x \in (-\infty, -1] \cup (2, \infty) Include $x=-1$ (zero, fraction equals 0 — satisfies $\geq 0$). Exclude $x=2$ (undefined).  \text{Find the domain of } f(x) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x^2-4}{x-1}\right). The argument of $\ln$ must be strictly positive. Solve $\frac{x^2-4}{x-1} > 0$ using a sign chart. x \in (-2,1) \cup (2,\infty)  x^2-4 = (x-2)(x+2) Factor numerator. \text{Critical points: } x=-2, 1, 2 Zeros at $\pm 2$, undefined at $1$. \text{Signs: } (-\infty,-2): +/- = - \;|\; (-2,1): -/- = + \;|\; (1,2): -/- = + \text{ (wait: check)} At $x=0$: $(0-2)(0+2)/(0-1) = (-4)/(-1) = 4 > 0$. At $x=1.5$: $(-.5)(3.5)/(0.5) < 0$. x \in (-2, 1) \cup (2, \infty) Where the expression is positive. Verify each interval carefully.
documents.applications: Domain restrictions in calculus (square roots, logarithms, denominators) are inequality problems. The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of a limit is built from inequalities. Intervals of increase and decrease for a function — central to curve sketching — are found using sign analysis of the derivative. Sign chart procedure \text{To solve } f(x) > 0: \\ 1.\text{ Find zeros/undefined points of } f. \\ 2.\text{ Mark on number line → creates intervals.} \\ 3.\text{ Test one point per interval.} \\ 4.\text{ Sign is constant throughout each interval.} Absolute value inequality rules |f(x)| < k \iff -k < f(x) < k \quad (k > 0) \\ |f(x)| > k \iff f(x) > k \text{ OR } f(x) < -k \\ |f(x)| = k \iff f(x) = k \text{ OR } f(x) = -k Multiplying by a negative — the sign flips -2x > 6 \implies x < -3 \quad (\text{dividing by } -2 \text{ flips} >) \\ \text{When multiplying/dividing both sides by a negative, the inequality reverses.}
documents.content: Solving $x^2 - x - 6 > 0$ is hard to do by algebra alone. A sign chart makes it visual and automatic — and is the same tool you'll use in calculus to find where a function is increasing. Domain restrictions in calculus (square roots, logarithms, denominators) are inequality problems. The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of a limit is built from inequalities. Intervals of increase and decrease for a function — central to curve sketching — are found using sign analysis of the derivative. An inequality is a comparison, not an equation. Its solution is usually an interval (a range of values) rather than isolated points. The solution lives on the number line — your job is to identify which portions satisfy the inequality. The sign chart method works for any inequality that can be written as $f(x) > 0$ (or $< 0$, $\geq 0$, $\leq 0$): (1) find all zeros and undefined points of $f(x)$ — these are the only places the sign can change; (2) plot them on a number line, dividing it into intervals; (3) test one point in each interval; (4) the sign is constant throughout each interval. Absolute value inequalities always split into two cases. $|f(x)| < k$ means $-k < f(x) < k$ — a compound inequality. $|f(x)| > k$ means $f(x) > k$ OR $f(x) < -k$ — a disjunction. Drawing the absolute value graph makes this geometrically obvious. Sign chart procedure \text{To solve } f(x) > 0: \\ 1.\text{ Find zeros/undefined points of } f. \\ 2.\text{ Mark on number line → creates intervals.} \\ 3.\text{ Test one point per interval.} \\ 4.\text{ Sign is constant throughout each interval.} Absolute value inequality rules |f(x)| < k \iff -k < f(x) < k \quad (k > 0) \\ |f(x)| > k \iff f(x) > k \text{ OR } f(x) < -k \\ |f(x)| = k \iff f(x) = k \text{ OR } f(x) = -k Multiplying by a negative — the sign flips -2x > 6 \implies x < -3 \quad (\text{dividing by } -2 \text{ flips} >) \\ \text{When multiplying/dividing both sides by a negative, the inequality reverses.} Interval notation (a,b): \text{ open (excludes endpoints)} \qquad [a,b]: \text{ closed (includes endpoints)} \\ [a,b): \text{ half-open} \qquad (-\infty, a): \text{ unbounded left} \\ \cup: \text{ union (OR)} \qquad \cap: \text{ intersection (AND)} Connecting to calculus: domain of composite functions \text{Domain of } \sqrt{f(x)}: \text{ solve } f(x) \geq 0 \text{ using sign chart.} \\ \text{Domain of } \ln(f(x)): \text{ solve } f(x) > 0. \\ \text{Domain of } \frac{1}{f(x)}: \text{ solve } f(x) \neq 0. Sign Chart — Visualising Inequality Solutions The sign only changes at zeros (odd multiplicity) or undefined points. It is constant everywhere else. Absolute value inequality — distance interpretation \text{Solve: } |x - 4| \leq 3. Geometric interpretation: $x$ is within distance 3 of 4. The solution is the interval $[1,7]$, centred at 4 with radius 3. -3 \leq x - 4 \leq 3 $|A| \leq 3$ means $-3 \leq A \leq 3$. 1 \leq x \leq 7 Add 4 throughout. Rational inequality using sign chart \text{Solve: } \dfrac{x+1}{x-2} \geq 0 For rational inequalities, always identify BOTH zeros (numerator) and undefined points (denominator). Zeros can be included or excluded based on the inequality sign; undefined points are always excluded. \text{Critical points: } x = -1 \text{ (zero)} \text{ and } x=2 \text{ (undefined)} The sign can only change at zeros of numerator or zeros of denominator. x=-2: \frac{-}{-}=+\geq 0 \checkmark \quad x=0: \frac{+}{-}=-<0 \times \quad x=3: \frac{+}{+}=+\geq 0 \checkmark Test each interval. x \in (-\infty, -1] \cup (2, \infty) Include $x=-1$ (zero, fraction equals 0 — satisfies $\geq 0$). Exclude $x=2$ (undefined). \text{Find the domain of } f(x) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x^2-4}{x-1}\right). The argument of $\ln$ must be strictly positive. Solve $\frac{x^2-4}{x-1} > 0$ using a sign chart. x \in (-2,1) \cup (2,\infty) x^2-4 = (x-2)(x+2) Factor numerator. \text{Critical points: } x=-2, 1, 2 Zeros at $\pm 2$, undefined at $1$. \text{Signs: } (-\infty,-2): +/- = - \;|\; (-2,1): -/- = + \;|\; (1,2): -/- = + \text{ (wait: check)} At $x=0$: $(0-2)(0+2)/(0-1) = (-4)/(-1) = 4 > 0$. At $x=1.5$: $(-.5)(3.5)/(0.5) < 0$. x \in (-2, 1) \cup (2, \infty) Where the expression is positive. Verify each interval carefully. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Linear Programming & Optimization

documents.tags: linear programming optimization constraints feasible region objective function vertex theorem

documents.assignment: Maximizing profit with two variables \text{Maximize } P = 3x + 2y \text{ subject to: } \\ x + 2y leq 4, quad x - y leq 1, quad x geq 0, quad y geq 0.   The maximum profit of 8 is achieved by producing 2 units of $x$ and 1 unit of $y$. \text{Vertices: } (0,0), (1,0), (2,1), (0,2) Graphing reveals the vertices. $(2,1)$ is the intersection of $x+2y=4$ and $x-y=1$. P(0,0)=0, \quad P(1,0)=3, \quad P(2,1)=3(2)+2(1)=8, \quad P(0,2)=4 Plug each vertex into $P = 3x + 2y$. P_{max} = 8 \text{ at } (2,1) Compare the values.
documents.applications: Linear programming is the backbone of logistics and supply chain management. From airline scheduling to hospital staffing and diet optimization, the goal is always to maximize a benefit (profit, throughput) or minimize a cost (waste, time) given limited resources (fuel, beds, budget). Problems involving thousands of variables are solved daily using the simplex algorithm, which builds on the graphical methods taught here. Objective Function vs. Constraints \text{Objective: } f(x,y) = ax + by quad \text{(what you want to maximize/minimize)} \\ \text{Constraints: } c_1x + d_1y leq k_1, dots quad \text{(the rules you must follow)} The Vertex Theorem \text{If a linear programming problem has a solution, it must occur at a vertex of the feasible region.} Bounded vs. Unbounded Regions \text{If the feasible region is bounded (a closed polygon), both max and min exist.} \\ \text{If it is unbounded, a maximum might not exist if the function can grow infinitely.}
documents.content: A factory makes two products with different profit margins and different resource requirements. How many of each should they make to maximize profit without running out of materials? Linear programming is the backbone of logistics and supply chain management. From airline scheduling to hospital staffing and diet optimization, the goal is always to maximize a benefit (profit, throughput) or minimize a cost (waste, time) given limited resources (fuel, beds, budget). Problems involving thousands of variables are solved daily using the simplex algorithm, which builds on the graphical methods taught here. Linear programming is a technique for finding the maximum or minimum value of a **linear objective function** (like profit $P = 5x + 3y$) subject to a set of **linear constraints** (inequalities like $x + y leq 10$). The set of all points that satisfy all constraints simultaneously is called the **feasible region**. Because the constraints are linear, this region is always a polygon (in 2D). The **Vertex Theorem** (or Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming) states that the optimal value (max or min) always occurs at one of the vertices (corners) of the feasible region. This means instead of checking infinitely many points, you only need to check the corners. To solve a linear programming problem: (1) Graph each constraint inequality to find the feasible region. (2) Find the coordinates of every vertex (intersection of boundary lines). (3) Evaluate the objective function at each vertex. (4) The largest resulting value is the maximum; the smallest is the minimum. Finding vertices involves solving systems of two linear equations (the boundary lines) at each corner of the polygon. Objective Function vs. Constraints \text{Objective: } f(x,y) = ax + by quad \text{(what you want to maximize/minimize)} \\ \text{Constraints: } c_1x + d_1y leq k_1, dots quad \text{(the rules you must follow)} The Vertex Theorem \text{If a linear programming problem has a solution, it must occur at a vertex of the feasible region.} Bounded vs. Unbounded Regions \text{If the feasible region is bounded (a closed polygon), both max and min exist.} \\ \text{If it is unbounded, a maximum might not exist if the function can grow infinitely.} Feasible Region \text{The intersection of all half-planes defined by the constraints. Every point in this region is a "possible" solution.} Maximizing profit with two variables \text{Maximize } P = 3x + 2y \text{ subject to: } \\ x + 2y leq 4, quad x - y leq 1, quad x geq 0, quad y geq 0. The maximum profit of 8 is achieved by producing 2 units of $x$ and 1 unit of $y$. \text{Vertices: } (0,0), (1,0), (2,1), (0,2) Graphing reveals the vertices. $(2,1)$ is the intersection of $x+2y=4$ and $x-y=1$. P(0,0)=0, \quad P(1,0)=3, \quad P(2,1)=3(2)+2(1)=8, \quad P(0,2)=4 Plug each vertex into $P = 3x + 2y$. P_{max} = 8 \text{ at } (2,1) Compare the values. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step maximize minimize maxima minima best value

documents.title: Angles: The Foundation of Trigonometry
documents.subtitle: What an angle actually is, how we measure it two different ways, and why radians are the natural choice for calculus
documents.tags: angles degrees radians complementary supplementary acute obtuse reflex angle measurement
documents.assignment: Converting between degrees and radians \text{Convert: (a) } 135° \text{ to radians, (b) } \frac{7\pi}{4} \text{ to degrees.}   $\pi$ always cancels in radian-to-degree conversion. If it does not cancel cleanly, leave the answer in terms of $\pi$. 135° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{135\pi}{180} = \frac{3\pi}{4} Multiply by $\pi/180$. Simplify the fraction: $\gcd(135,180)=45$, so $135/180 = 3/4$. \frac{7\pi}{4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \frac{7 \times 180}{4} = \frac{1260}{4} = 315° Multiply by $180/\pi$. The $\pi$ cancels. Arc length and sector area \text{A circle has radius 8 cm. Find (a) the arc length and (b) sector area for a central angle of } 150°.   Both formulas are clean in radians. In degrees they would each need a $\pi/180$ factor — one more reason radians are the natural choice. 150° = 150 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{5\pi}{6} \text{ rad} Convert to radians first — the formulas require it. s = r\theta = 8 \times \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{40\pi}{6} = \frac{20\pi}{3} \approx 20.9 \text{ cm} Arc length formula. A = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(64)\cdot\frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{320\pi}{12} = \frac{80\pi}{3} \approx 83.8 \text{ cm}^2 Sector area formula. Finding coterminal angles \text{Find a positive and a negative angle coterminal with } \frac{2\pi}{3}.   Infinitely many coterminal angles exist. The one in $[0, 2\pi)$ is the "principal" representative. To find it, add or subtract $2\pi$ repeatedly until the result lands in range. \frac{2\pi}{3} + 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{6\pi}{3} = \frac{8\pi}{3} Add one full rotation for a positive coterminal angle. \frac{2\pi}{3} - 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3} - \frac{6\pi}{3} = -\frac{4\pi}{3} Subtract one full rotation for a negative coterminal angle.  \text{A wheel of radius 30 cm rotates at 120 rpm. Find the linear speed of a point on the rim in m/s.} Convert rpm to radians per second first: 1 revolution = $2\pi$ rad. v = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s}  \omega = 120 \times 2\pi = 240\pi \text{ rad/min} = \frac{240\pi}{60} = 4\pi \text{ rad/s} Angular velocity in rad/s. v = r\omega = 0.30 \times 4\pi = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s} Linear speed = radius × angular velocity. Convert radius to metres.  \text{Two supplementary angles are in the ratio } 2:7. \text{ Find each in degrees and radians.} If the angles are $2k$ and $7k$, they sum to $180°$. 40° \text{ and } 140°  2k + 7k = 180° \Rightarrow 9k = 180° \Rightarrow k = 20° Set up and solve. 2k = 40° = \frac{2\pi}{9} \text{ rad} \qquad 7k = 140° = \frac{7\pi}{9} \text{ rad} The two angles, in both units.
documents.applications: The Babylonians chose 360 for a full circle around 2000 BCE, probably because 360 has many divisors and approximates the days in a year. Radians were not formalised until the 19th century — but they are the reason $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x$ holds without any ugly constants. In engineering, rotational speed in radians per second connects directly to frequency in a way degrees cannot. Angle classification by size \text{Acute: } 0° < \theta < 90° \qquad \text{Right: } \theta = 90° \\ \text{Obtuse: } 90° < \theta < 180° \qquad \text{Straight: } \theta = 180° \\ \text{Reflex: } 180° < \theta < 360° \qquad \text{Full: } \theta = 360° Angle pair relationships \text{Complementary: sum} = 90° \qquad \text{Supplementary: sum} = 180° \\ \text{Vertical angles: opposite angles when two lines cross — always equal} \\ \text{Co-interior (same-side): sum} = 180° \text{ when lines are parallel} Why the radian is natural s = r\theta \quad (\theta \text{ in radians}) \\ \text{Arc length} = \text{radius} \times \text{angle. No conversion factor needed.} \\ \text{In degrees: } s = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180} \text{ — that } \pi/180 \text{ never goes away.} Degree ↔ Radian conversion \theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\deg} \times \frac{\pi}{180} \qquad \theta_{\deg} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\ \text{Key values: } 30° = \frac{\pi}{6}, \; 45° = \frac{\pi}{4}, \; 60° = \frac{\pi}{3}, \; 90° = \frac{\pi}{2}, \; 180° = \pi
documents.content: Degrees are completely arbitrary — someone decided a full turn is 360, and that number stuck for thousands of years. Radians are different. They are the only angle measure that makes calculus formulas simple. Why? The Babylonians chose 360 for a full circle around 2000 BCE, probably because 360 has many divisors and approximates the days in a year. Radians were not formalised until the 19th century — but they are the reason $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x$ holds without any ugly constants. In engineering, rotational speed in radians per second connects directly to frequency in a way degrees cannot. An angle is formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint called the vertex. The angle measures the rotation from one ray (the initial side) to the other (the terminal side). Direction matters: counterclockwise is positive by convention, clockwise is negative. Degrees divide a full rotation into 360 equal parts. This is arbitrary but convenient — 360 is divisible by 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 18, 20, 24, 30, 36, 40, 45, 60, 72, 90, 120, 180. Almost any fraction of a circle comes out to a whole number of degrees. Radians measure angles by arc length. One radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius. Since the circumference is $2\pi r$, a full circle subtends $2\pi$ radians. This is not a convention — it follows directly from the definition of $\pi$. Radians are dimensionless: they are a ratio of lengths. The calculus reason radians win: $\lim_{\theta \to 0} \frac{\sin\theta}{\theta} = 1$ only when $\theta$ is in radians. This limit is the foundation of every trig derivative. In degrees, the limit gives $\pi/180$ instead — a constant that would infect every derivative formula forever. Degrees-minutes-seconds (DMS) notation subdivides degrees the same way hours subdivide time: $1° = 60^\prime$ (arcminutes) and $1^\prime = 60^{\prime\prime}$ (arcseconds). GPS coordinates use decimal degrees; astronomical measurements often use DMS. Converting: $37°22^\prime 48^{\prime\prime} = 37 + 22/60 + 48/3600 = 37.38°$. An angle in standard position has its vertex at the origin and its initial side along the positive $x$-axis. The terminal side determines the angle. Coterminal angles have the same terminal side but differ by full rotations: $\theta$ and $\theta + 360°k$ (or $\theta + 2\pi k$) are coterminal for any integer $k$. The arc length formula $s = r\theta$ (radians) and area of a sector $A = \frac{1}{2}r^2\theta$ (radians) are direct consequences of the radian definition. These formulas are used constantly in physics and engineering — rotational kinetic energy, moment of inertia, angular velocity. Angle classification by size \text{Acute: } 0° < \theta < 90° \qquad \text{Right: } \theta = 90° \\ \text{Obtuse: } 90° < \theta < 180° \qquad \text{Straight: } \theta = 180° \\ \text{Reflex: } 180° < \theta < 360° \qquad \text{Full: } \theta = 360° Angle pair relationships \text{Complementary: sum} = 90° \qquad \text{Supplementary: sum} = 180° \\ \text{Vertical angles: opposite angles when two lines cross — always equal} \\ \text{Co-interior (same-side): sum} = 180° \text{ when lines are parallel} Why the radian is natural s = r\theta \quad (\theta \text{ in radians}) \\ \text{Arc length} = \text{radius} \times \text{angle. No conversion factor needed.} \\ \text{In degrees: } s = \frac{\pi r \theta}{180} \text{ — that } \pi/180 \text{ never goes away.} Degree ↔ Radian conversion \theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\deg} \times \frac{\pi}{180} \qquad \theta_{\deg} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\ \text{Key values: } 30° = \frac{\pi}{6}, \; 45° = \frac{\pi}{4}, \; 60° = \frac{\pi}{3}, \; 90° = \frac{\pi}{2}, \; 180° = \pi Standard position and coterminal angles \text{Standard position: vertex at origin, initial side along positive }x\text{-axis} \\ \text{Coterminal: } \theta + 360°k \text{ or } \theta + 2\pi k \text{ for any integer } k \\ \text{e.g. } 390°, 30°, -330° \text{ are all coterminal} Arc length and sector area (radians only) s = r\theta \qquad A = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2\theta \qquad (\theta \text{ must be in radians}) \\ \text{Angular velocity: } \omega = \frac{\theta}{t} \text{ rad/s} \qquad \text{Linear speed: } v = r\omega The most common radian error s = r\theta \text{ requires } \theta \text{ in radians — not degrees.} \\ \text{An arc of a circle with } r=5, \theta=60°: \\ \text{Wrong: } s = 5 \times 60 = 300 \qquad \text{Right: } s = 5 \times \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 5.24 Angle Measurement — Degrees and Radians Live One radian is the angle where the arc length equals the radius. Drag to feel it. Introduction to Angles Angle Measurement in Degrees Introduction to Radians Converting between degrees and radians \text{Convert: (a) } 135° \text{ to radians, (b) } \frac{7\pi}{4} \text{ to degrees.} $\pi$ always cancels in radian-to-degree conversion. If it does not cancel cleanly, leave the answer in terms of $\pi$. 135° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{135\pi}{180} = \frac{3\pi}{4} Multiply by $\pi/180$. Simplify the fraction: $\gcd(135,180)=45$, so $135/180 = 3/4$. \frac{7\pi}{4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \frac{7 \times 180}{4} = \frac{1260}{4} = 315° Multiply by $180/\pi$. The $\pi$ cancels. Arc length and sector area \text{A circle has radius 8 cm. Find (a) the arc length and (b) sector area for a central angle of } 150°. Both formulas are clean in radians. In degrees they would each need a $\pi/180$ factor — one more reason radians are the natural choice. 150° = 150 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{5\pi}{6} \text{ rad} Convert to radians first — the formulas require it. s = r\theta = 8 \times \frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{40\pi}{6} = \frac{20\pi}{3} \approx 20.9 \text{ cm} Arc length formula. A = \tfrac{1}{2}r^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(64)\cdot\frac{5\pi}{6} = \frac{320\pi}{12} = \frac{80\pi}{3} \approx 83.8 \text{ cm}^2 Sector area formula. Finding coterminal angles \text{Find a positive and a negative angle coterminal with } \frac{2\pi}{3}. Infinitely many coterminal angles exist. The one in $[0, 2\pi)$ is the "principal" representative. To find it, add or subtract $2\pi$ repeatedly until the result lands in range. \frac{2\pi}{3} + 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3} + \frac{6\pi}{3} = \frac{8\pi}{3} Add one full rotation for a positive coterminal angle. \frac{2\pi}{3} - 2\pi = \frac{2\pi}{3} - \frac{6\pi}{3} = -\frac{4\pi}{3} Subtract one full rotation for a negative coterminal angle. \text{A wheel of radius 30 cm rotates at 120 rpm. Find the linear speed of a point on the rim in m/s.} Convert rpm to radians per second first: 1 revolution = $2\pi$ rad. v = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s} \omega = 120 \times 2\pi = 240\pi \text{ rad/min} = \frac{240\pi}{60} = 4\pi \text{ rad/s} Angular velocity in rad/s. v = r\omega = 0.30 \times 4\pi = 1.2\pi \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s} Linear speed = radius × angular velocity. Convert radius to metres. \text{Two supplementary angles are in the ratio } 2:7. \text{ Find each in degrees and radians.} If the angles are $2k$ and $7k$, they sum to $180°$. 40° \text{ and } 140° 2k + 7k = 180° \Rightarrow 9k = 180° \Rightarrow k = 20° Set up and solve. 2k = 40° = \frac{2\pi}{9} \text{ rad} \qquad 7k = 140° = \frac{7\pi}{9} \text{ rad} The two angles, in both units. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Triangle Geometry: The Foundation Everything Else Stands On
documents.subtitle: Thales' theorem, similar triangles, the Pythagorean theorem proved, and Pythagorean triples
documents.tags: triangles similar triangles congruent triangles Pythagorean theorem Pythagorean triples Thales' theorem triangle sum distance formula 3D
documents.assignment: Using similar triangles to find an unknown side \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF. \text{ If } AB=6, BC=9, AC=12, \text{ and } DE=4, \text{ find } EF \text{ and } DF.   Similar triangles: find the scale factor from any pair of corresponding sides, then multiply all others by it. \text{Scale factor: } k = \frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} Find the ratio of corresponding sides. DE corresponds to AB. EF = k \cdot BC = \frac{2}{3} \times 9 = 6 \qquad DF = k \cdot AC = \frac{2}{3} \times 12 = 8 Multiply all sides of the original triangle by the scale factor. Verifying and generating Pythagorean triples \text{(a) Verify 8-15-17 is a Pythagorean triple. (b) Generate a triple using } m=3, n=1.   The generating formula produces all primitive triples when $\gcd(m,n)=1$ and $m-n$ is odd. Multiples of primitives also work. 8^2 + 15^2 = 64 + 225 = 289 = 17^2 \checkmark Check: does $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$? m=3, n=1: \quad a = m^2-n^2 = 8, \quad b = 2mn = 6, \quad c = m^2+n^2 = 10 Apply the generating formula. This gives the triple (6, 8, 10) — a multiple of (3, 4, 5). 3D distance between two points \text{Find the distance between } A(1, -2, 4) \text{ and } B(3, 1, -1).   The 3D distance formula is exactly the Pythagorean theorem applied in three dimensions. There is nothing new to memorise — just one more square under the root. d = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (1-(-2))^2 + (-1-4)^2} Apply the 3D distance formula. = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 25} = \sqrt{38} Two applications of the Pythagorean theorem: first in the $xy$-plane, then adding the $z$ component.  \text{A 10-foot ladder leans against a wall. Its base is 6 feet from the wall. How high up the wall does it reach? Is the foot-to-top distance a Pythagorean triple?} This is a right triangle with hypotenuse 10 and one leg 6. h = 8 \text{ ft; yes, } (6,8,10) = 2\times(3,4,5)  h^2 + 6^2 = 10^2 \Rightarrow h^2 = 100-36 = 64 \Rightarrow h = 8 Pythagorean theorem. The height is 8 feet. (6, 8, 10) = 2 \times (3, 4, 5) \checkmark Yes — a multiple of the 3-4-5 triple. Recognising triples saves time on exams.  \text{Prove: the midpoint of the hypotenuse of a right triangle is equidistant from all three vertices.} Place the right angle at the origin. The midpoint of the hypotenuse is the average of the two other vertices. |OM| = |AM| = |BM| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} = \tfrac{1}{2}|AB|  \text{Let the right angle be at } O=(0,0), A=(2a,0), B=(0,2b). \text{ Midpoint } M = (a,b). Convenient coordinates. Using $2a$ and $2b$ makes the midpoint calculation clean. |OM| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad |AM| = \sqrt{(2a-a)^2+b^2} = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad |BM| = \sqrt{a^2+(2b-b)^2} = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} All three distances equal $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ — the midpoint is equidistant from all three vertices. \therefore M \text{ is the circumcentre of the right triangle, and the circumradius} = \tfrac{1}{2}|AB|. \qquad \blacksquare This is why Thales' theorem works: the circumcentre of a right triangle lies exactly at the midpoint of the hypotenuse.
documents.applications: Similar triangles are the engine behind trigonometry: the trig ratios are defined for a right triangle, but they work for any angle because all right triangles with the same angle are similar. Pythagorean triples appear in construction (the 3-4-5 rope trick to make a right angle), GPS signal processing, and error-correcting codes. The 3D distance formula underlies every computation in 3D graphics and physics. Triangle angle sum theorem \text{In any triangle: } \alpha + \beta + \gamma = 180° \\ \text{Exterior angle} = \text{sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles} Similar triangle properties \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF \iff \text{angles equal AND sides proportional} \\ \frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} = \frac{AC}{DF} = k \quad (\text{scale factor}) Thales' theorem — an angle inscribed in a semicircle \text{If } AB \text{ is a diameter and } C \text{ is any point on the circle, then } \angle ACB = 90°. \\ \text{This is why the unit circle definition of trig connects to right triangles.} Pythagorean triples — generating formula (m^2-n^2,\; 2mn,\; m^2+n^2) \text{ for integers } m > n > 0 \\ m=2,n=1 \to (3,4,5) \quad m=3,n=2 \to (5,12,13) \\ m=4,n=1 \to (15,8,17) \quad m=4,n=3 \to (7,24,25)
documents.content: Thales of Miletus, around 600 BCE, proved that any angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle. He reportedly discovered this while looking at reflections in water. How do you prove something that visually obvious — and why does it matter for trigonometry? Similar triangles are the engine behind trigonometry: the trig ratios are defined for a right triangle, but they work for any angle because all right triangles with the same angle are similar. Pythagorean triples appear in construction (the 3-4-5 rope trick to make a right angle), GPS signal processing, and error-correcting codes. The 3D distance formula underlies every computation in 3D graphics and physics. The angles of any triangle always sum to 180°. This is not just a rule — it follows from the parallel line axiom of Euclidean geometry. Draw a line through the apex of any triangle parallel to the base. The three angles of the triangle line up along it, making a straight angle of 180°. This proof works for every triangle, every time. Two triangles are similar (∼) if their angles match — one is a scaled copy of the other. Similar triangles have equal ratios of corresponding sides. This is why the trigonometric ratios are well-defined: in any right triangle with a given acute angle, the ratio opposite/hypotenuse is always the same number, regardless of the triangle's size. That number is the sine of the angle. Two triangles are congruent (≅) if they have the same angles and the same side lengths — they are identical, not just scaled copies. The congruence conditions (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL for right triangles) tell you the minimum information needed to guarantee two triangles are identical. The Pythagorean theorem $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ has over 370 known proofs. The most visual is the area rearrangement: start with a square of side $(a+b)$, place four copies of the right triangle inside it, and the remaining area is $c^2$. Rearrange the triangles differently and the remaining area shows $a^2 + b^2$. Same total area → same result. A Pythagorean triple is a set of three positive integers $(a, b, c)$ satisfying $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. All primitive triples (no common factor) are generated by $m^2-n^2$, $2mn$, $m^2+n^2$ for coprime integers $m > n > 0$ with $m-n$ odd. The 3-4-5 triple has been used in construction since ancient Egypt — stretch a 12-unit rope into a 3-4-5 triangle and you have a perfect right angle. The 3D distance formula extends the 2D version by one more Pythagorean step. Given points $(x_1,y_1,z_1)$ and $(x_2,y_2,z_2)$: first find the horizontal distance $d_{xy} = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2+(\Delta y)^2}$, then the full 3D distance $d = \sqrt{d_{xy}^2 + (\Delta z)^2} = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2+(\Delta y)^2+(\Delta z)^2}$. It is the Pythagorean theorem applied twice. Triangle angle sum theorem \text{In any triangle: } \alpha + \beta + \gamma = 180° \\ \text{Exterior angle} = \text{sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles} Similar triangle properties \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF \iff \text{angles equal AND sides proportional} \\ \frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} = \frac{AC}{DF} = k \quad (\text{scale factor}) Thales' theorem — an angle inscribed in a semicircle \text{If } AB \text{ is a diameter and } C \text{ is any point on the circle, then } \angle ACB = 90°. \\ \text{This is why the unit circle definition of trig connects to right triangles.} Pythagorean triples — generating formula (m^2-n^2,\; 2mn,\; m^2+n^2) \text{ for integers } m > n > 0 \\ m=2,n=1 \to (3,4,5) \quad m=3,n=2 \to (5,12,13) \\ m=4,n=1 \to (15,8,17) \quad m=4,n=3 \to (7,24,25) Pythagorean theorem and its converse a^2 + b^2 = c^2 \iff \text{the triangle is right-angled at } C \\ \text{Converse: if } a^2+b^2=c^2 \text{, the angle opposite } c \text{ is exactly } 90° 2D and 3D distance formulas d_{2D} = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2} \\ d_{3D} = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2} Common Pythagorean triples — memorise these 3{-}4{-}5 \quad 5{-}12{-}13 \quad 8{-}15{-}17 \quad 7{-}24{-}25 \\ \text{Multiples also work: } 6{-}8{-}10,\; 9{-}12{-}15,\; 10{-}24{-}26 \ldots Similar Triangles, Thales, and the Pythagorean Proof Every trig ratio is secretly a similar-triangle relationship. Triangle Geometry & Thales' Theorem The Pythagorean Theorem Proof: x² + y² = r²  →  dy/dx = −x/y A calculus preview: see how the Pythagorean circle equation yields the slope formula dy/dx = −x/y. Distance Between Points Using similar triangles to find an unknown side \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF. \text{ If } AB=6, BC=9, AC=12, \text{ and } DE=4, \text{ find } EF \text{ and } DF. Similar triangles: find the scale factor from any pair of corresponding sides, then multiply all others by it. \text{Scale factor: } k = \frac{DE}{AB} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} Find the ratio of corresponding sides. DE corresponds to AB. EF = k \cdot BC = \frac{2}{3} \times 9 = 6 \qquad DF = k \cdot AC = \frac{2}{3} \times 12 = 8 Multiply all sides of the original triangle by the scale factor. Verifying and generating Pythagorean triples \text{(a) Verify 8-15-17 is a Pythagorean triple. (b) Generate a triple using } m=3, n=1. The generating formula produces all primitive triples when $\gcd(m,n)=1$ and $m-n$ is odd. Multiples of primitives also work. 8^2 + 15^2 = 64 + 225 = 289 = 17^2 \checkmark Check: does $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$? m=3, n=1: \quad a = m^2-n^2 = 8, \quad b = 2mn = 6, \quad c = m^2+n^2 = 10 Apply the generating formula. This gives the triple (6, 8, 10) — a multiple of (3, 4, 5). 3D distance between two points \text{Find the distance between } A(1, -2, 4) \text{ and } B(3, 1, -1). The 3D distance formula is exactly the Pythagorean theorem applied in three dimensions. There is nothing new to memorise — just one more square under the root. d = \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (1-(-2))^2 + (-1-4)^2} Apply the 3D distance formula. = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 25} = \sqrt{38} Two applications of the Pythagorean theorem: first in the $xy$-plane, then adding the $z$ component. \text{A 10-foot ladder leans against a wall. Its base is 6 feet from the wall. How high up the wall does it reach? Is the foot-to-top distance a Pythagorean triple?} This is a right triangle with hypotenuse 10 and one leg 6. h = 8 \text{ ft; yes, } (6,8,10) = 2\times(3,4,5) h^2 + 6^2 = 10^2 \Rightarrow h^2 = 100-36 = 64 \Rightarrow h = 8 Pythagorean theorem. The height is 8 feet. (6, 8, 10) = 2 \times (3, 4, 5) \checkmark Yes — a multiple of the 3-4-5 triple. Recognising triples saves time on exams. \text{Prove: the midpoint of the hypotenuse of a right triangle is equidistant from all three vertices.} Place the right angle at the origin. The midpoint of the hypotenuse is the average of the two other vertices. |OM| = |AM| = |BM| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} = \tfrac{1}{2}|AB| \text{Let the right angle be at } O=(0,0), A=(2a,0), B=(0,2b). \text{ Midpoint } M = (a,b). Convenient coordinates. Using $2a$ and $2b$ makes the midpoint calculation clean. |OM| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad |AM| = \sqrt{(2a-a)^2+b^2} = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad |BM| = \sqrt{a^2+(2b-b)^2} = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} All three distances equal $\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ — the midpoint is equidistant from all three vertices. \therefore M \text{ is the circumcentre of the right triangle, and the circumradius} = \tfrac{1}{2}|AB|. \qquad \blacksquare This is why Thales' theorem works: the circumcentre of a right triangle lies exactly at the midpoint of the hypotenuse. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Trig Ratios: Six Functions From One Triangle
documents.subtitle: SOH-CAH-TOA is a memory aid, not a definition — here is the real story behind all six trig functions
documents.tags: trig ratios SOH-CAH-TOA sine cosine tangent cosecant secant cotangent unit circle exact values 30-45-60
documents.assignment: Finding all six trig ratios from one side ratio \text{If } \sin\theta = \frac{3}{5} \text{ and } \theta \text{ is acute, find all six trig ratios.}   One trig ratio in a right triangle determines all six. Draw the triangle, find the missing side, read off the ratios. \text{opp} = 3, \text{ hyp} = 5 \Rightarrow \text{adj} = \sqrt{25-9} = 4 Draw the triangle: opposite = 3, hypotenuse = 5. Pythagorean theorem gives the adjacent side. (Recognise 3-4-5!) \sin = \frac{3}{5}, \; \cos = \frac{4}{5}, \; \tan = \frac{3}{4} The three primary ratios. \csc = \frac{5}{3}, \; \sec = \frac{5}{4}, \; \cot = \frac{4}{3} The three reciprocals — flip each primary ratio. Exact values from the unit circle for angles beyond 90° \text{Find the exact value of } \sin(150°), \cos(225°), \tan(300°).   Reference angle method: (1) find which quadrant, (2) find the reference angle (acute angle to the x-axis), (3) apply the ASTC sign rule. \sin(150°) = \sin(180°-30°) = \sin(30°) = \frac{1}{2} QII: sin is positive. Reference angle is $180°-150°=30°$. Use the known value. \cos(225°) = -\cos(45°) = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} QIII: cos is negative. Reference angle is $225°-180°=45°$. \tan(300°) = -\tan(60°) = -\sqrt{3} QIV: tan is negative. Reference angle is $360°-300°=60°$.  \text{If } \tan\theta = -\frac{5}{12} \text{ and } \theta \text{ is in QIV, find } \sin\theta \text{ and } \cos\theta. $\tan = $ opp/adj. In QIV, sin is negative and cos is positive. \sin\theta = -5/13, \; \cos\theta = 12/13  \text{opp} = 5, \text{ adj} = 12 \Rightarrow \text{hyp} = \sqrt{25+144} = 13 In QIV: opposite is $-5$ (below x-axis), adjacent is $+12$. Hypotenuse is always positive. \sin\theta = -\frac{5}{13}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{12}{13} QIV: sin negative, cos positive. Verify: $(-5/13)/(12/13) = -5/12 = \tan\theta$ ✓  \text{Prove that } \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \sec\theta \csc\theta. Write everything in terms of sin and cos, then combine fractions. \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \sec\theta\csc\theta  \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} + \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} Convert to sin and cos. = \frac{\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta\cos\theta} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta} Common denominator, then Pythagorean identity. = \frac{1}{\sin\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\theta} = \csc\theta \cdot \sec\theta \qquad \blacksquare Split and recognise the reciprocal definitions.
documents.applications: Every GPS location, every 3D rendering, every robot arm position uses trig ratios. In manufacturing, the angle of a machined surface is specified as a trig ratio — not as an angle — because ratios are dimensionless and therefore independent of units. Structural engineers compute forces along and perpendicular to members using sine and cosine decomposition. The six trig ratios \sin\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} \qquad \cos\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} \qquad \tan\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} \\ \csc\theta = \frac{\text{hyp}}{\text{opp}} \qquad \sec\theta = \frac{\text{hyp}}{\text{adj}} \qquad \cot\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{opp}} Reciprocal pairs — one memory, two facts \csc = \frac{1}{\sin} \qquad \sec = \frac{1}{\cos} \qquad \cot = \frac{1}{\tan} \\ \text{Memory: } \csc/\sin,\; \sec/\cos \text{ — the "co" pairs with "co".} \\ \tan = \frac{\sin}{\cos} \qquad \cot = \frac{\cos}{\sin} The co-function symmetry \sin\theta = \cos(90°-\theta) \qquad \cos\theta = \sin(90°-\theta) \\ \tan\theta = \cot(90°-\theta) \qquad \sec\theta = \csc(90°-\theta) \\ \text{"co" means complement: the co-function of } \theta \text{ is the function of } (90°-\theta)
documents.content: Why is it called the co-sine? And what does the "co" mean in co-secant and co-tangent? The naming hides a beautiful symmetry that makes half the unit circle values free once you know the other half. Every GPS location, every 3D rendering, every robot arm position uses trig ratios. In manufacturing, the angle of a machined surface is specified as a trig ratio — not as an angle — because ratios are dimensionless and therefore independent of units. Structural engineers compute forces along and perpendicular to members using sine and cosine decomposition. In a right triangle, the six trig ratios are just the six ways to form a fraction from the three side lengths. With sides labelled relative to an acute angle $\theta$: opposite (opp), adjacent (adj), hypotenuse (hyp), the six ratios are $\sin = \text{opp/hyp}$, $\cos = \text{adj/hyp}$, $\tan = \text{opp/adj}$, and their reciprocals $\csc = \text{hyp/opp}$, $\sec = \text{hyp/adj}$, $\cot = \text{adj/opp}$. The "co" prefix means complementary. The co-sine of an angle is the sine of its complement: $\cos\theta = \sin(90°-\theta)$. Similarly $\cot\theta = \tan(90°-\theta)$ and $\csc\theta = \sec(90°-\theta)$.
