This is why co-functions appear in pairs — they share the same values, just on opposite angles of the same right triangle. These ratios are well-defined because of similar triangles. Every right triangle with a given acute angle $\theta$ is similar to every other — same angles, just different scale. The ratio opposite/hypotenuse is the same for all of them. That common ratio is exactly what we call $\sin\theta$. The ratio does not depend on the size of the triangle. The exact values for 30°, 45°, and 60° come from two special triangles. The 45-45-90 triangle has legs equal; if each leg is 1, the hypotenuse is $\sqrt{2}$. The 30-60-90 triangle comes from cutting an equilateral triangle in half; if the hypotenuse is 2, the short leg is 1 and the long leg is $\sqrt{3}$. The unit circle ($r=1$) unifies all of this. At angle $\theta$ measured from the positive $x$-axis, the terminal point is $P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. This is because in a unit-radius right triangle, the adjacent side has length $\cos\theta$ (by definition of cosine) and the opposite side has length $\sin\theta$. The unit circle extends trig to all angles — not just acute ones. For angles beyond 90°, the right-triangle definition breaks down (you cannot have an obtuse angle in a right triangle). The unit circle definition takes over: $\sin\theta$ is always the $y$-coordinate of the terminal point, $\cos\theta$ is always the $x$-coordinate, for any angle. The six trig ratios \sin\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}} \qquad \cos\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}} \qquad \tan\theta = \frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} \\ \csc\theta = \frac{\text{hyp}}{\text{opp}} \qquad \sec\theta = \frac{\text{hyp}}{\text{adj}} \qquad \cot\theta = \frac{\text{adj}}{\text{opp}} Reciprocal pairs — one memory, two facts \csc = \frac{1}{\sin} \qquad \sec = \frac{1}{\cos} \qquad \cot = \frac{1}{\tan} \\ \text{Memory: } \csc/\sin,\; \sec/\cos \text{ — the "co" pairs with "co".} \\ \tan = \frac{\sin}{\cos} \qquad \cot = \frac{\cos}{\sin} The co-function symmetry \sin\theta = \cos(90°-\theta) \qquad \cos\theta = \sin(90°-\theta) \\ \tan\theta = \cot(90°-\theta) \qquad \sec\theta = \csc(90°-\theta) \\ \text{"co" means complement: the co-function of } \theta \text{ is the function of } (90°-\theta) Exact values — the two special triangles \begin{array}{c|ccc} \theta & 30° & 45° & 60° \\ \hline \sin & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ \cos & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & \frac{1}{2} \\ \tan & \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} & 1 & \sqrt{3} \end{array} Memory pattern for sin/cos exact values \sin 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° = \frac{\sqrt{0}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{1}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{4}}{2} \\ = 0, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 1 \\ \text{Cosine is the same sequence reversed.} Unit circle definition — extends trig to all angles \text{Terminal point of angle } \theta: P = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) \\ \tan\theta = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} \quad (x \neq 0) \\ \text{Signs by quadrant: ASTC — All, Sin, Tan, Cos (positive)} ASTC — which functions are positive in each quadrant \text{QI: All positive} \quad \text{QII: Sin (and csc) positive} \\ \text{QIII: Tan (and cot) positive} \quad \text{QIV: Cos (and sec) positive} \\ \text{"All Students Take Calculus" or "Add Sugar To Coffee"} All Six Ratios — One Draggable Triangle The ratio is the number. The triangle size is irrelevant. Trigonometric Ratios Sine & Cosine of Common Angles All Six Trig Functions on the Unit Circle Sin and cos are coordinates. Tan is a segment on the tangent line. Sec is the distance to the tangent point. Finding all six trig ratios from one side ratio \text{If } \sin\theta = \frac{3}{5} \text{ and } \theta \text{ is acute, find all six trig ratios.} One trig ratio in a right triangle determines all six. Draw the triangle, find the missing side, read off the ratios. \text{opp} = 3, \text{ hyp} = 5 \Rightarrow \text{adj} = \sqrt{25-9} = 4 Draw the triangle: opposite = 3, hypotenuse = 5. Pythagorean theorem gives the adjacent side. (Recognise 3-4-5!) \sin = \frac{3}{5}, \; \cos = \frac{4}{5}, \; \tan = \frac{3}{4} The three primary ratios. \csc = \frac{5}{3}, \; \sec = \frac{5}{4}, \; \cot = \frac{4}{3} The three reciprocals — flip each primary ratio. Exact values from the unit circle for angles beyond 90° \text{Find the exact value of } \sin(150°), \cos(225°), \tan(300°). Reference angle method: (1) find which quadrant, (2) find the reference angle (acute angle to the x-axis), (3) apply the ASTC sign rule. \sin(150°) = \sin(180°-30°) = \sin(30°) = \frac{1}{2} QII: sin is positive. Reference angle is $180°-150°=30°$. Use the known value. \cos(225°) = -\cos(45°) = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} QIII: cos is negative. Reference angle is $225°-180°=45°$. \tan(300°) = -\tan(60°) = -\sqrt{3} QIV: tan is negative. Reference angle is $360°-300°=60°$. \text{If } \tan\theta = -\frac{5}{12} \text{ and } \theta \text{ is in QIV, find } \sin\theta \text{ and } \cos\theta. $\tan = $ opp/adj. In QIV, sin is negative and cos is positive. \sin\theta = -5/13, \; \cos\theta = 12/13 \text{opp} = 5, \text{ adj} = 12 \Rightarrow \text{hyp} = \sqrt{25+144} = 13 In QIV: opposite is $-5$ (below x-axis), adjacent is $+12$. Hypotenuse is always positive. \sin\theta = -\frac{5}{13}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{12}{13} QIV: sin negative, cos positive. Verify: $(-5/13)/(12/13) = -5/12 = \tan\theta$ ✓ \text{Prove that } \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \sec\theta \csc\theta. Write everything in terms of sin and cos, then combine fractions. \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \sec\theta\csc\theta \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} + \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} Convert to sin and cos. = \frac{\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta\cos\theta} = \frac{1}{\sin\theta\cos\theta} Common denominator, then Pythagorean identity. = \frac{1}{\sin\theta} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\theta} = \csc\theta \cdot \sec\theta \qquad \blacksquare Split and recognise the reciprocal definitions. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Graphing All Six Trig Functions
documents.subtitle: Period, amplitude, asymptotes, domain and range — and why the graphs look exactly the way they do
documents.tags: graphing sine cosine tangent cotangent secant cosecant period asymptotes domain range
documents.assignment: Identifying features of a trig graph from its equation \text{For } y = 3\sec(2x): \text{ find the period, amplitude (if any), asymptotes, and range.}   For reciprocal trig functions: period from the inner function, asymptotes from denominator zeros, range stretched by the amplitude factor. \sec(2x) = 1/\cos(2x) \Rightarrow \text{period of } \cos(2x) = \frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi \Rightarrow \text{period of } \sec(2x) = \pi Secant inherits the period of cosine. The coefficient 2 halves the period. \text{Amplitude: undefined (sec is unbounded). Vertical stretch: 3.} Amplitude is only defined for bounded functions like sin and cos. For sec, the 3 is a vertical stretch factor. \text{VAs: } \cos(2x) = 0 \Rightarrow 2x = \frac{\pi}{2}+k\pi \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{k\pi}{2} Find where the denominator is zero. \text{Range: } (-\infty, -3] \cup [3, \infty) The stretch factor 3 scales the range: $|\sec| \geq 1$ becomes $|3\sec| \geq 3$. Matching equations to graphs \text{A trig graph has: period } \pi/2, \text{ asymptotes at } x = \pi/4 + k\pi/2, \text{ passes through origin. Which function?}   Work backwards from features: period determines $B$, asymptote positions identify the function family, key points distinguish between candidates. \text{Asymptotes at } x = \pi/4 + k\pi/2 \Rightarrow \cos(Bx) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{tangent or secant} Asymptotes with this structure come from where cosine is zero. \text{Passes through origin} \Rightarrow \text{tangent (not secant, which never passes through origin)} Secant has range $\leq -1$ or $\geq 1$, so it cannot pass through $(0,0)$. \text{Period } \pi/2 = \pi/B \Rightarrow B = 2 \Rightarrow y = \tan(2x) The period of tangent is $\pi/B$. Solve for $B$.  \text{Sketch one full period of } y = -2\csc(x/2) \text{ and state its domain, range, and period.} Find where $\sin(x/2) = 0$ for the asymptotes, then sketch $y = -2\sin(x/2)$ as a guide curve. \text{Period } 4\pi. \text{ Domain: } x \neq 2k\pi. \text{ Range: } (-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty).  \text{Period of } \sin(x/2) = \frac{2\pi}{1/2} = 4\pi \Rightarrow \text{period of csc} = 4\pi The $1/2$ stretches the period to $4\pi$. \text{Asymptotes: } \sin(x/2) = 0 \Rightarrow x/2 = k\pi \Rightarrow x = 2k\pi At $x = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \ldots$ \text{Range: } (-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty) $-2\csc$ means we multiply by $-2$: the graph flips AND stretches. Arcs that normally go up now go down and vice versa.
documents.applications: Sine and cosine graphs model every oscillation in physics: sound waves, light waves, AC current, spring motion. Tangent appears in slope calculations and optics (angle of refraction). Secant appears in navigation and in calculus integrals. Knowing these graphs deeply — not just their shapes but exactly why each feature is where it is — makes reading physical phenomena from equations automatic. Period and range of all six trig functions \sin x: \text{ period } 2\pi, \text{ range } [-1,1] \\ \cos x: \text{ period } 2\pi, \text{ range } [-1,1] \\ \tan x: \text{ period } \pi, \text{ range } (-\infty,\infty) \\ \cot x: \text{ period } \pi, \text{ range } (-\infty,\infty) \\ \sec x: \text{ period } 2\pi, \text{ range } (-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty) \\ \csc x: \text{ period } 2\pi, \text{ range } (-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty) Where the asymptotes come from — no memorisation needed \tan x = \sin x / \cos x \Rightarrow \text{VA where } \cos x = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{\pi}{2} + k\pi \\ \cot x = \cos x / \sin x \Rightarrow \text{VA where } \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow x = k\pi \\ \sec x = 1/\cos x \Rightarrow \text{same VAs as tangent} \\ \csc x = 1/\sin x \Rightarrow \text{same VAs as cotangent} Why $|\sec x| \geq 1$ and $|\csc x| \geq 1$ — always |\cos x| \leq 1 \Rightarrow |\sec x| = \frac{1}{|\cos x|} \geq 1 \\ \text{The secant and cosecant graphs never enter the band } (-1, 1).
documents.content:
Why does the tangent graph have vertical asymptotes at $\pi/2$, $3\pi/2$, $5\pi/2$... but the sine graph never has any? The answer is in the definition: $	an x = \sin x / \cos x$, and the denominator hits zero exactly at those points. Sine and cosine graphs model every oscillation in physics: sound waves, light waves, AC current, spring motion. Tangent appears in slope calculations and optics (angle of refraction). Secant appears in navigation and in calculus integrals. Knowing these graphs deeply — not just their shapes but exactly why each feature is where it is — makes reading physical phenomena from equations automatic. Sine and cosine are the foundation. Their graphs are identical in shape — cosine is just sine shifted left by $\pi/2$. Both oscillate between $-1$ and $+1$, completing one full cycle every $2\pi$. The sine graph starts at $(0,0)$; the cosine graph starts at $(0,1)$. Everything else about them follows directly from the unit circle. Tangent and cotangent have a completely different character. They are not bounded — they shoot to $\pm\infty$ at their asymptotes. Tangent has period $\pi$ (not $2\pi$), because $	an(x+\pi) = 	an x$ — the sine and cosine both flip sign, and the negatives cancel. Tangent is zero where sine is zero and undefined where cosine is zero. Secant and cosecant are the reciprocals of cosine and sine. Wherever cosine is 1, secant is 1; wherever cosine approaches 0, secant shoots to infinity. The secant graph looks like a series of upward and downward parabola-like arcs, never crossing the band $(-1, 1)$ — because $|\sec x| = 1/|\cos x| \geq 1$ always. The domain of each trig function excludes the points where it is undefined. $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ are defined everywhere. $	an x$ and $\sec x$ are undefined where $\cos x = 0$: $x 
eq \pi/2 + k\pi$. $\cot x$ and $\csc x$ are undefined where $\sin x = 0$: $x 
eq k\pi$. The five key points of one cycle of $y = \sin x$ on $[0, 2\pi]$ are: $(0,0)$, $(\pi/2, 1)$, $(\pi, 0)$, $(3\pi/2, -1)$, $(2\pi, 0)$. For $y = \cos x$: $(0,1)$, $(\pi/2, 0)$, $(\pi, -1)$, $(3\pi/2, 0)$, $(2\pi, 1)$. Memorise these — they anchor every graph. The tangent graph on $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ passes through $(-\pi/4, -1)$, $(0, 0)$, $(\pi/4, 1)$ and increases without bound at both ends. Cotangent is the reflection: it passes through $(\pi/4, 1)$, $(\pi/2, 0)$, $(3\pi/4, -1)$ and has asymptotes at $x = 0$ and $x = \pi$. Period and range of all six trig functions \sin x: 	ext{ period } 2\pi, 	ext{ range } [-1,1] \ \cos x: 	ext{ period } 2\pi, 	ext{ range } [-1,1] \ 	an x: 	ext{ period } \pi, 	ext{ range } (-\infty,\infty) \ \cot x: 	ext{ period } \pi, 	ext{ range } (-\infty,\infty) \ \sec x: 	ext{ period } 2\pi, 	ext{ range } (-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty) \ \csc x: 	ext{ period } 2\pi, 	ext{ range } (-\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty) Where the asymptotes come from — no memorisation needed 	an x = \sin x / \cos x \Rightarrow 	ext{VA where } \cos x = 0 \Rightarrow x =  rac{\pi}{2} + k\pi \ \cot x = \cos x / \sin x \Rightarrow 	ext{VA where } \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow x = k\pi \ \sec x = 1/\cos x \Rightarrow 	ext{same VAs as tangent} \ \csc x = 1/\sin x \Rightarrow 	ext{same VAs as cotangent} Why $|\sec x| \geq 1$ and $|\csc x| \geq 1$ — always |\cos x| \leq 1 \Rightarrow |\sec x| =  rac{1}{|\cos x|} \geq 1 \ 	ext{The secant and cosecant graphs never enter the band } (-1, 1). Domain of all six trig functions \sin, \cos: 	ext{ domain } (-\infty, \infty) \ 	an, \sec: 	ext{ domain } \mathbb{R} \setminus \{	frac{\pi}{2}+k\pi\} \ \cot, \csc: 	ext{ domain } \mathbb{R} \setminus \{k\pi\} \ (k 	ext{ any integer}) Five key points for sine and cosine (one cycle) \sin x:\; (0,0),\, (	frac{\pi}{2},1),\, (\pi,0),\, (	frac{3\pi}{2},-1),\, (2\pi,0) \ \cos x:\; (0,1),\, (	frac{\pi}{2},0),\, (\pi,-1),\, (	frac{3\pi}{2},0),\, (2\pi,1) Secant and cosecant touch but never cross the range gap \sec x \geq 1 	ext{ or } \sec x \leq -1 	ext{ — never in } (-1,1) \ 	ext{The arcs of sec touch } y=1 	ext{ where } \cos x = 1, 	ext{ and touch } y=-1 	ext{ where } \cos x=-1. \ 	ext{They "hug" the sine and cosine curves from outside.} All Six Trig Functions — Toggle and Compare Sec and csc are defined exactly where cos and sin are nonzero — their asymptotes are each other's zeros. Using Trig Functions Practically Graphing Tan, Cot, Sec, Csc Trig Graph Variations Identifying features of a trig graph from its equation 	ext{For } y = 3\sec(2x): 	ext{ find the period, amplitude (if any), asymptotes, and range.} For reciprocal trig functions: period from the inner function, asymptotes from denominator zeros, range stretched by the amplitude factor. \sec(2x) = 1/\cos(2x) \Rightarrow 	ext{period of } \cos(2x) =  rac{2\pi}{2} = \pi \Rightarrow 	ext{period of } \sec(2x) = \pi Secant inherits the period of cosine. The coefficient 2 halves the period. 	ext{Amplitude: undefined (sec is unbounded). Vertical stretch: 3.} Amplitude is only defined for bounded functions like sin and cos. For sec, the 3 is a vertical stretch factor. 	ext{VAs: } \cos(2x) = 0 \Rightarrow 2x =  rac{\pi}{2}+k\pi \Rightarrow x =  rac{\pi}{4}+ rac{k\pi}{2} Find where the denominator is zero. 	ext{Range: } (-\infty, -3] \cup [3, \infty) The stretch factor 3 scales the range: $|\sec| \geq 1$ becomes $|3\sec| \geq 3$. Matching equations to graphs 	ext{A trig graph has: period } \pi/2, 	ext{ asymptotes at } x = \pi/4 + k\pi/2, 	ext{ passes through origin. Which function?} Work backwards from features: period determines $B$, asymptote positions identify the function family, key points distinguish between candidates. 	ext{Asymptotes at } x = \pi/4 + k\pi/2 \Rightarrow \cos(Bx) = 0 \Rightarrow 	ext{tangent or secant} Asymptotes with this structure come from where cosine is zero. 	ext{Passes through origin} \Rightarrow 	ext{tangent (not secant, which never passes through origin)} Secant has range $\leq -1$ or $\geq 1$, so it cannot pass through $(0,0)$. 	ext{Period } \pi/2 = \pi/B \Rightarrow B = 2 \Rightarrow y = 	an(2x) The period of tangent is $\pi/B$. Solve for $B$. 	ext{Sketch one full period of } y = -2\csc(x/2) 	ext{ and state its domain, range, and period.} Find where $\sin(x/2) = 0$ for the asymptotes, then sketch $y = -2\sin(x/2)$ as a guide curve. 	ext{Period } 4\pi. 	ext{ Domain: } x 
eq 2k\pi. 	ext{ Range: } (-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty). 	ext{Period of } \sin(x/2) =  rac{2\pi}{1/2} = 4\pi \Rightarrow 	ext{period of csc} = 4\pi The $1/2$ stretches the period to $4\pi$. 	ext{Asymptotes: } \sin(x/2) = 0 \Rightarrow x/2 = k\pi \Rightarrow x = 2k\pi At $x = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \ldots$ 	ext{Range: } (-\infty,-2]\cup[2,\infty) $-2\csc$ means we multiply by $-2$: the graph flips AND stretches. Arcs that normally go up now go down and vice versa. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Inverse Trig Functions: Undoing the Circle
documents.subtitle: Why domain restriction is not a technicality — it is the only way inverse trig can exist
documents.tags: inverse trig arcsin arccos arctan domain restriction principal value inverse functions
documents.assignment: Evaluating inverse trig exactly \text{Find: } (a)\; \arcsin\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad (b)\; \arctan(-1), \quad (c)\; \arccos 0.   Always check the principal range. The answer must lie in the correct interval for each inverse function. \arcsin\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \tfrac{\pi}{3} $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$ and $\pi/3 \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ — it is in the principal range. \arctan(-1) = -\tfrac{\pi}{4} $\tan(-\pi/4) = -1$ and $-\pi/4 \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. The negative angle is correct — arctan output is negative for negative inputs. \arccos 0 = \tfrac{\pi}{2} $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$ and $\pi/2 \in [0, \pi]$. Note: $\arcsin 0 = 0$ but $\arccos 0 = \pi/2$ — different principal ranges give different answers. Chain rule with inverse trig \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \arctan(x^2).   Chain rule with inverse trig: apply $1/(1+u^2)$ or $1/\sqrt{1-u^2}$ at the inner function, then multiply by the inner derivative. f'(x) = \frac{1}{1+(x^2)^2} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(x^2) Chain rule: derivative of arctan is $1/(1+u^2)$ evaluated at the inner function, times the inner derivative. = \frac{1}{1+x^4} \cdot 2x = \frac{2x}{1+x^4} Simplify. Note the denominator is $1+x^4$ not $1+x^2$ — the inner function was $x^2$, so it gets squared again. Inverse trig antiderivative — completing the square first \text{Evaluate } \displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{x^2 - 4x + 8}.   Completing the square converts a general quadratic denominator into the arctan standard form. This is the reverse path from the calculus bridge in the quadratics lesson. x^2 - 4x + 8 = (x-2)^2 + 4 Complete the square on the denominator. This converts it to the arctan standard form $u^2 + a^2$. \int \frac{dx}{(x-2)^2 + 4} Let $u = x-2$, $du = dx$. Then $a^2 = 4$, $a = 2$. = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\frac{x-2}{2} + C Apply $\int \frac{du}{u^2+a^2} = \frac{1}{a}\arctan\frac{u}{a} + C$ with $a=2$.  \text{Differentiate } g(x) = x\arcsin x + \sqrt{1-x^2}. Product rule on the first term, chain rule on the second. g'(x) = \arcsin x  \frac{d}{dx}[x\arcsin x] = \arcsin x + x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} Product rule: $u'v + uv'$ where $u=x$, $v=\arcsin x$. \frac{d}{dx}[\sqrt{1-x^2}] = \frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} Chain rule on the square root. g'(x) = \arcsin x + \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} - \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} = \arcsin x The two fractional terms cancel exactly. The derivative is simply $\arcsin x$ — a hint that $x\arcsin x + \sqrt{1-x^2}$ is an antiderivative of $\arcsin x$.  \text{Prove: } \arctan x + \arctan\frac{1}{x} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ for } x > 0. Differentiate the left side — if the derivative is zero, the expression is constant. Then find the constant by evaluating at a specific $x$. \arctan x + \arctan(1/x) = \pi/2 \text{ for } x > 0.  \frac{d}{dx}\left[\arctan x + \arctan\frac{1}{x}\right] = \frac{1}{1+x^2} + \frac{1}{1+(1/x)^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) Differentiate both terms. Chain rule on the second: derivative of $1/x$ is $-1/x^2$. = \frac{1}{1+x^2} + \frac{x^2}{x^2+1} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) = \frac{1}{1+x^2} - \frac{1}{1+x^2} = 0 Simplify: $1/(1+(1/x)^2) = x^2/(x^2+1)$. The terms cancel exactly. \text{At } x=1: \arctan 1 + \arctan 1 = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2} \qquad \blacksquare Zero derivative means constant. Evaluate at $x=1$ to find the constant is $\pi/2$. The identity holds for all $x > 0$.
documents.applications: Inverse trig functions appear any time you need an angle from a ratio: surveying (finding an angle from measured distances), robotics (computing joint angles from end-effector position), physics (finding launch angles for projectile motion). In calculus, $\frac{d}{dx}\arctan x = \frac{1}{1+x^2}$ is one of the most important antiderivative formulas — it is how integrals like $\int \frac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx$ are solved. The three principal inverse trig functions \arcsin x:\; [-1,1] \to [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}] \quad \text{(sin restricted to } [-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}]\text{)} \\ \arccos x:\; [-1,1] \to [0, \pi] \quad \text{(cos restricted to } [0,\pi]\text{)} \\ \arctan x:\; \mathbb{R} \to (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}) \quad \text{(tan restricted to } (-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2})\text{)} The principal value trap \arcsin(\sin(5\pi/6)) \neq 5\pi/6 \\ \arcsin(\sin(5\pi/6)) = \arcsin(1/2) = \pi/6 \\ \text{Because } 5\pi/6 \notin [-\pi/2, \pi/2]\text{, arcsin gives the equivalent angle IN the principal range.} Why arctan has asymptotes but arcsin does not \arctan x \to \pm\tfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ as } x \to \pm\infty \quad \text{(tan can take any real input)} \\ \arcsin x \text{ is only defined for } x \in [-1, 1] \quad \text{(sin output is bounded)} \\ \arccos x \text{ is only defined for } x \in [-1, 1] \quad \text{(same reason)}
documents.content: If $\sin(\pi/6) = 1/2$, then $\sin^{-1}(1/2)$ should be $\pi/6$. But $\sin(5\pi/6) = 1/2$ too — and infinitely many other angles. How does $\sin^{-1}$ give a single answer? Inverse trig functions appear any time you need an angle from a ratio: surveying (finding an angle from measured distances), robotics (computing joint angles from end-effector position), physics (finding launch angles for projectile motion). In calculus, $\frac{d}{dx}\arctan x = \frac{1}{1+x^2}$ is one of the most important antiderivative formulas — it is how integrals like $\int \frac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx$ are solved. The sine function is not one-to-one — it takes the same value at infinitely many angles. $\sin(\pi/6) = \sin(5\pi/6) = \sin(13\pi/6) = \cdots = 1/2$. A function that is not one-to-one has no inverse — there is no way to say "the angle whose sine is $1/2$" because there are infinitely many. The solution is domain restriction: we agree to only use a specific portion of the sine curve — the piece from $-\pi/2$ to $\pi/2$. On that interval, sine is strictly increasing and therefore one-to-one. The inverse of this restricted function is $\arcsin$. The output is always in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ — the "principal value." No ambiguity. Each inverse trig function has a different restriction, chosen to cover the full output range while keeping the function one-to-one: $\arcsin$ uses $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, $\arccos$ uses $[0, \pi]$, $\arctan$ uses $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. These choices are conventions — but they are universal conventions, and every calculator uses them. The derivatives of the inverse trig functions are algebraic — no trig appears in them. This is surprising until you see how they are derived: using implicit differentiation on $y = \arcsin x$ means differentiating $\sin y = x$, giving $\cos y \cdot y' = 1$, so $y' = 1/\cos y$. Then using the Pythagorean identity $\cos y = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 y} = \sqrt{1 - x^2}$, the final result is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$ — no trig at all. The key derivatives to know: $\frac{d}{dx}\arcsin x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$, $\frac{d}{dx}\arctan x = \frac{1}{1+x^2}$. These also give the two most important inverse trig antiderivatives, which appear constantly in calculus. The identity $\arcsin x + \arccos x = \pi/2$ holds for all $x \in [-1,1]$. Geometrically: in a right triangle, the two acute angles sum to $\pi/2$, and if one is $\arcsin x$, the other is $\arccos x$. This means you only really need to remember $\arcsin$ and $\arctan$ — the others follow from complementary angle relationships. The three principal inverse trig functions \arcsin x:\; [-1,1] \to [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}] \quad \text{(sin restricted to } [-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}]\text{)} \\ \arccos x:\; [-1,1] \to [0, \pi] \quad \text{(cos restricted to } [0,\pi]\text{)} \\ \arctan x:\; \mathbb{R} \to (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}) \quad \text{(tan restricted to } (-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2})\text{)} The principal value trap \arcsin(\sin(5\pi/6)) \neq 5\pi/6 \\ \arcsin(\sin(5\pi/6)) = \arcsin(1/2) = \pi/6 \\ \text{Because } 5\pi/6 \notin [-\pi/2, \pi/2]\text{, arcsin gives the equivalent angle IN the principal range.} Why arctan has asymptotes but arcsin does not \arctan x \to \pm\tfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ as } x \to \pm\infty \quad \text{(tan can take any real input)} \\ \arcsin x \text{ is only defined for } x \in [-1, 1] \quad \text{(sin output is bounded)} \\ \arccos x \text{ is only defined for } x \in [-1, 1] \quad \text{(same reason)} Inverse trig derivatives — all derived via implicit differentiation \frac{d}{dx}\arcsin x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \qquad \frac{d}{dx}\arccos x = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \\ \frac{d}{dx}\arctan x = \frac{1}{1+x^2} \qquad \frac{d}{dx}\text{arcsec}\, x = \frac{1}{|x|\sqrt{x^2-1}} The two inverse trig antiderivatives you must know cold \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} = \arcsin x + C \\ \int \frac{dx}{1+x^2} = \arctan x + C Generalised forms (used in practice) \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}} = \arcsin\frac{x}{a} + C \\ \int \frac{dx}{a^2+x^2} = \frac{1}{a}\arctan\frac{x}{a} + C Complementary identity \arcsin x + \arccos x = \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \text{for all } x \in [-1,1] \\ \text{So: } \frac{d}{dx}\arccos x = -\frac{d}{dx}\arcsin x \text{ — the derivatives are negatives of each other.} Restricting the Domain to Create an Inverse The inverse function is the reflection of the restricted portion — not the whole periodic curve. Universal Mirror Sandbox — Any Function Rise becomes run. The slope of f^-1 is always the reciprocal of the slope of f. e^x and ln(x) — Slope Reciprocity The most famous inverse pair shows slope reciprocity most clearly. Inverse Sine & Cosine Functions Evaluating Inverse Trig Functions The Triangle Behind the Denominator The denominator comes from Pythagorean structure, not a memorized trick. Evaluating inverse trig exactly \text{Find: } (a)\; \arcsin\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \quad (b)\; \arctan(-1), \quad (c)\; \arccos 0. Always check the principal range. The answer must lie in the correct interval for each inverse function. \arcsin\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \tfrac{\pi}{3} $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$ and $\pi/3 \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ — it is in the principal range. \arctan(-1) = -\tfrac{\pi}{4} $\tan(-\pi/4) = -1$ and $-\pi/4 \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. The negative angle is correct — arctan output is negative for negative inputs. \arccos 0 = \tfrac{\pi}{2} $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$ and $\pi/2 \in [0, \pi]$. Note: $\arcsin 0 = 0$ but $\arccos 0 = \pi/2$ — different principal ranges give different answers. Chain rule with inverse trig \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \arctan(x^2). Chain rule with inverse trig: apply $1/(1+u^2)$ or $1/\sqrt{1-u^2}$ at the inner function, then multiply by the inner derivative. f'(x) = \frac{1}{1+(x^2)^2} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(x^2) Chain rule: derivative of arctan is $1/(1+u^2)$ evaluated at the inner function, times the inner derivative. = \frac{1}{1+x^4} \cdot 2x = \frac{2x}{1+x^4} Simplify. Note the denominator is $1+x^4$ not $1+x^2$ — the inner function was $x^2$, so it gets squared again. Inverse trig antiderivative — completing the square first \text{Evaluate } \displaystyle\int \frac{dx}{x^2 - 4x + 8}. Completing the square converts a general quadratic denominator into the arctan standard form. This is the reverse path from the calculus bridge in the quadratics lesson. x^2 - 4x + 8 = (x-2)^2 + 4 Complete the square on the denominator. This converts it to the arctan standard form $u^2 + a^2$. \int \frac{dx}{(x-2)^2 + 4} Let $u = x-2$, $du = dx$. Then $a^2 = 4$, $a = 2$. = \frac{1}{2}\arctan\frac{x-2}{2} + C Apply $\int \frac{du}{u^2+a^2} = \frac{1}{a}\arctan\frac{u}{a} + C$ with $a=2$. \text{Differentiate } g(x) = x\arcsin x + \sqrt{1-x^2}. Product rule on the first term, chain rule on the second. g'(x) = \arcsin x \frac{d}{dx}[x\arcsin x] = \arcsin x + x \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} Product rule: $u'v + uv'$ where $u=x$, $v=\arcsin x$. \frac{d}{dx}[\sqrt{1-x^2}] = \frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} Chain rule on the square root. g'(x) = \arcsin x + \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} - \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} = \arcsin x The two fractional terms cancel exactly. The derivative is simply $\arcsin x$ — a hint that $x\arcsin x + \sqrt{1-x^2}$ is an antiderivative of $\arcsin x$. \text{Prove: } \arctan x + \arctan\frac{1}{x} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ for } x > 0. Differentiate the left side — if the derivative is zero, the expression is constant. Then find the constant by evaluating at a specific $x$. \arctan x + \arctan(1/x) = \pi/2 \text{ for } x > 0. \frac{d}{dx}\left[\arctan x + \arctan\frac{1}{x}\right] = \frac{1}{1+x^2} + \frac{1}{1+(1/x)^2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) Differentiate both terms. Chain rule on the second: derivative of $1/x$ is $-1/x^2$. = \frac{1}{1+x^2} + \frac{x^2}{x^2+1} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{x^2}\right) = \frac{1}{1+x^2} - \frac{1}{1+x^2} = 0 Simplify: $1/(1+(1/x)^2) = x^2/(x^2+1)$. The terms cancel exactly. \text{At } x=1: \arctan 1 + \arctan 1 = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2} \qquad \blacksquare Zero derivative means constant. Evaluate at $x=1$ to find the constant is $\pi/2$. The identity holds for all $x > 0$. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Solving Triangles: Law of Sines, Law of Cosines, and the Ambiguous Case
documents.subtitle: Every triangle can be solved — if you know which law to use and when
documents.tags: law of sines law of cosines solving triangles SSA ambiguous case AAS ASA SAS SSS oblique triangles
documents.assignment: AAS triangle — Law of Sines \text{In } \triangle ABC: A=35°, B=82°, a=9. \text{ Find } b \text{ and } c.   AAS: find the third angle first, then use Law of Sines twice. Always use the most accurate known pair $(a, A)$ as your reference ratio. C = 180° - 35° - 82° = 63° Find the third angle using the triangle angle sum. \frac{b}{\sin 82°} = \frac{9}{\sin 35°} \Rightarrow b = \frac{9\sin 82°}{\sin 35°} \approx \frac{9(0.9903)}{0.5736} \approx 15.53 Law of Sines: set up the ratio using the known side-angle pair $(a, A)$. c = \frac{9\sin 63°}{\sin 35°} \approx \frac{9(0.8910)}{0.5736} \approx 13.98 Same ratio, solve for $c$. SAS triangle — Law of Cosines \text{In } \triangle ABC: a=7, b=10, C=52°. \text{ Find } c, \text{ then complete the triangle.}   SAS: Law of Cosines gives the unknown side, then switch to Law of Sines for angles. Find the smaller angle first to avoid the ambiguous case. c^2 = 7^2 + 10^2 - 2(7)(10)\cos 52° = 49 + 100 - 140(0.6157) \approx 149 - 86.2 = 62.8 Law of Cosines: the included angle C is opposite the unknown side c. c \approx \sqrt{62.8} \approx 7.92 Take the square root. \sin A = \frac{a\sin C}{c} = \frac{7\sin 52°}{7.92} \approx \frac{7(0.7880)}{7.92} \approx 0.696 \Rightarrow A \approx 44.0° Now use Law of Sines for angle A. Since $a < c$, angle $A < C$ — no ambiguous case here. B = 180° - 52° - 44° = 84° Third angle by subtraction. SSA — the ambiguous case \text{In } \triangle ABC: a=8, b=11, A=35°. \text{ How many triangles exist? Solve all cases.}   SSA with two triangles: solve for both possible angles B, check that each gives a positive C, then complete each triangle separately. Both answers are valid. h = b\sin A = 11\sin 35° \approx 11(0.5736) \approx 6.31 Compute the critical height. h \approx 6.31 < a = 8 < b = 11 \Rightarrow \text{TWO triangles} Check: $h < a < b$ — the side $a$ is long enough to reach but short enough that it swings to two positions. \sin B = \frac{b\sin A}{a} = \frac{11\sin 35°}{8} \approx 0.7888 \Rightarrow B_1 \approx 52.0° \text{ or } B_2 = 180°-52° = 128.0° Two possible angles for $B$. Both are valid since both give a positive angle sum with $A=35°$. \text{Case 1: } B_1=52°, C_1=93°, c_1 = \frac{8\sin 93°}{\sin 35°} \approx 13.9 First triangle: $A+B_1+C_1 = 35+52+93 = 180°$ ✓ \text{Case 2: } B_2=128°, C_2=17°, c_2 = \frac{8\sin 17°}{\sin 35°} \approx 4.1 Second triangle: $A+B_2+C_2 = 35+128+17 = 180°$ ✓  \text{From a point } P \text{, the angles of elevation to the top of two towers are } 38° \text{ and } 54°. \text{ The towers are 80 m apart and } P \text{ is between them. Find the tower heights.} Each tower with point P forms a right triangle. You have one side (distance from P to each base) and the elevation angle. \text{Problem requires additional constraint. With } d_1+d_2=80: \; h_1 = d_1\tan 38°, \; h_2=(80-d_1)\tan 54°.  \text{Let } d_1 = \text{distance to tower 1}, d_2 = \text{distance to tower 2. Then } d_1 + d_2 = 80. Set up: P is between the towers. h_1 = d_1\tan 38°, \quad h_2 = d_2\tan 54° Right triangle trig for each tower. \text{Need more info (or this is a two-equation system if the towers are equal height). As stated, we get a family of solutions.} Without knowing one distance, the problem is underdetermined. In practice, a third measurement or equal tower assumption is needed — a good lesson in real-world problem setup.  \text{Prove the Law of Sines using the formula Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C. Express the area three different ways using three different angle-side pairs. Set them equal. \dfrac{\sin A}{a} = \dfrac{\sin B}{b} = \dfrac{\sin C}{c}  \text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2}bc\sin A = \tfrac{1}{2}ac\sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}ab\sin C The area of a triangle equals $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{two sides} \times \sin(\text{included angle})$ — three equivalent expressions. \frac{1}{2}bc\sin A = \frac{1}{2}ac\sin B \Rightarrow \frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b} Set first two equal, divide both sides by $\frac{1}{2}abc$. \frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b} = \frac{\sin C}{c} \qquad \blacksquare Same argument with the second and third. The Law of Sines follows from the area formula — a clean, elegant proof.
documents.applications: Surveyors use the Law of Sines to compute distances they cannot measure directly. GPS systems use the Law of Cosines to compute positions from three known signal distances (trilateration). Ship navigation uses triangle solving to determine position from bearings. The ambiguous SSA case is not a mathematical curiosity — it appears whenever you're given a bearing and two distances but don't know which side the target is on. Which law to use — decision tree \text{Know two angles + any side (AAS/ASA)} \to \text{Law of Sines} \\ \text{Know two sides + included angle (SAS)} \to \text{Law of Cosines} \\ \text{Know all three sides (SSS)} \to \text{Law of Cosines} \\ \text{Know two sides + non-included angle (SSA)} \to \text{Law of Sines + check ambiguous case} Law of Sines \frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C} = 2R \\ \text{where } R \text{ is the circumradius (radius of circumscribed circle)} Law of Cosines — three equivalent forms a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc\cos A \\ b^2 = a^2 + c^2 - 2ac\cos B \\ c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C
documents.content: Given two sides of a triangle and an angle not between them (SSA), you might expect one answer. But you can get zero, one, or two valid triangles from the same information. Why — and how do you tell which case you're in? Surveyors use the Law of Sines to compute distances they cannot measure directly. GPS systems use the Law of Cosines to compute positions from three known signal distances (trilateration). Ship navigation uses triangle solving to determine position from bearings. The ambiguous SSA case is not a mathematical curiosity — it appears whenever you're given a bearing and two distances but don't know which side the target is on. Right triangle trig (SOH-CAH-TOA) only works when there is a right angle. For oblique triangles (no right angle), two laws extend the power of trig: the Law of Sines and the Law of Cosines. The Law of Sines says: in any triangle, the ratio of a side to the sine of its opposite angle is constant. $a/\sin A = b/\sin B = c/\sin C$. This ratio is actually the diameter of the circumscribed circle. Use it when you know two angles and one side (AAS or ASA), or two sides and a non-included angle (SSA — but beware the ambiguous case). The Law of Cosines generalises the Pythagorean theorem to non-right triangles. $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$. When $C = 90°$, $\cos C = 0$ and it reduces to $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ exactly. Use it when you know two sides and the included angle (SAS), or all three sides (SSS). The ambiguous case (SSA) arises because the sine function is not one-to-one: $\sin\theta = \sin(180°-\theta)$. If you compute $\sin B$ from the Law of Sines and get a value in $(0,1)$, there are two angles in $[0°, 180°]$ with that sine value — $B$ and $180°-B$. Both might produce a valid triangle, only one might, or neither might (if the computed sine exceeds 1). The ambiguous case conditions: given sides $a$, $b$ and angle $A$ (opposite to $a$): if $A$ is obtuse, there is either 0 or 1 triangle. If $A$ is acute, compare $a$ to the height $h = b\sin A$: if $a < h$ there are 0 triangles, if $a = h$ there is exactly 1 (right triangle), if $h < a < b$ there are 2 triangles, if $a \geq b$ there is 1 triangle. The area of any triangle can be computed from two sides and the included angle: $\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$. This is because the height of the triangle with base $a$ is $b\sin C$. Combined with Heron's formula for SSS triangles, you can always find the area once the triangle is solved. Which law to use — decision tree \text{Know two angles + any side (AAS/ASA)} \to \text{Law of Sines} \\ \text{Know two sides + included angle (SAS)} \to \text{Law of Cosines} \\ \text{Know all three sides (SSS)} \to \text{Law of Cosines} \\ \text{Know two sides + non-included angle (SSA)} \to \text{Law of Sines + check ambiguous case} Law of Sines \frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C} = 2R \\ \text{where } R \text{ is the circumradius (radius of circumscribed circle)} Law of Cosines — three equivalent forms a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc\cos A \\ b^2 = a^2 + c^2 - 2ac\cos B \\ c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C SSA ambiguous case — four outcomes \text{Given } a, b, A \text{ with } A \text{ acute, } h = b\sin A: \\ a < h: \text{ NO triangle (side too short to reach)} \\ a = h: \text{ ONE right triangle} \\ h < a < b: \text{ TWO triangles} \\ a \geq b: \text{ ONE triangle} Triangle area formulas \text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2}bh = \tfrac{1}{2}ab\sin C \\ \text{Heron's: } A = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}, \; s = \tfrac{a+b+c}{2} Law of Cosines for finding angles — use the rearranged form \cos C = \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab} \\ \text{Always compute the largest angle first (opposite the longest side).} \\ \text{If } \cos C < 0 \text{, then } C \text{ is obtuse — the other angles must both be acute.} Law of Sines — Drag the Triangle The constant ratio is the diameter of the circumscribed circle — true for every triangle. The Law of Sines The Law of Cosines The Ambiguous Case — Watch the Triangle Count Change The same two sides and angle can produce 0, 1, or 2 triangles depending on the relative lengths. AAS triangle — Law of Sines \text{In } \triangle ABC: A=35°, B=82°, a=9. \text{ Find } b \text{ and } c. AAS: find the third angle first, then use Law of Sines twice. Always use the most accurate known pair $(a, A)$ as your reference ratio. C = 180° - 35° - 82° = 63° Find the third angle using the triangle angle sum. \frac{b}{\sin 82°} = \frac{9}{\sin 35°} \Rightarrow b = \frac{9\sin 82°}{\sin 35°} \approx \frac{9(0.9903)}{0.5736} \approx 15.53 Law of Sines: set up the ratio using the known side-angle pair $(a, A)$. c = \frac{9\sin 63°}{\sin 35°} \approx \frac{9(0.8910)}{0.5736} \approx 13.98 Same ratio, solve for $c$. SAS triangle — Law of Cosines \text{In } \triangle ABC: a=7, b=10, C=52°. \text{ Find } c, \text{ then complete the triangle.} SAS: Law of Cosines gives the unknown side, then switch to Law of Sines for angles. Find the smaller angle first to avoid the ambiguous case. c^2 = 7^2 + 10^2 - 2(7)(10)\cos 52° = 49 + 100 - 140(0.6157) \approx 149 - 86.2 = 62.8 Law of Cosines: the included angle C is opposite the unknown side c. c \approx \sqrt{62.8} \approx 7.92 Take the square root. \sin A = \frac{a\sin C}{c} = \frac{7\sin 52°}{7.92} \approx \frac{7(0.7880)}{7.92} \approx 0.696 \Rightarrow A \approx 44.0° Now use Law of Sines for angle A. Since $a < c$, angle $A < C$ — no ambiguous case here. B = 180° - 52° - 44° = 84° Third angle by subtraction. SSA — the ambiguous case \text{In } \triangle ABC: a=8, b=11, A=35°. \text{ How many triangles exist? Solve all cases.} SSA with two triangles: solve for both possible angles B, check that each gives a positive C, then complete each triangle separately. Both answers are valid. h = b\sin A = 11\sin 35° \approx 11(0.5736) \approx 6.31 Compute the critical height. h \approx 6.31 < a = 8 < b = 11 \Rightarrow \text{TWO triangles} Check: $h < a < b$ — the side $a$ is long enough to reach but short enough that it swings to two positions. \sin B = \frac{b\sin A}{a} = \frac{11\sin 35°}{8} \approx 0.7888 \Rightarrow B_1 \approx 52.0° \text{ or } B_2 = 180°-52° = 128.0° Two possible angles for $B$. Both are valid since both give a positive angle sum with $A=35°$. \text{Case 1: } B_1=52°, C_1=93°, c_1 = \frac{8\sin 93°}{\sin 35°} \approx 13.9 First triangle: $A+B_1+C_1 = 35+52+93 = 180°$ ✓ \text{Case 2: } B_2=128°, C_2=17°, c_2 = \frac{8\sin 17°}{\sin 35°} \approx 4.1 Second triangle: $A+B_2+C_2 = 35+128+17 = 180°$ ✓ \text{From a point } P \text{, the angles of elevation to the top of two towers are } 38° \text{ and } 54°. \text{ The towers are 80 m apart and } P \text{ is between them. Find the tower heights.} Each tower with point P forms a right triangle. You have one side (distance from P to each base) and the elevation angle. \text{Problem requires additional constraint. With } d_1+d_2=80: \; h_1 = d_1\tan 38°, \; h_2=(80-d_1)\tan 54°. \text{Let } d_1 = \text{distance to tower 1}, d_2 = \text{distance to tower 2. Then } d_1 + d_2 = 80. Set up: P is between the towers. h_1 = d_1\tan 38°, \quad h_2 = d_2\tan 54° Right triangle trig for each tower. \text{Need more info (or this is a two-equation system if the towers are equal height). As stated, we get a family of solutions.} Without knowing one distance, the problem is underdetermined. In practice, a third measurement or equal tower assumption is needed — a good lesson in real-world problem setup. \text{Prove the Law of Sines using the formula Area} = \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C. Express the area three different ways using three different angle-side pairs. Set them equal. \dfrac{\sin A}{a} = \dfrac{\sin B}{b} = \dfrac{\sin C}{c} \text{Area} = \tfrac{1}{2}bc\sin A = \tfrac{1}{2}ac\sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}ab\sin C The area of a triangle equals $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{two sides} \times \sin(\text{included angle})$ — three equivalent expressions. \frac{1}{2}bc\sin A = \frac{1}{2}ac\sin B \Rightarrow \frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b} Set first two equal, divide both sides by $\frac{1}{2}abc$. \frac{\sin A}{a} = \frac{\sin B}{b} = \frac{\sin C}{c} \qquad \blacksquare Same argument with the second and third. The Law of Sines follows from the area formula — a clean, elegant proof. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Trig Identities: Where They Come From
documents.subtitle: From the unit circle to the identities you actually use in calculus
documents.tags: trigonometry identities unit circle pythagorean identity angle addition
documents.assignment: Evaluating $\sin(75°)$ exactly using angle addition \text{Find the exact value of } \sin(75°).   Angle addition lets you find exact trig values for any angle that splits into 30°, 45°, or 60° pieces. \sin(75°) = \sin(45° + 30°) Split into angles whose exact values we know. = \sin 45°\cos 30° + \cos 45°\sin 30° Apply the angle addition formula for sin. = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} Substitute exact values from the unit circle. = \frac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4} Combine fractions. This is the exact answer — no calculator needed. Power Reduction — the setup for $\int \sin^2 x\, dx$ \text{Rewrite } \sin^2 x \text{ so it can be integrated.}   Power reduction is the only way to integrate even powers of sin and cos. It converts $\sin^2$ into a $\cos 2x$ term, which integrates cleanly. \cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x Start from the double angle formula. Choose the form that has $\sin^2 x$ in it. 2\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos 2x Rearrange to isolate $\sin^2 x$. \sin^2 x = \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2} Now the right side has no powers — it integrates directly term by term. \int \sin^2 x\, dx = \int \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}\, dx = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Integrate. Without power reduction this integral has no direct antiderivative. Why $\sin^2 x$ averages to $\frac{1}{2}$ The power reduction rewrite shows that $\sin^2 x$ oscillates symmetrically around $\frac{1}{2}$ — its area over a full period is exactly $\frac{\pi}{2}$. Choosing the right Pythagorean form under pressure \text{Simplify } \frac{\sin^2 x}{1 - \cos x}.   When you see $1 \pm \cos x$ or $1 \pm \sin x$ in a denominator, your first instinct should be the Pythagorean identity — it almost always creates a factorable numerator. \sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x Rewrite numerator using the Pythagorean identity. Key move: recognize $1 - \cos^2 x$ factors. = \frac{(1 - \cos x)(1 + \cos x)}{1 - \cos x} Factor as difference of squares: $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$. = 1 + \cos x \qquad (x \neq 0, 2\pi, \ldots) Cancel the $(1 - \cos x)$ factor. Valid wherever the denominator is nonzero.  \text{Prove: } \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x Start from $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ and divide every term by $\cos^2 x$. \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x  \frac{\sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x} + \frac{\cos^2 x}{\cos^2 x} = \frac{1}{\cos^2 x} Divide the Pythagorean identity through by $\cos^2 x$. \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x \qquad \blacksquare By definition $\sin/\cos = \tan$ and $1/\cos = \sec$. This identity is crucial for integrals involving $\sqrt{1 + x^2}$.  \text{Derive } \sin^2\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos x}{2} \text{ from the double angle formula.} Use $\cos 2A = 1 - 2\sin^2 A$ and substitute $A = x/2$. \sin^2\tfrac{x}{2} = \tfrac{1 - \cos x}{2}  \cos 2A = 1 - 2\sin^2 A Start from the double angle formula (the $\sin^2$ form). \text{Let } A = \frac{x}{2}: \quad \cos x = 1 - 2\sin^2\frac{x}{2} Substitute $A = x/2$ so $2A = x$. \sin^2\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos x}{2} \qquad \blacksquare Rearrange. This is the half-angle identity — useful for integrating $\sin^2(x/2)$ and appears in Fourier series.
documents.proofs: We can prove $\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta + \sin\alpha\sin\beta$ using only the distance formula and the unit circle definition. All other angle addition formulas follow from this one.
documents.applications: Trig identities are not facts to memorize — they are tools. In calculus, the choice of which identity to apply transforms an impossible integral into a trivial one. Signal processing, AC circuit analysis, and wave mechanics all reduce to identity manipulation. The single identity that generates all others \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1 Two more Pythagorean identities — divide, don't memorize \text{Divide by } \cos^2\theta: \tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta \qquad \text{Divide by } \sin^2\theta: 1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta How to see the angle addition formula geometrically \sin(\alpha+\beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta The identity family tree \text{Pythagorean} \xrightarrow{\theta+\theta} \text{Double Angle} \xrightarrow{\div 2} \text{Half Angle} \xrightarrow{\text{rearrange}} \text{Power Reduction}
documents.content: Why does $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ always? And how does knowing that save you in a calculus problem? Trig identities are not facts to memorize — they are tools. In calculus, the choice of which identity to apply transforms an impossible integral into a trivial one. Signal processing, AC circuit analysis, and wave mechanics all reduce to identity manipulation. Every trig identity is a geometric fact in disguise. The unit circle — radius 1, centered at the origin — is the source of all of them. A point on the unit circle at angle $\theta$ has coordinates $(\cos\theta,\, \sin\theta)$ by definition. Because the circle has radius 1, the Pythagorean theorem applied to that point gives $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ immediately. This is not a coincidence — it IS the Pythagorean theorem, just stated for a circle. The other identities flow from the same picture. The angle addition formulas come from rotating a point and reading off its new coordinates. The double angle formulas are just the angle addition formulas applied to $\theta + \theta$. Nothing is arbitrary. The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ has two children you derive by dividing both sides. These are the identities that appear in calculus integrals involving $\tan$ and $\sec$. The angle addition formulas are the workhorses. Every other compound identity derives from them. You should be able to re-derive the double angle formula in seconds by setting $\beta = \alpha$. Power reduction identities — expressing $\sin^2 x$ and $\cos^2 x$ as single-angle terms — are the key to integrating even powers of trig functions. If you see $\int \sin^2 x\, dx$ in calculus, power reduction is the move. We can prove $\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta + \sin\alpha\sin\beta$ using only the distance formula and the unit circle definition. All other angle addition formulas follow from this one. The single identity that generates all others \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1 Two more Pythagorean identities — divide, don't memorize \text{Divide by } \cos^2\theta: \tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta \qquad \text{Divide by } \sin^2\theta: 1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta How to see the angle addition formula geometrically \sin(\alpha+\beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta The identity family tree \text{Pythagorean} \xrightarrow{\theta+\theta} \text{Double Angle} \xrightarrow{\div 2} \text{Half Angle} \xrightarrow{\text{rearrange}} \text{Power Reduction} Angle Addition Formulas \sin(A \pm B) = \sin A\cos B \pm \cos A\sin B \qquad \cos(A \pm B) = \cos A\cos B \mp \sin A\sin B Double Angle Formulas (set $B = A$ above) \sin 2A = 2\sin A\cos A \qquad \cos 2A = \cos^2 A - \sin^2 A = 1 - 2\sin^2 A = 2\cos^2 A - 1 Power Reduction (solve double angle for $\sin^2$ and $\cos^2$) \sin^2 A = \frac{1 - \cos 2A}{2} \qquad \cos^2 A = \frac{1 + \cos 2A}{2} The most common calculus mistake with $\cos 2A$ \cos 2A \text{ has THREE equivalent forms. Pick the one that eliminates the variable you want.} \\ \text{Seeing } \sin^2? \text{ Use } 1 - 2\sin^2 A. \quad \text{Seeing } \cos^2? \text{ Use } 2\cos^2 A - 1. Unit Circle — Identities as Geometry Every trig value is a length on or inside the unit circle. Intro to Trig Identities & Proofs Pythagorean Trig Identities Symmetry, Conjugates & Reflections Sum & Difference Identities Double & Half Angle Identities Double Angle — Geometric Derivation The double angle formula is the angle addition formula applied to itself. Proof: x² + y² = r²  →  dy/dx = −x/y See what happens when calculus meets the Pythagorean identity: implicit differentiation of x² + y² = r². Evaluating $\sin(75°)$ exactly using angle addition \text{Find the exact value of } \sin(75°). Angle addition lets you find exact trig values for any angle that splits into 30°, 45°, or 60° pieces. \sin(75°) = \sin(45° + 30°) Split into angles whose exact values we know. = \sin 45°\cos 30° + \cos 45°\sin 30° Apply the angle addition formula for sin. = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} Substitute exact values from the unit circle. = \frac{\sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{4} Combine fractions. This is the exact answer — no calculator needed. Power Reduction — the setup for $\int \sin^2 x\, dx$ \text{Rewrite } \sin^2 x \text{ so it can be integrated.} Power reduction is the only way to integrate even powers of sin and cos. It converts $\sin^2$ into a $\cos 2x$ term, which integrates cleanly. \cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x Start from the double angle formula. Choose the form that has $\sin^2 x$ in it. 2\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos 2x Rearrange to isolate $\sin^2 x$. \sin^2 x = \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2} Now the right side has no powers — it integrates directly term by term. \int \sin^2 x\, dx = \int \frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}\, dx = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Integrate. Without power reduction this integral has no direct antiderivative. Why $\sin^2 x$ averages to $\frac{1}{2}$ The power reduction rewrite shows that $\sin^2 x$ oscillates symmetrically around $\frac{1}{2}$ — its area over a full period is exactly $\frac{\pi}{2}$. Choosing the right Pythagorean form under pressure \text{Simplify } \frac{\sin^2 x}{1 - \cos x}. When you see $1 \pm \cos x$ or $1 \pm \sin x$ in a denominator, your first instinct should be the Pythagorean identity — it almost always creates a factorable numerator. \sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x Rewrite numerator using the Pythagorean identity. Key move: recognize $1 - \cos^2 x$ factors. = \frac{(1 - \cos x)(1 + \cos x)}{1 - \cos x} Factor as difference of squares: $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$. = 1 + \cos x \qquad (x \neq 0, 2\pi, \ldots) Cancel the $(1 - \cos x)$ factor. Valid wherever the denominator is nonzero. \text{Prove: } \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x Start from $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ and divide every term by $\cos^2 x$. \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x \frac{\sin^2 x}{\cos^2 x} + \frac{\cos^2 x}{\cos^2 x} = \frac{1}{\cos^2 x} Divide the Pythagorean identity through by $\cos^2 x$. \tan^2 x + 1 = \sec^2 x \qquad \blacksquare By definition $\sin/\cos = \tan$ and $1/\cos = \sec$. This identity is crucial for integrals involving $\sqrt{1 + x^2}$. \text{Derive } \sin^2\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos x}{2} \text{ from the double angle formula.} Use $\cos 2A = 1 - 2\sin^2 A$ and substitute $A = x/2$. \sin^2\tfrac{x}{2} = \tfrac{1 - \cos x}{2} \cos 2A = 1 - 2\sin^2 A Start from the double angle formula (the $\sin^2$ form). \text{Let } A = \frac{x}{2}: \quad \cos x = 1 - 2\sin^2\frac{x}{2} Substitute $A = x/2$ so $2A = x$. \sin^2\frac{x}{2} = \frac{1 - \cos x}{2} \qquad \blacksquare Rearrange. This is the half-angle identity — useful for integrating $\sin^2(x/2)$ and appears in Fourier series. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Trig in Calculus: Knowing Which Identity to Reach For
documents.subtitle: The pattern-matching layer between trig identities and calculus problems
documents.tags: trig substitution chain rule integration trig derivatives strategy

documents.assignment: Chain Rule with Trig: $\frac{d}{dx}\sin(x^3)$ \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sin(x^3).   Chain rule with trig: differentiate the trig function (sin→cos, cos→−sin etc.), keep the inner function as the argument, then multiply by the inner derivative. f'(x) = \cos(\underbrace{x^3}_{\text{inner}}) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(x^3) Chain rule: derivative of outer (sin → cos) evaluated at inner, times derivative of inner. = 3x^2\cos(x^3) Power rule on the inner. Notice the argument of cos stays $x^3$ — never simplify it away. Odd Power Strategy: $\int \sin^3 x\, dx$ \text{Evaluate } \int \sin^3 x\, dx.   Odd power → peel one off, convert the rest with Pythagorean identity, $u$-substitute. Works every time. \int \sin^3 x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x \cdot \sin x\,dx Odd power of sin: factor off one $\sin x$ to pair with $dx$ for the substitution. = \int (1 - \cos^2 x)\sin x\,dx Convert $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$. Now let $u = \cos x$, $du = -\sin x\,dx$. = -\int (1 - u^2)\,du = -u + \frac{u^3}{3} + C Substitute. Clean polynomial integral. = -\cos x + \frac{\cos^3 x}{3} + C Back-substitute $u = \cos x$. Trig Sub with $\sqrt{x^2 + 4}$ \text{Evaluate } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4}}.   Pattern recognition is everything: see which radical form you have, choose the substitution, and the Pythagorean identity does the rest. \sqrt{x^2 + 4} = \sqrt{4 + x^2} \Rightarrow \text{form } \sqrt{a^2 + x^2},\ a=2 \Rightarrow x = 2\tan\theta Pattern: $\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$ → use $x = a\tan\theta$. The identity $1 + \tan^2 = \sec^2$ will eliminate the radical. dx = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta \qquad \sqrt{x^2+4} = \sqrt{4\tan^2\theta + 4} = 2\sec\theta Compute $dx$ and simplify the radical using $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$. \int \frac{2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta}{2\sec\theta} = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta = \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C Simplify and integrate. $\int \sec\theta\,d\theta$ is a standard result. = \ln\left|\frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right| + C = \ln\bigl|\sqrt{x^2+4} + x\bigr| + C Back-substitute: $\tan\theta = x/2$ so $\sec\theta = \sqrt{x^2+4}/2$. Absorb the constant $\ln(1/2)$ into $C$.  \text{Differentiate } g(x) = \tan^2(3x). This is a chain rule twice — power rule on the outer square, then chain through tan, then chain through 3x. g'(x) = 6\tan(3x)\sec^2(3x)  g(x) = [\tan(3x)]^2 \Rightarrow g'(x) = 2\tan(3x) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}[\tan(3x)] Power rule on the outer square: bring down the 2, reduce the exponent. \frac{d}{dx}[\tan(3x)] = \sec^2(3x) \cdot 3 Chain rule again: derivative of tan is $\sec^2$, times derivative of inner $3x$. g'(x) = 6\tan(3x)\sec^2(3x) Combine. This pattern — trig squared composed with a linear function — appears constantly.  \text{Evaluate } \int \sin^2 x \cos^3 x\, dx. cos has an odd power. Factor one cos off, convert the remaining $\cos^2$ using Pythagorean identity, let $u = \sin x$. \dfrac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \dfrac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C  \int \sin^2 x \cdot \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\, dx Factor off one $\cos x$ to sit with $dx$. = \int \sin^2 x (1 - \sin^2 x) \cos x\, dx Replace $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$. u = \sin x,\quad du = \cos x\,dx \Rightarrow \int u^2(1 - u^2)\,du Let $u = \sin x$. The $\cos x\, dx$ is absorbed as $du$. = \int (u^2 - u^4)\,du = \frac{u^3}{3} - \frac{u^5}{5} + C = \frac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \frac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C Integrate and back-substitute.
documents.proofs: This is the area under a semicircle — it should equal $\frac{\pi}{4}$ over $[0,1]$. We'll verify that too.
documents.applications: In signal processing, Fourier transforms decompose any signal into trig components. The integrals that arise are exactly the type covered here. In mechanics, the period of a pendulum involves an elliptic integral that requires trig substitution to approximate. Knowing the pattern lets you see the structure before you compute. Three trig situations — three different moves \text{Differentiating trig} \to \text{chain rule} \qquad \text{Integrating } \sin^n/\cos^n \to \text{parity strategy} \qquad \sqrt{a^2 \pm x^2} \to \text{trig substitution} Why trig substitution works geometrically \sqrt{1 - x^2} \text{ is the height of a point on a unit circle at horizontal position } x. \text{ Setting } x = \sin\theta \text{ makes } \sqrt{1-x^2} = \cos\theta \text{ — a leg of the same right triangle.}
documents.content: You see $\int \sqrt{1 - x^2}\, dx$. What do you do first — and why does $x = \sin\theta$ magically work? In signal processing, Fourier transforms decompose any signal into trig components. The integrals that arise are exactly the type covered here. In mechanics, the period of a pendulum involves an elliptic integral that requires trig substitution to approximate. Knowing the pattern lets you see the structure before you compute. Trig shows up in calculus in three distinct situations, each with its own strategy. Recognizing which situation you're in is more than half the work. Situation 1: you're differentiating a trig function (possibly composed with something else). This is chain rule territory. The inner function tells you what to multiply by. Situation 2: you're integrating a power of $\sin$ or $\cos$. The exponent tells you the strategy: odd power → factor one out and use $u$-sub. Even power → power reduction identity first. Situation 3: you see $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$, $\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$, or $\sqrt{x^2 - a^2}$ under a radical. This screams trig substitution — the substitution is chosen so the Pythagorean identity eliminates the radical entirely. The derivatives of all six trig functions follow from $\frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x$ and the quotient rule. You should be able to derive $\frac{d}{dx}\tan x = \sec^2 x$ on demand — it takes three lines. For integration strategy with $\sin^m x\cos^n x$: if either exponent is odd, save one copy of that function and convert the rest using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$, then $u$-substitute. If both are even, power reduce first. The trig substitution table is derived from three Pythagorean identities. You are not memorizing three arbitrary substitutions — you are choosing which Pythagorean identity will eliminate the square root. This is the area under a semicircle — it should equal $\frac{\pi}{4}$ over $[0,1]$. We'll verify that too. Three trig situations — three different moves \text{Differentiating trig} \to \text{chain rule} \qquad \text{Integrating } \sin^n/\cos^n \to \text{parity strategy} \qquad \sqrt{a^2 \pm x^2} \to \text{trig substitution} Why trig substitution works geometrically \sqrt{1 - x^2} \text{ is the height of a point on a unit circle at horizontal position } x. \text{ Setting } x = \sin\theta \text{ makes } \sqrt{1-x^2} = \cos\theta \text{ — a leg of the same right triangle.} Trig Derivatives (the six you must know cold) \frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x \quad \frac{d}{dx}\cos x = -\sin x \quad \frac{d}{dx}\tan x = \sec^2 x \\ \frac{d}{dx}\csc x = -\csc x\cot x \quad \frac{d}{dx}\sec x = \sec x\tan x \quad \frac{d}{dx}\cot x = -\csc^2 x Trig Substitution Table — read from the radical \sqrt{a^2 - x^2} \to x = a\sin\theta \quad (\text{uses } 1 - \sin^2 = \cos^2) \\ \sqrt{a^2 + x^2} \to x = a\tan\theta \quad (\text{uses } 1 + \tan^2 = \sec^2) \\ \sqrt{x^2 - a^2} \to x = a\sec\theta \quad (\text{uses } \sec^2 - 1 = \tan^2) Odd-power integration strategy \int \sin^5 x\cos^2 x\,dx: \quad \text{odd power of sin} \Rightarrow \text{factor out } \sin x, \text{ convert } \sin^4 = (1-\cos^2)^2, \text{ let } u = \cos x Don't forget $dx$ when substituting x = a\sin\theta \Rightarrow dx = a\cos\theta\,d\theta. \text{ The } dx \text{ replacement is essential and easy to drop under pressure.} Why $x = \sin\theta$ Kills the Radical Drag $x$ along the circle to see how $\sqrt{1 - x^2}$ corresponds to $\cos\theta$. Two Function Machines — Inner and Outer Shrinking Δx — Why Derivatives Multiply Rates Multiplying — Leibniz Notation Integration Strategy Decision Tree The strategy is determined by the structure of the integrand, not the specific numbers. The sin(x³) Proof — Step by Step Practice — 10 Forms of the Chain Rule Chain Rule with Trig: $\frac{d}{dx}\sin(x^3)$ \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sin(x^3). Chain rule with trig: differentiate the trig function (sin→cos, cos→−sin etc.), keep the inner function as the argument, then multiply by the inner derivative. f'(x) = \cos(\underbrace{x^3}_{\text{inner}}) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(x^3) Chain rule: derivative of outer (sin → cos) evaluated at inner, times derivative of inner. = 3x^2\cos(x^3) Power rule on the inner. Notice the argument of cos stays $x^3$ — never simplify it away. Odd Power Strategy: $\int \sin^3 x\, dx$ \text{Evaluate } \int \sin^3 x\, dx. Odd power → peel one off, convert the rest with Pythagorean identity, $u$-substitute. Works every time. \int \sin^3 x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x \cdot \sin x\,dx Odd power of sin: factor off one $\sin x$ to pair with $dx$ for the substitution. = \int (1 - \cos^2 x)\sin x\,dx Convert $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$. Now let $u = \cos x$, $du = -\sin x\,dx$. = -\int (1 - u^2)\,du = -u + \frac{u^3}{3} + C Substitute. Clean polynomial integral. = -\cos x + \frac{\cos^3 x}{3} + C Back-substitute $u = \cos x$. Trig Sub with $\sqrt{x^2 + 4}$ \text{Evaluate } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2 + 4}}. Pattern recognition is everything: see which radical form you have, choose the substitution, and the Pythagorean identity does the rest. \sqrt{x^2 + 4} = \sqrt{4 + x^2} \Rightarrow \text{form } \sqrt{a^2 + x^2},\ a=2 \Rightarrow x = 2\tan\theta Pattern: $\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$ → use $x = a\tan\theta$. The identity $1 + \tan^2 = \sec^2$ will eliminate the radical. dx = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta \qquad \sqrt{x^2+4} = \sqrt{4\tan^2\theta + 4} = 2\sec\theta Compute $dx$ and simplify the radical using $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$. \int \frac{2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta}{2\sec\theta} = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta = \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C Simplify and integrate. $\int \sec\theta\,d\theta$ is a standard result. = \ln\left|\frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{2} + \frac{x}{2}\right| + C = \ln\bigl|\sqrt{x^2+4} + x\bigr| + C Back-substitute: $\tan\theta = x/2$ so $\sec\theta = \sqrt{x^2+4}/2$. Absorb the constant $\ln(1/2)$ into $C$. \text{Differentiate } g(x) = \tan^2(3x). This is a chain rule twice — power rule on the outer square, then chain through tan, then chain through 3x. g'(x) = 6\tan(3x)\sec^2(3x) g(x) = [\tan(3x)]^2 \Rightarrow g'(x) = 2\tan(3x) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}[\tan(3x)] Power rule on the outer square: bring down the 2, reduce the exponent. \frac{d}{dx}[\tan(3x)] = \sec^2(3x) \cdot 3 Chain rule again: derivative of tan is $\sec^2$, times derivative of inner $3x$. g'(x) = 6\tan(3x)\sec^2(3x) Combine. This pattern — trig squared composed with a linear function — appears constantly. \text{Evaluate } \int \sin^2 x \cos^3 x\, dx. cos has an odd power. Factor one cos off, convert the remaining $\cos^2$ using Pythagorean identity, let $u = \sin x$. \dfrac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \dfrac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C \int \sin^2 x \cdot \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\, dx Factor off one $\cos x$ to sit with $dx$. = \int \sin^2 x (1 - \sin^2 x) \cos x\, dx Replace $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$. u = \sin x,\quad du = \cos x\,dx \Rightarrow \int u^2(1 - u^2)\,du Let $u = \sin x$. The $\cos x\, dx$ is absorbed as $du$. = \int (u^2 - u^4)\,du = \frac{u^3}{3} - \frac{u^5}{5} + C = \frac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \frac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C Integrate and back-substitute. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change

documents.title: Logarithms: Why $\ln$ Is Everywhere in Calculus
documents.subtitle: Log laws from geometry, the natural log as an area, and why $e$ is the right base
documents.tags: logarithms natural log ln exponentials log laws integration
documents.aliases: growth decay e^x logarithm ln
documents.assignment: Logarithmic Differentiation — when the exponent has a variable \text{Differentiate } f(x) = x^x.   When the exponent contains the variable, take $\ln$ of both sides first. This converts the exponential into a product and makes differentiation possible. \ln(f(x)) = \ln(x^x) = x\ln x Take $\ln$ of both sides. Use $\ln(a^r) = r\ln a$ to bring the exponent down. Now it's a product, not an exponential — much easier to differentiate. \frac{f'(x)}{f(x)} = \ln x + x \cdot \frac{1}{x} = \ln x + 1 Differentiate both sides. Left side uses $\frac{d}{dx}\ln(f) = f'/f$. Right side uses product rule. f'(x) = f(x)(\ln x + 1) = x^x(\ln x + 1) Multiply both sides by $f(x) = x^x$. This technique works for any function where the variable appears in the exponent. Integrating $\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}$ — recognizing the log form \text{Evaluate } \int \frac{2x}{x^2 + 1}\,dx.   Any time the numerator is (close to) the derivative of the denominator, $\ln$ is the antiderivative. Check for this pattern before trying anything else in rational integrals. \text{Numerator is } 2x = \frac{d}{dx}(x^2 + 1) Check: is the numerator the derivative of the denominator? Yes. This is the $\frac{f'}{f}$ pattern. u = x^2 + 1,\quad du = 2x\,dx Let $u$ equal the denominator. The numerator $2x\,dx$ becomes $du$ exactly. \int \frac{du}{u} = \ln|u| + C = \ln|x^2 + 1| + C = \ln(x^2+1) + C Standard $\int 1/u\,du = \ln|u|$. Since $x^2+1 > 0$ always, we drop the absolute value. Expanding with log laws before differentiating \text{Differentiate } y = \ln\!\left(\frac{x^3\sqrt{x+1}}{(2x-1)^4}\right).   Always expand $\ln$ using log laws before differentiating a product/quotient/power inside a log. It converts one hard problem into several easy ones. y = 3\ln x + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(x+1) - 4\ln(2x-1) Apply log laws to break apart the single log: product → sum, quotient → difference, power → coefficient. Always do this before differentiating a complex log. y' = \frac{3}{x} + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{x+1} - 4 \cdot \frac{2}{2x-1} Differentiate term by term. Each term is a simple $\frac{d}{dx}\ln(u) = u'/u$. y' = \frac{3}{x} + \frac{1}{2(x+1)} - \frac{8}{2x-1} Simplify. Without log expansion, this would have required the quotient rule and product rule on a complex expression.  \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \ln(\sin x). Chain rule: $\frac{d}{dx}\ln(u) = \frac{u'}{u}$. What is $u$ here? f'(x) = \cot x  f'(x) = \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x Chain rule: outer is $\ln$ (derivative $1/u$), inner is $\sin x$ (derivative $\cos x$). = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} = \cot x Simplify. This result is the antiderivative of $\cot x$: $\int \cot x\,dx = \ln|\sin x| + C$.  \text{Evaluate } \int_1^e \frac{(\ln x)^2}{x}\, dx. Spot the $f'/f$ or let $u = \ln x$. What is $du$? \dfrac{1}{3}  u = \ln x,\quad du = \frac{1}{x}\,dx Let $u = \ln x$. Then $du = (1/x)\,dx$ — which is exactly what appears in the integrand. x=1 \Rightarrow u=0;\quad x=e \Rightarrow u=1 \qquad \int_0^1 u^2\,du Change limits using $u = \ln x$. Clean power integral. \bigl[\tfrac{u^3}{3}\bigr]_0^1 = \frac{1}{3} Evaluate. The original definite integral equals $\frac{1}{3}$.
documents.proofs: With $\ln$ defined as area under $1/t$, the log laws become geometric facts. Here we prove the product rule for logs rigorously.
documents.applications: The natural log is not just a base-$e$ curiosity. It appears in entropy, information theory, population growth models, RC circuit discharge, radioactive decay, and every integral involving $1/x$ or rational functions. Understanding why $\ln$ is natural — not just that it is — changes how you recognize when to use it.
documents.content: Why does $\int \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln|x| + C$? What does area under a curve have to do with logarithms? The natural log is not just a base-$e$ curiosity. It appears in entropy, information theory, population growth models, RC circuit discharge, radioactive decay, and every integral involving $1/x$ or rational functions. Understanding why $\ln$ is natural — not just that it is — changes how you recognize when to use it. With $\ln$ defined as area under $1/t$, the log laws become geometric facts. Here we prove the product rule for logs rigorously. Logarithmic Differentiation — when the exponent has a variable \text{Differentiate } f(x) = x^x. Left side uses $\frac{d}{dx}\ln(f) = f'/f$. Right side uses product rule. f'(x) = f(x)(\ln x + 1) = x^x(\ln x + 1) Multiply both sides by $f(x) = x^x$. Expanding with log laws before differentiating \text{Differentiate } y = \ln\!\left(\frac{x^3\sqrt{x+1}}{(2x-1)^4}\right). Always expand $\ln$ using log laws before differentiating a product/quotient/power inside a log. This result is the antiderivative of $\cot x$: $\int \cot x\,dx = \ln|\sin x| + C$. \text{Evaluate } \int_1^e \frac{(\ln x)^2}{x}\, dx. Spot the $f'/f$ or let $u = \ln x$. Then $du = (1/x)\,dx$ — which is exactly what appears in the integrand. x=1 \Rightarrow u=0;\quad x=e \Rightarrow u=1 \qquad \int_0^1 u^2\,du Change limits using $u = \ln x$. documents.title: Trig in the Real World: Periodic Models & Solving Equations
documents.subtitle: Sinusoidal models, solving trig equations completely, and why periodicity matters in calculus
documents.tags: trig applications sinusoidal model periodic solving trig equations amplitude phase shift period
documents.aliases: real world engineering physics economics biology computer science
documents.assignment: Building a sinusoidal model from a description \text{A tide oscillates between 0.5 m and 3.5 m with a period of 12.4 hours. High tide occurs at } t=2 \text{ h. Write a model.}   Systematic model building: amplitude, midline, period, phase shift — in that order. Cosine is natural when you know where the maximum occurs. A = \frac{3.5 - 0.5}{2} = 1.5 \qquad D = \frac{3.5 + 0.5}{2} = 2 Amplitude = half the range. Midline = average of max and min. B = \frac{2\pi}{12.4} \approx 0.507 $B = 2\pi/\text{period}$. h(t) = 1.5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{12.4}(t - 2)\right) + 2 Use cosine — peaks at the phase shift. High tide at $t=2$ means shift right by 2. Check: $h(2) = 1.5 + 2 = 3.5$ ✓ Solving a trig equation using a double angle identity \text{Solve: } \sin 2x = \sin x \text{ on } [0, 2\pi).   When you see a double angle in an equation, apply the identity immediately to get everything in terms of the same angle. Then factor algebraically. 2\sin x\cos x - \sin x = 0 Replace $\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$, move everything to one side. \sin x(2\cos x - 1) = 0 Factor out $\sin x$. \sin x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 0, \pi \qquad \cos x = \tfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{\pi}{3}, \tfrac{5\pi}{3} Set each factor to zero. Four solutions total in $[0, 2\pi)$. Calculus connection — rate of change of a sinusoidal model \text{For the tide model } h(t) = 1.5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{12.4}(t-2)\right) + 2, \text{ how fast is the tide rising at } t=5 \text{ h?}   The derivative of a sinusoidal model is another sinusoidal model — same period, amplitude scaled by the frequency, phase shifted by $\pi/2$. h'(t) = -1.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{12.4}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{12.4}(t-2)\right) Differentiate: amplitude $\times$ frequency becomes the new amplitude. Cosine → negative sine. h'(5) = -1.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{12.4} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{12.4}(3)\right) \approx -0.507\sin(1.532) \approx -0.507 \times 0.9996 \approx -0.507 Substitute $t=5$. The negative value means the tide is falling at about 0.507 m/h at $t=5$.  \text{Solve } 2\sin^2 x + 3\cos x = 3 \text{ on } [0, 2\pi). Replace $\sin^2 x$ using the Pythagorean identity to get a quadratic in $\cos x$. x = 0,\; \pi/3,\; 5\pi/3  2(1-\cos^2 x) + 3\cos x = 3 Substitute $\sin^2 x = 1-\cos^2 x$. 2\cos^2 x - 3\cos x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow (2\cos x - 1)(\cos x - 1) = 0 Rearrange and factor the quadratic. \cos x = \tfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x = \tfrac{\pi}{3}, \tfrac{5\pi}{3} \qquad \cos x = 1 \Rightarrow x = 0 Three solutions: $x = 0, \pi/3, 5\pi/3$.  \text{A particle oscillates with position } s(t) = 3\sin(2t) + 4\cos(2t). \text{ Write this as } A\sin(2t + \phi) \text{ and find the amplitude and phase shift.} Use the identity $a\sin\theta + b\cos\theta = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\sin(\theta + \phi)$ where $\tan\phi = b/a$. A = 5, \; \phi = \arctan(4/3) \approx 0.927 \text{ rad}  A = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 Amplitude from the combining formula. \tan\phi = \frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \phi = \arctan\frac{4}{3} \approx 0.927 \text{ rad} Phase shift from $\tan\phi = b/a$ where $b=4$, $a=3$. s(t) = 5\sin(2t + 0.927) Combined form. Amplitude 5, phase shift $\approx 53.1°$. The maximum of 5 is larger than either original amplitude (3 or 4) — the two oscillations partially reinforce each other.
documents.applications: AC electrical circuits run at 60 Hz — the current is $I(t) = I_0 \sin(120\pi t)$. Manufacturing processes that involve rotating machinery produce sinusoidal vibrations — predictable and modelable. In calculus, knowing the period, amplitude, and phase shift of a function lets you immediately write down its integral and derivative without computation, just by reading the transformed formula.
documents.content: A sound wave, a tidal pattern, an electrical signal, the position of a piston — all are modelled by $A\sin(Bt + C) + D$. What do those four constants actually control, and how do you read them from a graph or a real-world description? AC electrical circuits run at 60 Hz — the current is $I(t) = I_0 \sin(120\pi t)$. Manufacturing processes that involve rotating machinery produce sinusoidal vibrations — predictable and modelable. In calculus, knowing the period, amplitude, and phase shift of a function lets you immediately write down its integral and derivative without computation, just by reading the transformed formula. Building a sinusoidal model from a description \text{A tide oscillates between 0.5 m and 3.5 m with a period of 12.4 hours. High tide occurs at } t=2 \text{ h. Write a model.} Systematic model building: amplitude, midline, period, phase shift — in that order. Cosine is natural when you know where the maximum occurs. Cosine → negative sine. h'(5) = -1.5 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{12.4} \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{12.4}(3)\right) \approx -0.507\sin(1.532) \approx -0.507 \times 0.9996 \approx -0.507 Substitute $t=5$. The negative value means the tide is falling at about 0.507 m/h at $t=5$. \text{Solve } 2\sin^2 x + 3\cos x = 3 \text{ on } [0, 2\pi). A = 5, \; \phi = \arctan(4/3) \approx 0.927 \text{ rad} A = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 Amplitude from the combining formula. \tan\phi = \frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \phi = \arctan\frac{4}{3} \approx 0.927 \text{ rad} Phase shift from $\tan\phi = b/a$ where $b=4$, $a=3$. s(t) = 5\sin(2t + 0.927) Combined form. Amplitude 5, phase shift $\approx 53.1°$. documents.title: Exponential Functions: When the Exponent Is the Variable
documents.subtitle: The difference between $x^2$ and $2^x$ is everything — one grows polynomially, the other eventually overtakes any polynomial
documents.tags: exponential functions exponential growth exponential decay base natural base e compound interest graphing exponentials transformations
