documents.title: Solving Exponential and Logarithmic Equations
documents.subtitle: Two moves unlock every equation in this family: take a log of both sides, or exponentiate both sides
documents.tags: solving exponential equations solving logarithmic equations natural log common log extraneous solutions one-to-one property different bases
documents.aliases: growth decay e^x logarithm ln
documents.assignment: Different bases — take ln of both sides \text{Solve: } 5^{x+2} = 3^{2x-1}   When bases differ, take ln immediately. The power rule converts the equation to linear — collect $x$ terms, factor, divide. \ln(5^{x+2}) = \ln(3^{2x-1}) Take ln of both sides. Valid because ln is one-to-one. (x+2)\ln 5 = (2x-1)\ln 3 Power rule: bring exponents down. x\ln 5 + 2\ln 5 = 2x\ln 3 - \ln 3 Expand both sides. x(\ln 5 - 2\ln 3) = -\ln 3 - 2\ln 5 Collect $x$ terms on one side. x = \frac{-\ln 3 - 2\ln 5}{\ln 5 - 2\ln 3} = \frac{\ln 3 + 2\ln 5}{2\ln 3 - \ln 5} \approx \frac{4.288}{1.415} \approx 3.03 Solve. Leave exact, approximate at the end. Log equation — extraneous solution \text{Solve: } \log_2(x+3) + \log_2(x-3) = 4   Quadratic log equations often produce two candidates but one is extraneous. Always check both. \log_2[(x+3)(x-3)] = 4 Condense using the product rule. \log_2(x^2-9) = 4 \Rightarrow x^2 - 9 = 2^4 = 16 Exponentiate both sides: $2^{\log_2(...)} = ...$ x^2 = 25 \Rightarrow x = \pm 5 Solve the resulting equation. \text{Check } x=5: \log_2(8)+\log_2(2)=3+1=4 \checkmark Valid. \text{Check } x=-5: \log_2(-2) \text{ is undefined.} \times \Rightarrow \text{extraneous} Discard $x=-5$. Only $x=5$ is valid. Natural exponential equation \text{Solve: } 3e^{2x} - 4 = 11   Always isolate the exponential before taking the log. Never take ln before the base is alone on one side. 3e^{2x} = 15 \Rightarrow e^{2x} = 5 Isolate the exponential first. \ln(e^{2x}) = \ln 5 \Rightarrow 2x = \ln 5 Take ln of both sides. $\ln(e^{2x}) = 2x$ by cancellation identity. x = \frac{\ln 5}{2} \approx \frac{1.609}{2} \approx 0.805 Exact answer first, decimal approximation second.  \text{Solve: } \log_3(x-1) - \log_3(x+1) = 2 Use the quotient rule to condense, then exponentiate base 3. \text{No solution (extraneous result)}  \log_3\frac{x-1}{x+1} = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{x+1} = 3^2 = 9 Condense and exponentiate. x - 1 = 9(x+1) = 9x + 9 \Rightarrow -8x = 10 \Rightarrow x = -\frac{5}{4} Solve the resulting equation. \text{Check: } x-1 = -\tfrac{9}{4} < 0. \text{ Logarithm undefined.} \Rightarrow \text{no solution} Extraneous! The original equation has NO solution.  \text{Solve } 2^x + 2^{-x} = 5. \text{ Hint: it is quadratic in } 2^x. Multiply through by $2^x$ to clear the negative exponent. x = \pm\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2}\right) \approx \pm 2.26  \text{Let } u = 2^x. \text{ Then } 2^{-x} = 1/u. \text{ Equation: } u + 1/u = 5. Substitution. u^2 + 1 = 5u \Rightarrow u^2 - 5u + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{21}}{2} Multiply by $u$, apply quadratic formula. u > 0 \text{ both roots}: u = \frac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 4.79 \text{ or } u = \frac{5-\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 0.21 Both are positive, so both give valid solutions. x = \log_2\frac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 2.26 \quad \text{or} \quad x = \log_2\frac{5-\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx -2.26 Note symmetry: the two solutions are negatives of each other (because $2^x + 2^{-x}$ is even).
documents.applications: Solving exponential equations is how scientists compute radioactive half-lives, how pharmacists calculate drug elimination times, and how financial analysts find the time for an investment to reach a target. Solving logarithmic equations appears in chemistry (pH calculations), acoustics (decibel levels), and seismology (Richter magnitudes). The same two algebraic moves — take a log, or exponentiate — unlock all of these. Master strategy — two types, two moves \text{Exponential equation (variable in exponent):} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{take } \ln \text{ (or } \log\text{) of both sides, use power rule} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{if same base: set exponents equal (one-to-one)} \\ \text{Logarithmic equation (variable inside log):} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{condense to one log, then exponentiate both sides} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{always check for extraneous solutions} One-to-one properties b^x = b^y \iff x = y \quad \text{(exponential one-to-one)} \\ \log_b x = \log_b y \iff x = y \quad \text{(log one-to-one)} \\ \text{These let you "cancel" matching bases or matching logs.} Extraneous solutions in log equations \text{Solve, then CHECK every answer.} \\ \text{Example: if you get } x = -5 \text{, and the equation has } \ln(x+3), \\ \text{then } \ln(-5+3) = \ln(-2) \text{ is undefined.} \\ x = -5 \text{ is extraneous — discard it.}
documents.content: To solve $2^x = 5$, you cannot just take a square root or factor. You need to "undo" the exponential — and that is exactly what logarithms are for. Once you know the move, every exponential equation becomes a linear or quadratic equation in disguise. Solving exponential equations is how scientists compute radioactive half-lives, how pharmacists calculate drug elimination times, and how financial analysts find the time for an investment to reach a target. Solving logarithmic equations appears in chemistry (pH calculations), acoustics (decibel levels), and seismology (Richter magnitudes). The same two algebraic moves — take a log, or exponentiate — unlock all of these. There are two master moves. To solve an exponential equation: take the logarithm of both sides. To solve a logarithmic equation: exponentiate both sides (raise the base to each side as an exponent). These two moves are inverses of each other — each undoes what the other does. The one-to-one property makes both moves valid: if $b^x = b^y$ then $x = y$ (exponentials are one-to-one), and if $\log_b x = \log_b y$ then $x = y$ (logs are one-to-one). This means equal outputs imply equal inputs — the foundation for all equation solving here. Logarithmic equations require a domain check — extraneous solutions are common. After solving, substitute every answer back into the original equation. Any answer that makes a log argument non-positive is extraneous and must be discarded. Exponential equations have no extraneous solutions because $b^x > 0$ always. When both sides have the same base, set exponents equal directly: $3^{2x+1} = 3^{x-4} \Rightarrow 2x+1 = x-4$. When bases differ, take $\ln$ of both sides and use the power rule: $5^x = 12 \Rightarrow x\ln 5 = \ln 12 \Rightarrow x = \ln 12 / \ln 5$. This is exact — do not approximate until the final step. Some exponential equations are quadratic in disguise. $e^{2x} - 5e^x + 6 = 0$ is a quadratic in $e^x$: let $u = e^x$, factor $u^2 - 5u + 6 = (u-2)(u-3) = 0$, giving $e^x = 2$ or $e^x = 3$, so $x = \ln 2$ or $x = \ln 3$. Recognising this substitution is a key skill. Equations with different log bases: use the change of base formula to convert everything to $\ln$ or $\log$, then solve algebraically. Alternatively, use the one-to-one property after getting logs with the same base on both sides. Master strategy — two types, two moves \text{Exponential equation (variable in exponent):} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{take } \ln \text{ (or } \log\text{) of both sides, use power rule} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{if same base: set exponents equal (one-to-one)} \\ \text{Logarithmic equation (variable inside log):} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \text{condense to one log, then exponentiate both sides} \\ \quad \Rightarrow \textbf{always check for extraneous solutions} One-to-one properties b^x = b^y \iff x = y \quad \text{(exponential one-to-one)} \\ \log_b x = \log_b y \iff x = y \quad \text{(log one-to-one)} \\ \text{These let you "cancel" matching bases or matching logs.} Extraneous solutions in log equations \text{Solve, then CHECK every answer.} \\ \text{Example: if you get } x = -5 \text{, and the equation has } \ln(x+3), \\ \text{then } \ln(-5+3) = \ln(-2) \text{ is undefined.} \\ x = -5 \text{ is extraneous — discard it.} Solving exponential equations — the full toolkit \text{Same base: } b^{f(x)} = b^{g(x)} \Rightarrow f(x) = g(x) \\ \text{Different bases: take ln both sides} \Rightarrow x = \frac{\ln(\text{RHS})}{\ln(\text{base})} \\ \text{Quadratic type: substitute } u = b^x, \text{ factor, solve for } u, \text{ then } x = \log_b u Solving logarithmic equations — the full toolkit \text{One log: } \log_b f(x) = c \Rightarrow f(x) = b^c \\ \text{Multiple logs: condense to one log first, then exponentiate} \\ \text{Equal logs: } \log_b f(x) = \log_b g(x) \Rightarrow f(x) = g(x) \\ \textbf{Always: check domain of original equation} Graphical Solution — Where the Curves Intersect Every equation is secretly "find where two functions are equal." Seeing the intersection makes the solution concrete. Different bases — take ln of both sides \text{Solve: } 5^{x+2} = 3^{2x-1} When bases differ, take ln immediately. The power rule converts the equation to linear — collect $x$ terms, factor, divide. \ln(5^{x+2}) = \ln(3^{2x-1}) Take ln of both sides. Valid because ln is one-to-one. (x+2)\ln 5 = (2x-1)\ln 3 Power rule: bring exponents down. x\ln 5 + 2\ln 5 = 2x\ln 3 - \ln 3 Expand both sides. x(\ln 5 - 2\ln 3) = -\ln 3 - 2\ln 5 Collect $x$ terms on one side. x = \frac{-\ln 3 - 2\ln 5}{\ln 5 - 2\ln 3} = \frac{\ln 3 + 2\ln 5}{2\ln 3 - \ln 5} \approx \frac{4.288}{1.415} \approx 3.03 Solve. Leave exact, approximate at the end. Log equation — extraneous solution \text{Solve: } \log_2(x+3) + \log_2(x-3) = 4 Quadratic log equations often produce two candidates but one is extraneous. Always check both. \log_2[(x+3)(x-3)] = 4 Condense using the product rule. \log_2(x^2-9) = 4 \Rightarrow x^2 - 9 = 2^4 = 16 Exponentiate both sides: $2^{\log_2(...)} = ...$ x^2 = 25 \Rightarrow x = \pm 5 Solve the resulting equation. \text{Check } x=5: \log_2(8)+\log_2(2)=3+1=4 \checkmark Valid. \text{Check } x=-5: \log_2(-2) \text{ is undefined.} \times \Rightarrow \text{extraneous} Discard $x=-5$. Only $x=5$ is valid. Natural exponential equation \text{Solve: } 3e^{2x} - 4 = 11 Always isolate the exponential before taking the log. Never take ln before the base is alone on one side. 3e^{2x} = 15 \Rightarrow e^{2x} = 5 Isolate the exponential first. \ln(e^{2x}) = \ln 5 \Rightarrow 2x = \ln 5 Take ln of both sides. $\ln(e^{2x}) = 2x$ by cancellation identity. x = \frac{\ln 5}{2} \approx \frac{1.609}{2} \approx 0.805 Exact answer first, decimal approximation second. \text{Solve: } \log_3(x-1) - \log_3(x+1) = 2 Use the quotient rule to condense, then exponentiate base 3. \text{No solution (extraneous result)} \log_3\frac{x-1}{x+1} = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{x-1}{x+1} = 3^2 = 9 Condense and exponentiate. x - 1 = 9(x+1) = 9x + 9 \Rightarrow -8x = 10 \Rightarrow x = -\frac{5}{4} Solve the resulting equation. \text{Check: } x-1 = -\tfrac{9}{4} < 0. \text{ Logarithm undefined.} \Rightarrow \text{no solution} Extraneous! The original equation has NO solution. \text{Solve } 2^x + 2^{-x} = 5. \text{ Hint: it is quadratic in } 2^x. Multiply through by $2^x$ to clear the negative exponent. x = \pm\log_2\!\left(\dfrac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2}\right) \approx \pm 2.26 \text{Let } u = 2^x. \text{ Then } 2^{-x} = 1/u. \text{ Equation: } u + 1/u = 5. Substitution. u^2 + 1 = 5u \Rightarrow u^2 - 5u + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow u = \frac{5 \pm \sqrt{21}}{2} Multiply by $u$, apply quadratic formula. u > 0 \text{ both roots}: u = \frac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 4.79 \text{ or } u = \frac{5-\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 0.21 Both are positive, so both give valid solutions. x = \log_2\frac{5+\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx 2.26 \quad \text{or} \quad x = \log_2\frac{5-\sqrt{21}}{2} \approx -2.26 Note symmetry: the two solutions are negatives of each other (because $2^x + 2^{-x}$ is even). assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step growth decay e^x logarithm ln

documents.title: Exponential Models: Growth, Decay, and Everything In Between
documents.subtitle: One equation — $A = A_0 e^{kt}$ — models populations, radioactive decay, cooling, and more. The sign of $k$ decides everything.
documents.tags: exponential growth exponential decay population growth radioactive decay half-life doubling time Newton law of cooling logistic growth carrying capacity modeling
documents.aliases: growth decay e^x logarithm ln
documents.assignment: Population growth — finding k and predicting \text{A city had population 250,000 in 2000 and 310,000 in 2010. Assuming exponential growth, predict the 2025 population.}   Always set a clear $t=0$ reference. Keep $k$ in exact form ($\ln(310/250)/10$) until the final calculation to avoid rounding errors. k = \frac{\ln(310000/250000)}{2010-2000} = \frac{\ln(1.24)}{10} \approx \frac{0.2151}{10} \approx 0.02151 \text{ yr}^{-1} Find $k$ from the two data points. A(t) = 250000\,e^{0.02151 t} \quad (t = \text{years after 2000}) Write the model. Use $A_0 = 250000$ (year 2000 as reference). A(25) = 250000\,e^{0.02151 \times 25} = 250000\,e^{0.5378} \approx 250000(1.712) \approx 428{,}000 $t=25$ for year 2025. Radioactive decay — carbon dating \text{A fossil contains 35\% of its original C-14. Estimate its age.}   Carbon dating is only reliable for samples up to ~50,000 years (about 9 half-lives). Beyond that, too little C-14 remains to measure accurately. t = \frac{5730}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln\frac{A_0}{A} = \frac{5730}{0.6931} \cdot \ln\frac{1}{0.35} Apply the carbon dating formula. $A/A_0 = 0.35$, so $A_0/A = 1/0.35$. = 8267 \cdot \ln(2.857) = 8267 \cdot 1.0498 \approx 8679 \text{ years} Calculate. The fossil is approximately 8,700 years old. Newton's Law of Cooling \text{Coffee at 90°C is placed in a 20°C room. After 10 min it is 70°C. When will it reach 40°C?}   The coffee cools to 40°C in about 37 minutes. Note that it approaches 20°C (room temperature) asymptotically — it theoretically never reaches exactly 20°C. T(t) = 20 + 70e^{kt} \quad [T_0=90, T_e=20, \text{ so } T_0-T_e=70] Set up the model. 70 = 20 + 70e^{10k} \Rightarrow 50 = 70e^{10k} \Rightarrow k = \frac{\ln(5/7)}{10} \approx -0.03365 Use the data point $(10, 70)$ to find $k$. 40 = 20 + 70e^{kt} \Rightarrow 20 = 70e^{kt} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(2/7)}{k} = \frac{\ln(2/7)}{\ln(5/7)/10} \approx \frac{-1.2528}{-0.03365} \approx 37.2 \text{ min} Solve for $t$ when $T=40$.  \text{A drug has a half-life of 4 hours in the bloodstream. A patient takes a 200 mg dose. How long until less than 10 mg remains?} Find $k$ from the half-life, then solve $10 = 200e^{kt}$ for $t$. t \approx 17.3 \text{ hours (just over 4 half-lives)}  k = -\ln 2 / 4 \approx -0.1733 \text{ hr}^{-1} Find the decay constant from the half-life. 10 = 200e^{-0.1733t} \Rightarrow 0.05 = e^{-0.1733t} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln 0.05}{-0.1733} \approx \frac{-2.996}{-0.1733} \approx 17.3 \text{ hr} Solve for $t$.  \text{A lake can support at most 10,000 fish (carrying capacity). Currently there are 2,000 fish and the population is growing at } k = 0.3 \text{ per year. Write the logistic model and find the population after 5 years.} Find $C = (L - P_0)/P_0$ first, then apply the logistic formula. P(t) = \dfrac{10000}{1+4e^{-0.3t}}; \quad P(5) \approx 5284  C = \frac{10000 - 2000}{2000} = 4 Compute $C$ from the initial condition. P(t) = \frac{10000}{1 + 4e^{-0.3t}} Logistic model. P(5) = \frac{10000}{1 + 4e^{-1.5}} = \frac{10000}{1 + 4(0.2231)} = \frac{10000}{1.8924} \approx 5284 \text{ fish} After 5 years — more than doubled but well below carrying capacity.
documents.applications: Exponential models describe the first phase of almost every growth or decay process. A bacteria colony, a chain reaction, a viral post, a rumour — all grow exponentially when resources are unlimited. But resources are never unlimited forever, which is why logistic models (which cap at a carrying capacity) better describe long-run behaviour. In manufacturing, exponential decay describes equipment degradation, signal attenuation in cables, and the discharge of a capacitor. In pharmacology, drug concentration in the bloodstream follows exponential decay between doses. The universal exponential model A(t) = A_0\,e^{kt} \\ A_0 = \text{initial amount} \quad k > 0: \text{growth} \quad k < 0: \text{decay} \\ T_{\text{double}} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \quad T_{1/2} = \frac{-\ln 2}{k} = \frac{\ln 2}{|k|} Finding k from two data points 1.\; \text{Write: } A_1 = A_0 e^{kt_1} \text{ and } A_2 = A_0 e^{kt_2} \\ 2.\; \text{Divide: } \frac{A_2}{A_1} = e^{k(t_2-t_1)} \\ 3.\; \text{Take ln: } k = \frac{\ln(A_2/A_1)}{t_2 - t_1} \\ 4.\; \text{Back-substitute to find } A_0 \text{ if needed} Newton's Law of Cooling — same structure T(t) = T_e + (T_0 - T_e)e^{kt} \quad k < 0 \\ T_e = \text{ambient temperature (environment)} \\ T_0 = \text{initial temperature of object} \\ \text{As } t \to \infty: T \to T_e \text{ — approaches room temperature} Logistic growth — when resources are limited P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + Ce^{-kt}} \quad L = \text{carrying capacity} \\ \text{Early: behaves like exponential} \\ \text{Later: slows as } P \to L \text{ (S-shaped curve)} \\ \text{Used for: population, disease spread, product adoption}
documents.content: In 1950 the world population was 2.5 billion. In 2000 it was 6.1 billion. Assuming exponential growth, what would it be in 2050? And why does this model eventually break down? Exponential models describe the first phase of almost every growth or decay process. A bacteria colony, a chain reaction, a viral post, a rumour — all grow exponentially when resources are unlimited. But resources are never unlimited forever, which is why logistic models (which cap at a carrying capacity) better describe long-run behaviour. In manufacturing, exponential decay describes equipment degradation, signal attenuation in cables, and the discharge of a capacitor. In pharmacology, drug concentration in the bloodstream follows exponential decay between doses. The general exponential model is $A(t) = A_0 e^{kt}$, where $A_0$ is the initial amount at $t=0$, $k$ is the growth rate constant, and $t$ is time. When $k > 0$, the quantity grows. When $k < 0$, it decays. The constant $k$ is not a percentage rate — it is the instantaneous relative rate of change, meaning $k = A'(t)/A(t)$ at every instant. To find $k$ from data: you need two data points $(t_1, A_1)$ and $(t_2, A_2)$. Write $A_1 = A_0 e^{kt_1}$ and $A_2 = A_0 e^{kt_2}$. Divide the equations to eliminate $A_0$: $A_2/A_1 = e^{k(t_2-t_1)}$. Take $\ln$ of both sides and solve for $k$. This is the standard procedure — eliminate the unknown you do not want first. Half-life ($T_{1/2}$) is the time for a decaying quantity to halve: $\frac{1}{2} = e^{kT_{1/2}} \Rightarrow T_{1/2} = -\ln 2 / k$ (negative because $k < 0$ for decay). Doubling time ($T_d$) is analogous for growth: $2 = e^{kT_d} \Rightarrow T_d = \ln 2 / k$. Both depend only on $k$, not on $A_0$ — the starting amount does not change how fast the process happens. Radioactive decay is the canonical decay model. Carbon-14 has a half-life of 5730 years. From $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / |k|$, we get $k = -\ln 2 / 5730 \approx -0.0001209$ per year. A sample that is $A_0$ grams at $t=0$ will be $A_0 e^{-0.0001209 t}$ grams at time $t$. To find the age of a sample given its current amount, solve for $t$ using $\ln$. Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of temperature change of an object is proportional to the difference between its temperature and the ambient temperature. The solution is $T(t) = T_e + (T_0 - T_e)e^{kt}$ where $k < 0$. Note that the exponential part starts at $(T_0 - T_e)$ and decays to zero, so $T(t)$ starts at $T_0$ and approaches $T_e$ — the object equilibrates with its environment. For logistic growth $P(t) = L/(1 + Ce^{-kt})$: the constant $C = (L - P_0)/P_0$ where $P_0$ is the initial population. At $t=0$, $P = L/(1+C) = P_0$ (verify this). The inflection point — where growth is fastest — occurs at $P = L/2$, exactly half the carrying capacity. This is when the S-curve transitions from accelerating to decelerating growth. The universal exponential model A(t) = A_0\,e^{kt} \\ A_0 = \text{initial amount} \quad k > 0: \text{growth} \quad k < 0: \text{decay} \\ T_{\text{double}} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \quad T_{1/2} = \frac{-\ln 2}{k} = \frac{\ln 2}{|k|} Finding k from two data points 1.\; \text{Write: } A_1 = A_0 e^{kt_1} \text{ and } A_2 = A_0 e^{kt_2} \\ 2.\; \text{Divide: } \frac{A_2}{A_1} = e^{k(t_2-t_1)} \\ 3.\; \text{Take ln: } k = \frac{\ln(A_2/A_1)}{t_2 - t_1} \\ 4.\; \text{Back-substitute to find } A_0 \text{ if needed} Newton's Law of Cooling — same structure T(t) = T_e + (T_0 - T_e)e^{kt} \quad k < 0 \\ T_e = \text{ambient temperature (environment)} \\ T_0 = \text{initial temperature of object} \\ \text{As } t \to \infty: T \to T_e \text{ — approaches room temperature} Logistic growth — when resources are limited P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + Ce^{-kt}} \quad L = \text{carrying capacity} \\ \text{Early: behaves like exponential} \\ \text{Later: slows as } P \to L \text{ (S-shaped curve)} \\ \text{Used for: population, disease spread, product adoption} Radioactive decay — carbon dating formula A(t) = A_0 e^{kt} \quad k = \frac{-\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \\ \text{C-14: } T_{1/2} = 5730 \text{ yr} \Rightarrow k \approx -0.0001209 \text{ yr}^{-1} \\ \text{Age: } t = \frac{\ln(A/A_0)}{k} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln 2}\ln\frac{A_0}{A} Logistic model P(t) = \frac{L}{1 + Ce^{-kt}} \quad C = \frac{L-P_0}{P_0} \\ \text{Inflection at } P = L/2 \text{, time } t^* = \frac{\ln C}{k} \\ \text{Range: } (0, L) \text{ — never reaches the carrying capacity} Rule of 70 — quick mental estimate T_{1/2} \approx \frac{70}{|k|\%} \quad T_d \approx \frac{70}{k\%} \\ \text{(Rule of 72 uses 72; 70 works better for continuous models)} \\ \text{Example: 7\% continuous growth} \Rightarrow \text{doubles in } \approx 10 \text{ years} Growth and Decay Models — Adjust k and See The same curve, two stories: positive $k$ means explosive growth, negative $k$ means relentless decay. Population growth — finding k and predicting \text{A city had population 250,000 in 2000 and 310,000 in 2010. Assuming exponential growth, predict the 2025 population.} Always set a clear $t=0$ reference. Keep $k$ in exact form ($\ln(310/250)/10$) until the final calculation to avoid rounding errors. k = \frac{\ln(310000/250000)}{2010-2000} = \frac{\ln(1.24)}{10} \approx \frac{0.2151}{10} \approx 0.02151 \text{ yr}^{-1} Find $k$ from the two data points. A(t) = 250000\,e^{0.02151 t} \quad (t = \text{years after 2000}) Write the model. Use $A_0 = 250000$ (year 2000 as reference). A(25) = 250000\,e^{0.02151 \times 25} = 250000\,e^{0.5378} \approx 250000(1.712) \approx 428{,}000 $t=25$ for year 2025. Radioactive decay — carbon dating \text{A fossil contains 35\% of its original C-14. Estimate its age.} Carbon dating is only reliable for samples up to ~50,000 years (about 9 half-lives). Beyond that, too little C-14 remains to measure accurately. t = \frac{5730}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln\frac{A_0}{A} = \frac{5730}{0.6931} \cdot \ln\frac{1}{0.35} Apply the carbon dating formula. $A/A_0 = 0.35$, so $A_0/A = 1/0.35$. = 8267 \cdot \ln(2.857) = 8267 \cdot 1.0498 \approx 8679 \text{ years} Calculate. The fossil is approximately 8,700 years old. Newton's Law of Cooling \text{Coffee at 90°C is placed in a 20°C room. After 10 min it is 70°C. When will it reach 40°C?} The coffee cools to 40°C in about 37 minutes. Note that it approaches 20°C (room temperature) asymptotically — it theoretically never reaches exactly 20°C. T(t) = 20 + 70e^{kt} \quad [T_0=90, T_e=20, \text{ so } T_0-T_e=70] Set up the model. 70 = 20 + 70e^{10k} \Rightarrow 50 = 70e^{10k} \Rightarrow k = \frac{\ln(5/7)}{10} \approx -0.03365 Use the data point $(10, 70)$ to find $k$. 40 = 20 + 70e^{kt} \Rightarrow 20 = 70e^{kt} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln(2/7)}{k} = \frac{\ln(2/7)}{\ln(5/7)/10} \approx \frac{-1.2528}{-0.03365} \approx 37.2 \text{ min} Solve for $t$ when $T=40$. \text{A drug has a half-life of 4 hours in the bloodstream. A patient takes a 200 mg dose. How long until less than 10 mg remains?} Find $k$ from the half-life, then solve $10 = 200e^{kt}$ for $t$. t \approx 17.3 \text{ hours (just over 4 half-lives)} k = -\ln 2 / 4 \approx -0.1733 \text{ hr}^{-1} Find the decay constant from the half-life. 10 = 200e^{-0.1733t} \Rightarrow 0.05 = e^{-0.1733t} \Rightarrow t = \frac{\ln 0.05}{-0.1733} \approx \frac{-2.996}{-0.1733} \approx 17.3 \text{ hr} Solve for $t$. \text{A lake can support at most 10,000 fish (carrying capacity). Currently there are 2,000 fish and the population is growing at } k = 0.3 \text{ per year. Write the logistic model and find the population after 5 years.} Find $C = (L - P_0)/P_0$ first, then apply the logistic formula. P(t) = \dfrac{10000}{1+4e^{-0.3t}}; \quad P(5) \approx 5284 C = \frac{10000 - 2000}{2000} = 4 Compute $C$ from the initial condition. P(t) = \frac{10000}{1 + 4e^{-0.3t}} Logistic model. P(5) = \frac{10000}{1 + 4e^{-1.5}} = \frac{10000}{1 + 4(0.2231)} = \frac{10000}{1.8924} \approx 5284 \text{ fish} After 5 years — more than doubled but well below carrying capacity. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step growth decay e^x logarithm ln

documents.title: Polar Coordinates: Distance and Direction Instead of Right and Up
documents.subtitle: Cartesian asks "how far right and up?" — polar asks "how far, and which direction?" Same point, two languages. Choosing the right one makes the math trivial or horrible.
documents.tags: polar coordinates polar form converting polar rectangular polar equations graphing polar r theta cardioid rose curve limaçon
documents.aliases: r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Form 1: Point from polar to Cartesian \text{Convert } (4, 135°) \text{ to Cartesian.}    x = 4\cos135° = 4\cdot(-\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) = -2\sqrt{2} \approx -2.83 $\cos135° = -\sqrt{2}/2$ (QII, reference angle 45°). y = 4\sin135° = 4\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 $\sin135° = \sqrt{2}/2$ (positive in QII). Form 2: Point from Cartesian to polar \text{Convert } (-3, -3) \text{ to polar form.}    r = \sqrt{9+9} = 3\sqrt{2} Always positive. \arctan(-3/-3) = \arctan(1) = 45° But the point $(-3,-3)$ is in QIII — arctan gives 45° which is in QI. Wrong quadrant. \theta = 45° + 180° = 225° Add 180° to shift into QIII. Always draw the point and check. Form 3: Polar equation to Cartesian \text{Convert } r = 4\sin\theta \text{ to Cartesian.}    r \cdot r = 4\sin\theta \cdot r \quad \Rightarrow \quad r^2 = 4r\sin\theta Multiply by $r$ to create $r^2$ and $r\sin\theta$. x^2+y^2 = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2+(y-2)^2 = 4 Substitute, then complete the square on $y$. Circle centred at $(0,2)$, radius 2. Form 4: Cartesian equation to polar \text{Convert } y = 3x + 2 \text{ to polar form.}    r\sin\theta = 3(r\cos\theta) + 2 Replace $y \to r\sin\theta$, $x \to r\cos\theta$. r\sin\theta - 3r\cos\theta = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r(\sin\theta - 3\cos\theta) = 2 Factor out $r$. r = \frac{2}{\sin\theta - 3\cos\theta} Solve for $r$. This is the polar form of a straight line not through the origin.  \text{Prove that } r = \sin\theta + \cos\theta \text{ is a circle. Find its centre and radius.} Multiply by $r$, substitute, then complete the square on both $x$ and $y$. \text{Circle, centre }(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2}), \text{ radius }\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}  r^2 = r\sin\theta + r\cos\theta \Rightarrow x^2+y^2 = y+x Multiply by $r$, substitute. (x-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 + (y-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} Complete the square on both variables.
documents.applications: Polar coordinates are native to anything that rotates or radiates outward: radar, sonar, radio antennas, robotic arms, turbines, circular saws. The equation of a circle centred at the origin is $x^2+y^2=r^2$ in Cartesian — complicated. In polar it is simply $r=5$ — trivial. The system whose equations are simplest is the one you should use. Choosing well is an engineering skill, not just a mathematical one. The right triangle IS the conversion — nothing to memorise \text{Draw the point. Drop a perpendicular to the } x\text{-axis.} \\ \text{Hypotenuse} = r \quad \text{Horizontal leg} = x = r\cos\theta \\ \text{Vertical leg} = y = r\sin\theta \quad \text{Angle at origin} = \theta \\ \text{These are the definitions of sin and cos in a right triangle.} Conversion formulas — read from the triangle x = r\cos\theta \qquad y = r\sin\theta \\ r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2} \qquad \theta = \arctan(y/x) \;(\text{+adjust for quadrant}) \\ r^2 = x^2+y^2 \quad \leftarrow \text{ most useful for equation conversion} Quadrant trap: arctan only covers two quadrants \arctan(y/x) \text{ returns values in } (-90°, 90°) \text{ only.} \\ \text{For QII: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 180° \\ \text{For QIII: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 180° \\ \text{For QIV: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 360° \text{ (if you want a positive angle)} \\ \text{Rule: always draw the point and check which quadrant it is in.} Non-uniqueness — one point, many addresses (r, \theta) = (r, \theta + 2\pi k) \quad k \in \mathbb{Z} \\ (r, \theta) = (-r, \theta + \pi) \quad \text{(negative }r\text{: go backwards)} \\ \text{The origin:} \; r = 0 \text{ for any } \theta.
documents.content: A radar operator sees a plane at distance 120 km, bearing 045°. Nobody converts that to $(x, y)$ before plotting — the distance-and-angle description IS the natural language. Why does the choice of coordinate system matter so much? Polar coordinates are native to anything that rotates or radiates outward: radar, sonar, radio antennas, robotic arms, turbines, circular saws. The equation of a circle centred at the origin is $x^2+y^2=r^2$ in Cartesian — complicated. In polar it is simply $r=5$ — trivial. The system whose equations are simplest is the one you should use. Choosing well is an engineering skill, not just a mathematical one. Every point in the plane has two addresses. The Cartesian address says: walk $x$ units right, then $y$ units up. The polar address says: face angle $\theta$ from the positive $x$-axis, then walk $r$ units in that direction. Both addresses are complete — they locate the same point. The triangle connecting these two descriptions is an ordinary right triangle, and the conversion formulas are just the sides of that triangle. The conversion is not a formula to memorise — it is a right triangle to draw. Drop a perpendicular from your point to the $x$-axis. The hypotenuse is $r$ (the polar distance). The horizontal leg is $x$ (= $r\cos\theta$ by definition of cosine). The vertical leg is $y$ (= $r\sin\theta$ by definition of sine). Going the other way: $r$ is the hypotenuse of that triangle ($r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ by Pythagoras), and $\theta$ is the angle at the origin ($\tan\theta = y/x$). A polar address is not unique, which surprises most students. Adding $360°$ to the angle returns to the same point. A negative $r$ means "walk in the opposite direction from $\theta$." So $(3, 60°)$, $(3, 420°)$, and $(-3, 240°)$ all describe the same point. This non-uniqueness is a feature — it gives flexibility when writing polar equations. Converting between Cartesian and polar equations uses four substitutions. The trick is recognising which substitution to use at each step — and knowing when to multiply by $r$ to create the right pattern. The right triangle IS the conversion — nothing to memorise \text{Draw the point. Drop a perpendicular to the } x\text{-axis.} \\ \text{Hypotenuse} = r \quad \text{Horizontal leg} = x = r\cos\theta \\ \text{Vertical leg} = y = r\sin\theta \quad \text{Angle at origin} = \theta \\ \text{These are the definitions of sin and cos in a right triangle.} Conversion formulas — read from the triangle x = r\cos\theta \qquad y = r\sin\theta \\ r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2} \qquad \theta = \arctan(y/x) \;(\text{+adjust for quadrant}) \\ r^2 = x^2+y^2 \quad \leftarrow \text{ most useful for equation conversion} Quadrant trap: arctan only covers two quadrants \arctan(y/x) \text{ returns values in } (-90°, 90°) \text{ only.} \\ \text{For QII: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 180° \\ \text{For QIII: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 180° \\ \text{For QIV: } \theta = \arctan(y/x) + 360° \text{ (if you want a positive angle)} \\ \text{Rule: always draw the point and check which quadrant it is in.} Non-uniqueness — one point, many addresses (r, \theta) = (r, \theta + 2\pi k) \quad k \in \mathbb{Z} \\ (r, \theta) = (-r, \theta + \pi) \quad \text{(negative }r\text{: go backwards)} \\ \text{The origin:} \; r = 0 \text{ for any } \theta. Common polar curves and why they have those shapes r = a: \text{ every point distance }a\text{ from origin} \Rightarrow \text{circle} \\ r = a\cos\theta: \text{ circle through origin (just proved)} \\ r = a(1+\cos\theta): \text{ cardioid — heart shape, }r=0\text{ at }\theta=180° \\ r = a\cos(n\theta): \text{ rose — }n\text{ petals (odd)}\text{ or }2n\text{ petals (even)} When to use polar vs Cartesian \text{Use polar when the problem involves:} \\ \bullet \text{ circles centred at the origin} \\ \bullet \text{ distance from the origin (radars, waves, lenses)} \\ \bullet \text{ angular symmetry (roses, spirals, cardioids)} \\ \text{Use Cartesian for lines, parabolas, anything with a clear x/y structure.} Drag the Point — The Triangle Appears The triangle is not an illustration — it IS the conversion formula. There is nothing else going on. Switch to Polar Curves Mode — See Many Equations Each curve shape is a consequence of the equation, not a coincidence. Trace slowly to see why. Form 1: Point from polar to Cartesian \text{Convert } (4, 135°) \text{ to Cartesian.} x = 4\cos135° = 4\cdot(-\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}) = -2\sqrt{2} \approx -2.83 $\cos135° = -\sqrt{2}/2$ (QII, reference angle 45°). y = 4\sin135° = 4\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 $\sin135° = \sqrt{2}/2$ (positive in QII). Form 2: Point from Cartesian to polar \text{Convert } (-3, -3) \text{ to polar form.} r = \sqrt{9+9} = 3\sqrt{2} Always positive. \arctan(-3/-3) = \arctan(1) = 45° But the point $(-3,-3)$ is in QIII — arctan gives 45° which is in QI. Wrong quadrant. \theta = 45° + 180° = 225° Add 180° to shift into QIII. Always draw the point and check. Form 3: Polar equation to Cartesian \text{Convert } r = 4\sin\theta \text{ to Cartesian.} r \cdot r = 4\sin\theta \cdot r \quad \Rightarrow \quad r^2 = 4r\sin\theta Multiply by $r$ to create $r^2$ and $r\sin\theta$. x^2+y^2 = 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2+(y-2)^2 = 4 Substitute, then complete the square on $y$. Circle centred at $(0,2)$, radius 2. Form 4: Cartesian equation to polar \text{Convert } y = 3x + 2 \text{ to polar form.} r\sin\theta = 3(r\cos\theta) + 2 Replace $y \to r\sin\theta$, $x \to r\cos\theta$. r\sin\theta - 3r\cos\theta = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r(\sin\theta - 3\cos\theta) = 2 Factor out $r$. r = \frac{2}{\sin\theta - 3\cos\theta} Solve for $r$. This is the polar form of a straight line not through the origin. \text{Prove that } r = \sin\theta + \cos\theta \text{ is a circle. Find its centre and radius.} Multiply by $r$, substitute, then complete the square on both $x$ and $y$. \text{Circle, centre }(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2}), \text{ radius }\tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} r^2 = r\sin\theta + r\cos\theta \Rightarrow x^2+y^2 = y+x Multiply by $r$, substitute. (x-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 + (y-\tfrac{1}{2})^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} Complete the square on both variables. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Complex Numbers in Polar Form and De Moivre's Theorem
documents.subtitle: Multiplying complex numbers rotates and scales — once you see that, De Moivre's theorem stops being a miracle and becomes obvious
documents.tags: complex polar form modulus argument De Moivre theorem nth roots complex multiplication rotation Euler formula
documents.aliases: r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Form 1: Converting to polar form \text{Write } z = -2 + 2i \text{ in polar form.}    r = \sqrt{4+4} = 2\sqrt{2} Modulus. \arctan(2/-2) = \arctan(-1) = -45°. \text{ But } z \text{ is in QII, so } \theta = -45°+180° = 135°. Argument — adjust for quadrant. z = 2\sqrt{2}\,(\cos135°+i\sin135°) Polar form. Form 2: Multiplying in polar form \text{Compute } (1+i)(\sqrt{3}-i) \text{ using polar form.}    1+i = \sqrt{2}\,e^{i\pi/4} \qquad \sqrt{3}-i = 2\,e^{-i\pi/6} Convert each to polar. \text{Product: modulus } \sqrt{2}\cdot2=2\sqrt{2}, \text{ angle } \pi/4-\pi/6=\pi/12. Multiply moduli, add angles. 2\sqrt{2}\,(\cos15°+i\sin15°) Answer in polar form. Form 3: Large power using De Moivre \text{Compute } (1+i)^{10}.    1+i = \sqrt{2}\,e^{i\pi/4} Polar form. (1+i)^{10} = (\sqrt{2})^{10}\,e^{i10\pi/4} = 32\,e^{i5\pi/2} De Moivre's. 5\pi/2 = 2\pi + \pi/2 \Rightarrow e^{i5\pi/2} = e^{i\pi/2} = i Reduce modulo $2\pi$. (1+i)^{10} = 32i Clean exact answer. Try expanding by hand to appreciate why polar is faster. Form 4: Finding all nth roots \text{Find all cube roots of } -8.    -8 = 8e^{i\pi} \quad (r=8, \theta=\pi) $-8$ is on the negative real axis, modulus 8. \rho = 8^{1/3} = 2 \quad \phi_k = \frac{\pi+2\pi k}{3}, \; k=0,1,2 Root modulus and angles. w_0 = 2e^{i\pi/3} = 1+\sqrt{3}i \quad w_1 = 2e^{i\pi} = -2 \quad w_2 = 2e^{i5\pi/3} = 1-\sqrt{3}i Convert each to rectangular. The real cube root $-2$ is $w_1$.  \text{Use De Moivre's to derive } \cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta. Expand $(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)^3$ with the binomial theorem and equate real parts. \cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta  (c+is)^3 = c^3+3c^2(is)+3c(is)^2+(is)^3 = c^3+3ic^2s-3cs^2-is^3 Expand. \text{Real part: } c^3-3cs^2 = \cos^3\theta-3\cos\theta\sin^2\theta = \cos^3\theta-3\cos\theta(1-\cos^2\theta) Use $\sin^2=1-\cos^2$. = 4\cos^3\theta-3\cos\theta = \cos 3\theta \qquad \blacksquare By De Moivre's, real part of $(c+is)^3$ is $\cos3\theta$.
documents.applications: AC circuit analysis, signal processing, and control systems all use complex polar form because multiplying two complex exponentials is trivial: magnitudes multiply, angles add. The Fourier transform — which underlies MP3, JPEG, MRI, and radar — is built entirely on this. Every time your phone plays music, it is performing millions of complex polar multiplications per second. Why i² = −1 is geometrically obvious i = (1, 90°) \text{ — unit length, pointing up.} \\ i \times i: \text{ lengths } 1\times1=1, \text{ angles } 90°+90°=180°. \\ (1, 180°) = -1 \quad \text{(180° is the negative real axis).} \\ \text{Two quarter-turns is a half-turn. A half-turn reverses direction. That IS } -1. Polar form and the multiplication rule z = a+bi = r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta) = re^{i\theta} \\ r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad \theta = \arctan(b/a) \text{ (adjust for quadrant)} \\ z_1 z_2 = r_1 r_2\,e^{i(\theta_1+\theta_2)} \\ |z_1 z_2| = |z_1||z_2| \quad \arg(z_1 z_2) = \arg z_1 + \arg z_2 De Moivre's theorem as repeated rotation z^n: \text{ do } n \text{ rotations of } \theta, \text{ each scaling by } r. \\ \text{Total angle: } n\theta. \quad \text{Total scale: } r^n. \\ [r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)]^n = r^n(\cos n\theta+i\sin n\theta) \\ \text{Not a theorem requiring proof — a direct consequence of the rotation interpretation.}
documents.content: Why does $(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n = \cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta)$? On its face this looks like a lucky coincidence. But if complex multiplication is rotation, then doing $n$ rotations of $\theta$ each is just one rotation of $n\theta$. The miracle evaporates completely. AC circuit analysis, signal processing, and control systems all use complex polar form because multiplying two complex exponentials is trivial: magnitudes multiply, angles add. The Fourier transform — which underlies MP3, JPEG, MRI, and radar — is built entirely on this. Every time your phone plays music, it is performing millions of complex polar multiplications per second. A complex number $a + bi$ is a point in a plane where the horizontal axis holds real numbers and the vertical axis holds imaginary numbers. Every complex number has a distance from the origin ($r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, called the modulus) and an angle from the positive real axis ($\theta = \arg(z)$, called the argument). These two numbers completely describe the complex number — this is polar form. Multiplying two complex numbers does something beautiful geometrically: the moduli multiply and the arguments add. So $z_1 \times z_2$ has distance $|z_1| \cdot |z_2|$ from the origin and angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$. In plain English: multiplication scales by the modulus and rotates by the argument. This rotation interpretation makes $i^2 = -1$ completely natural. In polar form, $i = (1, 90°)$ — unit length, pointing straight up. Multiplying $i \times i$ gives modulus $1 \times 1 = 1$ and angle $90° + 90° = 180°$. A complex number at angle $180°$ with length 1 is exactly $-1$. No axioms, no definition — just geometry. The nth roots formula is derived from asking: "what polar numbers $w$ satisfy $w^n = z$?" Every symbol in the result has a geometric meaning. Why i² = −1 is geometrically obvious i = (1, 90°) \text{ — unit length, pointing up.} \\ i \times i: \text{ lengths } 1\times1=1, \text{ angles } 90°+90°=180°. \\ (1, 180°) = -1 \quad \text{(180° is the negative real axis).} \\ \text{Two quarter-turns is a half-turn. A half-turn reverses direction. That IS } -1. Polar form and the multiplication rule z = a+bi = r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta) = re^{i\theta} \\ r = |z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad \theta = \arctan(b/a) \text{ (adjust for quadrant)} \\ z_1 z_2 = r_1 r_2\,e^{i(\theta_1+\theta_2)} \\ |z_1 z_2| = |z_1||z_2| \quad \arg(z_1 z_2) = \arg z_1 + \arg z_2 De Moivre's theorem as repeated rotation z^n: \text{ do } n \text{ rotations of } \theta, \text{ each scaling by } r. \\ \text{Total angle: } n\theta. \quad \text{Total scale: } r^n. \\ [r(\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)]^n = r^n(\cos n\theta+i\sin n\theta) \\ \text{Not a theorem requiring proof — a direct consequence of the rotation interpretation.} nth roots — equally spaced on a circle w_k = r^{1/n}\!\left(\cos\frac{\theta+2\pi k}{n} + i\sin\frac{\theta+2\pi k}{n}\right), \; k=0,\ldots,n-1 \\ \text{Count: exactly } n \text{ roots. Spacing: } 2\pi/n. \text{ Radius: } r^{1/n}. Using De Moivre's to derive trig identities (\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)^2 = \cos2\theta + i\sin2\theta \quad \text{(De Moivre's)} \\ \text{Also} = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta + 2i\sin\theta\cos\theta \quad \text{(expand)} \\ \text{Equate real/imaginary: } \cos2\theta = \cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta, \; \sin2\theta = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta Drag z₁ and z₂ — Watch z₁z₂ Rotate and Scale Multiplication is rotation + scaling. That is all it ever is. Switch to nth Roots — Polygon Appears The roots of unity are the vertices of a regular $n$-gon. One of the most surprising and beautiful results in all of mathematics. Argument — adjust for quadrant. z = 2\sqrt{2}\,(\cos135°+i\sin135°) Polar form. Form 2: Multiplying in polar form \text{Compute } (1+i)(\sqrt{3}-i) \text{ using polar form.} 1+i = \sqrt{2}\,e^{i\pi/4} \qquad \sqrt{3}-i = 2\,e^{-i\pi/6} Convert each to polar. \text{Product: modulus } \sqrt{2}\cdot2=2\sqrt{2}, \text{ angle } \pi/4-\pi/6=\pi/12. (1+i)^{10} = (\sqrt{2})^{10}\,e^{i10\pi/4} = 32\,e^{i5\pi/2} De Moivre's. 5\pi/2 = 2\pi + \pi/2 \Rightarrow e^{i5\pi/2} = e^{i\pi/2} = i Reduce modulo $2\pi$. (1+i)^{10} = 32i Clean exact answer. Try expanding by hand to appreciate why polar is faster. Form 4: Finding all nth roots \text{Find all cube roots of } -8. -8 = 8e^{i\pi} \quad (r=8, \theta=\pi) $-8$ is on the negative real axis, modulus 8. \rho = 8^{1/3} = 2 \quad \phi_k = \frac{\pi+2\pi k}{3}, \; k=0,1,2 Root modulus and angles. w_0 = 2e^{i\pi/3} = 1+\sqrt{3}i \quad w_1 = 2e^{i\pi} = -2 \quad w_2 = 2e^{i5\pi/3} = 1-\sqrt{3}i Convert each to rectangular. The real cube root $-2$ is $w_1$. \text{Use De Moivre's to derive } \cos 3\theta = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta. documents.title: Vectors: Quantities That Have Both Size and Direction
documents.subtitle: A vector is an arrow. Its length is magnitude, its direction is heading. Components are just that arrow described with coordinates — one description, two representations.
documents.tags: vectors 2D vectors vector addition scalar multiplication unit vector magnitude direction angle component form resultant i j notation
documents.aliases: r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Form 1: Finding a vector from two points \text{Find } \vec{AB} \text{ where } A=(2,-1), B=(-3,4). \text{ Find magnitude and unit vector.}    \vec{AB} = \langle -3-2,\, 4-(-1)\rangle = \langle -5,5\rangle Terminal minus initial: $B - A$. Always terminal minus initial. |\vec{AB}| = \sqrt{25+25} = 5\sqrt{2} Pythagorean theorem on components. \hat{u} = \frac{\langle-5,5\rangle}{5\sqrt{2}} = \langle-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\rangle Divide by magnitude. Verify: $(-1/\sqrt{2})^2+(1/\sqrt{2})^2 = 1$ ✓ Form 2: Resultant of two forces \text{Force 1: 500 N at 30°. Force 2: 300 N at 120°. Find the resultant.}    F_{1x}=500\cos30°=433.0,\; F_{1y}=500\sin30°=250.0 Decompose F1. F_{2x}=300\cos120°=-150.0,\; F_{2y}=300\sin120°=259.8 $\cos120°=-1/2$, $\sin120°=\sqrt{3}/2$. R_x=283.0,\; R_y=509.8 \quad |R|=\sqrt{283^2+509.8^2}\approx584\text{ N} Add components, find magnitude. \theta_R=\arctan(509.8/283.0)\approx61° Direction. Both components positive → QI, no adjustment needed. Form 3: Bearing problem (navigation) \text{Plane heads N60°E at 400 km/h. Wind blows due east at 80 km/h. Find actual velocity.}    v_{plane} = \langle 400\sin60°,\, 400\cos60°\rangle = \langle 346.4,\, 200\rangle N60°E: x-component uses sin (east), y-component uses cos (north). v_{wind} = \langle 80,\, 0\rangle Due east = positive x only. |v_{actual}| = |\langle 426.4,\, 200\rangle| \approx 472\text{ km/h at N25.1°E} Add components, reconstruct. Bearing angle: $\arctan(426.4/200) \approx 64.9°$ east of north = N64.9°E. Wait — that is wrong. $\arctan(\text{east}/\text{north}) = \arctan(426.4/200) \approx 64.9°$ east of north. Form 4: Scalar multiplication and unit vectors \text{Find a vector of length 7 in the direction of } \vec{v}=\langle 3,-4\rangle.    |\vec{v}|=\sqrt{9+16}=5 \quad \hat{v}=\langle 3/5, -4/5\rangle Find the unit vector in that direction. 7\hat{v} = 7\langle 3/5, -4/5\rangle = \langle 21/5, -28/5\rangle Scale the unit vector to length 7. Magnitude check: $\sqrt{(21/5)^2+(28/5)^2} = \sqrt{441+784}/5 = \sqrt{1225}/5 = 35/5 = 7$ ✓  \text{Three forces act on an object: } \vec{F_1}=\langle 2,5\rangle, \vec{F_2}=\langle -3,1\rangle, \vec{F_3}=\langle 1,-4\rangle. \text{ Find the force } \vec{F_4} \text{ needed for equilibrium (net force = 0).} Equilibrium means all forces sum to the zero vector. \vec{F_4} = \langle 0,-2\rangle  \vec{F_1}+\vec{F_2}+\vec{F_3} = \langle0,2\rangle Sum the three given forces. \vec{F_4} = -\langle0,2\rangle = \langle0,-2\rangle Equilibrant is the negative of the resultant.
documents.applications: Force, velocity, acceleration, electric and magnetic fields — all are vector quantities. In structural engineering, every joint in a truss has forces acting on it in multiple directions; they must balance as vectors. In robotics, a robotic arm is described by joint angles and link lengths — the tip position is computed by adding vectors. In game physics, every collision, every trajectory, every lighting calculation is a vector operation. Two representations of the same arrow \text{Magnitude-direction form: } (|\vec{v}|, \theta) \\ \text{Component form: } \vec{v} = \langle a, b \rangle = a\mathbf{i}+b\mathbf{j} \\ \text{Converting between them:} \\ a = |\vec{v}|\cos\theta \quad b = |\vec{v}|\sin\theta \quad (\text{same triangle as polar coordinates!}) \\ |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad \theta = \arctan(b/a) Vector operations in component form \vec{u}+\vec{v} = \langle u_1+v_1, u_2+v_2\rangle \quad \text{(add components)} \\ c\vec{v} = \langle cv_1, cv_2\rangle \quad \text{(scale each component)} \\ |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2} \quad \hat{v} = \vec{v}/|\vec{v}| \quad \text{(unit vector)} Vectors are not numbers — you cannot just add magnitudes |\vec{u}+\vec{v}| \neq |\vec{u}|+|\vec{v}| \text{ in general.} \\ \text{Example: } |\langle3,0\rangle+\langle0,4\rangle| = |\langle3,4\rangle| = 5 \neq 3+4=7. \\ \text{Adding magnitudes is only valid when both vectors point the same direction.}
documents.content: A plane flies northeast at 400 km/h while wind blows due east at 80 km/h. What is the plane's actual velocity? You cannot add 400 + 80 because the directions are different. Vectors are the tool for adding quantities that have both size and direction. Force, velocity, acceleration, electric and magnetic fields — all are vector quantities. In structural engineering, every joint in a truss has forces acting on it in multiple directions; they must balance as vectors. In robotics, a robotic arm is described by joint angles and link lengths — the tip position is computed by adding vectors. In game physics, every collision, every trajectory, every lighting calculation is a vector operation. A vector is an arrow. It has a tail (where it starts) and a tip (where it ends). Its magnitude is the length of the arrow. Its direction is which way the arrow points. Two vectors are equal if they have the same length and the same direction — position in the plane does not matter. You can slide the arrow anywhere and it is still the same vector. Adding two vectors follows the tip-to-tail rule: place the tail of the second vector at the tip of the first. The result (the resultant) goes from the first tail to the second tip. Order does not matter — $\vec{u} + \vec{v} = \vec{v} + \vec{u}$ because the diagonal of the parallelogram is the same regardless of which side you traverse first. Component form $\langle a, b \rangle$ is just the arrow described by its horizontal and vertical shadows. The component $a$ is how far the arrow goes right, and $b$ is how far it goes up. Once you have components, vector addition becomes ordinary addition: $\langle a_1, b_1 \rangle + \langle a_2, b_2 \rangle = \langle a_1+a_2, b_1+b_2 \rangle$. Adding the shadows gives the shadow of the resultant. Every real physics or engineering vector problem uses the same process: decompose each vector into components, add the components, reconstruct. Here is every step annotated so no symbol floats free. Two representations of the same arrow \text{Magnitude-direction form: } (|\vec{v}|, \theta) \\ \text{Component form: } \vec{v} = \langle a, b \rangle = a\mathbf{i}+b\mathbf{j} \\ \text{Converting between them:} \\ a = |\vec{v}|\cos\theta \quad b = |\vec{v}|\sin\theta \quad (\text{same triangle as polar coordinates!}) \\ |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2} \quad \theta = \arctan(b/a) Vector operations in component form \vec{u}+\vec{v} = \langle u_1+v_1, u_2+v_2\rangle \quad \text{(add components)} \\ c\vec{v} = \langle cv_1, cv_2\rangle \quad \text{(scale each component)} \\ |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{v_1^2+v_2^2} \quad \hat{v} = \vec{v}/|\vec{v}| \quad \text{(unit vector)} Vectors are not numbers — you cannot just add magnitudes |\vec{u}+\vec{v}| \neq |\vec{u}|+|\vec{v}| \text{ in general.} \\ \text{Example: } |\langle3,0\rangle+\langle0,4\rangle| = |\langle3,4\rangle| = 5 \neq 3+4=7. \\ \text{Adding magnitudes is only valid when both vectors point the same direction.} Resultant of multiple forces — the algorithm 1.\; F_x = |F|\cos\theta, \; F_y = |F|\sin\theta \text{ for each force} \\ 2.\; R_x = \sum F_x, \; R_y = \sum F_y \\ 3.\; |R| = \sqrt{R_x^2+R_y^2}, \; \theta_R = \arctan(R_y/R_x) \text{ (adjust quadrant)} Bearing vs standard angle — they measure differently \text{Standard: CCW from positive }x\text{-axis (east).} \\ \text{Bearing: CW from north (positive }y\text{-axis).} \\ \text{N60°E means: start north, rotate 60° toward east.} \\ \text{Standard equivalent: } 90°-60° = 30°. \\ \text{Always draw a sketch before decomposing.} Drag Two Vectors — Tip-to-Tail Addition The resultant is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors. Scalar Multiplication — Scale the Arrow Multiplying by 2 doubles the arrow. Multiplying by −1 reverses it. Multiplying by 0 collapses it to a point. Form 1: Finding a vector from two points \text{Find } \vec{AB} \text{ where } A=(2,-1), B=(-3,4). \text{ Find magnitude and unit vector.} \vec{AB} = \langle -3-2,\, 4-(-1)\rangle = \langle -5,5\rangle Terminal minus initial: $B - A$. Always terminal minus initial. |\vec{AB}| = \sqrt{25+25} = 5\sqrt{2} Pythagorean theorem on components. \hat{u} = \frac{\langle-5,5\rangle}{5\sqrt{2}} = \langle-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\rangle Divide by magnitude. Verify: $(-1/\sqrt{2})^2+(1/\sqrt{2})^2 = 1$ ✓ Form 2: Resultant of two forces \text{Force 1: 500 N at 30°. Force 2: 300 N at 120°. Find the resultant.} F_{1x}=500\cos30°=433.0,\; F_{1y}=500\sin30°=250.0 Decompose F1. F_{2x}=300\cos120°=-150.0,\; F_{2y}=300\sin120°=259.8 $\cos120°=-1/2$, $\sin120°=\sqrt{3}/2$. R_x=283.0,\; R_y=509.8 \quad |R|=\sqrt{283^2+509.8^2}\approx584\text{ N} Add components, find magnitude. \theta_R=\arctan(509.8/283.0)\approx61° Direction. Both components positive → QI, no adjustment needed. Form 3: Bearing problem (navigation) \text{Plane heads N60°E at 400 km/h. Wind blows due east at 80 km/h. Find actual velocity.} v_{plane} = \langle 400\sin60°,\, 400\cos60°\rangle = \langle 346.4,\, 200\rangle N60°E: x-component uses sin (east), y-component uses cos (north). v_{wind} = \langle 80,\, 0\rangle Due east = positive x only. |v_{actual}| = |\langle 426.4,\, 200\rangle| \approx 472\text{ km/h at N25.1°E} Add components, reconstruct. Bearing angle: $\arctan(426.4/200) \approx 64.9°$ east of north = N64.9°E. Wait — that is wrong. $\arctan(\text{east}/\text{north}) = \arctan(426.4/200) \approx 64.9°$ east of north. Form 4: Scalar multiplication and unit vectors \text{Find a vector of length 7 in the direction of } \vec{v}=\langle 3,-4\rangle. |\vec{v}|=\sqrt{9+16}=5 \quad \hat{v}=\langle 3/5, -4/5\rangle Find the unit vector in that direction. 7\hat{v} = 7\langle 3/5, -4/5\rangle = \langle 21/5, -28/5\rangle Scale the unit vector to length 7. Magnitude check: $\sqrt{(21/5)^2+(28/5)^2} = \sqrt{441+784}/5 = \sqrt{1225}/5 = 35/5 = 7$ ✓ \text{Three forces act on an object: } \vec{F_1}=\langle 2,5\rangle, \vec{F_2}=\langle -3,1\rangle, \vec{F_3}=\langle 1,-4\rangle. \text{ Find the force } \vec{F_4} \text{ needed for equilibrium (net force = 0).} Equilibrium means all forces sum to the zero vector. \vec{F_4} = \langle 0,-2\rangle \vec{F_1}+\vec{F_2}+\vec{F_3} = \langle0,2\rangle Sum the three given forces. \vec{F_4} = -\langle0,2\rangle = \langle0,-2\rangle Equilibrant is the negative of the resultant. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: The Dot Product: Measuring How Much Two Vectors Agree
documents.subtitle: The dot product answers one question: how much of $\vec{u}$ points in the direction of $\vec{v}$? The answer is a single number — and it encodes the angle, projection, and work all at once.
documents.tags: dot product scalar product angle between vectors orthogonal projection vector projection scalar projection work cosine similarity
documents.aliases: r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Form 1: Basic dot product and what its sign means \vec{u} = \langle 4, -1 \rangle, \; \vec{v} = \langle 2, 3 \rangle. \text{ Compute } \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} \text{ and describe the angle.}    \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = (4)(2)+(-1)(3) = 8-3 = 5 Multiply matching components, add. One step. \text{Dot product} = 5 > 0 \Rightarrow \theta < 90° \text{ (vectors lean toward each other)} Positive → acute angle. No need to compute θ to know this. Form 2: Finding the angle \text{Find the angle between } \vec{u}=\langle 3,-1\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 2,4\rangle.    \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = 6-4 = 2 \qquad |\vec{u}|=\sqrt{10} \qquad |\vec{v}|=2\sqrt{5} Three separate computations. Do them one at a time. \cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{10}\cdot2\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{2\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} Angle formula: divide dot product by product of magnitudes. \theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{50}}\right) \approx 81.9° arccos gives the angle. Range is always $[0°, 180°]$. Form 3: Testing for perpendicularity \text{Are } \vec{u}=\langle 3,-2\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 4,6\rangle \text{ perpendicular?}    \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = (3)(4)+(-2)(6) = 12-12 = 0 If the dot product is zero, the vectors are perpendicular. No arccos needed. \text{Yes, } \vec{u} \perp \vec{v}. Perpendicularity test: dot product = 0. The quickest check there is. Form 4: Vector projection onto a given direction \text{Find } \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} \text{ where } \vec{u}=\langle 4,3\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 2,1\rangle.    \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = 8+3=11 \qquad |\vec{v}|^2 = 4+1=5 Two preliminary numbers. Note: use $|\vec{v}|^2$, not $|\vec{v}|$ — avoids a square root. \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} = \frac{11}{5}\langle 2,1\rangle = \langle\tfrac{22}{5}, \tfrac{11}{5}\rangle Scale $\vec{v}$ by $\frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|^2}$. The result is a vector in the direction of $\vec{v}$. \vec{u}_{\perp} = \vec{u} - \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} = \langle 4-\tfrac{22}{5}, 3-\tfrac{11}{5}\rangle = \langle -\tfrac{2}{5}, \tfrac{4}{5}\rangle Perpendicular part: subtract the projection from the original. \text{Check: } \vec{u}_{\perp}\cdot\vec{v} = (-\tfrac{2}{5})(2)+(\tfrac{4}{5})(1) = 0 \checkmark Always verify: the perpendicular part should have zero dot product with $\vec{v}$. Form 5: Work done by a force \text{Force } \vec{F}=\langle 6,2\rangle \text{ N. Displacement } \vec{d}=\langle 4,0\rangle \text{ m. Find the work done.}    W = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{d} = (6)(4)+(2)(0) = 24 \text{ J} Work is a dot product. One computation. \text{The 2 N vertical component of force does zero work on horizontal motion.} The $(2)(0)$ term confirms it — zero displacement in the vertical direction means the vertical force component contributes nothing. Form 6: Finding an unknown component to satisfy a dot product condition \text{Find } k \text{ so that } \langle k, 3\rangle \perp \langle 2, k-1\rangle.    \langle k,3\rangle \cdot \langle 2,k-1\rangle = 0 \Rightarrow 2k + 3(k-1) = 0 Set up perpendicularity condition: dot product = 0. 2k + 3k - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow 5k = 3 \Rightarrow k = \tfrac{3}{5} Solve the linear equation.  \text{Find all vectors } \vec{v}=\langle x,y\rangle \text{ perpendicular to } \langle 3,-2\rangle \text{ with magnitude 5.} Two conditions: dot product = 0, magnitude = 5. Two equations, two unknowns. \vec{v} = \pm\left\langle\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}}, \dfrac{15}{\sqrt{13}}\right\rangle  3x-2y=0 \Rightarrow y = \tfrac{3x}{2} Perpendicularity. x^2+\tfrac{9x^2}{4}=25 \Rightarrow \tfrac{13x^2}{4}=25 \Rightarrow x=\pm\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}} Magnitude condition. \vec{v} = \pm\langle\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}}, \tfrac{15}{\sqrt{13}}\rangle Two answers — one pointing each perpendicular direction.  \text{A sled is pulled by a rope at 25° above horizontal with 80 N force over 12 m. Find work done. What angle minimises rope tension for the same horizontal work?} Part 1: work = force dot displacement. Part 2: express horizontal work in terms of angle, find the angle that achieves the target work with minimum $|\vec{F}|$. W \approx 870 \text{ J}; minimum tension at } \theta = 0°  W = 80\cos25° \cdot 12 \approx 72.5 \cdot 12 = 870 \text{ J} Displacement is horizontal so $\vec{d} = \langle 12, 0\rangle$. |F| = \frac{W}{d\cos\theta} = \frac{870}{12\cos\theta} Express required force for target work 870 J. \text{Minimum when } \cos\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 0°. \text{ Pull horizontally.} Horizontal pull is most efficient — all force goes into work.
documents.applications: Work in physics is $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$ — only the component of force in the direction of motion counts. In machine learning, how similar are two documents? Cosine similarity is a dot product. In 3D graphics, how bright is a surface? Lambert's law is a dot product between the light direction and the surface normal. In navigation, how efficiently is your velocity taking you toward your destination? Dot product. One formula, everywhere. Before any formula: the shadow test \text{Point the two vectors from the same origin.} \\ \text{Drop a perpendicular from the tip of } \vec{u} \text{ onto } \vec{v}. \\ \text{The shadow is positive (same side as } \vec{v}\text{): dot product} > 0 \\ \text{No shadow (perpendicular): dot product} = 0 \\ \text{Shadow on opposite side: dot product} < 0 The dot product — two equivalent forms \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta \quad \text{(angle form — geometric meaning)} \\ \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = u_1 v_1 + u_2 v_2 \quad \text{(component form — easy to compute)} \\ \text{The proof that these are equal is Pillar 2 (rigor section).} The dot product is NOT a vector \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{(a number, not an arrow)} \\ \text{Adding a dot product to a vector is meaningless: } \vec{u} + (\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}) \text{ makes no sense.} \\ \text{This trips up students constantly — the result has no direction.}
documents.content: You push a lawnmower with 60 N of force at 30° below horizontal. How much of that force actually moves the mower forward? You cannot just use 60 N — direction matters. The dot product extracts exactly the part of one vector that acts along another. Work in physics is $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$ — only the component of force in the direction of motion counts. In machine learning, how similar are two documents? Cosine similarity is a dot product. In 3D graphics, how bright is a surface? Lambert's law is a dot product between the light direction and the surface normal. In navigation, how efficiently is your velocity taking you toward your destination? Dot product. One formula, everywhere. Imagine two arrows pinned at the same point. The dot product is a measure of how much they point in the same direction. If they point exactly the same way, the dot product is large and positive. If they are perpendicular, it is zero — they share no direction at all. If they point opposite ways, it is negative. There is a physical way to see this. Drop a perpendicular from the tip of $\vec{u}$ onto the line of $\vec{v}$. The length of the shadow that $\vec{u}$ casts on $\vec{v}$ is the scalar projection. The dot product is that shadow length times $|\vec{v}|$. When the shadow is long (vectors nearly parallel), the dot product is large. When the shadow is zero (vectors perpendicular), the dot product is zero. This shadow interpretation is not a metaphor — it is the definition. Formally: $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between them. The $\cos\theta$ captures exactly how much of $\vec{u}$'s length is acting along $\vec{v}$'s direction. The projection formula is where most students lose the thread. Here is every step annotated so no symbol is floating free. We want the vector projection of $\vec{u}$ onto $\vec{v}$: the arrow in the direction of $\vec{v}$ that has the same shadow as $\vec{u}$. Start from the shadow length (scalar projection), then multiply by the unit vector in the direction of $\vec{v}$ to get the arrow. Before any formula: the shadow test \text{Point the two vectors from the same origin.} \\ \text{Drop a perpendicular from the tip of } \vec{u} \text{ onto } \vec{v}. \\ \text{The shadow is positive (same side as } \vec{v}\text{): dot product} > 0 \\ \text{No shadow (perpendicular): dot product} = 0 \\ \text{Shadow on opposite side: dot product} < 0 The dot product — two equivalent forms \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = |\vec{u}||\vec{v}|\cos\theta \quad \text{(angle form — geometric meaning)} \\ \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = u_1 v_1 + u_2 v_2 \quad \text{(component form — easy to compute)} \\ \text{The proof that these are equal is Pillar 2 (rigor section).} The dot product is NOT a vector \vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{(a number, not an arrow)} \\ \text{Adding a dot product to a vector is meaningless: } \vec{u} + (\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}) \text{ makes no sense.} \\ \text{This trips up students constantly — the result has no direction.} Projection formulas — unpacked \text{Scalar projection (a number): } \text{comp}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} = \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|} \\ \text{Vector projection (an arrow): } \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} = \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|^2}\vec{v} \\ \text{Perpendicular part: } \vec{u}_{\perp} = \vec{u} - \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} proj$_{\vec{v}}\vec{u}$ is NOT the same as proj$_{\vec{u}}\vec{v}$ \text{Order matters. } \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} \text{ is the shadow of } \vec{u} \text{ on } \vec{v}. \\ \text{proj}_{\vec{u}}\vec{v} \text{ is the shadow of } \vec{v} \text{ on } \vec{u}. \\ \text{These have different lengths unless } |\vec{u}|=|\vec{v}|. Drag Two Vectors — Watch the Shadow When vectors are perpendicular, the shadow vanishes and the dot product becomes zero — no computation needed. Projection Mode — Shadow Made Explicit proj + perp = original vector. They always add back to u exactly. Form 1: Basic dot product and what its sign means \vec{u} = \langle 4, -1 \rangle, \; \vec{v} = \langle 2, 3 \rangle. \text{ Compute } \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} \text{ and describe the angle.} \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = (4)(2)+(-1)(3) = 8-3 = 5 Multiply matching components, add. One step. \text{Dot product} = 5 > 0 \Rightarrow \theta < 90° \text{ (vectors lean toward each other)} Positive → acute angle. No need to compute θ to know this. Form 2: Finding the angle \text{Find the angle between } \vec{u}=\langle 3,-1\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 2,4\rangle. \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = 6-4 = 2 \qquad |\vec{u}|=\sqrt{10} \qquad |\vec{v}|=2\sqrt{5} Three separate computations. Do them one at a time. \cos\theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{10}\cdot2\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{2\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} Angle formula: divide dot product by product of magnitudes. \theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{50}}\right) \approx 81.9° arccos gives the angle. Range is always $[0°, 180°]$. Form 3: Testing for perpendicularity \text{Are } \vec{u}=\langle 3,-2\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 4,6\rangle \text{ perpendicular?} \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = (3)(4)+(-2)(6) = 12-12 = 0 If the dot product is zero, the vectors are perpendicular. No arccos needed. \text{Yes, } \vec{u} \perp \vec{v}. Perpendicularity test: dot product = 0. The quickest check there is. Form 4: Vector projection onto a given direction \text{Find } \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} \text{ where } \vec{u}=\langle 4,3\rangle \text{ and } \vec{v}=\langle 2,1\rangle. \vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = 8+3=11 \qquad |\vec{v}|^2 = 4+1=5 Two preliminary numbers. Note: use $|\vec{v}|^2$, not $|\vec{v}|$ — avoids a square root. \text{proj}_{\vec{v}}\vec{u} = \frac{11}{5}\langle 2,1\rangle = \langle\tfrac{22}{5}, \tfrac{11}{5}\rangle Scale $\vec{v}$ by $\frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{|\vec{v}|^2}$. Form 5: Work done by a force \text{Force } \vec{F}=\langle 6,2\rangle \text{ N. Displacement } \vec{d}=\langle 4,0\rangle \text{ m. Find the work done.} W = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{d} = (6)(4)+(2)(0) = 24 \text{ J} Work is a dot product. One computation. \text{The 2 N vertical component of force does zero work on horizontal motion.} The $(2)(0)$ term confirms it — zero displacement in the vertical direction means the vertical force component contributes nothing. Form 6: Finding an unknown component to satisfy a dot product condition \text{Find } k \text{ so that } \langle k, 3\rangle \perp \langle 2, k-1\rangle. \langle k,3\rangle \cdot \langle 2,k-1\rangle = 0 \Rightarrow 2k + 3(k-1) = 0 Set up perpendicularity condition: dot product = 0. 2k + 3k - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow 5k = 3 \Rightarrow k = \tfrac{3}{5} Solve the linear equation. \text{Find all vectors } \vec{v}=\langle x,y\rangle \text{ perpendicular to } \langle 3,-2\rangle \text{ with magnitude 5.} Two conditions: dot product = 0, magnitude = 5. Two equations, two unknowns. \vec{v} = \pm\left\langle\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}}, \dfrac{15}{\sqrt{13}}\right\rangle 3x-2y=0 \Rightarrow y = \tfrac{3x}{2} Perpendicularity. x^2+\tfrac{9x^2}{4}=25 \Rightarrow \tfrac{13x^2}{4}=25 \Rightarrow x=\pm\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}} Magnitude condition. \vec{v} = \pm\langle\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{13}}, \tfrac{15}{\sqrt{13}}\rangle Two answers — one pointing each perpendicular direction. \text{A sled is pulled by a rope at 25° above horizontal with 80 N force over 12 m. Find work done. What angle minimises rope tension for the same horizontal work?} Part 1: work = force dot displacement. Part 2: express horizontal work in terms of angle, find the angle that achieves the target work with minimum $|\vec{F}|$. W \approx 870 \text{ J}; minimum tension at } \theta = 0° W = 80\cos25° \cdot 12 \approx 72.5 \cdot 12 = 870 \text{ J} Displacement is horizontal so $\vec{d} = \langle 12, 0\rangle$. |F| = \frac{W}{d\cos\theta} = \frac{870}{12\cos\theta} Express required force for target work 870 J. \text{Minimum when } \cos\theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 0°. \text{ Pull horizontally.} Horizontal pull is most efficient — all force goes into work. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step r(theta) polar coordinates rose curve cardioid vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
