Prove the triangle inequality: |a + b| \leq |a| + |b| for all a, b \in \mathbb{R}. Square both sides. Since both sides are non-negative, |a+b| ≤ |a|+|b| is equivalent to |a+b|² ≤ (|a|+|b|)². Use the fact that any number is ≤ its absolute value. Proved: |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| \text{Since both sides are} \geq 0:\;\; |a+b| \leq |a|+|b| \iff |a+b|^2 \leq (|a|+|b|)^2 Squaring is valid here because both sides are non-negative. |a+b|^2 = (a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 Expand left side. (|a|+|b|)^2 = a^2 + 2|a||b| + b^2 Expand right side (|a|² = a², |b|² = b²). \text{Need to show: } a^2 + 2ab + b^2 \leq a^2 + 2|a||b| + b^2 \iff 2ab \leq 2|a||b| \iff ab \leq |ab| Simplify (cancel a² + b² from both sides, divide by 2). \text{Key fact: for any real number } c, \;\; c \leq |c|. If c ≥ 0: c = |c|, so c ≤ |c|. If c < 0: c < 0 ≤ |c|, so c ≤ |c|. Both cases verified. \therefore\; ab \leq |ab| \;\checkmark \implies |a+b|^2 \leq (|a|+|b|)^2 \implies |a+b| \leq |a|+|b| \;\blacksquare Solve the compound inequality |x - 1| + |x + 1| = 4. The expression |x−1| + |x+1| represents the sum of distances from x to 1 and from x to −1. Split into three cases based on the critical points x = −1 and x = 1. x = −2 and x = 2 \text{Critical points: } x = 1 \text{ and } x = -1. These are where the expressions inside the absolute values change sign. They divide the number line into three regions. \textbf{Case 1: } x < -1 Here x−1 < 0 and x+1 < 0. |x-1| + |x+1| = -(x-1) + -(x+1) = -x+1-x-1 = -2x Remove absolute values using the sign of each expression in this region. -2x = 4 \implies x = -2 Solve. Check: −2 < −1 ✓. So x = −2 is a solution. \textbf{Case 2: } -1 \leq x \leq 1 Here x−1 ≤ 0 and x+1 ≥ 0. |x-1| + |x+1| = -(x-1) + (x+1) = -x+1+x+1 = 2 The sum simplifies to the constant 2. 2 = 4\;?\quad \text{Never true.} No solutions in [−1, 1]. \textbf{Case 3: } x > 1 Here x−1 > 0 and x+1 > 0. |x-1|+|x+1| = (x-1)+(x+1) = 2x 2x = 4 \implies x = 2 Check: 2 > 1 ✓. So x = 2 is a solution. \text{Solution set: } \{-2, 2\} Next: Functions Functions map real numbers to real numbers — they are the main objects of calculus. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Sets & the Language of Logic
documents.subtitle: The vocabulary every proof is written in
documents.tags: sets logic quantifiers for all there exists set-builder notation union intersection complement subset element empty set Venn diagram connectives and or not implies if and only if iff proof foundations
documents.aliases: rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof
documents.proofs:
The claim that $\emptyset \subseteq A$ for every set $A$ follows from vacuous truth. We need to verify: $ orall x,\; x \in \emptyset \Rightarrow x \in A$. Since there is no $x$ with $x \in \emptyset$, the hypothesis is always false, so the implication is vacuously true for every $x$. Therefore $\emptyset \subseteq A$. De Morgan's Laws can be proved by showing mutual subset inclusion. To prove $(A \cup B)^c \subseteq A^c \cap B^c$: let $x \in (A \cup B)^c$. Then $x 
otin A \cup B$, meaning $x 
otin A$ AND $x 
otin B$ (if $x$ were in either, it would be in the union). So $x \in A^c$ and $x \in B^c$, hence $x \in A^c \cap B^c$. The reverse inclusion follows by reversing the argument. The logical equivalence of an implication and its contrapositive can be verified by truth table: $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $
eg Q \Rightarrow 
eg P$ have identical truth values for all combinations of P and Q. This is not true for the converse $Q \Rightarrow P$ or the inverse $
eg P \Rightarrow 
eg Q$. A subtle but important point: the order of quantifiers matters. The statement $ orall x \in \mathbb{R},\; \exists y \in \mathbb{R},\; y > x$ says "for every real number, there is a larger one" — this is true. The swapped version $\exists y \in \mathbb{R},\;  orall x \in \mathbb{R},\; y > x$ says "there is a real number larger than all others" — this is false. Swapping quantifiers changes the meaning entirely. Vacuous Truth 	ext{If } P 	ext{ is false, then } P \Rightarrow Q 	ext{ is true for any } Q. \ 	ext{Consequence: } \emptyset \subseteq A 	ext{ for every set } A. Proving Set Equality To prove A = B, prove A ⊆ B (pick arbitrary x ∈ A, show x ∈ B) AND B ⊆ A (pick arbitrary x ∈ B, show x ∈ A). This "double containment" technique is the standard method.
documents.applications: Every database query you run, every search filter you apply, and every logical condition in a program uses set operations and logical connectives. SQL uses UNION, INTERSECT, AND, OR — these are literally set theory and logic. When a doctor says "all patients with fever AND cough OR shortness of breath," the precise meaning depends on grouping — logic removes the ambiguity. Learning this language once means you can read any mathematical proof, any formal specification, and any legal contract with precision. Sets are Containers Think of a set as a bag of objects. You can ask: is this item in the bag? You can combine bags (union), find shared items (intersection), or remove items (difference). Order does not matter, and duplicates are ignored: {1, 2, 3} = {3, 1, 2} = {1, 1, 2, 3}. Reading Set-Builder Notation Read {x ∈ S : P(x)} as "the set of all x in S such that P(x) is true." Example: {x ∈ ℝ : x > 0} = "all positive real numbers" = (0, ∞). OR Means "One or Both" in Math In everyday English, "Would you like tea or coffee?" usually means pick one. In math, A ∨ B (A or B) is true when A is true, when B is true, or when BOTH are true. The exclusive "one but not both" is a different operation (XOR). Negating Quantifiers Flip each quantifier and negate the predicate. ¬(∀x, P(x)) becomes ∃x, ¬P(x). ¬(∃x, P(x)) becomes ∀x, ¬P(x). Apply this rule from left to right through nested quantifiers.
documents.content:
How can two sentences that sound completely different mean exactly the same thing mathematically? Every database query you run, every search filter you apply, and every logical condition in a program uses set operations and logical connectives. SQL uses UNION, INTERSECT, AND, OR — these are literally set theory and logic. When a doctor says "all patients with fever AND cough OR shortness of breath," the precise meaning depends on grouping — logic removes the ambiguity. Learning this language once means you can read any mathematical proof, any formal specification, and any legal contract with precision. A **set** is simply a collection of objects, called **elements** or **members**. We write $a \in S$ to mean "a is an element of S" and $a 
otin S$ to mean "a is not in S." That is the entire definition — sets are containers, and things are either in them or not. You can describe a set in two ways. **Roster notation** lists the elements explicitly: $\{2, 4, 6, 8\}$. **Set-builder notation** describes a rule: $\{x \in \mathbb{Z} : x 	ext{ is even and } 0 < x < 10\}$. The colon (or vertical bar |) reads as "such that." Both describe the same set. The **empty set** $\emptyset = \{\}$ contains no elements. It is a valid set — think of it as an empty box. Every set contains $\emptyset$ as a subset, which might feel strange, but it is true by a logical technicality we will see in the rigor tab. Now for combining sets. The **union** $A \cup B$ is everything in A or B (or both) — think of it as "gathering together." The **intersection** $A \cap B$ is everything in both A and B — think of it as "overlap." The **complement** $A^c$ (or $\overline{A}$) is everything NOT in A (relative to some universal set). The **set difference** $A \setminus B$ is everything in A that is not in B. Logic is the grammar of mathematics. The basic **connectives** are: AND ($\wedge$), OR ($\vee$), NOT ($
eg$), IMPLIES ($\Rightarrow$), and IF AND ONLY IF ($\Leftrightarrow$). In math, OR is always **inclusive** — "A or B" means "A or B or both." This differs from everyday English where "or" often means "one or the other but not both." The two **quantifiers** are the heart of formal mathematics. The **universal quantifier** $ orall$ means "for all" or "for every." The **existential quantifier** $\exists$ means "there exists" or "there is at least one." The statement $ orall x \in \mathbb{R},\; x^2 \geq 0$ says "every real number squared is non-negative." The statement $\exists x \in \mathbb{R} : x^2 = 2$ says "there is some real number whose square is 2." Negating quantified statements follows a beautiful pattern: the negation of "for all" is "there exists one that does not," and the negation of "there exists" is "for all, it is not." Symbolically: $
eg( orall x, P(x)) \equiv \exists x, 
eg P(x)$ and $
eg(\exists x, P(x)) \equiv  orall x, 
eg P(x)$. This is crucial for understanding epsilon-delta proofs, where you often need to negate a limit definition. We now formalize the key set operations. Let $U$ denote a **universal set** — the "universe" of all objects under consideration. For calculus, $U = \mathbb{R}$ most of the time. **Subset**: $A \subseteq B$ means every element of $A$ is also in $B$: $ orall x,\; x \in A \Rightarrow x \in B$. If additionally $A 
eq B$, we write $A \subsetneq B$ (proper subset). Two sets are equal when $A \subseteq B$ and $B \subseteq A$. **Union and intersection** generalize naturally. For a family of sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$, we write $\displaystyle  igcup_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ for the union (elements in at least one $A_i$) and $\displaystyle  igcap_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ for the intersection (elements in every $A_i$). An **implication** $P \Rightarrow Q$ is read "if P then Q." P is the **hypothesis** and Q is the **conclusion**. The implication is false only when P is true but Q is false. Crucially, when P is false, the implication $P \Rightarrow Q$ is **vacuously true** regardless of Q. This is why "all elements of the empty set are pink elephants" is a true statement. The **contrapositive** of $P \Rightarrow Q$ is $
eg Q \Rightarrow 
eg P$. These are logically equivalent — proving one proves the other. The **converse** $Q \Rightarrow P$ is a completely different statement that may or may not be true. Confusing an implication with its converse is one of the most common logical errors. A **biconditional** $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ means $P \Rightarrow Q$ AND $Q \Rightarrow P$. We say "P if and only if Q" (abbreviated "iff"). To prove an iff statement, you must prove both directions. The claim that $\emptyset \subseteq A$ for every set $A$ follows from vacuous truth. We need to verify: $ orall x,\; x \in \emptyset \Rightarrow x \in A$. Since there is no $x$ with $x \in \emptyset$, the hypothesis is always false, so the implication is vacuously true for every $x$. Therefore $\emptyset \subseteq A$. De Morgan's Laws can be proved by showing mutual subset inclusion. To prove $(A \cup B)^c \subseteq A^c \cap B^c$: let $x \in (A \cup B)^c$. Then $x 
otin A \cup B$, meaning $x 
otin A$ AND $x 
otin B$ (if $x$ were in either, it would be in the union). So $x \in A^c$ and $x \in B^c$, hence $x \in A^c \cap B^c$. The reverse inclusion follows by reversing the argument. The logical equivalence of an implication and its contrapositive can be verified by truth table: $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $
eg Q \Rightarrow 
eg P$ have identical truth values for all combinations of P and Q. This is not true for the converse $Q \Rightarrow P$ or the inverse $
eg P \Rightarrow 
eg Q$. A subtle but important point: the order of quantifiers matters. The statement $ orall x \in \mathbb{R},\; \exists y \in \mathbb{R},\; y > x$ says "for every real number, there is a larger one" — this is true. The swapped version $\exists y \in \mathbb{R},\;  orall x \in \mathbb{R},\; y > x$ says "there is a real number larger than all others" — this is false. Swapping quantifiers changes the meaning entirely. Vacuous Truth 	ext{If } P 	ext{ is false, then } P \Rightarrow Q 	ext{ is true for any } Q. \ 	ext{Consequence: } \emptyset \subseteq A 	ext{ for every set } A. Proving Set Equality To prove A = B, prove A ⊆ B (pick arbitrary x ∈ A, show x ∈ B) AND B ⊆ A (pick arbitrary x ∈ B, show x ∈ A). This "double containment" technique is the standard method. Sets are Containers Think of a set as a bag of objects. You can ask: is this item in the bag? You can combine bags (union), find shared items (intersection), or remove items (difference). Order does not matter, and duplicates are ignored: {1, 2, 3} = {3, 1, 2} = {1, 1, 2, 3}. Reading Set-Builder Notation Read {x ∈ S : P(x)} as "the set of all x in S such that P(x) is true." Example: {x ∈ ℝ : x > 0} = "all positive real numbers" = (0, ∞). OR Means "One or Both" in Math In everyday English, "Would you like tea or coffee?" usually means pick one. In math, A ∨ B (A or B) is true when A is true, when B is true, or when BOTH are true. The exclusive "one but not both" is a different operation (XOR). Negating Quantifiers Flip each quantifier and negate the predicate. ¬(∀x, P(x)) becomes ∃x, ¬P(x). ¬(∃x, P(x)) becomes ∀x, ¬P(x). Apply this rule from left to right through nested quantifiers. Set Operations A \cup B = \{x : x \in A 	ext{ or } x \in B\} \ A \cap B = \{x : x \in A 	ext{ and } x \in B\} \ A \setminus B = \{x : x \in A 	ext{ and } x 
otin B\} \ A^c = \{x \in U : x 
otin A\} De Morgan's Laws (A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c \[4pt] (A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c \[6pt] 	ext{Equivalently for logic:} \[4pt] 
eg(P \vee Q) \equiv (
eg P) \wedge (
eg Q) \ 
eg(P \wedge Q) \equiv (
eg P) \vee (
eg Q) Implication Truth Table P \Rightarrow Q 	ext{ is FALSE only when } P 	ext{ is TRUE and } Q 	ext{ is FALSE.} \ 	ext{All other combinations yield TRUE.} Translating Set-Builder Notation Write the set $S = \{x \in \mathbb{Z} : x^2 < 10\}$ in roster notation. Set-builder notation describes a rule; roster notation lists the results. For infinite sets, only set-builder works. x^2 < 10 \implies |x| < \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 Find which integers satisfy the condition. x \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\} List all integers with absolute value less than 3.16. 	ext{Check: } (-3)^2 = 9 < 10\;\checkmark, \quad (4)^2 = 16 
ot< 10\;	imes Verify the boundary cases. S = \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\} Seven elements in total. Union and Intersection of Intervals Let $A = [-2, 3)$ and $B = (1, 5]$. Find $A \cup B$, $A \cap B$, and $A \setminus B$. Visualizing intervals on a number line makes set operations concrete. Always check endpoints carefully. A = [-2, 3), \quad B = (1, 5] Draw both intervals on a number line to visualize overlap. A \cup B = [-2, 5] Union: everything in either interval. A covers [-2, 3) and B covers (1, 5], together they cover [-2, 5]. A \cap B = (1, 3) Intersection: the overlap region. Both intervals contain (1, 3). At x = 1: 1 ∉ B. At x = 3: 3 ∉ A. A \setminus B = [-2, 1] Difference: elements in A but not B. This is A with the overlap removed. x = 1 is in A but not in B, so it stays. Negating a Quantified Statement Negate the statement: $ orall \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists \delta > 0,\;  orall x,\; |x - a| < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$. The negation says: "there exists an epsilon such that no matter how small you make delta, you can find an x within delta of a where f(x) is at least epsilon away from L." This is exactly what it means for a limit NOT to exist. 
eg igl( orall \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists \delta > 0,\;  orall x,\; |x-a|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon igr) This is the epsilon-delta definition of a limit. We negate it step by step. \exists \varepsilon > 0,\; 
eg igl(\exists \delta > 0,\;  orall x,\; |x-a|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon igr) Flip ∀ to ∃, push negation inward. \exists \varepsilon > 0,\;  orall \delta > 0,\; 
eg igl( orall x,\; |x-a|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon igr) Flip ∃ to ∀, push negation inward. \exists \varepsilon > 0,\;  orall \delta > 0,\; \exists x,\; 
eg igl(|x-a|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon igr) Flip ∀ to ∃, push negation inward. \exists \varepsilon > 0,\;  orall \delta > 0,\; \exists x,\; |x-a|<\delta \;\wedge\; |f(x)-L| \geq \varepsilon Negate the implication: ¬(P ⟹ Q) ≡ P ∧ ¬Q. Contrapositive vs. Converse Consider the statement: "If $n^2$ is even, then $n$ is even." State its contrapositive, converse, and inverse. Which are equivalent to the original? Only the contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original implication. The converse and inverse are equivalent to each other but not to the original. 	ext{Original: } n^2 	ext{ even} \Rightarrow n 	ext{ even} This is P ⟹ Q. 	ext{Contrapositive: } n 	ext{ odd} \Rightarrow n^2 	ext{ odd} ¬Q ⟹ ¬P. This is logically equivalent to the original. 	ext{Converse: } n 	ext{ even} \Rightarrow n^2 	ext{ even} Q ⟹ P. This happens to be true here, but it is NOT logically equivalent. 	ext{Inverse: } n^2 	ext{ odd} \Rightarrow n 	ext{ odd} ¬P ⟹ ¬Q. Equivalent to the converse, not to the original. Let $A = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ and $B = \{3,4,5,6,7\}$. Find $A \cup B$, $A \cap B$, $A \setminus B$, and $B \setminus A$. Union gathers everything, intersection keeps only shared elements, difference removes the second set from the first. A ∪ B = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7}, A ∩ B = {3,4,5}, A \ B = {1,2}, B \ A = {6,7} A \cup B = \{1,2,3,4,5,6,7\} Every element that appears in either set. A \cap B = \{3,4,5\} Elements common to both sets. A \setminus B = \{1,2\} Elements in A but not in B. B \setminus A = \{6,7\} Elements in B but not in A. Negate the statement: "For every student in the class, there exists a problem on the exam that they answered correctly." Translate to symbols first: ∀ student s, ∃ problem p, s answered p correctly. Then flip quantifiers and negate the predicate. "There exists a student in the class who answered every problem on the exam incorrectly." In other words, at least one student got a zero.  orall s,\; \exists p,\; 	ext{Correct}(s, p) Symbolize the original statement. 
eg( orall s,\; \exists p,\; 	ext{Correct}(s, p)) We want the negation. \exists s,\;  orall p,\; 
eg	ext{Correct}(s, p) Flip each quantifier, negate the predicate. Prove using De Morgan's Laws that $(A \cup B) \setminus C = (A \setminus C) \cup (B \setminus C)$. Rewrite set difference using intersection and complement: X \ Y = X ∩ Y^c. Then distribute. The distributive law for intersection over union gives us the result directly: (A ∪ B) \ C = (A \ C) ∪ (B \ C). (A \cup B) \setminus C = (A \cup B) \cap C^c Rewrite difference as intersection with complement. = (A \cap C^c) \cup (B \cap C^c) Distribute intersection over union (this is a set-theoretic law). = (A \setminus C) \cup (B \setminus C) Rewrite back as set differences. Real Numbers Sets and logic provide the language to describe number systems precisely. Inequalities Set-builder notation is used heavily to describe solution sets of inequalities. Epsilon-Delta The epsilon-delta definition of a limit uses nested quantifiers — understanding how to read and negate them is essential. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof

documents.title: Coordinate Geometry Review
documents.subtitle: Lines, triangles, circles, and the coordinate plane
documents.tags: geometry coordinate plane distance formula midpoint slope parallel perpendicular equation of a line circle triangle area Pythagorean theorem similar triangles point-slope form slope-intercept standard form
documents.assignment: Distance and Midpoint Find the distance between $A = (-1, 3)$ and $B = (5, -1)$, and the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$.   The distance is $2\sqrt{13} \approx 7.21$ units and the midpoint is $(2, 1)$. d = \sqrt{(5-(-1))^2 + (-1-3)^2} Apply the distance formula. = \sqrt{6^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16} Compute the differences and square them. = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13} Simplify: 52 = 4 × 13. M = \left(\frac{-1+5}{2}, \frac{3+(-1)}{2}\right) = (2, 1) Average the coordinates for the midpoint. Equation of a Perpendicular Line Find the equation of the line perpendicular to $y = 3x - 2$ that passes through $(6, 1)$.   The perpendicular line is $y = -\frac{1}{3}x + 3$. You can verify: $3 \times (-\frac{1}{3}) = -1$. m_1 = 3 The given line has slope 3. m_2 = -\frac{1}{3} Perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal. y - 1 = -\frac{1}{3}(x - 6) Point-slope form with the point (6, 1). y - 1 = -\frac{1}{3}x + 2 Distribute. y = -\frac{1}{3}x + 3 Slope-intercept form. From General Form to Standard Form (Circle) Write $x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0$ in standard form and identify center and radius.   The circle has center $(3, -2)$ and radius 5. Completing the square is the key technique for converting to standard form. (x^2 - 6x) + (y^2 + 4y) = 12 Group x-terms and y-terms; move constant to the right. (x^2 - 6x + 9) + (y^2 + 4y + 4) = 12 + 9 + 4 Complete the square in both groups. Half of -6 is -3, squared is 9. Half of 4 is 2, squared is 4. (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25 Factor the perfect squares. \text{Center: } (3, -2), \quad r = \sqrt{25} = 5 Read off center and radius from standard form. Triangle Area Using the Shoelace Formula Find the area of the triangle with vertices $A = (1, 2)$, $B = (4, 6)$, $C = (7, 1)$.   The area is $\frac{27}{2} = 13.5$ square units. The shoelace formula works for any triangle without needing to find base and height. A = \frac{1}{2}|x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)| Apply the shoelace formula. = \frac{1}{2}|1(6 - 1) + 4(1 - 2) + 7(2 - 6)| Substitute the coordinates. = \frac{1}{2}|5 + (-4) + (-28)| Compute each term. = \frac{1}{2}|-27| = \frac{27}{2} Take absolute value and divide by 2.  Find the equation of the line through $(-2, 5)$ and $(4, -1)$. First find the slope using m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁), then use point-slope form. y = −x + 3  m = \frac{-1 - 5}{4 - (-2)} = \frac{-6}{6} = -1 Calculate the slope. y - 5 = -1(x - (-2)) = -(x + 2) Point-slope form using (-2, 5). y = -x - 2 + 5 = -x + 3 Simplify to slope-intercept form.  Prove that the triangle with vertices $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (4, 0)$, $C = (0, 3)$ is a right triangle, and find its area. Check if the Pythagorean theorem holds: compute all three side lengths. It is a right triangle (3-4-5) with area 6 square units.  AB = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 4 Distance from A to B. AC = \sqrt{(0-0)^2 + (3-0)^2} = 3 Distance from A to C. BC = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-3)^2} = \sqrt{16+9} = 5 Distance from B to C. AB^2 + AC^2 = 16 + 9 = 25 = BC^2\;\checkmark Pythagorean theorem holds, so the right angle is at A. A = \frac{1}{2}(4)(3) = 6 Area = ½ × base × height.  Find the equation of the circle passing through the points $(0, 0)$, $(6, 0)$, and $(0, 8)$. The center lies on the perpendicular bisector of each chord. Find two perpendicular bisectors and solve the system. $(x-3)^2 + (y-4)^2 = 25$. Center $(3,4)$, radius 5.  \text{Chord 1: } (0,0) \text{ to } (6,0). \text{ Midpoint: } (3,0). \text{ Perp. bisector: } x = 3. The chord is horizontal, so its perpendicular bisector is vertical through the midpoint. \text{Chord 2: } (0,0) \text{ to } (0,8). \text{ Midpoint: } (0,4). \text{ Perp. bisector: } y = 4. The chord is vertical, so its perpendicular bisector is horizontal. \text{Center: } (3, 4) Intersection of x = 3 and y = 4. r = \sqrt{(3-0)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5 Distance from center to (0,0). (x-3)^2 + (y-4)^2 = 25 The circle equation.  Explain geometrically why $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ for small positive $\theta$ (in radians). What property of arc length makes this work? The arc of the unit circle subtended by angle θ has length θ (since arc = rθ = 1·θ). For small θ, the arc length and the vertical chord (sin θ) are nearly equal. $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ because arc length $= \theta$ and the chord (sin θ) becomes indistinguishable from the arc for small θ. This is the geometric engine behind $\lim_{\theta\to 0}\sin\theta/\theta = 1$.  \text{On the unit circle: arc length} = r\theta = \theta For a unit circle (r=1), arc length equals the angle in radians. \sin\theta = \text{vertical distance from axis to point} This is the chord (straight-line) distance, always ≤ the arc. \theta \geq \sin\theta \geq \theta\cos\theta \quad \text{for } \theta \in [0, \pi/2] The arc is longer than the chord; the chord is at least θ·cosθ by similar-triangle geometry. \text{As } \theta \to 0: \cos\theta \to 1 \Rightarrow \frac{\sin\theta}{\theta} \to 1 Squeeze Theorem: the ratio is squeezed between cosθ and 1, both approaching 1.
documents.applications: GPS navigation uses the distance formula to calculate how far you are from your destination. Architects use slope to design wheelchair ramps that meet accessibility codes (no steeper than 1:12). Engineers model satellite orbits as circles and ellipses in coordinate geometry. Even video game collision detection relies on distance formulas and circle equations to determine when objects touch.
documents.content: Why does a² + b² = c² work, and how does one equation connect distances, slopes, and circles? GPS navigation uses the distance formula to calculate how far you are from your destination. Architects use slope to design wheelchair ramps that meet accessibility codes (no steeper than 1:12). Engineers model satellite orbits as circles and ellipses in coordinate geometry. Even video game collision detection relies on distance formulas and circle equations to determine when objects touch. Distance and Midpoint Find the distance between $A = (-1, 3)$ and $B = (5, -1)$, and the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$. The distance is $2\sqrt{13} \approx 7.21$ units and the midpoint is $(2, 1)$. d = \sqrt{(5-(-1))^2 + (-1-3)^2} Apply the distance formula. = \sqrt{6^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16} Compute the differences and square them. = \sqrt{52} = 2\sqrt{13} Simplify: 52 = 4 × 13. M = \left(\frac{-1+5}{2}, \frac{3+(-1)}{2}\right) = (2, 1) Average the coordinates for the midpoint. Equation of a Perpendicular Line Find the equation of the line perpendicular to $y = 3x - 2$ that passes through $(6, 1)$. The perpendicular line is $y = -\frac{1}{3}x + 3$. You can verify: $3 \times (-\frac{1}{3}) = -1$. m_1 = 3 The given line has slope 3. m_2 = -\frac{1}{3} Perpendicular slope is the negative reciprocal. y - 1 = -\frac{1}{3}(x - 6) Point-slope form with the point (6, 1). y - 1 = -\frac{1}{3}x + 2 Distribute. y = -\frac{1}{3}x + 3 Slope-intercept form. From General Form to Standard Form (Circle) Write $x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0$ in standard form and identify center and radius. The circle has center $(3, -2)$ and radius 5. Completing the square is the key technique for converting to standard form. (x^2 - 6x) + (y^2 + 4y) = 12 Group x-terms and y-terms; move constant to the right. (x^2 - 6x + 9) + (y^2 + 4y + 4) = 12 + 9 + 4 Complete the square in both groups. Half of -6 is -3, squared is 9. Half of 4 is 2, squared is 4. (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25 Factor the perfect squares. \text{Center: } (3, -2), \quad r = \sqrt{25} = 5 Read off center and radius from standard form. Triangle Area Using the Shoelace Formula Find the area of the triangle with vertices $A = (1, 2)$, $B = (4, 6)$, $C = (7, 1)$. The area is $\frac{27}{2} = 13.5$ square units. The shoelace formula works for any triangle without needing to find base and height. A = \frac{1}{2}|x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2)| Apply the shoelace formula. = \frac{1}{2}|1(6 - 1) + 4(1 - 2) + 7(2 - 6)| Substitute the coordinates. = \frac{1}{2}|5 + (-4) + (-28)| Compute each term. = \frac{1}{2}|-27| = \frac{27}{2} Take absolute value and divide by 2. Find the equation of the line through $(-2, 5)$ and $(4, -1)$. First find the slope using m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁), then use point-slope form. y = −x + 3 m = \frac{-1 - 5}{4 - (-2)} = \frac{-6}{6} = -1 Calculate the slope. y - 5 = -1(x - (-2)) = -(x + 2) Point-slope form using (-2, 5). y = -x - 2 + 5 = -x + 3 Simplify to slope-intercept form. Prove that the triangle with vertices $A = (0, 0)$, $B = (4, 0)$, $C = (0, 3)$ is a right triangle, and find its area. Check if the Pythagorean theorem holds: compute all three side lengths. It is a right triangle (3-4-5) with area 6 square units. AB = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 4 Distance from A to B. AC = \sqrt{(0-0)^2 + (3-0)^2} = 3 Distance from A to C. BC = \sqrt{(4-0)^2 + (0-3)^2} = \sqrt{16+9} = 5 Distance from B to C. AB^2 + AC^2 = 16 + 9 = 25 = BC^2\;\checkmark Pythagorean theorem holds, so the right angle is at A. A = \frac{1}{2}(4)(3) = 6 Area = ½ × base × height. Find the equation of the circle passing through the points $(0, 0)$, $(6, 0)$, and $(0, 8)$. The center lies on the perpendicular bisector of each chord. Find two perpendicular bisectors and solve the system. $(x-3)^2 + (y-4)^2 = 25$. Center $(3,4)$, radius 5. \text{Chord 1: } (0,0) \text{ to } (6,0). \text{ Midpoint: } (3,0). \text{ Perp. bisector: } x = 3. The chord is horizontal, so its perpendicular bisector is vertical through the midpoint. \text{Chord 2: } (0,0) \text{ to } (0,8). \text{ Midpoint: } (0,4). \text{ Perp. bisector: } y = 4. The chord is vertical, so its perpendicular bisector is horizontal. \text{Center: } (3, 4) Intersection of x = 3 and y = 4. r = \sqrt{(3-0)^2 + (4-0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5 Distance from center to (0,0). (x-3)^2 + (y-4)^2 = 25 The circle equation. Explain geometrically why $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ for small positive $\theta$ (in radians). What property of arc length makes this work? The arc of the unit circle subtended by angle θ has length θ (since arc = rθ = 1·θ). For small θ, the arc length and the vertical chord (sin θ) are nearly equal. $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ because arc length $= \theta$ and the chord (sin θ) becomes indistinguishable from the arc for small θ. This is the geometric engine behind $\lim_{\theta\to 0}\sin\theta/\theta = 1$. \text{On the unit circle: arc length} = r\theta = \theta For a unit circle (r=1), arc length equals the angle in radians. \sin\theta = \text{vertical distance from axis to point} This is the chord (straight-line) distance, always ≤ the arc. \theta \geq \sin\theta \geq \theta\cos\theta \quad \text{for } \theta \in [0, \pi/2] The arc is longer than the chord; the chord is at least θ·cosθ by similar-triangle geometry. \text{As } \theta \to 0: \cos\theta \to 1 \Rightarrow \frac{\sin\theta}{\theta} \to 1 Squeeze Theorem: the ratio is squeezed between cosθ and 1, both approaching 1. Real Numbers The coordinate plane is built on pairs of real numbers. Functions Lines, circles, and parabolas are all examples of functions or relations on the coordinate plane. Conic Sections Circles generalize to ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas — the conic sections. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Conic Sections Foundations
documents.subtitle: Quadratic geometry for algebra fluency and calculus readiness
documents.tags: conics parabola ellipse hyperbola circle focus-directrix completing the square quadratic forms
documents.assignment: Classify by Completing the Square Classify x^2 - 4x + y^2 + 6y - 12 = 0 and write standard form.   Circle with center (2,-3) and radius 5. x^2-4x + y^2+6y = 12 Move constant to the right. (x-2)^2-4 + (y+3)^2-9 = 12 Complete both squares. (x-2)^2 + (y+3)^2 = 25 Collect constants. Parabola from Focus/Directrix Find equation of parabola with focus (0,2) and directrix y=-2.   Parabola opens upward with vertex at (0,0) and p=2. sqrt(x^2+(y-2)^2)=|y+2| Distance to focus equals distance to directrix. x^2+(y-2)^2=(y+2)^2 Square both sides. x^2=8y Simplify to standard parabola form.  Put x^2 - y^2 - 6x - 4y = 3 into standard form and identify the conic. Complete x and y squares separately and keep the minus sign attached to the y-group. Hyperbola centered at (3,-2).  (x^2-6x) - (y^2+4y)=3 Group terms. (x-3)^2-9 - ((y+2)^2-4)=3 Complete squares. (x-3)^2 - (y+2)^2 = 8 Simplify.  For ellipse x^2/9 + y^2/4 = 1, find foci and eccentricity. Use c^2=a^2-b^2 with a^2=9, b^2=4. Foci (+-sqrt(5),0), eccentricity sqrt(5)/3.  a=3, b=2 Read denominators. c^2=9-4=5 => c=sqrt(5) Compute focal distance. foci=(+-sqrt(5),0), e=c/a=sqrt(5)/3 Focus and eccentricity.
documents.proofs: Conic classification is really a statement about quadratic forms. After translation (and sometimes rotation), every conic reduces to a canonical sign pattern. If both squared terms have the same sign, level sets are ellipse-type; with opposite signs, hyperbola-type; with one squared variable, parabola-type. Focus-directrix definitions can be converted to algebraic forms by equating distances and squaring carefully, giving geometric proofs of standard equations.
documents.applications: Conics model orbit paths, optical reflection geometry, and optimization constraints. Calculus keeps returning to these shapes in derivatives, integrals, and coordinate transforms, so conic fluency is a force multiplier. Conic Identification Workflow Group x/y terms, complete squares, normalize right side, then read type from signs and denominator structure. Parabolic Reflection Parabolic mirrors send rays from a focus into near-parallel beams. This drives headlight and dish design.
documents.content: Why do satellites, lenses, and trajectories keep producing the same four curve families? Conics model orbit paths, optical reflection geometry, and optimization constraints. Calculus keeps returning to these shapes in derivatives, integrals, and coordinate transforms, so conic fluency is a force multiplier. Conic sections are what you get by slicing a cone with a plane: circle, ellipse, parabola, or hyperbola. Each family has two views: an algebraic quadratic equation and a geometric distance rule. Both matter. Circle: fixed distance from one center. Ellipse: fixed sum of distances to two foci. Hyperbola: fixed absolute difference of distances to two foci. Parabola: equal distance to focus and directrix. Completing the square is the decoder that turns expanded equations into standard forms where centers, vertices, and axes are visible. This is not isolated algebra. Conic structure appears in optimization constraints, implicit differentiation, and substitution patterns in integration. Circle: (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2. Parabola (vertical): (x-h)^2 = 4p(y-k), where p controls focal distance and opening direction. Ellipse: (x-h)^2/a^2 + (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1, with a >= b and c^2 = a^2 - b^2. Hyperbola: (x-h)^2/a^2 - (y-k)^2/b^2 = 1 (or swapped). Focal relation is c^2 = a^2 + b^2. General quadratic Ax^2 + Bxy + Cy^2 + Dx + Ey + F = 0 (without rotation details) is often classified by sign pattern after completing squares; for B=0, same-sign squared terms imply ellipse type, opposite-sign imply hyperbola type, one squared variable implies parabola type. Eccentricity unifies all conics: e=0 circle, 0<e<1 ellipse, e=1 parabola, e>1 hyperbola. Conic classification is really a statement about quadratic forms. After translation (and sometimes rotation), every conic reduces to a canonical sign pattern. If both squared terms have the same sign, level sets are ellipse-type; with opposite signs, hyperbola-type; with one squared variable, parabola-type. Focus-directrix definitions can be converted to algebraic forms by equating distances and squaring carefully, giving geometric proofs of standard equations. Conic Identification Workflow Group x/y terms, complete squares, normalize right side, then read type from signs and denominator structure. Parabolic Reflection Parabolic mirrors send rays from a focus into near-parallel beams. This drives headlight and dish design. Eccentricity e = distance to focus / distance to directrix. It classifies the conic family and measures deviation from circular behavior. Complete-Square Pattern x^2+bx = (x+b/2)^2 - (b/2)^2. Apply separately in x and y groups. Classify by Completing the Square Classify x^2 - 4x + y^2 + 6y - 12 = 0 and write standard form. Circle with center (2,-3) and radius 5. x^2-4x + y^2+6y = 12 Move constant to the right. (x-2)^2-4 + (y+3)^2-9 = 12 Complete both squares. (x-2)^2 + (y+3)^2 = 25 Collect constants. Parabola from Focus/Directrix Find equation of parabola with focus (0,2) and directrix y=-2. Parabola opens upward with vertex at (0,0) and p=2. sqrt(x^2+(y-2)^2)=|y+2| Distance to focus equals distance to directrix. x^2+(y-2)^2=(y+2)^2 Square both sides. x^2=8y Simplify to standard parabola form. Put x^2 - y^2 - 6x - 4y = 3 into standard form and identify the conic. Complete x and y squares separately and keep the minus sign attached to the y-group. Hyperbola centered at (3,-2). (x^2-6x) - (y^2+4y)=3 Group terms. (x-3)^2-9 - ((y+2)^2-4)=3 Complete squares. (x-3)^2 - (y+2)^2 = 8 Simplify. For ellipse x^2/9 + y^2/4 = 1, find foci and eccentricity. Use c^2=a^2-b^2 with a^2=9, b^2=4. Foci (+-sqrt(5),0), eccentricity sqrt(5)/3. a=3, b=2 Read denominators. c^2=9-4=5 => c=sqrt(5) Compute focal distance. foci=(+-sqrt(5),0), e=c/a=sqrt(5)/3 Focus and eccentricity. Coordinate Geometry Review Conics extend line/circle geometry into full quadratic curves. Completing the Square Main algebraic transformation used to identify conic type. Optimization Quadratic and conic structure appears in many constrained optimization setups. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Inequalities & Interval Notation
documents.subtitle: The toolkit for epsilon-delta and optimization
documents.tags: inequalities linear inequalities polynomial inequalities rational inequalities sign chart sign analysis absolute value interval notation number line critical points test points epsilon-delta quadratic inequalities
documents.aliases: maximize minimize maxima minima best value epsilon-delta formal limit proof proof of limit
documents.assignment: Solving a Linear Inequality Solve $5 - 3x \geq 2$ and graph the solution.   The solution is all real numbers less than or equal to 1. The key step was flipping the inequality when dividing by −3. 5 - 3x \geq 2 Start with the inequality. -3x \geq 2 - 5 = -3 Subtract 5 from both sides. x \leq \frac{-3}{-3} = 1 Divide by −3 and FLIP the inequality sign. x \in (-\infty, 1] Solution in interval notation. Bracket at 1 because ≤. Quadratic Inequality with Sign Chart Solve $x^2 - 5x + 6 < 0$.   The parabola $y = x^2 - 5x + 6$ opens upward and is below the x-axis between its roots. The solution (2, 3) is the interval where the parabola is negative. x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x-2)(x-3) Factor the quadratic. \text{Critical points: } x = 2 \text{ and } x = 3 Set each factor equal to zero. \text{Intervals: } (-\infty, 2), \; (2, 3), \; (3, \infty) The critical points divide the number line into three intervals. \text{Test } x = 0: (0-2)(0-3) = (-2)(-3) = +6 > 0 Interval (−∞, 2): both factors negative, product positive. \text{Test } x = 2.5: (0.5)(-0.5) = -0.25 < 0 Interval (2, 3): first factor positive, second negative, product negative. \text{Test } x = 4: (2)(1) = 2 > 0 Interval (3, ∞): both factors positive, product positive. x \in (2, 3) The product is negative only on (2, 3). Endpoints excluded because strict inequality. Rational Inequality Solve $\displaystyle\frac{x+1}{x-3} \leq 0$.   The solution is [−1, 3). The key subtlety: x = −1 is included (makes the expression equal zero) but x = 3 is excluded (makes it undefined). \frac{x+1}{x-3} \leq 0 Already in the form "expression ≤ 0." Do NOT multiply by (x−3). \text{Numerator zero: } x = -1. \quad \text{Denominator zero: } x = 3. Find all critical points. \text{Intervals: } (-\infty, -1), \; (-1, 3), \; (3, \infty) Three intervals to test. \text{Test } x = -2: \frac{-1}{-5} = +\frac{1}{5} > 0 Both factors negative, so positive. \text{Test } x = 0: \frac{1}{-3} = -\frac{1}{3} < 0 Numerator positive, denominator negative, so negative. \text{Test } x = 4: \frac{5}{1} = 5 > 0 Both positive, so positive. x \in [-1, 3) Include x = −1 (where expression = 0, and ≤ allows 0). Exclude x = 3 (denominator zero). Higher-Degree Polynomial Inequality Solve $x^3 - 4x \geq 0$.   The solution is $[-2, 0] \cup [2, \infty)$. With three factors, the sign alternates across the four intervals (starting negative on the far left since the leading coefficient is positive and the degree is odd). x^3 - 4x = x(x^2 - 4) = x(x-2)(x+2) Factor completely. \text{Critical points: } x = -2, \; 0, \; 2 Zeros of each factor. \text{Test } x = -3: (-3)(-5)(-1) = -15 < 0 Three negative factors: negative. \text{Test } x = -1: (-1)(-3)(1) = 3 > 0 Two negative, one positive: positive. \text{Test } x = 1: (1)(-1)(3) = -3 < 0 One negative: negative. \text{Test } x = 3: (3)(1)(5) = 15 > 0 All positive: positive. x \in [-2, 0] \cup [2, \infty) Non-strict inequality, so include the critical points.  Solve $|2x - 1| < 5$ and write the solution in interval notation. Use the rule |A| < c ⟺ −c < A < c. x ∈ (−2, 3)  -5 < 2x - 1 < 5 Apply the absolute value inequality rule. -4 < 2x < 6 Add 1 to all three parts. -2 < x < 3 Divide by 2.  Solve $\displaystyle\frac{x^2 - 1}{x + 3} > 0$. Factor the numerator as (x−1)(x+1). Critical points: x = −3, −1, 1. Make a sign chart with four intervals. x ∈ (−3, −1) ∪ (1, ∞). Strict inequality, so endpoints excluded; x = −3 always excluded.  \frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x+3} > 0 Factor numerator. \text{Critical points: } x = -3, -1, 1 Zeros and undefined points. \text{Test } x=-4: \frac{(-5)(-3)}{-1} = -15 < 0 Interval (−∞, −3). \text{Test } x=-2: \frac{(-3)(-1)}{1} = 3 > 0 Interval (−3, −1). \text{Test } x=0: \frac{(-1)(1)}{3} = -\frac{1}{3} < 0 Interval (−1, 1). \text{Test } x=2: \frac{(1)(3)}{5} = \frac{3}{5} > 0 Interval (1, ∞).  Solve $|x^2 - 4| \leq 3x$. Note that the right side 3x must be non-negative, so x ≥ 0. Then |x² − 4| ≤ 3x becomes −3x ≤ x² − 4 ≤ 3x. Solve the two inequalities separately and intersect. x ∈ [1, 4]  \text{Require } 3x \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \geq 0 Absolute value is non-negative, so the right side must be too. x^2 - 4 \leq 3x \iff x^2 - 3x - 4 \leq 0 \iff (x-4)(x+1) \leq 0 Right half of the compound inequality. \Rightarrow x \in [-1, 4] Between the roots. x^2 - 4 \geq -3x \iff x^2 + 3x - 4 \geq 0 \iff (x+4)(x-1) \geq 0 Left half. \Rightarrow x \in (-\infty, -4] \cup [1, \infty) Outside the roots. \text{Intersect all three: } x \geq 0,\; x \in [-1,4],\; x \in (-\infty,-4]\cup[1,\infty) Take the overlap of all conditions. x \in [1, 4] Final answer.
documents.proofs: The sign chart method is rigorous because of the **Intermediate Value Theorem** (applied to continuous functions): a continuous function can only change sign by passing through zero. Since polynomials are continuous, the sign of $p(x)$ is constant on each interval between consecutive roots. Testing one point per interval is therefore sufficient. For rational functions $r(x) = p(x)/q(x)$, the function is continuous on each interval between roots of $p$ and $q$. A sign change can occur at zeros of $p$ (where $r(x) = 0$) or at zeros of $q$ (where $r(x)$ is undefined — vertical asymptotes). The sign chart accounts for both. The property that multiplying by a negative number reverses inequality follows from the ordered field axioms of $\mathbb{R}$. If $a < b$ and $c < 0$, then $a - b < 0$ and $c < 0$, so $(a-b) \cdot c > 0$ (product of two negatives is positive), giving $ac - bc > 0$, hence $ac > bc$. A common rigorous technique: to show $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$, we often need to bound $|f(x) - L|$ above by a simpler expression using the triangle inequality and then solve the simpler inequality for $\delta$. This requires comfortable manipulation of chains of inequalities, which is why this lesson is critical preparation for Chapter 1. Inequality Reversal Under Negation \text{If } a < b \text{ and } c < 0, \text{ then } ac > bc. \\ \text{Proof: } a < b \Rightarrow a - b < 0. \text{ Product of two negatives:} \\ (a-b) \cdot c > 0 \Rightarrow ac > bc.
documents.applications: Inequalities are everywhere in the real world. Speed limits say v ≤ 65. Drug dosages must satisfy min ≤ d ≤ max. Financial models require rates to stay within bounds. In calculus, the entire theory of limits rests on epsilon-delta inequalities: |f(x) − L| < ε whenever |x − a| < δ. If you cannot manipulate inequalities fluently, epsilon-delta proofs will feel impossible. This lesson builds that fluency.
documents.content: When an engineer designs a bridge to hold "at least 10,000 kg," how do they express that mathematically? Inequalities are everywhere in the real world. Speed limits say v ≤ 65. Drug dosages must satisfy min ≤ d ≤ max. Financial models require rates to stay within bounds. In calculus, the entire theory of limits rests on epsilon-delta inequalities: |f(x) − L| < ε whenever |x − a| < δ. If you cannot manipulate inequalities fluently, epsilon-delta proofs will feel impossible. This lesson builds that fluency. For a **linear inequality** $ax + b > 0$ with $a \neq 0$: if $a > 0$, then $x > -b/a$; if $a < 0$, then $x < -b/a$ (the inequality flips). The solution is always a single ray. For a **quadratic inequality** $ax^2 + bx + c > 0$, factor (if possible) or use the discriminant. If the quadratic has two real roots $r_1 < r_2$, then: for $a > 0$, the parabola opens up, so $ax^2 + bx + c > 0$ on $(-\infty, r_1) \cup (r_2, \infty)$ and $ax^2 + bx + c < 0$ on $(r_1, r_2)$. More generally, for any **polynomial inequality** $p(x) > 0$, factor $p(x) = a(x - r_1)^{m_1}(x - r_2)^{m_2} \cdots (x - r_k)^{m_k}$. The sign of $p(x)$ can only change at the roots $r_i$. At a root of **odd multiplicity**, the sign changes; at a root of **even multiplicity**, the sign does not change (the graph touches but does not cross the x-axis). For a **rational inequality** $\displaystyle\frac{p(x)}{q(x)} > 0$, the critical points are the zeros of $p(x)$ (where the expression equals zero) and the zeros of $q(x)$ (where the expression is undefined). Apply the sign chart method to each factor separately, then determine the sign of the overall fraction in each interval. Key **properties of inequalities**: (1) Adding the same quantity to both sides preserves the direction. (2) Multiplying both sides by a positive number preserves the direction. (3) Multiplying by a negative number reverses the direction. (4) If $0 < a < b$, then $\frac{1}{b} < \frac{1}{a}$ — taking reciprocals of positive quantities reverses the inequality. The **triangle inequality** $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$ and its reverse $|a - b| \geq ||a| - |b||$ are used constantly in epsilon-delta proofs to bound expressions. These allow you to "break apart" or "combine" absolute values when estimating. The sign chart method is rigorous because of the **Intermediate Value Theorem** (applied to continuous functions): a continuous function can only change sign by passing through zero. Since polynomials are continuous, the sign of $p(x)$ is constant on each interval between consecutive roots. Testing one point per interval is therefore sufficient. For rational functions $r(x) = p(x)/q(x)$, the function is continuous on each interval between roots of $p$ and $q$. A sign change can occur at zeros of $p$ (where $r(x) = 0$) or at zeros of $q$ (where $r(x)$ is undefined — vertical asymptotes). The sign chart accounts for both. The property that multiplying by a negative number reverses inequality follows from the ordered field axioms of $\mathbb{R}$. If $a < b$ and $c < 0$, then $a - b < 0$ and $c < 0$, so $(a-b) \cdot c > 0$ (product of two negatives is positive), giving $ac - bc > 0$, hence $ac > bc$. A common rigorous technique: to show $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$, we often need to bound $|f(x) - L|$ above by a simpler expression using the triangle inequality and then solve the simpler inequality for $\delta$. This requires comfortable manipulation of chains of inequalities, which is why this lesson is critical preparation for Chapter 1. Inequality Reversal Under Negation \text{If } a < b \text{ and } c < 0, \text{ then } ac > bc. \\ \text{Proof: } a < b \Rightarrow a - b < 0. \text{ Product of two negatives:} \\ (a-b) \cdot c > 0 \Rightarrow ac > bc. Sign Change at Roots \text{If } p(x) = a(x-r)^m \cdot q(x) \text{ where } q(r) \neq 0: \\ m \text{ odd} \Rightarrow p(x) \text{ changes sign at } x = r \\ m \text{ even} \Rightarrow p(x) \text{ does not change sign at } x = r Absolute Value Inequalities |A| < c \;(c > 0) \iff -c < A < c \\ |A| > c \;(c > 0) \iff A > c \text{ or } A < -c \\ |A| \leq c \iff -c \leq A \leq c \\ |A| \geq c \iff A \geq c \text{ or } A \leq -c Reciprocal Reversal \text{If } 0 < a < b, \text{ then } \frac{1}{b} < \frac{1}{a}. \\ \text{Taking reciprocals of positive numbers reverses the inequality.} The Triangle Inequality in 1D Play with this geometric proof to see why adding numbers before absolute value is always less than or equal to adding them after. Solving a Linear Inequality Solve $5 - 3x \geq 2$ and graph the solution. The solution is all real numbers less than or equal to 1. The key step was flipping the inequality when dividing by −3. 5 - 3x \geq 2 Start with the inequality. -3x \geq 2 - 5 = -3 Subtract 5 from both sides. x \leq \frac{-3}{-3} = 1 Divide by −3 and FLIP the inequality sign. x \in (-\infty, 1] Solution in interval notation. Bracket at 1 because ≤. Quadratic Inequality with Sign Chart Solve $x^2 - 5x + 6 < 0$. The parabola $y = x^2 - 5x + 6$ opens upward and is below the x-axis between its roots. The solution (2, 3) is the interval where the parabola is negative. x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x-2)(x-3) Factor the quadratic. \text{Critical points: } x = 2 \text{ and } x = 3 Set each factor equal to zero. \text{Intervals: } (-\infty, 2), \; (2, 3), \; (3, \infty) The critical points divide the number line into three intervals. \text{Test } x = 0: (0-2)(0-3) = (-2)(-3) = +6 > 0 Interval (−∞, 2): both factors negative, product positive. \text{Test } x = 2.5: (0.5)(-0.5) = -0.25 < 0 Interval (2, 3): first factor positive, second negative, product negative. \text{Test } x = 4: (2)(1) = 2 > 0 Interval (3, ∞): both factors positive, product positive. x \in (2, 3) The product is negative only on (2, 3). Endpoints excluded because strict inequality. Rational Inequality Solve $\displaystyle\frac{x+1}{x-3} \leq 0$. The solution is [−1, 3). The key subtlety: x = −1 is included (makes the expression equal zero) but x = 3 is excluded (makes it undefined). \frac{x+1}{x-3} \leq 0 Already in the form "expression ≤ 0." Do NOT multiply by (x−3). \text{Numerator zero: } x = -1. \quad \text{Denominator zero: } x = 3. Find all critical points. \text{Intervals: } (-\infty, -1), \; (-1, 3), \; (3, \infty) Three intervals to test. \text{Test } x = -2: \frac{-1}{-5} = +\frac{1}{5} > 0 Both factors negative, so positive. \text{Test } x = 0: \frac{1}{-3} = -\frac{1}{3} < 0 Numerator positive, denominator negative, so negative. \text{Test } x = 4: \frac{5}{1} = 5 > 0 Both positive, so positive. x \in [-1, 3) Include x = −1 (where expression = 0, and ≤ allows 0). Exclude x = 3 (denominator zero). Higher-Degree Polynomial Inequality Solve $x^3 - 4x \geq 0$. The solution is $[-2, 0] \cup [2, \infty)$. With three factors, the sign alternates across the four intervals (starting negative on the far left since the leading coefficient is positive and the degree is odd). x^3 - 4x = x(x^2 - 4) = x(x-2)(x+2) Factor completely. \text{Critical points: } x = -2, \; 0, \; 2 Zeros of each factor. \text{Test } x = -3: (-3)(-5)(-1) = -15 < 0 Three negative factors: negative. \text{Test } x = -1: (-1)(-3)(1) = 3 > 0 Two negative, one positive: positive. \text{Test } x = 1: (1)(-1)(3) = -3 < 0 One negative: negative. \text{Test } x = 3: (3)(1)(5) = 15 > 0 All positive: positive. x \in [-2, 0] \cup [2, \infty) Non-strict inequality, so include the critical points. Solve $|2x - 1| < 5$ and write the solution in interval notation. Use the rule |A| < c ⟺ −c < A < c. x ∈ (−2, 3) -5 < 2x - 1 < 5 Apply the absolute value inequality rule. -4 < 2x < 6 Add 1 to all three parts. -2 < x < 3 Divide by 2. Solve $\displaystyle\frac{x^2 - 1}{x + 3} > 0$. Factor the numerator as (x−1)(x+1). Critical points: x = −3, −1, 1. Make a sign chart with four intervals. x ∈ (−3, −1) ∪ (1, ∞). Strict inequality, so endpoints excluded; x = −3 always excluded. \frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x+3} > 0 Factor numerator. \text{Critical points: } x = -3, -1, 1 Zeros and undefined points. \text{Test } x=-4: \frac{(-5)(-3)}{-1} = -15 < 0 Interval (−∞, −3). \text{Test } x=-2: \frac{(-3)(-1)}{1} = 3 > 0 Interval (−3, −1). \text{Test } x=0: \frac{(-1)(1)}{3} = -\frac{1}{3} < 0 Interval (−1, 1). \text{Test } x=2: \frac{(1)(3)}{5} = \frac{3}{5} > 0 Interval (1, ∞). Solve $|x^2 - 4| \leq 3x$. Note that the right side 3x must be non-negative, so x ≥ 0. Then |x² − 4| ≤ 3x becomes −3x ≤ x² − 4 ≤ 3x. Solve the two inequalities separately and intersect. x ∈ [1, 4] \text{Require } 3x \geq 0 \Rightarrow x \geq 0 Absolute value is non-negative, so the right side must be too. x^2 - 4 \leq 3x \iff x^2 - 3x - 4 \leq 0 \iff (x-4)(x+1) \leq 0 Right half of the compound inequality. \Rightarrow x \in [-1, 4] Between the roots. x^2 - 4 \geq -3x \iff x^2 + 3x - 4 \geq 0 \iff (x+4)(x-1) \geq 0 Left half. \Rightarrow x \in (-\infty, -4] \cup [1, \infty) Outside the roots. \text{Intersect all three: } x \geq 0,\; x \in [-1,4],\; x \in (-\infty,-4]\cup[1,\infty) Take the overlap of all conditions. x \in [1, 4] Final answer. Real Numbers Inequalities describe subsets of the real line using interval notation. Sets & Logic Solution sets of inequalities are sets, and combining inequalities uses AND/OR logic. Epsilon-Delta The epsilon-delta definition of limit is built entirely on inequalities involving absolute values. Absolute Value Absolute value inequalities are a special but important case covered in depth here. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step maximize minimize maxima minima best value epsilon-delta formal limit proof proof of limit

documents.title: Mastering the Line: Rate, Form, and Flow
documents.tags: algebra foundation lines slope
documents.applications: From the slope of a roof to the trajectory of a plane, lines are the "atomic units" of steady growth. In calculus, we approximate even the most complex curves by treating them as a sequence of tiny lines.
documents.content:
What is the simplest possible way a system can change? From the slope of a roof to the trajectory of a plane, lines are the "atomic units" of steady growth. In calculus, we approximate even the most complex curves by treating them as a sequence of tiny lines. A line isn't just a shape; it is the mathematical embodiment of a **constant rate of change**. If you understand the line, you understand the baseline of all physical motion. Whether you describe it via the intercept or a single point, you are always just expressing one thing: the fixed ratio of "rise over run". The Slope Formula m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} The Three Forms 
- **Slope-Intercept**: $y = mx + b$ (Best for finding where you start)
- **Point-Slope**: $y - y_0 = m(x - x_0)$ (Best for Calculus and local rates)
- **Standard**: $Ax + By = C$ (Best for intersections and geometry)
         The Master Lab: Lines from First Principles Use this lab to explore how points, rates, and equations are all different ways of looking at the same steady flow. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Absolute Value as Distance
documents.subtitle: The geometry behind |x|, |x − a|, and inequalities
documents.tags: absolute value distance number line inequalities geometry
documents.assignment: Basic Absolute Value — Distance from Zero Solve |x| < 3.   The solution is all x between −3 and 3 (not including the endpoints). |x| < 3 Distance from x to 0 is less than 3. -3 < x < 3 Translate to interval: x is in (−3, 3). Distance from a Point Solve |x − 5| < 2.   x is between 3 and 7. Geometrically: x is within distance 2 of the point 5. |x − 5| < 2 Distance from x to 5 is less than 2. 5 - 2 < x < 5 + 2 Centered interval: (a − r, a + r) with a=5, r=2. 3 < x < 7 Simplify. The Outer Rays Case Solve |x − 2| > 1.   x is either less than 1 or greater than 3. Geometrically: x is more than distance 1 away from 2, so it lives in the two rays on either side. |x − 2| > 1 Distance from x to 2 is more than 1. x - 2 < -1  OR  x - 2 > 1 Split into two cases. x < 1  OR  x > 3 Solve each inequality. With a Coefficient Solve |3x − 6| < 9.   The solution is the interval (−1, 5). |3x - 6| < 9 Factor out the 3 from inside: |3(x − 2)| = 3|x − 2|. 3|x - 2| < 9 Use Rule 4. |x - 2| < 3 Divide both sides by 3. 2 - 3 < x < 2 + 3 Translate to interval centered at 2 with radius 3. -1 < x < 5 Simplify. Manufacturing Tolerance A machine produces bolts that should be 50 mm long, with tolerance ±0.2 mm. Write and solve the absolute value inequality for acceptable lengths.   Accept bolts between 49.8 mm and 50.2 mm. This is a real-world absolute value inequality. |length - 50| \leq 0.2 Distance from measured length to target is at most tolerance. 50 - 0.2 \leq length \leq 50 + 0.2 Substitute into the equivalence. 49.8 \leq length \leq 50.2 Simplify. Nonlinear: |x² − 4| < 5 Solve $|x^2 - 4| < 5$.   The solution is $(-3, 3)$. The nonlinear case still reduces to center ± radius once the compound inequality is unpacked. -5 < x^2 - 4 < 5 $|A| < 5$ means $-5 < A < 5$. Rewrite as a compound double inequality. -1 < x^2 < 9 Add 4 to all three parts. x^2 > -1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{always true (squares are non-negative)} Left inequality: ignore it — x² is never negative. x^2 < 9 \;\Rightarrow\; |x| < 3 \;\Rightarrow\; -3 < x < 3 Right inequality: x² < 9 means |x| < 3.  Solve |x + 3| ≤ 5. Hint: rewrite as |x − (−3)| first. Treat as distance from x to −3. [-8, 2]  |x - (-3)| \leq 5 Rewrite |x + 3| as distance from x to −3. -3 - 5 \leq x \leq -3 + 5 Apply the equivalence. -8 \leq x \leq 2 Simplify.  Solve 2|x − 1| − 3 > 7. Isolate the absolute value first, then translate. (-∞, −4) ∪ (6, ∞)  2|x - 1| - 3 > 7 Start here. 2|x - 1| > 10 Add 3 to both sides. |x - 1| > 5 Divide by 2. x - 1 < -5  OR  x - 1 > 5 Apply outer-rays rule. x < -4  OR  x > 6 Solve each.
documents.proofs: The formal definition |x| = max(x, −x) captures the absolute value as the larger of the two candidates. In proof writing, this leads to case splits whenever absolute values appear. For example, to prove |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b|, you check four cases based on the signs of a and b. In each case, the absolute value symbols disappear, revealing a numerical inequality. In epsilon-delta arguments (later in calculus), absolute values represent distance tolerances. The statement "find δ such that |x − c| < δ implies |f(x) − L| < ε" is really asking: "Find a neighborhood around c (within distance δ) such that f maps it into a neighborhood around L (within distance ε)."
documents.applications: A manufacturing process must keep a steel rod at exactly 100 mm, but tolerances allow ±0.5 mm. A quality inspector writes: "accept if |length − 100| < 0.5." This is not algebra — it is a distance picture. The rod is "close enough to 100" if the distance from its actual length to 100 is smaller than the tolerance. This same language appears everywhere: temperature control, GPS accuracy, measurement uncertainty, and later in calculus in epsilon-delta proofs. Understanding the distance picture first makes the algebra automatic. Absolute Value: Formal Definition |x| = x if x ≥ 0, and |x| = −x if x < 0. This removes the sign, keeping only the distance from zero. For any a and r: |x − a| < r ⟺ a − r < x < a + r. The Distance Picture Think of the number line. |x − a| is the distance from x to a. |x − a| < r means "x is less than radius r away from center a." On a number line, that is the interval (a − r, a + r). Two Inequalities in One The compound inequality a − r < x < a + r is a shorthand for: (x > a − r) AND (x < a + r). Both conditions must hold. When writing solutions, the interval notation [a − r, a + r] is cleaner. |x| Does NOT Mean "Make it Positive" It means "measure the distance to zero." The result is always non-negative (≥ 0), but the operation is geometric, not "make positive." This distinction matters when you have expressions like |−3x|: it becomes 3|x|, not −3x with a positive sign.
documents.content: Why is |x − a| < r the same as "x is within distance r of the point a"? A manufacturing process must keep a steel rod at exactly 100 mm, but tolerances allow ±0.5 mm. A quality inspector writes: "accept if |length − 100| < 0.5." This is not algebra — it is a distance picture. The rod is "close enough to 100" if the distance from its actual length to 100 is smaller than the tolerance. This same language appears everywhere: temperature control, GPS accuracy, measurement uncertainty, and later in calculus in epsilon-delta proofs. Understanding the distance picture first makes the algebra automatic. Absolute value answers one question: **How far is this number from zero?** |5| = 5 because 5 is five units to the right of zero. |−5| = 5 because −5 is five units to the left of zero. Both are the same distance away. More generally, |x − a| asks: **How far is x from the point a?** This is the key insight that unlocks the whole topic. Forget the formula for a moment and just think of the picture: mark the point a on the number line, measure the distance to x, and strip away any directional sign. That distance is |x − a|. This geometric view immediately explains why |x − a| < r is equivalent to "x is within a circle of radius r centered at a." In one dimension, that "circle" is an interval: all points between a − r and a + r. Watch what happens: • |x| < 2 means x is within distance 2 of zero, so −2 < x < 2. • |x − 5| < 3 means x is within distance 3 of the point 5, so 2 < x < 8 (which is 5 − 3 < x < 5 + 3). • |x − (−1)| < 0.5 means x is within distance 0.5 of the point −1, so −1.5 < x < −0.5. This pattern works every time. Once you see it as a distance picture, the algebra is just bookkeeping. What about |x − a| > r? This is the complement: x is **not** close to a. Instead of one interval in the middle, you get two rays on the outside: x < a − r OR x > a + r. Multiplying by a negative number inside an absolute value is weird at first. But remember: |−x| means "the distance from −x to zero," which is the same as "the distance from x to zero." So |−x| = |x|. More generally, |−5x| = |5| · |−x| = 5|x|. The negative sign gets stripped away by the absolute value, leaving only magnitudes to multiply. From the distance picture, we derive algebraic rules. **Rule 1: |x − a| < r ⟺ a − r < x < a + r** This is the centered interval. The distance from x to a is less than r means x is trapped in the interval (a − r, a + r). Both directions work: if you start with the inequality a − r < x < a + r, you can reverse the algebra to get |x − a| < r. **Rule 2: |x − a| > r ⟺ x < a − r OR x > a + r** The opposite direction: x is far from a (distance more than r). This gives two separate regions on the number line, not one interval. They are disconnected. **Rule 3: |x − a| = r ⟺ x = a − r OR x = a + r** Equality case: x is exactly distance r from a. There are exactly two such points, one on each side. **Rule 4: |kx| = |k| · |x| for any constant k** Absolute values split products. This is crucial when solving inequalities involving coefficients. Example: |−3x| = |−3| · |x| = 3|x|. **Rule 5: |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b|** (Triangle Inequality) The distance from 0 to (a + b) is no more than the sum of distances from 0 to a and from 0 to b. Equality holds when a and b have the same sign (or one is zero). From these rules, most absolute value inequalities reduce to compound inequalities or case analysis. The formal definition |x| = max(x, −x) captures the absolute value as the larger of the two candidates. In proof writing, this leads to case splits whenever absolute values appear. For example, to prove |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b|, you check four cases based on the signs of a and b. In each case, the absolute value symbols disappear, revealing a numerical inequality. In epsilon-delta arguments (later in calculus), absolute values represent distance tolerances. The statement "find δ such that |x − c| < δ implies |f(x) − L| < ε" is really asking: "Find a neighborhood around c (within distance δ) such that f maps it into a neighborhood around L (within distance ε)." Absolute Value: Formal Definition |x| = x if x ≥ 0, and |x| = −x if x < 0. This removes the sign, keeping only the distance from zero. For any a and r: |x − a| < r ⟺ a − r < x < a + r. The Distance Picture Think of the number line. |x − a| is the distance from x to a. |x − a| < r means "x is less than radius r away from center a." On a number line, that is the interval (a − r, a + r). Two Inequalities in One The compound inequality a − r < x < a + r is a shorthand for: (x > a − r) AND (x < a + r). Both conditions must hold. When writing solutions, the interval notation [a − r, a + r] is cleaner. |x| Does NOT Mean "Make it Positive" It means "measure the distance to zero." The result is always non-negative (≥ 0), but the operation is geometric, not "make positive." This distinction matters when you have expressions like |−3x|: it becomes 3|x|, not −3x with a positive sign. The Core Equivalences Memorize these: |x − a| < r ⟺ a − r < x < a + r.  |x − a| > r ⟺ x < a − r OR x > a + r. Solving |expression| < bound (1) Rewrite as: −bound < expression < bound. (2) Solve both inequalities separately. (3) Combine with AND. Solving |expression| > bound (1) Split into two cases: expression < −bound OR expression > bound. (2) Solve each separately. (3) Combine with OR. Reverse Triangle Inequality ||a| - |b|| \leq |a - b|. \text{ The difference of distances is at most the distance between points.} \\ \text{Useful in ε–δ proofs when you need a lower bound on } |f(x) - L|. Number Line with Distance Intervals Drag points to explore |x − a|, |x − a| < r, and |x − a| > r as distance geometry. Basic Absolute Value — Distance from Zero Solve |x| < 3. The solution is all x between −3 and 3 (not including the endpoints). |x| < 3 Distance from x to 0 is less than 3. -3 < x < 3 Translate to interval: x is in (−3, 3). Distance from a Point Solve |x − 5| < 2. x is between 3 and 7. Geometrically: x is within distance 2 of the point 5. |x − 5| < 2 Distance from x to 5 is less than 2. 5 - 2 < x < 5 + 2 Centered interval: (a − r, a + r) with a=5, r=2. 3 < x < 7 Simplify. The Outer Rays Case Solve |x − 2| > 1. x is either less than 1 or greater than 3. Geometrically: x is more than distance 1 away from 2, so it lives in the two rays on either side. |x − 2| > 1 Distance from x to 2 is more than 1. x - 2 < -1  OR  x - 2 > 1 Split into two cases. x < 1  OR  x > 3 Solve each inequality. With a Coefficient Solve |3x − 6| < 9. The solution is the interval (−1, 5). |3x - 6| < 9 Factor out the 3 from inside: |3(x − 2)| = 3|x − 2|. 3|x - 2| < 9 Use Rule 4. |x - 2| < 3 Divide both sides by 3. 2 - 3 < x < 2 + 3 Translate to interval centered at 2 with radius 3. -1 < x < 5 Simplify. Manufacturing Tolerance A machine produces bolts that should be 50 mm long, with tolerance ±0.2 mm. Write and solve the absolute value inequality for acceptable lengths. Accept bolts between 49.8 mm and 50.2 mm. This is a real-world absolute value inequality. |length - 50| \leq 0.2 Distance from measured length to target is at most tolerance. 50 - 0.2 \leq length \leq 50 + 0.2 Substitute into the equivalence. 49.8 \leq length \leq 50.2 Simplify. Nonlinear: |x² − 4| < 5 Solve $|x^2 - 4| < 5$. The solution is $(-3, 3)$. The nonlinear case still reduces to center ± radius once the compound inequality is unpacked. -5 < x^2 - 4 < 5 $|A| < 5$ means $-5 < A < 5$. Rewrite as a compound double inequality. -1 < x^2 < 9 Add 4 to all three parts. x^2 > -1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{always true (squares are non-negative)} Left inequality: ignore it — x² is never negative. x^2 < 9 \;\Rightarrow\; |x| < 3 \;\Rightarrow\; -3 < x < 3 Right inequality: x² < 9 means |x| < 3. Solve |x + 3| ≤ 5. Hint: rewrite as |x − (−3)| first. Treat as distance from x to −3. [-8, 2] |x - (-3)| \leq 5 Rewrite |x + 3| as distance from x to −3. -3 - 5 \leq x \leq -3 + 5 Apply the equivalence. -8 \leq x \leq 2 Simplify. Solve 2|x − 1| − 3 > 7. Isolate the absolute value first, then translate. (-∞, −4) ∪ (6, ∞) 2|x - 1| - 3 > 7 Start here. 2|x - 1| > 10 Add 3 to both sides. |x - 1| > 5 Divide by 2. x - 1 < -5  OR  x - 1 > 5 Apply outer-rays rule. x < -4  OR  x > 6 Solve each. Real Numbers & Intervals Absolute value measures distance on the number line. Epsilon-Delta Proofs Absolute value inequalities like |x − c| < δ encode distance tolerance. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Algebraic Techniques for Limits
documents.subtitle: Factoring, conjugates, and completing the square — the moves that solve indeterminate forms
documents.tags: algebra factoring conjugate completing the square limits
