documents.title: Algebra: The Parts That Trip Everyone Up
documents.tags: algebra common-mistakes factoring inequalities absolute-value
documents.assignment: Solving a Quadratic by Factoring Solve x² - 5x + 6 = 0    x^2 - 5x + 6 Need two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to -5. -2 \times -3 = 6, \quad -2 + (-3) = -5 Those numbers are -2 and -3. (x-2)(x-3) = 0 Factor. x = 2 \text{ or } x = 3 Zero product property: if AB=0, then A=0 or B=0. Absolute Value Inequality Solve |2x - 1| ≤ 7    -7 \leq 2x - 1 \leq 7 |expr| ≤ k means -k ≤ expr ≤ k. Write as compound inequality. -6 \leq 2x \leq 8 Add 1 to all three parts. -3 \leq x \leq 4 Divide by 2 (positive, no flip). Solution is [-3, 4]. Completing the Square Rewrite x² + 6x + 5 in vertex form.    x^2 + 6x + \_ + 5 Identify the coefficient of x: it's 6. Half of 6 is 3. 3²=9. x^2 + 6x + 9 - 9 + 5 Add and subtract 9 (add zero in disguise). (x+3)^2 - 4 The first three terms form a perfect square. Vertex is at (-3, -4). Rational Expression Simplification Simplify (x²-4)/(x²-x-2)    \frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{(x-2)(x+1)} Factor numerator (diff. of squares) and denominator (trinomial). = \frac{x+2}{x+1} \quad (x \neq 2) Cancel (x-2). Note: x≠2 because that would cause division by zero in the original.  Solve the inequality (x-1)(x+3) > 0 Find where each factor is zero (the critical points), then test the three resulting intervals. x ∈ (-∞, -3) ∪ (1, ∞)  x = 1 \text{ or } x = -3 \text{ are critical points} Each factor is zero at these values — the sign can only change here. x < -3: (-)(-)=+>0 \checkmark \quad -3<x<1: (+)(-)=-<0 \text{ ✗} \quad x>1:(+)(+)=+>0 \checkmark Test a value in each interval. x \in (-\infty, -3) \cup (1, \infty) Solution is both outer intervals (not including endpoints since strict >).  Factor completely: 2x³ - 8x Always pull out GCF first. 2x(x-2)(x+2)  2x(x^2 - 4) GCF is 2x. Factor it out. 2x(x-2)(x+2) x²-4 is a difference of squares. Factor again. This is fully factored.

documents.title: Geometry: Seeing Why It's True
documents.tags: geometry proofs visual area circles triangles
documents.aliases: rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof
documents.assignment: Finding Missing Side with Pythagorean Theorem A right triangle has legs 5 and 12. Find the hypotenuse.    a^2 + b^2 = c^2 Apply the Pythagorean theorem. 5^2 + 12^2 = c^2 Substitute the known legs. 25 + 144 = 169 = c^2 Calculate. c = \sqrt{169} = 13 This is a classic 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple. Similar Triangles — Finding Unknown Lengths Triangle ABC ~ Triangle DEF. AB=6, BC=8, DE=9. Find EF.    \frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} Corresponding sides of similar triangles are proportional. \frac{6}{9} = \frac{8}{EF} Substitute known values. EF = \frac{8 \times 9}{6} = 12 Cross-multiply and solve. Inscribed Angle A central angle subtends an arc of 80°. What is the inscribed angle on the same arc?    \text{Inscribed angle} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{central angle} Inscribed Angle Theorem. = \frac{1}{2} \times 80° = 40° The inscribed angle is always half the central angle.  A square has diagonal length 10. What is its side length and area? The diagonal of a square forms a right triangle with two equal legs. Side = 5√2 ≈ 7.07 units, Area = 50 square units  d = s\sqrt{2} \Rightarrow s = \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} For a square with side s, diagonal = s√2. s = \frac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2} Substitute d=10. A = s^2 = (5\sqrt{2})^2 = 50 Area is 50 square units.  Prove that the three medians of a triangle always meet at one point (the centroid). Use vectors or coordinate geometry — place the triangle at convenient coordinates. The centroid G divides each median in ratio 2:1 from vertex to midpoint.  A=(0,0),\ B=(2b,0),\ C=(2c,2d) Place triangle with convenient coordinates. M_{AB}=(b,0),\ M_{BC}=(b+c,d),\ M_{AC}=(c,d) Find midpoints of each side. G = \left(\frac{0+2b+2c}{3}, \frac{0+0+2d}{3}\right) Each median's 2/3 point gives the same G. All three medians meet there.

documents.assignment: When Direct Substitution Works Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 4} \frac{x^2 - 3x + 1}{x + 2}.   The limit is 5/6. Direct substitution works whenever the denominator is nonzero at the target point — which is exactly the definition of the function being continuous there. \lim_{x \to 4} \frac{x^2 - 3x + 1}{x + 2} Check: is the denominator zero at x = 4? x + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 ≠ 0. Direct substitution is valid. = \frac{(4)^2 - 3(4) + 1}{(4) + 2} Substitute x = 4 everywhere. = \frac{16 - 12 + 1}{6} Arithmetic in numerator: 16 − 12 = 4, then 4 + 1 = 5. = \frac{5}{6}  Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Factoring \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}   The limit is 6. The original function has a "hole" at x = 3 (it's undefined there), but the limit fills in that hole: the function wants to be 6 at x = 3, even though it isn't defined there. \lim_{x \to 3} \frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3} Try direct substitution: numerator = 9−9 = 0, denominator = 3−3 = 0. This is the indeterminate form 0/0. We cannot divide by zero — but we can factor. = \lim_{x \to 3} \frac{(x+3)(x-3)}{x-3} Factor numerator: x²−9 = (x−3)(x+3). Recognize this as a difference of squares: a²−b² = (a−b)(a+b) with a=x, b=3. = \lim_{x \to 3} (x + 3) Cancel (x−3). This cancellation is VALID because in a limit, x → 3 but x ≠ 3, so (x−3) ≠ 0 and we can divide by it. = 3 + 3 = 6 Now substitute x = 3. No problem — the simplified function x+3 is defined there. Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Rationalizing \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x}   The limit is 1/4. The conjugate technique is the go-to method whenever you have (√something − constant) or (√something + constant) in the numerator or denominator. \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x} Direct sub gives 0/0: numerator = √4 − 2 = 0, denominator = 0. Factoring won't help here. Strategy: multiply by the conjugate to eliminate the square root. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{x+4} + 2}{\sqrt{x+4} + 2} Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the numerator: (√(x+4) + 2). This equals 1, so we haven't changed the expression. \text{Numerator: } (\sqrt{x+4} - 2)(\sqrt{x+4} + 2) = (\sqrt{x+4})^2 - 2^2 Difference of squares pattern: (a−b)(a+b) = a²−b² with a = √(x+4), b = 2. = (x+4) - 4 = x The square root disappears! (√(x+4))² = x+4 and 2² = 4. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x}{x\,(\sqrt{x+4} + 2)} Numerator is now just x. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+4} + 2} Cancel x (valid since x ≠ 0 in the limit). = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0+4} + 2} = \frac{1}{2 + 2} = \frac{1}{4} Now substitute x = 0 safely. One-Sided Limits from a Piecewise Function Find both one-sided limits of f(x) = \begin{cases} x+1 & x < 2 \\ 5 & x = 2 \\ x^2 - 1 & x > 2 \end{cases} at x = 2. Does the two-sided limit exist? Is f continuous at x = 2?   The limit exists (= 3), but f is not continuous because f(2) = 5 ≠ 3. This is a "removable" discontinuity — redefining f(2) = 3 would make it continuous. \lim_{x \to 2^-} f(x) Approaching from the left means x < 2, so use the first piece: f(x) = x + 1. = \lim_{x \to 2^-} (x+1) = 2 + 1 = 3  \lim_{x \to 2^+} f(x) Approaching from the right means x > 2, so use the third piece: f(x) = x² − 1. = \lim_{x \to 2^+} (x^2-1) = 4 - 1 = 3  \lim_{x \to 2^-} f(x) = 3 = \lim_{x \to 2^+} f(x) Both one-sided limits are equal. \therefore \lim_{x \to 2} f(x) = 3 Two-sided limit exists and equals 3. f(2) = 5 \quad \text{(from the middle piece)} The function value at x = 2 is explicitly defined as 5. \lim_{x \to 2} f(x) = 3 \neq 5 = f(2) The limit (3) does not equal the function value (5). \therefore f \text{ is NOT continuous at } x = 2. Continuity requires that the limit equals the function value. Here they differ. A Limit That Does Not Exist — Jump Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{|x|}{x}.   This function is the "sign function" — it equals +1 for positive x and −1 for negative x. It has a jump discontinuity at x = 0, which is why the limit fails to exist. \text{Recall: } |x| = \begin{cases} x & x > 0 \\ -x & x < 0 \end{cases} The absolute value function has different formulas depending on the sign of x. \lim_{x \to 0^-} \frac{|x|}{x}: For x < 0 (approaching from the left), |x| = −x. = \lim_{x \to 0^-} \frac{-x}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0^-} (-1) = -1 −x/x = −1 for all x ≠ 0. \lim_{x \to 0^+} \frac{|x|}{x}: For x > 0 (approaching from the right), |x| = x. = \lim_{x \to 0^+} \frac{x}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0^+} (1) = 1 x/x = 1 for all x ≠ 0. \lim_{x \to 0^-} = -1 \neq 1 = \lim_{x \to 0^+} The two one-sided limits are different. \therefore \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{|x|}{x} \text{ DNE} Since the one-sided limits disagree, the two-sided limit does not exist. Limit at Infinity — Horizontal Asymptote Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{3x^2 - 2x + 1}{5x^2 + 4x - 7}.   The limit is 3/5. This means the function has a horizontal asymptote at y = 3/5. Quick rule for rational functions: when the degrees are equal, the limit at ±∞ is the ratio of the leading coefficients. \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{3x^2 - 2x + 1}{5x^2 + 4x - 7} Both numerator and denominator grow without bound — this is the indeterminate form ∞/∞. Strategy: divide every term by the highest power of x in the denominator, which is x². = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{\dfrac{3x^2}{x^2} - \dfrac{2x}{x^2} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{\dfrac{5x^2}{x^2} + \dfrac{4x}{x^2} - \dfrac{7}{x^2}} Divide every term (numerator and denominator) by x². = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{3 - \dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 + \dfrac{4}{x} - \dfrac{7}{x^2}} Simplify each fraction. \text{As } x \to \infty:\quad \frac{1}{x} \to 0, \quad \frac{1}{x^2} \to 0 Terms with x in the denominator vanish as x grows. = \frac{3 - 0 + 0}{5 + 0 - 0} = \frac{3}{5}  An Epsilon-Delta Proof Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove that \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 3} (5x - 7) = 8.   The scratchwork shows you what δ to choose. The formal proof then chains the inequalities to verify the choice works. For any tolerance ε in the output, choosing δ = ε/5 guarantees f(x) lands within ε of 8. \text{Claim: } \lim_{x \to 3}(5x-7) = 8. We must prove: for every ε > 0, we can find δ > 0 such that 0 < |x−3| < δ implies |(5x−7) − 8| < ε. \text{Scratchwork: simplify the output error.} Before writing the proof, figure out what δ should be. |(5x-7) - 8| = |5x - 15| = 5|x - 3| |5x − 15| = |5(x−3)| = 5|x−3|. The output error is exactly 5 times the input error. \text{We need } 5|x-3| < \varepsilon \iff |x-3| < \frac{\varepsilon}{5} So choosing δ = ε/5 will work. \textbf{Proof: }\text{Given } \varepsilon > 0, \text{ let } \delta = \frac{\varepsilon}{5}. This is the start of the formal proof. \text{Suppose } 0 < |x-3| < \delta. Assume x is within δ of 3 but x ≠ 3. \text{Then: } |(5x-7) - 8| = 5|x-3| < 5\delta = 5 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{5} = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare Chain the inequalities: output error = 5 × input error < 5δ = ε. QED. Average Velocity → Instantaneous Velocity (Physics) A ball is dropped from rest at a height of 80 ft. Its height (in feet) is $h(t) = 80 - 16t^2$ where $t$ is in seconds. (a) Compute the average velocity over $[1,\; 1+\Delta t]$ for $\Delta t = 1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01$. (b) What value does the average velocity approach as $\Delta t \to 0$? (c) Compute the limit directly.   The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is −32 ft/s (the ball is falling at 32 ft/s). The negative sign means the height is decreasing — the ball is moving downward. This is precisely the derivative h'(1) = −32t|_{t=1} = −32, which we'll compute using rules in Chapter 2. The limit is the engine that converts "average over an interval" into "instantaneous at a point." \text{Avg velocity} = \frac{h(1 + \Delta t) - h(1)}{\Delta t} Average velocity = change in height ÷ change in time. This is the difference quotient — the slope of the secant line. h(1) = 80 - 16(1)^2 = 64 \text{ ft} Height at t=1 second. h(1 + \Delta t) = 80 - 16(1+\Delta t)^2 = 80 - 16(1 + 2\Delta t + \Delta t^2) Expand (1+Δt)². = 64 - 32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2 Distribute and simplify: 80 - 16 = 64. \frac{h(1+Delta t)-h(1)}{\Delta t} = \frac{(64-32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2) - 64}{\Delta t} = \frac{-32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2}{\Delta t} Subtract h(1)=64 and form the quotient. = \frac{\Delta t(-32 - 16\Delta t)}{\Delta t} = -32 - 16\Delta t Factor Δt from numerator and cancel (valid for Δt ≠ 0). \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}(-32 - 16\Delta t) = -32 \text{ ft/s} As Δt → 0, the term −16Δt → 0. The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is exactly −32 ft/s. \Delta t=1:\;-48,\quad \Delta t=0.5:\;-40,\quad \Delta t=0.1:\;-33.6,\quad \Delta t=0.01:\;-32.16 Numerical check: the average velocities converge to −32 ft/s from above.  Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x^3 - 1}{x^2 - 1}. Factor both numerator and denominator completely. Sum of cubes: a³−b³ = (a−b)(a²+ab+b²). Difference of squares: a²−b² = (a−b)(a+b). 3/2  \lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x^3-1}{x^2-1} Direct substitution gives 0/0. Factor both. = \lim_{x \to 1} \frac{(x-1)(x^2+x+1)}{(x-1)(x+1)} x³−1 = (x−1)(x²+x+1) [sum of cubes]; x²−1 = (x−1)(x+1) [difference of squares]. = \lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x^2+x+1}{x+1} Cancel (x−1), valid since x ≠ 1. = \frac{1+1+1}{1+1} = \frac{3}{2} Substitute x = 1.  Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(\sqrt{x^2 + x} - x\right). This is ∞ − ∞, an indeterminate form. Rationalize by multiplying by the conjugate: (√(x²+x) + x)/(√(x²+x) + x). 1/2  \lim_{x \to \infty}(\sqrt{x^2+x} - x) ∞ − ∞ is indeterminate. Multiply by the conjugate. = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{(\sqrt{x^2+x}-x)(\sqrt{x^2+x}+x)}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x}  = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{(x^2+x) - x^2}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x} Numerator: (√(x²+x))² − x² = x²+x − x² = x. = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x}  = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{x}{x\sqrt{1+1/x}+x} Factor x from the denominator: √(x²+x) = x√(1+1/x) for x > 0. = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+1/x}+1} Cancel x from numerator and denominator. = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+0}+1} = \frac{1}{2} 1/x → 0 as x → ∞.  Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} x^2 = 4. Write |x²−4| = |x−2||x+2|. You need to bound |x+2|. Restrict δ ≤ 1 first (so x ∈ (1,3)), which bounds |x+2| ≤ 5. Proved via ε-δ with δ = min(1, ε/5)  |x^2 - 4| = |(x-2)(x+2)| = |x-2|\cdot|x+2| Factor and use the multiplicative property of absolute value. \text{We need to bound } |x+2|. It depends on x, so we restrict x to a bounded neighborhood first. \text{Restrict: assume } \delta \leq 1,\; \text{so } |x-2|<1 \implies 1 < x < 3. With δ ≤ 1, we know x is between 1 and 3. \text{Then } 3 < x+2 < 5 \implies |x+2| < 5.  \text{So: } |x^2-4| = |x-2|\cdot|x+2| < 5|x-2|  \text{We need } 5|x-2| < \varepsilon \iff |x-2| < \varepsilon/5  \text{Choose } \delta = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\varepsilon}{5}\right) Take the smaller of 1 (to keep |x+2| < 5) and ε/5 (to make the product small). \text{Proof: Given } \varepsilon>0, \text{ let } \delta=\min(1,\varepsilon/5).  \text{If } 0<|x-2|<\delta: \quad |x^2-4| = |x-2||x+2| < \delta\cdot 5 \leq \frac{\varepsilon}{5}\cdot 5 = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare
documents.proofs: The informal idea "f(x) gets close to L as x gets close to c" is made mathematically precise by the **epsilon-delta definition**, developed by Augustin-Louis Cauchy and Karl Weierstrass in the 19th century. Before this definition, calculus was powerful but logically shaky — mathematicians could compute correctly but couldn't fully justify why. The definition uses two small positive numbers: **ε (epsilon)**: a small tolerance on the *output*. We specify how close f(x) must be to L. **δ (delta)**: a small tolerance on the *input*. This is what we choose to guarantee the output tolerance. The game works like this: an adversary picks any ε > 0 (no matter how tiny). You must respond with a δ > 0 such that: whenever x is within δ of c (but x ≠ c), f(x) is within ε of L. If you can always win this game — for every possible ε — then the limit is L. The condition |x − c| < δ (with x ≠ c, written 0 < |x − c| < δ) translates to "x is within δ of c but not equal to c." The condition |f(x) − L| < ε translates to "f(x) is within ε of L." Crucially, δ is allowed to depend on ε. For a linear function like f(x) = 2x + 1, if you want |f(x) − 5| < ε near c = 2, you can set δ = ε/2. For nonlinear functions, finding δ requires more algebra — but the definition guarantees one exists whenever the limit truly holds. Epsilon-Delta Definition of a Limit \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L \iff \forall\, \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta > 0 \text{ such that:} \\ 0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon How to read the definition "For EVERY ε > 0 (no matter how tiny the demanded accuracy), there EXISTS a δ > 0 (some input radius) such that: for ALL x within δ of c (but x ≠ c), f(x) is within ε of L."
documents.applications: A speedometer measures average speed: distance divided by time. Over a one-hour drive you might average 60 mph. Over the last 10 minutes you averaged 70 mph. Over the last 1 second you averaged 72 mph. But what is your speed at the precise instant t = 3.00000 seconds? To measure it, you'd need zero time interval — giving 0 miles / 0 seconds = 0/0, which is undefined. Yet your car's speedometer reads a definite value at every instant. Physics tells us instantaneous speed is real. The resolution to this paradox is the concept of a **limit**: we don't evaluate at the instant, we ask what value the average speed *approaches* as the time interval shrinks toward zero. This is the central idea of calculus, discovered independently by Newton (who called it a "fluxion") and Leibniz in the 1600s. You may have seen average speed before Average speed = distance ÷ time. You calculated this in physics class. The limit is what happens when you let that time interval shrink toward zero — turning "average speed over 1 second" into "speed at one instant." That is exactly what a speedometer reads. The Core Idea $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ means $f(x)$ gets arbitrarily close to $L$ whenever $x$ is sufficiently close to $c$ (but $x \neq c$). The value of $f$ at $c$ is irrelevant. Indeterminate Forms 0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞−∞ are called "indeterminate forms." They do NOT mean the limit is 0 or undefined — they mean we need more work. (x²−4)/(x−2) → 0/0 at x=2, but the limit is 4. Cancel Factors, Not Terms You may cancel only common factors, never added/subtracted terms. Example: (x^2-1)/(x-1) simplifies after factoring, but (x+1)/(x-1) does not let you cancel x. 0/0 Triage When substitution gives 0/0, try this order: (1) factor and cancel, (2) multiply by a conjugate, (3) combine to a common denominator, then re-evaluate. The Limit is NOT the Function Value lim(x→c) f(x) can exist even when f(c) doesn't. And even when f(c) does exist, the limit can be a DIFFERENT number. The limit only cares about what happens NEAR c, not AT c.
documents.content:
What is the speed of a car at exactly one instant in time? A speedometer measures average speed: distance divided by time. Over a one-hour drive you might average 60 mph. Over the last 10 minutes you averaged 70 mph. Over the last 1 second you averaged 72 mph. But what is your speed at the precise instant t = 3.00000 seconds? To measure it, you'd need zero time interval — giving 0 miles / 0 seconds = 0/0, which is undefined. Yet your car's speedometer reads a definite value at every instant. Physics tells us instantaneous speed is real. The resolution to this paradox is the concept of a **limit**: we don't evaluate at the instant, we ask what value the average speed *approaches* as the time interval shrinks toward zero. This is the central idea of calculus, discovered independently by Newton (who called it a "fluxion") and Leibniz in the 1600s. **You have already met the seed of the limit.** In Chapter 0, you learned that the slope of a line is Δy / Δx — the ratio of rise to run. That slope is the rate of change averaged over the full run. Now ask: what if the run shrinks? What if Δx keeps getting smaller and smaller, never quite reaching zero? The number you are approaching is the limit. This is not a brand new idea — it is the slope idea taken to its extreme. Imagine walking toward a wall, cutting the remaining distance in half each step. You get closer and closer — 1 m, 0.5 m, 0.25 m, 0.125 m — but you never quite touch it. A **limit** describes the value a function approaches as its input gets close to some target value. The key insight: the function does not need to be defined *at* the target — only *near* it. Consider the function f(x) = (x² − 4)/(x − 2). At x = 2, we get 0/0, which is completely undefined. But watch what happens when x gets close to 2 from either side: **From the left (x → 2⁻):**  x = 1.9 → f = 3.9,  x = 1.99 → f = 3.99,  x = 1.999 → f = 3.999 **From the right (x → 2⁺):**  x = 2.1 → f = 4.1,  x = 2.01 → f = 4.01,  x = 2.001 → f = 4.001 From both sides, f(x) is approaching 4. We write: lim(x→2) (x²−4)/(x−2) = 4. The limit is 4, even though f(2) is undefined. Why can we simplify (x²−4)/(x−2)? Because x²−4 = (x+2)(x−2), and for x ≠ 2 we can cancel: (x+2)(x−2)/(x−2) = x+2. At x = 2 this gives 4. The limit captured the "natural" value of this function at the hole. **The limit is about the journey, not the destination.** Whether f(2) is undefined, or defined but equal to something else — the limit doesn't care. It only asks: what value does f(x) approach as x gets close to 2? There is also the possibility of **one-sided limits**: the function might approach a different value from the left than from the right. If that happens, the two-sided limit does not exist (DNE). Think of a cliff edge: approaching from land gives height H, approaching from sea gives height 0. The "height" limit at the cliff edge DNE. The formal limit notation is $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L$, read "the limit of $f(x)$ as $x$ approaches $c$ equals $L$." **One-sided limits**: We distinguish approaching from the left versus the right. The **left-hand limit** $\lim_{x 	o c^-} f(x) = L_-$ means $x$ approaches $c$ while staying strictly less than $c$. The **right-hand limit** $\lim_{x 	o c^+} f(x) = L_+$ means $x$ approaches $c$ while staying strictly greater than $c$. The two-sided limit exists if and only if both one-sided limits exist AND they produce the same value: $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L \iff \lim_{x 	o c^-} f(x) = L = \lim_{x 	o c^+} f(x)$. When either side fails to exist, or they produce different values, we say the limit **Does Not Exist (DNE)**. **Three techniques for evaluating limits** when direct substitution fails (producing $0/0$ or similar indeterminate forms): 1. **Factoring and canceling**: factor the numerator or denominator to expose a common factor that can be cancelled. Works whenever the numerator and denominator share a root at $c$. 2. **Rationalizing** (multiplying by conjugate): used when a square root creates the $0/0$ form. Multiply top and bottom by $(\sqrt{\cdot} + k)$ to clear the radical via the difference-of-squares identity. 3. **The Squeeze Theorem**: if you cannot simplify $f(x)$ directly, but can trap it between two simpler functions that share the same limit, then $f$ is squeezed to that same value. Used for the fundamental trig limits. **Limits at infinity**: $\lim_{x 	o \infty} f(x) = L$ means $f(x)$ approaches $L$ as $x$ grows without bound — this describes **horizontal asymptotes**. For rational functions, divide every term by the highest power of $x$ in the denominator, then let $1/x 	o 0$. The informal idea "f(x) gets close to L as x gets close to c" is made mathematically precise by the **epsilon-delta definition**, developed by Augustin-Louis Cauchy and Karl Weierstrass in the 19th century. Before this definition, calculus was powerful but logically shaky — mathematicians could compute correctly but couldn't fully justify why. The definition uses two small positive numbers: **ε (epsilon)**: a small tolerance on the *output*. We specify how close f(x) must be to L. **δ (delta)**: a small tolerance on the *input*. This is what we choose to guarantee the output tolerance. The game works like this: an adversary picks any ε > 0 (no matter how tiny). You must respond with a δ > 0 such that: whenever x is within δ of c (but x ≠ c), f(x) is within ε of L. If you can always win this game — for every possible ε — then the limit is L. The condition |x − c| < δ (with x ≠ c, written 0 < |x − c| < δ) translates to "x is within δ of c but not equal to c." The condition |f(x) − L| < ε translates to "f(x) is within ε of L." Crucially, δ is allowed to depend on ε. For a linear function like f(x) = 2x + 1, if you want |f(x) − 5| < ε near c = 2, you can set δ = ε/2. For nonlinear functions, finding δ requires more algebra — but the definition guarantees one exists whenever the limit truly holds. Epsilon-Delta Definition of a Limit \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L \iff  orall\, \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta > 0 	ext{ such that:} \ 0 < |x - c| < \delta \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon How to read the definition "For EVERY ε > 0 (no matter how tiny the demanded accuracy), there EXISTS a δ > 0 (some input radius) such that: for ALL x within δ of c (but x ≠ c), f(x) is within ε of L." You may have seen average speed before Average speed = distance ÷ time. You calculated this in physics class. The limit is what happens when you let that time interval shrink toward zero — turning "average speed over 1 second" into "speed at one instant." That is exactly what a speedometer reads. The Core Idea $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L$ means $f(x)$ gets arbitrarily close to $L$ whenever $x$ is sufficiently close to $c$ (but $x 
eq c$). The value of $f$ at $c$ is irrelevant. Indeterminate Forms 0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞−∞ are called "indeterminate forms." They do NOT mean the limit is 0 or undefined — they mean we need more work. (x²−4)/(x−2) → 0/0 at x=2, but the limit is 4. Cancel Factors, Not Terms You may cancel only common factors, never added/subtracted terms. Example: (x^2-1)/(x-1) simplifies after factoring, but (x+1)/(x-1) does not let you cancel x. 0/0 Triage When substitution gives 0/0, try this order: (1) factor and cancel, (2) multiply by a conjugate, (3) combine to a common denominator, then re-evaluate. The Limit is NOT the Function Value lim(x→c) f(x) can exist even when f(c) doesn't. And even when f(c) does exist, the limit can be a DIFFERENT number. The limit only cares about what happens NEAR c, not AT c. The Two-Sided Limit \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L \iff \lim_{x 	o c^-} f(x) = L = \lim_{x 	o c^+} f(x) When a Limit DNE \lim_{x	o c} f(x) 	ext{ DNE if } \lim_{x	o c^-} f(x) 
eq \lim_{x	o c^+} f(x), 	ext{ or if either one-sided limit fails to exist.} When can I just substitute? If f is a polynomial, rational function (with nonzero denominator at c), or built from trig/exp/log functions: just substitute x = c. Direct substitution works when the function is continuous at c. Limit Bridge Lab — Intuition to Rigor Control the approach distance h, watch both sides close on the same target, then test your own limit estimate in game mode. Zeno's Paradox — Infinite Steps, Finite Sum Each bar is half the previous. The total approaches 1 — an infinite process with a finite result. This is a limit. Approaching the Limit Watch f(x) = (x²−4)/(x−2) as x approaches 2 from both sides. The hole at x=2 is real, but the limit is 4. Racing Car Approaching a Hole A car drives along the function curve toward the hole at x=2. It gets arbitrarily close but never arrives — yet the limit is clear. Left-Hand and Right-Hand Limits Two particles approach from opposite sides. When they meet at the same value, the limit exists. When they land at different values — or one diverges — the limit does not exist. Limit vs. Function Value The table updates in sync with the graph: both sides approach 4, even though f(2) is undefined. Hole vs. Function Value — Three Cases Switch between three scenarios: undefined (hole), redefined (value ≠ limit), and continuous (value = limit). This is the core distinction calculus builds on. Epsilon-Delta in Action Drag ε to set the output tolerance. Watch the required δ adjust automatically. This is what "the limit is 5" means formally. When the Limit Does NOT Exist — sin(1/x) near 0 Zoom in toward x=0: the function never settles. No ε-δ box can trap it at a single value — the limit DNE. When Direct Substitution Works Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 4}  rac{x^2 - 3x + 1}{x + 2}. The limit is 5/6. Direct substitution works whenever the denominator is nonzero at the target point — which is exactly the definition of the function being continuous there. \lim_{x 	o 4}  rac{x^2 - 3x + 1}{x + 2} Check: is the denominator zero at x = 4? x + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 ≠ 0. Direct substitution is valid. =  rac{(4)^2 - 3(4) + 1}{(4) + 2} Substitute x = 4 everywhere. =  rac{16 - 12 + 1}{6} Arithmetic in numerator: 16 − 12 = 4, then 4 + 1 = 5. =  rac{5}{6} Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Factoring \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 3}  rac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3} The limit is 6. The original function has a "hole" at x = 3 (it's undefined there), but the limit fills in that hole: the function wants to be 6 at x = 3, even though it isn't defined there. \lim_{x 	o 3}  rac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3} Try direct substitution: numerator = 9−9 = 0, denominator = 3−3 = 0. This is the indeterminate form 0/0. We cannot divide by zero — but we can factor. = \lim_{x 	o 3}  rac{(x+3)(x-3)}{x-3} Factor numerator: x²−9 = (x−3)(x+3). Recognize this as a difference of squares: a²−b² = (a−b)(a+b) with a=x, b=3. = \lim_{x 	o 3} (x + 3) Cancel (x−3). This cancellation is VALID because in a limit, x → 3 but x ≠ 3, so (x−3) ≠ 0 and we can divide by it. = 3 + 3 = 6 Now substitute x = 3. No problem — the simplified function x+3 is defined there. Evaluating a 0/0 Form by Rationalizing \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x} The limit is 1/4. The conjugate technique is the go-to method whenever you have (√something − constant) or (√something + constant) in the numerator or denominator. \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x} Direct sub gives 0/0: numerator = √4 − 2 = 0, denominator = 0. Factoring won't help here. Strategy: multiply by the conjugate to eliminate the square root. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sqrt{x+4} - 2}{x} \cdot  rac{\sqrt{x+4} + 2}{\sqrt{x+4} + 2} Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate of the numerator: (√(x+4) + 2). This equals 1, so we haven't changed the expression. 	ext{Numerator: } (\sqrt{x+4} - 2)(\sqrt{x+4} + 2) = (\sqrt{x+4})^2 - 2^2 Difference of squares pattern: (a−b)(a+b) = a²−b² with a = √(x+4), b = 2. = (x+4) - 4 = x The square root disappears! (√(x+4))² = x+4 and 2² = 4. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{x}{x\,(\sqrt{x+4} + 2)} Numerator is now just x. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{1}{\sqrt{x+4} + 2} Cancel x (valid since x ≠ 0 in the limit). =  rac{1}{\sqrt{0+4} + 2} =  rac{1}{2 + 2} =  rac{1}{4} Now substitute x = 0 safely. One-Sided Limits from a Piecewise Function Find both one-sided limits of f(x) =  egin{cases} x+1 & x < 2 \ 5 & x = 2 \ x^2 - 1 & x > 2 \end{cases} at x = 2. Does the two-sided limit exist? Is f continuous at x = 2? The limit exists (= 3), but f is not continuous because f(2) = 5 ≠ 3. This is a "removable" discontinuity — redefining f(2) = 3 would make it continuous. \lim_{x 	o 2^-} f(x) Approaching from the left means x < 2, so use the first piece: f(x) = x + 1. = \lim_{x 	o 2^-} (x+1) = 2 + 1 = 3 \lim_{x 	o 2^+} f(x) Approaching from the right means x > 2, so use the third piece: f(x) = x² − 1. = \lim_{x 	o 2^+} (x^2-1) = 4 - 1 = 3 \lim_{x 	o 2^-} f(x) = 3 = \lim_{x 	o 2^+} f(x) Both one-sided limits are equal. 	herefore \lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) = 3 Two-sided limit exists and equals 3. f(2) = 5 \quad 	ext{(from the middle piece)} The function value at x = 2 is explicitly defined as 5. \lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) = 3 
eq 5 = f(2) The limit (3) does not equal the function value (5). 	herefore f 	ext{ is NOT continuous at } x = 2. Continuity requires that the limit equals the function value. Here they differ. A Limit That Does Not Exist — Jump Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{|x|}{x}. This function is the "sign function" — it equals +1 for positive x and −1 for negative x. It has a jump discontinuity at x = 0, which is why the limit fails to exist. 	ext{Recall: } |x| =  egin{cases} x & x > 0 \ -x & x < 0 \end{cases} The absolute value function has different formulas depending on the sign of x. \lim_{x 	o 0^-}  rac{|x|}{x}: For x < 0 (approaching from the left), |x| = −x. = \lim_{x 	o 0^-}  rac{-x}{x} = \lim_{x 	o 0^-} (-1) = -1 −x/x = −1 for all x ≠ 0. \lim_{x 	o 0^+}  rac{|x|}{x}: For x > 0 (approaching from the right), |x| = x. = \lim_{x 	o 0^+}  rac{x}{x} = \lim_{x 	o 0^+} (1) = 1 x/x = 1 for all x ≠ 0. \lim_{x 	o 0^-} = -1 
eq 1 = \lim_{x 	o 0^+} The two one-sided limits are different. 	herefore \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{|x|}{x} 	ext{ DNE} Since the one-sided limits disagree, the two-sided limit does not exist. Limit at Infinity — Horizontal Asymptote Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{3x^2 - 2x + 1}{5x^2 + 4x - 7}. The limit is 3/5. This means the function has a horizontal asymptote at y = 3/5. Quick rule for rational functions: when the degrees are equal, the limit at ±∞ is the ratio of the leading coefficients. \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{3x^2 - 2x + 1}{5x^2 + 4x - 7} Both numerator and denominator grow without bound — this is the indeterminate form ∞/∞. Strategy: divide every term by the highest power of x in the denominator, which is x². = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{\dfrac{3x^2}{x^2} - \dfrac{2x}{x^2} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{\dfrac{5x^2}{x^2} + \dfrac{4x}{x^2} - \dfrac{7}{x^2}} Divide every term (numerator and denominator) by x². = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{3 - \dfrac{2}{x} + \dfrac{1}{x^2}}{5 + \dfrac{4}{x} - \dfrac{7}{x^2}} Simplify each fraction. 	ext{As } x 	o \infty:\quad  rac{1}{x} 	o 0, \quad  rac{1}{x^2} 	o 0 Terms with x in the denominator vanish as x grows. =  rac{3 - 0 + 0}{5 + 0 - 0} =  rac{3}{5} An Epsilon-Delta Proof Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove that \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 3} (5x - 7) = 8. The scratchwork shows you what δ to choose. The formal proof then chains the inequalities to verify the choice works. For any tolerance ε in the output, choosing δ = ε/5 guarantees f(x) lands within ε of 8. 	ext{Claim: } \lim_{x 	o 3}(5x-7) = 8. We must prove: for every ε > 0, we can find δ > 0 such that 0 < |x−3| < δ implies |(5x−7) − 8| < ε. 	ext{Scratchwork: simplify the output error.} Before writing the proof, figure out what δ should be. |(5x-7) - 8| = |5x - 15| = 5|x - 3| |5x − 15| = |5(x−3)| = 5|x−3|. The output error is exactly 5 times the input error. 	ext{We need } 5|x-3| < \varepsilon \iff |x-3| <  rac{\varepsilon}{5} So choosing δ = ε/5 will work. 	extbf{Proof: }	ext{Given } \varepsilon > 0, 	ext{ let } \delta =  rac{\varepsilon}{5}. This is the start of the formal proof. 	ext{Suppose } 0 < |x-3| < \delta. Assume x is within δ of 3 but x ≠ 3. 	ext{Then: } |(5x-7) - 8| = 5|x-3| < 5\delta = 5 \cdot  rac{\varepsilon}{5} = \varepsilon.\; lacksquare Chain the inequalities: output error = 5 × input error < 5δ = ε. QED. Average Velocity → Instantaneous Velocity (Physics) A ball is dropped from rest at a height of 80 ft. Its height (in feet) is $h(t) = 80 - 16t^2$ where $t$ is in seconds. (a) Compute the average velocity over $[1,\; 1+\Delta t]$ for $\Delta t = 1, 0.5, 0.1, 0.01$. (b) What value does the average velocity approach as $\Delta t 	o 0$? (c) Compute the limit directly. The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is −32 ft/s (the ball is falling at 32 ft/s). The negative sign means the height is decreasing — the ball is moving downward. This is precisely the derivative h'(1) = −32t|_{t=1} = −32, which we'll compute using rules in Chapter 2. The limit is the engine that converts "average over an interval" into "instantaneous at a point." 	ext{Avg velocity} =  rac{h(1 + \Delta t) - h(1)}{\Delta t} Average velocity = change in height ÷ change in time. This is the difference quotient — the slope of the secant line. h(1) = 80 - 16(1)^2 = 64 	ext{ ft} Height at t=1 second. h(1 + \Delta t) = 80 - 16(1+\Delta t)^2 = 80 - 16(1 + 2\Delta t + \Delta t^2) Expand (1+Δt)². = 64 - 32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2 Distribute and simplify: 80 - 16 = 64.  rac{h(1+Delta t)-h(1)}{\Delta t} =  rac{(64-32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2) - 64}{\Delta t} =  rac{-32\Delta t - 16\Delta t^2}{\Delta t} Subtract h(1)=64 and form the quotient. =  rac{\Delta t(-32 - 16\Delta t)}{\Delta t} = -32 - 16\Delta t Factor Δt from numerator and cancel (valid for Δt ≠ 0). \lim_{\Delta t 	o 0}(-32 - 16\Delta t) = -32 	ext{ ft/s} As Δt → 0, the term −16Δt → 0. The instantaneous velocity at t=1 is exactly −32 ft/s. \Delta t=1:\;-48,\quad \Delta t=0.5:\;-40,\quad \Delta t=0.1:\;-33.6,\quad \Delta t=0.01:\;-32.16 Numerical check: the average velocities converge to −32 ft/s from above. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 1}  rac{x^3 - 1}{x^2 - 1}. Factor both numerator and denominator completely. Sum of cubes: a³−b³ = (a−b)(a²+ab+b²). Difference of squares: a²−b² = (a−b)(a+b). 3/2 \lim_{x 	o 1}  rac{x^3-1}{x^2-1} Direct substitution gives 0/0. Factor both. = \lim_{x 	o 1}  rac{(x-1)(x^2+x+1)}{(x-1)(x+1)} x³−1 = (x−1)(x²+x+1) [sum of cubes]; x²−1 = (x−1)(x+1) [difference of squares]. = \lim_{x 	o 1}  rac{x^2+x+1}{x+1} Cancel (x−1), valid since x ≠ 1. =  rac{1+1+1}{1+1} =  rac{3}{2} Substitute x = 1. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o \infty} \left(\sqrt{x^2 + x} - xight). This is ∞ − ∞, an indeterminate form. Rationalize by multiplying by the conjugate: (√(x²+x) + x)/(√(x²+x) + x). 1/2 \lim_{x 	o \infty}(\sqrt{x^2+x} - x) ∞ − ∞ is indeterminate. Multiply by the conjugate. = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{(\sqrt{x^2+x}-x)(\sqrt{x^2+x}+x)}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x} = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{(x^2+x) - x^2}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x} Numerator: (√(x²+x))² − x² = x²+x − x² = x. = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+x}+x} = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{x}{x\sqrt{1+1/x}+x} Factor x from the denominator: √(x²+x) = x√(1+1/x) for x > 0. = \lim_{x 	o \infty}  rac{1}{\sqrt{1+1/x}+1} Cancel x from numerator and denominator. =  rac{1}{\sqrt{1+0}+1} =  rac{1}{2} 1/x → 0 as x → ∞. Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 2} x^2 = 4. Write |x²−4| = |x−2||x+2|. You need to bound |x+2|. Restrict δ ≤ 1 first (so x ∈ (1,3)), which bounds |x+2| ≤ 5. Proved via ε-δ with δ = min(1, ε/5) |x^2 - 4| = |(x-2)(x+2)| = |x-2|\cdot|x+2| Factor and use the multiplicative property of absolute value. 	ext{We need to bound } |x+2|. It depends on x, so we restrict x to a bounded neighborhood first. 	ext{Restrict: assume } \delta \leq 1,\; 	ext{so } |x-2|<1 \implies 1 < x < 3. With δ ≤ 1, we know x is between 1 and 3. 	ext{Then } 3 < x+2 < 5 \implies |x+2| < 5. 	ext{So: } |x^2-4| = |x-2|\cdot|x+2| < 5|x-2| 	ext{We need } 5|x-2| < \varepsilon \iff |x-2| < \varepsilon/5 	ext{Choose } \delta = \min\!\left(1,\;  rac{\varepsilon}{5}ight) Take the smaller of 1 (to keep |x+2| < 5) and ε/5 (to make the product small). 	ext{Proof: Given } \varepsilon>0, 	ext{ let } \delta=\min(1,\varepsilon/5). 	ext{If } 0<|x-2|<\delta: \quad |x^2-4| = |x-2||x+2| < \delta\cdot 5 \leq  rac{\varepsilon}{5}\cdot 5 = \varepsilon.\; lacksquare Prerequisite: Functions Limits describe behavior of functions near a point. Next: Limit Laws Algebraic rules for computing limits without always going back to the definition. See Also: Continuity Continuity is defined using limits. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity

documents.title: Limit Laws
documents.subtitle: Algebraic rules so you don't need tables every time
documents.tags: limit laws sum rule product rule quotient rule squeeze theorem sandwich theorem direct substitution sin(x)/x

documents.assignment: Using Direct Substitution and Limit Laws Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{3x^2 - x + 1}{x + 5}.   11/7. Since the denominator is nonzero, this is simply function evaluation. \lim_{x \to 2} \frac{3x^2-x+1}{x+5} Check denominator first: $x + 5 = 2 + 5 = 7 \neq 0$ at $x = 2$. Since the denominator is nonzero, the Direct Substitution Property applies and we can simply plug in $x = 2$. = \frac{3(2)^2 - (2) + 1}{(2)+5} Substitute x = 2 in both numerator and denominator. = \frac{3 \cdot 4 - 2 + 1}{7} = \frac{12 - 2 + 1}{7} = \frac{11}{7}  The Squeeze Theorem in Action Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} x^2 \sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right).   The limit is 0. The key was recognizing that the complicated oscillating part (sin(1/x)) is bounded, and the bounding function (x²) controls the overall limit. \text{Attempt: direct substitution gives } 0 \cdot \sin(1/0) sin(1/x) is undefined at x = 0, and oscillates infinitely rapidly near 0. We cannot substitute or factor. -1 \leq \sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \leq 1 \quad \text{for all } x \neq 0 The sine function is always between −1 and 1, regardless of its argument. -x^2 \leq x^2 \sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \leq x^2 Multiply all three parts by x² ≥ 0. Multiplying by a non-negative number preserves inequalities. \lim_{x \to 0}(-x^2) = 0 \qquad \lim_{x \to 0}(x^2) = 0 Both bounding functions approach 0 (direct substitution for polynomials). \therefore \lim_{x \to 0} x^2 \sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 0 By the Squeeze Theorem: our function is sandwiched between −x² and x², both → 0, so it is squeezed to 0. Using the Fundamental Limit sin(x)/x Evaluate (a) \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{x}, \quad (b) \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 3x}{\sin 7x}.   (a) 5, (b) 3/7. General pattern: lim(x→0) sin(ax)/(bx) = a/b. \textbf{(a) } \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{x} Direct substitution: 0/0. The fundamental limit is sin(u)/u = 1, so we need to create the pattern sin(5x)/(5x). = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{x} \cdot \frac{5}{5} Multiply and divide by 5 (multiply by 1 = 5/5). = 5 \cdot \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{5x} Factor out 5. = 5 \cdot 1 = 5 Let u = 5x. As x → 0, u → 0. So lim sin(5x)/(5x) = lim sin(u)/u = 1.   \textbf{(b) } \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 3x}{\sin 7x} Divide top and bottom by x to create two copies of the pattern. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 3x / x}{\sin 7x / x} Divide numerator and denominator by x. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{3 \cdot \frac{\sin 3x}{3x}}{7 \cdot \frac{\sin 7x}{7x}} Factor to create sin(3x)/(3x) and sin(7x)/(7x). = \frac{3 \cdot 1}{7 \cdot 1} = \frac{3}{7} Both → 1 as x → 0. Using the Second Fundamental Trig Limit Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos x}{x^2}.   1/2. This limit appears when computing d/dx[cos x] from the definition of the derivative. \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1-\cos x}{x^2} 0/0 form. Strategy: multiply by (1+cos x)/(1+cos x) to use the Pythagorean identity. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{(1-\cos x)(1+\cos x)}{x^2(1+\cos x)}  = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos^2 x}{x^2(1+\cos x)} Numerator: (1−cos x)(1+cos x) = 1 − cos²x. = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin^2 x}{x^2(1+\cos x)} 1 − cos²x = sin²x (Pythagorean identity). = \lim_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\cos x} Split into recognizable pieces. = (1)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2} sin(x)/x → 1, and cos(0) = 1 by direct substitution. Limit of a Composite Function Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \cos\!\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right).   cos(1) ≈ 0.5403. Rule: for a continuous outer function, lim f(g(x)) = f(lim g(x)) — you can pull continuous functions out of limits. \lim_{x \to 0} \cos\!\left(\frac{\sin x}{x}\right) This is a composition: cos applied to sin(x)/x. \text{Step 1: find the inner limit.}  \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} = 1 The fundamental trig limit. \text{Step 2: apply cos to the inner limit.} Since cos is continuous everywhere, we can "pass the limit through" the outer function. = \cos(1)  \approx 0.5403 Numerical value for reference.  Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x}{x}. Write tan x = sin x / cos x, then separate into a product of two limits. 1  \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x}{x} = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x \cos x}  = \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} \cdot \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1}{\cos x} Product Law — both limits exist. = 1 \cdot \frac{1}{\cos 0} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1   Prove the Product Law: if \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L and \lim_{x \to c} g(x) = M, then \lim_{x \to c} f(x)g(x) = LM. Write |f·g − LM| = |f·g − Lg + Lg − LM| and use the triangle inequality. You will need to bound |f| near c. Proved via ε-δ with a bounding argument  |f(x)g(x) - LM| We want to show this is < ε. = |f(x)g(x) - Lg(x) + Lg(x) - LM| Add and subtract Lg(x) — a standard "add zero" trick. \leq |f(x)-L|\cdot|g(x)| + |L|\cdot|g(x)-M| Triangle inequality, then factor. \text{Bound } |g(x)|: \text{ since } g \to M, \exists\,\delta_1: |g(x)| < |M|+1. Near c, g(x) is within 1 of M, so |g| < |M|+1. |f(x)g(x)-LM| < |f(x)-L|\cdot(|M|+1) + |L|\cdot|g(x)-M|  \text{Choose } \delta_2: |f-L|<\frac{\varepsilon}{2(|M|+1)}, \quad \delta_3: |g-M|<\frac{\varepsilon}{2(|L|+1)} Use ε/2 for each part (with +1 to avoid division by zero when L = 0). \text{Total: } < \frac{\varepsilon}{2}+\frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon \;\blacksquare Set δ = min(δ₁, δ₂, δ₃).
documents.proofs: Each limit law is a provable theorem. The proofs all follow the same template: assume the limits of f and g exist, invoke the definitions to get δ₁ and δ₂ for each, then combine them (usually taking δ = min(δ₁, δ₂)). The proof of the Sum Law (given below) uses the **triangle inequality** |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| (play with the interactive geometric proof of this below, or check the Prerequisites inequalities lesson). This is the fundamental tool in analysis — it lets you break one error estimate into the sum of two smaller ones. The Squeeze Theorem proof is especially clean: if g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) and both g and h are within ε of L, then g(x) > L − ε and h(x) < L + ε, which forces L − ε < f(x) < L + ε, i.e., |f(x) − L| < ε. Each Limit Law is a precise theorem proven from the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition. The strategy is always the same: start from the limits of $f$ and $g$ individually, then show that combining them gives the combined limit. Here is how it works for the Sum Law. The heart of the argument is the **Triangle Inequality**: $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$. This lets us split one error into two smaller errors — one for $f$ and one for $g$. We make each piece smaller than $\varepsilon/2$, so the total error is smaller than $\varepsilon$. **How to read the proof**: You are given an arbitrary (possibly very tiny) $\varepsilon > 0$. You must produce a $\delta > 0$ that works. The choice $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$ ensures that being within $\delta$ of $c$ simultaneously makes $|f - L| < \varepsilon/2$ AND $|g - M| < \varepsilon/2$. The Triangle Inequality then closes the argument. The proofs of the Product and Quotient Laws require a bounding argument: near $c$, $|g(x)|$ cannot be arbitrarily large (it is close to $M$), so we can control the interaction term $|f||g - M|$ as well. These are slightly more involved than the Sum Law proof but follow the same template. Proof of the Sum Law \text{Given } \varepsilon > 0. \text{ Choose } \delta_1: |x-c|<\delta_1 \Rightarrow |f-L|<\varepsilon/2. \\ \text{Choose } \delta_2: |x-c|<\delta_2 \Rightarrow |g-M|<\varepsilon/2. \\ \text{Let } \delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2). \text{ Then:} \\ |(f+g)-(L+M)| \leq |f-L|+|g-M| < \tfrac{\varepsilon}{2}+\tfrac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon \;\blacksquare
documents.applications: Of course — just plug in x = 2 and get 3(4) − 2 + 5 = 15. You already knew this intuitively. The limit laws are the mathematical justification for why direct substitution works for "nice" functions. They also let you break complicated limits into simpler pieces, each of which is easy to compute. And the Squeeze Theorem is the secret weapon for limits like lim(x→0) x·sin(1/x) that completely defy algebraic manipulation — the function oscillates wildly near 0, yet its limit is pinned to 0 by being sandwiched between −x and x. The Squeeze in a picture g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) \text{ near } c. \text{ Both } g,h \to L. \Rightarrow f \to L. \text{ The middle function has no choice.} When to reach for the Squeeze Theorem \text{Trigger: bounded oscillation} \times \text{something} \to 0. \\ \text{e.g. } \sin(1/x) \cdot x, \; \cos(1/x) \cdot x^2, \; (-1)^n / n. The Trig Limit Pattern \lim_{x\to 0}\frac{\sin(ax)}{bx} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \underbrace{\lim_{u\to 0}\frac{\sin u}{u}}_{=1} = \frac{a}{b}
documents.content:
Can you compute lim(x→2)(3x² − x + 5) without making a table of values? Of course — just plug in x = 2 and get 3(4) − 2 + 5 = 15. You already knew this intuitively. The limit laws are the mathematical justification for why direct substitution works for "nice" functions. They also let you break complicated limits into simpler pieces, each of which is easy to compute. And the Squeeze Theorem is the secret weapon for limits like lim(x→0) x·sin(1/x) that completely defy algebraic manipulation — the function oscillates wildly near 0, yet its limit is pinned to 0 by being sandwiched between −x and x. **You already know the limit laws intuitively.** Suppose your friend tells you the temperature in Room A is approaching 20°C and the temperature in Room B is approaching 30°C. Without thinking, you know the average of the two temperatures is approaching 25°C. You just used the Sum Law for limits. The Limit Laws are not new ideas — they are the precise mathematical justification for arithmetic that already feels obvious. Here is the key insight: if two functions are each "settling down" to their own limits, then any arithmetic combination of those functions settles down to the same arithmetic combination of those limits. Addition distributes through limits. Multiplication distributes through limits. The only exceptions are when you might be dividing by zero or taking an even root of a negative number — situations that would be problematic in ordinary arithmetic too. **So what does this buy us?** It means we can break complicated limits into simpler pieces. To evaluate lim(x→2) (3x²−x+5), you do not need a table of values. You can write: limit of 3x² is 3·4 = 12, limit of x is 2, limit of 5 is 5, and then combine: 12 − 2 + 5 = 15. This is exactly what "direct substitution" means and why it works: the Limit Laws guarantee the pieces combine correctly. **But there is a catch.** Direct substitution only works when the denominator is nonzero and the function is "nice" (continuous) at the point. What happens when it gives 0/0? The function (x²−4)/(x−2) seems to equal 0/0 at x = 2. But remember: a limit cares about the journey toward 2, not the value at 2. Factor the top: (x+2)(x−2)/(x−2). For any x≠2, you can cancel (x−2)/(x−2) = 1, leaving x+2. As x approaches 2, x+2 approaches 4. The limit is 4, even though the function has a hole there. This algebraic cancellation technique — factor and cancel before substituting — is the standard first move when you get 0/0. **The Squeeze Theorem is a completely different kind of tool.** Sometimes a function is so wild that you cannot factor or simplify it. The classic example is f(x) = x·sin(1/x) near x = 0. As x approaches 0, sin(1/x) oscillates infinitely many times back and forth between −1 and +1 — it has no limit of its own. You cannot factor it. You cannot simplify it. But here is the key observation: |sin(anything)| ≤ 1 always. So −|x| ≤ x·sin(1/x) ≤ |x| for all x ≠ 0. Both −|x| and |x| approach 0. The function is sandwiched (squeezed) between two things that both go to 0, so it must go to 0 too. No algebra involved — just the logical consequence of being trapped. **The fundamental trig limits, sin(x)/x → 1 and (1−cos x)/x → 0 as x→0, are the most important applications of the Squeeze Theorem in Calculus 1.** They cannot be obtained by algebra. They require the Squeeze Theorem plus a geometric argument about areas on the unit circle. And they come back in every single derivative of a trig function. If you ever see $ rac{\sin(5x)}{x}$, the trick is to force the pattern: multiply and divide by 5 to get $5 \cdot  rac{\sin(5x)}{5x}$, and now the piece in brackets approaches 1 as x goes to 0. So the answer is 5. Assume $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L$ and $\lim_{x 	o c} g(x) = M$. The Limit Laws tell you exactly how to compute limits of combinations built from $f$ and $g$. **The most useful consequence: the Direct Substitution Property.** If $p(x)$ is a polynomial, then $\lim_{x 	o c} p(x) = p(c)$ — just plug in. If $r(x) = p(x)/q(x)$ is a rational function and $q(c) 
eq 0$, then again you just substitute. The same goes for trig, exponential, and log functions at points where they are defined. This is a direct consequence of the Limit Laws applied repeatedly to the building blocks of the function. **What to do when substitution gives 0/0.** Factor, cancel, then substitute. The function $ rac{x^2-4}{x-2}$ equals $(x+2)$ for every $x 
eq 2$ and is undefined only at $x = 2$ itself. But the limit as $x 	o 2$ only cares about values near 2, not at 2. So $\lim_{x 	o 2}  rac{x^2-4}{x-2} = \lim_{x 	o 2}(x+2) = 4$. The Quotient Law requires that the denominator's limit is nonzero — but after we cancel, the new simpler function is defined at 2. **The two fundamental trig limits** require the Squeeze Theorem and a unit-circle area argument. They cannot be obtained by algebra. They are used in every proof of a trig derivative: $\displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0} rac{\sin x}{x} = 1$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0} rac{1-\cos x}{x} = 0$ When you see $\sin(ax)/x$, multiply and divide by $a$ to force the pattern: $ rac{\sin(5x)}{x} = 5 \cdot  rac{\sin(5x)}{5x}$. Let $u = 5x$; as $x 	o 0$, $u 	o 0$ too, so the factor $\sin(u)/u 	o 1$. The limit is $5$. Each limit law is a provable theorem. The proofs all follow the same template: assume the limits of f and g exist, invoke the definitions to get δ₁ and δ₂ for each, then combine them (usually taking δ = min(δ₁, δ₂)). The proof of the Sum Law (given below) uses the **triangle inequality** |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| (play with the interactive geometric proof of this below, or check the Prerequisites inequalities lesson). This is the fundamental tool in analysis — it lets you break one error estimate into the sum of two smaller ones. The Squeeze Theorem proof is especially clean: if g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) and both g and h are within ε of L, then g(x) > L − ε and h(x) < L + ε, which forces L − ε < f(x) < L + ε, i.e., |f(x) − L| < ε. Each Limit Law is a precise theorem proven from the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition. The strategy is always the same: start from the limits of $f$ and $g$ individually, then show that combining them gives the combined limit. Here is how it works for the Sum Law. The heart of the argument is the **Triangle Inequality**: $|a + b| \leq |a| + |b|$. This lets us split one error into two smaller errors — one for $f$ and one for $g$. We make each piece smaller than $\varepsilon/2$, so the total error is smaller than $\varepsilon$. **How to read the proof**: You are given an arbitrary (possibly very tiny) $\varepsilon > 0$. You must produce a $\delta > 0$ that works. The choice $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$ ensures that being within $\delta$ of $c$ simultaneously makes $|f - L| < \varepsilon/2$ AND $|g - M| < \varepsilon/2$. The Triangle Inequality then closes the argument. The proofs of the Product and Quotient Laws require a bounding argument: near $c$, $|g(x)|$ cannot be arbitrarily large (it is close to $M$), so we can control the interaction term $|f||g - M|$ as well. These are slightly more involved than the Sum Law proof but follow the same template. Proof of the Sum Law 	ext{Given } \varepsilon > 0. 	ext{ Choose } \delta_1: |x-c|<\delta_1 \Rightarrow |f-L|<\varepsilon/2. \ 	ext{Choose } \delta_2: |x-c|<\delta_2 \Rightarrow |g-M|<\varepsilon/2. \ 	ext{Let } \delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2). 	ext{ Then:} \ |(f+g)-(L+M)| \leq |f-L|+|g-M| < 	frac{\varepsilon}{2}+	frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon \; lacksquare The Squeeze in a picture g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) 	ext{ near } c. 	ext{ Both } g,h 	o L. \Rightarrow f 	o L. 	ext{ The middle function has no choice.} When to reach for the Squeeze Theorem 	ext{Trigger: bounded oscillation} 	imes 	ext{something} 	o 0. \ 	ext{e.g. } \sin(1/x) \cdot x, \; \cos(1/x) \cdot x^2, \; (-1)^n / n. The Trig Limit Pattern \lim_{x	o 0} rac{\sin(ax)}{bx} =  rac{a}{b} \cdot \underbrace{\lim_{u	o 0} rac{\sin u}{u}}_{=1} =  rac{a}{b} The Limit Laws  egin{aligned} \lim[f \pm g] &= L \pm M \ \lim[f \cdot g] &= L \cdot M \ \lim[f/g] &= L/M \quad (M 
eq 0) \ \lim[f^n] &= L^n \quad (n \in \mathbb{Z}^+) \ \lim[k \cdot f] &= k \cdot L \ \lim[\sqrt[n]{f}] &= \sqrt[n]{L} \quad (	ext{if defined}) \end{aligned} Squeeze Theorem 	ext{If } g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) 	ext{ near } c 	ext{ and } \lim_{x 	o c} g = \lim_{x 	o c} h = L, 	ext{ then } \lim_{x 	o c} f = L. Two Fundamental Trig Limits \lim_{x 	o 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1 \qquad \lim_{x 	o 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos x}{x} = 0 Story Viz — Getting Closer Book 3 Chapter 2 story visualization focused on applying limit laws in context. Limit Law Bridge Lab Test limit law conditions interactively. Observe when decomposition rules are safe and when naive substitution fails. Squeeze Theorem in Action Watch a bounded oscillating function get pinned to zero by two tame bounding functions. Triangle Inequality — The Tool Behind Every Limit Law Proof Drag both vectors and observe |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b| always holds. This is the geometric foundation of every limit law proof. Using Direct Substitution and Limit Laws Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 2}  rac{3x^2 - x + 1}{x + 5}. 11/7. Since the denominator is nonzero, this is simply function evaluation. \lim_{x 	o 2}  rac{3x^2-x+1}{x+5} Check denominator first: $x + 5 = 2 + 5 = 7 
eq 0$ at $x = 2$. Since the denominator is nonzero, the Direct Substitution Property applies and we can simply plug in $x = 2$. =  rac{3(2)^2 - (2) + 1}{(2)+5} Substitute x = 2 in both numerator and denominator. =  rac{3 \cdot 4 - 2 + 1}{7} =  rac{12 - 2 + 1}{7} =  rac{11}{7} The Squeeze Theorem in Action Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0} x^2 \sin\!\left( rac{1}{x}ight). The limit is 0. The key was recognizing that the complicated oscillating part (sin(1/x)) is bounded, and the bounding function (x²) controls the overall limit. 	ext{Attempt: direct substitution gives } 0 \cdot \sin(1/0) sin(1/x) is undefined at x = 0, and oscillates infinitely rapidly near 0. We cannot substitute or factor. -1 \leq \sin\!\left( rac{1}{x}ight) \leq 1 \quad 	ext{for all } x 
eq 0 The sine function is always between −1 and 1, regardless of its argument. -x^2 \leq x^2 \sin\!\left( rac{1}{x}ight) \leq x^2 Multiply all three parts by x² ≥ 0. Multiplying by a non-negative number preserves inequalities. \lim_{x 	o 0}(-x^2) = 0 \qquad \lim_{x 	o 0}(x^2) = 0 Both bounding functions approach 0 (direct substitution for polynomials). 	herefore \lim_{x 	o 0} x^2 \sin\!\left( rac{1}{x}ight) = 0 By the Squeeze Theorem: our function is sandwiched between −x² and x², both → 0, so it is squeezed to 0. Using the Fundamental Limit sin(x)/x Evaluate (a) \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 5x}{x}, \quad (b) \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 3x}{\sin 7x}. (a) 5, (b) 3/7. General pattern: lim(x→0) sin(ax)/(bx) = a/b. 	extbf{(a) } \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 5x}{x} Direct substitution: 0/0. The fundamental limit is sin(u)/u = 1, so we need to create the pattern sin(5x)/(5x). = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 5x}{x} \cdot  rac{5}{5} Multiply and divide by 5 (multiply by 1 = 5/5). = 5 \cdot \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 5x}{5x} Factor out 5. = 5 \cdot 1 = 5 Let u = 5x. As x → 0, u → 0. So lim sin(5x)/(5x) = lim sin(u)/u = 1. 	extbf{(b) } \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 3x}{\sin 7x} Divide top and bottom by x to create two copies of the pattern. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin 3x / x}{\sin 7x / x} Divide numerator and denominator by x. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{3 \cdot  rac{\sin 3x}{3x}}{7 \cdot  rac{\sin 7x}{7x}} Factor to create sin(3x)/(3x) and sin(7x)/(7x). =  rac{3 \cdot 1}{7 \cdot 1} =  rac{3}{7} Both → 1 as x → 0. Using the Second Fundamental Trig Limit Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{1 - \cos x}{x^2}. 1/2. This limit appears when computing d/dx[cos x] from the definition of the derivative. \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{1-\cos x}{x^2} 0/0 form. Strategy: multiply by (1+cos x)/(1+cos x) to use the Pythagorean identity. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{(1-\cos x)(1+\cos x)}{x^2(1+\cos x)} = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{1 - \cos^2 x}{x^2(1+\cos x)} Numerator: (1−cos x)(1+cos x) = 1 − cos²x. = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin^2 x}{x^2(1+\cos x)} 1 − cos²x = sin²x (Pythagorean identity). = \lim_{x 	o 0} \left( rac{\sin x}{x}ight)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{1+\cos x} Split into recognizable pieces. = (1)^2 \cdot  rac{1}{1 + 1} =  rac{1}{2} sin(x)/x → 1, and cos(0) = 1 by direct substitution. Limit of a Composite Function Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0} \cos\!\left( rac{\sin x}{x}ight). cos(1) ≈ 0.5403. Rule: for a continuous outer function, lim f(g(x)) = f(lim g(x)) — you can pull continuous functions out of limits. \lim_{x 	o 0} \cos\!\left( rac{\sin x}{x}ight) This is a composition: cos applied to sin(x)/x. 	ext{Step 1: find the inner limit.} \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin x}{x} = 1 The fundamental trig limit. 	ext{Step 2: apply cos to the inner limit.} Since cos is continuous everywhere, we can "pass the limit through" the outer function. = \cos(1) \approx 0.5403 Numerical value for reference. Evaluate \displaystyle\lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{	an x}{x}. Write tan x = sin x / cos x, then separate into a product of two limits. 1 \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{	an x}{x} = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin x}{x \cos x} = \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin x}{x} \cdot \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{1}{\cos x} Product Law — both limits exist. = 1 \cdot  rac{1}{\cos 0} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 Prove the Product Law: if \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L and \lim_{x 	o c} g(x) = M, then \lim_{x 	o c} f(x)g(x) = LM. Write |f·g − LM| = |f·g − Lg + Lg − LM| and use the triangle inequality. You will need to bound |f| near c. Proved via ε-δ with a bounding argument |f(x)g(x) - LM| We want to show this is < ε. = |f(x)g(x) - Lg(x) + Lg(x) - LM| Add and subtract Lg(x) — a standard "add zero" trick. \leq |f(x)-L|\cdot|g(x)| + |L|\cdot|g(x)-M| Triangle inequality, then factor. 	ext{Bound } |g(x)|: 	ext{ since } g 	o M, \exists\,\delta_1: |g(x)| < |M|+1. Near c, g(x) is within 1 of M, so |g| < |M|+1. |f(x)g(x)-LM| < |f(x)-L|\cdot(|M|+1) + |L|\cdot|g(x)-M| 	ext{Choose } \delta_2: |f-L|< rac{\varepsilon}{2(|M|+1)}, \quad \delta_3: |g-M|< rac{\varepsilon}{2(|L|+1)} Use ε/2 for each part (with +1 to avoid division by zero when L = 0). 	ext{Total: } <  rac{\varepsilon}{2}+ rac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon \; lacksquare Set δ = min(δ₁, δ₂, δ₃). Prerequisite: Inequalities Understanding the Triangle Inequality is strictly required to prove the limit laws. Deep Dive: Fundamental Trig Limits If sin(x)/x and (1-cos x)/x feel shaky, use the focused lesson for slower proofs and pattern drills. Previous: Introduction to Limits Informal concept and ε-δ definition. Next: Continuity A function is continuous if its limit equals its value. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity

documents.subtitle: Functions you can draw without lifting your pencil
