documents.assignment:
Checking Continuity at a Point Is f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2} continuous at x = 2? If not, what type of discontinuity?   Not continuous at x = 2 — removable discontinuity. Define f(2) = 4 to make it continuous. \text{Check (1): Is } f(2) \text{ defined?} Substitute x = 2: (4−4)/(2−2) = 0/0. Undefined. ✗ \text{Check (2): Does } \lim_{x \to 2} f(x) \text{ exist?} Factor and cancel: (x²−4)/(x−2) = (x+2)(x−2)/(x−2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. \lim_{x \to 2} (x+2) = 4 The limit exists and equals 4. \text{Since f(2) is undefined, f is NOT continuous at x = 2.} Condition (1) fails. \text{Type: removable discontinuity (hole at (2, 4)).} The limit exists — we just have a missing point. Fixable by defining f(2) = 4. Applying the Intermediate Value Theorem Show that the equation x^5 - x - 1 = 0 has at least one real root.   The equation has at least one root in (1, 2). IVT guarantees existence — it doesn't find the root explicitly. f(x) = x^5 - x - 1 Define f. It is a polynomial, hence continuous everywhere. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0 Evaluate at x = 1. f(2) = 32 - 2 - 1 = 29 > 0 Evaluate at x = 2. f(1) < 0 < f(2) f changes sign on [1, 2]. The value 0 is between f(1) and f(2). \text{By IVT: } \exists\, c \in (1, 2) \text{ s.t. } f(c) = 0 f is continuous on [1,2] and 0 is between f(1) = −1 and f(2) = 29. Making a Piecewise Function Continuous Find k so that f(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2 - 3 & x \leq 2 \\ x + k & x > 2 \end{cases} is continuous at x = 2.   k = 5/3 makes the function continuous at x = 2. Check: left limit = 4(5/3)−3 = 20/3−9/3 = 11/3. Right limit = 2 + 5/3 = 11/3. ✓ \lim_{x \to 2^-} f(x) = k(2)^2 - 3 = 4k - 3 Left-hand limit: use the first piece. \lim_{x \to 2^+} f(x) = 2 + k Right-hand limit: use the second piece. f(2) = k(2)^2 - 3 = 4k - 3 Function value at x = 2 (use piece defined for x ≤ 2). \text{For continuity: } 4k - 3 = 2 + k Set left limit = right limit = f(2). 3k = 5 \implies k = \frac{5}{3} Solve for k. Physics: Is There a Jump in Velocity When a Parachute Opens? A skydiver's vertical velocity is modeled by v(t)= -50 + 4t for t<8 (free fall before chute deployment) and v(t)= -18 - 2e^{-0.7(t-8)} for t>=8 (after deployment). Is v(t) continuous at t=8?   In this model the velocity has a jump discontinuity at deployment time. Real systems usually smooth this transition over a short interval, but piecewise models often include intentional jumps to represent abrupt regime changes. v(8^-) = -50 + 4(8) = -18 Compute the left-hand limit from the pre-deployment model. v(8^+) = -18 - 2e^{-0.7(0)} = -18 - 2 = -20 Compute the right-hand limit from the post-deployment model. v(8^-) = -18 
eq -20 = v(8^+) Left and right limits disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist. \therefore v(t) \text{ is not continuous at } t=8 The model predicts a jump in velocity at deployment. Piecewise Continuity Checklist — Pass Case Check continuity at x=2 for f(x)=x^2-1 (x<2), f(2)=3, f(x)=2x-1 (x>2).   All three conditions pass, so f is continuous at x=2. f(2)=3 Condition 1: value exists. \lim_{x\to2^-}(x^2-1)=3 Condition 2a: left limit. \lim_{x\to2^+}(2x-1)=3 Condition 2b: right limit. \lim_{x\to2}f(x)=3=f(2) Condition 3: limit equals value. Piecewise Continuity Checklist — Fail Case Check continuity at x=0 for h(x)=x^2 (x<0), h(x)=x+1 (x>=0).   h is not continuous at x=0 because the two-sided limit fails. h(0)=1 Value exists. \lim_{x\to0^-}x^2=0 Left limit. \lim_{x\to0^+}(x+1)=1 Right limit. 0\neq 1 \Rightarrow \lim_{x\to0}h(x)\text{ does not exist} Left and right disagree. Solve Parameter c for Continuity at a Join Find c so f is continuous at x=2: f(x)=cx^2+1 (x<=2), f(x)=x+c (x>2).   c=1/3 is the unique value that removes the join discontinuity. \lim_{x\to2^-}f(x)=4c+1 Left-branch value at the join. \lim_{x\to2^+}f(x)=2+c Right-branch value at the join. 4c+1=2+c \Rightarrow 3c=1 \Rightarrow c=1/3 Set equal to enforce continuity.  Use IVT to prove that every cubic polynomial has at least one real root. Consider a cubic p(x) = x³ + bx² + cx + d. What happens to p(x) as x → +∞ and as x → −∞? Proved: every cubic has a real root  p(x) = x^3 + bx^2 + cx + d General cubic (leading coefficient 1, WLOG). \lim_{x \to +\infty} p(x) = +\infty x³ dominates as x → +∞. \lim_{x \to -\infty} p(x) = -\infty x³ dominates and is negative as x → −∞. \exists\; M > 0 \text{ s.t. } p(M) > 0 \text{ and } p(-M) < 0 By the limit definition, we can find large enough M. p \text{ is a polynomial, hence continuous on } [-M, M]  p(-M) < 0 < p(M) \implies \exists\, c \in (-M,M) \text{ s.t. } p(c) = 0 By IVT, p hits 0 somewhere between −M and M. ∎
documents.proofs: A function is called **uniformly continuous** on an interval if the δ in the ε-δ definition can be chosen to work for all points simultaneously (not just near one point). The **Extreme Value Theorem**: if f is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], then f attains its maximum and minimum values somewhere on [a,b]. These theorems (IVT, EVT) are consequences of the completeness of ℝ — they fail for rational numbers. Extreme Value Theorem (EVT) If f is continuous on [a, b], then \exists\, x_{\min}, x_{\max} \in [a,b] such that f(x_{\min}) \leq f(x) \leq f(x_{\max}) for all x \in [a,b].
documents.applications: Yes! If you're going 0 mph and then 60 mph, your speed must have been every value between 0 and 60 at some point. This is the **Intermediate Value Theorem** — and it only works because your speed is a continuous function of time. Continuity is the formal way of saying "no sudden jumps" — and it's what makes IVT, the Mean Value Theorem, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus all work. The Three Conditions for Continuity at $c$ \text{(1) } f(c) \text{ exists.} \\ \text{(2) } \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \text{ exists.} \\ \text{(3) } \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c). \\ \text{All three must hold simultaneously.} Continuous Does NOT Mean Differentiable $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous at $x=0$: $f(0) = 0$, both one-sided limits are $0$, and they match. But it has a sharp corner there, so it is NOT differentiable at $0$. Continuity allows corners; differentiability requires smoothness. Bolzano's 1817 Breakthrough Bernard Bolzano gave one of the first rigorous statements of continuity and the Intermediate Value Principle in 1817, years before Cauchy formalized it in his textbook. His motivation was purely logical: he wanted to prove that a continuous function that changes sign must cross zero, without appealing to geometric intuition.
documents.content:
When you drive from point A to point B, must your speedometer pass through every speed in between? Yes! If you're going 0 mph and then 60 mph, your speed must have been every value between 0 and 60 at some point. This is the **Intermediate Value Theorem** — and it only works because your speed is a continuous function of time. Continuity is the formal way of saying "no sudden jumps" — and it's what makes IVT, the Mean Value Theorem, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus all work. **You have been computing limits for two lessons now. Here is the payoff.** A limit tells you what $f(x)$ is *heading toward* as $x$ approaches $c$. But every time you evaluated a limit, you checked: is the denominator zero? Can I substitute directly? The reason some functions let you substitute and others don't is **continuity**. Continuous functions are the "well-behaved" ones where the limit and the value agree. Everything else is a special case. **The pencil test.** Imagine drawing the graph of a function without lifting your pencil. If you can do it over an interval, the function is continuous there. The moment you have to lift your pencil — to jump to a new height, or skip over a hole, or avoid a vertical spike — you have a discontinuity. This physical test captures the essential idea, even if it is not quite rigorous enough for proofs. **Three conditions, all required.** A function $f$ is continuous at a point $c$ if and only if ALL three of these hold simultaneously: (1) $f(c)$ exists — the function is actually defined there; (2) $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x)$ exists — both sides approach the same value; (3) $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = f(c)$ — the limit and the function value match. Miss any one of them and the function is discontinuous at $c$. Each condition catches a different failure mode. **Removable discontinuity (hole).** The limit exists but the function either is not defined at $c$, or is defined but at the wrong height. Example: $f(x) = (x^2-1)/(x-1)$ simplifies to $x+1$ for $x 
eq 1$, but is undefined at $x=1$. The limit as $x 	o 1$ is $2$. There is a hole at $(1, 2)$. We say this discontinuity is "removable" because we can patch it: just define $f(1) = 2$ and the function becomes continuous. The hole is an artifact of how the function was written, not a genuine break in the graph. **Jump discontinuity.** Both one-sided limits exist but they give different values, so the two-sided limit does not exist. The function literally jumps from one level to another. The floor function $\lfloor x floor$ is the classic example: at every integer, the left-hand limit is one number and the right-hand limit is one higher. You cannot patch a jump by redefining one point — it is a genuine break. **Infinite discontinuity.** The function grows without bound (heads toward $\pm \infty$) as $x 	o c$. The example everyone knows: $f(x) = 1/x$ at $x = 0$. From the right, $1/x 	o +\infty$; from the left, $1/x 	o -\infty$. There is a vertical asymptote at $x = 0$. No limit exists, and no amount of redefining fixes it. **Continuity on an interval.** A function is continuous on an open interval $(a, b)$ if it is continuous at every single point in that interval. For a closed interval $[a, b]$, we also require one-sided continuity at the endpoints: the right-hand limit equals $f(a)$ at $a$, and the left-hand limit equals $f(b)$ at $b$. Polynomials, trig functions, exponentials, and logarithms (on their domains) are all continuous everywhere they are defined. These are your "safe" functions for direct substitution. **Why does any of this matter?** Because continuity is the hypothesis of every major theorem in Calculus 1: the Intermediate Value Theorem, the Extreme Value Theorem, the Mean Value Theorem, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. The reason those theorems work is that continuous functions do not have hidden jumps or holes that could cause the conclusion to fail. The formal definition packages the three intuitive conditions into one equation: $f$ is continuous at $c$ if $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = f(c)$. This single equation secretly requires all three conditions: for it to make sense, $f(c)$ must be defined (condition 1); for $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x)$ to exist, both sides must agree (condition 2); and the equation requires they agree with $f(c)$ (condition 3). Continuity is **closed under algebra**: if $f$ and $g$ are both continuous at $c$, then so are $f+g$, $f-g$, $f \cdot g$, and $f/g$ (as long as $g(c) 
eq 0$). Composition is also preserved: if $g$ is continuous at $c$ and $f$ is continuous at $g(c)$, then $f(g(x))$ is continuous at $c$. These facts are what justify saying polynomials, rational functions (where defined), trig, exponentials, and logs are continuous on their domains without proving it from scratch every time. **The Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)** is the first great consequence of continuity. It says that continuous functions cannot skip values. If $f(a) = -3$ and $f(b) = 7$, then somewhere in between, $f$ must equal every value from $-3$ to $7$: every integer, every irrational, $\pi$, $\sqrt{2}$, all of them. A function with a gap or jump could avoid a target value entirely — continuity prevents that. **How to use IVT**: to show a function has a root (solution to $f(c) = 0$), find two points $a$ and $b$ where $f$ changes sign: $f(a) < 0$ and $f(b) > 0$ (or vice versa). If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then IVT guarantees a root exists in $(a, b)$. IVT does not tell you where the root is — only that it exists. Numerical methods (like the Bisection Method in Chapter 3) can then find it. A function is called **uniformly continuous** on an interval if the δ in the ε-δ definition can be chosen to work for all points simultaneously (not just near one point). The **Extreme Value Theorem**: if f is continuous on a closed interval [a,b], then f attains its maximum and minimum values somewhere on [a,b]. These theorems (IVT, EVT) are consequences of the completeness of ℝ — they fail for rational numbers. Extreme Value Theorem (EVT) If f is continuous on [a, b], then \exists\, x_{\min}, x_{\max} \in [a,b] such that f(x_{\min}) \leq f(x) \leq f(x_{\max}) for all x \in [a,b]. The Three Conditions for Continuity at $c$ 	ext{(1) } f(c) 	ext{ exists.} \ 	ext{(2) } \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) 	ext{ exists.} \ 	ext{(3) } \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = f(c). \ 	ext{All three must hold simultaneously.} Continuous Does NOT Mean Differentiable $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous at $x=0$: $f(0) = 0$, both one-sided limits are $0$, and they match. But it has a sharp corner there, so it is NOT differentiable at $0$. Continuity allows corners; differentiability requires smoothness. Bolzano's 1817 Breakthrough Bernard Bolzano gave one of the first rigorous statements of continuity and the Intermediate Value Principle in 1817, years before Cauchy formalized it in his textbook. His motivation was purely logical: he wanted to prove that a continuous function that changes sign must cross zero, without appealing to geometric intuition. Continuity at a Point f 	ext{ is continuous at } c \iff \lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = f(c). \ 	ext{(Requires: } f(c) 	ext{ defined, limit exists, and they equal.)} Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) 	ext{If } f 	ext{ is continuous on } [a, b] 	ext{ and } k 	ext{ is any value with } f(a) \leq k \leq f(b), \ 	ext{then } \exists\, c \in (a, b) 	ext{ such that } f(c) = k. Story Viz — The Infinite Staircase Book 3 Chapter 3 story visualization for continuity breakdowns and jump discontinuities. Continuity Repair Game Drag the broken point to match the limit and repair continuity in real time. Checking Continuity at a Point Is f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2} continuous at x = 2? If not, what type of discontinuity? Not continuous at x = 2 — removable discontinuity. Define f(2) = 4 to make it continuous. 	ext{Check (1): Is } f(2) 	ext{ defined?} Substitute x = 2: (4−4)/(2−2) = 0/0. Undefined. ✗ 	ext{Check (2): Does } \lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) 	ext{ exist?} Factor and cancel: (x²−4)/(x−2) = (x+2)(x−2)/(x−2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. \lim_{x 	o 2} (x+2) = 4 The limit exists and equals 4. 	ext{Since f(2) is undefined, f is NOT continuous at x = 2.} Condition (1) fails. 	ext{Type: removable discontinuity (hole at (2, 4)).} The limit exists — we just have a missing point. Fixable by defining f(2) = 4. Applying the Intermediate Value Theorem Show that the equation x^5 - x - 1 = 0 has at least one real root. The equation has at least one root in (1, 2). IVT guarantees existence — it doesn't find the root explicitly. f(x) = x^5 - x - 1 Define f. It is a polynomial, hence continuous everywhere. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0 Evaluate at x = 1. f(2) = 32 - 2 - 1 = 29 > 0 Evaluate at x = 2. f(1) < 0 < f(2) f changes sign on [1, 2]. The value 0 is between f(1) and f(2). 	ext{By IVT: } \exists\, c \in (1, 2) 	ext{ s.t. } f(c) = 0 f is continuous on [1,2] and 0 is between f(1) = −1 and f(2) = 29. Making a Piecewise Function Continuous Find k so that f(x) =  egin{cases} kx^2 - 3 & x \leq 2 \ x + k & x > 2 \end{cases} is continuous at x = 2. k = 5/3 makes the function continuous at x = 2. Check: left limit = 4(5/3)−3 = 20/3−9/3 = 11/3. Right limit = 2 + 5/3 = 11/3. ✓ \lim_{x 	o 2^-} f(x) = k(2)^2 - 3 = 4k - 3 Left-hand limit: use the first piece. \lim_{x 	o 2^+} f(x) = 2 + k Right-hand limit: use the second piece. f(2) = k(2)^2 - 3 = 4k - 3 Function value at x = 2 (use piece defined for x ≤ 2). 	ext{For continuity: } 4k - 3 = 2 + k Set left limit = right limit = f(2). 3k = 5 \implies k =  rac{5}{3} Solve for k. Physics: Is There a Jump in Velocity When a Parachute Opens? A skydiver's vertical velocity is modeled by v(t)= -50 + 4t for t<8 (free fall before chute deployment) and v(t)= -18 - 2e^{-0.7(t-8)} for t>=8 (after deployment). Is v(t) continuous at t=8? In this model the velocity has a jump discontinuity at deployment time. Real systems usually smooth this transition over a short interval, but piecewise models often include intentional jumps to represent abrupt regime changes. v(8^-) = -50 + 4(8) = -18 Compute the left-hand limit from the pre-deployment model. v(8^+) = -18 - 2e^{-0.7(0)} = -18 - 2 = -20 Compute the right-hand limit from the post-deployment model. v(8^-) = -18 
eq -20 = v(8^+) Left and right limits disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist. 	herefore v(t) 	ext{ is not continuous at } t=8 The model predicts a jump in velocity at deployment. Piecewise Continuity Checklist — Pass Case Check continuity at x=2 for f(x)=x^2-1 (x<2), f(2)=3, f(x)=2x-1 (x>2). All three conditions pass, so f is continuous at x=2. f(2)=3 Condition 1: value exists. \lim_{x	o2^-}(x^2-1)=3 Condition 2a: left limit. \lim_{x	o2^+}(2x-1)=3 Condition 2b: right limit. \lim_{x	o2}f(x)=3=f(2) Condition 3: limit equals value. Piecewise Continuity Checklist — Fail Case Check continuity at x=0 for h(x)=x^2 (x<0), h(x)=x+1 (x>=0). h is not continuous at x=0 because the two-sided limit fails. h(0)=1 Value exists. \lim_{x	o0^-}x^2=0 Left limit. \lim_{x	o0^+}(x+1)=1 Right limit. 0
eq 1 \Rightarrow \lim_{x	o0}h(x)	ext{ does not exist} Left and right disagree. Solve Parameter c for Continuity at a Join Find c so f is continuous at x=2: f(x)=cx^2+1 (x<=2), f(x)=x+c (x>2). c=1/3 is the unique value that removes the join discontinuity. \lim_{x	o2^-}f(x)=4c+1 Left-branch value at the join. \lim_{x	o2^+}f(x)=2+c Right-branch value at the join. 4c+1=2+c \Rightarrow 3c=1 \Rightarrow c=1/3 Set equal to enforce continuity. Use IVT to prove that every cubic polynomial has at least one real root. Consider a cubic p(x) = x³ + bx² + cx + d. What happens to p(x) as x → +∞ and as x → −∞? Proved: every cubic has a real root p(x) = x^3 + bx^2 + cx + d General cubic (leading coefficient 1, WLOG). \lim_{x 	o +\infty} p(x) = +\infty x³ dominates as x → +∞. \lim_{x 	o -\infty} p(x) = -\infty x³ dominates and is negative as x → −∞. \exists\; M > 0 	ext{ s.t. } p(M) > 0 	ext{ and } p(-M) < 0 By the limit definition, we can find large enough M. p 	ext{ is a polynomial, hence continuous on } [-M, M] p(-M) < 0 < p(M) \implies \exists\, c \in (-M,M) 	ext{ s.t. } p(c) = 0 By IVT, p hits 0 somewhere between −M and M. ∎ Previous: Limit Laws Continuity is defined using limits. Next: Epsilon-Delta Make the definition of limit rigorous. Coming: Derivatives Differentiability implies continuity. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity

documents.title: The Intermediate Value Theorem
documents.subtitle: You cannot jump over a value without passing through it
documents.tags: IVT intermediate value theorem existence theorem root finding bisection method continuous function zeros Bolzano fixed point connectedness existence proof polynomial roots

documents.assignment: Show a Root Exists: x³ − x − 1 = 0 Show that the equation $x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ has at least one real solution in the interval $(1, 2)$.   The IVT guarantees existence without finding the root. (The actual root is c ≈ 1.3247, called the "plastic constant.") f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 Define f. It is a polynomial, so it is continuous everywhere — no need to verify continuity for polynomials. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 Evaluate at the left endpoint. f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 Evaluate at the right endpoint. f(1) = -1 < 0 < 5 = f(2) The function changes sign. Since 0 is between -1 and 5, and f is continuous on [1,2], the IVT guarantees a root. \therefore \exists c \in (1, 2) \text{ with } f(c) = 0 The equation x³ − x − 1 = 0 has at least one solution between 1 and 2. Two Applications in One Step Show that $\cos x = x$ has a solution in $(0, \pi/2)$.   The fixed point equation cos(x) = x has a solution ≈ 0.7391 (called the Dottie number). This is also a fixed-point theorem application. g(x) = \cos x - x Reformulate: we need g(x) = 0. The function g is continuous (difference of continuous functions). g(0) = \cos 0 - 0 = 1 - 0 = 1 > 0 At x = 0: cos(0) = 1, so g is positive. g(\pi/2) = \cos(\pi/2) - \pi/2 = 0 - \pi/2 \approx -1.57 < 0 At x = π/2: cos(π/2) = 0, which is less than π/2 ≈ 1.57. g(0) > 0 > g(\pi/2) g changes sign, so by IVT there exists c ∈ (0, π/2) with g(c) = 0, i.e., cos(c) = c. Bisection Method: Three Iterations Use bisection to localize the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2$ in $[1, 2]$ to within 0.125.   3 bisection steps reduced the interval from width 1 to width 0.125. After n steps, width = 1/2ⁿ. To get 6 decimal places, we need about n = 20 steps. f(1) = -1 < 0, \quad f(2) = 6 > 0 Root is in [1, 2]. Interval width = 1. m_1 = 1.5, \quad f(1.5) = 1.375 > 0 f(1.5) > 0, so root is in [1, 1.5]. Width = 0.5. m_2 = 1.25, \quad f(1.25) = -0.047 < 0 f(1.25) < 0, so root is in [1.25, 1.5]. Width = 0.25. m_3 = 1.375, \quad f(1.375) = 0.600 > 0 f(1.375) > 0, so root is in [1.25, 1.375]. Width = 0.125. \therefore c \approx 1.3125 \pm 0.0625 After 3 steps, we know the root (which is ∛2 ≈ 1.2599) is in [1.25, 1.375]. Fixed Point Theorem Application Show that $f(x) = \frac{1}{x+1}$ has a fixed point in $[0, 1]$ (i.e., $f(c) = c$ for some $c \in [0,1]$).   The fixed point of f(x) = 1/(x+1) on [0,1] is the golden ratio (√5−1)/2 ≈ 0.618, since φ satisfies 1/(φ+1) = φ ⟺ φ² + φ = 1. g(x) = f(x) - x = \frac{1}{x+1} - x Define g(x) = f(x) − x. A fixed point of f is a zero of g. g(0) = \frac{1}{1} - 0 = 1 > 0 At x = 0: f(0) = 1 > 0. g(1) = \frac{1}{2} - 1 = -\frac{1}{2} < 0 At x = 1: f(1) = 1/2 < 1. g \text{ is continuous on } [0,1] \text{ (denominator } x+1 \neq 0) No discontinuities since x+1 ≥ 1 > 0 on [0,1]. \therefore \exists c \in (0,1): g(c) = 0 \Rightarrow f(c) = c IVT guarantees a fixed point. (c = (√5 − 1)/2 ≈ 0.618, the golden ratio!) IVT for a Temperature Problem A thermometer reads 68°F at noon and 85°F at 6pm. Assuming temperature changed continuously, prove it was exactly 75°F at some point between noon and 6pm.   The IVT applies to any quantity that changes continuously — temperature, pressure, stock prices (approximately), population size. The theorem formalizes "you have to pass through every intermediate value." T(t): \text{temperature at time } t \text{ (noon} = 0\text{, 6pm} = 6\text{)}. Model temperature as a continuous function of time. T(0) = 68 \quad \text{and} \quad T(6) = 85 Given values. 68 < 75 < 85 75 lies strictly between T(0) and T(6). T \text{ is continuous (physical temperature changes continuously)} Continuity assumption: the physical world does not allow temperature to jump instantaneously. \therefore \exists t^* \in (0, 6) \text{ with } T(t^*) = 75°F By IVT, the temperature was exactly 75°F at some time between noon and 6pm.  Prove that for any continuous function $f: [0,1] \to [0,1]$, there exists $c \in [0,1]$ such that $f(c) = c$. (This is the 1D Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem.) Define $g(x) = f(x) - x$. Evaluate $g$ at the endpoints of $[0,1]$. In all three cases, there exists a fixed point c ∈ [0,1] with f(c) = c. QED.  g(x) = f(x) - x Define g. We need to show g has a zero. g(0) = f(0) - 0 = f(0) \geq 0 Since f maps [0,1] to [0,1], f(0) ≥ 0. g(1) = f(1) - 1 \leq 0 Since f(1) ≤ 1 (f maps [0,1] to [0,1]). \text{Case 1: } g(0) = 0 \Rightarrow f(0) = 0 \checkmark Fixed point found. \text{Case 2: } g(1) = 0 \Rightarrow f(1) = 1 \checkmark Fixed point found. \text{Case 3: } g(0) > 0 > g(1) \Rightarrow \exists c \in (0,1): g(c) = 0 By IVT (g is continuous as a composition/difference of continuous functions).  Show that at any given moment, there exist two antipodal points on Earth's equator with exactly the same temperature. (Borsuk-Ulam Theorem in 1D.) Let T(θ) be temperature at angle θ on the equator. Consider the function g(θ) = T(θ) - T(θ + π). What is g(0) + g(π)? By IVT applied to g(θ) = T(θ) − T(θ+π), there always exist antipodal points on the equator with the same temperature. This is the 1D Borsuk-Ulam theorem.  T(\theta): \text{continuous temperature function on the equator.} θ ranges from 0 to 2π. g(\theta) = T(\theta) - T(\theta + \pi) g measures the temperature difference between antipodal points. g(0) = T(0) - T(\pi) Evaluate at θ = 0. g(\pi) = T(\pi) - T(2\pi) = T(\pi) - T(0) = -g(0) Using T(2π) = T(0) (temperature is periodic, same point). g(0) \cdot g(\pi) = g(0) \cdot (-g(0)) = -[g(0)]^2 \leq 0 Either g(0) = 0 (antipodal match at θ=0) or g(0) and g(π) have opposite signs. \therefore \exists \theta^* \in [0, \pi]: g(\theta^*) = 0 By IVT, there is an angle where the temperature difference is zero — antipodal equal temperatures.
documents.proofs: **Proof of the IVT.** Assume $f(a) < N < f(b)$ (the case $f(b) < N < f(a)$ is symmetric). Define $S = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) < N\}$. The set $S$ is nonempty (since $a \in S$) and bounded above (by $b$). Let $c = \sup S$ — the least upper bound. We claim $f(c) = N$. Since $c = \sup S$, there is a sequence $x_n \in S$ with $x_n \to c$. Since $f$ is continuous, $f(x_n) \to f(c)$. Since $f(x_n) < N$ for all $n$, we get $f(c) \leq N$. Similarly, if $f(c) < N$, then by continuity there exists $\delta > 0$ with $f(x) < N$ for all $x \in (c, c+\delta)$, contradicting $c = \sup S$. Therefore $f(c) = N$. Since $f(a) < N$ and $f(b) > N$, we have $a < c < b$, so $c \in (a, b)$. **The key step.** This proof uses the Completeness Axiom of the real numbers: every nonempty set bounded above has a least upper bound. This is the foundational property that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$. The IVT is actually FALSE over the rationals! Consider $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0, 2]$: $f(0) = 0 < 2 < 4 = f(2)$, but there is no rational $c$ with $f(c) = 2$ (since $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational). So the IVT is a theorem about the completeness of the real number system, not just continuity. Why IVT Fails Over the Rationals The IVT is fundamentally about the "no gaps" property of the real numbers. Over $\mathbb{Q}$, $f(x) = x^2$ maps $[0,2] \cap \mathbb{Q}$ into $[0,4] \cap \mathbb{Q}$, but $\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}$ so there is a "gap" where $f(c) = 2$ should be. This shows IVT requires the completeness of $\mathbb{R}$.
documents.applications: You were definitely at 0 ft and definitely at 3,000 ft. At no point did you teleport — you drove continuously. Therefore you must have passed through every elevation in between, including exactly 1,000 ft. This seems obvious for driving, but the same logic proves something profound in mathematics: every polynomial of odd degree has at least one real root, every equation f(x) = 0 where f is continuous and changes sign must have a solution, and every physical system that changes continuously between two states must pass through every intermediate state. This is the Intermediate Value Theorem — one of the most useful existence theorems in all of mathematics. Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) If $f$ is continuous on the closed interval $[a, b]$, and $N$ is any number strictly between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c$ in $(a, b)$ such that $f(c) = N$. How to Use IVT to Prove a Root Exists (1) Show $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ (polynomials, trig, exp, ln are continuous on their domains). (2) Compute $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. (3) Check they have opposite signs. (4) Conclude: $f(c) = 0$ for some $c in (a, b)$. The "No Teleportation" Principle Continuity means the function has no "jumps" — you cannot get from $f(a)$ to $f(b)$ without passing through every value in between. The IVT is just the formal statement of this physical intuition. IVT Proves Existence, Not Uniqueness The IVT says at least one such $c$ exists. There may be many. For example, $f(x) = \sin x$ takes every value in $[-1, 1]$ infinitely many times. The IVT only guarantees one — it cannot tell you how many. Bisection Method for Numerical Root Finding After IVT guarantees a root in $[a, b]$: (1) Compute $m = (a+b)/2$. (2) Evaluate $f(m)$. (3) If $f(a)$ and $f(m)$ have opposite signs, root is in $[a, m]$; otherwise in $[m, b]$. (4) Repeat. Accuracy doubles each step. IVT in Computer Science: Zero-Sum Games In game theory, the minimax theorem (guaranteeing optimal strategies in two-player games) relies on IVT-like arguments. The Nash equilibrium proof uses Brouwer's Fixed Point Theorem, which is a generalization of IVT. These are the mathematical foundations of economics and AI game playing.
documents.content: You drive from Miami (sea level) to the top of a mountain (3,000 ft). At what exact moment were you at exactly 1,000 ft elevation? You were definitely at 0 ft and definitely at 3,000 ft. At no point did you teleport — you drove continuously. Therefore you must have passed through every elevation in between, including exactly 1,000 ft. This seems obvious for driving, but the same logic proves something profound in mathematics: every polynomial of odd degree has at least one real root, every equation f(x) = 0 where f is continuous and changes sign must have a solution, and every physical system that changes continuously between two states must pass through every intermediate state. This is the Intermediate Value Theorem — one of the most useful existence theorems in all of mathematics. **The core idea is about continuous motion.** If you are walking along a path and you end up higher than where you started, you must have passed through every height in between. You cannot jump. You cannot teleport. You must go through every intermediate value. Mathematically: if $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, and $f(a) = 2$ and $f(b) = 7$, then $f$ must take every value between 2 and 7 somewhere in $(a, b)$. Want to know if $f$ ever equals 5? Yes — guaranteed. Does $f$ equal 3.14159? Yes. Does $f$ equal 6.9999? Yes. Every single value between 2 and 7 is hit at least once. **The sign-change corollary is the workhorse.** If $f(a) < 0$ and $f(b) > 0$, then somewhere between $a$ and $b$ the function must cross zero. This is how we prove that equations have solutions: evaluate at two points with opposite signs, and the IVT guarantees a root between them. Example: $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1$. We have $f(1) = -1 < 0$ and $f(2) = 5 > 0$. Therefore there is a root between 1 and 2. Done. We haven't found it exactly, but we've proved it exists. **The bisection method turns IVT into an algorithm.** Once you know a root lies between $a$ and $b$, evaluate at the midpoint $m = (a+b)/2$. If $f(m) = 0$, you're done. If $f(m)$ has the same sign as $f(a)$, the root is in $[m, b]$. If $f(m)$ has the same sign as $f(b)$, the root is in $[a, m]$. Repeat. Each step cuts the interval in half, so after $n$ steps you've localized the root to within $(b-a)/2^n$ of its true location. This is the oldest root-finding algorithm in existence. **Why does continuity matter?** Remove it, and the theorem fails immediately. Consider $f(x) = -1$ for $x < 0$ and $f(x) = +1$ for $x geq 0$. Then $f(-1) = -1$ and $f(1) = +1$, but $f$ never equals 0. The discontinuity at $x = 0$ is the escape hatch that lets the function "jump over" zero. Continuity is not just a technical condition — it is the precise mathematical formalization of "no teleporting." **The IVT is secretly a theorem about connected sets.** In topology, the IVT says that the continuous image of a connected set is connected. The interval $[a, b]$ is connected (it has no gaps), so its image under $f$ must also be connected (no gaps), which means the image must contain every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. This "big picture" view explains why the theorem is true at a deep level. **Real-world applications are everywhere.** In economics: if supply exceeds demand at price $p_1$ and is less than demand at price $p_2$, there must be an equilibrium price between them. In engineering: if a beam deflects upward at one end and downward at another under a load, it has a neutral point in between. In medicine: if body temperature was 98.6°F at 8am and 103°F at noon, it passed through every temperature in between — so doctors can say "the fever first reached 101°F sometime between 8am and noon." **Formal statement.** Let $f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous. Let $N$ be any real number with $f(a) < N < f(b)$ (or $f(b) < N < f(a)$). Then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f(c) = N$. **The sign-change corollary (most useful form).** If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ (they have opposite signs), then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with $f(c) = 0$. **Key applications.** (1) Every polynomial of odd degree has at least one real root. Proof: if $p(x) = a_n x^n + \ldots + a_0$ with $n$ odd and $a_n > 0$, then $p(x) \to +\infty$ as $x \to +\infty$ and $p(x) \to -\infty$ as $x \to -\infty$. So $p(a) < 0$ and $p(b) > 0$ for some $a < b$. By IVT, $p(c) = 0$ for some $c \in (a, b)$. (2) Fixed Point Theorem: if $f: [0,1] \to [0,1]$ is continuous, then $f(c) = c$ for some $c$. Proof: let $g(x) = f(x) - x$. Then $g(0) = f(0) \geq 0$ and $g(1) = f(1) - 1 \leq 0$. By IVT, $g(c) = 0$, i.e., $f(c) = c$. **Bisection algorithm.** Input: continuous $f$, interval $[a_0, b_0]$ with $f(a_0)f(b_0) < 0$, tolerance $\varepsilon > 0$. Set $n = 0$. While $(b_n - a_n)/2 > \varepsilon$: compute $m_n = (a_n + b_n)/2$; if $f(m_n) = 0$, return $m_n$; if $f(a_n)f(m_n) < 0$, set $a_{n+1} = a_n$, $b_{n+1} = m_n$; else set $a_{n+1} = m_n$, $b_{n+1} = b_n$. Increment $n$. Return $m_n$. Error bound: after $n$ steps, $|c - m_n| \leq (b_0 - a_0)/2^{n+1}$. **Comparison with Newton's method.** Bisection always converges (linear convergence, halves error each step) but is slow. Newton's method converges much faster (quadratic: error squares each step) but can fail if the initial guess is bad or $f'$ is zero. Bisection is used when stability matters most; Newton's method when speed matters. **Proof of the IVT.** Assume $f(a) < N < f(b)$ (the case $f(b) < N < f(a)$ is symmetric). Define $S = \{x \in [a,b] : f(x) < N\}$. The set $S$ is nonempty (since $a \in S$) and bounded above (by $b$). Let $c = \sup S$ — the least upper bound. We claim $f(c) = N$. Since $c = \sup S$, there is a sequence $x_n \in S$ with $x_n \to c$. Since $f$ is continuous, $f(x_n) \to f(c)$. Since $f(x_n) < N$ for all $n$, we get $f(c) \leq N$. Similarly, if $f(c) < N$, then by continuity there exists $\delta > 0$ with $f(x) < N$ for all $x \in (c, c+\delta)$, contradicting $c = \sup S$. Therefore $f(c) = N$. Since $f(a) < N$ and $f(b) > N$, we have $a < c < b$, so $c \in (a, b)$. **The key step.** This proof uses the Completeness Axiom of the real numbers: every nonempty set bounded above has a least upper bound. This is the foundational property that distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$. The IVT is actually FALSE over the rationals! Consider $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0, 2]$: $f(0) = 0 < 2 < 4 = f(2)$, but there is no rational $c$ with $f(c) = 2$ (since $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational). So the IVT is a theorem about the completeness of the real number system, not just continuity. Why IVT Fails Over the Rationals The IVT is fundamentally about the "no gaps" property of the real numbers. Over $\mathbb{Q}$, $f(x) = x^2$ maps $[0,2] \cap \mathbb{Q}$ into $[0,4] \cap \mathbb{Q}$, but $\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}$ so there is a "gap" where $f(c) = 2$ should be. This shows IVT requires the completeness of $\mathbb{R}$. Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) If $f$ is continuous on the closed interval $[a, b]$, and $N$ is any number strictly between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c$ in $(a, b)$ such that $f(c) = N$. How to Use IVT to Prove a Root Exists (1) Show $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ (polynomials, trig, exp, ln are continuous on their domains). (2) Compute $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. (3) Check they have opposite signs. (4) Conclude: $f(c) = 0$ for some $c in (a, b)$. The "No Teleportation" Principle Continuity means the function has no "jumps" — you cannot get from $f(a)$ to $f(b)$ without passing through every value in between. The IVT is just the formal statement of this physical intuition. IVT Proves Existence, Not Uniqueness The IVT says at least one such $c$ exists. There may be many. For example, $f(x) = \sin x$ takes every value in $[-1, 1]$ infinitely many times. The IVT only guarantees one — it cannot tell you how many. Bisection Method for Numerical Root Finding After IVT guarantees a root in $[a, b]$: (1) Compute $m = (a+b)/2$. (2) Evaluate $f(m)$. (3) If $f(a)$ and $f(m)$ have opposite signs, root is in $[a, m]$; otherwise in $[m, b]$. (4) Repeat. Accuracy doubles each step. IVT in Computer Science: Zero-Sum Games In game theory, the minimax theorem (guaranteeing optimal strategies in two-player games) relies on IVT-like arguments. The Nash equilibrium proof uses Brouwer's Fixed Point Theorem, which is a generalization of IVT. These are the mathematical foundations of economics and AI game playing. Fixed Point Theorem (Brouwer, 1-D) If $f: [a,b] \to [a,b]$ is continuous, then $f$ has a fixed point: $\exists c \in [a,b]$ such that $f(c) = c$. Proof: apply IVT to $g(x) = f(x) - x$. Every Odd-Degree Polynomial Has a Real Root If $p(x)$ is a polynomial of odd degree, then $p(c) = 0$ for some $c \in \mathbb{R}$. Proof: use the end behavior $p(x) \to \pm\infty$ as $x \to \pm\infty$ and apply IVT on a sufficiently large interval. Continuity and the Intermediate Value Theorem A continuous function cannot jump over a horizontal line. Drag the target value $N$ and watch the theorem in action. Show a Root Exists: x³ − x − 1 = 0 Show that the equation $x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ has at least one real solution in the interval $(1, 2)$. The IVT guarantees existence without finding the root. (The actual root is c ≈ 1.3247, called the "plastic constant.") f(x) = x^3 - x - 1 Define f. It is a polynomial, so it is continuous everywhere — no need to verify continuity for polynomials. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 Evaluate at the left endpoint. f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 Evaluate at the right endpoint. f(1) = -1 < 0 < 5 = f(2) The function changes sign. Since 0 is between -1 and 5, and f is continuous on [1,2], the IVT guarantees a root. \therefore \exists c \in (1, 2) \text{ with } f(c) = 0 The equation x³ − x − 1 = 0 has at least one solution between 1 and 2. Two Applications in One Step Show that $\cos x = x$ has a solution in $(0, \pi/2)$. The fixed point equation cos(x) = x has a solution ≈ 0.7391 (called the Dottie number). This is also a fixed-point theorem application. g(x) = \cos x - x Reformulate: we need g(x) = 0. The function g is continuous (difference of continuous functions). g(0) = \cos 0 - 0 = 1 - 0 = 1 > 0 At x = 0: cos(0) = 1, so g is positive. g(\pi/2) = \cos(\pi/2) - \pi/2 = 0 - \pi/2 \approx -1.57 < 0 At x = π/2: cos(π/2) = 0, which is less than π/2 ≈ 1.57. g(0) > 0 > g(\pi/2) g changes sign, so by IVT there exists c ∈ (0, π/2) with g(c) = 0, i.e., cos(c) = c. Bisection Method: Three Iterations Use bisection to localize the root of $f(x) = x^3 - 2$ in $[1, 2]$ to within 0.125. 3 bisection steps reduced the interval from width 1 to width 0.125. After n steps, width = 1/2ⁿ. To get 6 decimal places, we need about n = 20 steps. f(1) = -1 < 0, \quad f(2) = 6 > 0 Root is in [1, 2]. Interval width = 1. m_1 = 1.5, \quad f(1.5) = 1.375 > 0 f(1.5) > 0, so root is in [1, 1.5]. Width = 0.5. m_2 = 1.25, \quad f(1.25) = -0.047 < 0 f(1.25) < 0, so root is in [1.25, 1.5]. Width = 0.25. m_3 = 1.375, \quad f(1.375) = 0.600 > 0 f(1.375) > 0, so root is in [1.25, 1.375]. Width = 0.125. \therefore c \approx 1.3125 \pm 0.0625 After 3 steps, we know the root (which is ∛2 ≈ 1.2599) is in [1.25, 1.375]. Fixed Point Theorem Application Show that $f(x) = \frac{1}{x+1}$ has a fixed point in $[0, 1]$ (i.e., $f(c) = c$ for some $c \in [0,1]$). The fixed point of f(x) = 1/(x+1) on [0,1] is the golden ratio (√5−1)/2 ≈ 0.618, since φ satisfies 1/(φ+1) = φ ⟺ φ² + φ = 1. g(x) = f(x) - x = \frac{1}{x+1} - x Define g(x) = f(x) − x. A fixed point of f is a zero of g. g(0) = \frac{1}{1} - 0 = 1 > 0 At x = 0: f(0) = 1 > 0. g(1) = \frac{1}{2} - 1 = -\frac{1}{2} < 0 At x = 1: f(1) = 1/2 < 1. g \text{ is continuous on } [0,1] \text{ (denominator } x+1 \neq 0) No discontinuities since x+1 ≥ 1 > 0 on [0,1]. \therefore \exists c \in (0,1): g(c) = 0 \Rightarrow f(c) = c IVT guarantees a fixed point. (c = (√5 − 1)/2 ≈ 0.618, the golden ratio!) IVT for a Temperature Problem A thermometer reads 68°F at noon and 85°F at 6pm. Assuming temperature changed continuously, prove it was exactly 75°F at some point between noon and 6pm. The IVT applies to any quantity that changes continuously — temperature, pressure, stock prices (approximately), population size. The theorem formalizes "you have to pass through every intermediate value." T(t): \text{temperature at time } t \text{ (noon} = 0\text{, 6pm} = 6\text{)}. Model temperature as a continuous function of time. T(0) = 68 \quad \text{and} \quad T(6) = 85 Given values. 68 < 75 < 85 75 lies strictly between T(0) and T(6). T \text{ is continuous (physical temperature changes continuously)} Continuity assumption: the physical world does not allow temperature to jump instantaneously. \therefore \exists t^* \in (0, 6) \text{ with } T(t^*) = 75°F By IVT, the temperature was exactly 75°F at some time between noon and 6pm. Prove that for any continuous function $f: [0,1] \to [0,1]$, there exists $c \in [0,1]$ such that $f(c) = c$. (This is the 1D Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem.) Define $g(x) = f(x) - x$. Evaluate $g$ at the endpoints of $[0,1]$. In all three cases, there exists a fixed point c ∈ [0,1] with f(c) = c. QED. g(x) = f(x) - x Define g. We need to show g has a zero. g(0) = f(0) - 0 = f(0) \geq 0 Since f maps [0,1] to [0,1], f(0) ≥ 0. g(1) = f(1) - 1 \leq 0 Since f(1) ≤ 1 (f maps [0,1] to [0,1]). \text{Case 1: } g(0) = 0 \Rightarrow f(0) = 0 \checkmark Fixed point found. \text{Case 2: } g(1) = 0 \Rightarrow f(1) = 1 \checkmark Fixed point found. \text{Case 3: } g(0) > 0 > g(1) \Rightarrow \exists c \in (0,1): g(c) = 0 By IVT (g is continuous as a composition/difference of continuous functions). Show that at any given moment, there exist two antipodal points on Earth's equator with exactly the same temperature. (Borsuk-Ulam Theorem in 1D.) Let T(θ) be temperature at angle θ on the equator. Consider the function g(θ) = T(θ) - T(θ + π). What is g(0) + g(π)? By IVT applied to g(θ) = T(θ) − T(θ+π), there always exist antipodal points on the equator with the same temperature. This is the 1D Borsuk-Ulam theorem. T(\theta): \text{continuous temperature function on the equator.} θ ranges from 0 to 2π. g(\theta) = T(\theta) - T(\theta + \pi) g measures the temperature difference between antipodal points. g(0) = T(0) - T(\pi) Evaluate at θ = 0. g(\pi) = T(\pi) - T(2\pi) = T(\pi) - T(0) = -g(0) Using T(2π) = T(0) (temperature is periodic, same point). g(0) \cdot g(\pi) = g(0) \cdot (-g(0)) = -[g(0)]^2 \leq 0 Either g(0) = 0 (antipodal match at θ=0) or g(0) and g(π) have opposite signs. \therefore \exists \theta^* \in [0, \pi]: g(\theta^*) = 0 By IVT, there is an angle where the temperature difference is zero — antipodal equal temperatures. Continuity IVT requires continuity — understand what continuity means first. Introduction to Limits Limits underpin the definition of continuity which underpins IVT. Newton's Method Newton's method is a faster root-finding algorithm that also relies on continuity and differentiability. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof

documents.subtitle: The four ways a function can fail to be continuous — and why each is different

The graph of f is the line y = x+2 with a hole at (2, 4). f(2) =  rac{0}{0} 	ext{ (undefined)}. The function is undefined at x = 2.  rac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2} =  rac{(x-2)(x+2)}{x-2} = x+2 \quad (x 
eq 2) Factor and cancel for x ≠ 2. \lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) = \lim_{x 	o 2} (x+2) = 4 The limit exists and equals 4. 	ext{Type: Removable discontinuity (hole at } (2, 4)	ext{)}. Limit exists but function undefined there. Fix: define f(2) = 4. Classify Discontinuity in a Piecewise Function Classify discontinuities of $f(x) =  egin{cases} x + 1 & x < 1 \ 3 & x = 1 \ x^2 & x > 1 \end{cases}$   Jump discontinuity at x = 1. The function value 3 is irrelevant to the classification — what matters is the one-sided limits. Classify Infinite Discontinuity Classify the discontinuity of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{(x-3)^2}$ at $x = 3$.   Infinite discontinuity (vertical asymptote) at x = 3. Both sides approach +∞, so the graph shoots upward on both sides of x = 3. \lim_{x 	o 3^-}  rac{1}{(x-3)^2} As x → 3 from the left, (x−3)² → 0⁺, so the fraction → +∞. \lim_{x 	o 3^+}  rac{1}{(x-3)^2} = +\infty As x → 3 from the right, same: (x−3)² → 0⁺, fraction → +∞. 	ext{Type: Infinite discontinuity.} Both one-sided limits are +∞. This is a vertical asymptote. 	ext{Note: Both sides go to } +\infty 	ext{ (not opposite signs).} Unlike 1/x, the square in the denominator makes both sides positive. Identify and Remove a Discontinuity Find all discontinuities of $g(x) = \dfrac{\sin(x)}{x}$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$, classify them, and extend $g$ to a continuous function.   One removable discontinuity at x = 0. The sinc function is the continuous extension. It appears everywhere in Fourier analysis and signal processing. g(0) 	ext{ is undefined (division by zero)}. Potential discontinuity only at x = 0. \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin x}{x} = 1 The fundamental trig limit (proven geometrically via Squeeze Theorem). 	ext{Type: Removable discontinuity at } x = 0. Limit exists (= 1) but g(0) is undefined. 	ilde{g}(x) =  egin{cases} \sin(x)/x & x 
eq 0 \ 1 & x = 0 \end{cases} Define ĝ(0) = 1 to fill the hole. 	ilde{g} 	ext{ is continuous everywhere.} This extended function is called the sinc function (unnormalized) and is fundamental in signal processing.  For what value(s) of $a$ is $f(x) =  egin{cases} x^2 - 3 & x < 2 \ ax + 1 & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$ continuous everywhere? Then f(x) = x² − 3 for x < 2 and f(x) = 1 for x ≥ 2, with both pieces agreeing at x = 2 (value = 1).  \lim_{x 	o 2^-} f(x) = (2)^2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1 Left-hand limit: use the formula for x < 2. \lim_{x 	o 2^+} f(x) = a(2) + 1 = 2a + 1 Right-hand limit: use the formula for x ≥ 2. f(2) = a(2) + 1 = 2a + 1 Function value at x = 2 (since 2 ≥ 2, use the second piece). 1 = 2a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 0 Set left-hand limit = right-hand limit and solve for a.  Prove that $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ cannot be extended to a continuous function at $x = 0$ by any definition of $f(0)$. Show that for any proposed value L = f(0), you can find x values arbitrarily close to 0 where f(x) is far from L. Use the fact that sin(1/x) = 1 when x = 2/(π + 4kπ) and sin(1/x) = -1 when x = 2/(3π + 4kπ). No value of f(0) makes sin(1/x) continuous at 0. The oscillating discontinuity is essential.  	ext{Suppose } f(0) = L 	ext{ for some } L \in [-1, 1]. Assume for contradiction that some extension works. x_k =  rac{2}{\pi + 4k\pi} 	o 0 	ext{ as } k 	o \infty, \quad \sin(1/x_k) = 1 These x-values approach 0 but f(x_k) = 1 always. y_k =  rac{2}{3\pi + 4k\pi} 	o 0 	ext{ as } k 	o \infty, \quad \sin(1/y_k) = -1 These x-values approach 0 but f(y_k) = -1 always. 	ext{If } L = 1: |f(y_k) - L| = 2

documents.proofs: **Why oscillating discontinuities are "worse."** For a removable discontinuity, we can extend the function continuously. For a jump discontinuity, we can integrate over it (the single "gap" has zero width and does not affect the integral). But for oscillating discontinuities like $\sin(1/x)$ at $x = 0$: the function is bounded but its set of oscillations accumulates at 0, making it Riemann integrable (the discontinuity set $\{0\}$ has measure zero) — yet the behavior is chaotic and no simple description of the limit exists. **Connection to differentiability.** A function that is differentiable at $c$ must be continuous at $c$ (contrapositive: discontinuous $\Rightarrow$ not differentiable). But continuity does NOT imply differentiability — the absolute value function $|x|$ is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at $x = 0$ (a corner, not a discontinuity). The Weierstrass function is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere — an extreme case. **Lebesgue's improvement.** Henri Lebesgue (1902) developed a theory of integration that handles more discontinuities than Riemann's. The Lebesgue integral of any bounded function exists as long as the discontinuity set has "Lebesgue measure zero." Roughly: if the set of discontinuities fits inside intervals of total length less than $\varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, it is OK. This is why Lebesgue integration is used in advanced probability theory and functional analysis. Differentiability Implies Continuity If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$. Proof: $f(c+h) - f(c) = h \cdot \frac{f(c+h)-f(c)}{h} \to 0 \cdot f'(c) = 0$ as $h \to 0$. So $\lim_{x\to c} f(x) = f(c)$.
documents.applications:
Engineers, physicists, and economists encounter discontinuous functions constantly: electrical switches (voltage jumps from 0 to 120V instantly), tax brackets (your marginal rate jumps at income thresholds), fluid mechanics (shock waves create jumps in pressure and density). Knowing the type of discontinuity tells you whether it can be fixed, whether limits exist, and what mathematical tools apply. A "removable" discontinuity is just a missing point — easily patched. A "jump" discontinuity means the function truly has two distinct behaviors. An "infinite" discontinuity signals a vertical asymptote. Each type has different physical meaning. The Four Types at a Glance **Removable**: lim exists, but f(c) ≠ limit (or f undefined). Graph: a hole.
**Jump**: left and right limits exist but are unequal. Graph: a vertical gap.
**Infinite**: at least one one-sided limit is ±∞. Graph: vertical asymptote.
**Oscillating**: no limit exists and it is not infinite. Graph: infinite wiggling. How to Classify a Discontinuity (1) Check if the limit from the left equals the limit from the right. (2) If yes and it's finite → removable. (3) If both one-sided limits are finite but unequal → jump. (4) If either one-sided limit is ±∞ → infinite. (5) If no limit exists for any other reason → oscillating. "Removable" Does Not Mean the Function Is Continuous A removable discontinuity means we COULD make the function continuous by defining or redefining f at one point. But the original function is still discontinuous there. We just have an easy fix available. Jump + Infinite = Essential (Non-Removable) Both jump and infinite discontinuities are sometimes called "essential" or "non-removable" discontinuities, because no single value of f(c) makes the function continuous. Only removable discontinuities can be fixed by redefining the function at one point. Tax Brackets: Jump Discontinuities in Your Life In many tax systems, the marginal rate changes at income thresholds: you pay 10% up to $10K, 22% from $10K-$40K, etc. The marginal tax rate function has jump discontinuities at each bracket boundary. This is why tax planning exists — behavior near the jump point matters a lot.
documents.content:
A GPS plots your position every second. Between updates, it interpolates smoothly. But if it loses signal and jumps to a new location — that's a discontinuity. Can you tell, just by looking at a graph, exactly what went wrong? Engineers, physicists, and economists encounter discontinuous functions constantly: electrical switches (voltage jumps from 0 to 120V instantly), tax brackets (your marginal rate jumps at income thresholds), fluid mechanics (shock waves create jumps in pressure and density). Knowing the type of discontinuity tells you whether it can be fixed, whether limits exist, and what mathematical tools apply. A "removable" discontinuity is just a missing point — easily patched. A "jump" discontinuity means the function truly has two distinct behaviors. An "infinite" discontinuity signals a vertical asymptote. Each type has different physical meaning. Continuity means the graph has no breaks — you can draw it without lifting your pencil. A discontinuity is any break, gap, or bad behavior in the graph. But not all breaks are equal. Mathematicians classify discontinuities into four types based on what the limits are doing. **Type 1: Removable Discontinuity (a "hole").** The two-sided limit exists — approaching from the left and right gives the same value — but either $f(c)$ is undefined, or $f(c)$ equals something different from the limit. The graph looks like a normal curve with a single point missing or relocated. Example: $f(x) = (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2)$. At $x = 2$: division by zero. But $\lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) = 4$. The limit exists! If we just define $f(2) = 4$, the function becomes continuous. The discontinuity was "removable" — a hole we can fill. **Type 2: Jump Discontinuity.** The left-hand limit and right-hand limit both exist but are different. The graph "jumps" from one value to another. Example: $f(x) = 0$ for $x < 0$ and $f(x) = 1$ for $x \geq 0$. As $x 	o 0^-$, $f(x) 	o 0$. As $x 	o 0^+$, $f(x) 	o 1$. These are different, so the two-sided limit DNE. You cannot remove this discontinuity — the function has fundamentally different behavior on the two sides. Think: a switch that goes from OFF (0) to ON (1) in zero time. **Type 3: Infinite Discontinuity (vertical asymptote).** At least one one-sided limit is $\pm \infty$. The graph shoots off to infinity. Example: $f(x) = 1/x$ at $x = 0$. As $x 	o 0^+$, $f(x) 	o +\infty$. As $x 	o 0^-$, $f(x) 	o -\infty$. Neither limit exists as a real number. Vertical asymptotes are infinite discontinuities. In physics: gravitational force $F = Gm_1 m_2/r^2$ has an infinite discontinuity at $r = 0$ — two objects cannot occupy the same point. **Type 4: Oscillating Discontinuity (essential).** The function oscillates so wildly near the point that no limit exists, even $\pm\infty$. The classic example is $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ at $x = 0$. As $x 	o 0$, the argument $1/x 	o \infty$ and $\sin(1/x)$ oscillates infinitely many times between $-1$ and $+1$, never settling. No limit exists. This is the "worst" kind of discontinuity — no reasonable value can patch it. **The classification matters for calculus.** Integration: removable discontinuities do not affect integrals (a single point has zero area). Jump discontinuities create a change in the integral's value at that point. Infinite discontinuities may create improper integrals that diverge. Differentiation: none of these discontinuous functions are differentiable at the point of discontinuity — differentiability implies continuity. **Historical note.** Bernhard Riemann (1826–1866) gave the first systematic analysis of discontinuities. He showed that a function with only finitely many jump discontinuities is still integrable in his sense (the Riemann integral). This was revolutionary — before Riemann, most mathematicians thought discontinuous functions were "pathological." Today we know that many important functions (step functions, square waves, probability distributions) are naturally discontinuous. **Formal definition of continuity.** $f$ is continuous at $c$ iff (1) $f(c)$ is defined, (2) $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x)$ exists, and (3) $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = f(c)$. A discontinuity is any failure of (1), (2), or (3). **Removable discontinuity.** $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x) = L$ exists (and is finite), but either $f(c) 
eq L$ or $f(c)$ is undefined. The extended function $	ilde{f}(x) = f(x)$ for $x 
eq c$, $	ilde{f}(c) = L$ is continuous. Common cause: factor cancellation creates a hole. Example: $f(x) =  rac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} = x + 1$ for $x 
eq 1$; hole at $x = 1$. **Jump discontinuity.** Both one-sided limits exist and are finite: $\lim_{x 	o c^-} f(x) = L^-$ and $\lim_{x 	o c^+} f(x) = L^+$, but $L^- 
eq L^+$. The "jump" is $|L^+ - L^-|$. Example: the Heaviside step function $H(x) = 0$ for $x < 0$, $H(x) = 1$ for $x \geq 0$ has a jump of 1 at $x = 0$. **Infinite discontinuity.** At least one of $\lim_{x 	o c^-} f(x)$ or $\lim_{x 	o c^+} f(x)$ is $+\infty$ or $-\infty$. This signals a vertical asymptote. Example: $f(x) = 	an x$ has infinite discontinuities at $x = \pi/2 + n\pi$. **Oscillating (essential) discontinuity.** $\lim_{x 	o c} f(x)$ does not exist and is not $\pm \infty$. Example: $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ at $x = 0$. For any $L$, we can find $x$ arbitrarily close to 0 with $|f(x) - L| > 0.5$, so no limit can exist. **Why oscillating discontinuities are "worse."** For a removable discontinuity, we can extend the function continuously. For a jump discontinuity, we can integrate over it (the single "gap" has zero width and does not affect the integral). But for oscillating discontinuities like $\sin(1/x)$ at $x = 0$: the function is bounded but its set of oscillations accumulates at 0, making it Riemann integrable (the discontinuity set $\{0\}$ has measure zero) — yet the behavior is chaotic and no simple description of the limit exists. **Connection to differentiability.** A function that is differentiable at $c$ must be continuous at $c$ (contrapositive: discontinuous $\Rightarrow$ not differentiable). But continuity does NOT imply differentiability — the absolute value function $|x|$ is continuous everywhere but not differentiable at $x = 0$ (a corner, not a discontinuity). The Weierstrass function is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere — an extreme case. **Lebesgue's improvement.** Henri Lebesgue (1902) developed a theory of integration that handles more discontinuities than Riemann's. The Lebesgue integral of any bounded function exists as long as the discontinuity set has "Lebesgue measure zero." Roughly: if the set of discontinuities fits inside intervals of total length less than $\varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon > 0$, it is OK. This is why Lebesgue integration is used in advanced probability theory and functional analysis. Differentiability Implies Continuity If $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f$ is continuous at $c$. Proof: $f(c+h) - f(c) = h \cdot  rac{f(c+h)-f(c)}{h} 	o 0 \cdot f'(c) = 0$ as $h 	o 0$. So $\lim_{x	o c} f(x) = f(c)$. The Four Types at a Glance **Removable**: lim exists, but f(c) ≠ limit (or f undefined). Graph: a hole.
**Jump**: left and right limits exist but are unequal. Graph: a vertical gap.
**Infinite**: at least one one-sided limit is ±∞. Graph: vertical asymptote.
**Oscillating**: no limit exists and it is not infinite. Graph: infinite wiggling. How to Classify a Discontinuity (1) Check if the limit from the left equals the limit from the right. (2) If yes and it's finite → removable. (3) If both one-sided limits are finite but unequal → jump. (4) If either one-sided limit is ±∞ → infinite. (5) If no limit exists for any other reason → oscillating. "Removable" Does Not Mean the Function Is Continuous A removable discontinuity means we COULD make the function continuous by defining or redefining f at one point. But the original function is still discontinuous there. We just have an easy fix available. Jump + Infinite = Essential (Non-Removable) Both jump and infinite discontinuities are sometimes called "essential" or "non-removable" discontinuities, because no single value of f(c) makes the function continuous. Only removable discontinuities can be fixed by redefining the function at one point. Tax Brackets: Jump Discontinuities in Your Life In many tax systems, the marginal rate changes at income thresholds: you pay 10% up to $10K, 22% from $10K-$40K, etc. The marginal tax rate function has jump discontinuities at each bracket boundary. This is why tax planning exists — behavior near the jump point matters a lot. Classification Summary **Removable**: $\lim_{x	o c} f(x)$ exists (finite), $f(c) 
eq$ limit or undefined. **Jump**: $\lim_{x	o c^-}$ and $\lim_{x	o c^+}$ both finite but unequal. **Infinite**: $\lim_{x	o c^\pm} = \pm\infty$. **Oscillating**: none of the above. Riemann Integrability A bounded function on $[a,b]$ is Riemann integrable if and only if its set of discontinuities has measure zero. Equivalently: finitely many jump or removable discontinuities are OK for integration; infinitely many in a "dense" set are not. Removable Discontinuity: Hole vs. Redefined Value Compare three scenarios: the function has a hole (undefined), the hole is filled at the wrong height (still discontinuous), and the hole is filled correctly (continuous). Only the last is continuous. Jump Discontinuity Left and right particles approach from opposite sides but land at different heights — the two-sided limit does not exist. Oscillating Discontinuity: sin(1/x) As x → 0, sin(1/x) oscillates infinitely rapidly. No limit exists. Story Viz — Broken Function Book 3 Chapter 5 story visualization on indeterminate forms and removable holes. Classify the Discontinuity of a Rational Function Classify the discontinuity of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$ at $x = 2$. Removable discontinuity at x = 2. The graph of f is the line y = x+2 with a hole at (2, 4). f(2) =  rac{0}{0} 	ext{ (undefined)}. The function is undefined at x = 2.  rac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2} =  rac{(x-2)(x+2)}{x-2} = x+2 \quad (x 
eq 2) Factor and cancel for x ≠ 2. \lim_{x 	o 2} f(x) = \lim_{x 	o 2} (x+2) = 4 The limit exists and equals 4. 	ext{Type: Removable discontinuity (hole at } (2, 4)	ext{)}. Limit exists but function undefined there. Fix: define f(2) = 4. Classify Discontinuity in a Piecewise Function Classify discontinuities of $f(x) =  egin{cases} x + 1 & x < 1 \ 3 & x = 1 \ x^2 & x > 1 \end{cases}$ Jump discontinuity at x = 1. The left limit (2) and right limit (1) are both finite but different. Neither equals f(1) = 3. \lim_{x 	o 1^-} f(x) = \lim_{x 	o 1^-} (x+1) = 2 Left-hand limit: approach from x < 1. \lim_{x 	o 1^+} f(x) = \lim_{x 	o 1^+} x^2 = 1 Right-hand limit: approach from x > 1. f(1) = 3 Function value at x = 1. L^- = 2 
eq 1 = L^+ Both one-sided limits exist but are unequal. 	ext{Type: Jump discontinuity with jump size } |2 - 1| = 1. The function value 3 is irrelevant to the classification — what matters is the one-sided limits. Classify Infinite Discontinuity Classify the discontinuity of $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{(x-3)^2}$ at $x = 3$. Infinite discontinuity (vertical asymptote) at x = 3. Both sides approach +∞, so the graph shoots upward on both sides of x = 3. \lim_{x 	o 3^-}  rac{1}{(x-3)^2} As x → 3 from the left, (x−3)² → 0⁺, so the fraction → +∞. \lim_{x 	o 3^+}  rac{1}{(x-3)^2} = +\infty As x → 3 from the right, same: (x−3)² → 0⁺, fraction → +∞. 	ext{Type: Infinite discontinuity.} Both one-sided limits are +∞. This is a vertical asymptote. 	ext{Note: Both sides go to } +\infty 	ext{ (not opposite signs).} Unlike 1/x, the square in the denominator makes both sides positive. Identify and Remove a Discontinuity Find all discontinuities of $g(x) = \dfrac{\sin(x)}{x}$ for $x \in \mathbb{R}$, classify them, and extend $g$ to a continuous function. One removable discontinuity at x = 0. The sinc function is the continuous extension. It appears everywhere in Fourier analysis and signal processing. g(0) 	ext{ is undefined (division by zero)}. Potential discontinuity only at x = 0. \lim_{x 	o 0}  rac{\sin x}{x} = 1 The fundamental trig limit (proven geometrically via Squeeze Theorem). 	ext{Type: Removable discontinuity at } x = 0. Limit exists (= 1) but g(0) is undefined. 	ilde{g}(x) =  egin{cases} \sin(x)/x & x 
eq 0 \ 1 & x = 0 \end{cases} Define ĝ(0) = 1 to fill the hole. 	ilde{g} 	ext{ is continuous everywhere.} This extended function is called the sinc function (unnormalized) and is fundamental in signal processing. For what value(s) of $a$ is $f(x) =  egin{cases} x^2 - 3 & x < 2 \ ax + 1 & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$ continuous everywhere? For continuity at x = 2, you need the left-hand limit = right-hand limit = f(2). Compute both and solve for a. a = 0. Then f(x) = x² − 3 for x < 2 and f(x) = 1 for x ≥ 2, with both pieces agreeing at x = 2 (value = 1). \lim_{x 	o 2^-} f(x) = (2)^2 - 3 = 4 - 3 = 1 Left-hand limit: use the formula for x < 2. \lim_{x 	o 2^+} f(x) = a(2) + 1 = 2a + 1 Right-hand limit: use the formula for x ≥ 2. f(2) = a(2) + 1 = 2a + 1 Function value at x = 2 (since 2 ≥ 2, use the second piece). 1 = 2a + 1 \Rightarrow a = 0 Set left-hand limit = right-hand limit and solve for a. Prove that $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ cannot be extended to a continuous function at $x = 0$ by any definition of $f(0)$. Show that for any proposed value L = f(0), you can find x values arbitrarily close to 0 where f(x) is far from L. Use the fact that sin(1/x) = 1 when x = 2/(π + 4kπ) and sin(1/x) = -1 when x = 2/(3π + 4kπ). No value of f(0) makes sin(1/x) continuous at 0. The oscillating discontinuity is essential. 	ext{Suppose } f(0) = L 	ext{ for some } L \in [-1, 1]. Assume for contradiction that some extension works. x_k =  rac{2}{\pi + 4k\pi} 	o 0 	ext{ as } k 	o \infty, \quad \sin(1/x_k) = 1 These x-values approach 0 but f(x_k) = 1 always. y_k =  rac{2}{3\pi + 4k\pi} 	o 0 	ext{ as } k 	o \infty, \quad \sin(1/y_k) = -1 These x-values approach 0 but f(y_k) = -1 always. 	ext{If } L = 1: |f(y_k) - L| = 2 
ot	o 0. f stays at -1 along the y_k sequence, not at L = 1. 	ext{If } L = -1: |f(x_k) - L| = 2 
ot	o 0. f stays at 1 along the x_k sequence, not at L = -1. 	ext{Any other } L: 	ext{ both sequences give } |f - L| \geq 1 > 0. For any L, at least one of the sequences is distance ≥ 1 from L. 	herefore 
exists \lim_{x 	o 0} \sin(1/x) \Rightarrow 	ext{no continuous extension.} QED. The oscillating discontinuity cannot be removed by any choice of f(0). Continuity This lesson builds directly on the definition of continuity. Intermediate Value Theorem IVT applies only to continuous functions — discontinuities break it. Limits at Infinity Infinite discontinuities and vertical asymptotes connect to horizontal asymptotes. Improper Integrals Infinite discontinuities in the integrand create improper integrals. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity

documents.title: The Epsilon-Delta Definition
documents.subtitle: Making "close to" mathematically airtight
documents.tags: epsilon-delta ε-δ formal definition proof technique Cauchy Weierstrass rigor
documents.aliases: approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior continuous discontinuous jump discontinuity removable discontinuity epsilon-delta formal limit proof proof of limit rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof
documents.assignment: ε-δ Proof for a Linear Function (Scratchwork + Proof) Prove using ε-δ that \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 4} (3x - 5) = 7.   For linear functions f(x) = mx + b with m ≠ 0, δ = ε/|m| always works. The proof is essentially one line: |f(x)−L| = |m|·|x−c| < |m|·δ = ε. The scaling factor is the slope. \textbf{Phase 1 — Scratch work:} Figure out what δ should be before writing the formal proof. |(3x-5) - 7| = |3x - 12| = 3|x - 4| Simplify the output error. It factors as 3 times the input error. \text{Need: } 3|x-4| < \varepsilon \iff |x-4| < \frac{\varepsilon}{3} So δ = ε/3 should work. Now write the formal proof. \textbf{Phase 2 — Formal proof:}  \text{Given } \varepsilon > 0. \text{ Let } \delta = \frac{\varepsilon}{3}. State your choice of δ upfront. \text{Suppose } 0 < |x-4| < \delta. Assume x is within δ of 4 (but x ≠ 4). |(3x-5) - 7| = 3|x-4| < 3\delta = 3 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{3} = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare The chain: output error = 3·(input error) < 3·δ = 3·(ε/3) = ε. ε-δ Proof for a Quadratic (The min Trick) Prove that \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 3} x^2 = 9.   The min(1, ε/7) trick is the standard technique for nonlinear ε-δ proofs: restrict δ once to bound variable factors, then solve. This method works for any polynomial — the only change is the specific bound M for the variable factor. |x^2 - 9| = |x-3|\cdot|x+3| Factor the output error using the difference of squares. \text{Problem: } |x+3| \text{ is not a constant — it depends on } x. Unlike the linear case, we can't immediately solve for δ. \text{Trick: restrict } \delta \leq 1, \text{ so } |x-3| < 1 \implies 2 < x < 4. By keeping x within 1 of 3, we bound the variable factor. \text{Then } 5 < x+3 < 7, \text{ so } |x+3| < 7. Now |x+3| has a constant upper bound on this interval. |x^2-9| = |x-3|\cdot|x+3| < 7|x-3| Substitute the bound. \text{Need: } 7|x-3| < \varepsilon \iff |x-3| < \varepsilon/7.  \text{Choose } \delta = \min\!\left(1,\; \frac{\varepsilon}{7}\right). The min ensures BOTH conditions: δ ≤ 1 (so the bound holds) AND δ ≤ ε/7 (so the product is small). \textbf{Proof: } \text{Given } \varepsilon > 0, \text{ let } \delta = \min(1, \varepsilon/7).  \text{If } 0 < |x-3| < \delta:\quad |x^2-9| = |x-3||x+3| < \delta \cdot 7 \leq \frac{\varepsilon}{7} \cdot 7 = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare  ε-δ Proof for 1/x (Bounding Away from Zero) Prove that \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{2}.   For rational functions, the extra challenge is keeping the denominator away from zero. The first restriction (δ ≤ 1) solves this. Once you have a lower bound on |x|, the rest follows the same pattern. \left|\frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{2}\right| = \left|\frac{2-x}{2x}\right| = \frac{|x-2|}{2|x|} Combine fractions and simplify. The output error involves 1/|x|, which blows up near 0. \text{Restrict: } \delta \leq 1, \text{ so } |x-2| < 1 \implies 1 < x < 3. This keeps x away from 0, preventing 1/|x| from exploding. \text{Then } |x| > 1, \text{ so } \frac{1}{|x|} < 1. On (1,3), the factor 1/|x| is bounded by 1. \frac{|x-2|}{2|x|} < \frac{|x-2|}{2 \cdot 1} = \frac{|x-2|}{2}  \text{Need: } \frac{|x-2|}{2} < \varepsilon \iff |x-2| < 2\varepsilon.  \text{Choose } \delta = \min(1, 2\varepsilon).  \textbf{Proof: } \text{Given } \varepsilon > 0, \delta = \min(1, 2\varepsilon). \text{ If } 0 < |x-2| < \delta:  \left|\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2}\right| = \frac{|x-2|}{2|x|} < \frac{\delta}{2} \leq \frac{2\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare  Using ε-δ to Prove a Limit Does NOT Exist Prove that \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 0} \sin\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) does not exist.   The function oscillates between −1 and +1 infinitely often near x=0, so no single value L can satisfy the ε-δ condition. This is the formal version of "the function has no settling point." The key technique: finding two sequences approaching 0 whose function values disagree by more than 2ε. \text{Suppose for contradiction: } \lim_{x \to 0} \sin(1/x) = L \text{ for some } L. Try to derive a contradiction from the ε-δ definition. \text{Take } \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2}. \text{ Then } \exists\, \delta > 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 < |x| < \delta \implies |\sin(1/x) - L| < \frac{1}{2}. If the limit existed, we could satisfy this tolerance. \text{Choose } x_1 = \frac{1}{2n\pi + \pi/2} \text{ (so } \sin(1/x_1) = 1\text{)} For large enough n, x₁ is within δ of 0. \text{Choose } x_2 = \frac{1}{2n\pi - \pi/2} \text{ (so } \sin(1/x_2) = -1\text{)} x₂ is also within δ of 0 (for large n). |\sin(1/x_1) - L| < 1/2 \implies |1 - L| < 1/2 \implies L > 1/2 From the ε-δ condition applied to x₁. |\sin(1/x_2) - L| < 1/2 \implies |-1 - L| < 1/2 \implies L < -1/2 From the ε-δ condition applied to x₂. L > 1/2 \text{ AND } L < -1/2 \quad \Rightarrow\!\Leftarrow Contradiction. No single L can be within 1/2 of both 1 and −1. Physics: Precision Measurement as an ε-δ Problem A thermometer measures temperature T(x) = 100 + 0.3x − 0.01x² degrees Celsius, where x is the sensor voltage in millivolts. To guarantee the temperature reading is within ε = 0.5°C of the true value T(10) = 102°C, how precisely must the voltage be controlled?   The voltage must be controlled within ±1 mV of 10 mV to guarantee the temperature reading is within ±0.5°C. This is a real engineering ε-δ problem: given an output tolerance, find the required input precision. Every calibration procedure in science and manufacturing follows this pattern. |T(x) - 102| = |100 + 0.3x - 0.01x^2 - 102| The output error: how far is the reading from the true value? = |-0.01x^2 + 0.3x - 2| Simplify. = 0.01|x^2 - 30x + 200| = 0.01|(x-10)(x-20)| Factor: x²−30x+200 = (x−10)(x−20). = 0.01|x-10|\cdot|x-20| The error is proportional to |x−10| (the input deviation). \text{Restrict: } |x-10| < 1, \text{ so } 9 < x < 11. Bound the variable factor |x−20|. \text{Then } |x-20| \in (9, 11), \text{ so } |x-20| < 11.  |T(x)-102| < 0.01 \cdot |x-10| \cdot 11 = 0.11|x-10|  \text{Need: } 0.11|x-10| < 0.5 \implies |x-10| < 4.55  \delta = \min(1, 4.55) = 1 \text{ mV} The restriction δ ≤ 1 is the binding constraint. Graph-First ε-δ Extraction (From the Calc Problem Lab) For f(x)=\sqrt{x+1} at a=3 and L=2, find a valid δ when ε=0.5 using the graph-box method.   The graph method and formal definition agree: choosing δ=1.75 guarantees |f(x)-2|<0.5 whenever 0<|x-3|<δ. L\pm\varepsilon: \ y=2\pm0.5 \Rightarrow y=2.5,\ 1.5 Draw horizontal bounds of the epsilon-strip. \sqrt{x+1}=2.5 \Rightarrow x=5.25, \quad \sqrt{x+1}=1.5 \Rightarrow x=1.25 Read/solve x exit points where graph meets strip boundaries. |5.25-3|=2.25,\quad |1.25-3|=1.75 Measure both horizontal distances from a. \delta=\min(2.25,1.75)=1.75 Take the smaller side so both directions stay in-band. Scratch-Then-Formal Workflow (From the Calc Problem Lab) Prove with full workflow that lim_{x->2} x^2 = 4.   This two-phase pattern is the stable template for nearly every nonlinear ε-δ proof. \textbf{Scratch: } |x^2-4|=|x-2||x+2| Start by isolating the controllable factor |x-2|. |x-2|<1 \Rightarrow 1<x<3 \Rightarrow |x+2|<5 Local cage to bound the uncontrolled factor. |x^2-4|<5|x-2| Now only one controllable factor remains. \delta=\min(1,\varepsilon/5) One part keeps the cage valid, one part hits epsilon. 0<|x-2|<\delta \Rightarrow |x^2-4|<5\delta\le 5(\varepsilon/5)=\varepsilon Formal proof chain.  Use ε-δ to prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 5} (4x + 1) = 21. Compute |f(x) − 21| = |4x+1−21| = |4x−20| = 4|x−5|. Choose δ = ε/4. δ = ε/4  |(4x+1) - 21| = |4x-20| = 4|x-5| Output error = 4 × input error. \text{Need: } 4|x-5| < \varepsilon \iff |x-5| < \varepsilon/4  \text{Let } \delta = \varepsilon/4. \text{ Then } |(4x+1)-21| = 4|x-5| < 4\delta = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare   Prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 1} (x^2 + 2x) = 3 using ε-δ. |x²+2x−3| = |(x−1)(x+3)|. Restrict δ ≤ 1 so |x+3| < 5. δ = min(1, ε/5)  |x^2+2x-3| = |(x-1)(x+3)| = |x-1||x+3|  \delta \leq 1 \implies 0 < x < 2 \implies 3 < x+3 < 5 \implies |x+3| < 5  |x^2+2x-3| < 5|x-1|. \text{ Need: } 5|x-1| < \varepsilon \iff |x-1| < \varepsilon/5  \delta = \min(1, \varepsilon/5).\;\blacksquare   Prove \displaystyle\lim_{x \to 4} \sqrt{x} = 2 using ε-δ. |√x − 2| = |x−4|/(√x + 2). Since √x + 2 ≥ 2, we get |√x−2| ≤ |x−4|/2. So δ = 2ε works. δ = 2ε  |\sqrt{x} - 2| = \frac{|\sqrt{x}-2||\sqrt{x}+2|}{|\sqrt{x}+2|} = \frac{|x-4|}{\sqrt{x}+2} Rationalize by multiplying and dividing by the conjugate √x + 2. \sqrt{x}+2 \geq 0+2 = 2 \quad \text{(for } x \geq 0\text{)} The denominator is at LEAST 2, so the fraction is at MOST |x−4|/2. |\sqrt{x}-2| \leq \frac{|x-4|}{2} No min trick needed! The bound works for all x ≥ 0. \text{Need: } \frac{|x-4|}{2} < \varepsilon \iff |x-4| < 2\varepsilon  \delta = 2\varepsilon. \text{ Then } |\sqrt{x}-2| \leq \frac{|x-4|}{2} < \frac{2\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare   Prove $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2}(x^2 + 3x) = 10$ using ε–δ. Factor: $|x^2+3x-10| = |(x-2)(x+5)|$. Restrict $\delta \leq 1$ so that $x \in (1,3)$ and $|x+5| < 8$. δ = min(1, ε/8)  |x^2 + 3x - 10| = |(x-2)(x+5)| = |x-2|\cdot|x+5| Factor the output error. \delta \leq 1 \Rightarrow 1 < x < 3 \Rightarrow 6 < x+5 < 8 \Rightarrow |x+5| < 8 Cage x near 2 to bound the variable factor. |(x^2+3x)-10| < 8|x-2|. \text{ Need: } 8|x-2| < \varepsilon \Rightarrow |x-2| < \varepsilon/8  \delta = \min(1,\, \varepsilon/8). \quad |x^2+3x-10| < 8\delta \leq 8 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{8} = \varepsilon.\;\blacksquare Both the cage (δ≤1) and the bound (δ≤ε/8) are satisfied simultaneously.
documents.proofs: Let us prove the Sum Law using ε-δ, to see how these proofs work at the foundational level. CLAIM: If lim(x→c) f(x) = L and lim(x→c) g(x) = M, then lim(x→c) [f(x)+g(x)] = L+M. PROOF: Given ε > 0. We need |(f(x)+g(x)) − (L+M)| < ε. By the triangle inequality: |(f+g) − (L+M)| = |(f−L) + (g−M)| ≤ |f−L| + |g−M|. So if we can make each term less than ε/2, the sum will be less than ε. Since lim f = L: ∃δ₁ > 0 such that 0 < |x−c| < δ₁ ⟹ |f(x)−L| < ε/2. Since lim g = M: ∃δ₂ > 0 such that 0 < |x−c| < δ₂ ⟹ |g(x)−M| < ε/2. Let δ = min(δ₁, δ₂). Then 0 < |x−c| < δ implies BOTH conditions hold simultaneously: |(f+g) − (L+M)| ≤ |f−L| + |g−M| < ε/2 + ε/2 = ε. ∎ This proof illustrates several key techniques: (1) the {{algebra:triangle-inequality|triangle inequality}} |a+b| ≤ |a|+|b| to safely split error accumulation; (2) the **ε/2 trick** — budget half the absolute tolerance for each term; (3) **taking the minimum** of multiple δ values so all local conditions explicitly hold simultaneously. These three tools govern nearly every ε-δ proof. Historically, this level of rigor was developed because of actual mathematical crises. In the 1800s, mathematicians discovered continuous functions that are nowhere differentiable (Weierstrass, 1872), convergent series of continuous functions with discontinuous limits (Cauchy got this wrong!), and space-filling curves (Peano, 1890). These "pathological" examples showed that geometric intuition could not be trusted — only rigorous definitions could prevent errors. Weierstrass's Monster (1872) Karl Weierstrass constructed a function that is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere — a curve so jagged it has no tangent line at any point. This shocked the mathematical world: how could something continuous be so chaotic? The example proved that rigorous definitions (not pictures) were essential. The function is f(x) = Σ aⁿ cos(bⁿπx), with 0 < a < 1 and b an odd integer with ab > 1+3π/2. ε-δ for One-Sided Limits \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) = L \iff \forall\, \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta > 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 < x - c < \delta \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon \\ \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L \iff \forall\, \varepsilon > 0,\; \exists\, \delta > 0 \text{ s.t. } 0 < c - x < \delta \implies |f(x) - L| < \varepsilon
documents.applications: For 150 years after Newton and Leibniz, calculus worked. Engineers built bridges, astronomers predicted eclipses, and physicists derived the laws of motion — all using limits, derivatives, and integrals. But nobody could explain *why* it worked. The philosopher George Berkeley mocked the foundations: Newton's "infinitesimals" were "the ghosts of departed quantities." Was calculus built on mysticism? In the 1820s, Augustin-Louis Cauchy finally gave limits a precise definition: instead of vague talk about "approaching," he described a concrete game you can play with two numbers, ε and δ. Karl Weierstrass later polished the idea into the form we use today. The ε-δ definition didn't change a single computation — every answer Newton got was still correct — but it gave calculus an unshakable logical foundation. It's the mathematical equivalent of building codes: the bridge already stood, but now we can *prove* it won't collapse. Absolute Value as Distance |a − b| is the distance between a and b on the number line. So |x − 3| < 0.5 means "x is within 0.5 of 3," and |f(x) − 7| < ε means "f(x) is within ε of 7." The entire ε-δ definition is stated in the language of distances.
