This local product is exactly the pattern repeated layer-by-layer in deep learning backpropagation. \frac{dL}{dy} = y-t Differentiate the squared loss with respect to output y. \frac{dy}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) = y(1-y) Derivative of sigmoid activation. \frac{dz}{dw} = x For z = wx+b, derivative with respect to w is x. \frac{dL}{dw} = \frac{dL}{dy}\cdot\frac{dy}{dz}\cdot\frac{dz}{dw} = (y-t)\,y(1-y)\,x Multiply local derivatives along the computation graph. Hook Problem: Balloon Volume Rate A sphere has radius r(t)=2t. With V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3, find dV/dt at t=3.   At t=3, volume is increasing at 288\pi cubic units per time unit. V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3,\quad r=2t Volume depends on radius, and radius depends on time. \frac{dV}{dr}=4\pi r^2,\quad \frac{dr}{dt}=2 Differentiate each stage with respect to its own variable. \frac{dV}{dt}=\frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt}=4\pi r^2\cdot 2=8\pi r^2 Apply Leibniz chain form for related rates. Unit tracker: (cm^3/cm)*(cm/s)=cm^3/s, so multiplication is physically required. t=3 \Rightarrow r=2(3)=6 Evaluate the intermediate quantity first. \left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{t=3}=8\pi(6)^2=288\pi Substitute r=6 into the rate formula. Abstract Function Trap — Reading a Table \begin{array}{l} \text{Let } h(x) = f(g(x)). \text{ The table gives values at selected points:} \\[6pt] \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & f(x) & g(x) & f'(x) & g'(x) \\\hline 1 & 4 & 3 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 & 5 & 3 \\ 3 & 7 & 2 & -2 & 4 \end{array} \\[6pt] \text{Find } h'(2) \text{ and } h'(3). \end{array}   h'(2)=6 and h'(3)=20. The two-step rule: evaluate the inner function first, then look up the outer derivative at that value. On exams, reading f'(a) instead of f'(g(a)) is by far the most common Chain Rule error with tables. h'(x) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) Write the Chain Rule formula. This holds for any differentiable f and g — no explicit formula needed. h'(2) = f'\!\left(\underbrace{g(2)}_{?}\right) \cdot g'(2) To compute h'(2): we need f' evaluated at g(2), not at 2. Look up g(2) from the table first. g(2) = 1, \quad g'(2) = 3 \quad \text{(row } x=2\text{)} From the x=2 row: g(2)=1 and g'(2)=3. h'(2) = f'(\underbrace{1}_{g(2)}) \cdot 3 Substitute g(2)=1. We now need f'(1) — read from the x=1 row, not the x=2 row. f'(1) = 2 \implies h'(2) = 2 \cdot 3 = 6 From the x=1 row: f'(1)=2. Final answer: 2·3=6. h'(3) = f'(g(3)) \cdot g'(3) = f'(2) \cdot 4 = 5 \cdot 4 = 20 For h'(3): g(3)=2 (row x=3), g'(3)=4 (row x=3). Then f'(g(3))=f'(2)=5 (row x=2). Result: 5·4=20. Combo Rule Boss Fight — Product Rule + Chain Rule f(x) = x^3\sqrt{4x-1}. \text{ Find } f'(x).   f'(x) = x²(14x-3)/√(4x-1). The outer rule was Product Rule; Chain Rule fired inside √(4x-1). The #1 mistake is reaching for Chain Rule first on a product — always identify the outermost operation before picking a rule. f(x) = \underbrace{x^3}_{A(x)} \cdot \underbrace{\sqrt{4x-1}}_{B(x)} The outermost operation is multiplication of x³ and √(4x-1). Product Rule is the outer rule. Chain Rule will appear only when differentiating B(x). A(x) = x^3 \implies A'(x) = 3x^2 Differentiate A = x³ by the power rule. B(x) = (4x-1)^{1/2} \implies B'(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4x-1)^{-1/2}\cdot 4 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Differentiate B = (4x-1)^(1/2) by Chain Rule: outer gives (1/2)u^(-1/2), inner gives 4. Simplify: (1/2)·4 = 2. f'(x) = A'B + AB' = 3x^2\sqrt{4x-1} + x^3 \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Apply Product Rule: d/dx[AB] = A'B + AB'. = \frac{3x^2(4x-1) + 2x^3}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Write over common denominator √(4x-1). Multiply the first term top and bottom by √(4x-1). = \frac{x^2(12x - 3 + 2x)}{\sqrt{4x-1}} = \frac{x^2(14x-3)}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Factor out x² from the numerator: 3x²(4x-1)+2x³ = 12x³-3x²+2x³ = 14x³-3x² = x²(14x-3). The Ultimate Chain Rule Boss Fight h(x) = (2x + 1)^5 (3x - 2)^7. \; \text{Find } h'(x).   h'(x) = (2x + 1)⁴(3x - 2)⁶(72x + 1). This is the 'Ultimate Boss' check: Product Rule on the outside, two Chain Rules on the inside, and a sophisticated factoring finale. h(x) = f(x)g(x) \implies h'(x) = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x) This is first and foremost a Product Rule problem because two distinct blocks are being multiplied. f(x) = (2x+1)^5 \implies f'(x) = 10(2x+1)^4 Differentiate the first factor using the Chain Rule (outer power 5, inner deriv 2). g(x) = (3x-2)^7 \implies g'(x) = 21(3x-2)^6 Differentiate the second factor using the Chain Rule (outer power 7, inner deriv 3). h'(x) = 10(2x+1)^4(3x-2)^7 + 21(2x+1)^5(3x-2)^6 Substitute derivatives into the Product Rule formula. h'(x) = (2x+1)^4(3x-2)^6 \left[ 10(3x-2) + 21(2x+1) \right] Factor out the common terms: the lowest powers (4 and 6) of each block. = (2x+1)^4(3x-2)^6 (72x + 1) Expand and simplify inside the square brackets: 30x - 20 + 42x + 21 = 72x + 1.  \text{Differentiate } f(x) = (2x+1)^{10}. Treat (2x+1)^10 as a composition: outer function u^10, inner function 2x+1, and inner derivative 2. f'(x) = 20(2x+1)^9  \text{Outer: } F(u) = u^{10},\quad \text{Inner: } u = 2x+1 Identify the composition structure. F'(u) = 10u^9, \quad u' = 2 Differentiate each piece. f'(x) = 10(2x+1)^9 \cdot 2 = 20(2x+1)^9 Apply the chain rule and simplify.  \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{x^2+1}{x-1}}. Write as ((x²+1)/(x-1))^(1/2). The outer function is u^(1/2) and the inner function is the quotient (x²+1)/(x-1), which requires the quotient rule to differentiate. f'(x) = \dfrac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^{3/2}\sqrt{x^2+1}}  f(x) = \left(\frac{x^2+1}{x-1}\right)^{1/2} Rewrite the square root as a power. \text{Let } u = \frac{x^2+1}{x-1}, \text{ so } f = u^{1/2} Define the inner function as the quotient. \frac{df}{du} = \frac{1}{2}u^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}} Differentiate the outer square root. \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{2x(x-1) - (x^2+1)(1)}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{2x^2-2x-x^2-1}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{(x-1)^2} Apply the quotient rule to u = (x²+1)/(x-1). f'(x) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{x^2+1}{x-1}\right)^{-1/2} \cdot \frac{x^2-2x-1}{(x-1)^2} Combine via chain rule. = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{x-1}{x^2+1}} = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^{3/2}\sqrt{x^2+1}} Simplify u^(-1/2) = √((x-1)/(x²+1)) and combine with the denominator.  \text{If } f \text{ is differentiable and } g(x) = [f(x^2+1)]^2, \text{ find } g'(x) \text{ in terms of } f \text{ and } f'. This is a triple composition: the outermost is squaring, the middle is f, the innermost is x²+1. Apply the extended chain rule. g'(x) = 4x\,f(x^2+1)\,f'(x^2+1)  g(x) = [f(x^2+1)]^2 Recognize this as a composition of three layers: outermost is u², middle is f, innermost is x²+1. \text{Outer: } H(u) = u^2, \quad \text{Middle: } f(v) \text{ (given)}, \quad \text{Inner: } v = x^2+1 Name each layer. H'(u) = 2u, \quad f'(v) \text{ (given)}, \quad v' = 2x Differentiate each layer. g'(x) = H'(f(x^2+1)) \cdot f'(x^2+1) \cdot (x^2+1)' Apply the extended chain rule: outer derivative evaluated at middle-of-inner, times middle derivative evaluated at inner, times inner derivative. = 2[f(x^2+1)] \cdot f'(x^2+1) \cdot 2x Substitute: H'(f(x²+1)) = 2f(x²+1), the middle derivative at x²+1 is f'(x²+1), inner derivative is 2x. = 4x\,f(x^2+1)\,f'(x^2+1) Collect constants: 2·2x = 4x.  \text{Given: } g(4)=2,\; g'(4)=-3,\; f(2)=7,\; f'(2)=5. \text{ Let } h(x)=f(g(x)). \text{ Find } h'(4). Apply h'(a) = f'(g(a))·g'(a). Read g(4) first, then look up f' at that value — not at 4. h'(4) = -15  h'(4) = f'(g(4)) \cdot g'(4) Write the Chain Rule formula for h = f∘g. g(4) = 2, \quad g'(4) = -3 Read the given values: the inner function value is 2 and its rate of change is −3. f'(g(4)) = f'(2) = 5 Evaluate f' at g(4)=2, not at 4. The given data says f'(2)=5. h'(4) = 5 \cdot (-3) = -15 Multiply the two factors: f'(g(4))·g'(4) = 5·(−3) = −15.  \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sin^2(x) \cdot e^{3x}. Outermost operation is multiplication (two factors) — Product Rule is outer. Each factor requires its own Chain Rule: d/dx[sin²x] and d/dx[e^(3x)]. f'(x) = e^{3x}\sin(x)\bigl[2\cos(x) + 3\sin(x)\bigr]  f(x) = \underbrace{\sin^2(x)}_{A} \cdot \underbrace{e^{3x}}_{B} Outermost operation is multiplication — Product Rule governs the structure. A'(x) = 2\sin(x)\cos(x) Differentiate A = (sin x)² by Chain Rule: outer is u², inner is sin x. Result: 2sin(x)·cos(x). B'(x) = 3e^{3x} Differentiate B = e^(3x) by Chain Rule: outer is e^u, inner is 3x. Result: e^(3x)·3. f'(x) = 2\sin(x)\cos(x)\cdot e^{3x} + \sin^2(x)\cdot 3e^{3x} Apply Product Rule: A'B + AB'. = e^{3x}\sin(x)\bigl[2\cos(x) + 3\sin(x)\bigr] Factor out e^(3x) and sin(x) from both terms to reach the fully simplified form.
documents.proofs: The most natural "proof" of the chain rule goes like this: form the difference quotient for (f∘g) at x, multiply top and bottom by g(x+h) - g(x), and "cancel" to get [f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x))] / [g(x+h)-g(x)] times [g(x+h)-g(x)] / h. As h → 0, the first factor approaches f'(g(x)) and the second approaches g'(x), giving the product f'(g(x))·g'(x). This argument has a fatal flaw: we divided by g(x+h) - g(x), but this quantity might equal zero for infinitely many values of h near 0 (even if g'(x) ≠ 0), making the division illegal. A simple example of this flaw: take g(x) = x²sin(1/x) for x ≠ 0 and g(0) = 0. Then g'(0) = 0, but g(h) = 0 for h = 1/(nπ) for any integer n ≠ 0, which are values of h arbitrarily close to 0 where g(h) - g(0) = 0. If we divide by g(x+h) - g(x) in the naive proof at x = 0, we are dividing by zero for these special values of h. The correct proof uses the following approach. Define an auxiliary function Φ(k) = [f(g(x)+k) - f(g(x))] / k for k ≠ 0, and Φ(0) = f'(g(x)). Because f is differentiable at g(x), we have lim(k→0) Φ(k) = f'(g(x)) = Φ(0), so Φ is continuous at 0. Now write: f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x)) = Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) · [g(x+h)-g(x)]. This is valid because when g(x+h)-g(x) ≠ 0, it follows the definition of Φ with k = g(x+h)-g(x), and when g(x+h)-g(x) = 0, both sides are 0. Dividing by h: [f(g(x+h))-f(g(x))]/h = Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) · [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h. As h→0: Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) → Φ(0) = f'(g(x)) (because g is continuous, g(x+h)-g(x)→0, and Φ is continuous at 0); [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h → g'(x). The product of the limits is f'(g(x))·g'(x). This completes the rigorous proof. Safety-slope bridge to microscope mode: zooming says the curve locally looks linear with a local slope. Φ(k) is the rigorous patch that keeps this local-slope story valid even when the inner change g(x+h)-g(x) is exactly zero, so no illegal division occurs. Use the annotated proof map as a translation layer: hover the algebra term Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) and watch microscope mode light up. This is where symbol and geometry become the same idea. Expert trap: if one link is not differentiable at the evaluation point, chain rule cannot be applied there. Example y=|sin x| at x=0: inner sin x is smooth, but outer |u| has a corner at u=0, so the chain snaps at that point. The Naive Proof Has a Flaw \text{Multiplying and dividing by } g(x+h)-g(x) \text{ is invalid} \\ \text{when } g(x+h) - g(x) = 0, \text{ which can happen for } h \neq 0. Correct Proof Sketch \Phi(k) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{f(g(x)+k)-f(g(x))}{k} & k\neq 0 \\ f'(g(x)) & k=0 \end{cases} \implies (f\circ g)'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \Phi(g(x+h)-g(x))\cdot\frac{g(x+h)-g(x)}{h} = f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x) Annotated Proof Hover \text{Hover } \Phi(g(x+h)-g(x)) \text{ in the proof map to see microscope mode highlight the safety patch idea.} Leibniz Form in Related Rates \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt}, \quad \frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{dA}{d\theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt}. \text{ Intermediate-rate factors multiply along dependency chains.} Broken Chain Condition \text{Chain rule needs every link differentiable at the point. } y=|\sin x| \text{ fails at } x=0 \text{ because } |u| \text{ is not differentiable at } u=0.
documents.applications: This is the fundamental challenge of all real-world calculus: quantities rarely depend directly on the variable we care about. Temperature depends on altitude, and altitude depends on time as an airplane climbs. A company's profit depends on price, and optimal price depends on consumer demand, which depends on the economy. In modern AI, a loss function depends on output, output depends on hidden layers, and hidden layers depend on millions of weights; computing each gradient is repeated chain rule (backpropagation). In every case, we have a chain of dependencies, and computing the overall rate of change requires the chain rule. It is not an exaggeration to say the chain rule is used more than any other single rule in applied calculus. Prerequisite Reminder Product and quotient rules are proved in the previous lesson. This lesson builds on those results and shows how composition adds the extra inner-derivative factor. The Outside-Inside Method \frac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = \underbrace{f'(g(x))}_{\text{differentiate outside, keep inside}} \cdot \underbrace{g'(x)}_{\text{times derivative of inside}} Rates Multiply Along a Chain \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} Physics Hover: Unit Pipeline \frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{cm}}\cdot\frac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}}=\frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{s}} Balloon Volume Example V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3,\; r = 2t \implies \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot 2 = 8\pi r^2 = 8\pi(2t)^2 = 32\pi t^2 Backpropagation Is Chain Rule at Scale \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a_2}\cdot\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial z_2}\cdot\frac{\partial z_2}{\partial a_1}\cdot\frac{\partial a_1}{\partial z_1}\cdot\frac{\partial z_1}{\partial w_1}\;\;\text{(one weight in a deep net)} Identify the Outermost Operation First \text{Before applying any rule, ask: "What is the LAST operation done?"} \\ f(x) = x^3\sqrt{4x-1}: \text{ last op is } \times \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Product Rule is the outer rule.} \\ \text{Chain Rule only fires inside } \sqrt{4x-1}. \text{ Skipping this check is the \#1 exam error.} Abstract Function Trap — Tables and Graphs h'(a) = f'\!\left(\underbrace{g(a)}_{\text{Step 1: look up}}\right) \cdot g'(a) \\ \text{Step 1: find } g(a) \text{ from the table or graph.} \\ \text{Step 2: evaluate } f' \text{ at } g(a) \text{, NOT at } a. \\ \text{Writing } f'(a)\cdot g'(a) \text{ is the classic exam mistake.}
documents.content: Here is a real puzzle: the volume of a balloon depends on its radius, and the radius depends on time as you inflate it. If you know how fast you are pumping air (dV/dt) and how volume relates to radius (V = (4/3)πr³), can you figure out how fast the radius is growing at any instant? This is a chain of dependencies — and the chain rule is precisely the tool that breaks it open. This is the fundamental challenge of all real-world calculus: quantities rarely depend directly on the variable we care about. Temperature depends on altitude, and altitude depends on time as an airplane climbs. A company's profit depends on price, and optimal price depends on consumer demand, which depends on the economy. In modern AI, a loss function depends on output, output depends on hidden layers, and hidden layers depend on millions of weights; computing each gradient is repeated chain rule (backpropagation). In every case, we have a chain of dependencies, and computing the overall rate of change requires the chain rule. It is not an exaggeration to say the chain rule is used more than any other single rule in applied calculus. Sequencing map: first derivatives are built from limits, then power/product/quotient rules are proved, and only then does the chain rule appear. The chain rule is layered on those earlier results. Let's build intuition for composition before worrying about differentiation. The expression f(g(x)) means: first apply g to x, get an intermediate result, then apply f to that intermediate result. For example, if g(x) = x² + 1 and f(u) = √u, then f(g(x)) = √(x²+1). The function g acts "on the inside" and f acts "on the outside." To evaluate at x = 3: first g(3) = 10, then f(10) = √10. This two-step process is composition. Now think about rates. If u = g(x) changes by a certain rate as x changes, and y = f(u) changes at a certain rate as u changes, then what is the rate of change of y with respect to x? The answer is found by multiplying the rates: if u increases 3 times as fast as x, and y increases 4 times as fast as u, then y increases 12 times as fast as x. Rates along a chain multiply. The gear analogy makes this precise. Suppose gear A meshes with gear B, and gear B meshes with gear C. If gear A turns 3 times for every revolution of gear B (dA/dB = 3), and gear B turns 2 times for every revolution of gear C (dB/dC = 2), then gear A turns 6 times for every revolution of gear C: dA/dC = (dA/dB)·(dB/dC) = 3·2 = 6. The rates multiply along the chain. This is the chain rule in its essence. In Leibniz notation, the chain rule is dy/dx = (dy/du)·(du/dx). If you squint, it looks like the du terms "cancel" — and while this is not literally true (dy/du and du/dx are limits, not fractions), it serves as a perfect mnemonic and is essentially justified by the fact that in the limit, these ratio quantities do behave multiplicatively. There is a beautifully practical way to execute the chain rule called the "outside-inside" method. To differentiate f(g(x)): (1) Differentiate the outside function f, evaluated at the inside g(x), leaving the inside completely unchanged. (2) Multiply by the derivative of the inside g'(x). In symbols: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). The key is to NOT differentiate the inside first — leave it intact and only differentiate the outer wrapper. Let's see this with a concrete example: d/dx[(3x²-1)⁵]. The outside function is u⁵ (raise to the fifth), and the inside is u = 3x²-1. Differentiate the outside: the derivative of u⁵ is 5u⁴, evaluated at u = 3x²-1, giving 5(3x²-1)⁴. Multiply by the derivative of the inside: d/dx[3x²-1] = 6x. Final answer: 5(3x²-1)⁴ · 6x = 30x(3x²-1)⁴. This two-step process — differentiate outer (keep inner), multiply by derivative of inner — is all there is to the chain rule. A note on identifying the outer and inner functions: look for the "last operation" that would be performed if you were evaluating the function at a specific number. For √(x²+1), the last operation is taking a square root, so the outer function is u^(1/2) and the inner is x²+1. For sin(5x³), the last operation is taking the sine, so outer is sin(u) and inner is 5x³. Beginner survival skill: before differentiating, run a layer scan. For tan^3(4x), the outermost skin is (... )^3, the middle layer is tan(...), and the inner core is 4x. If you can label layers correctly, peeling becomes automatic. Pipeline view: composition is a relay race, not one giant jump. x flows through g to become u, then u flows through f to become y. If the first machine doubles speed and the second triples speed, output speed is six times faster. This is exactly dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx). The chain rule extends to triple and longer compositions. For y = f(g(h(x))), the derivative is f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x). Differentiate the outermost layer first, then the next, and so on, each time multiplying by the derivative of the layer below. This is like peeling an onion, one layer at a time. There are two equivalent forms of the chain rule. The Lagrange/function-composition form names the outer and inner functions explicitly and is cleaner for algebraic manipulation. The Leibniz form uses dy/du and du/dx and is more intuitive for applied problems involving rates. An approachable derivation idea: if Δu ≈ g'(x)Δx and Δy ≈ f'(u)Δu, then Δy ≈ f'(u)g'(x)Δx. Dividing by Δx and taking the limiting view gives dy/dx = f'(g(x))g'(x). The chain rule combined with the power rule gives one of the most common differentiation formulas in all of calculus: d/dx[uⁿ] = nuⁿ⁻¹·u', where u = g(x) is any differentiable function. This formula applies whenever you have "something to a power" — the power rule applies to the outer function, and the chain rule contributes the u' factor. The extended chain rule for triple compositions states that for y = f(g(h(x))), the derivative is f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x). The pattern is clear: differentiate each layer from outside to inside, evaluate each outer derivative at everything inside it, and multiply all the factors together. Identifying the outer and inner functions correctly is a skill that develops with practice. The best approach is to always ask: "What is the last thing done to x if I evaluate this at a number?" That last operation is the outer function. Everything that comes before is the inner function. The most natural "proof" of the chain rule goes like this: form the difference quotient for (f∘g) at x, multiply top and bottom by g(x+h) - g(x), and "cancel" to get [f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x))] / [g(x+h)-g(x)] times [g(x+h)-g(x)] / h. As h → 0, the first factor approaches f'(g(x)) and the second approaches g'(x), giving the product f'(g(x))·g'(x). This argument has a fatal flaw: we divided by g(x+h) - g(x), but this quantity might equal zero for infinitely many values of h near 0 (even if g'(x) ≠ 0), making the division illegal. A simple example of this flaw: take g(x) = x²sin(1/x) for x ≠ 0 and g(0) = 0. Then g'(0) = 0, but g(h) = 0 for h = 1/(nπ) for any integer n ≠ 0, which are values of h arbitrarily close to 0 where g(h) - g(0) = 0. If we divide by g(x+h) - g(x) in the naive proof at x = 0, we are dividing by zero for these special values of h. The correct proof uses the following approach. Define an auxiliary function Φ(k) = [f(g(x)+k) - f(g(x))] / k for k ≠ 0, and Φ(0) = f'(g(x)). Because f is differentiable at g(x), we have lim(k→0) Φ(k) = f'(g(x)) = Φ(0), so Φ is continuous at 0. Now write: f(g(x+h)) - f(g(x)) = Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) · [g(x+h)-g(x)]. This is valid because when g(x+h)-g(x) ≠ 0, it follows the definition of Φ with k = g(x+h)-g(x), and when g(x+h)-g(x) = 0, both sides are 0. Dividing by h: [f(g(x+h))-f(g(x))]/h = Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) · [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h. As h→0: Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) → Φ(0) = f'(g(x)) (because g is continuous, g(x+h)-g(x)→0, and Φ is continuous at 0); [g(x+h)-g(x)]/h → g'(x). The product of the limits is f'(g(x))·g'(x). This completes the rigorous proof. Safety-slope bridge to microscope mode: zooming says the curve locally looks linear with a local slope. Φ(k) is the rigorous patch that keeps this local-slope story valid even when the inner change g(x+h)-g(x) is exactly zero, so no illegal division occurs. Use the annotated proof map as a translation layer: hover the algebra term Φ(g(x+h)-g(x)) and watch microscope mode light up. This is where symbol and geometry become the same idea. Expert trap: if one link is not differentiable at the evaluation point, chain rule cannot be applied there. Example y=|sin x| at x=0: inner sin x is smooth, but outer |u| has a corner at u=0, so the chain snaps at that point. The Naive Proof Has a Flaw \text{Multiplying and dividing by } g(x+h)-g(x) \text{ is invalid} \\ \text{when } g(x+h) - g(x) = 0, \text{ which can happen for } h \neq 0. Correct Proof Sketch \Phi(k) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{f(g(x)+k)-f(g(x))}{k} & k\neq 0 \\ f'(g(x)) & k=0 \end{cases} \implies (f\circ g)'(x) = \lim_{h\to 0} \Phi(g(x+h)-g(x))\cdot\frac{g(x+h)-g(x)}{h} = f'(g(x))\cdot g'(x) Annotated Proof Hover \text{Hover } \Phi(g(x+h)-g(x)) \text{ in the proof map to see microscope mode highlight the safety patch idea.} Leibniz Form in Related Rates \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt}, \quad \frac{dA}{dt} = \frac{dA}{d\theta}\cdot\frac{d\theta}{dt}. \text{ Intermediate-rate factors multiply along dependency chains.} Broken Chain Condition \text{Chain rule needs every link differentiable at the point. } y=|\sin x| \text{ fails at } x=0 \text{ because } |u| \text{ is not differentiable at } u=0. Prerequisite Reminder Product and quotient rules are proved in the previous lesson. This lesson builds on those results and shows how composition adds the extra inner-derivative factor. The Outside-Inside Method \frac{d}{dx}[f(g(x))] = \underbrace{f'(g(x))}_{\text{differentiate outside, keep inside}} \cdot \underbrace{g'(x)}_{\text{times derivative of inside}} Rates Multiply Along a Chain \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} Physics Hover: Unit Pipeline \frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{cm}}\cdot\frac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}}=\frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{s}} Balloon Volume Example V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3,\; r = 2t \implies \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt} = 4\pi r^2 \cdot 2 = 8\pi r^2 = 8\pi(2t)^2 = 32\pi t^2 Backpropagation Is Chain Rule at Scale \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a_2}\cdot\frac{\partial a_2}{\partial z_2}\cdot\frac{\partial z_2}{\partial a_1}\cdot\frac{\partial a_1}{\partial z_1}\cdot\frac{\partial z_1}{\partial w_1}\;\;\text{(one weight in a deep net)} Identify the Outermost Operation First \text{Before applying any rule, ask: "What is the LAST operation done?"} \\ f(x) = x^3\sqrt{4x-1}: \text{ last op is } \times \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Product Rule is the outer rule.} \\ \text{Chain Rule only fires inside } \sqrt{4x-1}. \text{ Skipping this check is the \#1 exam error.} Abstract Function Trap — Tables and Graphs h'(a) = f'\!\left(\underbrace{g(a)}_{\text{Step 1: look up}}\right) \cdot g'(a) \\ \text{Step 1: find } g(a) \text{ from the table or graph.} \\ \text{Step 2: evaluate } f' \text{ at } g(a) \text{, NOT at } a. \\ \text{Writing } f'(a)\cdot g'(a) \text{ is the classic exam mistake.} Chain Rule (Composition Form) (f \circ g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) Chain Rule (Leibniz Form) \text{If } y = f(u) \text{ and } u = g(x), \text{ then } \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy}{du} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx} Generalized Power Rule \frac{d}{dx}[u^n] = n\,u^{n-1}\cdot u', \quad u = g(x) Triple Composition (Extended Chain Rule) \frac{d}{dx}[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) \cdot g'(h(x)) \cdot h'(x) Combo Rule Decision Tree \text{Identify the outermost operation before choosing a rule:} \\ \text{1. Outermost is } {\times} \text{ or } {\div} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Product / Quotient Rule. Chain Rule fires inside.} \\ \text{2. Outermost is composition } f(g(x)) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Chain Rule.} \\ \text{3. Outermost is } {+} \text{ or } {-} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Split and differentiate term-by-term.} Function Pipeline Relay Watch stage speeds multiply in real time: inner rate times outer rate equals the total output rate. Interlocking Gears — Rates Multiply The chain rule is not symbolic manipulation — it is rates multiplying through a mechanical chain. Composition is an Onion: Layers to Peel Label outer, middle, and inner layers correctly before computing — the prerequisite for any chain-rule problem. Chain Rule Assembler Game Build derivatives by selecting outside and inside gears, then verify the full assembled result. Layer Identification Mini-Game Label outer, middle, and inner layers correctly before computing — the prerequisite for any chain-rule problem. Trig Trap: sin(x²) vs sin²(x) Toggle between sin(x²) and sin²(x) to see how the layer swap changes the derivative completely. Chain Rule Category Drill (Standard) Master the 10 most common composition forms found in calculus exams. Chain Rule Category Drill (Interactive) An alternative view of the 10 most common composition forms. Peel the Onion — Layer-by-Layer Differentiation Differentiate from the outside in. Each peel produces exactly one factor in the chain-rule product. Blindfolded Chain Rule — Table Version Select the correct table cells to assemble h'(a). The grammar doesn't change when the functions have no formulas. Microscope Mode: The Visual Derivation Zoom in to see both curves linearize, then watch the composed slope = m₁ × m₂. Leibniz Unit Tracker Verify that Leibniz notation forces units to cancel correctly along the dependency chain. Annotated Proof Map Hover proof terms to sync algebra with microscope intuition — every symbol has a geometric meaning. Microscope and Safety Slope Non-Differentiable Trap Test y = |sin x| near 0 — a single non-differentiable link breaks the entire chain. Annotated Proof: d/dx[sin(x³)] (Basic) Step through every algebraic reduction in the rigorous limit-based proof. Annotated Proof: d/dx[sin(x³)] (Detailed) A more detailed version of the rigorous limit-based proof. Dependency Hierarchy Solver: H(x) = sin(x³) Visualize the full dependency graph of a composition and read off the chain rule product from the graph edges. Recursive Proof Stepper: d/dx[sin(x³)] Step through the chain rule proof one sub-problem at a time, deepening the recursive structure of the argument. Why Chain Rule Works (Check by Expansion) Differentiate f(x) = (x^2+1)^3 two ways: (1) expand first, (2) use chain rule. Verify both match. Both methods agree exactly. Chain rule is the compact form of algebra you would otherwise repeatedly expand. (x^2+1)^3 = x^6 + 3x^4 + 3x^2 + 1 Method 1: expand using the binomial identity. f'(x)=6x^5+12x^3+6x Differentiate the expanded polynomial term by term. \text{Method 2: let } u=x^2+1,\;f=u^3 Set up outer and inner layers for chain rule. f'(x)=3u^2\cdot u'=3(x^2+1)^2\cdot2x=6x(x^2+1)^2 Apply outer derivative times inner derivative. 6x(x^2+1)^2=6x(x^4+2x^2+1)=6x^5+12x^3+6x Expand to confirm exact agreement with Method 1. Chain Rule with Power Function f(x) = (3x^2 - 1)^5. \text{ Find } f'(x). f'(x) = 30x(3x²-1)⁴. The chain rule contributed the factor of 6x (the derivative of the inner function), which is what distinguishes this from just differentiating u⁵. \text{Outer function: } F(u) = u^5 \quad \text{Inner function: } u = g(x) = 3x^2 - 1 Identify the outer and inner functions. The outer function is "raise to the fifth power" and the inner is 3x²-1. F'(u) = 5u^4, \quad g'(x) = 6x Differentiate the outer function (power rule: bring down 5, reduce exponent to 4) and differentiate the inner function separately. f'(x) = F'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) = 5(3x^2-1)^4 \cdot 6x Apply the chain rule: differentiate the outer function (giving 5u⁴) evaluated at the inner function (replacing u with 3x²-1), then multiply by the derivative of the inner function. = 30x(3x^2-1)^4 Multiply the constants 5 and 6 together: 5·6 = 30. The (3x²-1)⁴ factor remains as is. Chain Rule with Square Root f(x) = \sqrt{x^2+1}. \text{ Find } f'(x). f'(x) = x/√(x²+1). Note that the derivative is 0 at x = 0 (horizontal tangent at the bottom of the U-shaped curve) and grows toward ±1 as x → ±∞. f(x) = (x^2+1)^{1/2} Rewrite the square root as a power of 1/2 to clearly see the power function structure. \text{Outer: } F(u) = u^{1/2}, \quad \text{Inner: } u = x^2 + 1 Identify the outer function as the square root (or equivalently the 1/2 power) and the inner function as x²+1. F'(u) = \frac{1}{2}u^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}}, \quad g'(x) = 2x Differentiate outer (power rule with n=1/2) and inner separately. f'(x) = \frac{1}{2}(x^2+1)^{-1/2} \cdot 2x Chain rule: F'(g(x)) · g'(x). Replace u with x²+1 in the outer derivative, then multiply by 2x. = \frac{2x}{2(x^2+1)^{1/2}} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} Simplify: the factor of 2 in the numerator cancels with the 2 from the power rule denominator. Rewrite the negative power as a square root. Chain Rule with Sine f(x) = \sin(5x^3). \text{ Find } f'(x). f'(x) = 15x² cos(5x³). This illustrates the general pattern: d/dx[sin(g(x))] = cos(g(x))·g'(x). The argument of cosine is the same as the argument of sine was. \text{Outer: } F(u) = \sin(u), \quad \text{Inner: } u = 5x^3 The outer function is the sine function and the inner function is 5x³. F'(u) = \cos(u), \quad g'(x) = 15x^2 The derivative of sine is cosine (covered in the trig derivatives lesson). The derivative of the inner function 5x³ is 15x² by the power and constant multiple rules. f'(x) = \cos(5x^3) \cdot 15x^2 Apply the chain rule: differentiate the outer function (giving cos(u)), evaluated at the inner (replacing u with 5x³), then multiply by the inner derivative 15x². = 15x^2 \cos(5x^3) Rewrite with the constant factor first for standard form. Chain Rule with Exponential (Preview) f(x) = e^{x^2}. \text{ Find } f'(x). \text{ (Use the fact that } \frac{d}{du}[e^u] = e^u.) f'(x) = 2xe^(x²). The function e^(x²) grows enormously fast but its derivative is not just itself — the chain rule contributes the factor of 2x from the exponent. \text{Outer: } F(u) = e^u, \quad \text{Inner: } u = x^2 The outer function is e to the power u, and the inner function is x². F'(u) = e^u, \quad g'(x) = 2x The key property of e^u is that it equals its own derivative (proved in the exponential derivatives lesson). The inner derivative is 2x. f'(x) = e^{x^2} \cdot 2x Apply the chain rule: the outer derivative e^u evaluated at u = x² gives e^(x²), times the inner derivative 2x. = 2x\,e^{x^2} Rewrite with the constant factor first. Chain Rule: Do Not Confuse (sin x)⁴ with sin(x⁴) f(x) = (\sin x)^4. \text{ Find } f'(x). \text{ Also note why this is different from } \sin(x^4). f'(x) = 4(sin x)³ cos x = 4 sin³(x) cos(x). The position of the exponent completely changes the meaning: (sin x)⁴ means sin^4(x) [sine, then power], while sin(x⁴) means the sine of x⁴ [power, then sine]. \text{Outer: } F(u) = u^4, \quad \text{Inner: } u = \sin x In (sin x)⁴, the outer function is "raise to the fourth power" and the inner function is sin x. The entire sine is being raised to a power. F'(u) = 4u^3, \quad g'(x) = \cos x Differentiate outer: 4u³. Differentiate inner: d/dx[sin x] = cos x. f'(x) = 4(\sin x)^3 \cdot \cos x Chain rule result. This is often written as 4 sin³(x) cos(x). \text{Compare: } \frac{d}{dx}[\sin(x^4)] = \cos(x^4) \cdot 4x^3 For sin(x⁴), the outer function is sine and the inner is x⁴. The derivative of sine is cosine evaluated at x⁴, times the inner derivative 4x³. These are completely different functions. Triple Composition f(x) = \sin^4(x^2). \text{ Find } f'(x). \text{ (This is } (\sin(x^2))^4.) f'(x) = 8x sin³(x²) cos(x²). Triple compositions peel away one layer at a time, each contributing its own derivative as a multiplicative factor. f(x) = [\sin(x^2)]^4 Rewrite explicitly to show the structure: we are raising sin(x²) to the fourth power. This is a triple composition: the outermost is u⁴, the middle is sin, the innermost is x². \text{Layer 1 (outermost): } F(u) = u^4 Peel layer 1: apply the power rule outside and keep sin(x²) completely untouched inside the brackets. \text{Layer 2 (middle): } G(v) = \sin(v) Peel layer 2: now derive sine, again keeping its inner argument x² exactly as-is. \text{Layer 3 (innermost): } H(x) = x^2 The innermost function is x². F'(u) = 4u^3, \quad G'(v) = \cos(v), \quad H'(x) = 2x Core layer: derive x² last to get 2x, then multiply by everything collected from outer and middle layers. f'(x) = F'(G(H(x))) \cdot G'(H(x)) \cdot H'(x) Apply the extended chain rule: outer derivative (evaluated all the way at the inside) times middle derivative (evaluated at innermost) times innermost derivative. = 4[\sin(x^2)]^3 \cdot \cos(x^2) \cdot 2x Substitute: F'(G(H(x))) = 4[sin(x²)]³, G'(H(x)) = cos(x²), H'(x) = 2x. = 8x\sin^3(x^2)\cos(x^2) Collect constants: 4·2x = 8x. Write in standard form. Leibniz Form of Chain Rule If y = u^5 \text{ and } u = 3x^2 - 1, \text{ find } dy/dx \text{ using Leibniz form.} This gives the same answer as the composition form: dy/dx = 30x(3x²-1)⁴. The Leibniz form is particularly clear for related rates problems where the intermediate variable u has physical meaning. \frac{dy}{du} = 5u^4 Differentiate y = u⁵ with respect to u, treating u as the independent variable. \frac{du}{dx} = 6x Differentiate u = 3x² - 1 with respect to x. \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \cdot \frac{du}{dx} = 5u^4 \cdot 6x Multiply the two rates together per the chain rule in Leibniz form. The du "terms" appear to cancel like fractions, though this is a notational convenience, not literal cancellation. = 30x \cdot u^4 = 30x(3x^2-1)^4 Substitute back u = 3x²-1 to express everything in terms of x. The final answer must be in terms of the original variable x. Chain Rule Combined with Quotient Rule f(x) = \dfrac{(x^2+1)^{3/2}}{x+1}. \text{ Find } f'(x). f'(x) = √(x²+1) · (2x²+3x-1) / (x+1)². Factoring out the common (x²+1)^(1/2) factor from the quotient rule numerator is a key simplification technique. f(x) = \frac{N(x)}{D(x)} \text{ where } N(x) = (x^2+1)^{3/2}, \; D(x) = x+1 Identify the numerator and denominator. The numerator requires the chain rule; the denominator is simple. N'(x) = \frac{3}{2}(x^2+1)^{1/2} \cdot 2x = 3x(x^2+1)^{1/2} = \frac{3x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} \cdot (x^2+1) Differentiate the numerator using the chain rule: outer is u^(3/2) giving (3/2)u^(1/2), inner is x²+1 giving 2x. Simplify: (3/2)·2x = 3x. N'(x) = 3x(x^2+1)^{1/2} The derivative of the numerator is 3x√(x²+1). D'(x) = 1 The derivative of the denominator x+1 is simply 1. f'(x) = \frac{N'(x)D(x) - N(x)D'(x)}{[D(x)]^2} Apply the quotient rule formula. = \frac{3x(x^2+1)^{1/2}(x+1) - (x^2+1)^{3/2}(1)}{(x+1)^2} Substitute N, N', D, D' into the quotient rule. = \frac{(x^2+1)^{1/2}\left[3x(x+1) - (x^2+1)\right]}{(x+1)^2} {{algebra:factoring-fractional-powers|Factor out the lowest exponent (1/2)}} from both terms in the numerator. = \frac{(x^2+1)^{1/2}(3x^2 + 3x - x^2 - 1)}{(x+1)^2} Expand inside the brackets: 3x(x+1) = 3x²+3x, then subtract x²+1. = \frac{\sqrt{x^2+1}\,(2x^2 + 3x - 1)}{(x+1)^2} Combine like terms in the numerator: 3x²-x²=2x². Rewrite (x²+1)^(1/2) as √(x²+1). Mini Backprop Chain (Single Neuron) Let y = \sigma(z), z = wx+b, L = \tfrac12(y-t)^2. Find dL/dw. This local product is exactly the pattern repeated layer-by-layer in deep learning backpropagation. \frac{dL}{dy} = y-t Differentiate the squared loss with respect to output y. \frac{dy}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) = y(1-y) Derivative of sigmoid activation. \frac{dz}{dw} = x For z = wx+b, derivative with respect to w is x. \frac{dL}{dw} = \frac{dL}{dy}\cdot\frac{dy}{dz}\cdot\frac{dz}{dw} = (y-t)\,y(1-y)\,x Multiply local derivatives along the computation graph. Hook Problem: Balloon Volume Rate A sphere has radius r(t)=2t. With V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3, find dV/dt at t=3. At t=3, volume is increasing at 288\pi cubic units per time unit. V=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3,\quad r=2t Volume depends on radius, and radius depends on time. \frac{dV}{dr}=4\pi r^2,\quad \frac{dr}{dt}=2 Differentiate each stage with respect to its own variable. \frac{dV}{dt}=\frac{dV}{dr}\cdot\frac{dr}{dt}=4\pi r^2\cdot 2=8\pi r^2 Apply Leibniz chain form for related rates. Unit tracker: (cm^3/cm)*(cm/s)=cm^3/s, so multiplication is physically required. t=3 \Rightarrow r=2(3)=6 Evaluate the intermediate quantity first. \left.\frac{dV}{dt}\right|_{t=3}=8\pi(6)^2=288\pi Substitute r=6 into the rate formula. Abstract Function Trap — Reading a Table \begin{array}{l} \text{Let } h(x) = f(g(x)). \text{ The table gives values at selected points:} \\[6pt] \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & f(x) & g(x) & f'(x) & g'(x) \\\hline 1 & 4 & 3 & 2 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 & 5 & 3 \\ 3 & 7 & 2 & -2 & 4 \end{array} \\[6pt] \text{Find } h'(2) \text{ and } h'(3). \end{array} h'(2)=6 and h'(3)=20. The two-step rule: evaluate the inner function first, then look up the outer derivative at that value. On exams, reading f'(a) instead of f'(g(a)) is by far the most common Chain Rule error with tables. h'(x) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x) Write the Chain Rule formula. This holds for any differentiable f and g — no explicit formula needed. h'(2) = f'\!\left(\underbrace{g(2)}_{?}\right) \cdot g'(2) To compute h'(2): we need f' evaluated at g(2), not at 2. Look up g(2) from the table first. g(2) = 1, \quad g'(2) = 3 \quad \text{(row } x=2\text{)} From the x=2 row: g(2)=1 and g'(2)=3. h'(2) = f'(\underbrace{1}_{g(2)}) \cdot 3 Substitute g(2)=1. We now need f'(1) — read from the x=1 row, not the x=2 row. f'(1) = 2 \implies h'(2) = 2 \cdot 3 = 6 From the x=1 row: f'(1)=2. Final answer: 2·3=6. h'(3) = f'(g(3)) \cdot g'(3) = f'(2) \cdot 4 = 5 \cdot 4 = 20 For h'(3): g(3)=2 (row x=3), g'(3)=4 (row x=3). Then f'(g(3))=f'(2)=5 (row x=2). Result: 5·4=20. Combo Rule Boss Fight — Product Rule + Chain Rule f(x) = x^3\sqrt{4x-1}. \text{ Find } f'(x). f'(x) = x²(14x-3)/√(4x-1). The outer rule was Product Rule; Chain Rule fired inside √(4x-1). The #1 mistake is reaching for Chain Rule first on a product — always identify the outermost operation before picking a rule. f(x) = \underbrace{x^3}_{A(x)} \cdot \underbrace{\sqrt{4x-1}}_{B(x)} The outermost operation is multiplication of x³ and √(4x-1). Product Rule is the outer rule. Chain Rule will appear only when differentiating B(x). A(x) = x^3 \implies A'(x) = 3x^2 Differentiate A = x³ by the power rule. B(x) = (4x-1)^{1/2} \implies B'(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(4x-1)^{-1/2}\cdot 4 = \frac{2}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Differentiate B = (4x-1)^(1/2) by Chain Rule: outer gives (1/2)u^(-1/2), inner gives 4. Simplify: (1/2)·4 = 2. f'(x) = A'B + AB' = 3x^2\sqrt{4x-1} + x^3 \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Apply Product Rule: d/dx[AB] = A'B + AB'. = \frac{3x^2(4x-1) + 2x^3}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Write over common denominator √(4x-1). Multiply the first term top and bottom by √(4x-1). = \frac{x^2(12x - 3 + 2x)}{\sqrt{4x-1}} = \frac{x^2(14x-3)}{\sqrt{4x-1}} Factor out x² from the numerator: 3x²(4x-1)+2x³ = 12x³-3x²+2x³ = 14x³-3x² = x²(14x-3). The Ultimate Chain Rule Boss Fight h(x) = (2x + 1)^5 (3x - 2)^7. \; \text{Find } h'(x). h'(x) = (2x + 1)⁴(3x - 2)⁶(72x + 1). This is the 'Ultimate Boss' check: Product Rule on the outside, two Chain Rules on the inside, and a sophisticated factoring finale. h(x) = f(x)g(x) \implies h'(x) = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x) This is first and foremost a Product Rule problem because two distinct blocks are being multiplied. f(x) = (2x+1)^5 \implies f'(x) = 10(2x+1)^4 Differentiate the first factor using the Chain Rule (outer power 5, inner deriv 2). g(x) = (3x-2)^7 \implies g'(x) = 21(3x-2)^6 Differentiate the second factor using the Chain Rule (outer power 7, inner deriv 3). h'(x) = 10(2x+1)^4(3x-2)^7 + 21(2x+1)^5(3x-2)^6 Substitute derivatives into the Product Rule formula. h'(x) = (2x+1)^4(3x-2)^6 \left[ 10(3x-2) + 21(2x+1) \right] Factor out the common terms: the lowest powers (4 and 6) of each block. = (2x+1)^4(3x-2)^6 (72x + 1) Expand and simplify inside the square brackets: 30x - 20 + 42x + 21 = 72x + 1. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = (2x+1)^{10}. Treat (2x+1)^10 as a composition: outer function u^10, inner function 2x+1, and inner derivative 2. f'(x) = 20(2x+1)^9 \text{Outer: } F(u) = u^{10},\quad \text{Inner: } u = 2x+1 Identify the composition structure. F'(u) = 10u^9, \quad u' = 2 Differentiate each piece. f'(x) = 10(2x+1)^9 \cdot 2 = 20(2x+1)^9 Apply the chain rule and simplify. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sqrt{\dfrac{x^2+1}{x-1}}. Write as ((x²+1)/(x-1))^(1/2). The outer function is u^(1/2) and the inner function is the quotient (x²+1)/(x-1), which requires the quotient rule to differentiate. f'(x) = \dfrac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^{3/2}\sqrt{x^2+1}} f(x) = \left(\frac{x^2+1}{x-1}\right)^{1/2} Rewrite the square root as a power. \text{Let } u = \frac{x^2+1}{x-1}, \text{ so } f = u^{1/2} Define the inner function as the quotient. \frac{df}{du} = \frac{1}{2}u^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}} Differentiate the outer square root. \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{2x(x-1) - (x^2+1)(1)}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{2x^2-2x-x^2-1}{(x-1)^2} = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{(x-1)^2} Apply the quotient rule to u = (x²+1)/(x-1). f'(x) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{x^2+1}{x-1}\right)^{-1/2} \cdot \frac{x^2-2x-1}{(x-1)^2} Combine via chain rule. = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{x-1}{x^2+1}} = \frac{x^2-2x-1}{2(x-1)^{3/2}\sqrt{x^2+1}} Simplify u^(-1/2) = √((x-1)/(x²+1)) and combine with the denominator. \text{If } f \text{ is differentiable and } g(x) = [f(x^2+1)]^2, \text{ find } g'(x) \text{ in terms of } f \text{ and } f'. This is a triple composition: the outermost is squaring, the middle is f, the innermost is x²+1. Apply the extended chain rule. g'(x) = 4x\,f(x^2+1)\,f'(x^2+1) g(x) = [f(x^2+1)]^2 Recognize this as a composition of three layers: outermost is u², middle is f, innermost is x²+1. \text{Outer: } H(u) = u^2, \quad \text{Middle: } f(v) \text{ (given)}, \quad \text{Inner: } v = x^2+1 Name each layer. H'(u) = 2u, \quad f'(v) \text{ (given)}, \quad v' = 2x Differentiate each layer. g'(x) = H'(f(x^2+1)) \cdot f'(x^2+1) \cdot (x^2+1)' Apply the extended chain rule: outer derivative evaluated at middle-of-inner, times middle derivative evaluated at inner, times inner derivative. = 2[f(x^2+1)] \cdot f'(x^2+1) \cdot 2x Substitute: H'(f(x²+1)) = 2f(x²+1), the middle derivative at x²+1 is f'(x²+1), inner derivative is 2x. = 4x\,f(x^2+1)\,f'(x^2+1) Collect constants: 2·2x = 4x. \text{Given: } g(4)=2,\; g'(4)=-3,\; f(2)=7,\; f'(2)=5. \text{ Let } h(x)=f(g(x)). \text{ Find } h'(4). Apply h'(a) = f'(g(a))·g'(a). Read g(4) first, then look up f' at that value — not at 4. h'(4) = -15 h'(4) = f'(g(4)) \cdot g'(4) Write the Chain Rule formula for h = f∘g. g(4) = 2, \quad g'(4) = -3 Read the given values: the inner function value is 2 and its rate of change is −3. f'(g(4)) = f'(2) = 5 Evaluate f' at g(4)=2, not at 4. The given data says f'(2)=5. h'(4) = 5 \cdot (-3) = -15 Multiply the two factors: f'(g(4))·g'(4) = 5·(−3) = −15. \text{Differentiate } f(x) = \sin^2(x) \cdot e^{3x}. Outermost operation is multiplication (two factors) — Product Rule is outer. Each factor requires its own Chain Rule: d/dx[sin²x] and d/dx[e^(3x)]. f'(x) = e^{3x}\sin(x)\bigl[2\cos(x) + 3\sin(x)\bigr] f(x) = \underbrace{\sin^2(x)}_{A} \cdot \underbrace{e^{3x}}_{B} Outermost operation is multiplication — Product Rule governs the structure. A'(x) = 2\sin(x)\cos(x) Differentiate A = (sin x)² by Chain Rule: outer is u², inner is sin x. Result: 2sin(x)·cos(x). B'(x) = 3e^{3x} Differentiate B = e^(3x) by Chain Rule: outer is e^u, inner is 3x. Result: e^(3x)·3. f'(x) = 2\sin(x)\cos(x)\cdot e^{3x} + \sin^2(x)\cdot 3e^{3x} Apply Product Rule: A'B + AB'. = e^{3x}\sin(x)\bigl[2\cos(x) + 3\sin(x)\bigr] Factor out e^(3x) and sin(x) from both terms to reach the fully simplified form. Power, Product, Quotient Rules The chain rule is combined with these rules constantly. The generalized power rule is chain rule plus power rule. The Combo Rule Boss Fight (Example 12) shows how to identify which outer rule applies. Trig Derivatives — sin(x²) vs sin²(x) The most common Chain Rule mistake in trig: sin²(x) has outer u² and inner sin x, while sin(x²) has outer sin and inner x². The NestedTrigMachine visualization on the Trig Derivatives page shows this side-by-side. Implicit Differentiation Implicit differentiation is the chain rule applied to expressions in y where y = y(x). assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change

documents.title: Derivatives of Inverse Functions
documents.subtitle: General inverse-derivative rule, formal proof, and inverse trig formulas
documents.tags: inverse functions derivative of inverse arcsin arccos arctan implicit differentiation domain restriction monotone one-to-one horizontal line test
documents.aliases: instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof
documents.assignment: Finding the Derivative of an Inverse from a Table \text{Given } f(5) = 2 \text{ and } f'(5) = 4 \text{, find } (f^{-1})'(2).   The slope at y=2 on the original function translates to 1/4 at x=2 on the inverse. (f^{-1})'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(x))} Write the theorem for the derivative of an inverse function. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(2))} Substitute x = 2. f^{-1}(2) = 5 Since f(5) = 2, it implies f^{-1}(2) = 5. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(5)} Substitute 5 for f^{-1}(2). (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{4} Substitute the given slope f'(5) = 4. Why the Slope Becomes 1/4 Set a ≈ 2.24. The blue slope will be ≈ 4.47 (= 2a) and the green slope will be ≈ 0.224 (= 1/(2a)). This is the same reciprocal relationship as in the example. Differentiating an Inverse Trig Function with the Chain Rule \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arctan(e^x).   The Chain Rule works seamlessly with inverse trig functions. \frac{d}{dx}[\arctan(u)] = \frac{1}{1+u^2} \cdot u' Write the derivative rule for arctan combined with the Chain Rule. u = e^x \implies u' = e^x Identify the inside function as e^x and its derivative. y' = \frac{1}{1+(e^x)^2} \cdot e^x Substitute into the Chain Rule formula. y' = \frac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}} Simplify the power using (e^x)^2 = e^{2x}. Where 1/(1+u²) Comes From Drag x in the lab to see how the triangle and the result 1/(1+x²) vary. For ex. 2, substitute u = eˣ into this result and multiply by (eˣ)'. Derivative of arcsin(sqrt(x)) \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arcsin(\sqrt{x}).   y' = 1/(2sqrt(x)sqrt(1-x)), valid for 0 < x < 1. \frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin(u)] = \frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} Use inverse trig derivative with chain rule. u = \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2},\; u' = 1/(2\sqrt{x}) Differentiate inner function. y' = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{x})^2}} Substitute u and u'. y' = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1-x}} Simplify (sqrt(x))^2 = x. The arcsin Triangle (same proof structure) Compare the arctan triangle here with the arcsin triangle: the role of hypotenuse and adjacent swap. Both proofs use the same implicit differentiation + triangle reading strategy. Derivative of arccos(3x) \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arccos(3x).   y' = -3/sqrt(1-9x^2), valid for |x| < 1/3. \frac{d}{dx}[\arccos(u)] = -\frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} Use arccos derivative rule. u = 3x,\; u' = 3 Inner derivative. y' = -\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-9x^2}} Substitute and simplify. Why the Chain Rule Multiplies in a Factor of 3 The proof structure (implicit diff → triangle reading) is the same for arccos as for arctan. The only differences are which leg is which in the triangle and the sign. Finding an Inverse Algebraically \text{Find } f^{-1}(x) \text{ for } f(x) = 3x - 7.   The algebraic method: write $y = f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, solve for $y$, rename it $f^{-1}(x)$. y = 3x - 7 Step 1: Write $y = f(x)$. This just renames the output to give us something concrete to work with. x = 3y - 7 Step 2: Swap $x$ and $y$. This is the algebraic version of reflecting across $y = x$. Now $x$ is the input to the inverse and $y$ is the output. x + 7 = 3y Step 3: Solve for $y$. Add 7 to both sides. y = \dfrac{x + 7}{3} Divide both sides by 3. f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x + 7}{3} Step 4: Rename $y$ as $f^{-1}(x)$. Done. Quick check: $f(f^{-1}(x)) = 3 \cdot \frac{x+7}{3} - 7 = x + 7 - 7 = x$. ✓ When You Must Restrict the Domain \text{Find an inverse for } f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5 \text{ on an appropriate domain.}   For non-monotone functions: find the vertex/turning point using $f'$, pick a monotone branch, apply the $\pm$ rule using your restriction. f'(x) = 2x - 4 Step 1: Find $f'(x)$ to locate where $f$ is monotone. $f'(x) = 0$ at $x = 2$, which is the vertex of this upward parabola. f'(x) > 0 \text{ for } x > 2, \quad f'(x) < 0 \text{ for } x < 2 Step 2: Read the sign of $f'$. To the right of $x = 2$ the function increases; to the left it decreases. Either piece is one-to-one. \text{Restrict to } [2, \infty) Step 3: Choose a restriction. We pick $[2, \infty)$ — the increasing branch — because it includes the output range $[1, \infty)$ that starts at the minimum value $f(2) = 1$. y = x^2 - 4x + 5 = (x-2)^2 + 1 Step 4: Complete the square to make solving easier. Write $y = (x-2)^2 + 1$. x = (y - 2)^2 + 1 Swap $x$ and $y$. (y-2)^2 = x - 1 Isolate the squared term. y - 2 = \sqrt{x - 1} Take the positive square root because we restricted to $y \geq 2$ (the increasing branch). This is where the domain restriction earns its keep — without it, $\pm\sqrt{x-1}$ would give two answers. f^{-1}(x) = 2 + \sqrt{x - 1}, \quad x \geq 1 Final answer. Domain of $f^{-1}$ is $[1, \infty)$, which equals the range of $f$ on $[2, \infty)$. Using the Derivative Formula at a Point \text{Let } f(x) = x^5 + x. \text{ Find } (f^{-1})'(2).   To use the formula at a point: (1) solve $f(a) = x$ to find $f^{-1}(x) = a$, (2) compute $f'(a)$, (3) take the reciprocal. You never need an explicit formula for $f^{-1}$. f(1) = 1^5 + 1 = 2 Step 1: Find $a$ such that $f(a) = 2$ — this tells us which input to $f$ maps to the output $2$, i.e., $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. Try small integers. f^{-1}(2) = 1 We just found that $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. This is the value we will plug into $f'$. f'(x) = 5x^4 + 1 Step 2: Differentiate $f$. f'(1) = 5(1)^4 + 1 = 6 Step 3: Evaluate $f'$ at $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(2))} = \frac{1}{f'(1)} = \frac{1}{6} Step 4: Apply the formula. The derivative of the inverse at $x = 2$ is $1/6$. Note: we never had to find a formula for $f^{-1}(x)$ — just one specific value. Derivative of $\arcsin(x)$ \text{Derive the formula } \frac{d}{dx}\arcsin(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}.   The derivation pattern — let $y = \arcsin(x)$, write $\sin(y) = x$, differentiate implicitly, use a trig identity to eliminate $y$ — applies to all inverse trig functions. y = \arcsin(x) \implies \sin(y) = x, \quad y \in \bigl[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr] Let $y = \arcsin(x)$. By definition this means $\sin(y) = x$ and $y$ lives in the restricted domain where $\sin$ is one-to-one. \frac{d}{dx}[\sin(y)] = \frac{d}{dx}[x] Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ (implicit differentiation). \cos(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 1 Chain rule on the left: $\frac{d}{dx}\sin(y) = \cos(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$. \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{\cos(y)} Solve for $dy/dx$. \sin^2(y) + \cos^2(y) = 1 \implies \cos(y) = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2(y)} = \sqrt{1 - x^2} Rewrite $\cos(y)$ in terms of $x$ using the Pythagorean identity and $\sin(y) = x$. The positive square root is correct because $y \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ keeps $\cos(y) \geq 0$. \frac{d}{dx}\arcsin(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}, \quad -1 < x < 1 Substitute $\cos(y) = \sqrt{1-x^2}$. Domain excludes $\pm 1$ because $\cos(y) = 0$ there — the tangent to $\arcsin$ is vertical at the endpoints. Derivative of $\arctan(x)$ \text{Derive } \frac{d}{dx}\arctan(x) = \frac{1}{1+x^2}.   Key move: eliminate the trig function of $y$ by applying the right Pythagorean identity and substituting the original equation. For $\arctan$ that is $\sec^2 = 1 + \tan^2$; for $\arcsin$ it is $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$. y = \arctan(x) \implies \tan(y) = x, \quad y \in \bigl(-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr) Set up: $\tan(y) = x$ with the standard restriction. \sec^2(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 1 Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$. Left side: $\frac{d}{dx}\tan(y) = \sec^2(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$ by chain rule. \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1}{\sec^2(y)} = \cos^2(y) Isolate $dy/dx$. \sec^2(y) = 1 + \tan^2(y) = 1 + x^2 Use the Pythagorean identity $\sec^2(y) = 1 + \tan^2(y)$ and substitute $\tan(y) = x$. \frac{d}{dx}\arctan(x) = \frac{1}{1+x^2} Therefore $dy/dx = 1/(1+x^2)$. This is defined for all real $x$ — $\arctan$ has no domain restriction issues in its derivative. Chain Rule with Inverse Trig \text{Find } \frac{d}{dx}\arctan(3x^2).   Inverse trig derivatives combined with the chain rule always follow the same pattern: apply the standard formula with $u$ replacing $x$, then multiply by $du/dx$. \frac{d}{dx}\arctan(u) = \frac{1}{1+u^2} \cdot \frac{du}{dx}, \quad u = 3x^2 This is a composition: $\arctan$ of $(3x^2)$. Set up the chain rule: derivative of outer times derivative of inner. \frac{du}{dx} = 6x Differentiate the inside: $u = 3x^2$, so $du/dx = 6x$. \frac{d}{dx}\arctan(3x^2) = \frac{1}{1+(3x^2)^2} \cdot 6x Substitute $u = 3x^2$ and $du/dx = 6x$ into the chain rule expression. = \frac{6x}{1 + 9x^4} Simplify $(3x^2)^2 = 9x^4$. Final answer.  \text{Find } \frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin(2x)]. Use arcsin chain rule with u=2x. 2/sqrt(1-4x^2)  \frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin(u)] = \frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}}  u=2x,\;u'=2  y'=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-4x^2}}   \text{Given } f(2)=5, f'(2)=-3, \text{ find } (f^{-1})'(5). Use reciprocal slope at corresponding points. -1/3  (f^(-1))'(x)=1/f'(f^(-1)(x))  f^(-1)(5)=2 Because f(2)=5. (f^(-1))'(5)=1/f'(2)=1/(-3)=-1/3   \text{Find } f^{-1}(x) \text{ for } f(x) = \frac{x+1}{2}. Write $y = (x+1)/2$, swap $x$ and $y$, solve for $y$. f^{-1}(x) = 2x - 1  x = \dfrac{y+1}{2} Swap $x$ and $y$. 2x = y + 1 Multiply both sides by 2. f^{-1}(x) = 2x - 1 Solve for $y$ and rename.  \text{Let } g(x) = x^3 + 2. \text{ Find } (g^{-1})'(10). Find $a$ such that $g(a) = 10$. Then use the formula $(g^{-1})'(10) = 1/g'(a)$. (g^{-1})'(10) = \dfrac{1}{12}  g(2) = 8 + 2 = 10 \implies g^{-1}(10) = 2 Identify which input to $g$ gives output 10. g'(x) = 3x^2 \implies g'(2) = 12 Differentiate $g$ and evaluate at $x = 2$. (g^{-1})'(10) = \frac{1}{g'(2)} = \frac{1}{12} Apply the inverse derivative formula.  \text{Differentiate } h(x) = \arcsin(\sqrt{x}). Use chain rule. The outer function is $\arcsin(u)$ where $u = \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2}$. h'(x) = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x(1-x)}}  h'(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (\sqrt{x})^2}} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{x}) Chain rule: derivative of $\arcsin(u)$ times derivative of the inside. \frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt{x}) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} Power rule on the inside. h'(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - x}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} Substitute and simplify $(\sqrt{x})^2 = x$. h'(x) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x(1-x)}} Combine denominators. Valid for $0 < x < 1$.  \text{Show that } f(x) = x + e^x \text{ is one-to-one on } \mathbb{R}, \text{ then find } (f^{-1})'(1). Check the sign of $f'(x)$ to confirm monotonicity. To find $(f^{-1})'(1)$, first solve $f(a) = 1$. (f^{-1})'(1) = \dfrac{1}{2}  f'(x) = 1 + e^x > 0 \text{ for all } x \in \mathbb{R} Since $e^x > 0$ always, $f'(x) > 0$ everywhere. So $f$ is strictly increasing and one-to-one on all of $\mathbb{R}$. f(0) = 0 + e^0 = 1 \implies f^{-1}(1) = 0 Solve $f(a) = 1$: $a + e^a = 1$ is satisfied by $a = 0$. f'(0) = 1 + e^0 = 2 Evaluate the derivative at the identified input. (f^{-1})'(1) = \frac{1}{f'(0)} = \frac{1}{2} Apply the formula.  \text{Derive } \frac{d}{dx}\arccos(x) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \text{ from scratch using implicit differentiation.} Let $y = \arccos(x)$, write $\cos(y) = x$, differentiate implicitly, and use the identity $\sin^2(y) + \cos^2(y) = 1$. Watch the sign — $\sin(y) \geq 0$ on $[0, \pi]$. \frac{d}{dx}\arccos(x) = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}  y = \arccos(x) \implies \cos(y) = x, \quad y \in [0, \pi] Set up with the standard restriction. -\sin(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 1 Differentiate both sides: $\frac{d}{dx}\cos(y) = -\sin(y) \cdot \frac{dy}{dx}$. \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-1}{\sin(y)} Solve for $dy/dx$. \sin(y) = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2(y)} = \sqrt{1-x^2} Since $y \in [0, \pi]$, $\sin(y) \geq 0$, so we take the positive root. Substitute $\cos(y) = x$. \frac{d}{dx}\arccos(x) = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} Substitute. The negative sign comes from differentiating $\cos$, not from the square root choice. This is why $\frac{d}{dx}\arccos(x)$ and $\frac{d}{dx}\arcsin(x)$ are negatives of each other — they sum to a constant: $\arcsin(x) + \arccos(x) = \pi/2$.
documents.proofs: Formal proof of inverse derivative rule: start from identity f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Differentiate both sides with respect to x: f'(f^(-1)(x)) * (f^(-1))'(x) = 1. Solve for (f^(-1))'(x): (f^(-1))'(x) = 1 / f'(f^(-1)(x)). This requires f'(f^(-1)(x)) != 0. Now for the main event of Calc 1: differentiating an inverse function. The key formula is simple once you see where it comes from. Start from the defining equation $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$. Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ using the chain rule on the left: \[ f'(f^{-1}(x)) \cdot (f^{-1})'(x) = 1 \] Divide both sides by $f'(f^{-1}(x))$ (assuming it is nonzero): \[ (f^{-1})'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(x))} \] That is the formula. In words: the derivative of the inverse at $x$ equals one over the derivative of the original function evaluated at $f^{-1}(x)$. The geometry makes this obvious. Recall that the graph of $f^{-1}$ is the graph of $f$ reflected across $y = x$. A slope of $m$ on $f$ becomes a slope of $1/m$ on $f^{-1}$. Reflection across $y = x$ swaps rise and run, turning rise/run into run/rise $= 1/(\text{rise/run})$. The formula requires $f'(f^{-1}(x)) \neq 0$. If $f$ has a horizontal tangent at the point being reflected, $f^{-1}$ would need a vertical tangent there — undefined slope. This is why we can only apply the formula at points where $f$ is not flat. Practical note: you rarely need an explicit formula for $f^{-1}(x)$ to compute $(f^{-1})'(a)$. Solve $f(c)=a$ for the input $c$, compute $f'(c)$, and take the reciprocal: $(f^{-1})'(a)=1/f'(c)$. This saves algebra and is especially useful when $f^{-1}$ has no simple closed form. Edge cases: the formula fails if $f$ is not differentiable at $c$ or if $f'(c)=0$ (vertical tangent for the inverse). Also ensure $f$ is one-to-one on a neighborhood of $c$ so a local inverse exists. If any of these fail, the inverse may exist but not be differentiable there. Common student mistakes: (1) evaluating $f'$ at the wrong point (plug into $f'$ the input $c$, not the output $a$), (2) confusing $f^{-1}(x)$ with $1/f(x)$, and (3) forgetting to check that $f$ is one-to-one near the point of interest. Quick checklist before applying the inverse-derivative formula: (i) find $c$ with $f(c)=a$, (ii) verify $f$ is one-to-one near $c$, (iii) check $f'(c)$ exists and $\neq 0$, (iv) compute $(f^{-1})'(a)=1/f'(c)$. Formal derivation of arcsin: let y = arcsin x so sin y = x. Differentiate: cos y * dy/dx = 1, hence dy/dx = 1/cos y. From sin y = x and principal range y in [-pi/2, pi/2], cos y = sqrt(1-x^2), giving dy/dx = 1/sqrt(1-x^2). For arccos: let y = arccos x so cos y = x. Differentiate: -sin y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = -1/sin y = -1/sqrt(1-x^2). For arctan: let y = arctan x so tan y = x. Differentiate: sec^2 y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/sec^2 y = 1/(1+tan^2 y) = 1/(1+x^2). Formal Proof (General Rule) f(f^(-1)(x))=x => f'(f^(-1)(x))*(f^(-1))'(x)=1 => (f^(-1))'(x)=1/f'(f^(-1)(x)).
documents.applications: Sensor calibration, coordinate transforms, control systems, and inverse kinematics all rely on inverse mappings. When you reverse a relationship, rates invert too - but at the correct corresponding point. This rule is also the engine behind derivatives of arcsin, arccos, and arctan. Inverse Function If $f$ is one-to-one on its domain, the inverse function $f^{-1}$ satisfies \[ f^{-1}(f(x)) = x \quad \text{for all } x \in \text{dom}(f) \] \[ f(f^{-1}(y)) = y \quad \text{for all } y \in \text{range}(f) \] Note: $f^{-1}(x)$ is not $\frac{1}{f(x)}$. The $-1$ is a label, not an exponent. What swapping input and output does to the graph Domain and range swap. If $f$ has domain $[a, b]$ and range $[c, d]$, then $f^{-1}$ has domain $[c, d]$ and range $[a, b]$. The graph reflects across $y = x$. $f^{-1}$ is not $1/f$ $f^{-1}(x)$ means the inverse function. $[f(x)]^{-1} = \frac{1}{f(x)}$ means the reciprocal. These are completely different. Example: if $f(x) = x^2$ restricted to $x \geq 0$, then $f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt{x}$, not $\frac{1}{x^2}$. Evaluation Point Matters (f^(-1))'(x) = 1 / f'(f^(-1)(x)). You must evaluate f' at f^(-1)(x), not at x itself. Slope Reciprocity At corresponding points (a, b) and (b, a), slopes multiply to 1: f'(a) * (f^(-1))'(b) = 1, provided f'(a) != 0.
documents.content: If a function maps x to y, how do slopes transform when we reverse that mapping and view x as a function of y? Sensor calibration, coordinate transforms, control systems, and inverse kinematics all rely on inverse mappings. When you reverse a relationship, rates invert too - but at the correct corresponding point. This rule is also the engine behind derivatives of arcsin, arccos, and arctan. If y = f(x), then the inverse relation is x = f^(-1)(y). Intuitively, moving forward through f and then backward through f^(-1) cancels out. On a graph, f and f^(-1) are reflections across y = x. Reflection swaps x and y coordinates, so tangent rise/run becomes run/rise. That is why inverse slopes are reciprocals. The most common mistake is evaluating at the wrong point. If f(a) = b, then the reciprocal slope relation is between f'(a) and (f^(-1))'(b), not at the same x-value. This general rule plus implicit differentiation gives the inverse trig derivatives cleanly, with domain restrictions built in. Think of a function as a machine. You put in $x$, it produces $y = f(x)$. An inverse function is a second machine that takes the output $y$ and hands back the original input $x$. We write it $f^{-1}(y) = x$, or equivalently $f^{-1}(f(x)) = x$ for every $x$ in the domain. Here is the single most important visual fact: the graph of $f^{-1}$ is the graph of $f$ reflected across the line $y = x$. Every point $(a, b)$ on $f$ becomes the point $(b, a)$ on $f^{-1}$. That reflection is not a trick — it is the geometric meaning of swapping input and output. But there is an immediate problem. If $f$ ever produces the same output from two different inputs — say $f(2) = 9$ and $f(-2) = 9$ — then the "undo" machine is confused: given output $9$, does it return $2$ or $-2$? It cannot do both and still be a function. This is why we need the function to be one-to-one: each output must come from exactly one input. The quick visual check is the horizontal line test. Draw any horizontal line across the graph of $f$. If any horizontal line hits the graph more than once, $f$ is not one-to-one and has no inverse on that full domain. Before we can talk about the derivative, we need to be precise about when an inverse exists. The key word is monotone. A function is monotonically increasing on an interval if, whenever $x_1 < x_2$ in that interval, $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$. It is monotonically decreasing if $x_1 < x_2$ implies $f(x_1) > f(x_2)$. Either way — strictly increasing or strictly decreasing — the function is one-to-one on that interval, so an inverse exists. How do you check monotonicity using the derivative? On an interval where $f'(x) > 0$ throughout, $f$ is strictly increasing. Where $f'(x) < 0$ throughout, $f$ is strictly decreasing. A single sign throughout means one-to-one. This is the calculus-powered version of the horizontal line test. Domain restriction is how we rescue functions that are not globally one-to-one. The classic example is $f(x) = x^2$. On all of $\mathbb{R}$ it fails the horizontal line test. But on $[0, \infty)$ it is strictly increasing and invertible, giving $f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt{x}$. On $(-\infty, 0]$ it is strictly decreasing and also invertible, giving $f^{-1}(x) = -\sqrt{x}$. The choice of restriction is a convention — we pick the piece that is most useful. The inverse trig functions arise exactly this way. $\sin(x)$ is not one-to-one globally — it repeats forever. We restrict to $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, where it is strictly increasing (derivative $\cos x > 0$ on the open interval). That gives us $\arcsin(x)$ with domain $[-1, 1]$ and range $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. Similarly for $\arccos$ (restricted to $[0, \pi]$) and $\arctan$ (restricted to $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, and because $\tan$ is already one-to-one on that open interval, the range is all of $\mathbb{R}$). General inverse derivative rule: if f is differentiable and one-to-one near a, with f(a) = b and f'(a) != 0, then f^(-1) is differentiable near b and (f^(-1))'(b) = 1/f'(a). Equivalent formula in x-form: (f^(-1))'(x) = 1 / f'(f^(-1)(x)). Inverse trig derivatives (principal branches): d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/sqrt(1-x^2),   |x| < 1 d/dx[arccos x] = -1/sqrt(1-x^2),  |x| < 1 d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x^2),       all x These follow from implicit differentiation plus right-triangle identities. Formal proof of inverse derivative rule: start from identity f(f^(-1)(x)) = x. Differentiate both sides with respect to x: f'(f^(-1)(x)) * (f^(-1))'(x) = 1. Solve for (f^(-1))'(x): (f^(-1))'(x) = 1 / f'(f^(-1)(x)). This requires f'(f^(-1)(x)) != 0. Now for the main event of Calc 1: differentiating an inverse function. The key formula is simple once you see where it comes from. Start from the defining equation $f(f^{-1}(x)) = x$. Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$ using the chain rule on the left: \[ f'(f^{-1}(x)) \cdot (f^{-1})'(x) = 1 \] Divide both sides by $f'(f^{-1}(x))$ (assuming it is nonzero): \[ (f^{-1})'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(x))} \] That is the formula. In words: the derivative of the inverse at $x$ equals one over the derivative of the original function evaluated at $f^{-1}(x)$. The geometry makes this obvious. Recall that the graph of $f^{-1}$ is the graph of $f$ reflected across $y = x$. A slope of $m$ on $f$ becomes a slope of $1/m$ on $f^{-1}$. Reflection across $y = x$ swaps rise and run, turning rise/run into run/rise $= 1/(\text{rise/run})$. The formula requires $f'(f^{-1}(x)) \neq 0$. If $f$ has a horizontal tangent at the point being reflected, $f^{-1}$ would need a vertical tangent there — undefined slope. This is why we can only apply the formula at points where $f$ is not flat. Practical note: you rarely need an explicit formula for $f^{-1}(x)$ to compute $(f^{-1})'(a)$. Solve $f(c)=a$ for the input $c$, compute $f'(c)$, and take the reciprocal: $(f^{-1})'(a)=1/f'(c)$. This saves algebra and is especially useful when $f^{-1}$ has no simple closed form. Edge cases: the formula fails if $f$ is not differentiable at $c$ or if $f'(c)=0$ (vertical tangent for the inverse). Also ensure $f$ is one-to-one on a neighborhood of $c$ so a local inverse exists. If any of these fail, the inverse may exist but not be differentiable there. Common student mistakes: (1) evaluating $f'$ at the wrong point (plug into $f'$ the input $c$, not the output $a$), (2) confusing $f^{-1}(x)$ with $1/f(x)$, and (3) forgetting to check that $f$ is one-to-one near the point of interest. Quick checklist before applying the inverse-derivative formula: (i) find $c$ with $f(c)=a$, (ii) verify $f$ is one-to-one near $c$, (iii) check $f'(c)$ exists and $\neq 0$, (iv) compute $(f^{-1})'(a)=1/f'(c)$. Formal derivation of arcsin: let y = arcsin x so sin y = x. Differentiate: cos y * dy/dx = 1, hence dy/dx = 1/cos y. From sin y = x and principal range y in [-pi/2, pi/2], cos y = sqrt(1-x^2), giving dy/dx = 1/sqrt(1-x^2). For arccos: let y = arccos x so cos y = x. Differentiate: -sin y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = -1/sin y = -1/sqrt(1-x^2). For arctan: let y = arctan x so tan y = x. Differentiate: sec^2 y * dy/dx = 1, so dy/dx = 1/sec^2 y = 1/(1+tan^2 y) = 1/(1+x^2). Formal Proof (General Rule) f(f^(-1)(x))=x => f'(f^(-1)(x))*(f^(-1))'(x)=1 => (f^(-1))'(x)=1/f'(f^(-1)(x)). Inverse Function If $f$ is one-to-one on its domain, the inverse function $f^{-1}$ satisfies \[ f^{-1}(f(x)) = x \quad \text{for all } x \in \text{dom}(f) \] \[ f(f^{-1}(y)) = y \quad \text{for all } y \in \text{range}(f) \] Note: $f^{-1}(x)$ is not $\frac{1}{f(x)}$. The $-1$ is a label, not an exponent. What swapping input and output does to the graph Domain and range swap. If $f$ has domain $[a, b]$ and range $[c, d]$, then $f^{-1}$ has domain $[c, d]$ and range $[a, b]$. The graph reflects across $y = x$. $f^{-1}$ is not $1/f$ $f^{-1}(x)$ means the inverse function. $[f(x)]^{-1} = \frac{1}{f(x)}$ means the reciprocal. These are completely different. Example: if $f(x) = x^2$ restricted to $x \geq 0$, then $f^{-1}(x) = \sqrt{x}$, not $\frac{1}{x^2}$. Evaluation Point Matters (f^(-1))'(x) = 1 / f'(f^(-1)(x)). You must evaluate f' at f^(-1)(x), not at x itself. Slope Reciprocity At corresponding points (a, b) and (b, a), slopes multiply to 1: f'(a) * (f^(-1))'(b) = 1, provided f'(a) != 0. Inverse Derivative Rule \frac{d}{dx}[f^{-1}(x)] = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(x))} Inverse Trig Derivatives \frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin x]=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}},\;\frac{d}{dx}[\arccos x]=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}},\;\frac{d}{dx}[\arctan x]=\frac{1}{1+x^2} Monotone Inverse Theorem If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and strictly monotone (increasing or decreasing) on $(a, b)$, then $f$ has an inverse $f^{-1}$ defined on $[f(a), f(b)]$ (or $[f(b), f(a)]$ if decreasing), and $f^{-1}$ is also continuous and strictly monotone. What to look for: the monotone checklist (1) Find $f'(x)$. (2) Determine where $f'(x) > 0$ (increasing) or $f'(x) < 0$ (decreasing). (3) Each maximal interval of constant sign is a domain on which $f$ is one-to-one and invertible. (4) Choose the restriction that includes the output range you care about. Standard Inverse Trig Domain Restrictions \begin{aligned} \arcsin &: [-1,1] \to [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}] \\[4pt] \arccos &: [-1,1] \to [0, \pi] \\[4pt] \arctan &: (-\infty,\infty) \to (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}) \end{aligned} Step-by-Step Inverse Function Explainer Walk through the logic of inverse functions, mirror geometry, and slope reciprocity. Slope Reciprocity via Reflection Drag the point on f(x) = x². The mirrored green point on f⁻¹(x) = √x always carries the reciprocal slope. Their product is always 1. Universal Inverse Lab: Try More Than x² Switch families and move the point. The geometry changes, but slope reciprocity at reflected points does not. Reflection and Reciprocal Slopes (Symbolic View) As a point moves on f, the reflected point on f^(-1) shows reciprocal tangent slope at matching coordinates. Geometric Reflection Lab Watch points swap as they reflect across y=x. See why domain and range must swap too. Slope Reciprocity Calculator Directly compare the slope of f at (a,b) with the slope of f⁻¹ at (b,a). The Rule in Action: f(x) = x² Verify the formula numerically: move to any a, read f'(a) = 2a, then check that (f⁻¹)'(a²) = 1/(2a). Product is always 1. Inverse Trig Denominators from Geometry Toggle arcsin/arctan mode and watch the geometric denominator update live with x. Trig Domain Restriction Map Visualize why we must cut the trig functions to make them one-to-one. Monotonicity & Inverses Check the sign of f' to see where the inverse legally exists. arctan Derivative: Full Geometric Proof Drag x and watch the triangle update. Each proof step stays fixed — only the live numerical values change. This is implicit differentiation made visual. Proof: The Power Rule for Rational Exponents Step through the formal derivation of d/dx[x^(m/n)] = (m/n)x^(m/n-1) using the inverse rule. The Implicit Proof Machine Step through the formal derivation of the inverse rule using implicit differentiation. Finding the Derivative of an Inverse from a Table \text{Given } f(5) = 2 \text{ and } f'(5) = 4 \text{, find } (f^{-1})'(2). The slope at y=2 on the original function translates to 1/4 at x=2 on the inverse. (f^{-1})'(x) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(x))} Write the theorem for the derivative of an inverse function. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(2))} Substitute x = 2. f^{-1}(2) = 5 Since f(5) = 2, it implies f^{-1}(2) = 5. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(5)} Substitute 5 for f^{-1}(2). (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{4} Substitute the given slope f'(5) = 4. Why the Slope Becomes 1/4 Set a ≈ 2.24. The blue slope will be ≈ 4.47 (= 2a) and the green slope will be ≈ 0.224 (= 1/(2a)). This is the same reciprocal relationship as in the example. Differentiating an Inverse Trig Function with the Chain Rule \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arctan(e^x). The Chain Rule works seamlessly with inverse trig functions. \frac{d}{dx}[\arctan(u)] = \frac{1}{1+u^2} \cdot u' Write the derivative rule for arctan combined with the Chain Rule. u = e^x \implies u' = e^x Identify the inside function as e^x and its derivative. y' = \frac{1}{1+(e^x)^2} \cdot e^x Substitute into the Chain Rule formula. y' = \frac{e^x}{1+e^{2x}} Simplify the power using (e^x)^2 = e^{2x}. Where 1/(1+u²) Comes From Drag x in the lab to see how the triangle and the result 1/(1+x²) vary. For ex. 2, substitute u = eˣ into this result and multiply by (eˣ)'. Derivative of arcsin(sqrt(x)) \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arcsin(\sqrt{x}). y' = 1/(2sqrt(x)sqrt(1-x)), valid for 0 < x < 1. \frac{d}{dx}[\arcsin(u)] = \frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} Use inverse trig derivative with chain rule. u = \sqrt{x} = x^{1/2},\; u' = 1/(2\sqrt{x}) Differentiate inner function. y' = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{x})^2}} Substitute u and u'. y' = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1-x}} Simplify (sqrt(x))^2 = x. The arcsin Triangle (same proof structure) Compare the arctan triangle here with the arcsin triangle: the role of hypotenuse and adjacent swap. Both proofs use the same implicit differentiation + triangle reading strategy. Derivative of arccos(3x) \text{Find } y' \text{ for } y = \arccos(3x). y' = -3/sqrt(1-9x^2), valid for |x| < 1/3. \frac{d}{dx}[\arccos(u)] = -\frac{u'}{\sqrt{1-u^2}} Use arccos derivative rule. u = 3x,\; u' = 3 Inner derivative. y' = -\frac{3}{\sqrt{1-9x^2}} Substitute and simplify. Why the Chain Rule Multiplies in a Factor of 3 The proof structure (implicit diff → triangle reading) is the same for arccos as for arctan. The only differences are which leg is which in the triangle and the sign. Finding an Inverse Algebraically \text{Find } f^{-1}(x) \text{ for } f(x) = 3x - 7. The algebraic method: write $y = f(x)$, swap $x$ and $y$, solve for $y$, rename it $f^{-1}(x)$. y = 3x - 7 Step 1: Write $y = f(x)$. This just renames the output to give us something concrete to work with. x = 3y - 7 Step 2: Swap $x$ and $y$. This is the algebraic version of reflecting across $y = x$. Now $x$ is the input to the inverse and $y$ is the output. x + 7 = 3y Step 3: Solve for $y$. Add 7 to both sides. y = \dfrac{x + 7}{3} Divide both sides by 3. f^{-1}(x) = \dfrac{x + 7}{3} Step 4: Rename $y$ as $f^{-1}(x)$. Done. Quick check: $f(f^{-1}(x)) = 3 \cdot \frac{x+7}{3} - 7 = x + 7 - 7 = x$. ✓ When You Must Restrict the Domain \text{Find an inverse for } f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 5 \text{ on an appropriate domain.} For non-monotone functions: find the vertex/turning point using $f'$, pick a monotone branch, apply the $\pm$ rule using your restriction. f'(x) = 2x - 4 Step 1: Find $f'(x)$ to locate where $f$ is monotone. $f'(x) = 0$ at $x = 2$, which is the vertex of this upward parabola. f'(x) > 0 \text{ for } x > 2, \quad f'(x) < 0 \text{ for } x < 2 Step 2: Read the sign of $f'$. To the right of $x = 2$ the function increases; to the left it decreases. Either piece is one-to-one. \text{Restrict to } [2, \infty) Step 3: Choose a restriction. We pick $[2, \infty)$ — the increasing branch — because it includes the output range $[1, \infty)$ that starts at the minimum value $f(2) = 1$. y = x^2 - 4x + 5 = (x-2)^2 + 1 Step 4: Complete the square to make solving easier. Write $y = (x-2)^2 + 1$. x = (y - 2)^2 + 1 Swap $x$ and $y$. (y-2)^2 = x - 1 Isolate the squared term. y - 2 = \sqrt{x - 1} Take the positive square root because we restricted to $y \geq 2$ (the increasing branch). This is where the domain restriction earns its keep — without it, $\pm\sqrt{x-1}$ would give two answers. f^{-1}(x) = 2 + \sqrt{x - 1}, \quad x \geq 1 Final answer. Domain of $f^{-1}$ is $[1, \infty)$, which equals the range of $f$ on $[2, \infty)$. Using the Derivative Formula at a Point \text{Let } f(x) = x^5 + x. \text{ Find } (f^{-1})'(2). To use the formula at a point: (1) solve $f(a) = x$ to find $f^{-1}(x) = a$, (2) compute $f'(a)$, (3) take the reciprocal. You never need an explicit formula for $f^{-1}$. f(1) = 1^5 + 1 = 2 Step 1: Find $a$ such that $f(a) = 2$ — this tells us which input to $f$ maps to the output $2$, i.e., $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. Try small integers. f^{-1}(2) = 1 We just found that $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. This is the value we will plug into $f'$. f'(x) = 5x^4 + 1 Step 2: Differentiate $f$. f'(1) = 5(1)^4 + 1 = 6 Step 3: Evaluate $f'$ at $f^{-1}(2) = 1$. (f^{-1})'(2) = \frac{1}{f'(f^{-1}(2))} = \frac{1}{f'(1)} = \frac{1}{6} Step 4: Apply the formula. The derivative of the inverse at $x = 2$ is $1/6$. Note: we never had to find a formula for $f^{-1}(x)$ — just one specific value. Derivative of $\arcsin(x)$ \text{Derive the formula } \frac{d}{dx}\arcsin(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}. The derivation pattern — let $y = \arcsin(x)$, write $\sin(y) = x$, differentiate implicitly, use a trig identity to eliminate $y$ — applies to all inverse trig functions. y = \arcsin(x) \implies \sin(y) = x, \quad y \in \bigl[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr] Let $y = \arcsin(x)$.
