documents.assignment: Verify MVT for f(x) = x² on [1, 3] \text{Verify the MVT for } f(x) = x^2 \text{ on } [1, 3] \text{ and find all guaranteed points } c.   For f(x) = x² on [1,3], the unique MVT point is c = 2, the midpoint of [1,3]. This is not a coincidence for quadratic functions — for any f(x) = x² on [a,b], the MVT point is always c = (a+b)/2 (the midpoint), because f'(x) = 2x is linear and hits the average value exactly at the midpoint. \frac{f(3)-f(1)}{3-1} = \frac{9-1}{2} = 4 Compute the average rate of change (slope of the secant from (1,1) to (3,9)). f'(x) = 2x Compute the derivative. f'(c) = 4 \Rightarrow 2c = 4 \Rightarrow c = 2 Set f'(c) equal to the average rate and solve. c = 2 ∈ (1, 3) ✓. \text{The tangent at } c=2 \text{ has slope } f'(2) = 4 = \text{slope of secant} Verify: f'(2) = 4 matches the secant slope. The MVT is satisfied with c = 2. Verify MVT for f(x) = x³ on [-1, 1] \text{Find all } c \text{ satisfying the MVT for } f(x) = x^3 \text{ on } [-1, 1].   There are two MVT points for x³ on [-1,1]: c = ±1/√3. The MVT promised at least one; we found two. The function x³ is odd (symmetric about the origin), and the two c values are symmetric as well — another symmetry consequence. \frac{f(1)-f(-1)}{1-(-1)} = \frac{1-(-1)}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 Compute the average rate of change. f'(x) = 3x^2 Derivative of x³. f'(c) = 1 \Rightarrow 3c^2 = 1 \Rightarrow c^2 = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow c = \pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx \pm 0.577 Solve for c. Two solutions: c = ±1/√3. c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \in (-1, 1) \checkmark, \quad c = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \in (-1, 1) \checkmark Both values are in the open interval (-1, 1), confirming the MVT. This illustrates that the MVT can have multiple c values. MVT Proof: |sin(x) - sin(y)| ≤ |x - y| \text{Use the MVT to prove that } |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| \leq |x - y| \text{ for all } x, y \in \mathbb{R}.   The sine function is Lipschitz continuous with constant 1 — it cannot change faster than the identity function. This is an MVT corollary (the error bound): |f(b)-f(a)| ≤ |b-a|·max|f'|. Here max|cos| = 1 gives the result. f(t) = \sin(t), \quad f'(t) = \cos(t) Apply the MVT to f(t) = sin(t) on the interval between x and y (WLOG x < y). \sin(x) - \sin(y) = \cos(c)(x - y) \text{ for some } c \in (y, x) By the MVT: f(x) - f(y) = f'(c)(x-y). |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| = |\cos(c)| \cdot |x - y| Take absolute values of both sides. Use |ab| = |a|·|b|. |\cos(c)| \leq 1 \text{ for all } c The cosine function satisfies |cos(c)| ≤ 1 everywhere. \therefore |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| \leq 1 \cdot |x - y| = |x - y| Multiply the inequality by |x-y| ≥ 0. ∎ Speed Trap Application \text{A car travels 200 miles in exactly 3 hours. Prove it exceeded 60 mph at some moment.}   At time c (some moment during the 3 hours), the instantaneous speed was exactly 200/3 ≈ 66.7 mph > 60 mph. The car definitely exceeded 60 mph at that moment. The MVT makes this a mathematical certainty, not just a suspicion. s(t) = \text{position (miles), } s(0) = 0, s(3) = 200 Set up: s(t) is the position function. s is continuous (the car moves continuously) and differentiable (it has a velocity) on (0,3). \frac{s(3) - s(0)}{3 - 0} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.7 \text{ mph} Compute the average velocity over the 3-hour trip. \text{By MVT: } \exists\, c \in (0, 3) \text{ such that } s'(c) = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.7 \text{ mph} Apply the MVT: since s satisfies the hypotheses, there exists c with s'(c) = 200/3. s'(c) = \frac{200}{3} > 60 \text{ mph} The instantaneous speed at time c exceeds 60 mph. ∎ Rolle's Theorem: Exactly One Solution in an Interval \text{Prove that } x^4 + x = 2 \text{ has exactly one solution in } [0, 1].   x⁴ + x = 2 has exactly one solution in [0,1], namely x = 1. The MVT/Rolle's approach (via the derivative) proves both existence and uniqueness. Existence: g(0) < 0 < g(1) and IVT (or just g(1) = 0). Uniqueness: g'> 0 so g is strictly increasing, hence at most one zero. g(x) = x^4 + x - 2 Reformulate: find zeros of g(x) = x⁴ + x - 2. g(0) = 0 + 0 - 2 = -2 < 0, \quad g(1) = 1 + 1 - 2 = 0 Evaluate at the endpoints. g(1) = 0 — so x = 1 is a solution! We need to show it is the ONLY one in [0,1]. g'(x) = 4x^3 + 1 > 0 \text{ for all } x \in [0, 1] g'(x) = 4x³ + 1 ≥ 1 > 0 for x ∈ [0,1]. So g is strictly increasing on [0,1]. \text{Strictly increasing} \Rightarrow \text{at most one zero} A strictly increasing function can cross zero at most once — it can only go from negative to positive once. So there is at most one solution. \text{g(0) < 0, g(1) = 0: exactly one zero at } x = 1 Since g is strictly increasing and g(1) = 0, the only zero in [0,1] is x = 1. MVT Inequality: √(1+x) < 1 + x/2 for x > 0 \text{Use the MVT to prove } \sqrt{1+x} < 1 + \frac{x}{2} \text{ for all } x > 0.   The MVT can be used to prove inequalities by bounding f'(c) on the relevant interval. The key steps are: (1) write f(b) - f(a) = f'(c)(b-a), then (2) bound f'(c) using the known range of c. This technique appears constantly in analysis. f(t) = \sqrt{t}, \text{ apply MVT on } [1, 1+x] Consider f(t) = √t on [1, 1+x] for x > 0. f(1+x) - f(1) = f'(c) \cdot x \text{ for some } c \in (1, 1+x) MVT: √(1+x) - 1 = f'(c)·x for some c strictly between 1 and 1+x. f'(t) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{t}} Derivative of √t. c > 1 \Rightarrow \sqrt{c} > 1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2\sqrt{c}} < \frac{1}{2} Since c > 1, √c > 1, so 1/(2√c) < 1/2. The c is strictly interior to (1, 1+x), so the strict inequality holds. \sqrt{1+x} - 1 = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{c}} < \frac{x}{2} Multiply the inequality 1/(2√c) < 1/2 by x > 0. \therefore \sqrt{1+x} < 1 + \frac{x}{2} \quad \text{for all } x > 0 \quad \square Add 1 to both sides.  Prove that |e^x - e^y| ≥ |x - y| for all x, y ≥ 0. Apply the MVT to f(t) = e^t on [x,y] (or [y,x]). Bound e^c from below. |e^x - e^y| ≥ |x-y| for x,y ≥ 0. The exponential function is "expanding" on [0,∞) — it stretches distances rather than contracting them. This contrasts with sin (which contracts: |sin(x) - sin(y)| ≤ |x-y|). Both proofs use the MVT with bounds on |f'(c)|.  f(t) = e^t, \quad f'(t) = e^t Apply MVT to e^t on [y, x] (assume x ≥ y ≥ 0 WLOG). e^x - e^y = e^c(x - y) \text{ for some } c \in (y, x) MVT gives: e^x - e^y = e^c(x-y). c \geq y \geq 0 \Rightarrow e^c \geq e^0 = 1 Since c ≥ y ≥ 0, and e^t is increasing, e^c ≥ e^0 = 1. |e^x - e^y| = e^c|x - y| \geq 1 \cdot |x - y| = |x - y| Take absolute values and apply the bound e^c ≥ 1. ∎  If f'(x) = f(x) for all x and f(0) = 0, prove that f is identically zero. Consider g(x) = f(x)·e^{-x} and apply the MVT (or show g' = 0 everywhere, hence g is constant, hence f = 0). The only function satisfying f'= f and f(0) = 0 is the zero function. This characterizes e^x uniquely: up to scaling, the exponential is the only function equal to its own derivative. With initial condition f(0) = 1 instead of 0, the unique solution is e^x.  g(x) = f(x) \cdot e^{-x} Define the helper function g. We will show g is constant. g'(x) = f'(x)e^{-x} + f(x)(-e^{-x}) = [f'(x) - f(x)]e^{-x} Differentiate g using the product rule. f'(x) = f(x) \Rightarrow f'(x) - f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow g'(x) = 0 Since f'(x) = f(x), the bracket is zero, so g'(x) = 0 for all x. g'(x) = 0 \text{ everywhere} \Rightarrow g \text{ is constant (by MVT corollary)} By Corollary 1 of the MVT, g is constant. g(0) = f(0) \cdot e^0 = 0 \cdot 1 = 0 Evaluate g at 0 using f(0) = 0. g(x) = 0 \text{ for all } x \Rightarrow f(x) \cdot e^{-x} = 0 \Rightarrow f(x) = 0 Since g is the constant 0 and e^{-x} ≠ 0, we conclude f(x) = 0. ∎  Prove Cauchy's MVT: if f, g are continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then ∃c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c)(g(b)-g(a)) = g'(c)(f(b)-f(a)). Define h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)) and verify h(a) = h(b), then apply Rolle's Theorem. Cauchy's MVT follows from Rolle's Theorem applied to h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)). The classical MVT is the special case g(x) = x.  h(x) = f(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(x)[f(b)-f(a)] Define the helper function h. It is a linear combination of f and g, so h is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b). h(a) = f(a)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(a)[f(b)-f(a)] Evaluate h at a. h(b) = f(b)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(b)[f(b)-f(a)] Evaluate h at b. h(b) - h(a) = [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0 Compute h(b) - h(a). It factors as [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0 (the two terms are equal). So h(a) = h(b). \text{By Rolle's Theorem: } \exists\, c \in (a,b) \text{ with } h'(c) = 0 h satisfies Rolle's hypotheses (continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), h(a) = h(b)). h'(x) = f'(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g'(x)[f(b)-f(a)] Differentiate h. h'(c) = 0 \Rightarrow f'(c)[g(b)-g(a)] = g'(c)[f(b)-f(a)] \quad \square Set h'(c) = 0 and rearrange. This is Cauchy's MVT. ∎
documents.proofs: Proof of Rolle's Theorem in full: Suppose f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b). By the Extreme Value Theorem (which follows from the completeness of ℝ), f attains a maximum value M and a minimum value m on [a,b]. Case 1: M = m. Then f is constant on [a,b], so f'(x) = 0 for all x ∈ (a,b). Any interior point c serves as the guaranteed point. Case 2: M > m. Then M ≠ m, and since f(a) = f(b), it is impossible for both M and m to be attained only at the endpoints a and b (at least one extreme value must occur at an interior point). Suppose (WLOG) that the maximum M is attained at c ∈ (a,b). Then f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x ∈ [a,b]. For h > 0 small enough: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ≤ 0 (since f(c+h) ≤ f(c)). Taking h → 0⁺: f'(c) ≤ 0 (right derivative ≤ 0). For h < 0: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ≥ 0. Taking h → 0⁻: f'(c) ≥ 0 (left derivative ≥ 0). Since f is differentiable at c, left and right derivatives are equal, so 0 ≤ f'(c) ≤ 0, giving f'(c) = 0. ∎ Proof of the MVT from Rolle's Theorem: Define h(x) = f(x) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]·(x-a). Then: h is continuous on [a,b] (linear combination of continuous functions). h is differentiable on (a,b). h(a) = f(a) - f(a) - 0 = 0. h(b) = f(b) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]·(b-a) = f(b) - f(a) - (f(b)-f(a)) = 0. So h satisfies all hypotheses of Rolle's Theorem. Therefore ∃c ∈ (a,b) with h'(c) = 0. Computing: h'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Setting h'(c) = 0: f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). This is the MVT. ∎ Proof that f'=0 everywhere implies f is constant (using MVT directly): Suppose f'(x) = 0 for all x ∈ (a,b) and let x₁, x₂ ∈ (a,b) with x₁ < x₂. By the MVT applied to f on [x₁, x₂]: f(x₂) - f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂ - x₁) for some c ∈ (x₁, x₂). But f'(c) = 0 by hypothesis. Therefore f(x₂) - f(x₁) = 0·(x₂ - x₁) = 0, giving f(x₂) = f(x₁). Since x₁ and x₂ were arbitrary, f is constant on (a,b). ∎ This proof is the archetype for many other MVT applications — find a function satisfying MVT's hypotheses, identify what its derivative is, and conclude about the function's behavior. Cauchy's MVT — proof and application to L'Hôpital: Define h(x) = f(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(x)[f(b)-f(a)]. Then h(a) = f(a)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(a)[f(b)-f(a)] = f(a)g(b) - f(a)g(a) - g(a)f(b) + g(a)f(a) = f(a)g(b) - g(a)f(b). Similarly h(b) = f(b)g(b) - f(b)g(a) - g(b)f(b) + g(a)f(b) = -f(b)g(a) + g(a)f(b)... Actually h(a) = h(b): verify directly: h(b) - h(a) = [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0. So h satisfies Rolle's hypothesis. Therefore ∃c with h'(c) = 0: f'(c)[g(b)-g(a)] - g'(c)[f(b)-f(a)] = 0, rearranging: f'(c)/g'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/[g(b)-g(a)]. For L'Hôpital: with f(a) = g(a) = 0, the ratio [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)] = f(x)/g(x), and Cauchy's MVT gives f(x)/g(x) = f'(c)/g'(c) where c is between a and x. As x → a, c → a, so f(x)/g(x) → f'(a)/g'(a) if the latter limit exists. ∎ Extreme Value Theorem (Used in Rolle's Proof) If f is continuous on the closed interval [a, b], then f attains its maximum and minimum values on [a, b]. This theorem relies on the completeness of ℝ (every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence) and is proved in real analysis. MVT is the Bridge Theorem The MVT connects local information (f' at one point) to global information (f's change over an interval). This is the fundamental structure of much of analysis. Almost every theorem of the form "f' has property P ⟹ f has property Q on an interval" is proved using the MVT.
documents.applications:
The MVT says that if your average speed over an interval was 75 mph, there must have been at least one moment when your instantaneous speed was exactly 75 mph. Some European toll roads already use "average speed cameras": they record your entry and exit times and compute your average speed. If it exceeds the limit, you get a ticket — and the MVT guarantees the ticket is legally valid. Beyond traffic enforcement, the MVT is one of the most-used theorems in mathematical analysis. It underlies the proof that functions with zero derivative everywhere are constant, it is the key step in proving the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, it appears in error bounds for numerical methods and Taylor polynomials, and Cauchy's generalized version is how L'Hôpital's Rule is proved. The MVT is the bridge between local information (the derivative at a point) and global information (how the function behaves over an interval). Rolle's Paradox: A Theorem Against Calculus Michel Rolle (1652–1719) proved what we now call Rolle's Theorem in 1691, intending it as a counterargument against calculus. He believed infinitesimals were self-contradictory. He was eventually persuaded by Varignon and Bernoulli that calculus was valid. The theorem named after him — now a cornerstone of calculus — is a monument to the irony that rigorous opposition can produce rigorous results. Mean Value Theorem If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists c \in (a, b) such that
\[f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}\] MVT Guarantees Existence, Not Uniqueness The MVT guarantees at least one c where f'(c) equals the average slope. There may be many such points — consider f(x) = sin(x) over multiple periods. The theorem only promises existence of at least one c. Do not assume the c is unique unless you have additional information about f. Average Speed Cameras Some toll roads in Europe (notably in the UK, Italy, and the Netherlands) use "average speed enforcement." Your license plate is photographed at entry and exit. If the elapsed time implies an average speed exceeding the limit, you are fined — even if no radar caught you speeding at any single point. The legal basis is the MVT: average speed > limit ⟹ instantaneous speed exceeded the limit at some moment. Average Velocity Equals Instantaneous Velocity Somewhere In physics, if the average velocity of an object over [t₁, t₂] is v̄, then the instantaneous velocity v(t) = v̄ at some t ∈ (t₁, t₂). This is the MVT applied to position functions s(t): v̄ = (s(t₂) - s(t₁))/(t₂ - t₁) = s'(c) = v(c) for some c. Every physics problem involving "at some moment the velocity equaled the average" is an application of MVT.
documents.content:
You're driving from city A to city B, 300 miles apart, and arrive in exactly 4 hours. Your average speed was 75 mph. But the speed limit is 70 mph. Can the police prove you were speeding, even without radar catching you at any specific moment? The Mean Value Theorem gives a definitive YES. The MVT says that if your average speed over an interval was 75 mph, there must have been at least one moment when your instantaneous speed was exactly 75 mph. Some European toll roads already use "average speed cameras": they record your entry and exit times and compute your average speed. If it exceeds the limit, you get a ticket — and the MVT guarantees the ticket is legally valid. Beyond traffic enforcement, the MVT is one of the most-used theorems in mathematical analysis. It underlies the proof that functions with zero derivative everywhere are constant, it is the key step in proving the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, it appears in error bounds for numerical methods and Taylor polynomials, and Cauchy's generalized version is how L'Hôpital's Rule is proved. The MVT is the bridge between local information (the derivative at a point) and global information (how the function behaves over an interval). The geometric picture makes the MVT obvious once you see it. Draw any smooth curve from point A = (a, f(a)) to point B = (b, f(b)). The secant line from A to B has slope m = (f(b) - f(a))/(b - a) — the average rate of change over the interval. Now imagine a horizontal line sliding up from below, parallel to the secant. At some point, this sliding line first touches the curve — at a tangent point. At that tangent point c, the tangent line is parallel to the secant, meaning f'(c) = m. That is the entire content of the Mean Value Theorem: somewhere between a and b, the instantaneous slope equals the average slope. Rolle's Theorem is the special case where f(a) = f(b) — the starting and ending values are equal. In this case, the secant is horizontal (slope 0), and the tangent must also be horizontal somewhere. Geometrically: if you start and end at the same height, the graph must have a horizontal tangent somewhere in between. A ball thrown upward eventually falls — it must stop (instantaneous velocity = 0) at the peak. A pendulum swinging back to its starting position must momentarily stop at the peak. Rolle's Theorem captures this intuition precisely. To prove the MVT from Rolle's Theorem, define a new function g(x) that subtracts the secant line from f: g(x) = f(x) - [f(a) + ((f(b)-f(a))/(b-a))·(x-a)]. This g measures how far f is above the secant line. Note that g(a) = 0 and g(b) = 0 (f equals the secant at both endpoints). So g satisfies Rolle's hypotheses. Rolle's Theorem gives a point c in (a,b) with g'(c) = 0. But g'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a), so g'(c) = 0 means f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). That is the MVT. The trick is simply to subtract the secant line to create a function that satisfies Rolle's hypothesis. The most important consequence of the MVT is the characterization of constant functions. If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then the MVT applied to any two points x₁ < x₂ in (a,b) gives: f(x₂) - f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂ - x₁) = 0·(x₂ - x₁) = 0. So f(x₂) = f(x₁) for all pairs — f is constant. This theorem, which feels obvious, is actually non-trivial to prove without the MVT. It is the first theorem about antiderivatives: if F and G are both antiderivatives of f (F' = G' = f), then (F - G)' = 0, so F - G is constant, so F = G + C for some constant C. This is why every antiderivative has "+ C". The MVT also proves that positive derivative implies increasing function. If f'(x) > 0 for all x in (a,b), then for any x₁ < x₂ in (a,b), the MVT gives f(x₂) - f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂ - x₁) > 0 (since f'(c) > 0 and x₂ - x₁ > 0). So f(x₂) > f(x₁): f is increasing. Similarly, f'(x) < 0 everywhere means f is decreasing. This result, which seems geometrically obvious, is proved rigorously only through the MVT. History: the Mean Value Theorem was proved by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 1797 in his landmark book Théorie des fonctions analytiques, where he attempted to put calculus on a rigorous footing without infinitesimals. Lagrange called it the théorème des accroissements finis (theorem of finite increments). Rolle's Theorem was proved 100 years earlier, in 1691, by Michel Rolle — interestingly, as part of an argument AGAINST calculus. Rolle believed that infinitesimals were logically incoherent, and he published his theorem to demonstrate a paradox in the new calculus. The irony is that the theorem named after him is now used to prove the foundations of the subject he opposed. Rolle's Theorem: Suppose f is continuous on the closed interval [a, b], differentiable on the open interval (a, b), and f(a) = f(b). Then there exists at least one c ∈ (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0. The proof uses the Extreme Value Theorem (EVT): since f is continuous on [a, b], it attains its maximum M and minimum m on [a, b]. If M = m, then f is constant and f' = 0 everywhere — any interior c works. If M ≠ m, then at least one extreme value is attained at some interior point c (it cannot be both maximum and minimum at the endpoints, since f(a) = f(b)). At an interior maximum or minimum c, f'(c) = 0 (by Fermat's theorem: if f has a local extremum at an interior differentiable point, the derivative is zero there). This completes the proof. The MVT follows from Rolle's Theorem by a tilt transformation. Define the helper function h(x) = f(x) - L(x), where L(x) = f(a) + [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]·(x-a) is the secant line through the endpoints. Then h(a) = f(a) - f(a) = 0 and h(b) = f(b) - [f(a) + (f(b)-f(a))] = 0. Also, h is continuous on [a,b] (difference of continuous functions) and differentiable on (a,b). By Rolle's Theorem, there exists c ∈ (a,b) with h'(c) = 0. But h'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a), so h'(c) = 0 gives f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). That is the MVT. The corollaries of the MVT are its main applications. Corollary 1: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in (a,b), then f is constant on (a,b). Proof: for any x₁, x₂ ∈ (a,b) with x₁ < x₂, MVT gives f(x₂) - f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂ - x₁) = 0. Corollary 2: If f'(x) > 0 for all x ∈ (a,b), then f is strictly increasing on (a,b). Corollary 3: If F'(x) = G'(x) for all x ∈ (a,b), then F(x) = G(x) + C for some constant C. This is the uniqueness-up-to-constant of antiderivatives. Corollary 4 (Error bound): |f(b) - f(a)| ≤ (b-a)·max_{[a,b]}|f'|. This bound is used in Taylor polynomial error estimates and numerical analysis. Cauchy's Generalized Mean Value Theorem: If f and g are continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), with g'(x) ≠ 0 on (a,b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) such that f'(c)/g'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(g(b)-g(a)). Proof: apply Rolle's Theorem to h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)). This generalization is the key to proving L'Hôpital's Rule: in the 0/0 case with f(a) = g(a) = 0, the Cauchy MVT gives f(x)/g(x) = [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)] = f'(c)/g'(c) for some c between a and x. As x → a, c → a too (since c is trapped between a and x), giving lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(c)/g'(c) = f'(a)/g'(a) when the derivative ratio is continuous. Proof of Rolle's Theorem in full: Suppose f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b). By the Extreme Value Theorem (which follows from the completeness of ℝ), f attains a maximum value M and a minimum value m on [a,b]. Case 1: M = m. Then f is constant on [a,b], so f'(x) = 0 for all x ∈ (a,b). Any interior point c serves as the guaranteed point. Case 2: M > m. Then M ≠ m, and since f(a) = f(b), it is impossible for both M and m to be attained only at the endpoints a and b (at least one extreme value must occur at an interior point). Suppose (WLOG) that the maximum M is attained at c ∈ (a,b). Then f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x ∈ [a,b]. For h > 0 small enough: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ≤ 0 (since f(c+h) ≤ f(c)). Taking h → 0⁺: f'(c) ≤ 0 (right derivative ≤ 0). For h < 0: [f(c+h) - f(c)]/h ≥ 0. Taking h → 0⁻: f'(c) ≥ 0 (left derivative ≥ 0). Since f is differentiable at c, left and right derivatives are equal, so 0 ≤ f'(c) ≤ 0, giving f'(c) = 0. ∎ Proof of the MVT from Rolle's Theorem: Define h(x) = f(x) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]·(x-a). Then: h is continuous on [a,b] (linear combination of continuous functions). h is differentiable on (a,b). h(a) = f(a) - f(a) - 0 = 0. h(b) = f(b) - f(a) - [(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)]·(b-a) = f(b) - f(a) - (f(b)-f(a)) = 0. So h satisfies all hypotheses of Rolle's Theorem. Therefore ∃c ∈ (a,b) with h'(c) = 0. Computing: h'(x) = f'(x) - (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Setting h'(c) = 0: f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). This is the MVT. ∎ Proof that f'=0 everywhere implies f is constant (using MVT directly): Suppose f'(x) = 0 for all x ∈ (a,b) and let x₁, x₂ ∈ (a,b) with x₁ < x₂. By the MVT applied to f on [x₁, x₂]: f(x₂) - f(x₁) = f'(c)(x₂ - x₁) for some c ∈ (x₁, x₂). But f'(c) = 0 by hypothesis. Therefore f(x₂) - f(x₁) = 0·(x₂ - x₁) = 0, giving f(x₂) = f(x₁). Since x₁ and x₂ were arbitrary, f is constant on (a,b). ∎ This proof is the archetype for many other MVT applications — find a function satisfying MVT's hypotheses, identify what its derivative is, and conclude about the function's behavior. Cauchy's MVT — proof and application to L'Hôpital: Define h(x) = f(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(x)[f(b)-f(a)]. Then h(a) = f(a)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(a)[f(b)-f(a)] = f(a)g(b) - f(a)g(a) - g(a)f(b) + g(a)f(a) = f(a)g(b) - g(a)f(b). Similarly h(b) = f(b)g(b) - f(b)g(a) - g(b)f(b) + g(a)f(b) = -f(b)g(a) + g(a)f(b)... Actually h(a) = h(b): verify directly: h(b) - h(a) = [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0. So h satisfies Rolle's hypothesis. Therefore ∃c with h'(c) = 0: f'(c)[g(b)-g(a)] - g'(c)[f(b)-f(a)] = 0, rearranging: f'(c)/g'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/[g(b)-g(a)]. For L'Hôpital: with f(a) = g(a) = 0, the ratio [f(x)-f(a)]/[g(x)-g(a)] = f(x)/g(x), and Cauchy's MVT gives f(x)/g(x) = f'(c)/g'(c) where c is between a and x. As x → a, c → a, so f(x)/g(x) → f'(a)/g'(a) if the latter limit exists. ∎ Extreme Value Theorem (Used in Rolle's Proof) If f is continuous on the closed interval [a, b], then f attains its maximum and minimum values on [a, b]. This theorem relies on the completeness of ℝ (every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence) and is proved in real analysis. MVT is the Bridge Theorem The MVT connects local information (f' at one point) to global information (f's change over an interval). This is the fundamental structure of much of analysis. Almost every theorem of the form "f' has property P ⟹ f has property Q on an interval" is proved using the MVT. Rolle's Paradox: A Theorem Against Calculus Michel Rolle (1652–1719) proved what we now call Rolle's Theorem in 1691, intending it as a counterargument against calculus. He believed infinitesimals were self-contradictory. He was eventually persuaded by Varignon and Bernoulli that calculus was valid. The theorem named after him — now a cornerstone of calculus — is a monument to the irony that rigorous opposition can produce rigorous results. Mean Value Theorem If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists c \in (a, b) such that
\[f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}\] MVT Guarantees Existence, Not Uniqueness The MVT guarantees at least one c where f'(c) equals the average slope. There may be many such points — consider f(x) = sin(x) over multiple periods. The theorem only promises existence of at least one c. Do not assume the c is unique unless you have additional information about f. Average Speed Cameras Some toll roads in Europe (notably in the UK, Italy, and the Netherlands) use "average speed enforcement." Your license plate is photographed at entry and exit. If the elapsed time implies an average speed exceeding the limit, you are fined — even if no radar caught you speeding at any single point. The legal basis is the MVT: average speed > limit ⟹ instantaneous speed exceeded the limit at some moment. Average Velocity Equals Instantaneous Velocity Somewhere In physics, if the average velocity of an object over [t₁, t₂] is v̄, then the instantaneous velocity v(t) = v̄ at some t ∈ (t₁, t₂). This is the MVT applied to position functions s(t): v̄ = (s(t₂) - s(t₁))/(t₂ - t₁) = s'(c) = v(c) for some c. Every physics problem involving "at some moment the velocity equaled the average" is an application of MVT. Rolle's Theorem If f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b), then
\[\exists\, c \in (a,b): f'(c) = 0\] Key Corollaries of the MVT (1) f'=0 on (a,b) \implies f \text{ is constant.}\\ (2) f'>0 \implies f \text{ strictly increasing.}\\ (3) F'=G' \implies F = G + C.\\ (4) |f(b)-f(a)| \le (b-a)\max|f'|. Cauchy's Generalized MVT If f, g \text{ continuous on } [a,b] \text{ and differentiable on } (a,b) \text{ with } g' \ne 0:\\ \exists\, c \in (a,b): \dfrac{f'(c)}{g'(c)} = \dfrac{f(b)-f(a)}{g(b)-g(a)} The Speeding Ticket Paradox Average speed vs. Instantaneous speed. If the average exceeds the limit, the MVT guarantees you were speeding at some point. The Energy Cost of Speeding Why 10 km/h over the limit is more than 10 km/h of danger. Explore the v² relationship between speed and impact energy. Mean Value Theorem — Interactive Drag the interval endpoints a and b. The green tangent line at c is always parallel to the amber secant. There is always at least one such c. Verify MVT for f(x) = x² on [1, 3] \text{Verify the MVT for } f(x) = x^2 \text{ on } [1, 3] \text{ and find all guaranteed points } c. For f(x) = x² on [1,3], the unique MVT point is c = 2, the midpoint of [1,3]. This is not a coincidence for quadratic functions — for any f(x) = x² on [a,b], the MVT point is always c = (a+b)/2 (the midpoint), because f'(x) = 2x is linear and hits the average value exactly at the midpoint. \frac{f(3)-f(1)}{3-1} = \frac{9-1}{2} = 4 Compute the average rate of change (slope of the secant from (1,1) to (3,9)). f'(x) = 2x Compute the derivative. f'(c) = 4 \Rightarrow 2c = 4 \Rightarrow c = 2 Set f'(c) equal to the average rate and solve. c = 2 ∈ (1, 3) ✓. \text{The tangent at } c=2 \text{ has slope } f'(2) = 4 = \text{slope of secant} Verify: f'(2) = 4 matches the secant slope. The MVT is satisfied with c = 2. Verify MVT for f(x) = x³ on [-1, 1] \text{Find all } c \text{ satisfying the MVT for } f(x) = x^3 \text{ on } [-1, 1]. There are two MVT points for x³ on [-1,1]: c = ±1/√3. The MVT promised at least one; we found two. The function x³ is odd (symmetric about the origin), and the two c values are symmetric as well — another symmetry consequence. \frac{f(1)-f(-1)}{1-(-1)} = \frac{1-(-1)}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 Compute the average rate of change. f'(x) = 3x^2 Derivative of x³. f'(c) = 1 \Rightarrow 3c^2 = 1 \Rightarrow c^2 = \frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow c = \pm\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \approx \pm 0.577 Solve for c. Two solutions: c = ±1/√3. c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \in (-1, 1) \checkmark, \quad c = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \in (-1, 1) \checkmark Both values are in the open interval (-1, 1), confirming the MVT. This illustrates that the MVT can have multiple c values. MVT Proof: |sin(x) - sin(y)| ≤ |x - y| \text{Use the MVT to prove that } |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| \leq |x - y| \text{ for all } x, y \in \mathbb{R}. The sine function is Lipschitz continuous with constant 1 — it cannot change faster than the identity function. This is an MVT corollary (the error bound): |f(b)-f(a)| ≤ |b-a|·max|f'|. Here max|cos| = 1 gives the result. f(t) = \sin(t), \quad f'(t) = \cos(t) Apply the MVT to f(t) = sin(t) on the interval between x and y (WLOG x < y). \sin(x) - \sin(y) = \cos(c)(x - y) \text{ for some } c \in (y, x) By the MVT: f(x) - f(y) = f'(c)(x-y). |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| = |\cos(c)| \cdot |x - y| Take absolute values of both sides. Use |ab| = |a|·|b|. |\cos(c)| \leq 1 \text{ for all } c The cosine function satisfies |cos(c)| ≤ 1 everywhere. \therefore |\sin(x) - \sin(y)| \leq 1 \cdot |x - y| = |x - y| Multiply the inequality by |x-y| ≥ 0. ∎ Speed Trap Application \text{A car travels 200 miles in exactly 3 hours. Prove it exceeded 60 mph at some moment.} At time c (some moment during the 3 hours), the instantaneous speed was exactly 200/3 ≈ 66.7 mph > 60 mph. The car definitely exceeded 60 mph at that moment. The MVT makes this a mathematical certainty, not just a suspicion. s(t) = \text{position (miles), } s(0) = 0, s(3) = 200 Set up: s(t) is the position function. s is continuous (the car moves continuously) and differentiable (it has a velocity) on (0,3). \frac{s(3) - s(0)}{3 - 0} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.7 \text{ mph} Compute the average velocity over the 3-hour trip. \text{By MVT: } \exists\, c \in (0, 3) \text{ such that } s'(c) = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.7 \text{ mph} Apply the MVT: since s satisfies the hypotheses, there exists c with s'(c) = 200/3. s'(c) = \frac{200}{3} > 60 \text{ mph} The instantaneous speed at time c exceeds 60 mph. ∎ Rolle's Theorem: Exactly One Solution in an Interval \text{Prove that } x^4 + x = 2 \text{ has exactly one solution in } [0, 1]. x⁴ + x = 2 has exactly one solution in [0,1], namely x = 1. The MVT/Rolle's approach (via the derivative) proves both existence and uniqueness. Existence: g(0) < 0 < g(1) and IVT (or just g(1) = 0). Uniqueness: g'> 0 so g is strictly increasing, hence at most one zero. g(x) = x^4 + x - 2 Reformulate: find zeros of g(x) = x⁴ + x - 2. g(0) = 0 + 0 - 2 = -2 < 0, \quad g(1) = 1 + 1 - 2 = 0 Evaluate at the endpoints. g(1) = 0 — so x = 1 is a solution! We need to show it is the ONLY one in [0,1]. g'(x) = 4x^3 + 1 > 0 \text{ for all } x \in [0, 1] g'(x) = 4x³ + 1 ≥ 1 > 0 for x ∈ [0,1]. So g is strictly increasing on [0,1]. \text{Strictly increasing} \Rightarrow \text{at most one zero} A strictly increasing function can cross zero at most once — it can only go from negative to positive once. So there is at most one solution. \text{g(0) < 0, g(1) = 0: exactly one zero at } x = 1 Since g is strictly increasing and g(1) = 0, the only zero in [0,1] is x = 1. MVT Inequality: √(1+x) < 1 + x/2 for x > 0 \text{Use the MVT to prove } \sqrt{1+x} < 1 + \frac{x}{2} \text{ for all } x > 0. The MVT can be used to prove inequalities by bounding f'(c) on the relevant interval. The key steps are: (1) write f(b) - f(a) = f'(c)(b-a), then (2) bound f'(c) using the known range of c. This technique appears constantly in analysis. f(t) = \sqrt{t}, \text{ apply MVT on } [1, 1+x] Consider f(t) = √t on [1, 1+x] for x > 0. f(1+x) - f(1) = f'(c) \cdot x \text{ for some } c \in (1, 1+x) MVT: √(1+x) - 1 = f'(c)·x for some c strictly between 1 and 1+x. f'(t) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{t}} Derivative of √t. c > 1 \Rightarrow \sqrt{c} > 1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2\sqrt{c}} < \frac{1}{2} Since c > 1, √c > 1, so 1/(2√c) < 1/2. The c is strictly interior to (1, 1+x), so the strict inequality holds. \sqrt{1+x} - 1 = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{c}} < \frac{x}{2} Multiply the inequality 1/(2√c) < 1/2 by x > 0. \therefore \sqrt{1+x} < 1 + \frac{x}{2} \quad \text{for all } x > 0 \quad \square Add 1 to both sides. Prove that |e^x - e^y| ≥ |x - y| for all x, y ≥ 0. Apply the MVT to f(t) = e^t on [x,y] (or [y,x]). Bound e^c from below. |e^x - e^y| ≥ |x-y| for x,y ≥ 0. The exponential function is "expanding" on [0,∞) — it stretches distances rather than contracting them. This contrasts with sin (which contracts: |sin(x) - sin(y)| ≤ |x-y|). Both proofs use the MVT with bounds on |f'(c)|. f(t) = e^t, \quad f'(t) = e^t Apply MVT to e^t on [y, x] (assume x ≥ y ≥ 0 WLOG). e^x - e^y = e^c(x - y) \text{ for some } c \in (y, x) MVT gives: e^x - e^y = e^c(x-y). c \geq y \geq 0 \Rightarrow e^c \geq e^0 = 1 Since c ≥ y ≥ 0, and e^t is increasing, e^c ≥ e^0 = 1. |e^x - e^y| = e^c|x - y| \geq 1 \cdot |x - y| = |x - y| Take absolute values and apply the bound e^c ≥ 1. ∎ If f'(x) = f(x) for all x and f(0) = 0, prove that f is identically zero. Consider g(x) = f(x)·e^{-x} and apply the MVT (or show g' = 0 everywhere, hence g is constant, hence f = 0). The only function satisfying f'= f and f(0) = 0 is the zero function. This characterizes e^x uniquely: up to scaling, the exponential is the only function equal to its own derivative. With initial condition f(0) = 1 instead of 0, the unique solution is e^x. g(x) = f(x) \cdot e^{-x} Define the helper function g. We will show g is constant. g'(x) = f'(x)e^{-x} + f(x)(-e^{-x}) = [f'(x) - f(x)]e^{-x} Differentiate g using the product rule. f'(x) = f(x) \Rightarrow f'(x) - f(x) = 0 \Rightarrow g'(x) = 0 Since f'(x) = f(x), the bracket is zero, so g'(x) = 0 for all x. g'(x) = 0 \text{ everywhere} \Rightarrow g \text{ is constant (by MVT corollary)} By Corollary 1 of the MVT, g is constant. g(0) = f(0) \cdot e^0 = 0 \cdot 1 = 0 Evaluate g at 0 using f(0) = 0. g(x) = 0 \text{ for all } x \Rightarrow f(x) \cdot e^{-x} = 0 \Rightarrow f(x) = 0 Since g is the constant 0 and e^{-x} ≠ 0, we conclude f(x) = 0. ∎ Prove Cauchy's MVT: if f, g are continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then ∃c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c)(g(b)-g(a)) = g'(c)(f(b)-f(a)). Define h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)) and verify h(a) = h(b), then apply Rolle's Theorem. Cauchy's MVT follows from Rolle's Theorem applied to h(x) = f(x)(g(b)-g(a)) - g(x)(f(b)-f(a)). The classical MVT is the special case g(x) = x. h(x) = f(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(x)[f(b)-f(a)] Define the helper function h. It is a linear combination of f and g, so h is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b). h(a) = f(a)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(a)[f(b)-f(a)] Evaluate h at a. h(b) = f(b)[g(b)-g(a)] - g(b)[f(b)-f(a)] Evaluate h at b. h(b) - h(a) = [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0 Compute h(b) - h(a). It factors as [f(b)-f(a)][g(b)-g(a)] - [g(b)-g(a)][f(b)-f(a)] = 0 (the two terms are equal). So h(a) = h(b). \text{By Rolle's Theorem: } \exists\, c \in (a,b) \text{ with } h'(c) = 0 h satisfies Rolle's hypotheses (continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), h(a) = h(b)). h'(x) = f'(x)[g(b)-g(a)] - g'(x)[f(b)-f(a)] Differentiate h. h'(c) = 0 \Rightarrow f'(c)[g(b)-g(a)] = g'(c)[f(b)-f(a)] \quad \square Set h'(c) = 0 and rearrange. This is Cauchy's MVT. ∎ The Derivative The MVT is a theorem about derivatives. The concepts of differentiability and instantaneous rate of change from Chapter 2 are the foundations. L'Hôpital's Rule L'Hôpital's Rule is proved using Cauchy's Generalized MVT — the most important application of the MVT in this chapter. Curve Sketching The increasing/decreasing test (f' > 0 ⟹ increasing) is proved from the MVT corollaries. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change rigor formal proof show that theorem proof geometric proof visual proof real world engineering physics economics biology computer science

documents.title: Rolle's Theorem
documents.subtitle: If you start and end at the same height, there must be a horizontal tangent somewhere in between
documents.tags: Rolle theorem horizontal tangent critical point existence theorem mean value theorem

documents.assignment: Verifying Rolle's Theorem for a Quadratic \text{Verify Rolle's Theorem for } f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 \text{ on } [1, 3] \text{ and find the value of } c.   Rolle's Theorem is verified: $c = 2$ is the point where $f'(c) = 0$. Geometrically, $f(x) = (x-1)(x-3)$ is a parabola opening upward with roots at 1 and 3, and its vertex (minimum) is at $x = 2$ — the horizontal tangent. f(1) = 1 - 4 + 3 = 0, \quad f(3) = 9 - 12 + 3 = 0 Check that $f(a) = f(b)$. Both endpoints give 0, so condition (iii) is satisfied. f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 \text{ is a polynomial} Polynomials are continuous everywhere and differentiable everywhere. Conditions (i) and (ii) hold on any interval. f'(x) = 2x - 4 Differentiate $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$. 2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2 Solve $f'(c) = 0$. The only solution is $c = 2$. c = 2 \in (1, 3) \; \checkmark Confirm that $c = 2$ lies in the open interval $(1, 3)$. Rolle's Theorem with a Trigonometric Function \text{Show that } f(x) = \sin(x) \text{ on } [0, \pi] \text{ satisfies Rolle's Theorem and find all values of } c.   The unique value is $c = \pi/2$. This is the peak of the sine arch — exactly where the sine function changes from increasing to decreasing, with a horizontal tangent at its crest. f(0) = \sin(0) = 0, \quad f(\pi) = \sin(\pi) = 0 Condition (iii): $f(0) = f(\pi) = 0$. \sin(x) \text{ is continuous on } [0, \pi] \text{ and differentiable on } (0, \pi) Sine is infinitely differentiable everywhere. Conditions (i) and (ii) hold. f'(x) = \cos(x) Differentiate. \cos(c) = 0 \Rightarrow c = \frac{\pi}{2} Solve $f'(c) = 0$ on $(0, \pi)$. The only solution is $c = \pi/2$. f(\pi/2) = \sin(\pi/2) = 1 \text{ (the maximum)} At $c = \pi/2$, the function attains its maximum on $[0, \pi]$. The horizontal tangent occurs at the peak of the sine wave. Why Rolle Fails Without Differentiability \text{Show that } f(x) = |x| \text{ on } [-1, 1] \text{ does NOT satisfy the conclusion of Rolle's Theorem, and identify which hypothesis fails.}   The absolute value function satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but not (ii) — it is not differentiable at $x = 0$. This single point of non-differentiability is enough to break Rolle's Theorem. f(-1) = |-1| = 1, \quad f(1) = |1| = 1 Condition (iii): $f(-1) = f(1) = 1$. This condition holds. f(x) = |x| \text{ is continuous on } [-1, 1] \; \checkmark Condition (i): The absolute value function is continuous everywhere. This holds. f'(0) \text{ does not exist: } \lim_{h \to 0^+} \frac{|h|}{h} = 1, \quad \lim_{h \to 0^-} \frac{|h|}{h} = -1 At $x = 0$, the left and right derivatives differ. The function has a corner at the origin. f'(x) = \begin{cases} -1 & x < 0 \\ +1 & x > 0 \end{cases} For $x \neq 0$, $f'(x)$ is either $-1$ or $+1$. Neither value is zero. \text{No } c \in (-1, 1) \text{ with } f'(c) = 0 There is no point in $(-1, 1)$ where the derivative is zero. Rolle's conclusion fails because hypothesis (ii) fails at $x = 0$. Using Rolle to Prove a Polynomial Has Exactly One Real Root \text{Prove that } p(x) = x^3 + 3x + 1 \text{ has exactly one real root.}   The polynomial $p(x) = x^3 + 3x + 1$ has exactly one real root (in $(-1, 0)$). The IVT gives existence, and Rolle's Theorem (via the contrapositive) gives uniqueness. p(0) = 1 > 0, \quad p(-1) = -1 - 3 + 1 = -3 < 0 By the Intermediate Value Theorem, since $p$ is continuous and changes sign on $[-1, 0]$, there is at least one root in $(-1, 0)$. p'(x) = 3x^2 + 3 = 3(x^2 + 1) Differentiate $p(x)$. 3(x^2 + 1) > 0 \text{ for all real } x Since $x^2 \geq 0$, we have $x^2 + 1 \geq 1 > 0$. So $p'(x) > 0$ everywhere — $p$ is strictly increasing. \text{Suppose } p \text{ had two roots } r_1 < r_2 Assume for contradiction that $p$ has two distinct roots. \text{Rolle's Theorem} \Rightarrow \exists\, c \in (r_1, r_2) \text{ with } p'(c) = 0 Rolle's Theorem would guarantee a point $c$ between the roots where $p'(c) = 0$. \text{But } p'(x) > 0 \text{ for all } x. \text{ Contradiction.} We showed $p'(x) = 3(x^2 + 1) > 0$ everywhere, so no such $c$ can exist. The assumption of two roots is false. Rolle's Theorem with Multiple Interior Zeros \text{Find all values of } c \text{ satisfying Rolle's Theorem for } f(x) = \sin(2\pi x) \text{ on } [0, 1].   There are two values: $c = 1/4$ and $c = 3/4$. Rolle's Theorem guarantees at least one, but this function — a complete period of a sine wave — has two horizontal tangents: a maximum at $x = 1/4$ and a minimum at $x = 3/4$. f(0) = \sin(0) = 0, \quad f(1) = \sin(2\pi) = 0 Condition (iii): $f(0) = f(1) = 0$. f'(x) = 2\pi\cos(2\pi x) Differentiate using the chain rule. 2\pi\cos(2\pi c) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos(2\pi c) = 0 Set $f'(c) = 0$. Since $2\pi \neq 0$, we need $\cos(2\pi c) = 0$. 2\pi c = \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi \Rightarrow c = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{n}{2} Solve for $c$. c \in (0, 1): \quad c = \frac{1}{4} \;(n=0), \quad c = \frac{3}{4} \;(n=1) Keep only values in the open interval $(0, 1)$.  Use Rolle's Theorem to prove that the equation $x^5 + 10x + 3 = 0$ has at most one real root. Compute $f'(x)$ and show it is always positive. Then use Rolle's Theorem by contradiction. \text{Since } f'(x) = 5x^4 + 10 > 0 \text{ for all } x, \text{ Rolle's Theorem precludes two or more roots.}  f(x) = x^5 + 10x + 3 \Rightarrow f'(x) = 5x^4 + 10 Differentiate the polynomial. 5x^4 + 10 \geq 10 > 0 \text{ for all } x Since $x^4 \geq 0$, we have $f'(x) \geq 10 > 0$ everywhere. \text{If } f \text{ had two roots } r_1 < r_2, \text{ Rolle's gives } c \in (r_1, r_2) \text{ with } f'(c) = 0 Apply Rolle's Theorem to the interval $[r_1, r_2]$.  f'(c) \geq 10 > 0 — contradiction No such $c$ exists since $f'$ is always positive.  Verify Rolle's Theorem for $f(x) = x(x-2)e^x$ on $[0, 2]$ and find the value(s) of $c$. Check $f(0) = f(2) = 0$. Use the product rule to find $f'(x)$ and solve $f'(c) = 0$. c = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414  f(0) = 0 \cdot (-2) \cdot 1 = 0, \quad f(2) = 2 \cdot 0 \cdot e^2 = 0 Verify $f(0) = f(2) = 0$. f(x) = (x^2 - 2x)e^x \Rightarrow f'(x) = (2x - 2)e^x + (x^2 - 2x)e^x = (x^2 - 2)e^x Product rule: combine $(2x-2) + (x^2 - 2x) = x^2 - 2$. (c^2 - 2)e^c = 0 \Rightarrow c^2 = 2 \Rightarrow c = \pm\sqrt{2} Since $e^c > 0$ always, we need $c^2 - 2 = 0$. c = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \in (0, 2) \; \checkmark Only $c = \sqrt{2}$ lies in $(0, 2)$.   If $f$ is a polynomial of degree 4 with 4 distinct real roots, how many real roots does $f'$ have at minimum? What about $f''$? Apply Rolle's Theorem between each consecutive pair of roots, then repeat for $f'$. f' \text{ has at least 3 real roots (exactly 3). } f'' \text{ has at least 2 real roots (exactly 2).}  \text{Let } f \text{ have roots } r_1 < r_2 < r_3 < r_4 Label the four distinct real roots in order. \text{Rolle's: } f' \text{ has roots } c_1 \in (r_1,r_2),\; c_2 \in (r_2,r_3),\; c_3 \in (r_3,r_4) Between each consecutive pair of roots, Rolle gives at least one root of $f'$. At least 3 roots. f' \text{ has degree 3, so exactly 3 real roots} A cubic has at most 3 roots. Combined with at least 3, $f'$ has exactly 3 real roots. \text{Apply Rolle's again: } f'' \text{ has at least 2 real roots} Between consecutive roots of $f'$, Rolle gives roots of $f''$. Since $f''$ has degree 2, it has exactly 2 real roots.
documents.proofs:
**Proof of Rolle's Theorem.** Assume $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, differentiable on $(a, b)$, and $f(a) = f(b)$. By the Extreme Value Theorem (which requires continuity on a closed bounded interval), $f$ attains an absolute maximum $M$ at some point $x_M \in [a, b]$ and an absolute minimum $m$ at some point $x_m \in [a, b]$. If $M = m$, then $f$ is constant on $[a, b]$. Every point $c \in (a, b)$ satisfies $f'(c) = 0$, and the theorem holds trivially. If $M 
eq m$, then at least one of $M, m$ is not equal to $f(a) = f(b)$. Without loss of generality, suppose $M 
eq f(a)$. Then the maximum is not attained at $a$, and since $f(a) = f(b)$, the maximum is not attained at $b$ either. So $x_M \in (a, b)$. Since $f$ is differentiable at $x_M$ and $x_M$ is an interior maximum, Fermat's Theorem gives $f'(x_M) = 0$. Set $c = x_M$. **Fermat's Theorem (used above).** If $f$ has a local extremum at an interior point $c$ and $f'(c)$ exists, then $f'(c) = 0$. Proof: Suppose $c$ is a local maximum. For small $h > 0$, $f(c + h) \leq f(c)$, so $ rac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \leq 0$. Taking $h 	o 0^+$ gives $f'(c) \leq 0$. For small $h < 0$, $f(c + h) \leq f(c)$, so $ rac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \geq 0$ (the denominator is negative). Taking $h 	o 0^-$ gives $f'(c) \geq 0$. Together: $f'(c) = 0$. **Why the hypotheses cannot be weakened.** (1) Continuity failure: define $f(x) = x$ for $x \in [0, 1)$ and $f(1) = 0$. Then $f(0) = f(1) = 0$ but $f'(x) = 1 
eq 0$ for all $x \in (0, 1)$. The jump at $x = 1$ breaks the Extreme Value Theorem. (2) Differentiability failure: $f(x) = 1 - |x|$ on $[-1, 1]$ has $f(-1) = f(1) = 0$, is continuous, but has a corner at $x = 0$ (the maximum) where $f'$ does not exist. The derivative is $-1$ for $x > 0$ and $+1$ for $x < 0$, never zero. Fermat's Theorem (Interior Extrema) If $f$ has a local extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f'(c) = 0$. This does NOT say that $f'(c) = 0$ implies a local extremum — that is the converse, which is false. Counterexamples When Hypotheses Fail Continuity dropped: $f(x) = x$ for $x \in [0,1)$ and $f(1) = 0$. Then $f(0) = f(1) = 0$ but $f'(x) = 1 
eq 0$. Differentiability dropped: $f(x) = 1 - |x|$ on $[-1,1]$ has $f(-1) = f(1) = 0$ but the maximum at $x = 0$ is a corner.
documents.applications: Imagine tossing a ball straight up. It leaves your hand going upward and returns to your hand going downward. At some instant in between, the ball's velocity must have been exactly zero — the peak of the trajectory. This is Rolle's Theorem in action: whenever a continuous quantity starts and ends at the same value, its rate of change must hit zero at some intermediate point. This principle underlies root-counting arguments in algebra, stability analysis in engineering, and error bounds in numerical analysis. Rolle's Theorem is also the stepping stone to the Mean Value Theorem, one of the most powerful results in all of calculus. Extreme Value Theorem Connection Rolle's Theorem relies on the Extreme Value Theorem: a continuous function on a closed interval $[a, b]$ attains its maximum and minimum. If the max or min occurs in the interior and the function is differentiable there, Fermat's Theorem says the derivative is zero. All Three Hypotheses Are Necessary Rolle's Theorem requires: (1) $f$ continuous on $[a, b]$, (2) $f$ differentiable on $(a, b)$, (3) $f(a) = f(b)$. Drop any one and the conclusion can fail. A jump discontinuity, a corner, or unequal endpoint values can each prevent the existence of a horizontal tangent. The Mountain Pass Analogy Imagine hiking from a trailhead at elevation $h$ to another trailhead at the same elevation $h$, following a continuous smooth path. If you go uphill at any point, you must come back down — and at the highest point of your hike, the trail is momentarily flat (horizontal tangent). If you go downhill, the lowest point is flat. Either way, a flat point exists. Multiple Horizontal Tangents Rolle's Theorem guarantees at least one $c$ with $f'(c) = 0$, but there may be many. The function $f(x) = \sin(x)$ on $[0, 2\pi]$ has $f(0) = f(2\pi) = 0$ and two points where $f'(c) = \cos(c) = 0$: $c = \pi/2$ and $c = 3\pi/2$. Michel Rolle (1652-1719) Ironically, Michel Rolle was a vocal critic of calculus who considered infinitesimals to be logically unsound. His theorem, published in 1691, was originally stated only for polynomials. The modern version for differentiable functions came later, after the foundations of calculus were made rigorous by Cauchy and Weierstrass.
documents.content:
If you drive from your house to a friend's house and back home again, must there be a moment during the trip when your velocity is exactly zero? Why? Imagine tossing a ball straight up. It leaves your hand going upward and returns to your hand going downward. At some instant in between, the ball's velocity must have been exactly zero — the peak of the trajectory. This is Rolle's Theorem in action: whenever a continuous quantity starts and ends at the same value, its rate of change must hit zero at some intermediate point. This principle underlies root-counting arguments in algebra, stability analysis in engineering, and error bounds in numerical analysis. Rolle's Theorem is also the stepping stone to the Mean Value Theorem, one of the most powerful results in all of calculus. Rolle's Theorem makes a beautifully simple claim: if a function starts and ends at the same height on some interval, and if the function is smooth enough (continuous on the closed interval, differentiable on the open interval), then somewhere in between there must be a point where the tangent line is perfectly horizontal. The derivative is zero at that point. Think about it graphically. You are drawing a curve that starts at the point $(a, f(a))$ and must end at the point $(b, f(b))$ where $f(a) = f(b)$. The curve must be unbroken (continuous) and smooth (differentiable). If the curve goes up at any point, it must eventually come back down to meet the same height at $b$ — and that means there is a peak somewhere with a horizontal tangent. If it goes down first, there must be a valley. If it stays perfectly flat, then $f'(c) = 0$ everywhere. No matter what path the curve takes, a horizontal tangent is unavoidable. The ball-tossing analogy is the most natural example. Let $f(t)$ be the height of a ball at time $t$. You throw it at time $a$ and catch it at time $b$ at the same height: $f(a) = f(b)$. The height function is continuous and differentiable (we ignore air resistance). Therefore, there exists some time $c$ between $a$ and $b$ where $f'(c) = 0$ — the ball is momentarily stationary at its peak. You do not need to solve any equations to know this; Rolle's Theorem guarantees it purely from the boundary conditions. Why do we need the hypotheses? Continuity is essential: if the function can jump, it could leap from $f(a)$ up to some value and then jump back down to $f(b) = f(a)$ without ever having a horizontal tangent. Differentiability is also needed: the function $f(x) = |x|$ on $[-1, 1]$ has $f(-1) = f(1) = 1$ and is continuous, but its only candidate for a horizontal tangent is $x = 0$, where the function has a corner and is not differentiable. The minimum at $x = 0$ exists, but there is no derivative there — so Rolle's Theorem does not apply (and indeed $f'(c) = 0$ has no solution). Rolle's Theorem is actually a special case of the Mean Value Theorem. The MVT says that for any continuous, differentiable function on $[a, b]$, there exists $c$ with $f'(c) =  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$. When $f(a) = f(b)$, the right-hand side becomes zero, and the MVT reduces exactly to Rolle's Theorem. Historically, however, Rolle's Theorem came first, and the MVT is proved using Rolle's Theorem — so the logical order is reversed from the historical order. One of the most elegant applications of Rolle's Theorem is proving bounds on the number of roots a function can have. If a polynomial $p(x)$ of degree $n$ had $n + 1$ roots, then between each consecutive pair of roots Rolle's Theorem would give a root of $p'(x)$. That produces $n$ roots of $p'(x)$, which is a polynomial of degree $n - 1$. But a degree $n - 1$ polynomial can have at most $n - 1$ roots — contradiction. Therefore $p(x)$ has at most $n$ roots. This argument generalizes to any sufficiently smooth function. Rolle's Theorem is an existence theorem: it tells you that a point $c$ with $f'(c) = 0$ exists, but it does not tell you what $c$ is or how to find it. This is a common pattern in analysis — many of the deepest results guarantee existence without providing a formula. To actually locate $c$, you must solve $f'(x) = 0$ by other means (algebra, Newton's method, etc.). **Rolle's Theorem (Formal Statement).** Let $f$ be a function satisfying: (i) $f$ is continuous on the closed interval $[a, b]$, (ii) $f$ is differentiable on the open interval $(a, b)$, and (iii) $f(a) = f(b)$. Then there exists at least one point $c \in (a, b)$ such that $f'(c) = 0$. The proof uses the Extreme Value Theorem. Since $f$ is continuous on the closed interval $[a, b]$, it attains its absolute maximum value $M$ and absolute minimum value $m$ on $[a, b]$. Case 1: if $M = m$, then $f$ is constant on $[a, b]$, so $f'(c) = 0$ for every $c$ in $(a, b)$. Case 2: if $M 
eq m$, then at least one of $M, m$ differs from $f(a) = f(b)$. The extreme value that differs from the endpoint value must be attained at an interior point $c \in (a, b)$ (since both endpoints give the same value). At an interior extremum of a differentiable function, Fermat's Theorem gives $f'(c) = 0$. The relationship between Rolle's Theorem and the Mean Value Theorem is precise. The MVT states: if $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and differentiable on $(a, b)$, then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with $f'(c) =  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$. To prove the MVT from Rolle's Theorem, define the auxiliary function $g(x) = f(x) - \left[f(a) +  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}(x - a)ight]$. This $g$ subtracts the secant line from $f$, so $g(a) = 0$ and $g(b) = 0$. Applying Rolle's Theorem to $g$ gives a point $c$ with $g'(c) = 0$, which yields $f'(c) =  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$. **Root-counting application.** If $p(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $n$, then $p$ has at most $n$ real roots. Proof by contradiction: suppose $p$ has $n + 1$ distinct roots $r_1 < r_2 < \cdots < r_{n+1}$. Between consecutive roots $r_k$ and $r_{k+1}$, we have $p(r_k) = p(r_{k+1}) = 0$, so Rolle's Theorem gives a point $c_k$ with $p'(c_k) = 0$. This produces $n$ distinct roots $c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n$ of $p'(x)$. But $p'(x)$ is a polynomial of degree $n - 1$, which can have at most $n - 1$ roots — a contradiction. **Generalized Rolle's Theorem.** If $f$ vanishes at $n + 1$ distinct points, then $f'$ vanishes at $n$ distinct points (between consecutive roots of $f$), $f''$ vanishes at $n - 1$ points, and by induction $f^{(n)}$ vanishes at least once. This is used to prove error bounds in polynomial interpolation and Taylor's theorem with remainder. **Proof of Rolle's Theorem.** Assume $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, differentiable on $(a, b)$, and $f(a) = f(b)$. By the Extreme Value Theorem (which requires continuity on a closed bounded interval), $f$ attains an absolute maximum $M$ at some point $x_M \in [a, b]$ and an absolute minimum $m$ at some point $x_m \in [a, b]$. If $M = m$, then $f$ is constant on $[a, b]$. Every point $c \in (a, b)$ satisfies $f'(c) = 0$, and the theorem holds trivially. If $M 
eq m$, then at least one of $M, m$ is not equal to $f(a) = f(b)$. Without loss of generality, suppose $M 
eq f(a)$. Then the maximum is not attained at $a$, and since $f(a) = f(b)$, the maximum is not attained at $b$ either. So $x_M \in (a, b)$. Since $f$ is differentiable at $x_M$ and $x_M$ is an interior maximum, Fermat's Theorem gives $f'(x_M) = 0$. Set $c = x_M$. **Fermat's Theorem (used above).** If $f$ has a local extremum at an interior point $c$ and $f'(c)$ exists, then $f'(c) = 0$. Proof: Suppose $c$ is a local maximum. For small $h > 0$, $f(c + h) \leq f(c)$, so $ rac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \leq 0$. Taking $h 	o 0^+$ gives $f'(c) \leq 0$. For small $h < 0$, $f(c + h) \leq f(c)$, so $ rac{f(c+h) - f(c)}{h} \geq 0$ (the denominator is negative). Taking $h 	o 0^-$ gives $f'(c) \geq 0$. Together: $f'(c) = 0$. **Why the hypotheses cannot be weakened.** (1) Continuity failure: define $f(x) = x$ for $x \in [0, 1)$ and $f(1) = 0$. Then $f(0) = f(1) = 0$ but $f'(x) = 1 
eq 0$ for all $x \in (0, 1)$. The jump at $x = 1$ breaks the Extreme Value Theorem. (2) Differentiability failure: $f(x) = 1 - |x|$ on $[-1, 1]$ has $f(-1) = f(1) = 0$, is continuous, but has a corner at $x = 0$ (the maximum) where $f'$ does not exist. The derivative is $-1$ for $x > 0$ and $+1$ for $x < 0$, never zero. Fermat's Theorem (Interior Extrema) If $f$ has a local extremum at $c$ and $f$ is differentiable at $c$, then $f'(c) = 0$. This does NOT say that $f'(c) = 0$ implies a local extremum — that is the converse, which is false. Counterexamples When Hypotheses Fail Continuity dropped: $f(x) = x$ for $x \in [0,1)$ and $f(1) = 0$. Then $f(0) = f(1) = 0$ but $f'(x) = 1 
eq 0$. Differentiability dropped: $f(x) = 1 - |x|$ on $[-1,1]$ has $f(-1) = f(1) = 0$ but the maximum at $x = 0$ is a corner. Extreme Value Theorem Connection Rolle's Theorem relies on the Extreme Value Theorem: a continuous function on a closed interval $[a, b]$ attains its maximum and minimum. If the max or min occurs in the interior and the function is differentiable there, Fermat's Theorem says the derivative is zero. All Three Hypotheses Are Necessary Rolle's Theorem requires: (1) $f$ continuous on $[a, b]$, (2) $f$ differentiable on $(a, b)$, (3) $f(a) = f(b)$. Drop any one and the conclusion can fail. A jump discontinuity, a corner, or unequal endpoint values can each prevent the existence of a horizontal tangent. The Mountain Pass Analogy Imagine hiking from a trailhead at elevation $h$ to another trailhead at the same elevation $h$, following a continuous smooth path. If you go uphill at any point, you must come back down — and at the highest point of your hike, the trail is momentarily flat (horizontal tangent). If you go downhill, the lowest point is flat. Either way, a flat point exists. Multiple Horizontal Tangents Rolle's Theorem guarantees at least one $c$ with $f'(c) = 0$, but there may be many. The function $f(x) = \sin(x)$ on $[0, 2\pi]$ has $f(0) = f(2\pi) = 0$ and two points where $f'(c) = \cos(c) = 0$: $c = \pi/2$ and $c = 3\pi/2$. Michel Rolle (1652-1719) Ironically, Michel Rolle was a vocal critic of calculus who considered infinitesimals to be logically unsound. His theorem, published in 1691, was originally stated only for polynomials. The modern version for differentiable functions came later, after the foundations of calculus were made rigorous by Cauchy and Weierstrass. Rolle's Theorem If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$, differentiable on $(a, b)$, and $f(a) = f(b)$, then there exists $c \in (a, b)$ with $f'(c) = 0$. From Rolle to MVT Define $g(x) = f(x) - f(a) -  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}(x - a)$. Then $g(a) = g(b) = 0$, so by Rolle's Theorem there exists $c$ with $g'(c) = 0$, giving $f'(c) =  rac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}$. Rolle Does Not Find the Point Rolle's Theorem is purely existential. It guarantees $c$ exists but gives no formula for it. To locate $c$, solve $f'(x) = 0$ explicitly. Rolle's Theorem: when $f(a) = f(b)$, the MVT guarantees a horizontal tangent between $a$ and $b$. When $f(a) = f(b)$, the secant line is horizontal, and Rolle's Theorem guarantees a parallel (horizontal) tangent. Verifying Rolle's Theorem for a Quadratic 	ext{Verify Rolle's Theorem for } f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 	ext{ on } [1, 3] 	ext{ and find the value of } c. Rolle's Theorem is verified: $c = 2$ is the point where $f'(c) = 0$. Geometrically, $f(x) = (x-1)(x-3)$ is a parabola opening upward with roots at 1 and 3, and its vertex (minimum) is at $x = 2$ — the horizontal tangent. f(1) = 1 - 4 + 3 = 0, \quad f(3) = 9 - 12 + 3 = 0 Check that $f(a) = f(b)$. Both endpoints give 0, so condition (iii) is satisfied. f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 	ext{ is a polynomial} Polynomials are continuous everywhere and differentiable everywhere. Conditions (i) and (ii) hold on any interval. f'(x) = 2x - 4 Differentiate $f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$. 2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 2 Solve $f'(c) = 0$. The only solution is $c = 2$. c = 2 \in (1, 3) \; \checkmark Confirm that $c = 2$ lies in the open interval $(1, 3)$. Rolle's Theorem with a Trigonometric Function 	ext{Show that } f(x) = \sin(x) 	ext{ on } [0, \pi] 	ext{ satisfies Rolle's Theorem and find all values of } c. The unique value is $c = \pi/2$. This is the peak of the sine arch — exactly where the sine function changes from increasing to decreasing, with a horizontal tangent at its crest. f(0) = \sin(0) = 0, \quad f(\pi) = \sin(\pi) = 0 Condition (iii): $f(0) = f(\pi) = 0$. \sin(x) 	ext{ is continuous on } [0, \pi] 	ext{ and differentiable on } (0, \pi) Sine is infinitely differentiable everywhere. Conditions (i) and (ii) hold. f'(x) = \cos(x) Differentiate. \cos(c) = 0 \Rightarrow c =  rac{\pi}{2} Solve $f'(c) = 0$ on $(0, \pi)$. The only solution is $c = \pi/2$. f(\pi/2) = \sin(\pi/2) = 1 	ext{ (the maximum)} At $c = \pi/2$, the function attains its maximum on $[0, \pi]$. The horizontal tangent occurs at the peak of the sine wave. Why Rolle Fails Without Differentiability 	ext{Show that } f(x) = |x| 	ext{ on } [-1, 1] 	ext{ does NOT satisfy the conclusion of Rolle's Theorem, and identify which hypothesis fails.} The absolute value function satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) but not (ii) — it is not differentiable at $x = 0$. This single point of non-differentiability is enough to break Rolle's Theorem. f(-1) = |-1| = 1, \quad f(1) = |1| = 1 Condition (iii): $f(-1) = f(1) = 1$. This condition holds. f(x) = |x| 	ext{ is continuous on } [-1, 1] \; \checkmark Condition (i): The absolute value function is continuous everywhere. This holds. f'(0) 	ext{ does not exist: } \lim_{h 	o 0^+}  rac{|h|}{h} = 1, \quad \lim_{h 	o 0^-}  rac{|h|}{h} = -1 At $x = 0$, the left and right derivatives differ. The function has a corner at the origin. f'(x) =  egin{cases} -1 & x < 0 \ +1 & x > 0 \end{cases} For $x 
eq 0$, $f'(x)$ is either $-1$ or $+1$. Neither value is zero. 	ext{No } c \in (-1, 1) 	ext{ with } f'(c) = 0 There is no point in $(-1, 1)$ where the derivative is zero. Rolle's conclusion fails because hypothesis (ii) fails at $x = 0$. Using Rolle to Prove a Polynomial Has Exactly One Real Root 	ext{Prove that } p(x) = x^3 + 3x + 1 	ext{ has exactly one real root.} The polynomial $p(x) = x^3 + 3x + 1$ has exactly one real root (in $(-1, 0)$). The IVT gives existence, and Rolle's Theorem (via the contrapositive) gives uniqueness. p(0) = 1 > 0, \quad p(-1) = -1 - 3 + 1 = -3 < 0 By the Intermediate Value Theorem, since $p$ is continuous and changes sign on $[-1, 0]$, there is at least one root in $(-1, 0)$. p'(x) = 3x^2 + 3 = 3(x^2 + 1) Differentiate $p(x)$. 3(x^2 + 1) > 0 	ext{ for all real } x Since $x^2 \geq 0$, we have $x^2 + 1 \geq 1 > 0$. So $p'(x) > 0$ everywhere — $p$ is strictly increasing. 	ext{Suppose } p 	ext{ had two roots } r_1 < r_2 Assume for contradiction that $p$ has two distinct roots. 	ext{Rolle's Theorem} \Rightarrow \exists\, c \in (r_1, r_2) 	ext{ with } p'(c) = 0 Rolle's Theorem would guarantee a point $c$ between the roots where $p'(c) = 0$. 	ext{But } p'(x) > 0 	ext{ for all } x. 	ext{ Contradiction.} We showed $p'(x) = 3(x^2 + 1) > 0$ everywhere, so no such $c$ can exist. The assumption of two roots is false. Rolle's Theorem with Multiple Interior Zeros 	ext{Find all values of } c 	ext{ satisfying Rolle's Theorem for } f(x) = \sin(2\pi x) 	ext{ on } [0, 1]. There are two values: $c = 1/4$ and $c = 3/4$. Rolle's Theorem guarantees at least one, but this function — a complete period of a sine wave — has two horizontal tangents: a maximum at $x = 1/4$ and a minimum at $x = 3/4$. f(0) = \sin(0) = 0, \quad f(1) = \sin(2\pi) = 0 Condition (iii): $f(0) = f(1) = 0$. f'(x) = 2\pi\cos(2\pi x) Differentiate using the chain rule. 2\pi\cos(2\pi c) = 0 \Rightarrow \cos(2\pi c) = 0 Set $f'(c) = 0$. Since $2\pi 
eq 0$, we need $\cos(2\pi c) = 0$. 2\pi c =  rac{\pi}{2} + n\pi \Rightarrow c =  rac{1}{4} +  rac{n}{2} Solve for $c$. c \in (0, 1): \quad c =  rac{1}{4} \;(n=0), \quad c =  rac{3}{4} \;(n=1) Keep only values in the open interval $(0, 1)$. Use Rolle's Theorem to prove that the equation $x^5 + 10x + 3 = 0$ has at most one real root. Compute $f'(x)$ and show it is always positive. Then use Rolle's Theorem by contradiction. 	ext{Since } f'(x) = 5x^4 + 10 > 0 	ext{ for all } x, 	ext{ Rolle's Theorem precludes two or more roots.} f(x) = x^5 + 10x + 3 \Rightarrow f'(x) = 5x^4 + 10 Differentiate the polynomial. 5x^4 + 10 \geq 10 > 0 	ext{ for all } x Since $x^4 \geq 0$, we have $f'(x) \geq 10 > 0$ everywhere. 	ext{If } f 	ext{ had two roots } r_1 < r_2, 	ext{ Rolle's gives } c \in (r_1, r_2) 	ext{ with } f'(c) = 0 Apply Rolle's Theorem to the interval $[r_1, r_2]$.  f'(c) \geq 10 > 0 — contradiction No such $c$ exists since $f'$ is always positive. Verify Rolle's Theorem for $f(x) = x(x-2)e^x$ on $[0, 2]$ and find the value(s) of $c$. Check $f(0) = f(2) = 0$. Use the product rule to find $f'(x)$ and solve $f'(c) = 0$. c = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 f(0) = 0 \cdot (-2) \cdot 1 = 0, \quad f(2) = 2 \cdot 0 \cdot e^2 = 0 Verify $f(0) = f(2) = 0$. f(x) = (x^2 - 2x)e^x \Rightarrow f'(x) = (2x - 2)e^x + (x^2 - 2x)e^x = (x^2 - 2)e^x Product rule: combine $(2x-2) + (x^2 - 2x) = x^2 - 2$. (c^2 - 2)e^c = 0 \Rightarrow c^2 = 2 \Rightarrow c = \pm\sqrt{2} Since $e^c > 0$ always, we need $c^2 - 2 = 0$. c = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \in (0, 2) \; \checkmark Only $c = \sqrt{2}$ lies in $(0, 2)$.  If $f$ is a polynomial of degree 4 with 4 distinct real roots, how many real roots does $f'$ have at minimum? What about $f''$? Apply Rolle's Theorem between each consecutive pair of roots, then repeat for $f'$. f' 	ext{ has at least 3 real roots (exactly 3). } f'' 	ext{ has at least 2 real roots (exactly 2).} 	ext{Let } f 	ext{ have roots } r_1 < r_2 < r_3 < r_4 Label the four distinct real roots in order. 	ext{Rolle's: } f' 	ext{ has roots } c_1 \in (r_1,r_2),\; c_2 \in (r_2,r_3),\; c_3 \in (r_3,r_4) Between each consecutive pair of roots, Rolle gives at least one root of $f'$. At least 3 roots. f' 	ext{ has degree 3, so exactly 3 real roots} A cubic has at most 3 roots. Combined with at least 3, $f'$ has exactly 3 real roots. 	ext{Apply Rolle's again: } f'' 	ext{ has at least 2 real roots} Between consecutive roots of $f'$, Rolle gives roots of $f''$. Since $f''$ has degree 2, it has exactly 2 real roots. Mean Value Theorem The MVT generalizes Rolle's Theorem by dropping the requirement $f(a) = f(b)$. Rolle's Theorem is used to prove the MVT. Curve Sketching Rolle's Theorem explains why between consecutive zeros of $f$, the derivative $f'$ must vanish — a fact used when analyzing the shape of graphs. Optimization Rolle's Theorem guarantees critical points exist under the right conditions, which is foundational for optimization problems. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change real world engineering physics economics biology computer science

documents.title: Curve Sketching
documents.subtitle: From formula to complete graph: critical points, inflection points, concavity, and asymptotes — all from derivatives alone
documents.tags: curve sketching critical points local extrema concavity inflection points first derivative test second derivative test asymptotes sign chart
