Since x²+3x+7 = (x+3/2)²+19/4 > 0 always, the absolute value is not needed here. Substitution with Algebra — ∫ x√(x+1) dx \text{Evaluate } \displaystyle\int x\sqrt{x+1}\,dx. Answer: (2/5)(x+1)^(5/2) − (2/3)(x+1)^(3/2) + C. When x appears separately from the composite, algebraically solve for x in terms of u to replace it. u = x+1 \implies x = u-1, \quad du = dx Let u = x+1 (the radicand). Crucially, solve for x: x = u−1. This lets us replace the remaining factor x. \int x\sqrt{x+1}\,dx = \int (u-1)\sqrt{u}\,du x = u−1, √(x+1) = √u, dx = du. All substituted. = \int (u-1)u^{1/2}\,du = \int (u^{3/2} - u^{1/2})\,du Distribute: (u−1)u^(1/2) = u^(3/2) − u^(1/2). = \frac{u^{5/2}}{5/2} - \frac{u^{3/2}}{3/2} + C = \frac{2}{5}u^{5/2} - \frac{2}{3}u^{3/2} + C Power rule: ∫uⁿ du = uⁿ⁺¹/(n+1). Here n=3/2 and n=1/2. = \frac{2}{5}(x+1)^{5/2} - \frac{2}{3}(x+1)^{3/2} + C Back-substitute u = x+1. Integral with Exponential — Area Under a Bell Curve Piece \text{Evaluate } \displaystyle\int_0^1 2x e^{-x^2}\,dx. \text{ (Part of the error function — core to probability and statistics.)} Answer: 1 − 1/e ≈ 0.6321. Note: the integrand 2xe^(−x²) is the Rayleigh distribution's PDF — it gives the probability that a Rayleigh-distributed distance (arising in 2D random walks and wireless signal strength) falls in [0,1]. The related error function erf(x) = (2/√π)∫₀ˣ e^(−t²) dt generalizes the idea to Gaussian integrals; both require substitution as their first step. u = -x^2, \quad du = -2x\,dx \implies 2x\,dx = -du Let u = −x². Its derivative −2x is almost the factor 2x present (off by a sign). \text{Limits: } x=0 \Rightarrow u=0; \quad x=1 \Rightarrow u=-1 Convert limits using u = −x². \int_0^1 2x e^{-x^2}\,dx = \int_0^{-1} e^u \cdot (-du) = \int_{-1}^0 e^u\,du Substitute: e^(−x²) = eᵘ, 2x dx = −du. Flip limits (negating the integral) to get ∫_{−1}^{0}. = [e^u]_{-1}^0 = e^0 - e^{-1} = 1 - \frac{1}{e} Evaluate. e⁰ = 1, e⁻¹ = 1/e. Evaluate ∫ cos(x)·sin²(x) dx. Let u = sin(x). What is du? \dfrac{\sin^3 x}{3} + C u = \sin x, \quad du = \cos x\,dx The derivative of sin is cos, which is already present. \int \cos x \cdot \sin^2 x\,dx = \int u^2\,du = \frac{u^3}{3} + C Substitute. cos(x) dx = du, sin²(x) = u². = \frac{\sin^3 x}{3} + C Back-substitute. Evaluate \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/4} \tan x\,dx. (Recall \tan x = \sin x / \cos x.) Write tan x = sin(x)/cos(x). Let u = cos(x). \dfrac{\ln 2}{2} \approx 0.347 \int_0^{\pi/4} \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\,dx Rewrite tan x. u = \cos x, \quad du = -\sin x\,dx \implies \sin x\,dx = -du The numerator sin x is (−1) times the derivative of cos x. \text{Limits: } x=0 \Rightarrow u=1; \quad x=\pi/4 \Rightarrow u=1/\sqrt{2} cos(0)=1, cos(π/4)=1/√2. \int_0^{\pi/4}\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}\,dx = \int_1^{1/\sqrt{2}}\frac{-du}{u} = -[\ln|u|]_1^{1/\sqrt{2}} Substitute. = -(\ln(1/\sqrt{2}) - \ln 1) = -(-\tfrac{1}{2}\ln 2 - 0) = \frac{\ln 2}{2} ln(1/√2) = ln(2^(−1/2)) = −(1/2)ln 2. And ln(1) = 0. Evaluate \displaystyle\int \frac{e^x}{e^{2x}+1}\,dx. Let u = eˣ. What does the integrand become? You'll get ∫ 1/(u²+1) du. \arctan(e^x) + C u = e^x, \quad du = e^x\,dx Let u = eˣ. Then eˣ dx = du. \int \frac{e^x}{e^{2x}+1}\,dx = \int \frac{du}{u^2+1} Substitute: eˣ = u, e²ˣ = u², eˣ dx = du. = \arctan(u) + C = \arctan(e^x) + C Standard integral: ∫ 1/(u²+1) du = arctan(u) + C. Chain Rule (Prerequisite) U-substitution reverses the chain rule. If you can differentiate with the chain rule, you can integrate by substitution. Antiderivative Formulas The basic antiderivative table (∫xⁿ dx, ∫sin dx, etc.) provides f(u) once you make the substitution. Next: Integration by Parts When substitution fails (e.g., ∫x·sin(x) dx), integration by parts is the next technique to try. FTC Part 2 For definite integrals, the fundamental theorem allows evaluation once the antiderivative is found via substitution. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change

documents.title: Volumes of Revolution
documents.subtitle: Rotating curves around axes generates 3D solids — Disk, Washer, and Shell methods
documents.tags: volume disk method washer method shell method solid of revolution Pappus rotation definite integral 3D
documents.aliases: antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change
documents.assignment: Disk Method: Volume of a Cone \text{Find the volume of a cone of radius } R \text{ and height } h \text{ using the disk method. (Rotate } y = Rx/h \text{ around the x-axis on [0,h].)}   V = (1/3)πR²h — the classic cone volume formula, now derived rigorously from calculus. The 1/3 comes from the ∫x² factor. Compare with a cylinder (no 1/3): the cone has 1/3 the volume of the cylinder with same base and height. f(x) = \frac{R}{h}x \text{ (cross-sections are disks of radius } r = Rx/h\text{)} The slant side of the cone is a straight line from (0,0) to (h, R). V = \pi\int_0^h \left[\frac{R}{h}x\right]^2 dx = \pi\cdot\frac{R^2}{h^2}\int_0^h x^2\,dx Disk method: V = π∫r² dx. Factor out the constant. = \frac{\pi R^2}{h^2}\cdot\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^h = \frac{\pi R^2}{h^2}\cdot\frac{h^3}{3} = \frac{\pi R^2 h}{3} Antiderivative of x² is x³/3. Evaluate at limits. Washer Method: Volume Between Two Curves \text{Find the volume generated by rotating the region between } y = \sqrt{x} \text{ and } y = x \text{ on [0,1] around the x-axis.}   V = π/6. Interesting: this is the same as the AREA between y=x and y=x² (from earlier) times π — but that is a coincidence of the specific functions here, not a general rule. \text{On } [0,1]: \sqrt{x} \geq x \geq 0 \text{ (check at } x=1/4: 1/2 > 1/4\text{)} Outer radius R = √x, inner radius r = x. V = \pi\int_0^1 \left[(\sqrt{x})^2 - x^2\right]dx = \pi\int_0^1 (x - x^2)\,dx Washer formula: π∫(R² − r²) dx = π∫(x − x²) dx. = \pi\left[\frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6} Evaluate: 1/2 − 1/3 = 1/6. Shell Method: Rotation Around the y-Axis \text{Find the volume generated by rotating the region under } y = x^2 \text{ on [0,2] around the y-axis.}   V = 8π. The shell method was simpler here because expressing x as a function of y (inverting y = x² to get x = √y) would have required disk integration from y = 0 to y = 4 — still doable, but with a square root in the integrand. V = 2\pi\int_0^2 x \cdot f(x)\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^2 x \cdot x^2\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^2 x^3\,dx Shell method for y-axis rotation: V = 2π∫x·f(x)dx. Shell radius = x, height = f(x) = x². = 2\pi\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2 = 2\pi\cdot\frac{16}{4} = 2\pi\cdot 4 = 8\pi Antiderivative of x³ is x⁴/4. Evaluate at x=2: 16/4 = 4. Volume of a Sphere (Disk Method) \text{Derive the volume of a sphere of radius } R \text{ by rotating the semicircle } y = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2} \text{ around the x-axis.}   V = (4/3)πR³ — the sphere volume formula from calculus. Archimedes proved this without calculus using exhaustion, but note how naturally integration handles it: once you square the semicircle equation, the √ disappears and you integrate a simple polynomial! V = \pi\int_{-R}^R \left(\sqrt{R^2-x^2}\right)^2 dx = \pi\int_{-R}^R (R^2-x^2)\,dx Disk method. Squaring eliminates the square root! = \pi\left[R^2 x - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-R}^R Antiderivative: ∫(R²−x²)dx = R²x − x³/3. = \pi\left[\left(R^3 - \frac{R^3}{3}\right) - \left(-R^3 + \frac{R^3}{3}\right)\right] Evaluate at x = R and x = −R. = \pi\left[\frac{2R^3}{3} + \frac{2R^3}{3}\right] = \frac{4\pi R^3}{3} Combine: 2(2R³/3) = 4R³/3. Rotation Around a Non-Axis Line \text{Find the volume generated by rotating } y = x^2 \text{ from } x=0 \text{ to } x=2 \text{ around the line } y = 4.   V = 256π/15. When the axis of rotation is y = k, the disk radius is |f(x) − k|. Always draw the problem first — identify which side of the axis the curve lives on to get the correct sign. \text{Disk radius: } r(x) = 4 - x^2 We are rotating around y=4. At each x, the radius = distance from the axis y=4 down to the curve y=x². Since x²≤4 on [0,2], this is always positive: r = 4−x². \text{This is a disk (not washer) — the axis } y=4 \text{ is the outer boundary itself, so no hole.} The region sweeps solid disks of radius (4−x²) at each x. There is no inner hole because we are rotating the region between y=x² and the axis y=4. V = \pi\int_0^2 (4-x^2)^2\,dx = \pi\int_0^2(16-8x^2+x^4)\,dx Disk method with radius = 4−x². Expand the square. = \pi\left[16x - \frac{8x^3}{3} + \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2 Integrate term by term. = \pi\left[32 - \frac{64}{3} + \frac{32}{5}\right] = \pi\cdot\frac{480 - 320 + 96}{15} = \frac{256\pi}{15} Common denominator 15: 32 = 480/15, 64/3 = 320/15, 32/5 = 96/15.  Use the disk method to find the volume generated by rotating y = 2x on [0, 3] around the x-axis. Verify using the cone formula V = (1/3)πR²h. Disk radius = 2x. R = f(3) = 6, h = 3. V = 36\pi  V = \pi\int_0^3(2x)^2\,dx = 4\pi\int_0^3 x^2\,dx Apply disk method. = 4\pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^3 = 4\pi\cdot 9 = 36\pi Evaluate. \text{Verify: }\frac{1}{3}\pi(6)^2(3) = \frac{1}{3}\pi\cdot 36\cdot 3 = 36\pi\;\checkmark Cone formula with R=6, h=3.  Use BOTH the disk/washer method AND the shell method to find the volume of the solid generated by rotating the region bounded by y = x and y = x² around the y-axis. Show the two give the same answer. Disk (around y-axis): integrate in y, slicing perpendicular to y-axis. Shell (around y-axis): integrate in x. V = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \text{ by both methods}  \text{Shell method: } V = 2\pi\int_0^1 x(x-x^2)\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^1(x^2-x^3)\,dx Shell radius = x, height = x − x² (top curve minus bottom). = 2\pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^1 = 2\pi\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}\right) = 2\pi\cdot\frac{1}{12} = \frac{\pi}{6} Evaluate. \text{Disk/Washer: rotate around y-axis. Slice at height } y: x_{\text{outer}}=\sqrt{y}, x_{\text{inner}}=y For y-axis disk, solve for x in terms of y. y=x² → x=√y; y=x → x=y. V = \pi\int_0^1(y - y^2)\,dy = \pi\left[\frac{y^2}{2}-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}\;\checkmark Outer radius √y, inner radius y. R²−r² = y−y². Same answer!  Use Pappus's centroid theorem to find the volume of the torus (donut) generated by rotating a disk of radius r with center at distance R from the rotation axis (R > r). Then verify with the washer method. The centroid of the disk is at distance R from the axis. Area of disk = πr². Pappus: V = 2π·R·(πr²). V = 2\pi^2 R r^2  V = 2\pi \bar{r} A = 2\pi R \cdot \pi r^2 = 2\pi^2 R r^2 Pappus's theorem directly. \text{Washer verify: disk has outer radius } R+r, \text{ inner radius } R-r, \text{ for each y-slice} More precisely, at height y above the disk center, the outer radius = R + √(r²−y²), inner = R − √(r²−y²). V = \pi\int_{-r}^{r}\left[(R+\sqrt{r^2-y^2})^2 - (R-\sqrt{r^2-y^2})^2\right]dy Washer method integrating in y. = \pi\int_{-r}^{r} 4R\sqrt{r^2-y^2}\,dy = 4\pi R \cdot \frac{\pi r^2}{2} = 2\pi^2 Rr^2\;\checkmark Since (A+B)²−(A−B)² = 4AB, and ∫₋ᵣʳ√(r²−y²)dy = πr²/2 (semicircle area).
documents.proofs:
The disk method formula V = π∫ₐᵇ [f(x)]² dx is rigorously justified by Cavalieri's principle and the definition of the definite integral. The volume of a solid with known cross-sectional area A(x) is V = ∫ₐᵇ A(x) dx (Cross-Section Formula). For a solid of revolution around the x-axis, the cross-section at x is a disk of radius f(x) and area A(x) = π[f(x)]². The disk method is therefore a special case of the cross-section formula. The shell method requires a different argument. Consider a thin cylindrical shell at radius x with height f(x) and thickness Δx. Its volume is (outer volume) − (inner volume) = π(x+Δx)²f(x) − πx²f(x) = π[(x+Δx)² − x²]f(x) = π[2xΔx + (Δx)²]f(x) ≈ 2πx·f(x)·Δx for small Δx. Summing: ΣᵢΔxᵢ 2πxᵢ·f(xᵢ) → ∫ 2πx·f(x) dx as Δx → 0. The equivalence of the disk and shell methods for the same solid follows from the fact that both compute the same volume — they are just different decompositions of the same 3D region. A rigorous proof uses Fubini's theorem (change of the order of integration in 2D), but the intuitive justification is sufficient for Calc 1. Cross-Section Formula (Cavalieri's Principle) If a solid has cross-sectional area $A(x)$ at position $x$, then its volume is
\[V = \int_a^b A(x)\,dx.\]
The disk method is the special case $A(x) = \pi[f(x)]^2$.
documents.applications:
Volumes of revolution are fundamental to manufacturing and engineering. Every lathe-turned object — wheels, axles, bolts, pistons, wine glasses, vases, turbine blades — is a solid of revolution. Pharmaceutical capsules are ellipsoids of revolution; their fill volumes require integration. The fuel tank on a rocket nose cone is a paraboloid (parabola rotated around its axis); its volume is π∫r²dx. In medicine, CT scans reconstruct 3D organ volumes by rotating 2D cross-sections — the mathematical structure is the disk method. Pappus's theorem (a 4th-century Greek result) computes the volume of any solid of revolution using the centroid of the generating region: V = 2πr̄A — no calculus, just clever geometry. Disk-Washer vs. Shell — Quick Decision Disk/Washer: slice ⊥ to rotation axis. Volume = π∫R² dx (or π∫(R²−r²) dx for washer).
Shell: slice ∥ to rotation axis. Volume = 2π∫r·h dr.
Choose whichever gives a simpler integral. Often: disk for x-axis rotation with y=f(x); shell for y-axis rotation with y=f(x) (avoids inverting). The Disk is Just a Very Thin Cylinder V = π r² h → for a thin slice of thickness dx, this becomes π[f(x)]² dx. Integrating sums up infinitely many disks. The same reasoning that gives ∫F(x)dx as work (summing tiny force × distance pieces) applies here: summing tiny volume pieces gives total volume. Pappus's Centroid Theorem The volume of a solid of revolution = 2π × (centroid distance to axis) × (area of the region). Classic application — the torus: rotate a disk of radius r whose center is at distance R from the axis (R > r). The centroid of the disk is at distance R, and the area is πr². So V = 2π·R·(πr²) = 2π²Rr². This gives the exact torus volume formula without setting up any integral — a stunning shortcut from 4th-century geometry! π is Outside the Integral V = π∫[f(x)]² dx, NOT ∫π[f(x)]² dx ... actually these are equal! Since π is a constant, it can move in and out of the integral. Both forms are correct. The first form (π outside) is traditional. Just don't forget π — it is the area of the circular cross-section and is always present in disk/washer methods.
documents.content:
How do you compute the volume of a vase, a bullet, or a donut? If the object has rotational symmetry — like virtually any machined part — you can generate it by rotating a 2D curve around an axis. Its volume is then a definite integral. Volumes of revolution are fundamental to manufacturing and engineering. Every lathe-turned object — wheels, axles, bolts, pistons, wine glasses, vases, turbine blades — is a solid of revolution. Pharmaceutical capsules are ellipsoids of revolution; their fill volumes require integration. The fuel tank on a rocket nose cone is a paraboloid (parabola rotated around its axis); its volume is π∫r²dx. In medicine, CT scans reconstruct 3D organ volumes by rotating 2D cross-sections — the mathematical structure is the disk method. Pappus's theorem (a 4th-century Greek result) computes the volume of any solid of revolution using the centroid of the generating region: V = 2πr̄A — no calculus, just clever geometry. Imagine spinning the region under y = f(x) on [a, b] around the x-axis. At each x-position, the spinning creates a circular cross-section (a disk) of radius r = f(x) and area πr² = π[f(x)]². The volume is the limit of the sum of thin disk volumes: ∑ π[f(x)]² Δx → ∫ₐᵇ π[f(x)]² dx. This is the **Disk Method**. When the region is between two curves f(x) ≥ g(x) ≥ 0 and you rotate around the x-axis, each cross-section is a **washer** with outer radius R = f(x) and inner radius r = g(x). Area of washer = π(R² − r²). Volume = ∫ₐᵇ π[f(x)² − g(x)²] dx. This is the **Washer Method** — disk method with a hole. The **Shell Method** takes a different slice. Instead of slicing perpendicular to the axis of rotation (giving disks), we slice parallel to the axis (giving cylindrical shells). The volume of a cylindrical shell of radius r, height h, and thickness dr is approximately 2πr·h·dr. When rotating around the y-axis: V = ∫ₐᵇ 2πx·f(x) dx. The shell method is often simpler when the natural integration variable is perpendicular to the rotation axis (e.g., rotating around the y-axis but expressing the curve as y = f(x)). Choosing disk vs. shell: if the axis of rotation is the x-axis, disk/washer slices in the x-direction and integrates in x (natural for y = f(x)). Shell slices in the x-direction and also integrates in x, but applies when rotating around the y-axis. A good rule: if you would need to invert the function x = f(y) to use disk method, try shells first. Rotation around other lines (y = k or x = h): shift the problem. If rotating y = f(x) around y = 2, the disk radius is |f(x) − 2| instead of f(x). If rotating around x = -1, the shell radius is (x + 1) instead of x. Always draw the problem to identify the correct radii. Disk Method (rotation around x-axis): if f(x) ≥ 0 on [a, b], then V = π∫ₐᵇ [f(x)]² dx. Washer Method (rotation around x-axis, f ≥ g ≥ 0): V = π∫ₐᵇ {[f(x)]² − [g(x)]²} dx. Outer radius R = f(x), inner radius r = g(x). Shell Method (rotation around y-axis): V = 2π∫ₐᵇ x·f(x) dx, where [a,b] is on the x-axis and f(x) ≥ 0. Rotation around y = k: disk radius = |f(x) − k|, washer: outer and inner radii both adjusted by subtracting k. Rotation around x = h (shell method): shell radius = |x − h| instead of x. Converting between methods: any disk/washer integral can be converted to a shell integral and vice versa — they give the same answer. The choice is purely about which integral is easier to evaluate. The disk method formula V = π∫ₐᵇ [f(x)]² dx is rigorously justified by Cavalieri's principle and the definition of the definite integral. The volume of a solid with known cross-sectional area A(x) is V = ∫ₐᵇ A(x) dx (Cross-Section Formula). For a solid of revolution around the x-axis, the cross-section at x is a disk of radius f(x) and area A(x) = π[f(x)]². The disk method is therefore a special case of the cross-section formula. The shell method requires a different argument. Consider a thin cylindrical shell at radius x with height f(x) and thickness Δx. Its volume is (outer volume) − (inner volume) = π(x+Δx)²f(x) − πx²f(x) = π[(x+Δx)² − x²]f(x) = π[2xΔx + (Δx)²]f(x) ≈ 2πx·f(x)·Δx for small Δx. Summing: ΣᵢΔxᵢ 2πxᵢ·f(xᵢ) → ∫ 2πx·f(x) dx as Δx → 0. The equivalence of the disk and shell methods for the same solid follows from the fact that both compute the same volume — they are just different decompositions of the same 3D region. A rigorous proof uses Fubini's theorem (change of the order of integration in 2D), but the intuitive justification is sufficient for Calc 1. Cross-Section Formula (Cavalieri's Principle) If a solid has cross-sectional area $A(x)$ at position $x$, then its volume is
\[V = \int_a^b A(x)\,dx.\]
The disk method is the special case $A(x) = \pi[f(x)]^2$. Disk-Washer vs. Shell — Quick Decision Disk/Washer: slice ⊥ to rotation axis. Volume = π∫R² dx (or π∫(R²−r²) dx for washer).
Shell: slice ∥ to rotation axis. Volume = 2π∫r·h dr.
Choose whichever gives a simpler integral. Often: disk for x-axis rotation with y=f(x); shell for y-axis rotation with y=f(x) (avoids inverting). The Disk is Just a Very Thin Cylinder V = π r² h → for a thin slice of thickness dx, this becomes π[f(x)]² dx. Integrating sums up infinitely many disks. The same reasoning that gives ∫F(x)dx as work (summing tiny force × distance pieces) applies here: summing tiny volume pieces gives total volume. Pappus's Centroid Theorem The volume of a solid of revolution = 2π × (centroid distance to axis) × (area of the region). Classic application — the torus: rotate a disk of radius r whose center is at distance R from the axis (R > r). The centroid of the disk is at distance R, and the area is πr². So V = 2π·R·(πr²) = 2π²Rr². This gives the exact torus volume formula without setting up any integral — a stunning shortcut from 4th-century geometry! π is Outside the Integral V = π∫[f(x)]² dx, NOT ∫π[f(x)]² dx ... actually these are equal! Since π is a constant, it can move in and out of the integral. Both forms are correct. The first form (π outside) is traditional. Just don't forget π — it is the area of the circular cross-section and is always present in disk/washer methods. Volume Formulas Summary \text{Disk (x-axis): } V = \pi\int_a^b [f(x)]^2\,dx\\
\text{Washer (x-axis): } V = \pi\int_a^b \left([f(x)]^2-[g(x)]^2\right)dx\\
\text{Shell (y-axis): } V = 2\pi\int_a^b x\,f(x)\,dx Pappus's Centroid Theorem If a plane region $\mathcal{R}$ with area $A$ is revolved around an external axis, the volume of the solid of revolution is:
\[V = 2\pi \bar{r}\, A\]
where $\bar{r}$ is the distance from the centroid of $\mathcal{R}$ to the axis of revolution. 3D Solid of Revolution — Interactive Rotate the plane region around the axis and see the 3D solid form. Toggle between disk, washer, and shell representations. Drag n to see how more slices approximate the volume better. Biology: Integrating Laminar Blood Flow The Washer Method describes more than physical geometry—it describes total accumulation of concentric rings. In medicine, Poiseuilles Law integrates the velocity of blood layers across an artery to find total flow Q. Interactive Volume Calculator Choose disk or shell method and see exact volume calculations update in real time as you adjust the function and bounds. Disk Method: Volume of a Cone \text{Find the volume of a cone of radius } R \text{ and height } h \text{ using the disk method. (Rotate } y = Rx/h \text{ around the x-axis on [0,h].)} V = (1/3)πR²h — the classic cone volume formula, now derived rigorously from calculus. The 1/3 comes from the ∫x² factor. Compare with a cylinder (no 1/3): the cone has 1/3 the volume of the cylinder with same base and height. f(x) = \frac{R}{h}x \text{ (cross-sections are disks of radius } r = Rx/h\text{)} The slant side of the cone is a straight line from (0,0) to (h, R). V = \pi\int_0^h \left[\frac{R}{h}x\right]^2 dx = \pi\cdot\frac{R^2}{h^2}\int_0^h x^2\,dx Disk method: V = π∫r² dx. Factor out the constant. = \frac{\pi R^2}{h^2}\cdot\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^h = \frac{\pi R^2}{h^2}\cdot\frac{h^3}{3} = \frac{\pi R^2 h}{3} Antiderivative of x² is x³/3. Evaluate at limits. Washer Method: Volume Between Two Curves \text{Find the volume generated by rotating the region between } y = \sqrt{x} \text{ and } y = x \text{ on [0,1] around the x-axis.} V = π/6. Interesting: this is the same as the AREA between y=x and y=x² (from earlier) times π — but that is a coincidence of the specific functions here, not a general rule. \text{On } [0,1]: \sqrt{x} \geq x \geq 0 \text{ (check at } x=1/4: 1/2 > 1/4\text{)} Outer radius R = √x, inner radius r = x. V = \pi\int_0^1 \left[(\sqrt{x})^2 - x^2\right]dx = \pi\int_0^1 (x - x^2)\,dx Washer formula: π∫(R² − r²) dx = π∫(x − x²) dx. = \pi\left[\frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6} Evaluate: 1/2 − 1/3 = 1/6. Shell Method: Rotation Around the y-Axis \text{Find the volume generated by rotating the region under } y = x^2 \text{ on [0,2] around the y-axis.} V = 8π. The shell method was simpler here because expressing x as a function of y (inverting y = x² to get x = √y) would have required disk integration from y = 0 to y = 4 — still doable, but with a square root in the integrand. V = 2\pi\int_0^2 x \cdot f(x)\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^2 x \cdot x^2\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^2 x^3\,dx Shell method for y-axis rotation: V = 2π∫x·f(x)dx. Shell radius = x, height = f(x) = x². = 2\pi\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2 = 2\pi\cdot\frac{16}{4} = 2\pi\cdot 4 = 8\pi Antiderivative of x³ is x⁴/4. Evaluate at x=2: 16/4 = 4. Volume of a Sphere (Disk Method) \text{Derive the volume of a sphere of radius } R \text{ by rotating the semicircle } y = \sqrt{R^2 - x^2} \text{ around the x-axis.} V = (4/3)πR³ — the sphere volume formula from calculus. Archimedes proved this without calculus using exhaustion, but note how naturally integration handles it: once you square the semicircle equation, the √ disappears and you integrate a simple polynomial! V = \pi\int_{-R}^R \left(\sqrt{R^2-x^2}\right)^2 dx = \pi\int_{-R}^R (R^2-x^2)\,dx Disk method. Squaring eliminates the square root! = \pi\left[R^2 x - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-R}^R Antiderivative: ∫(R²−x²)dx = R²x − x³/3. = \pi\left[\left(R^3 - \frac{R^3}{3}\right) - \left(-R^3 + \frac{R^3}{3}\right)\right] Evaluate at x = R and x = −R. = \pi\left[\frac{2R^3}{3} + \frac{2R^3}{3}\right] = \frac{4\pi R^3}{3} Combine: 2(2R³/3) = 4R³/3. Rotation Around a Non-Axis Line \text{Find the volume generated by rotating } y = x^2 \text{ from } x=0 \text{ to } x=2 \text{ around the line } y = 4. V = 256π/15. When the axis of rotation is y = k, the disk radius is |f(x) − k|. Always draw the problem first — identify which side of the axis the curve lives on to get the correct sign. \text{Disk radius: } r(x) = 4 - x^2 We are rotating around y=4. At each x, the radius = distance from the axis y=4 down to the curve y=x². Since x²≤4 on [0,2], this is always positive: r = 4−x². \text{This is a disk (not washer) — the axis } y=4 \text{ is the outer boundary itself, so no hole.} The region sweeps solid disks of radius (4−x²) at each x. There is no inner hole because we are rotating the region between y=x² and the axis y=4. V = \pi\int_0^2 (4-x^2)^2\,dx = \pi\int_0^2(16-8x^2+x^4)\,dx Disk method with radius = 4−x². Expand the square. = \pi\left[16x - \frac{8x^3}{3} + \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2 Integrate term by term. = \pi\left[32 - \frac{64}{3} + \frac{32}{5}\right] = \pi\cdot\frac{480 - 320 + 96}{15} = \frac{256\pi}{15} Common denominator 15: 32 = 480/15, 64/3 = 320/15, 32/5 = 96/15. Use the disk method to find the volume generated by rotating y = 2x on [0, 3] around the x-axis. Verify using the cone formula V = (1/3)πR²h. Disk radius = 2x. R = f(3) = 6, h = 3. V = 36\pi V = \pi\int_0^3(2x)^2\,dx = 4\pi\int_0^3 x^2\,dx Apply disk method. = 4\pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^3 = 4\pi\cdot 9 = 36\pi Evaluate. \text{Verify: }\frac{1}{3}\pi(6)^2(3) = \frac{1}{3}\pi\cdot 36\cdot 3 = 36\pi\;\checkmark Cone formula with R=6, h=3. Use BOTH the disk/washer method AND the shell method to find the volume of the solid generated by rotating the region bounded by y = x and y = x² around the y-axis. Show the two give the same answer. Disk (around y-axis): integrate in y, slicing perpendicular to y-axis. Shell (around y-axis): integrate in x. V = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \text{ by both methods} \text{Shell method: } V = 2\pi\int_0^1 x(x-x^2)\,dx = 2\pi\int_0^1(x^2-x^3)\,dx Shell radius = x, height = x − x² (top curve minus bottom). = 2\pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^1 = 2\pi\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}\right) = 2\pi\cdot\frac{1}{12} = \frac{\pi}{6} Evaluate. \text{Disk/Washer: rotate around y-axis. Slice at height } y: x_{\text{outer}}=\sqrt{y}, x_{\text{inner}}=y For y-axis disk, solve for x in terms of y. y=x² → x=√y; y=x → x=y. V = \pi\int_0^1(y - y^2)\,dy = \pi\left[\frac{y^2}{2}-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^1 = \pi\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}\;\checkmark Outer radius √y, inner radius y. R²−r² = y−y². Same answer! Use Pappus's centroid theorem to find the volume of the torus (donut) generated by rotating a disk of radius r with center at distance R from the rotation axis (R > r). Then verify with the washer method. The centroid of the disk is at distance R from the axis. Area of disk = πr². Pappus: V = 2π·R·(πr²). V = 2\pi^2 R r^2 V = 2\pi \bar{r} A = 2\pi R \cdot \pi r^2 = 2\pi^2 R r^2 Pappus's theorem directly. \text{Washer verify: disk has outer radius } R+r, \text{ inner radius } R-r, \text{ for each y-slice} More precisely, at height y above the disk center, the outer radius = R + √(r²−y²), inner = R − √(r²−y²). V = \pi\int_{-r}^{r}\left[(R+\sqrt{r^2-y^2})^2 - (R-\sqrt{r^2-y^2})^2\right]dy Washer method integrating in y. = \pi\int_{-r}^{r} 4R\sqrt{r^2-y^2}\,dy = 4\pi R \cdot \frac{\pi r^2}{2} = 2\pi^2 Rr^2\;\checkmark Since (A+B)²−(A−B)² = 4AB, and ∫₋ᵣʳ√(r²−y²)dy = πr²/2 (semicircle area). Applications of Integration Area between curves, average value, and work — the foundational applications that lead into 3D volumes here. U-Substitution Some volume integrals require substitution to evaluate — trig sub for spheres and ellipsoids, power sub for compound forms. The Definite Integral All volume formulas are definite integrals. The cross-section formula V = ∫A(x)dx is the definite integral applied to 3D. Polar Coordinates Volumes of surfaces like spheres and cones often arise in polar/spherical coordinates in multivariable calculus. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change

documents.title: Integration by Parts
documents.subtitle: The product rule in reverse — trading one integral for an easier one
documents.tags: integration by parts LIATE tabular method product rule reduction formula uv formula
documents.aliases: antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change
documents.assignment: Classic: ∫x·eˣ dx \text{Find } \int x e^x\,dx.   ∫xeˣ dx = eˣ(x−1) + C. One round of by-parts reduces the polynomial factor from degree 1 to degree 0. \text{Let } u = x, \quad dv = e^x\,dx LIATE: x is algebraic (A), eˣ is exponential (E). A has higher priority, so u = x. du = dx, \quad v = e^x Differentiate u, integrate dv. \int xe^x\,dx = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx Apply ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du. = xe^x - e^x + C ∫eˣ dx = eˣ. = e^x(x-1) + C Factor out eˣ for a clean final form. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}[e^x(x-1)] = e^x(x-1) + e^x = e^x \cdot x \checkmark Product rule: eˣ(x−1) + eˣ·1 = xeˣ. ✓ Repeated Parts: ∫x²·sin(x) dx \text{Find } \int x^2\sin x\,dx.   ∫x²sin x dx = −x²cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x + C. Two rounds of by-parts reduce x² to a constant. \text{Let } u = x^2, \; dv = \sin x\,dx \Rightarrow du = 2x\,dx, \; v = -\cos x First round: u = x² (algebraic), dv = sin x dx. \int x^2\sin x\,dx = -x^2\cos x + \int 2x\cos x\,dx Apply by-parts. The new integral still has a polynomial × trig — apply again. \text{Second round: } u = 2x, \; dv = \cos x\,dx \Rightarrow du = 2\,dx, \; v = \sin x Reduce the polynomial degree by one more. \int 2x\cos x\,dx = 2x\sin x - \int 2\sin x\,dx = 2x\sin x + 2\cos x + C ∫2 sin x dx = −2 cos x. \int x^2\sin x\,dx = -x^2\cos x + 2x\sin x + 2\cos x + C Combine all terms. \text{Verify: differentiate } -x^2\cos x + 2x\sin x + 2\cos x Product rule on each term: 2x cos x + x² sin x + 2 sin x + 2x cos x − 2 sin x = x² sin x. ✓ Lone Function: ∫ln(x) dx \text{Find } \int \ln x\,dx.   ∫ln x dx = x ln x − x + C. The "trick" is writing ∫ln x dx as ∫1·ln x dx and choosing u = ln x. \text{Let } u = \ln x, \quad dv = dx Pair ln x with dv = dx. LIATE: logarithmic has highest priority. du = \frac{1}{x}\,dx, \quad v = x Differentiate u, integrate dv. \int \ln x\,dx = x\ln x - \int x \cdot \frac{1}{x}\,dx Apply the formula: uv − ∫v du. = x\ln x - \int 1\,dx = x\ln x - x + C The integral simplifies to ∫1 dx = x. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}[x\ln x - x] = \ln x + 1 - 1 = \ln x \checkmark Product rule on x ln x gives ln x + 1. Subtract 1. ✓ Cycling: ∫eˣ·sin(x) dx \text{Find } \int e^x\sin x\,dx.   ∫eˣ sin x dx = eˣ(sin x − cos x)/2 + C. The cycling trick: apply by-parts twice, then solve algebraically for the original integral. \text{Let } I = \int e^x\sin x\,dx. \text{ Round 1: } u = \sin x,\; dv = e^x\,dx Either choice of u works for cycling. We pick u = sin x. I = e^x\sin x - \int e^x\cos x\,dx du = cos x dx, v = eˣ. Apply formula. \text{Round 2: } u = \cos x,\; dv = e^x\,dx \Rightarrow du = -\sin x\,dx,\; v = e^x Apply by-parts to ∫eˣ cos x dx. \int e^x\cos x\,dx = e^x\cos x - \int e^x(-\sin x)\,dx = e^x\cos x + I The original integral I reappears! I = e^x\sin x - (e^x\cos x + I) = e^x\sin x - e^x\cos x - I Substitute back into the Round 1 result. 2I = e^x(\sin x - \cos x) \Rightarrow I = \frac{e^x(\sin x - \cos x)}{2} + C Add I to both sides and divide by 2. Inverse Trig: ∫arctan(x) dx \text{Find } \int \arctan x\,dx.   ∫arctan x dx = x arctan x − (1/2)ln(1+x²) + C. By-parts + u-sub working together — a common combination. \text{Let } u = \arctan x, \quad dv = dx LIATE: inverse trig (I) has high priority. Pair with dv = dx. du = \frac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx, \quad v = x Differentiate and integrate. \int \arctan x\,dx = x\arctan x - \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx Apply the formula. \text{For } \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx: \text{ let } w = 1+x^2,\; dw = 2x\,dx The remaining integral is a u-sub problem. \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\ln|1+x^2| + C = \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^2) + C 1+x² > 0 always, so absolute value is unnecessary. \int \arctan x\,dx = x\arctan x - \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^2) + C Combine the results.  Find ∫x·cos x dx and ∫x·e^(−x) dx. For both: u = x (algebraic), dv = the other factor. One round of by-parts each. \int x\cos x\,dx = x\sin x + \cos x + C; \quad \int xe^{-x}dx = -e^{-x}(x+1)+C  \int x\cos x\,dx: u=x, dv=\cos x\,dx. \; = x\sin x - \int \sin x\,dx = x\sin x + \cos x + C du = dx, v = sin x. ∫sin x dx = −cos x. \int xe^{-x}dx: u=x, dv=e^{-x}dx. \; = -xe^{-x} - \int -e^{-x}dx = -xe^{-x} - e^{-x} + C = -e^{-x}(x+1)+C du = dx, v = −e^(−x). ∫−e^(−x) dx = e^(−x)... wait: ∫e^(−x) dx = −e^(−x). So −∫−e^(−x) dx = −e^(−x).  Use the tabular method to find ∫x³·eˣ dx. Derivatives of x³: x³, 3x², 6x, 6, 0. Antiderivatives of eˣ: eˣ, eˣ, eˣ, eˣ, eˣ. Alternate signs +, −, +, −. e^x(x^3-3x^2+6x-6)+C  \text{Derivatives: } x^3,\; 3x^2,\; 6x,\; 6,\; 0 Differentiate x³ until you reach 0. \text{Antiderivatives: } e^x,\; e^x,\; e^x,\; e^x Integrate eˣ repeatedly (it stays eˣ). \text{Signs: } +,\; -,\; +,\; - Alternate starting with +. = (+)(x^3)(e^x) + (-)(3x^2)(e^x) + (+)(6x)(e^x) + (-)(6)(e^x) + C Multiply each derivative by the antiderivative one row below, with alternating sign. = e^x(x^3 - 3x^2 + 6x - 6) + C Factor out eˣ.  Derive the reduction formula: ∫sin^n(x) dx = −(1/n)sin^(n−1)(x)cos(x) + (n−1)/n · ∫sin^(n−2)(x) dx. Then use it to compute ∫sin⁴(x) dx. Write sin^n x = sin^(n−1) x · sin x. Let u = sin^(n−1) x, dv = sin x dx. Use cos²x = 1 − sin²x to handle the resulting integral. \int \sin^4 x\,dx = \frac{3x}{8} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C  u = \sin^{n-1}x,\; dv = \sin x\,dx \Rightarrow du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}x\cos x\,dx,\; v = -\cos x Set up by-parts. \int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\cos^2 x\,dx Apply formula. Note the cos²x factor. = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x(1-\sin^2 x)\,dx Replace cos²x = 1 − sin²x. = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx - (n-1)\int \sin^n x\,dx Distribute. The last term contains the original integral! n\int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx Move the sin^n integral to the left: 1 + (n−1) = n. \int \sin^4 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{4}\sin^3 x\cos x + \frac{3}{4}\int \sin^2 x\,dx Apply with n=4. \int \sin^2 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{2}\sin x\cos x + \frac{1}{2}x + C = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Apply with n=2 (or use half-angle identity). \int \sin^4 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{4}\sin^3 x\cos x + \frac{3}{8}x - \frac{3}{16}\sin 2x + C Substitute back.
documents.proofs:
Derivation of integration by parts from the product rule. The product rule states: $d/dx[u(x)v(x)] = u'(x)v(x) + u(x)v'(x)$. Rearrange: $u(x)v'(x) = d/dx[u(x)v(x)] - u'(x)v(x)$. Integrate both sides over $[a,b]$: $\int_a^b u(x)v'(x)\,dx = [u(x)v(x)]_a^b - \int_a^b u'(x)v(x)\,dx$. This is exact — no approximation. The formula requires $u$ and $v$ to be differentiable, with $u'v$ and $uv'$ integrable. For continuously differentiable functions (the usual case), all conditions are satisfied. The indefinite version follows by omitting the evaluation limits: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du + C$. The constant is conventionally absorbed into the final answer. Note: when computing $v = \int dv$, we do NOT include a $+C$ at this intermediate step — any choice of antiderivative for $v$ works, and the simplest (with $C = 0$) is preferred. Validity of the cycling method: when $\int u\,dv$ is computed by two rounds of by-parts and yields $I = A - I$ (where $A$ collects boundary terms), we solve algebraically: $2I = A$, so $I = A/2$. This is valid because $I$ is a NUMBER (for definite integrals) or a FUNCTION (for indefinite integrals, defined up to $+C$). The algebraic manipulation is justified by the linearity of integration. Reduction formulas and mathematical induction. The reduction $\int \sin^n x\,dx = -(1/n)\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)/n \int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx$ is proved by one round of by-parts with $u = \sin^{n-1}x$ and $dv = \sin x\,dx$. The identity $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$ is used to eliminate $\cos^2 x$ from the result. By induction, this gives the Wallis-type closed form for $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx$. Derivation from the Product Rule \((uv)' = u'v + uv'\) implies \(uv' = (uv)' - u'v\). Integrating:
\[\int uv' = uv - \int u'v.\]
This is exact, not an approximation. It holds whenever \(u, v\) are \(C^1\) (continuously differentiable).
documents.applications: Integration by parts appears throughout engineering and physics. Fourier coefficients — the foundation of signal processing, audio compression (MP3), and image analysis (JPEG) — are computed by integrating products of functions with sines and cosines, requiring repeated by-parts. In quantum mechanics, expectation values involve integrals like ∫ψ*(x)·x·ψ(x)dx, solved by parts. Control engineers use Laplace transforms that rely on by-parts internally. Statisticians computing moments of probability distributions (mean, variance, skewness) integrate x^n times density functions — by parts is the standard method. When to Use Integration by Parts Use by-parts when the integrand is a product of two different "types" of functions (e.g., polynomial × exponential, polynomial × trig, logarithm × anything) and u-substitution does not apply (no inner derivative present). The LIATE rule tells you which factor to differentiate. Choosing u and dv Badly Bad choices make the integral harder. In ∫x·eˣ dx, if you choose u = eˣ and dv = x dx, then du = eˣ dx and v = x²/2, giving ∫x·eˣ dx = (x²/2)eˣ − ∫(x²/2)eˣ dx — the new integral is MORE complex (x² instead of x). LIATE prevents this: x (algebraic) has higher priority than eˣ (exponential), so u = x. By-Parts Is Not a Last Resort Students often try u-sub first and reach for by-parts only when stuck. But many integrals (∫x cos x dx, ∫ln x dx, ∫arctan x dx) are immediately recognizable as by-parts problems. Train yourself to spot products of different function types — that is the signal for by-parts. The Product Rule Is the Foundation By-parts IS the product rule, rearranged and integrated. If you remember (uv)' = u'v + uv', then ∫uv' = uv − ∫u'v follows immediately. Every by-parts problem is just the product rule run backwards, with a strategic choice of which factor to call u.
documents.content:
How do you integrate x·eˣ? There is no substitution that works — the x factor is not the derivative of the exponent, and the exponent is not the derivative of x. Yet d/dx[x·eˣ] = eˣ + x·eˣ by the product rule, so x·eˣ = d/dx[x·eˣ] − eˣ. Integrating both sides: ∫x·eˣ dx = x·eˣ − eˣ + C. Integration by parts formalizes this trick for any product. Integration by parts appears throughout engineering and physics. Fourier coefficients — the foundation of signal processing, audio compression (MP3), and image analysis (JPEG) — are computed by integrating products of functions with sines and cosines, requiring repeated by-parts. In quantum mechanics, expectation values involve integrals like ∫ψ*(x)·x·ψ(x)dx, solved by parts. Control engineers use Laplace transforms that rely on by-parts internally. Statisticians computing moments of probability distributions (mean, variance, skewness) integrate x^n times density functions — by parts is the standard method. The product rule says $(uv)' = u'v + uv'$. Rearranging: $uv' = (uv)' - u'v$. Integrate both sides: $\int u\,v'\,dx = uv - \int u'\,v\,dx$. That is the integration by parts formula. It trades the integral $\int u\,dv$ for a (hopefully simpler) integral $\int v\,du$. The art is choosing $u$ and $dv$ so that the new integral is easier than the original. Think of it as a strategic trade. You start with a product of two functions that you cannot integrate directly. You "differentiate one factor and integrate the other." If differentiating the first factor makes it simpler while integrating the second factor does not make it worse, you win: the new integral is easier. The classic example is $\int x \cdot e^x\,dx$: differentiating $x$ gives 1 (simpler!), and integrating $e^x$ gives $e^x$ (no worse). So the new integral $\int 1 \cdot e^x\,dx = e^x$ is trivial. The LIATE rule gives a priority order for choosing $u$: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic (polynomials), Trigonometric, Exponential. Choose $u$ as the function highest on the list; the remaining factor becomes $dv$. Why? Functions higher on the list simplify when differentiated (ln x → 1/x, arctan x → 1/(1+x²), x² → 2x → 2 → 0), while functions lower on the list do not get worse when integrated (e^x → e^x, sin x → −cos x). Sometimes you need to apply by-parts more than once. For $\int x^2 \sin x\,dx$, one round reduces $x^2$ to $2x$; a second round reduces $2x$ to $2$; then the remaining integral is elementary. Each round peels off one power of $x$. The tabular method (also called the "tic-tac-toe" method) organizes repeated by-parts: list successive derivatives of $u$ in one column and successive antiderivatives of $dv$ in the other, alternating signs (+, −, +, −, …). Multiply diagonally and add. A beautiful special case is the "cycling" trick. For $\int e^x \sin x\,dx$, applying by-parts twice brings you back to an integral of the same form: $\int e^x \sin x\,dx = -e^x \cos x + e^x \sin x - \int e^x \sin x\,dx$. The original integral appears on both sides! Move it to the left: $2\int e^x \sin x\,dx = e^x(\sin x - \cos x)$. Divide by 2. This is algebra, not magic — it works whenever the same integral recurs after two rounds of by-parts. Integration by parts also handles functions that seem to have no partner to multiply. For $\int \ln x\,dx$, write it as $\int 1 \cdot \ln x\,dx$ with $u = \ln x$ and $dv = dx$. Then $du = dx/x$ and $v = x$, giving $x \ln x - \int x \cdot (1/x)\,dx = x \ln x - x + C$. The same trick works for $\int \arctan x\,dx$ and $\int \arcsin x\,dx$ — pair the inverse function with $dv = dx$. Reduction formulas use by-parts to express $\int f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $\int f_{n-2}(x)\,dx$ or $\int f_{n-1}(x)\,dx$. For example, $\int \sin^n x\,dx$ can be reduced by writing $\sin^n x = \sin^{n-1}x \cdot \sin x$, integrating by parts, and using $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$. The result is a formula linking the $n$-th integral to the $(n-2)$-th. Applied repeatedly, this reduces any $\int \sin^n x\,dx$ to either $\int \sin x\,dx$ or $\int dx$. The integration by parts formula: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$. In function notation: if $u = u(x)$ and $v = v(x)$, then $\int u(x)v'(x)\,dx = u(x)v(x) - \int u'(x)v(x)\,dx$. For definite integrals: $\int_a^b u\,dv = [uv]_a^b - \int_a^b v\,du$. LIATE priority for choosing $u$: (L)ogarithmic: $\ln x$, $\log x$; (I)nverse trig: $\arctan x$, $\arcsin x$; (A)lgebraic: $x^n$, polynomials; (T)rigonometric: $\sin x$, $\cos x$; (E)xponential: $e^x$, $a^x$. Choose $u$ from the highest available category. The other factor (together with $dx$) is $dv$. Tabular method for repeated by-parts: when $u$ is a polynomial of degree $n$ and $dv$ involves $e^x$, $\sin x$, or $\cos x$, create two columns. Left column: successive derivatives of $u$ (each row differentiates once more, ending at 0). Right column: successive antiderivatives of $dv$. Alternate signs: $+, -, +, -, \ldots$ Multiply each left entry by the right entry one row below it, with the alternating sign. Sum all products. This gives the answer without writing intermediate integrals. Cycling integrals: for $\int e^{ax}\sin(bx)\,dx$ or $\int e^{ax}\cos(bx)\,dx$, two rounds of by-parts produce the original integral $I$ on the right side: $I = (\text{boundary terms}) - I$ or $I = (\text{terms}) + I$. Collect $I$: $2I = (\text{boundary terms})$, so $I = (\text{boundary terms})/2$. Results: $\int e^{ax}\sin(bx)\,dx = \frac{e^{ax}(a\sin bx - b\cos bx)}{a^2+b^2}+C$ and $\int e^{ax}\cos(bx)\,dx = \frac{e^{ax}(a\cos bx + b\sin bx)}{a^2+b^2}+C$. Reduction formula for $\int x^n e^x\,dx$: let $u = x^n$, $dv = e^x\,dx$. Then $\int x^n e^x\,dx = x^n e^x - n\int x^{n-1} e^x\,dx$. Applied $n$ times, this reduces to $\int e^x\,dx = e^x + C$. The general result is $\int x^n e^x\,dx = e^x \sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^k \frac{n!}{(n-k)!} x^{n-k} + C$. Derivation of integration by parts from the product rule. The product rule states: $d/dx[u(x)v(x)] = u'(x)v(x) + u(x)v'(x)$. Rearrange: $u(x)v'(x) = d/dx[u(x)v(x)] - u'(x)v(x)$. Integrate both sides over $[a,b]$: $\int_a^b u(x)v'(x)\,dx = [u(x)v(x)]_a^b - \int_a^b u'(x)v(x)\,dx$. This is exact — no approximation. The formula requires $u$ and $v$ to be differentiable, with $u'v$ and $uv'$ integrable. For continuously differentiable functions (the usual case), all conditions are satisfied. The indefinite version follows by omitting the evaluation limits: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du + C$. The constant is conventionally absorbed into the final answer. Note: when computing $v = \int dv$, we do NOT include a $+C$ at this intermediate step — any choice of antiderivative for $v$ works, and the simplest (with $C = 0$) is preferred. Validity of the cycling method: when $\int u\,dv$ is computed by two rounds of by-parts and yields $I = A - I$ (where $A$ collects boundary terms), we solve algebraically: $2I = A$, so $I = A/2$. This is valid because $I$ is a NUMBER (for definite integrals) or a FUNCTION (for indefinite integrals, defined up to $+C$). The algebraic manipulation is justified by the linearity of integration. Reduction formulas and mathematical induction. The reduction $\int \sin^n x\,dx = -(1/n)\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)/n \int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx$ is proved by one round of by-parts with $u = \sin^{n-1}x$ and $dv = \sin x\,dx$. The identity $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$ is used to eliminate $\cos^2 x$ from the result. By induction, this gives the Wallis-type closed form for $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx$. Derivation from the Product Rule \((uv)' = u'v + uv'\) implies \(uv' = (uv)' - u'v\). Integrating:
\[\int uv' = uv - \int u'v.\]
This is exact, not an approximation. It holds whenever \(u, v\) are \(C^1\) (continuously differentiable). When to Use Integration by Parts Use by-parts when the integrand is a product of two different "types" of functions (e.g., polynomial × exponential, polynomial × trig, logarithm × anything) and u-substitution does not apply (no inner derivative present). The LIATE rule tells you which factor to differentiate. Choosing u and dv Badly Bad choices make the integral harder. In ∫x·eˣ dx, if you choose u = eˣ and dv = x dx, then du = eˣ dx and v = x²/2, giving ∫x·eˣ dx = (x²/2)eˣ − ∫(x²/2)eˣ dx — the new integral is MORE complex (x² instead of x). LIATE prevents this: x (algebraic) has higher priority than eˣ (exponential), so u = x. By-Parts Is Not a Last Resort Students often try u-sub first and reach for by-parts only when stuck. But many integrals (∫x cos x dx, ∫ln x dx, ∫arctan x dx) are immediately recognizable as by-parts problems. Train yourself to spot products of different function types — that is the signal for by-parts. The Product Rule Is the Foundation By-parts IS the product rule, rearranged and integrated. If you remember (uv)' = u'v + uv', then ∫uv' = uv − ∫u'v follows immediately. Every by-parts problem is just the product rule run backwards, with a strategic choice of which factor to call u. Integration by Parts Formula \[\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du\]
Definite integral form:
\[\int_a^b u\,dv = [uv]_a^b - \int_a^b v\,du\] The Tabular Method For \(\int x^n f(x)\,dx\) where \(f\) has a pattern of antiderivatives (e.g., \(e^x, \sin x, \cos x\)):
1. Differentiate \(x^n\) repeatedly: \(x^n, nx^{n-1}, n(n-1)x^{n-2}, \ldots, n!, 0\)
2. Integrate \(f\) repeatedly: \(f, F_1, F_2, \ldots\)
3. Multiply diagonally with alternating signs and sum. Cycling Formula \[\int e^{ax}\sin(bx)\,dx = \frac{e^{ax}(a\sin bx - b\cos bx)}{a^2+b^2}+C\]
\[\int e^{ax}\cos(bx)\,dx = \frac{e^{ax}(a\cos bx + b\sin bx)}{a^2+b^2}+C\] Method Selection Trainer Try product-style integrands and see why by-parts ranks above substitution when no inner derivative is available. Integration by Parts: Trading Integrals The graph shows the original integrand u·v' (shaded) and the new integrand u'·v (lighter shade). By-parts trades the area under one curve for the area under a simpler curve, plus a boundary term uv. When the lighter region is easier to compute, the trade is profitable. Classic: ∫x·eˣ dx \text{Find } \int x e^x\,dx. ∫xeˣ dx = eˣ(x−1) + C. One round of by-parts reduces the polynomial factor from degree 1 to degree 0. \text{Let } u = x, \quad dv = e^x\,dx LIATE: x is algebraic (A), eˣ is exponential (E). A has higher priority, so u = x. du = dx, \quad v = e^x Differentiate u, integrate dv. \int xe^x\,dx = xe^x - \int e^x\,dx Apply ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du. = xe^x - e^x + C ∫eˣ dx = eˣ. = e^x(x-1) + C Factor out eˣ for a clean final form. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}[e^x(x-1)] = e^x(x-1) + e^x = e^x \cdot x \checkmark Product rule: eˣ(x−1) + eˣ·1 = xeˣ. ✓ Repeated Parts: ∫x²·sin(x) dx \text{Find } \int x^2\sin x\,dx. ∫x²sin x dx = −x²cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x + C. Two rounds of by-parts reduce x² to a constant. \text{Let } u = x^2, \; dv = \sin x\,dx \Rightarrow du = 2x\,dx, \; v = -\cos x First round: u = x² (algebraic), dv = sin x dx. \int x^2\sin x\,dx = -x^2\cos x + \int 2x\cos x\,dx Apply by-parts. The new integral still has a polynomial × trig — apply again. \text{Second round: } u = 2x, \; dv = \cos x\,dx \Rightarrow du = 2\,dx, \; v = \sin x Reduce the polynomial degree by one more. \int 2x\cos x\,dx = 2x\sin x - \int 2\sin x\,dx = 2x\sin x + 2\cos x + C ∫2 sin x dx = −2 cos x. \int x^2\sin x\,dx = -x^2\cos x + 2x\sin x + 2\cos x + C Combine all terms. \text{Verify: differentiate } -x^2\cos x + 2x\sin x + 2\cos x Product rule on each term: 2x cos x + x² sin x + 2 sin x + 2x cos x − 2 sin x = x² sin x. ✓ Lone Function: ∫ln(x) dx \text{Find } \int \ln x\,dx. ∫ln x dx = x ln x − x + C. The "trick" is writing ∫ln x dx as ∫1·ln x dx and choosing u = ln x. \text{Let } u = \ln x, \quad dv = dx Pair ln x with dv = dx. LIATE: logarithmic has highest priority. du = \frac{1}{x}\,dx, \quad v = x Differentiate u, integrate dv. \int \ln x\,dx = x\ln x - \int x \cdot \frac{1}{x}\,dx Apply the formula: uv − ∫v du. = x\ln x - \int 1\,dx = x\ln x - x + C The integral simplifies to ∫1 dx = x. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}[x\ln x - x] = \ln x + 1 - 1 = \ln x \checkmark Product rule on x ln x gives ln x + 1. Subtract 1. ✓ Cycling: ∫eˣ·sin(x) dx \text{Find } \int e^x\sin x\,dx. ∫eˣ sin x dx = eˣ(sin x − cos x)/2 + C. The cycling trick: apply by-parts twice, then solve algebraically for the original integral. \text{Let } I = \int e^x\sin x\,dx. \text{ Round 1: } u = \sin x,\; dv = e^x\,dx Either choice of u works for cycling. We pick u = sin x. I = e^x\sin x - \int e^x\cos x\,dx du = cos x dx, v = eˣ. Apply formula. \text{Round 2: } u = \cos x,\; dv = e^x\,dx \Rightarrow du = -\sin x\,dx,\; v = e^x Apply by-parts to ∫eˣ cos x dx. \int e^x\cos x\,dx = e^x\cos x - \int e^x(-\sin x)\,dx = e^x\cos x + I The original integral I reappears! I = e^x\sin x - (e^x\cos x + I) = e^x\sin x - e^x\cos x - I Substitute back into the Round 1 result. 2I = e^x(\sin x - \cos x) \Rightarrow I = \frac{e^x(\sin x - \cos x)}{2} + C Add I to both sides and divide by 2. Inverse Trig: ∫arctan(x) dx \text{Find } \int \arctan x\,dx. ∫arctan x dx = x arctan x − (1/2)ln(1+x²) + C. By-parts + u-sub working together — a common combination. \text{Let } u = \arctan x, \quad dv = dx LIATE: inverse trig (I) has high priority. Pair with dv = dx. du = \frac{1}{1+x^2}\,dx, \quad v = x Differentiate and integrate. \int \arctan x\,dx = x\arctan x - \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx Apply the formula. \text{For } \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx: \text{ let } w = 1+x^2,\; dw = 2x\,dx The remaining integral is a u-sub problem. \int \frac{x}{1+x^2}\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\ln|1+x^2| + C = \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^2) + C 1+x² > 0 always, so absolute value is unnecessary. \int \arctan x\,dx = x\arctan x - \frac{1}{2}\ln(1+x^2) + C Combine the results. Find ∫x·cos x dx and ∫x·e^(−x) dx. For both: u = x (algebraic), dv = the other factor. One round of by-parts each. \int x\cos x\,dx = x\sin x + \cos x + C; \quad \int xe^{-x}dx = -e^{-x}(x+1)+C \int x\cos x\,dx: u=x, dv=\cos x\,dx. \; = x\sin x - \int \sin x\,dx = x\sin x + \cos x + C du = dx, v = sin x. ∫sin x dx = −cos x. \int xe^{-x}dx: u=x, dv=e^{-x}dx. \; = -xe^{-x} - \int -e^{-x}dx = -xe^{-x} - e^{-x} + C = -e^{-x}(x+1)+C du = dx, v = −e^(−x). ∫−e^(−x) dx = e^(−x)... wait: ∫e^(−x) dx = −e^(−x). So −∫−e^(−x) dx = −e^(−x). Use the tabular method to find ∫x³·eˣ dx. Derivatives of x³: x³, 3x², 6x, 6, 0. Antiderivatives of eˣ: eˣ, eˣ, eˣ, eˣ, eˣ. Alternate signs +, −, +, −. e^x(x^3-3x^2+6x-6)+C \text{Derivatives: } x^3,\; 3x^2,\; 6x,\; 6,\; 0 Differentiate x³ until you reach 0. \text{Antiderivatives: } e^x,\; e^x,\; e^x,\; e^x Integrate eˣ repeatedly (it stays eˣ). \text{Signs: } +,\; -,\; +,\; - Alternate starting with +. = (+)(x^3)(e^x) + (-)(3x^2)(e^x) + (+)(6x)(e^x) + (-)(6)(e^x) + C Multiply each derivative by the antiderivative one row below, with alternating sign. = e^x(x^3 - 3x^2 + 6x - 6) + C Factor out eˣ. Derive the reduction formula: ∫sin^n(x) dx = −(1/n)sin^(n−1)(x)cos(x) + (n−1)/n · ∫sin^(n−2)(x) dx. Then use it to compute ∫sin⁴(x) dx. Write sin^n x = sin^(n−1) x · sin x. Let u = sin^(n−1) x, dv = sin x dx. Use cos²x = 1 − sin²x to handle the resulting integral. \int \sin^4 x\,dx = \frac{3x}{8} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C u = \sin^{n-1}x,\; dv = \sin x\,dx \Rightarrow du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}x\cos x\,dx,\; v = -\cos x Set up by-parts. \int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\cos^2 x\,dx Apply formula. Note the cos²x factor. = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x(1-\sin^2 x)\,dx Replace cos²x = 1 − sin²x. = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx - (n-1)\int \sin^n x\,dx Distribute. The last term contains the original integral! n\int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx Move the sin^n integral to the left: 1 + (n−1) = n. \int \sin^4 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{4}\sin^3 x\cos x + \frac{3}{4}\int \sin^2 x\,dx Apply with n=4. \int \sin^2 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{2}\sin x\cos x + \frac{1}{2}x + C = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Apply with n=2 (or use half-angle identity). \int \sin^4 x\,dx = -\frac{1}{4}\sin^3 x\cos x + \frac{3}{8}x - \frac{3}{16}\sin 2x + C Substitute back. U-Substitution When u-sub fails (no inner derivative present), by-parts is the next technique. Many problems combine both methods. Trig Integrals Reduction formulas derived by parts are the foundation of trig integral techniques in Lesson 8. Indefinite Integrals By-parts extends the basic antiderivative table to products of functions. The Chain Rule The product rule gives by-parts; the chain rule gives u-sub. Together they handle most integrals. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change

documents.title: Trigonometric Integrals
documents.subtitle: Systematic strategies for integrating powers and products of sine, cosine, tangent, and secant
