documents.assignment: Even Power: ∫sin²(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x\,dx.   ∫sin²x dx = x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C. This is the most important trig integral — memorize it or be able to derive it instantly. \sin^2 x = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2} Both exponents are even (sin² = sin² · cos⁰). Use the half-angle identity. \int \sin^2 x\,dx = \int \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\int(1-\cos 2x)\,dx Factor out 1/2. = \frac{1}{2}\left(x - \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right) + C = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C ∫cos 2x dx = sin(2x)/2 by linear substitution. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x}{2}-\frac{\sin 2x}{4}\right] = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{2\cos 2x}{4} = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2} = \sin^2 x \checkmark Differentiate and use the half-angle identity backwards. Odd Power: ∫sin³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^3 x\,dx.   ∫sin³x dx = −cos x + cos³x/3 + C. The "save one, convert the rest" strategy makes odd powers straightforward. \int \sin^3 x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x \cdot \sin x\,dx Odd power of sin: save one sin x. = \int (1-\cos^2 x)\sin x\,dx Replace sin²x = 1 − cos²x. \text{Let } u = \cos x, \; du = -\sin x\,dx Now u-sub with u = cos x. = -\int (1-u^2)\,du = -u + \frac{u^3}{3} + C Integrate the polynomial in u. = -\cos x + \frac{\cos^3 x}{3} + C Substitute back u = cos x. Mixed: ∫sin²(x)cos³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x \cos^3 x\,dx.   ∫sin²x cos³x dx = sin³x/3 − sin⁵x/5 + C. When one exponent is odd, the integral reduces to a polynomial. \text{cos has odd power (3). Save one cos x:} Decision: n = 3 is odd → use Pythagorean identity strategy. \int \sin^2 x \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x(1-\sin^2 x)\cos x\,dx Replace cos²x = 1 − sin²x. \text{Let } u = \sin x,\; du = \cos x\,dx Substitute u = sin x. = \int u^2(1-u^2)\,du = \int (u^2-u^4)\,du Expand. = \frac{u^3}{3} - \frac{u^5}{5} + C = \frac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \frac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C Power rule, then substitute back. Both Even: ∫sin²(x)cos²(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x \cos^2 x\,dx.   ∫sin²x cos²x dx = x/8 − sin(4x)/32 + C. Both even → half-angle, possibly twice. \sin^2 x\cos^2 x = \left(\frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2 2x}{4} Use sin x cos x = sin(2x)/2, then square. = \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{1-\cos 4x}{2} = \frac{1-\cos 4x}{8} Apply half-angle to sin²(2x): sin²(2x) = (1 − cos 4x)/2. \int \sin^2 x\cos^2 x\,dx = \frac{1}{8}\int(1-\cos 4x)\,dx Now integrate a first-degree trig function. = \frac{1}{8}\left(x - \frac{\sin 4x}{4}\right) + C = \frac{x}{8} - \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C ∫cos 4x dx = sin(4x)/4. Tangent Power: ∫tan⁴(x) dx \text{Find } \int \tan^4 x\,dx.   ∫tan⁴x dx = tan³x/3 − tan x + x + C. The reduction tan²x = sec²x − 1 is applied twice. \tan^4 x = \tan^2 x \cdot \tan^2 x = \tan^2 x(\sec^2 x - 1) Peel off tan²x and use tan²x = sec²x − 1. = \tan^2 x \sec^2 x - \tan^2 x Distribute. \int \tan^2 x\sec^2 x\,dx: u=\tan x,\; du=\sec^2 x\,dx \Rightarrow \int u^2\,du = \frac{\tan^3 x}{3} First integral: direct u-sub. \int \tan^2 x\,dx = \int(\sec^2 x-1)\,dx = \tan x - x + C Second integral: replace tan²x = sec²x − 1 again. \int \tan^4 x\,dx = \frac{\tan^3 x}{3} - \tan x + x + C Combine results. Secant Cubed: ∫sec³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sec^3 x\,dx.   ∫sec³x dx = (1/2)[sec x tan x + ln|sec x + tan x|] + C. This is one of the most important results in trig integrals — it appears frequently in applications (arc length of parabolas, etc.). \text{Let } I = \int \sec^3 x\,dx. \text{ By parts: } u = \sec x,\; dv = \sec^2 x\,dx Odd power of sec → integration by parts. du = \sec x\tan x\,dx, \quad v = \tan x Differentiate and integrate. I = \sec x\tan x - \int \sec x\tan^2 x\,dx Apply the by-parts formula. = \sec x\tan x - \int \sec x(\sec^2 x - 1)\,dx Replace tan²x = sec²x − 1. = \sec x\tan x - I + \int \sec x\,dx Split: −∫sec³x dx = −I, and +∫sec x dx. 2I = \sec x\tan x + \ln|\sec x+\tan x| Collect I on the left. Recall ∫sec x dx = ln|sec x + tan x|. I = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sec x\tan x + \ln|\sec x + \tan x|\right] + C Divide by 2.  Evaluate: (a) ∫cos²(x) dx, (b) ∫sin(x)cos³(x) dx. (a) Half-angle: cos²x = (1+cos 2x)/2. (b) Odd power of cos: save one cos x, let u = sin x. \text{(a) } \frac{x}{2}+\frac{\sin 2x}{4}+C \quad \text{(b) } -\frac{\cos^4 x}{4}+C  \text{(a) } \int \cos^2 x\,dx = \int\frac{1+\cos 2x}{2}dx = \frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Half-angle identity, then integrate. \text{(b) } u = \sin x,\; du = \cos x\,dx.\; \int \sin x \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\,dx = \int u(1-u^2)du Wait — cos has odd power 3. Save one cos x. sin x · cos²x · cos x dx. But more directly: just u = sin x... \text{Actually: } \int \sin x\cos^3 x\,dx,\; u=\cos x,\; du=-\sin x\,dx: -\int u^3\,du = -\frac{\cos^4 x}{4}+C Simpler: let u = cos x since sin x dx = −du. Then cos³x = u³.  Evaluate ∫₀^(π/2) sin⁵(x) dx using the odd-power strategy, then verify using the Wallis formula. Save one sin x, convert sin⁴x = (1−cos²x)², substitute u = cos x. For Wallis: (4·2)/(5·3·1) · 1. \frac{8}{15}  \int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^5 x\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2}(1-\cos^2 x)^2\sin x\,dx Save one sin x, write sin⁴x = (1−cos²x)². u=\cos x,\; x=0\Rightarrow u=1,\; x=\pi/2\Rightarrow u=0 Change limits. = -\int_1^0 (1-u^2)^2\,du = \int_0^1(1-2u^2+u^4)\,du Flip limits and expand. = \left[u-\frac{2u^3}{3}+\frac{u^5}{5}\right]_0^1 = 1-\frac{2}{3}+\frac{1}{5} = \frac{15-10+3}{15} = \frac{8}{15} Evaluate. \text{Wallis: } \frac{4!!}{5!!} \cdot 1 = \frac{4\cdot2}{5\cdot3\cdot1} = \frac{8}{15} \checkmark n=5 is odd, so multiply by 1 (not π/2). Confirmed.  Find ∫tan³(x)sec³(x) dx. Save sec x tan x (so m is odd for tan). Write tan²x = sec²x − 1. Let u = sec x. \frac{\sec^5 x}{5}-\frac{\sec^3 x}{3}+C  \int \tan^3 x\sec^3 x\,dx = \int \tan^2 x\sec^2 x\cdot\sec x\tan x\,dx Save sec x tan x factor. Remaining: tan²x · sec²x. = \int (\sec^2 x-1)\sec^2 x\cdot\sec x\tan x\,dx Replace tan²x = sec²x − 1. u = \sec x,\; du = \sec x\tan x\,dx Substitute. = \int (u^2-1)u^2\,du = \int(u^4-u^2)\,du Everything in terms of u. = \frac{u^5}{5}-\frac{u^3}{3}+C = \frac{\sec^5 x}{5}-\frac{\sec^3 x}{3}+C Integrate and substitute back.
documents.proofs:
The half-angle identities are derived from the double-angle formula $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x = 2\cos^2 x - 1$. Solving for $\sin^2 x$: $\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos 2x)/2$. Solving for $\cos^2 x$: $\cos^2 x = (1 + \cos 2x)/2$. These are exact algebraic identities, not approximations. The reduction formula for $\int \sin^n x\,dx$ is derived by integration by parts. Let $u = \sin^{n-1} x$, $dv = \sin x\,dx$. Then $du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}x\cos x\,dx$ and $v = -\cos x$. Applying by-parts: $\int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\cos^2 x\,dx$. Replace $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$: $= -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx - (n-1)\int \sin^n x\,dx$. Let $I_n = \int \sin^n x\,dx$. Then $I_n = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)I_{n-2} - (n-1)I_n$. Solving: $nI_n = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)I_{n-2}$, giving $I_n = -\frac{1}{n}\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + \frac{n-1}{n}I_{n-2}$. The Wallis formula for definite integrals over $[0, \pi/2]$ follows from the reduction formula evaluated at the endpoints. Since $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, the boundary term $[-\sin^{n-1}x\cos x]_0^{\pi/2}$ equals $-1^{n-1}\cdot 0 + 0 = 0$ for all $n \geq 1$. Thus $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^n x\,dx = \frac{n-1}{n}\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{n-2}x\,dx$. Iterating: for even $n = 2k$, the chain terminates at $\int_0^{\pi/2}1\,dx = \pi/2$; for odd $n = 2k+1$, it terminates at $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin x\,dx = 1$. The Wallis product formula for $\pi$ follows from comparing $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n}x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n+1}x\,dx$. Since $0 \leq \sin x \leq 1$ on $[0, \pi/2]$, we have $\sin^{2n+1}x \leq \sin^{2n}x \leq \sin^{2n-1}x$. Integrating and dividing by $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n+1}x\,dx$, then taking $n \to \infty$ (using the squeeze theorem), yields the Wallis product: $\pi/2 = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{4k^2}{4k^2-1}$. Reduction Formula for sin^n (Proof Sketch) By parts with \(u = \sin^{n-1}x\), \(dv = \sin x\,dx\):
\[I_n = -\frac{1}{n}\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + \frac{n-1}{n}I_{n-2}\]
The \(\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x\) substitution is the key algebraic step that makes the original integral reappear.
documents.applications:
Trigonometric integrals arise naturally in any field involving periodic phenomena. Electrical engineers compute power dissipation in AC circuits by integrating sin²(ωt) over a period — giving the "RMS" (root mean square) values that appear on every voltmeter. Structural engineers compute bending moments in beams under sinusoidal loads. In optics, interference patterns involve integrals of cos² and sin·cos products. Fourier analysis — the mathematical backbone of audio processing, telecommunications, and image compression — requires evaluating ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx for all combinations of m and n. The Decision Flowchart for sin^m · cos^n 1. Is one exponent odd? → Save one factor of that trig function, convert the rest using sin²+cos²=1, then u-sub.
2. Are both exponents even? → Use half-angle identities: sin²x = (1−cos 2x)/2, cos²x = (1+cos 2x)/2.
3. Special case: both zero → ∫1 dx = x + C.
This flowchart handles EVERY integral of the form ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x) dx. Essential Trig Identities You need these memorized:
• sin²x + cos²x = 1
• sin²x = (1 − cos 2x)/2
• cos²x = (1 + cos 2x)/2
• sin x cos x = sin(2x)/2
• tan²x = sec²x − 1
• 1 + tan²x = sec²x Do Not Mix Up the Strategies Odd power? → Pythagorean identity + u-sub. Even power? → Half-angle identity. Using half-angle when you have an odd power creates unnecessary complexity. Using the Pythagorean identity when both powers are even does not reduce the powers. RMS Voltage and sin² The average power dissipated in a resistor carrying AC current I(t) = I₀ sin(ωt) is P_avg = (1/T)∫₀ᵀ I₀² sin²(ωt)·R dt. The integral of sin² over a full period gives T/2 (by the half-angle identity). So P_avg = I₀²R/2, and the "RMS current" is I₀/√2. Every electrical rating you see (120V, 240V) is an RMS value — computed by integrating sin². ∫sin²x dx ≠ sin³x/3 The power rule ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) applies to powers of x, NOT powers of sin x. sin²x is a composition, not a simple power. You must use the half-angle identity: ∫sin²x dx = ∫(1−cos 2x)/2 dx = x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C.
documents.content:
How do you integrate sin²(x)? The power rule does not apply (sin²(x) is not x^n), and u-sub fails (the derivative of sin x is cos x, but there is no cos x factor). The key is a trig identity: sin²(x) = (1 − cos 2x)/2. This converts the integral to a basic form. Trig integrals are all about choosing the right identity to simplify the integrand — and this lesson gives you a complete decision flowchart. Trigonometric integrals arise naturally in any field involving periodic phenomena. Electrical engineers compute power dissipation in AC circuits by integrating sin²(ωt) over a period — giving the "RMS" (root mean square) values that appear on every voltmeter. Structural engineers compute bending moments in beams under sinusoidal loads. In optics, interference patterns involve integrals of cos² and sin·cos products. Fourier analysis — the mathematical backbone of audio processing, telecommunications, and image compression — requires evaluating ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx for all combinations of m and n. The fundamental challenge with trig integrals is that powers of trig functions are not in the basic antiderivative table. You know $\int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x$ and $\int \cos x\,dx = \sin x$, but $\int \sin^2 x\,dx$ is not directly available. The strategy is always the same: use trigonometric identities to convert the integrand into a form you CAN integrate. For products $\sin^m x \cos^n x$, the strategy depends on whether $m$ or $n$ is odd. If one exponent is odd, peel off one factor and use the Pythagorean identity to convert the rest. For example, $\int \sin^3 x \cos^2 x\,dx$: peel off one $\sin x$ to get $\sin^2 x \cos^2 x \cdot \sin x\,dx$, replace $\sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x$, and substitute $u = \cos x$. The integral becomes a polynomial in $u$. If BOTH exponents are even, peeling off one factor does not help (it leaves an odd power that cannot be converted using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ cleanly). Instead, use the half-angle identities: $\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos 2x)/2$ and $\cos^2 x = (1 + \cos 2x)/2$. These reduce the powers by converting $\sin^2$ and $\cos^2$ into first-degree trig functions of $2x$. You may need to apply half-angle identities multiple times for higher even powers. For powers of tangent and secant, the strategies are different. The key identity is $\tan^2 x = \sec^2 x - 1$. For $\int \tan^n x\,dx$, write $\tan^n x = \tan^{n-2} x \cdot \tan^2 x = \tan^{n-2} x(\sec^2 x - 1)$, which splits into $\int \tan^{n-2} x \sec^2 x\,dx$ (u-sub with $u = \tan x$) minus $\int \tan^{n-2} x\,dx$ (a lower power). This is a reduction formula. For $\int \sec^n x\,dx$ with even $n$, save a $\sec^2 x$ factor and convert the rest using $\sec^2 x = 1 + \tan^2 x$, then substitute $u = \tan x$. For odd powers of secant, integration by parts is needed — and $\int \sec^3 x\,dx$ is a famously tricky integral that combines by-parts with the identity $\sec^2 x = 1 + \tan^2 x$. Here is the complete decision flowchart for $\int \sin^m x \cos^n x\,dx$: (1) If $n$ is odd: save one $\cos x$, write the rest as $(1-\sin^2 x)^{(n-1)/2}$, substitute $u = \sin x$. (2) If $m$ is odd: save one $\sin x$, write the rest as $(1-\cos^2 x)^{(m-1)/2}$, substitute $u = \cos x$. (3) If both are even: use half-angle identities to reduce powers. For $\int \tan^m x \sec^n x\,dx$: (1) If $n$ is even: save $\sec^2 x$, convert remaining $\sec$ to $\tan$ via $\sec^2 = 1 + \tan^2$, substitute $u = \tan x$. (2) If $m$ is odd: save $\sec x \tan x$, convert remaining $\tan$ to $\sec$ via $\tan^2 = \sec^2 - 1$, substitute $u = \sec x$. The Wallis formulas give closed-form results for $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^n x\,dx$. By the reduction formula, $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx = \frac{n-1}{n}\cdot\frac{n-3}{n-2}\cdots$ The product ends at $\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2}$ if $n$ is even, or at $1$ if $n$ is odd. These formulas are used in physics (computing moments of inertia of spheres) and lead to the beautiful Wallis product for $\pi$: $\pi/2 = (2/1)(2/3)(4/3)(4/5)(6/5)(6/7)\cdots$ Case 1 — odd power of cosine: $\int \sin^m x \cos^{2k+1} x\,dx$. Save one $\cos x$: $= \int \sin^m x (\cos^2 x)^k \cos x\,dx = \int \sin^m x (1-\sin^2 x)^k \cos x\,dx$. Substitute $u = \sin x$, $du = \cos x\,dx$: $= \int u^m(1-u^2)^k\,du$. Expand $(1-u^2)^k$ and integrate each term by the power rule. Case 2 — odd power of sine: $\int \sin^{2k+1} x \cos^n x\,dx$. Save one $\sin x$: $= \int (\sin^2 x)^k \cos^n x \sin x\,dx = \int (1-\cos^2 x)^k \cos^n x \sin x\,dx$. Substitute $u = \cos x$, $du = -\sin x\,dx$: $= -\int (1-u^2)^k u^n\,du$. Case 3 — both even: use half-angle identities repeatedly. $\int \sin^{2m} x \cos^{2n} x\,dx$. Replace $\sin^2 x = (1-\cos 2x)/2$ and $\cos^2 x = (1+\cos 2x)/2$. Expand the product and integrate each term. For the resulting $\cos^2(2x)$ terms, apply the half-angle identity again with argument $4x$. Tangent powers: $\int \tan^n x\,dx = \int \tan^{n-2} x(\sec^2 x - 1)\,dx = \int \tan^{n-2} x \sec^2 x\,dx - \int \tan^{n-2} x\,dx$. The first integral is $\tan^{n-1} x/(n-1)$ by $u = \tan x$. This gives the reduction formula: $\int \tan^n x\,dx = \frac{\tan^{n-1} x}{n-1} - \int \tan^{n-2} x\,dx$. Secant powers (even): $\int \sec^{2k} x\,dx = \int (\sec^2 x)^{k-1} \sec^2 x\,dx = \int (1+\tan^2 x)^{k-1} \sec^2 x\,dx$. Substitute $u = \tan x$: $= \int (1+u^2)^{k-1}\,du$, a polynomial integral. Secant powers (odd, $\geq 3$): use integration by parts. The key result is $\int \sec^3 x\,dx = \frac{1}{2}[\sec x \tan x + \ln|\sec x + \tan x|] + C$. Wallis formulas: $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^n x\,dx = \frac{(n-1)!!}{n!!} \cdot \begin{cases} \pi/2 & n \text{ even} \\ 1 & n \text{ odd} \end{cases}$, where $n!! = n(n-2)(n-4)\cdots$ (double factorial). For example, $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^6 x\,dx = \frac{5\cdot3\cdot1}{6\cdot4\cdot2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{5\pi}{32}$. The half-angle identities are derived from the double-angle formula $\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x = 2\cos^2 x - 1$. Solving for $\sin^2 x$: $\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos 2x)/2$. Solving for $\cos^2 x$: $\cos^2 x = (1 + \cos 2x)/2$. These are exact algebraic identities, not approximations. The reduction formula for $\int \sin^n x\,dx$ is derived by integration by parts. Let $u = \sin^{n-1} x$, $dv = \sin x\,dx$. Then $du = (n-1)\sin^{n-2}x\cos x\,dx$ and $v = -\cos x$. Applying by-parts: $\int \sin^n x\,dx = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\cos^2 x\,dx$. Replace $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$: $= -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)\int \sin^{n-2}x\,dx - (n-1)\int \sin^n x\,dx$. Let $I_n = \int \sin^n x\,dx$. Then $I_n = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)I_{n-2} - (n-1)I_n$. Solving: $nI_n = -\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + (n-1)I_{n-2}$, giving $I_n = -\frac{1}{n}\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + \frac{n-1}{n}I_{n-2}$. The Wallis formula for definite integrals over $[0, \pi/2]$ follows from the reduction formula evaluated at the endpoints. Since $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, the boundary term $[-\sin^{n-1}x\cos x]_0^{\pi/2}$ equals $-1^{n-1}\cdot 0 + 0 = 0$ for all $n \geq 1$. Thus $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^n x\,dx = \frac{n-1}{n}\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{n-2}x\,dx$. Iterating: for even $n = 2k$, the chain terminates at $\int_0^{\pi/2}1\,dx = \pi/2$; for odd $n = 2k+1$, it terminates at $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin x\,dx = 1$. The Wallis product formula for $\pi$ follows from comparing $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n}x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n+1}x\,dx$. Since $0 \leq \sin x \leq 1$ on $[0, \pi/2]$, we have $\sin^{2n+1}x \leq \sin^{2n}x \leq \sin^{2n-1}x$. Integrating and dividing by $\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^{2n+1}x\,dx$, then taking $n \to \infty$ (using the squeeze theorem), yields the Wallis product: $\pi/2 = \prod_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{4k^2}{4k^2-1}$. Reduction Formula for sin^n (Proof Sketch) By parts with \(u = \sin^{n-1}x\), \(dv = \sin x\,dx\):
\[I_n = -\frac{1}{n}\sin^{n-1}x\cos x + \frac{n-1}{n}I_{n-2}\]
The \(\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x\) substitution is the key algebraic step that makes the original integral reappear. The Decision Flowchart for sin^m · cos^n 1. Is one exponent odd? → Save one factor of that trig function, convert the rest using sin²+cos²=1, then u-sub.
2. Are both exponents even? → Use half-angle identities: sin²x = (1−cos 2x)/2, cos²x = (1+cos 2x)/2.
3. Special case: both zero → ∫1 dx = x + C.
This flowchart handles EVERY integral of the form ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x) dx. Essential Trig Identities You need these memorized:
• sin²x + cos²x = 1
• sin²x = (1 − cos 2x)/2
• cos²x = (1 + cos 2x)/2
• sin x cos x = sin(2x)/2
• tan²x = sec²x − 1
• 1 + tan²x = sec²x Do Not Mix Up the Strategies Odd power? → Pythagorean identity + u-sub. Even power? → Half-angle identity. Using half-angle when you have an odd power creates unnecessary complexity. Using the Pythagorean identity when both powers are even does not reduce the powers. RMS Voltage and sin² The average power dissipated in a resistor carrying AC current I(t) = I₀ sin(ωt) is P_avg = (1/T)∫₀ᵀ I₀² sin²(ωt)·R dt. The integral of sin² over a full period gives T/2 (by the half-angle identity). So P_avg = I₀²R/2, and the "RMS current" is I₀/√2. Every electrical rating you see (120V, 240V) is an RMS value — computed by integrating sin². ∫sin²x dx ≠ sin³x/3 The power rule ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) applies to powers of x, NOT powers of sin x. sin²x is a composition, not a simple power. You must use the half-angle identity: ∫sin²x dx = ∫(1−cos 2x)/2 dx = x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C. Half-Angle Identities for Integration \[\sin^2 x = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}, \quad \cos^2 x = \frac{1+\cos 2x}{2}\]
\[\sin x \cos x = \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\]
These convert even powers of sin and cos into first-degree trig functions. Tangent Reduction Formula \[\int \tan^n x\,dx = \frac{\tan^{n-1}x}{n-1} - \int \tan^{n-2}x\,dx \quad (n \geq 2)\] Wallis Formula \[\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^n x\,dx = \frac{(n-1)!!}{n!!} \cdot \begin{cases} \pi/2 & n \text{ even} \\ 1 & n \text{ odd}\end{cases}\] Method Selection Trainer Toggle trig-pattern signals to decide when to use odd/even power identities versus other major integration techniques. Powers of Sine and Cosine on the Unit Circle As the exponent increases, sin^n(x) and cos^n(x) become more "peaked" near their maxima and flatter near zero. This geometric view explains why ∫₀^(π/2) sin^n x dx decreases as n grows — the function spends more time near zero. Even Power: ∫sin²(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x\,dx. ∫sin²x dx = x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C. This is the most important trig integral — memorize it or be able to derive it instantly. \sin^2 x = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2} Both exponents are even (sin² = sin² · cos⁰). Use the half-angle identity. \int \sin^2 x\,dx = \int \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}\,dx = \frac{1}{2}\int(1-\cos 2x)\,dx Factor out 1/2. = \frac{1}{2}\left(x - \frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right) + C = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C ∫cos 2x dx = sin(2x)/2 by linear substitution. \text{Verify: } \frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x}{2}-\frac{\sin 2x}{4}\right] = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{2\cos 2x}{4} = \frac{1-\cos 2x}{2} = \sin^2 x \checkmark Differentiate and use the half-angle identity backwards. Odd Power: ∫sin³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^3 x\,dx. ∫sin³x dx = −cos x + cos³x/3 + C. The "save one, convert the rest" strategy makes odd powers straightforward. \int \sin^3 x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x \cdot \sin x\,dx Odd power of sin: save one sin x. = \int (1-\cos^2 x)\sin x\,dx Replace sin²x = 1 − cos²x. \text{Let } u = \cos x, \; du = -\sin x\,dx Now u-sub with u = cos x. = -\int (1-u^2)\,du = -u + \frac{u^3}{3} + C Integrate the polynomial in u. = -\cos x + \frac{\cos^3 x}{3} + C Substitute back u = cos x. Mixed: ∫sin²(x)cos³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x \cos^3 x\,dx. ∫sin²x cos³x dx = sin³x/3 − sin⁵x/5 + C. When one exponent is odd, the integral reduces to a polynomial. \text{cos has odd power (3). Save one cos x:} Decision: n = 3 is odd → use Pythagorean identity strategy. \int \sin^2 x \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\,dx = \int \sin^2 x(1-\sin^2 x)\cos x\,dx Replace cos²x = 1 − sin²x. \text{Let } u = \sin x,\; du = \cos x\,dx Substitute u = sin x. = \int u^2(1-u^2)\,du = \int (u^2-u^4)\,du Expand. = \frac{u^3}{3} - \frac{u^5}{5} + C = \frac{\sin^3 x}{3} - \frac{\sin^5 x}{5} + C Power rule, then substitute back. Both Even: ∫sin²(x)cos²(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sin^2 x \cos^2 x\,dx. ∫sin²x cos²x dx = x/8 − sin(4x)/32 + C. Both even → half-angle, possibly twice. \sin^2 x\cos^2 x = \left(\frac{\sin 2x}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\sin^2 2x}{4} Use sin x cos x = sin(2x)/2, then square. = \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{1-\cos 4x}{2} = \frac{1-\cos 4x}{8} Apply half-angle to sin²(2x): sin²(2x) = (1 − cos 4x)/2. \int \sin^2 x\cos^2 x\,dx = \frac{1}{8}\int(1-\cos 4x)\,dx Now integrate a first-degree trig function. = \frac{1}{8}\left(x - \frac{\sin 4x}{4}\right) + C = \frac{x}{8} - \frac{\sin 4x}{32} + C ∫cos 4x dx = sin(4x)/4. Tangent Power: ∫tan⁴(x) dx \text{Find } \int \tan^4 x\,dx. ∫tan⁴x dx = tan³x/3 − tan x + x + C. The reduction tan²x = sec²x − 1 is applied twice. \tan^4 x = \tan^2 x \cdot \tan^2 x = \tan^2 x(\sec^2 x - 1) Peel off tan²x and use tan²x = sec²x − 1. = \tan^2 x \sec^2 x - \tan^2 x Distribute. \int \tan^2 x\sec^2 x\,dx: u=\tan x,\; du=\sec^2 x\,dx \Rightarrow \int u^2\,du = \frac{\tan^3 x}{3} First integral: direct u-sub. \int \tan^2 x\,dx = \int(\sec^2 x-1)\,dx = \tan x - x + C Second integral: replace tan²x = sec²x − 1 again. \int \tan^4 x\,dx = \frac{\tan^3 x}{3} - \tan x + x + C Combine results. Secant Cubed: ∫sec³(x) dx \text{Find } \int \sec^3 x\,dx. ∫sec³x dx = (1/2)[sec x tan x + ln|sec x + tan x|] + C. This is one of the most important results in trig integrals — it appears frequently in applications (arc length of parabolas, etc.). \text{Let } I = \int \sec^3 x\,dx. \text{ By parts: } u = \sec x,\; dv = \sec^2 x\,dx Odd power of sec → integration by parts. du = \sec x\tan x\,dx, \quad v = \tan x Differentiate and integrate. I = \sec x\tan x - \int \sec x\tan^2 x\,dx Apply the by-parts formula. = \sec x\tan x - \int \sec x(\sec^2 x - 1)\,dx Replace tan²x = sec²x − 1. = \sec x\tan x - I + \int \sec x\,dx Split: −∫sec³x dx = −I, and +∫sec x dx. 2I = \sec x\tan x + \ln|\sec x+\tan x| Collect I on the left. Recall ∫sec x dx = ln|sec x + tan x|. I = \frac{1}{2}\left[\sec x\tan x + \ln|\sec x + \tan x|\right] + C Divide by 2. Evaluate: (a) ∫cos²(x) dx, (b) ∫sin(x)cos³(x) dx. (a) Half-angle: cos²x = (1+cos 2x)/2. (b) Odd power of cos: save one cos x, let u = sin x. \text{(a) } \frac{x}{2}+\frac{\sin 2x}{4}+C \quad \text{(b) } -\frac{\cos^4 x}{4}+C \text{(a) } \int \cos^2 x\,dx = \int\frac{1+\cos 2x}{2}dx = \frac{x}{2} + \frac{\sin 2x}{4} + C Half-angle identity, then integrate. \text{(b) } u = \sin x,\; du = \cos x\,dx.\; \int \sin x \cos^2 x \cdot \cos x\,dx = \int u(1-u^2)du Wait — cos has odd power 3. Save one cos x. sin x · cos²x · cos x dx. But more directly: just u = sin x... \text{Actually: } \int \sin x\cos^3 x\,dx,\; u=\cos x,\; du=-\sin x\,dx: -\int u^3\,du = -\frac{\cos^4 x}{4}+C Simpler: let u = cos x since sin x dx = −du. Then cos³x = u³. Evaluate ∫₀^(π/2) sin⁵(x) dx using the odd-power strategy, then verify using the Wallis formula. Save one sin x, convert sin⁴x = (1−cos²x)², substitute u = cos x. For Wallis: (4·2)/(5·3·1) · 1. \frac{8}{15} \int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^5 x\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2}(1-\cos^2 x)^2\sin x\,dx Save one sin x, write sin⁴x = (1−cos²x)². u=\cos x,\; x=0\Rightarrow u=1,\; x=\pi/2\Rightarrow u=0 Change limits. = -\int_1^0 (1-u^2)^2\,du = \int_0^1(1-2u^2+u^4)\,du Flip limits and expand. = \left[u-\frac{2u^3}{3}+\frac{u^5}{5}\right]_0^1 = 1-\frac{2}{3}+\frac{1}{5} = \frac{15-10+3}{15} = \frac{8}{15} Evaluate. \text{Wallis: } \frac{4!!}{5!!} \cdot 1 = \frac{4\cdot2}{5\cdot3\cdot1} = \frac{8}{15} \checkmark n=5 is odd, so multiply by 1 (not π/2). Confirmed. Find ∫tan³(x)sec³(x) dx. Save sec x tan x (so m is odd for tan). Write tan²x = sec²x − 1. Let u = sec x. \frac{\sec^5 x}{5}-\frac{\sec^3 x}{3}+C \int \tan^3 x\sec^3 x\,dx = \int \tan^2 x\sec^2 x\cdot\sec x\tan x\,dx Save sec x tan x factor. Remaining: tan²x · sec²x. = \int (\sec^2 x-1)\sec^2 x\cdot\sec x\tan x\,dx Replace tan²x = sec²x − 1. u = \sec x,\; du = \sec x\tan x\,dx Substitute. = \int (u^2-1)u^2\,du = \int(u^4-u^2)\,du Everything in terms of u. = \frac{u^5}{5}-\frac{u^3}{3}+C = \frac{\sec^5 x}{5}-\frac{\sec^3 x}{3}+C Integrate and substitute back. U-Substitution Most trig integral strategies end with a u-substitution after applying an identity. Integration by Parts Reduction formulas and ∫sec³x dx are derived using by-parts. Trig Substitution Trig substitution creates trig integrals — you need the techniques from this lesson to finish those problems. Trig Review Review the unit circle and identities from Chapter 0 before tackling this lesson. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change trig sine cosine tangent unit circle

documents.title: Trigonometric Substitution

documents.assignment: Semicircle Area: ∫√(4−x²) dx \text{Find } \int \sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx.   ∫√(4−x²) dx = 2 arcsin(x/2) + x√(4−x²)/2 + C. Evaluating from −2 to 2 gives 2π — the area of a semicircle of radius 2. \sqrt{a^2-x^2} \text{ form with } a=2. \text{ Let } x = 2\sin\theta, \; dx = 2\cos\theta\,d\theta Case 1 substitution. \sqrt{4-x^2} = \sqrt{4-4\sin^2\theta} = 2\cos\theta The radical becomes 2cosθ. \int \sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx = \int 2\cos\theta \cdot 2\cos\theta\,d\theta = 4\int \cos^2\theta\,d\theta Substitute everything. = 4\cdot\frac{1}{2}(\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta) + C = 2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + C ∫cos²θ dθ = θ/2 + sin(2θ)/4 = (θ + sinθ cosθ)/2. \text{Back-sub: } \theta = \arcsin(x/2),\; \sin\theta = x/2,\; \cos\theta = \sqrt{4-x^2}/2 Use the reference triangle: opp = x, hyp = 2, adj = √(4−x²). = 2\arcsin\frac{x}{2} + 2\cdot\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{4-x^2}}{2} + C = 2\arcsin\frac{x}{2} + \frac{x\sqrt{4-x^2}}{2} + C Final answer in terms of x. ∫dx/(x²+1)^(3/2) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{(x^2+1)^{3/2}}.   ∫dx/(x²+1)^(3/2) = x/√(x²+1) + C. The tan substitution turned a formidable-looking integral into ∫cosθ dθ. \sqrt{x^2+a^2} \text{ with } a=1. \text{ Let } x = \tan\theta, \; dx = \sec^2\theta\,d\theta Case 2 substitution. x^2+1 = \tan^2\theta+1 = \sec^2\theta, \; (x^2+1)^{3/2} = \sec^3\theta Simplify the denominator. \int \frac{\sec^2\theta}{\sec^3\theta}\,d\theta = \int \frac{d\theta}{\sec\theta} = \int \cos\theta\,d\theta Cancel sec²θ/sec³θ = 1/secθ = cosθ. = \sin\theta + C ∫cosθ dθ = sinθ + C. \text{Reference triangle: adj}=1, \text{ opp}=x, \text{ hyp}=\sqrt{x^2+1}. \; \sin\theta = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} Read sinθ from the triangle. = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} + C Final answer. ∫x²/√(9−x²) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{9-x^2}}\,dx.   ∫x²/√(9−x²) dx = (9/2)arcsin(x/3) − x√(9−x²)/2 + C. \text{Let } x = 3\sin\theta, \; dx = 3\cos\theta\,d\theta, \; \sqrt{9-x^2} = 3\cos\theta Case 1 with a = 3. \int \frac{9\sin^2\theta}{3\cos\theta}\cdot 3\cos\theta\,d\theta = 9\int \sin^2\theta\,d\theta The cosθ factors cancel. = 9\cdot\frac{1}{2}(\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta) + C ∫sin²θ dθ = θ/2 − sin(2θ)/4 = (θ − sinθ cosθ)/2. = \frac{9}{2}\arcsin\frac{x}{3} - \frac{9}{2}\cdot\frac{x}{3}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{9-x^2}}{3} + C Back-sub: θ = arcsin(x/3), sinθ = x/3, cosθ = √(9−x²)/3. = \frac{9}{2}\arcsin\frac{x}{3} - \frac{x\sqrt{9-x^2}}{2} + C Simplify: 9/(2·9) = 1/2. ∫dx/√(x²−9) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2-9}} \text{ for } x > 3.   ∫dx/√(x²−9) = ln|x + √(x²−9)| + C. This is equivalent to arccosh(x/3) + C. \text{Let } x = 3\sec\theta, \; dx = 3\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta, \; \sqrt{x^2-9} = 3\tan\theta Case 3 with a = 3. \int \frac{3\sec\theta\tan\theta}{3\tan\theta}\,d\theta = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta The 3tanθ factors cancel. = \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C Standard integral of secθ. \text{Reference triangle: hyp}=x,\text{ adj}=3,\text{ opp}=\sqrt{x^2-9} secθ = x/3, tanθ = √(x²−9)/3. = \ln\left|\frac{x}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{x^2-9}}{3}\right| + C = \ln\left|x+\sqrt{x^2-9}\right| - \ln 3 + C Simplify. The −ln 3 can be absorbed into C. = \ln\left|x+\sqrt{x^2-9}\right| + C Final simplified form. Completing the Square: ∫dx/√(x²+4x+8) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2+4x+8}}.   ∫dx/√(x²+4x+8) = ln|x+2+√(x²+4x+8)| + C. Completing the square is the essential first step when the quadratic has a linear term. x^2+4x+8 = (x+2)^2 + 4 Complete the square: x²+4x+4+4 = (x+2)²+4. \text{Let } u = x+2,\; du = dx. \text{ Then } \int\frac{du}{\sqrt{u^2+4}} Shift to standard form. \text{Let } u = 2\tan\theta,\; du = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{u^2+4} = 2\sec\theta Case 2 with a = 2. \int\frac{2\sec^2\theta}{2\sec\theta}d\theta = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta = \ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|+C Simplify and integrate. \sec\theta = \frac{\sqrt{u^2+4}}{2},\; \tan\theta = \frac{u}{2} = \frac{x+2}{2} Back-substitute using the reference triangle. = \ln\left|\frac{\sqrt{(x+2)^2+4}}{2}+\frac{x+2}{2}\right|+C = \ln|x+2+\sqrt{x^2+4x+8}|+C Simplify, absorbing −ln 2 into C.  Find ∫dx/√(1−x²). (You should recognize this from the antiderivative table — verify it using the sin substitution.) Let x = sinθ, dx = cosθ dθ. √(1−x²) = cosθ. \arcsin x + C  x = \sin\theta,\; dx = \cos\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{1-x^2}=\cos\theta Standard sin substitution with a = 1. \int \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta}d\theta = \int d\theta = \theta + C = \arcsin x + C The integral is trivially θ, which equals arcsin(x).  Evaluate ∫₀² √(4+x²) dx. Let x = 2tanθ. Change limits: x=0 → θ=0, x=2 → θ=π/4. You will need ∫sec³θ dθ from Lesson 8. 2\sqrt{2}+2\ln(1+\sqrt{2}) \approx 4.591  x = 2\tan\theta,\; dx = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{4+x^2} = 2\sec\theta Case 2 with a = 2. \int_0^{\pi/4} 2\sec\theta\cdot 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta = 4\int_0^{\pi/4}\sec^3\theta\,d\theta Limits: x=0→θ=0, x=2→θ=π/4. \int \sec^3\theta\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2}[\sec\theta\tan\theta + \ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|] From Lesson 8. = 4\cdot\frac{1}{2}[\sec\theta\tan\theta+\ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|]_0^{\pi/4} Evaluate at limits. = 2[(\sqrt{2})(1)+\ln(\sqrt{2}+1) - (1)(0) - \ln(1+0)] At θ=π/4: secθ=√2, tanθ=1. At θ=0: secθ=1, tanθ=0. = 2[\sqrt{2}+\ln(1+\sqrt{2})] ln 1 = 0.  Find ∫x³/√(x²+4) dx using trig substitution. Then verify by using the algebraic substitution u = x²+4 instead. Trig: x = 2tanθ. Algebraic: u = x²+4, then x² = u−4 and x dx = du/2. \frac{(x^2-8)\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}+C  \text{Trig sub: } x=2\tan\theta,\; x^3 = 8\tan^3\theta,\; \sqrt{x^2+4}=2\sec\theta,\; dx=2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta Set up. \int \frac{8\tan^3\theta}{2\sec\theta}\cdot 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta = 8\int \tan^3\theta\sec\theta\,d\theta Simplify: 8·2/(2)·sec = 8 tanθ³ secθ. = 8\int \tan^2\theta\cdot\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta = 8\int(\sec^2\theta-1)\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta Write tan²θ = sec²θ−1, save secθ tanθ for du. u=\sec\theta: 8\int(u^2-1)du = 8(u^3/3-u)+C = \frac{8}{3}\sec^3\theta - 8\sec\theta + C Integrate. \sec\theta = \sqrt{x^2+4}/2: \; = \frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{(x^2+4)^{3/2}}{8} - 8\cdot\frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{2}+C Back-substitute. = \frac{(x^2+4)^{3/2}}{3} - 4\sqrt{x^2+4} + C = \frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}(x^2+4-12)+C = \frac{(x^2-8)\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}+C Factor and simplify.
documents.proofs:
The validity of trig substitution rests on the invertibility of the substitution on the relevant domain. For $x = a\sin\theta$ with $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, the function $\sin$ is a bijection from $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ to $[-a, a]$ (after scaling by $a$). This ensures that $\theta = \arcsin(x/a)$ is well-defined, and the back-substitution is unambiguous. For $x = a\tan\theta$ with $\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, $\tan$ is a bijection from $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ to $(-\infty, \infty)$, so $\theta = \arctan(x/a)$ is always well-defined. For $x = a\sec\theta$, the situation is slightly more delicate: $\sec$ on $[0, \pi/2)$ maps to $[1, \infty)$, and on $(\pi/2, \pi]$ maps to $(-\infty, -1]$. We choose the branch matching the sign of $x$. The substitution theorem for definite integrals guarantees that $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} f(g(\theta))g'(\theta)\,d\theta$ when $x = g(\theta)$ is $C^1$ with $g(\alpha) = a$ and $g(\beta) = b$. The requirement that $g$ be $C^1$ is satisfied by $a\sin\theta$, $a\tan\theta$, and $a\sec\theta$ on their respective domains. The theorem does NOT require $g$ to be one-to-one for definite integrals (the formula holds regardless), but one-to-one ensures the back-substitution for indefinite integrals is well-defined. Geometric verification for the semicircle: $\int_{-a}^{a} \sqrt{a^2-x^2}\,dx$ should equal $\pi a^2/2$ (area of a semicircle). With $x = a\sin\theta$: the integral becomes $\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} a\cos\theta \cdot a\cos\theta\,d\theta = a^2 \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos^2\theta\,d\theta = a^2 \cdot \pi/2 = \pi a^2/2$. The trig substitution method correctly reproduces the geometric result. Domain Restrictions Ensure Well-Defined Back-Substitution For \(x = a\sin\theta\): restrict \(\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]\) so \(\cos\theta \geq 0\) and \(\theta = \arcsin(x/a)\) is unique.
For \(x = a\tan\theta\): restrict \(\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)\) so \(\sec\theta > 0\) and \(\theta = \arctan(x/a)\).
For \(x = a\sec\theta\): choose the branch matching \(\text{sgn}(x)\).
documents.applications:
Trig substitution appears whenever geometry meets calculus. Computing the area of an ellipse (∫√(a²−x²) dx) uses the sin substitution and yields πab. Gravitational and electric field calculations involve integrals with √(x²+a²) denominators. Orbital mechanics integrates √(r²−b²) terms for trajectory computations. The arc length of a parabola requires ∫√(1+4x²) dx — a tan substitution. In statistics, normalizing the t-distribution uses ∫(1+x²/n)^(−(n+1)/2) dx, simplified by trig substitution. Which Substitution to Use • √(a² − x²) → x = a sin θ (think: Pythagorean identity sin²+cos²=1)
• √(a² + x²) → x = a tan θ (think: 1+tan²=sec²)
• √(x² − a²) → x = a sec θ (think: sec²−1=tan²)
Mnemonic: the sign between a² and x² determines the identity. Always Draw the Reference Triangle After integrating in θ, you must convert back to x. Draw the right triangle: label the sides based on your substitution (e.g., for x = a sin θ: opp = x, hyp = a, adj = √(a²−x²)). Read off cos θ, tan θ, etc. from the triangle. This avoids algebraic errors in back-substitution. Trig Sub Is Not Just for Radicals Trig substitution also works for expressions like 1/(a²+x²)^n without a visible radical. For instance, ∫dx/(1+x²)² is solved by x = tan θ, giving ∫cos²θ dθ. Any expression involving a²±x² or x²−a² (even without a √) can potentially benefit from trig sub. Completing the Square If the expression under the radical is ax² + bx + c, complete the square first: a(x + b/2a)² + (c − b²/4a). Then let u = x + b/(2a) to put it in standard form (u² ± k²) before applying trig substitution.
documents.content:
How do you integrate √(4 − x²)? No algebraic trick or u-substitution removes the square root. But if you let x = 2 sin θ, then √(4 − x²) = √(4 − 4sin²θ) = 2cos θ — the radical vanishes! The integral becomes a trig integral, which you already know how to solve. Trigonometric substitution is a technique that uses the Pythagorean identity to eliminate radicals. Trig substitution appears whenever geometry meets calculus. Computing the area of an ellipse (∫√(a²−x²) dx) uses the sin substitution and yields πab. Gravitational and electric field calculations involve integrals with √(x²+a²) denominators. Orbital mechanics integrates √(r²−b²) terms for trajectory computations. The arc length of a parabola requires ∫√(1+4x²) dx — a tan substitution. In statistics, normalizing the t-distribution uses ∫(1+x²/n)^(−(n+1)/2) dx, simplified by trig substitution. The idea behind trig substitution is beautifully geometric. Consider $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$. This expression describes the height of a semicircle of radius $a$ at horizontal position $x$. On the unit circle, $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$, which means $\cos\theta = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta}$. If we set $x = a\sin\theta$, then $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2} = \sqrt{a^2 - a^2\sin^2\theta} = a\cos\theta$. The radical is gone — replaced by a trig function. There are three cases, one for each Pythagorean identity. For $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$: use $x = a\sin\theta$ (from $1 - \sin^2\theta = \cos^2\theta$). For $\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$: use $x = a\tan\theta$ (from $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$). For $\sqrt{x^2 - a^2}$: use $x = a\sec\theta$ (from $\sec^2\theta - 1 = \tan^2\theta$). Each substitution transforms a radical into a simple trig function. The reference triangle is your best friend for converting back to $x$. After integrating in $\theta$, you need to express the answer in terms of $x$. Draw a right triangle where the sides are determined by the substitution. For $x = a\sin\theta$: opposite side $= x$, hypotenuse $= a$, so adjacent side $= \sqrt{a^2-x^2}$. Read off any trig function of $\theta$ directly from the triangle: $\cos\theta = \sqrt{a^2-x^2}/a$, $\tan\theta = x/\sqrt{a^2-x^2}$, etc. Do not forget to substitute $dx$ as well. If $x = a\sin\theta$, then $dx = a\cos\theta\,d\theta$. This extra factor is essential — it changes the differential and often simplifies nicely with the radical. The combination of eliminating the radical AND transforming $dx$ usually converts the integral into a standard trig integral from Lesson 8. Sometimes you need to complete the square before applying trig substitution. For $\int dx/\sqrt{x^2 + 6x + 13}$: complete the square to get $x^2 + 6x + 13 = (x+3)^2 + 4$. Now let $u = x + 3$, and the integral becomes $\int du/\sqrt{u^2+4}$, which is a standard tan substitution with $u = 2\tan\theta$. A common error is forgetting the domain restrictions on $\theta$. For $x = a\sin\theta$, we restrict $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ so that $\cos\theta \geq 0$ and $\sqrt{a^2-x^2} = a\cos\theta$ (not $-a\cos\theta$). For $x = a\tan\theta$, restrict $\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ so that $\sec\theta > 0$. For $x = a\sec\theta$, restrict to $\theta \in [0, \pi/2) \cup [\pi, 3\pi/2)$ depending on the sign of $x$. For definite integrals, you can either convert the limits to $\theta$-values (often cleaner) or integrate in $\theta$, convert back to $x$ using the reference triangle, and evaluate at the original $x$-limits. Converting limits: if $x$ goes from $0$ to $a$ and $x = a\sin\theta$, then $\theta$ goes from $0$ to $\pi/2$. Case 1: $\sqrt{a^2 - x^2}$. Let $x = a\sin\theta$, $dx = a\cos\theta\,d\theta$, $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. Then $\sqrt{a^2-x^2} = a\cos\theta$. Reference triangle: hyp $= a$, opp $= x$, adj $= \sqrt{a^2-x^2}$. Case 2: $\sqrt{a^2 + x^2}$. Let $x = a\tan\theta$, $dx = a\sec^2\theta\,d\theta$, $\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$. Then $\sqrt{a^2+x^2} = a\sec\theta$. Reference triangle: adj $= a$, opp $= x$, hyp $= \sqrt{a^2+x^2}$. Case 3: $\sqrt{x^2 - a^2}$. Let $x = a\sec\theta$, $dx = a\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta$, $\theta \in [0, \pi/2)$ for $x > a$. Then $\sqrt{x^2-a^2} = a\tan\theta$. Reference triangle: hyp $= x$, adj $= a$, opp $= \sqrt{x^2-a^2}$. After the substitution, the integral becomes a trig integral in $\theta$. Evaluate using the techniques of Lesson 8 (powers of sin, cos, tan, sec). Then use the reference triangle to convert the answer back to $x$. Definite integrals: either (a) convert $x$-limits to $\theta$-limits and evaluate directly in $\theta$, or (b) convert the antiderivative back to $x$ first, then evaluate at the original limits. Option (a) avoids back-substitution entirely and is usually preferred. The validity of trig substitution rests on the invertibility of the substitution on the relevant domain. For $x = a\sin\theta$ with $\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]$, the function $\sin$ is a bijection from $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$ to $[-a, a]$ (after scaling by $a$). This ensures that $\theta = \arcsin(x/a)$ is well-defined, and the back-substitution is unambiguous. For $x = a\tan\theta$ with $\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, $\tan$ is a bijection from $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ to $(-\infty, \infty)$, so $\theta = \arctan(x/a)$ is always well-defined. For $x = a\sec\theta$, the situation is slightly more delicate: $\sec$ on $[0, \pi/2)$ maps to $[1, \infty)$, and on $(\pi/2, \pi]$ maps to $(-\infty, -1]$. We choose the branch matching the sign of $x$. The substitution theorem for definite integrals guarantees that $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \int_{\alpha}^{\beta} f(g(\theta))g'(\theta)\,d\theta$ when $x = g(\theta)$ is $C^1$ with $g(\alpha) = a$ and $g(\beta) = b$. The requirement that $g$ be $C^1$ is satisfied by $a\sin\theta$, $a\tan\theta$, and $a\sec\theta$ on their respective domains. The theorem does NOT require $g$ to be one-to-one for definite integrals (the formula holds regardless), but one-to-one ensures the back-substitution for indefinite integrals is well-defined. Geometric verification for the semicircle: $\int_{-a}^{a} \sqrt{a^2-x^2}\,dx$ should equal $\pi a^2/2$ (area of a semicircle). With $x = a\sin\theta$: the integral becomes $\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} a\cos\theta \cdot a\cos\theta\,d\theta = a^2 \int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} \cos^2\theta\,d\theta = a^2 \cdot \pi/2 = \pi a^2/2$. The trig substitution method correctly reproduces the geometric result. Domain Restrictions Ensure Well-Defined Back-Substitution For \(x = a\sin\theta\): restrict \(\theta \in [-\pi/2, \pi/2]\) so \(\cos\theta \geq 0\) and \(\theta = \arcsin(x/a)\) is unique.
For \(x = a\tan\theta\): restrict \(\theta \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)\) so \(\sec\theta > 0\) and \(\theta = \arctan(x/a)\).
For \(x = a\sec\theta\): choose the branch matching \(\text{sgn}(x)\). Which Substitution to Use • √(a² − x²) → x = a sin θ (think: Pythagorean identity sin²+cos²=1)
• √(a² + x²) → x = a tan θ (think: 1+tan²=sec²)
• √(x² − a²) → x = a sec θ (think: sec²−1=tan²)
Mnemonic: the sign between a² and x² determines the identity. Always Draw the Reference Triangle After integrating in θ, you must convert back to x. Draw the right triangle: label the sides based on your substitution (e.g., for x = a sin θ: opp = x, hyp = a, adj = √(a²−x²)). Read off cos θ, tan θ, etc. from the triangle. This avoids algebraic errors in back-substitution. Trig Sub Is Not Just for Radicals Trig substitution also works for expressions like 1/(a²+x²)^n without a visible radical. For instance, ∫dx/(1+x²)² is solved by x = tan θ, giving ∫cos²θ dθ. Any expression involving a²±x² or x²−a² (even without a √) can potentially benefit from trig sub. Completing the Square If the expression under the radical is ax² + bx + c, complete the square first: a(x + b/2a)² + (c − b²/4a). Then let u = x + b/(2a) to put it in standard form (u² ± k²) before applying trig substitution. Three Trig Substitutions \(\sqrt{a^2-x^2}\): let \(x = a\sin\theta\), get \(a\cos\theta\)
\(\sqrt{a^2+x^2}\): let \(x = a\tan\theta\), get \(a\sec\theta\)
\(\sqrt{x^2-a^2}\): let \(x = a\sec\theta\), get \(a\tan\theta\) Complete the Square First Before applying trig sub, put the expression in standard form. Example: \(\sqrt{-x^2+4x+5} = \sqrt{9-(x-2)^2}\). Let \(u = x-2\), then \(u = 3\sin\theta\). Method Selection Trainer Mark radical patterns like a^2-x^2 or a^2+x^2 and compare trig substitution against competing techniques. Trig Substitution Reference Triangles Three reference triangles for the three substitution types. For x = a sin θ: the triangle has hypotenuse a, opposite side x, adjacent side √(a²−x²). All trig functions of θ can be read directly from the triangle, enabling back-substitution without memorization. Semicircle Area: ∫√(4−x²) dx \text{Find } \int \sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx. ∫√(4−x²) dx = 2 arcsin(x/2) + x√(4−x²)/2 + C. Evaluating from −2 to 2 gives 2π — the area of a semicircle of radius 2. \sqrt{a^2-x^2} \text{ form with } a=2. \text{ Let } x = 2\sin\theta, \; dx = 2\cos\theta\,d\theta Case 1 substitution. \sqrt{4-x^2} = \sqrt{4-4\sin^2\theta} = 2\cos\theta The radical becomes 2cosθ. \int \sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx = \int 2\cos\theta \cdot 2\cos\theta\,d\theta = 4\int \cos^2\theta\,d\theta Substitute everything. = 4\cdot\frac{1}{2}(\theta + \sin\theta\cos\theta) + C = 2\theta + 2\sin\theta\cos\theta + C ∫cos²θ dθ = θ/2 + sin(2θ)/4 = (θ + sinθ cosθ)/2. \text{Back-sub: } \theta = \arcsin(x/2),\; \sin\theta = x/2,\; \cos\theta = \sqrt{4-x^2}/2 Use the reference triangle: opp = x, hyp = 2, adj = √(4−x²). = 2\arcsin\frac{x}{2} + 2\cdot\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{4-x^2}}{2} + C = 2\arcsin\frac{x}{2} + \frac{x\sqrt{4-x^2}}{2} + C Final answer in terms of x. ∫dx/(x²+1)^(3/2) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{(x^2+1)^{3/2}}. ∫dx/(x²+1)^(3/2) = x/√(x²+1) + C. The tan substitution turned a formidable-looking integral into ∫cosθ dθ. \sqrt{x^2+a^2} \text{ with } a=1. \text{ Let } x = \tan\theta, \; dx = \sec^2\theta\,d\theta Case 2 substitution. x^2+1 = \tan^2\theta+1 = \sec^2\theta, \; (x^2+1)^{3/2} = \sec^3\theta Simplify the denominator. \int \frac{\sec^2\theta}{\sec^3\theta}\,d\theta = \int \frac{d\theta}{\sec\theta} = \int \cos\theta\,d\theta Cancel sec²θ/sec³θ = 1/secθ = cosθ. = \sin\theta + C ∫cosθ dθ = sinθ + C. \text{Reference triangle: adj}=1, \text{ opp}=x, \text{ hyp}=\sqrt{x^2+1}. \; \sin\theta = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} Read sinθ from the triangle. = \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+1}} + C Final answer. ∫x²/√(9−x²) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^2}{\sqrt{9-x^2}}\,dx. ∫x²/√(9−x²) dx = (9/2)arcsin(x/3) − x√(9−x²)/2 + C. \text{Let } x = 3\sin\theta, \; dx = 3\cos\theta\,d\theta, \; \sqrt{9-x^2} = 3\cos\theta Case 1 with a = 3. \int \frac{9\sin^2\theta}{3\cos\theta}\cdot 3\cos\theta\,d\theta = 9\int \sin^2\theta\,d\theta The cosθ factors cancel. = 9\cdot\frac{1}{2}(\theta - \sin\theta\cos\theta) + C ∫sin²θ dθ = θ/2 − sin(2θ)/4 = (θ − sinθ cosθ)/2. = \frac{9}{2}\arcsin\frac{x}{3} - \frac{9}{2}\cdot\frac{x}{3}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{9-x^2}}{3} + C Back-sub: θ = arcsin(x/3), sinθ = x/3, cosθ = √(9−x²)/3. = \frac{9}{2}\arcsin\frac{x}{3} - \frac{x\sqrt{9-x^2}}{2} + C Simplify: 9/(2·9) = 1/2. ∫dx/√(x²−9) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2-9}} \text{ for } x > 3. ∫dx/√(x²−9) = ln|x + √(x²−9)| + C. This is equivalent to arccosh(x/3) + C. \text{Let } x = 3\sec\theta, \; dx = 3\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta, \; \sqrt{x^2-9} = 3\tan\theta Case 3 with a = 3. \int \frac{3\sec\theta\tan\theta}{3\tan\theta}\,d\theta = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta The 3tanθ factors cancel. = \ln|\sec\theta + \tan\theta| + C Standard integral of secθ. \text{Reference triangle: hyp}=x,\text{ adj}=3,\text{ opp}=\sqrt{x^2-9} secθ = x/3, tanθ = √(x²−9)/3. = \ln\left|\frac{x}{3}+\frac{\sqrt{x^2-9}}{3}\right| + C = \ln\left|x+\sqrt{x^2-9}\right| - \ln 3 + C Simplify. The −ln 3 can be absorbed into C. = \ln\left|x+\sqrt{x^2-9}\right| + C Final simplified form. Completing the Square: ∫dx/√(x²+4x+8) \text{Find } \int \frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2+4x+8}}. ∫dx/√(x²+4x+8) = ln|x+2+√(x²+4x+8)| + C. Completing the square is the essential first step when the quadratic has a linear term. x^2+4x+8 = (x+2)^2 + 4 Complete the square: x²+4x+4+4 = (x+2)²+4. \text{Let } u = x+2,\; du = dx. \text{ Then } \int\frac{du}{\sqrt{u^2+4}} Shift to standard form. \text{Let } u = 2\tan\theta,\; du = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{u^2+4} = 2\sec\theta Case 2 with a = 2. \int\frac{2\sec^2\theta}{2\sec\theta}d\theta = \int \sec\theta\,d\theta = \ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|+C Simplify and integrate. \sec\theta = \frac{\sqrt{u^2+4}}{2},\; \tan\theta = \frac{u}{2} = \frac{x+2}{2} Back-substitute using the reference triangle. = \ln\left|\frac{\sqrt{(x+2)^2+4}}{2}+\frac{x+2}{2}\right|+C = \ln|x+2+\sqrt{x^2+4x+8}|+C Simplify, absorbing −ln 2 into C. Find ∫dx/√(1−x²). (You should recognize this from the antiderivative table — verify it using the sin substitution.) Let x = sinθ, dx = cosθ dθ. √(1−x²) = cosθ. \arcsin x + C x = \sin\theta,\; dx = \cos\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{1-x^2}=\cos\theta Standard sin substitution with a = 1. \int \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta}d\theta = \int d\theta = \theta + C = \arcsin x + C The integral is trivially θ, which equals arcsin(x). Evaluate ∫₀² √(4+x²) dx. Let x = 2tanθ. Change limits: x=0 → θ=0, x=2 → θ=π/4. You will need ∫sec³θ dθ from Lesson 8. 2\sqrt{2}+2\ln(1+\sqrt{2}) \approx 4.591 x = 2\tan\theta,\; dx = 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta,\; \sqrt{4+x^2} = 2\sec\theta Case 2 with a = 2. \int_0^{\pi/4} 2\sec\theta\cdot 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta = 4\int_0^{\pi/4}\sec^3\theta\,d\theta Limits: x=0→θ=0, x=2→θ=π/4. \int \sec^3\theta\,d\theta = \frac{1}{2}[\sec\theta\tan\theta + \ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|] From Lesson 8. = 4\cdot\frac{1}{2}[\sec\theta\tan\theta+\ln|\sec\theta+\tan\theta|]_0^{\pi/4} Evaluate at limits. = 2[(\sqrt{2})(1)+\ln(\sqrt{2}+1) - (1)(0) - \ln(1+0)] At θ=π/4: secθ=√2, tanθ=1. At θ=0: secθ=1, tanθ=0. = 2[\sqrt{2}+\ln(1+\sqrt{2})] ln 1 = 0. Find ∫x³/√(x²+4) dx using trig substitution. Then verify by using the algebraic substitution u = x²+4 instead. Trig: x = 2tanθ. Algebraic: u = x²+4, then x² = u−4 and x dx = du/2. \frac{(x^2-8)\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}+C \text{Trig sub: } x=2\tan\theta,\; x^3 = 8\tan^3\theta,\; \sqrt{x^2+4}=2\sec\theta,\; dx=2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta Set up. \int \frac{8\tan^3\theta}{2\sec\theta}\cdot 2\sec^2\theta\,d\theta = 8\int \tan^3\theta\sec\theta\,d\theta Simplify: 8·2/(2)·sec = 8 tanθ³ secθ. = 8\int \tan^2\theta\cdot\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta = 8\int(\sec^2\theta-1)\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta Write tan²θ = sec²θ−1, save secθ tanθ for du. u=\sec\theta: 8\int(u^2-1)du = 8(u^3/3-u)+C = \frac{8}{3}\sec^3\theta - 8\sec\theta + C Integrate. \sec\theta = \sqrt{x^2+4}/2: \; = \frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{(x^2+4)^{3/2}}{8} - 8\cdot\frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{2}+C Back-substitute. = \frac{(x^2+4)^{3/2}}{3} - 4\sqrt{x^2+4} + C = \frac{\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}(x^2+4-12)+C = \frac{(x^2-8)\sqrt{x^2+4}}{3}+C Factor and simplify. Trig Integrals After trig substitution, you always face a trig integral. Master Lesson 8 first. U-Substitution Sometimes an algebraic u-sub is simpler than trig sub — always consider both approaches. Trig Review The three Pythagorean identities are the foundation of trig substitution. Improper Integrals Trig substitution often appears in improper integrals involving radicals. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step trig sine cosine tangent unit circle

documents.title: Partial Fraction Decomposition
documents.subtitle: Breaking rational functions into simple pieces for integration
documents.tags: partial fractions rational functions Heaviside cover-up decomposition linear factors integration technique
documents.assignment: Distinct Linear: ∫(2x+1)/((x−1)(x+2)) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx.   ∫(2x+1)/((x−1)(x+2)) dx = ln|(x−1)(x+2)| + C. Heaviside cover-up makes finding A and B almost instant. \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)} = \frac{A}{x-1} + \frac{B}{x+2} Set up the partial fraction form: distinct linear factors. \text{Heaviside: } A = \frac{2(1)+1}{1+2} = \frac{3}{3} = 1 Cover up (x−1), evaluate at x = 1. B = \frac{2(-2)+1}{-2-1} = \frac{-3}{-3} = 1 Cover up (x+2), evaluate at x = −2. \int \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx = \int\frac{1}{x-1}dx + \int\frac{1}{x+2}dx Both coefficients are 1. = \ln|x-1| + \ln|x+2| + C = \ln|(x-1)(x+2)| + C Each integrates to a logarithm. Combine using ln a + ln b = ln(ab). Repeated Factor: ∫x/((x−1)²(x+1)) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)}\,dx.   ∫x/((x−1)²(x+1)) dx = (1/4)ln|(x−1)/(x+1)| − 1/(2(x−1)) + C. \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1} + \frac{B}{(x-1)^2} + \frac{C}{x+1} Repeated factor (x−1)² requires TWO terms. \text{Heaviside for } B: \text{cover } (x-1)^2, \text{ eval at } x=1: \; B = \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2} B is found directly. \text{Heaviside for } C: \text{cover } (x+1), \text{ eval at } x=-1: \; C = \frac{-1}{(-1-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{4} C is found directly. \text{For } A: \text{multiply out and compare. Set } x=0: \; \frac{0}{(−1)^2(1)} = A(−1)(1)+B(1)+C(−1)^2 Substitute a convenient value x = 0. 0 = -A + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{-1}{4} = -A + \frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow A = \frac{1}{4} Solve for A. \int \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)}dx = \frac{1}{4}\ln|x-1| - \frac{1}{2(x-1)} - \frac{1}{4}\ln|x+1| + C ∫A/(x−1) = A ln|x−1|; ∫B/(x−1)² = −B/(x−1); ∫C/(x+1) = C ln|x+1|. = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right| - \frac{1}{2(x-1)} + C Combine logarithms. Irreducible Quadratic: ∫(x²+1)/(x³+x) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^2+1}{x^3+x}\,dx.   ∫(x²+1)/(x³+x) dx = ln|x| + C. Lesson: always check for cancellation before setting up partial fractions. x^3+x = x(x^2+1) Factor the denominator. x²+1 is irreducible (discriminant = −4 < 0). \frac{x^2+1}{x(x^2+1)} = \frac{1}{x} Wait — x²+1 cancels! The fraction simplifies before decomposition. \int \frac{x^2+1}{x^3+x}\,dx = \int \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln|x| + C Always simplify first. True Quadratic: ∫(3x+5)/(x²+2x+5) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{3x+5}{x^2+2x+5}\,dx.   ∫(3x+5)/(x²+2x+5) dx = (3/2)ln(x²+2x+5) + arctan((x+1)/2) + C. The split into "derivative of denominator" and "constant over denominator" is the standard technique for irreducible quadratics. x^2+2x+5 = (x+1)^2+4 Complete the square. Discriminant = 4−20 < 0, so irreducible. 3x+5 = 3(x+1)+2 Rewrite numerator in terms of (x+1) to match the completed square. \int \frac{3(x+1)}{(x+1)^2+4}dx + \int \frac{2}{(x+1)^2+4}dx Split into two integrals. \text{First: } u=(x+1)^2+4,\; du=2(x+1)dx: \; \frac{3}{2}\ln[(x+1)^2+4] U-sub gives a logarithm. \text{Second: } \frac{2}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{1}\arctan\frac{x+1}{2} = \arctan\frac{x+1}{2} ∫du/(u²+a²) = (1/a)arctan(u/a). Here a = 2: 2·(1/2)arctan((x+1)/2). = \frac{3}{2}\ln(x^2+2x+5) + \arctan\frac{x+1}{2} + C Combine. Note: (x+1)²+4 = x²+2x+5 is always positive, no absolute value needed. Long Division First: ∫(x³−2x+3)/(x²−1) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^3-2x+3}{x^2-1}\,dx.   ∫(x³−2x+3)/(x²−1) dx = x²/2 + ln|x−1| − 2ln|x+1| + C. Always do long division when the numerator degree ≥ denominator degree. \frac{x^3-2x+3}{x^2-1} = x + \frac{-x+3}{x^2-1} Degree of numerator (3) ≥ degree of denominator (2). Long divide: x³ ÷ (x²−1) = x remainder −x+3. \frac{-x+3}{(x-1)(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+1} Factor x²−1 and decompose the proper fraction. A = \frac{-1+3}{1+1} = 1, \quad B = \frac{1+3}{-1-1} = -2 Heaviside cover-up. \int\left(x + \frac{1}{x-1} - \frac{2}{x+1}\right)dx Three simple integrals. = \frac{x^2}{2} + \ln|x-1| - 2\ln|x+1| + C Integrate each term.  Find ∫dx/((x+1)(x+3)) using the Heaviside cover-up method. A = 1/(1+3−1+1)... actually: cover (x+1), eval at x = −1: A = 1/(−1+3) = 1/2. Cover (x+3), eval at x = −3: B = 1/(−3+1) = −1/2. \frac{1}{2}\ln\left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+C  \frac{1}{(x+1)(x+3)} = \frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x+3} Standard form for distinct linear factors. A = \frac{1}{-1+3} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad B = \frac{1}{-3+1} = -\frac{1}{2} Cover-up method. \int \frac{dx}{(x+1)(x+3)} = \frac{1}{2}\ln|x+1|-\frac{1}{2}\ln|x+3|+C = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+C Integrate and combine logs.  Find ∫(2x²+3x+1)/(x³+x²) dx. Factor: x³+x² = x²(x+1). Repeated factor x² needs terms A/x + B/x² + C/(x+1). 2\ln|x|-\frac{1}{x}+C  \frac{2x^2+3x+1}{x^2(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x^2}+\frac{C}{x+1} Set up with repeated linear factor x². B:\text{ cover } x^2, x=0: B = \frac{1}{0+1} = 1. \quad C:\text{ cover } (x+1), x=-1: C = \frac{2-3+1}{(-1)^2} = 0 Heaviside for B and C. \text{For } A: \text{multiply by } x^2(x+1): 2x^2+3x+1 = Ax(x+1)+1\cdot(x+1)+0\cdot x^2 Substitute known B=1, C=0. \text{Compare } x^2: 2 = A. \text{ Check } x^1: 3 = A+1 = 3.\checkmark A = 2 confirmed. \int\left(\frac{2}{x}+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)dx = 2\ln|x|-\frac{1}{x}+C Integrate (C=0 term vanishes).  Find ∫(x²+2)/((x−1)(x²+x+1)) dx. Note that x³−1 = (x−1)(x²+x+1). Decompose into A/(x−1) + (Bx+C)/(x²+x+1). For the quadratic integral, complete the square: x²+x+1 = (x+1/2)² + 3/4. \ln|x-1|-\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}\arctan\frac{2x+1}{\sqrt{3}}+C  \frac{x^2+2}{(x-1)(x^2+x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{Bx+C}{x^2+x+1} x²+x+1 is irreducible (discriminant = 1−4 = −3 < 0). A = \frac{1+2}{1+1+1} = 1 Cover up (x−1), evaluate at x = 1. \text{Multiply out: } x^2+2 = 1\cdot(x^2+x+1)+(Bx+C)(x-1) Expand right side. x^2+2 = x^2+x+1+Bx^2-Bx+Cx-C = (1+B)x^2+(1-B+C)x+(1-C) Collect terms. x^2: 1=1+B \Rightarrow B=0. \quad x^0: 2=1-C \Rightarrow C=-1. \quad \text{Check } x^1: 0=1-0-1=0.\checkmark Compare coefficients. \int\frac{1}{x-1}dx + \int\frac{-1}{x^2+x+1}dx = \ln|x-1| - \int\frac{dx}{(x+1/2)^2+3/4} Complete the square: x²+x+1 = (x+1/2)² + 3/4. = \ln|x-1| - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}/2}\arctan\frac{x+1/2}{\sqrt{3}/2} + C = \ln|x-1|-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\arctan\frac{2x+1}{\sqrt{3}}+C Apply ∫du/(u²+a²) = (1/a)arctan(u/a).
documents.proofs: The existence and uniqueness of partial fraction decomposition is guaranteed by algebra. Over a field $F$, the ring of polynomials $F[x]$ is a principal ideal domain, and partial fraction decomposition follows from the Chinese Remainder Theorem for $F[x]$-modules. In concrete terms: if $Q(x) = Q_1(x)Q_2(x)$ with $\gcd(Q_1, Q_2) = 1$, then for any $P$ with $\deg P < \deg Q$, there exist unique $P_1, P_2$ with $\deg P_i < \deg Q_i$ such that $P/Q = P_1/Q_1 + P_2/Q_2$. The proof of the Heaviside cover-up formula: $A_k = \lim_{x \to r_k}(x-r_k)\cdot P(x)/Q(x)$. Since $Q(x)$ has a simple root at $r_k$, write $Q(x) = (x-r_k)\tilde{Q}(x)$ where $\tilde{Q}(r_k) \neq 0$. Then $(x-r_k)P(x)/Q(x) = P(x)/\tilde{Q}(x) \to P(r_k)/\tilde{Q}(r_k)$ as $x \to r_k$. This equals the residue of $P/Q$ at $r_k$ — the same concept used in complex analysis for contour integrals. For repeated roots, the generalization uses derivatives: if $Q(x) = (x-r)^m \tilde{Q}(x)$, define $\phi(x) = (x-r)^m P(x)/Q(x) = P(x)/\tilde{Q}(x)$. Then $A_m = \phi(r)$, $A_{m-1} = \phi'(r)/1!$, $A_{m-2} = \phi''(r)/2!$, and in general $A_{m-j} = \phi^{(j)}(r)/j!$. This is the Taylor expansion of $\phi$ at $r$. The fact that every real polynomial factors into linear and irreducible quadratic factors follows from the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (every polynomial of degree $n$ has exactly $n$ complex roots, counted with multiplicity). Complex roots of a real polynomial come in conjugate pairs $\alpha \pm \beta i$. Each pair gives a real quadratic factor $(x-\alpha)^2+\beta^2 = x^2-2\alpha x+\alpha^2+\beta^2$, which is irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$ (its discriminant $4\alpha^2-4(\alpha^2+\beta^2) = -4\beta^2 < 0$). Existence and Uniqueness For any proper rational function \(P(x)/Q(x)\), the partial fraction decomposition exists and is unique. This follows from the Chinese Remainder Theorem in the polynomial ring \(\mathbb{R}[x]\).
documents.applications: Partial fractions are essential in engineering. In control theory, the transfer function of a system H(s) = P(s)/Q(s) is decomposed into partial fractions to find the inverse Laplace transform — this is how engineers determine the time-domain response of circuits, mechanical systems, and feedback controllers. In signal processing, partial fractions decompose frequency-domain filters into first-order components. In pharmacokinetics, drug concentration models with multiple compartments produce rational functions of the Laplace variable, and partial fractions give the time-domain solution. The technique also appears in probability (generating functions) and number theory (formal power series). When to Use Partial Fractions Use partial fractions when the integrand is a rational function P(x)/Q(x) (polynomial over polynomial) and Q(x) can be factored. If the degree of P ≥ degree of Q, do long division first. This technique works for ALL rational functions — it is a complete method. Polynomial Long Division If deg(P) ≥ deg(Q), divide P by Q first. Example: (x³+2)/(x²−1) = x + remainder (x+2)/(x²−1). Only the proper fraction (x+2)/(x²−1) needs partial fraction decomposition. The polynomial part integrates directly. Factor Completely Before Decomposing Partial fractions require a fully factored denominator. x⁴−1 = (x²+1)(x−1)(x+1), not just (x²−1)(x²+1). If you miss a factorization, you will set up the wrong form and get stuck. Always check: can any quadratic factor be factored further? (Test discriminant: b²−4c < 0 means irreducible.) Laplace Transforms and Circuit Analysis In electrical engineering, the response of an RLC circuit to a step input is found by (1) writing the transfer function H(s) = 1/(s²+s+1), (2) decomposing into partial fractions (with complex or irreducible quadratic terms), and (3) inverse-transforming each piece. Partial fractions convert one hard transform into several easy ones. Repeated Factors Need Multiple Terms For (x−1)³ in the denominator, you need THREE terms: A/(x−1) + B/(x−1)² + C/(x−1)³. A common error is writing only A/(x−1)³. This gives too few unknowns and the decomposition fails.
documents.content:
How do you integrate 1/((x−1)(x+2))? Neither u-sub nor by-parts helps. But if you write 1/((x−1)(x+2)) = A/(x−1) + B/(x+2) for suitable constants A and B, each piece integrates to a logarithm. Partial fraction decomposition breaks any rational function into a sum of simple fractions, each of which has a known antiderivative. It is the algebraic key to integrating all rational functions. Partial fractions are essential in engineering. In control theory, the transfer function of a system H(s) = P(s)/Q(s) is decomposed into partial fractions to find the inverse Laplace transform — this is how engineers determine the time-domain response of circuits, mechanical systems, and feedback controllers. In signal processing, partial fractions decompose frequency-domain filters into first-order components. In pharmacokinetics, drug concentration models with multiple compartments produce rational functions of the Laplace variable, and partial fractions give the time-domain solution. The technique also appears in probability (generating functions) and number theory (formal power series). You already know how to add fractions: $1/(x-1) + 1/(x+2) = ((x+2)+(x-1))/((x-1)(x+2)) = (2x+1)/((x-1)(x+2))$. Partial fraction decomposition is this process in REVERSE. Given a complicated fraction like $(2x+1)/((x-1)(x+2))$, we break it back into $A/(x-1) + B/(x+2)$. Each simple fraction is easy to integrate: $\int A/(x-1)\,dx = A\ln|x-1| + C$. The method works because rational functions (polynomial over polynomial) can always be decomposed into a polynomial part (if the degree of the numerator is $\geq$ the degree of the denominator) plus a sum of "partial fractions" — terms of the form $A/(x-a)^k$ for real roots and $(Bx+C)/(x^2+bx+c)^k$ for irreducible quadratic factors. Each type has a known antiderivative. The first step is always: check degrees. If the numerator degree $\geq$ denominator degree, perform polynomial long division first to get a polynomial plus a proper fraction (numerator degree $<$ denominator degree). Only proper fractions get decomposed. For example, $\int (x^3+2)/(x^2-1)\,dx$: divide to get $x + (x+2)/(x^2-1)$, then decompose the proper fraction. For distinct linear factors, the decomposition is straightforward. Factor the denominator completely: $Q(x) = (x-r_1)(x-r_2)\cdots(x-r_n)$. Write $P(x)/Q(x) = A_1/(x-r_1) + A_2/(x-r_2) + \cdots + A_n/(x-r_n)$. Find $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ by multiplying both sides by $Q(x)$ and substituting the roots, or by comparing coefficients. The Heaviside cover-up method is a fast shortcut for distinct linear factors. To find the coefficient $A_k$ of $1/(x-r_k)$: "cover up" the factor $(x-r_k)$ in the denominator and evaluate the rest at $x = r_k$. Formally, $A_k = P(r_k)/\prod_{j \neq k}(r_k - r_j)$. This avoids setting up a system of equations and is extremely efficient for denominators with 3 or more distinct factors. For repeated linear factors like $(x-r)^m$, you need $m$ terms: $A_1/(x-r) + A_2/(x-r)^2 + \cdots + A_m/(x-r)^m$. The Heaviside method gives $A_m$ directly (plug in $x = r$), but the other coefficients require either comparing coefficients or differentiating the cover-up equation. Each term $A_k/(x-r)^k$ integrates as $A_k(x-r)^{1-k}/(1-k)$ for $k \geq 2$, or $A_1 \ln|x-r|$ for $k = 1$. For irreducible quadratic factors $(x^2+bx+c)$ (where $b^2-4c < 0$), the partial fraction has the form $(Bx+C)/(x^2+bx+c)$. To integrate: complete the square in the denominator, then split into two integrals — one giving a logarithm (via u-sub) and one giving an arctangent. For repeated quadratics $(x^2+bx+c)^k$, you need terms $(B_j x + C_j)/(x^2+bx+c)^j$ for $j = 1, 2, \ldots, k$. Setup for partial fractions. Let $P(x)/Q(x)$ be a proper rational function (deg $P$ < deg $Q$). Factor $Q(x)$ into linear and irreducible quadratic factors over $\mathbb{R}$: $Q(x) = (x-r_1)^{m_1}\cdots(x-r_p)^{m_p}(x^2+b_1x+c_1)^{n_1}\cdots(x^2+b_qx+c_q)^{n_q}$. The partial fraction decomposition is: $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} = \sum_{i=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{m_i}\frac{A_{ij}}{(x-r_i)^j} + \sum_{i=1}^{q}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}\frac{B_{ij}x+C_{ij}}{(x^2+b_ix+c_i)^j}$. The constants $A_{ij}$, $B_{ij}$, $C_{ij}$ are uniquely determined. Finding coefficients — three methods: (1) Multiply both sides by $Q(x)$ and substitute the roots $x = r_i$ to find some constants (Heaviside). (2) Compare coefficients of like powers of $x$ on both sides. (3) Substitute convenient non-root values of $x$ (e.g., $x = 0$) to generate additional equations. In practice, a mix of all three is fastest. Integration of each type: $\int A/(x-r)\,dx = A\ln|x-r|+C$. $\int A/(x-r)^k\,dx = A(x-r)^{1-k}/(1-k)+C$ for $k \geq 2$. $\int (Bx+C)/(x^2+bx+c)\,dx$: complete the square, split into $\int (\text{du/u}) + \int (\text{d}\theta/(1+\theta^2))$, giving a $\ln$ and an $\arctan$. Integration of the quadratic type in detail: let $x^2+bx+c = (x+b/2)^2 + (c-b^2/4) = (x+b/2)^2+d^2$ where $d = \sqrt{c-b^2/4}$. Split $Bx+C = B(x+b/2) + (C-Bb/2)$. Then: $\int \frac{B(x+b/2)}{(x+b/2)^2+d^2}dx = \frac{B}{2}\ln[(x+b/2)^2+d^2]+C_1$ (u-sub with $u = (x+b/2)^2+d^2$). And: $\int \frac{C-Bb/2}{(x+b/2)^2+d^2}dx = \frac{C-Bb/2}{d}\arctan\frac{x+b/2}{d}+C_2$. The existence and uniqueness of partial fraction decomposition is guaranteed by algebra. Over a field $F$, the ring of polynomials $F[x]$ is a principal ideal domain, and partial fraction decomposition follows from the Chinese Remainder Theorem for $F[x]$-modules. In concrete terms: if $Q(x) = Q_1(x)Q_2(x)$ with $\gcd(Q_1, Q_2) = 1$, then for any $P$ with $\deg P < \deg Q$, there exist unique $P_1, P_2$ with $\deg P_i < \deg Q_i$ such that $P/Q = P_1/Q_1 + P_2/Q_2$. The proof of the Heaviside cover-up formula: $A_k = \lim_{x \to r_k}(x-r_k)\cdot P(x)/Q(x)$. Since $Q(x)$ has a simple root at $r_k$, write $Q(x) = (x-r_k)\tilde{Q}(x)$ where $\tilde{Q}(r_k) \neq 0$. Then $(x-r_k)P(x)/Q(x) = P(x)/\tilde{Q}(x) \to P(r_k)/\tilde{Q}(r_k)$ as $x \to r_k$. This equals the residue of $P/Q$ at $r_k$ — the same concept used in complex analysis for contour integrals. For repeated roots, the generalization uses derivatives: if $Q(x) = (x-r)^m \tilde{Q}(x)$, define $\phi(x) = (x-r)^m P(x)/Q(x) = P(x)/\tilde{Q}(x)$. Then $A_m = \phi(r)$, $A_{m-1} = \phi'(r)/1!$, $A_{m-2} = \phi''(r)/2!$, and in general $A_{m-j} = \phi^{(j)}(r)/j!$. This is the Taylor expansion of $\phi$ at $r$. The fact that every real polynomial factors into linear and irreducible quadratic factors follows from the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (every polynomial of degree $n$ has exactly $n$ complex roots, counted with multiplicity). Complex roots of a real polynomial come in conjugate pairs $\alpha \pm \beta i$. Each pair gives a real quadratic factor $(x-\alpha)^2+\beta^2 = x^2-2\alpha x+\alpha^2+\beta^2$, which is irreducible over $\mathbb{R}$ (its discriminant $4\alpha^2-4(\alpha^2+\beta^2) = -4\beta^2 < 0$). Existence and Uniqueness For any proper rational function \(P(x)/Q(x)\), the partial fraction decomposition exists and is unique. This follows from the Chinese Remainder Theorem in the polynomial ring \(\mathbb{R}[x]\). When to Use Partial Fractions Use partial fractions when the integrand is a rational function P(x)/Q(x) (polynomial over polynomial) and Q(x) can be factored. If the degree of P ≥ degree of Q, do long division first. This technique works for ALL rational functions — it is a complete method. Polynomial Long Division If deg(P) ≥ deg(Q), divide P by Q first. Example: (x³+2)/(x²−1) = x + remainder (x+2)/(x²−1). Only the proper fraction (x+2)/(x²−1) needs partial fraction decomposition. The polynomial part integrates directly. Factor Completely Before Decomposing Partial fractions require a fully factored denominator. x⁴−1 = (x²+1)(x−1)(x+1), not just (x²−1)(x²+1). If you miss a factorization, you will set up the wrong form and get stuck. Always check: can any quadratic factor be factored further? (Test discriminant: b²−4c < 0 means irreducible.) Laplace Transforms and Circuit Analysis In electrical engineering, the response of an RLC circuit to a step input is found by (1) writing the transfer function H(s) = 1/(s²+s+1), (2) decomposing into partial fractions (with complex or irreducible quadratic terms), and (3) inverse-transforming each piece. Partial fractions convert one hard transform into several easy ones. Repeated Factors Need Multiple Terms For (x−1)³ in the denominator, you need THREE terms: A/(x−1) + B/(x−1)² + C/(x−1)³. A common error is writing only A/(x−1)³. This gives too few unknowns and the decomposition fails. Partial Fraction Forms Distinct linear \((x-r)\): \(\frac{A}{x-r}\)
Repeated linear \((x-r)^m\): \(\frac{A_1}{x-r}+\frac{A_2}{(x-r)^2}+\cdots+\frac{A_m}{(x-r)^m}\)
Irreducible quadratic \((x^2+bx+c)\): \(\frac{Bx+C}{x^2+bx+c}\)
Repeated quadratic: similar pattern with increasing powers. Heaviside Cover-Up Method For distinct linear factors: to find the coefficient of \(1/(x-r_k)\), cover up \((x-r_k)\) in the original fraction and evaluate at \(x = r_k\).
Example: \(\frac{5}{(x-1)(x+3)}\): coefficient of \(1/(x-1)\) is \(5/(1+3) = 5/4\). Coefficient of \(1/(x+3)\) is \(5/(-3-1) = -5/4\). Every Rational Function Is Integrable Since every polynomial factors over \(\mathbb{R}\) into linear and irreducible quadratic factors, and each partial fraction type has a known antiderivative (ln, power, arctan), every rational function has an elementary antiderivative. This is one of the great computational triumphs of calculus. Method Selection Trainer Switch on the rational/factorable trait and verify when partial fractions should be your first move. Decomposing a Rational Function The original rational function (dark curve) equals the sum of its partial fractions (lighter curves). Each simple fraction has a vertical asymptote at a root of the denominator. The decomposition separates the behavior near each asymptote into independent, integrable pieces. Distinct Linear: ∫(2x+1)/((x−1)(x+2)) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx. ∫(2x+1)/((x−1)(x+2)) dx = ln|(x−1)(x+2)| + C. Heaviside cover-up makes finding A and B almost instant. \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)} = \frac{A}{x-1} + \frac{B}{x+2} Set up the partial fraction form: distinct linear factors. \text{Heaviside: } A = \frac{2(1)+1}{1+2} = \frac{3}{3} = 1 Cover up (x−1), evaluate at x = 1. B = \frac{2(-2)+1}{-2-1} = \frac{-3}{-3} = 1 Cover up (x+2), evaluate at x = −2. \int \frac{2x+1}{(x-1)(x+2)}\,dx = \int\frac{1}{x-1}dx + \int\frac{1}{x+2}dx Both coefficients are 1. = \ln|x-1| + \ln|x+2| + C = \ln|(x-1)(x+2)| + C Each integrates to a logarithm. Combine using ln a + ln b = ln(ab). Repeated Factor: ∫x/((x−1)²(x+1)) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)}\,dx. ∫x/((x−1)²(x+1)) dx = (1/4)ln|(x−1)/(x+1)| − 1/(2(x−1)) + C. \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1} + \frac{B}{(x-1)^2} + \frac{C}{x+1} Repeated factor (x−1)² requires TWO terms. \text{Heaviside for } B: \text{cover } (x-1)^2, \text{ eval at } x=1: \; B = \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2} B is found directly. \text{Heaviside for } C: \text{cover } (x+1), \text{ eval at } x=-1: \; C = \frac{-1}{(-1-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{4} C is found directly. \text{For } A: \text{multiply out and compare. Set } x=0: \; \frac{0}{(−1)^2(1)} = A(−1)(1)+B(1)+C(−1)^2 Substitute a convenient value x = 0. 0 = -A + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{-1}{4} = -A + \frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow A = \frac{1}{4} Solve for A. \int \frac{x}{(x-1)^2(x+1)}dx = \frac{1}{4}\ln|x-1| - \frac{1}{2(x-1)} - \frac{1}{4}\ln|x+1| + C ∫A/(x−1) = A ln|x−1|; ∫B/(x−1)² = −B/(x−1); ∫C/(x+1) = C ln|x+1|. = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right| - \frac{1}{2(x-1)} + C Combine logarithms. Irreducible Quadratic: ∫(x²+1)/(x³+x) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^2+1}{x^3+x}\,dx. ∫(x²+1)/(x³+x) dx = ln|x| + C. Lesson: always check for cancellation before setting up partial fractions. x^3+x = x(x^2+1) Factor the denominator. x²+1 is irreducible (discriminant = −4 < 0). \frac{x^2+1}{x(x^2+1)} = \frac{1}{x} Wait — x²+1 cancels! The fraction simplifies before decomposition. \int \frac{x^2+1}{x^3+x}\,dx = \int \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln|x| + C Always simplify first. True Quadratic: ∫(3x+5)/(x²+2x+5) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{3x+5}{x^2+2x+5}\,dx. ∫(3x+5)/(x²+2x+5) dx = (3/2)ln(x²+2x+5) + arctan((x+1)/2) + C. The split into "derivative of denominator" and "constant over denominator" is the standard technique for irreducible quadratics. x^2+2x+5 = (x+1)^2+4 Complete the square. Discriminant = 4−20 < 0, so irreducible. 3x+5 = 3(x+1)+2 Rewrite numerator in terms of (x+1) to match the completed square. \int \frac{3(x+1)}{(x+1)^2+4}dx + \int \frac{2}{(x+1)^2+4}dx Split into two integrals. \text{First: } u=(x+1)^2+4,\; du=2(x+1)dx: \; \frac{3}{2}\ln[(x+1)^2+4] U-sub gives a logarithm. \text{Second: } \frac{2}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{1}\arctan\frac{x+1}{2} = \arctan\frac{x+1}{2} ∫du/(u²+a²) = (1/a)arctan(u/a). Here a = 2: 2·(1/2)arctan((x+1)/2). = \frac{3}{2}\ln(x^2+2x+5) + \arctan\frac{x+1}{2} + C Combine. Note: (x+1)²+4 = x²+2x+5 is always positive, no absolute value needed. Long Division First: ∫(x³−2x+3)/(x²−1) dx \text{Find } \int \frac{x^3-2x+3}{x^2-1}\,dx. ∫(x³−2x+3)/(x²−1) dx = x²/2 + ln|x−1| − 2ln|x+1| + C. Always do long division when the numerator degree ≥ denominator degree. \frac{x^3-2x+3}{x^2-1} = x + \frac{-x+3}{x^2-1} Degree of numerator (3) ≥ degree of denominator (2). Long divide: x³ ÷ (x²−1) = x remainder −x+3. \frac{-x+3}{(x-1)(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{B}{x+1} Factor x²−1 and decompose the proper fraction. A = \frac{-1+3}{1+1} = 1, \quad B = \frac{1+3}{-1-1} = -2 Heaviside cover-up. \int\left(x + \frac{1}{x-1} - \frac{2}{x+1}\right)dx Three simple integrals. = \frac{x^2}{2} + \ln|x-1| - 2\ln|x+1| + C Integrate each term. Find ∫dx/((x+1)(x+3)) using the Heaviside cover-up method. A = 1/(1+3−1+1)... actually: cover (x+1), eval at x = −1: A = 1/(−1+3) = 1/2. Cover (x+3), eval at x = −3: B = 1/(−3+1) = −1/2. \frac{1}{2}\ln\left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+C \frac{1}{(x+1)(x+3)} = \frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{x+3} Standard form for distinct linear factors. A = \frac{1}{-1+3} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad B = \frac{1}{-3+1} = -\frac{1}{2} Cover-up method. \int \frac{dx}{(x+1)(x+3)} = \frac{1}{2}\ln|x+1|-\frac{1}{2}\ln|x+3|+C = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+C Integrate and combine logs. Find ∫(2x²+3x+1)/(x³+x²) dx. Factor: x³+x² = x²(x+1). Repeated factor x² needs terms A/x + B/x² + C/(x+1). 2\ln|x|-\frac{1}{x}+C \frac{2x^2+3x+1}{x^2(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x^2}+\frac{C}{x+1} Set up with repeated linear factor x². B:\text{ cover } x^2, x=0: B = \frac{1}{0+1} = 1. \quad C:\text{ cover } (x+1), x=-1: C = \frac{2-3+1}{(-1)^2} = 0 Heaviside for B and C. \text{For } A: \text{multiply by } x^2(x+1): 2x^2+3x+1 = Ax(x+1)+1\cdot(x+1)+0\cdot x^2 Substitute known B=1, C=0. \text{Compare } x^2: 2 = A. \text{ Check } x^1: 3 = A+1 = 3.\checkmark A = 2 confirmed. \int\left(\frac{2}{x}+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)dx = 2\ln|x|-\frac{1}{x}+C Integrate (C=0 term vanishes). Find ∫(x²+2)/((x−1)(x²+x+1)) dx. Note that x³−1 = (x−1)(x²+x+1). Decompose into A/(x−1) + (Bx+C)/(x²+x+1). For the quadratic integral, complete the square: x²+x+1 = (x+1/2)² + 3/4. \ln|x-1|-\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}\arctan\frac{2x+1}{\sqrt{3}}+C \frac{x^2+2}{(x-1)(x^2+x+1)} = \frac{A}{x-1}+\frac{Bx+C}{x^2+x+1} x²+x+1 is irreducible (discriminant = 1−4 = −3 < 0). A = \frac{1+2}{1+1+1} = 1 Cover up (x−1), evaluate at x = 1. \text{Multiply out: } x^2+2 = 1\cdot(x^2+x+1)+(Bx+C)(x-1) Expand right side. x^2+2 = x^2+x+1+Bx^2-Bx+Cx-C = (1+B)x^2+(1-B+C)x+(1-C) Collect terms. x^2: 1=1+B \Rightarrow B=0. \quad x^0: 2=1-C \Rightarrow C=-1. \quad \text{Check } x^1: 0=1-0-1=0.\checkmark Compare coefficients. \int\frac{1}{x-1}dx + \int\frac{-1}{x^2+x+1}dx = \ln|x-1| - \int\frac{dx}{(x+1/2)^2+3/4} Complete the square: x²+x+1 = (x+1/2)² + 3/4. = \ln|x-1| - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}/2}\arctan\frac{x+1/2}{\sqrt{3}/2} + C = \ln|x-1|-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}\arctan\frac{2x+1}{\sqrt{3}}+C Apply ∫du/(u²+a²) = (1/a)arctan(u/a). U-Substitution Partial fractions reduce rational integrals to simple forms, some of which need u-sub (e.g., ∫x/(x²+1) dx). Indefinite Integrals The antiderivative table entries ln|x|, arctan x, and power rules are the building blocks for partial fraction integration. Improper Integrals Partial fractions help evaluate improper integrals of rational functions by splitting them into convergent pieces. Integration by Parts Irreducible quadratic terms with higher powers may require by-parts in addition to partial fractions. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Improper Integrals
documents.subtitle: Extending integration to infinite intervals and unbounded functions — convergence, divergence, and surprising finite answers
documents.tags: improper integral convergence divergence infinite limits p-integral comparison test Gabriel horn
documents.aliases: approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change
documents.assignment: Type 1: ∫₁^∞ 1/x² dx \text{Evaluate } \int_1^\infty \frac{1}{x^2}\,dx.   ∫₁^∞ 1/x² dx = 1. The infinite tail has finite area. This is the p-test with p = 2 > 1. \int_1^\infty \frac{1}{x^2}\,dx = \lim_{b \to \infty}\int_1^b x^{-2}\,dx Replace ∞ with a finite limit b. = \lim_{b \to \infty}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]_1^b = \lim_{b \to \infty}\left(-\frac{1}{b}+1\right) Antiderivative of x⁻² is −x⁻¹.
