Ignoring air resistance (or treating the object as a point mass).   \text{If drag is strong, does } x(t)=5t^2 \text{ overestimate or underestimate fall distance?} Drag slows falling speed. It overestimates distance because real motion falls less than the vacuum model.
documents.applications: Engineers, athletes, and pilots all use simplified models that ignore some details to make fast and useful predictions. Pattern First Model -> Equation -> Prediction -> Check against reality.
documents.content: How do we predict the future of a system using math? Engineers, athletes, and pilots all use simplified models that ignore some details to make fast and useful predictions. Physics is a model-building language. We intentionally simplify the world so we can compute, explain, and predict. A model keeps dominant causes and temporarily drops weak effects. Ignoring air resistance or treating an object as a point mass are useful simplifications that create solvable first models. The purpose of a model is not to be perfectly realistic. The purpose is to preserve the key causal structure so predictions are directionally and numerically useful. Good physics always asks two follow-up questions: what assumptions did we make, and when do those assumptions stop being valid? We define position by the function $x(t)$: input is time $t$ in seconds, output is position $x$ in meters. If time increases, the model outputs a new position value. The function itself is the rule mapping cause (time) to effect (position). Variable meaning: $t$ tells when, $x$ tells where. Unit meaning: seconds for $t$, meters for $x$. Behavior meaning: if $x(t)$ grows faster, motion is faster in that interval. Pattern First Model -> Equation -> Prediction -> Check against reality. Variable Meaning and Units $t$: time (s), $x$: position (m), $x(t)$: predicted position at time $t$. Model vs Reality Same initial drop, different assumptions. Predicting a Fall with an Ideal Model \text{Given } x(t)=5t^2, \text{ find } x(2). Predictions are only as good as assumptions. x(2)=5(2)^2 Substitute the input time $t=2$ s. x(2)=20 The model predicts 20 meters. \text{Name one simplification in an ideal free-fall model.} Think about effects the model ignores. Ignoring air resistance (or treating the object as a point mass). \text{If drag is strong, does } x(t)=5t^2 \text{ overestimate or underestimate fall distance?} Drag slows falling speed. It overestimates distance because real motion falls less than the vacuum model. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Units, Dimensions, and Scaling
documents.subtitle: Every valid equation must be dimensionally consistent.
documents.tags: units dimensions scaling
documents.assignment: Testing a Suspicious Formula \text{Check if } d = vt^2 \text{ is dimensionally valid.}   A formula can look algebraically clean but still be physically impossible. [d]=[L] Distance must have length dimension. [vt^2]=\frac{L}{T}\cdot T^2 = LT Right side simplifies to $LT$, not $L$. [L]\neq[LT] So the formula fails dimensional consistency.  \text{What are the dimensions of acceleration?} Acceleration is change in velocity over time. $[a]=L/T^2$.   \text{Why can } x = vt + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \text{ be added term-by-term?} Check dimensions of both terms. Both $vt$ and $at^2$ reduce to length, so addition is valid.
documents.applications: Unit mistakes have crashed spacecraft and broken engineering systems. Units are safety checks for thought.
documents.content: Why is $5\text{ m} + 10\text{ s}$ meaningless? Unit mistakes have crashed spacecraft and broken engineering systems. Units are safety checks for thought. Dimensions are the physical type of a quantity: length [L], mass [M], time [T]. Units are labels (m, kg, s), while dimensions are structure. Different units can represent the same dimension, but incompatible dimensions cannot be added. If left and right sides of an equation do not match dimensionally, the equation cannot represent reality no matter how neat it looks algebraically. Dimensional checks are fast sanity tests: they catch conceptual mistakes before you waste time on arithmetic. Velocity has dimensions $[v]=\frac{L}{T}$ and units m/s. This means larger distance per same time or same distance in less time both increase speed. Dimensional analysis does not tell you exact constants, but it strongly constrains legal equation forms. Edge-case interpretation: formulas can be numerically plausible and still physically impossible if units do not balance. Homogeneity Rule [\text{LHS}] = [\text{RHS}] Live Unit Checker Build equations and test dimensional homogeneity. Testing a Suspicious Formula \text{Check if } d = vt^2 \text{ is dimensionally valid.} A formula can look algebraically clean but still be physically impossible. [d]=[L] Distance must have length dimension. [vt^2]=\frac{L}{T}\cdot T^2 = LT Right side simplifies to $LT$, not $L$. [L]\neq[LT] So the formula fails dimensional consistency. \text{What are the dimensions of acceleration?} Acceleration is change in velocity over time. $[a]=L/T^2$. \text{Why can } x = vt + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \text{ be added term-by-term?} Check dimensions of both terms. Both $vt$ and $at^2$ reduce to length, so addition is valid. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Variables and Functions in Physics
documents.subtitle: How physical quantities depend on each other.
documents.tags: functions variables x(t) v(t) a(t)
documents.assignment: Reading a Function Value \text{If } x(t)=2t+1, \text{ what is } x(3)?   A function is a rule that converts input to physical output. x(3)=2(3)+1 Insert $t=3$ s into the mapping. x(3)=7 Output is 7 meters.  \text{Which is independent in } x(t): x \text{ or } t? The independent variable is the input. $t$ is independent.   \text{If } x(t) \text{ is in meters and } t \text{ is in seconds, what are units of slope of } x\text{-}t \text{ graph?} Slope is output over input. m/s, which is velocity.
documents.applications: GPS navigation continuously maps time to location, speed, and acceleration.
documents.content: What does it mean to say position depends on time? GPS navigation continuously maps time to location, speed, and acceleration. Physics uses functions to describe dependence: one quantity changes because another changes. A function is not just notation. It is a causal contract telling you what output you get for every allowed input. In early mechanics, time is the universal input and position, velocity, and acceleration are outputs. When a student says "I know the formula," the deeper question is whether they can explain the dependency graph among variables. Core notation: $x(t)$ for position (m), $v(t)$ for velocity (m/s), and $a(t)$ for acceleration (m/s$^2$). If $t$ increases, these outputs update according to the model rule, which may be linear, curved, periodic, or piecewise. Interpretation rule: when a function output rises with $t$, that physical quantity is increasing over time. Independent vs Dependent $t$ is independent. $x, v, a$ are dependent variables in basic kinematics models. Function Machine Cause (time) to effect (position). Reading a Function Value \text{If } x(t)=2t+1, \text{ what is } x(3)? A function is a rule that converts input to physical output. x(3)=2(3)+1 Insert $t=3$ s into the mapping. x(3)=7 Output is 7 meters. \text{Which is independent in } x(t): x \text{ or } t? The independent variable is the input. $t$ is independent. \text{If } x(t) \text{ is in meters and } t \text{ is in seconds, what are units of slope of } x\text{-}t \text{ graph?} Slope is output over input. m/s, which is velocity. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Change: Average vs Instantaneous
documents.subtitle: From secant slope to tangent slope.
documents.tags: limits derivative velocity
documents.aliases: approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change
documents.assignment: Average Velocity on an Interval \text{If } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compute } v_{avg} \text{ from } t=1 \text{ to } t=3.   Instantaneous velocity at a specific time may differ from the interval average. v_{avg}=\frac{x(3)-x(1)}{3-1} Use endpoint positions. v_{avg}=\frac{9-1}{2}=4 Average velocity is 4 m/s. Curved Motion and Local Velocity \text{For } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compare } v_{avg} \text{ on } [2,3] \text{ with } v(2).   Curvature causes average and instantaneous values to diverge over finite windows. v_{avg}=\frac{x(3)-x(2)}{3-2}=\frac{9-4}{1}=5 Finite interval average gives 5 m/s. v(t)=\frac{dx}{dt}=2t \Rightarrow v(2)=4 Instantaneous velocity at t=2 is 4 m/s. 5 \neq 4 Different values confirm average over an interval is not identical to point velocity.  \text{Compute } v_{avg} \text{ for } x(t)=3t+1 \text{ on } [1,5]. Find endpoint positions and divide by total time. 3 m/s.  \text{If stuck, revisit: Symbol Decoder and Average Definition} Re-read the Symbol Decoder callout and the first two math paragraphs before computing. v_{avg}=\frac{x(5)-x(1)}{5-1} Use endpoint displacement over total elapsed time. x(5)=16,\ x(1)=4 \Rightarrow v_{avg}=\frac{12}{4}=3 Compute values and simplify to 3 m/s.  \text{From the graph of } x(t)=t^2, \text{ estimate } v(2) \text{ using secant points } t=1.9,2.1. Use central secant: $[x(2.1)-x(1.9)]/0.2$. Approximately 4.0 m/s.  \text{If stuck, revisit: Secant-to-Tangent and Local Zoom visuals} Those visuals show why a narrow secant is an estimate for instantaneous slope. v(2)\approx\frac{x(2.1)-x(1.9)}{0.2}=\frac{4.41-3.61}{0.2} Apply central secant around t=2. v(2)\approx 4.0 Estimated instantaneous velocity is about 4 m/s.  \text{Explain why derivative fails for } x(t)=|t| \text{ at } t=0. Compare left-hand and right-hand slope limits. Left slope is -1 and right slope is +1, so the limit slopes disagree; derivative does not exist at the corner.  \text{If stuck, revisit: Edge Case Non-Smooth Motion} That callout explains derivative failure at corners/cusps. m_{left}=\lim_{h\to0^-}\frac{|h|-|0|}{h}=\lim_{h\to0^-}\frac{-h}{h}=-1 Compute left-hand slope. m_{right}=\lim_{h\to0^+}\frac{|h|-|0|}{h}=\lim_{h\to0^+}\frac{h}{h}=+1 Compute right-hand slope. -1\neq +1 \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}\text{ does not exist at }t=0 Left and right limits disagree, so derivative fails at the corner.
documents.applications: Braking systems, autonomous driving, and robotics need instantaneous change, not delayed averages. Pre-Flight Prediction Before touching any slider: if $\Delta t$ gets smaller, do you expect average velocity to move closer to or farther from the speedometer-style instantaneous velocity? Instantaneous = Local Linear Approximation At sufficiently small time scales, smooth motion behaves like a line segment, and slope becomes locally stable. Average Velocity Can Hide Direction Changes A near-zero average over a long interval can still include fast forward and backward motion inside the interval. Average vs Instantaneous Definitions Average: $v_{avg}=\Delta x/\Delta t$ over a finite interval. Instantaneous: $v=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\Delta x/\Delta t$ at a point. Limit Process Map Secant slope on interval -> shrink interval -> limit exists -> tangent slope (derivative). Symbol Decoder (Plain English) $\Delta$ means "change in", $x$ means position, and $t$ means time. So $\Delta x/\Delta t$ literally means "how much position changed per time changed." Analogy: Average Mood vs Instant Mood You can have a good average mood this month but feel frustrated right now after dropping your phone. Same logic: interval average and moment value are different quantities. Safety Stakes If a self-driving car brakes using a long average instead of local instantaneous data, it can react late and crash.
documents.content: Why is average speed not enough for real-time control? Braking systems, autonomous driving, and robotics need instantaneous change, not delayed averages. Average velocity over an interval blurs detail. It tells what happened across a chunk of time, not at one moment. For safety systems and control loops, that blur is often too slow. They require local, immediate change information. When we zoom into a tiny interval, curved motion starts to look linear. This local linearity motivates instantaneous velocity. Calculus enters exactly here: it formalizes the zoom process instead of treating it as a hand-wave. Car analogy: your trip average speed can be 60 km/h while your speedometer is reading 15 km/h in traffic or 95 km/h on a clear stretch. Average and instantaneous values answer different questions. Failure case: two motions can have the same average velocity over 0 to 10 s but one can reverse direction midway. The average hides that sign change unless you inspect local slope. Flashbulb analogy: average velocity is like a long-exposure blur across a whole interval, while instantaneous velocity is like a high-speed flash frame that freezes one exact moment. Try-at-home experiment: walk toward a wall slowly for 3 seconds, then speed up in the final second. Your average speed for the full walk is moderate, but your instantaneous speed near the wall is much higher. Plain-English bridge before symbols: we are measuring "steepness of the motion story" on a graph. Average steepness uses a whole chunk; instantaneous steepness uses one location via a limit process. Average velocity: $v_{avg}=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ with units m/s over a finite interval. Instantaneous velocity: $v=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$. As the time window shrinks, the estimate approaches local slope. Interpretation: if instantaneous velocity changes sign, direction changes; if magnitude grows, motion becomes faster at that instant. Why plugging in a single point fails: slope needs two points. The limit process supplies that by using two nearby points and then collapsing the gap. Equation depth check: $x$ is position (m), $t$ is time (s), $\Delta x$ is displacement (m), and $\Delta t$ is elapsed time (s), so both average and instantaneous velocity have units m/s. Graph interpretation: secant slope is interval behavior, tangent slope is local behavior. Physical interpretation: secant is trip report, tangent is speedometer reading now. Pre-Flight Prediction Before touching any slider: if $\Delta t$ gets smaller, do you expect average velocity to move closer to or farther from the speedometer-style instantaneous velocity? Instantaneous = Local Linear Approximation At sufficiently small time scales, smooth motion behaves like a line segment, and slope becomes locally stable. Average Velocity Can Hide Direction Changes A near-zero average over a long interval can still include fast forward and backward motion inside the interval. Average vs Instantaneous Definitions Average: $v_{avg}=\Delta x/\Delta t$ over a finite interval. Instantaneous: $v=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\Delta x/\Delta t$ at a point. Limit Process Map Secant slope on interval -> shrink interval -> limit exists -> tangent slope (derivative). Symbol Decoder (Plain English) $\Delta$ means "change in", $x$ means position, and $t$ means time. So $\Delta x/\Delta t$ literally means "how much position changed per time changed." Analogy: Average Mood vs Instant Mood You can have a good average mood this month but feel frustrated right now after dropping your phone. Same logic: interval average and moment value are different quantities. Safety Stakes If a self-driving car brakes using a long average instead of local instantaneous data, it can react late and crash. Dual-Track Rigor Toggle Algebra track: secant slope over a finite gap. Calculus track: the same secant process with gap size driven to zero by a limit. Interpretation $v>0$ means position increases; larger magnitude means faster position change per second. Edge Case: Dt = 0 is Undefined The ratio $\Delta x/\Delta t$ is undefined at exactly $\Delta t=0$. Instantaneous velocity is defined by a limit, not direct substitution. Edge Case: Non-Smooth Motion At corners, cusps, or jumps, left and right slopes can disagree, so the derivative may fail to exist. Misconception Check Instantaneous velocity is not zero just because the interval is tiny; it is the limiting slope value, which can be nonzero. Misconception: Average Always Equals Instantaneous Average approaches instantaneous only as interval width shrinks. For finite intervals they can differ significantly. Algebra-to-Calculus Bridge Calculus is not a separate idea here; it is repeated algebraic secant estimation with shrinking intervals until a stable local value emerges. Unity Principle Calculus is algebra under a magnifying glass: the same ratio, evaluated on smaller and smaller neighborhoods. Master Graph: Algebra -> Limit -> Calculus One visual backbone instead of repeated coordinate planes. Dual-Track Sync: Algebra -> Calculus The two tracks are one process at different scales. 3D Space-Time Ribbon From 2D slope to 3D motion intuition. Brake or Crash: Stale vs Instant Data Safety stakes become visceral through interaction. Average Velocity on an Interval \text{If } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compute } v_{avg} \text{ from } t=1 \text{ to } t=3. Instantaneous velocity at a specific time may differ from the interval average. v_{avg}=\frac{x(3)-x(1)}{3-1} Use endpoint positions. v_{avg}=\frac{9-1}{2}=4 Average velocity is 4 m/s. Curved Motion and Local Velocity \text{For } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compare } v_{avg} \text{ on } [2,3] \text{ with } v(2). Curvature causes average and instantaneous values to diverge over finite windows. v_{avg}=\frac{x(3)-x(2)}{3-2}=\frac{9-4}{1}=5 Finite interval average gives 5 m/s. v(t)=\frac{dx}{dt}=2t \Rightarrow v(2)=4 Instantaneous velocity at t=2 is 4 m/s. 5 \neq 4 Different values confirm average over an interval is not identical to point velocity. \text{Compute } v_{avg} \text{ for } x(t)=3t+1 \text{ on } [1,5]. Find endpoint positions and divide by total time. 3 m/s. \text{If stuck, revisit: Symbol Decoder and Average Definition} Re-read the Symbol Decoder callout and the first two math paragraphs before computing. v_{avg}=\frac{x(5)-x(1)}{5-1} Use endpoint displacement over total elapsed time. x(5)=16,\ x(1)=4 \Rightarrow v_{avg}=\frac{12}{4}=3 Compute values and simplify to 3 m/s. \text{From the graph of } x(t)=t^2, \text{ estimate } v(2) \text{ using secant points } t=1.9,2.1. Use central secant: $[x(2.1)-x(1.9)]/0.2$. Approximately 4.0 m/s. \text{If stuck, revisit: Secant-to-Tangent and Local Zoom visuals} Those visuals show why a narrow secant is an estimate for instantaneous slope. v(2)\approx\frac{x(2.1)-x(1.9)}{0.2}=\frac{4.41-3.61}{0.2} Apply central secant around t=2. v(2)\approx 4.0 Estimated instantaneous velocity is about 4 m/s. \text{Explain why derivative fails for } x(t)=|t| \text{ at } t=0. Compare left-hand and right-hand slope limits. Left slope is -1 and right slope is +1, so the limit slopes disagree; derivative does not exist at the corner. \text{If stuck, revisit: Edge Case Non-Smooth Motion} That callout explains derivative failure at corners/cusps. m_{left}=\lim_{h\to0^-}\frac{|h|-|0|}{h}=\lim_{h\to0^-}\frac{-h}{h}=-1 Compute left-hand slope. m_{right}=\lim_{h\to0^+}\frac{|h|-|0|}{h}=\lim_{h\to0^+}\frac{h}{h}=+1 Compute right-hand slope. -1\neq +1 \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}\text{ does not exist at }t=0 Left and right limits disagree, so derivative fails at the corner. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change

documents.title: Graphs as Physical Objects
documents.subtitle: Slope and area carry physical meaning.
documents.tags: graphs slope area interpretation
documents.assignment: Flat vs Steep Position Graph \text{Interpret two segments: one flat and one steep positive.}   Graph steepness directly communicates speed and direction. \text{flat segment} \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}=0 Object is at rest. \text{steep positive segment} \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}\text{ large and positive} Object moves quickly in positive direction.  \text{If slope on } x\text{-}t \text{ graph is negative, what is sign of velocity?} Velocity sign follows slope sign. Negative velocity.   \text{Can velocity be zero while acceleration is nonzero?} Think of turning points. Yes. At a peak, velocity can be 0 while acceleration is still nonzero.
documents.applications: Sensor dashboards and telemetry are interpreted through graph shape, slope, and area every second.
documents.content: How can a graph predict motion without seeing the object? Sensor dashboards and telemetry are interpreted through graph shape, slope, and area every second. A graph is a compressed story of motion. Geometry on the graph maps to physics in the world. On a position-time graph, slope is velocity. On a velocity-time graph, area under the curve is displacement. This is why graph reading is not optional in physics. It is the bridge between qualitative behavior and quantitative prediction. Changing shape means changing mechanism: flat regions, sharp bends, and curvature each encode different motion behavior. $\frac{dx}{dt}$ gives instantaneous velocity (m/s). Steeper positive slope means larger positive velocity. $\int v(t)dt$ accumulates displacement (m). If velocity is zero, area does not grow. Edge behavior: negative velocity contributes signed negative area, meaning displacement can decrease even while time increases. Edge Case Flat position graph means slope 0, so velocity 0 even though time continues. Graph Interpreter Shape to meaning mapping. Flat vs Steep Position Graph \text{Interpret two segments: one flat and one steep positive.} Graph steepness directly communicates speed and direction. \text{flat segment} \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}=0 Object is at rest. \text{steep positive segment} \Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dt}\text{ large and positive} Object moves quickly in positive direction. \text{If slope on } x\text{-}t \text{ graph is negative, what is sign of velocity?} Velocity sign follows slope sign. Negative velocity. \text{Can velocity be zero while acceleration is nonzero?} Think of turning points. Yes. At a peak, velocity can be 0 while acceleration is still nonzero. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Vectors vs Scalars
documents.subtitle: Magnitude alone is not enough for motion.
documents.tags: vectors scalars components
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Same Speed, Different Direction \text{Compare } \vec v_1=(3,4) \text{ and } \vec v_2=(-3,4).   Equal speed does not imply equal velocity. |\vec v_1|=|\vec v_2|=5 Same speed by Pythagorean magnitude. \vec v_1 \neq \vec v_2 Different $x$ direction means different velocity vectors.  \text{Is temperature a scalar or vector?} Does it have direction? Scalar.   \text{If } \vec v=(0,-6), \text{ what is direction of motion?} Negative $y$ component. Straight downward along negative y-axis.
documents.applications: Air traffic control tracks direction and speed separately because collision risk depends on vector velocity.
documents.content: Can two objects have same speed but different velocity? Air traffic control tracks direction and speed separately because collision risk depends on vector velocity. Scalars only tell how much: mass, temperature, speed. Vectors tell how much and where: displacement, velocity, force. Direction changes the physical outcome. Two cars can each move at 20 m/s and still approach each other, separate, or travel parallel depending on velocity direction. Component form turns directional ideas into computable pieces you can add, compare, and resolve. Write velocity vector as $\vec v=(v_x,v_y)$ with component units m/s. Increasing $v_x$ shifts motion rightward; increasing $v_y$ shifts motion upward. Magnitude is $|\vec v|=\sqrt{v_x^2+v_y^2}$, but magnitude alone cannot distinguish opposite directions. Speed vs Velocity Speed is scalar magnitude $|\vec v|$. Velocity includes direction and components. Vector Builder Components are coordinates of direction-aware motion. Same Speed, Different Direction \text{Compare } \vec v_1=(3,4) \text{ and } \vec v_2=(-3,4). Equal speed does not imply equal velocity. |\vec v_1|=|\vec v_2|=5 Same speed by Pythagorean magnitude. \vec v_1 \neq \vec v_2 Different $x$ direction means different velocity vectors. \text{Is temperature a scalar or vector?} Does it have direction? Scalar. \text{If } \vec v=(0,-6), \text{ what is direction of motion?} Negative $y$ component. Straight downward along negative y-axis. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Coordinate Systems and Reference Frames
documents.subtitle: Motion depends on who is observing.
documents.tags: reference frame relative motion coordinates
documents.assignment: Walking in a Moving Train \text{Train speed is } 20\,m/s \text{ right. Passenger walks } 2\,m/s \text{ right relative to train. Find passenger speed relative to ground.}   Frame choice changes measured velocity values. v_{passenger/ground}=v_{passenger/train}+v_{train/ground} Use frame composition in same direction. v_{passenger/ground}=2+20=22\,m/s Ground observer sees 22 m/s to the right.  \text{A bag sits on train floor. Is it moving in train frame?} Compare bag to train interior. No, it is at rest in train frame.   \text{Can the same object be at rest and moving at once?} Different observers. Yes, depending on reference frame.
documents.applications: A passenger sitting on a train is at rest in train frame and moving in ground frame at the same time.
documents.content: Is an object at rest or moving? A passenger sitting on a train is at rest in train frame and moving in ground frame at the same time. Position is always measured relative to a chosen origin and coordinate axes. There is no absolute position in classical mechanics; statements about motion need a reference frame. A passenger can be stationary in the train frame and moving quickly in the ground frame without contradiction. Frame choice changes coordinate values, not physical events. Physics is in the relations, not a privileged observer. Relative velocity in 1D is $v_{A/B}=v_A-v_B$ (all in m/s). If observer speed increases in the same direction, measured relative speed decreases. Sign convention matters: swapping observer and object reverses sign, clarifying who is moving relative to whom. No Absolute Rest Rest and motion are frame-dependent descriptions. Train and Ground Frames Same event, different coordinate descriptions. Walking in a Moving Train \text{Train speed is } 20\,m/s \text{ right. Passenger walks } 2\,m/s \text{ right relative to train. Find passenger speed relative to ground.} Frame choice changes measured velocity values. v_{passenger/ground}=v_{passenger/train}+v_{train/ground} Use frame composition in same direction. v_{passenger/ground}=2+20=22\,m/s Ground observer sees 22 m/s to the right. \text{A bag sits on train floor. Is it moving in train frame?} Compare bag to train interior. No, it is at rest in train frame. \text{Can the same object be at rest and moving at once?} Different observers. Yes, depending on reference frame. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Algebra vs Calculus in Physics
documents.subtitle: Finite-step reasoning versus local predictive modeling.
documents.tags: algebra calculus limits derivative
documents.aliases: approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change
documents.assignment: Discrete Approximation Improves with Smaller Step \text{For } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compare average velocity near } t=2 \text{ using } \Delta t=1 \text{ and } 0.01.   Calculus emerges as the limit of increasingly fine algebraic approximations. \Delta t=1: \frac{x(3)-x(2)}{1}=5 Coarse interval gives rough estimate. \Delta t=0.01: \frac{x(2.01)-x(2)}{0.01}=4.01 Smaller interval approaches exact instantaneous value 4.  \text{Which framework is local: algebra averages or calculus derivatives?} Local means at an instant. Calculus derivatives are local.   \text{Why does reducing } \Delta t \text{ improve predictive accuracy?} Think about how quickly slopes can change. Smaller intervals better capture local behavior and reduce averaging error.
documents.applications: Real control systems need local predictions each instant, not only block averages over long intervals.
documents.content: Why does physics move from average values to derivatives? Real control systems need local predictions each instant, not only block averages over long intervals. Algebra-based physics works with finite chunks: average speed across intervals and piecewise updates. Those finite chunks are often enough for rough estimates but can hide fast local changes. Calculus-based physics takes interval size toward zero and models local change continuously, improving prediction and control. In practice, calculus is the limit of repeated algebraic refinement, not a separate universe. Average velocity over interval: $v_{avg}=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ (m/s). Derivative velocity at an instant: $v=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}=\frac{dx}{dt}$. As $\Delta t$ gets smaller, approximation error decreases and local predictions become more reliable. Derivation Flow Average ratio -> shrink interval -> limit -> derivative. Stepwise vs Continuous Motion Finite approximation converges toward calculus model. Discrete Approximation Improves with Smaller Step \text{For } x(t)=t^2, \text{ compare average velocity near } t=2 \text{ using } \Delta t=1 \text{ and } 0.01. Calculus emerges as the limit of increasingly fine algebraic approximations. \Delta t=1: \frac{x(3)-x(2)}{1}=5 Coarse interval gives rough estimate. \Delta t=0.01: \frac{x(2.01)-x(2)}{0.01}=4.01 Smaller interval approaches exact instantaneous value 4. \text{Which framework is local: algebra averages or calculus derivatives?} Local means at an instant. Calculus derivatives are local. \text{Why does reducing } \Delta t \text{ improve predictive accuracy?} Think about how quickly slopes can change. Smaller intervals better capture local behavior and reduce averaging error. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step approach approaching value as x approaches end behavior instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change

documents.title: What Is a Vector?
documents.subtitle: The first tool of physics: quantities that carry both size and direction.
documents.tags: vector scalar magnitude direction arrow notation
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.proofs: Formally, a vector is an element of a *vector space* — a mathematical structure that supports two operations: addition of vectors and scalar multiplication. For now, $\mathbb{R}^2$ (pairs of real numbers) and $\mathbb{R}^3$ (triples) are all we need. The notation $\vec{A} = (A_x,\, A_y)$ means the ordered pair. Two vectors are equal if and only if every corresponding component is equal: $(A_x, A_y) = (B_x, B_y) \iff A_x = B_x \text{ and } A_y = B_y$. The **zero vector** $\vec{0} = (0, 0)$ has magnitude zero and, importantly, no defined direction — this is a special case that trips up many students. Formal equality of vectors \vec{A} = \vec{B} \iff A_x = B_x \text{ and } A_y = B_y \quad (\text{position in space is irrelevant}) The zero vector has no direction $|\vec{0}| = 0$ and $\theta$ is undefined. Writing $\theta = 0°$ for the zero vector is an error. Why components beat angles for calculation Magnitude–angle form is intuitive but algebraically awkward. Component form reduces every vector operation (addition, dot product, cross product) to ordinary arithmetic. The entire machinery of linear algebra is built on components.
documents.applications: Speed tells you how fast. Velocity tells you fast AND which way. Force tells you how hard AND which direction to push. Almost every interesting quantity in physics needs both a size and a direction — and that is exactly what a vector is. Vector A quantity with both magnitude and direction. Written $\vec{A}$ or in bold $\mathbf{A}$. Scalar A quantity with magnitude only. Examples: mass $m$, time $t$, temperature $T$, speed $v$. The free-vector rule You may translate (slide) a vector arrow anywhere in space without changing the vector. Position does not matter — only length and direction do.

documents.title: Vector Notation
documents.subtitle: Every symbol physicists use for vectors — and why there are so many.
documents.tags: vector notation unit vector hat notation component form bold notation
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Writing a vector in every notation \text{A force } \vec{F} \text{ has components } F_x = 3\,N,\; F_y = 4\,N. \text{Write it in (a) component notation, (b) unit-vector notation, (c) magnitude–angle form, (d) as a unit vector.}   Any vector can be translated between all four notations. Practice until the conversions are automatic. \text{(a) } \vec{F} = (3,\,4)\,N Component/bracket notation. \text{(b) } \vec{F} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}\,N Unit-vector notation — write each component times its basis vector. \text{(c) } |\vec{F}| = \sqrt{9+16} = 5\,N,\quad \theta = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1° Magnitude–angle form. Both components positive → Quadrant I, no correction needed. \text{(d) } \hat{F} = \frac{\vec{F}}{|\vec{F}|} = \frac{3\hat{i}+4\hat{j}}{5} = 0.6\hat{i}+0.8\hat{j} Divide each component by the magnitude. The result has magnitude 1. Identifying notation in the wild \text{Match each expression to its notation type: } (a)\;\mathbf{v},\; (b)\;|\vec{v}|,\; (c)\;v_x\hat{i}+v_y\hat{j},\; (d)\;(v_x,v_y),\; (e)\;\hat{v}.   Notation fluency is a prerequisite for every subsequent topic. If any of these felt slow, drill the pattern viz. \text{(a) } \mathbf{v} \Rightarrow \text{bold notation (vector)} Bold in print = arrow in handwriting. \text{(b) } |\vec{v}| \Rightarrow \text{magnitude (scalar)} Vertical bars strip the direction — result is a positive number. \text{(c) } v_x\hat{i}+v_y\hat{j} \Rightarrow \text{unit-vector notation (vector)} Expanded into basis vectors. \text{(d) } (v_x,v_y) \Rightarrow \text{component notation (vector)} Ordered pair of components. \text{(e) } \hat{v} \Rightarrow \text{unit vector (magnitude = 1)} The hat signals direction only, magnitude = 1.  \text{Find the unit vector in the direction of } \vec{A} = (-5,\,12). Compute $|\vec{A}|$ first, then divide each component. \hat{A} = \bigl(-\tfrac{5}{13},\;\tfrac{12}{13}\bigr)  |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25+144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 The 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple. \hat{A} = \frac{(-5,\,12)}{13} = \bigl(-\tfrac{5}{13},\;\tfrac{12}{13}\bigr) Divide each component by 13. \text{Verify: } \bigl|\hat{A}\bigr| = \sqrt{(5/13)^2+(12/13)^2} = \sqrt{25/169+144/169} = \sqrt{169/169} = 1\checkmark Always verify the result has magnitude 1.  \text{A 3-D vector is given as } \vec{B} = 2\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}. \text{Find } |\vec{B}| \text{ and } \hat{B}. The Pythagorean theorem extends to 3-D: $|\vec{B}| = \sqrt{B_x^2+B_y^2+B_z^2}$. |\vec{B}| = 7,\quad \hat{B} = \tfrac{2}{7}\hat{i} - \tfrac{6}{7}\hat{j} + \tfrac{3}{7}\hat{k}  |\vec{B}| = \sqrt{2^2+(-6)^2+3^2} = \sqrt{4+36+9} = \sqrt{49} = 7 3-D Pythagorean theorem. \hat{B} = \frac{2\hat{i}-6\hat{j}+3\hat{k}}{7} = \tfrac{2}{7}\hat{i} - \tfrac{6}{7}\hat{j} + \tfrac{3}{7}\hat{k} Divide each component by 7.
documents.proofs: The basis vectors $\hat{i}$, $\hat{j}$, $\hat{k}$ are special because they are **orthonormal**: mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) and each of unit length (normal). Any vector in 3-D space can be written uniquely as a linear combination of them. The uniqueness of the representation is what makes component form so powerful: there is exactly one set of numbers $(A_x, A_y, A_z)$ for each vector $\vec{A}$ relative to a given orthonormal basis. Orthonormal basis A set of vectors $\{\hat{e}_1, \hat{e}_2, \hat{e}_3\}$ is orthonormal if \hat{e}_i \cdot \hat{e}_j = \delta_{ij} \text{ where } \delta_{ij}=1 \text{ if } i=j \text{ else } 0. Why orthonormal? Any other basis works mathematically but makes arithmetic messy. Orthonormal bases make dot products, magnitudes, and projections clean — which is why $\hat{i},\hat{j},\hat{k}$ are used everywhere in physics.
documents.applications: Physics uses several notational systems for vectors — each invented for a different context. Handwriting uses arrows, print uses bold, engineering uses unit-vector notation. Not knowing the equivalences leads to confusion on every exam and in every textbook. Unit vector A vector of magnitude exactly 1. Written with a "hat": $\hat{u}$. Unit vectors only carry direction — no size. $\hat{i}$ points along $+x$, $\hat{j}$ along $+y$, $\hat{k}$ along $+z$. All notations are the same object $\vec{A}$, $\mathbf{A}$, $(A_x,A_y)$, $A_x\hat{i}+A_y\hat{j}$, and $|\vec{A}|\angle\theta$ all describe the same vector. The notation choice is a matter of context, not meaning.
documents.content: You open three different physics textbooks. One writes $\vec{F}$, one writes $\mathbf{F}$, one writes $F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j}$. Are they talking about the same thing? Physics uses several notational systems for vectors — each invented for a different context. Handwriting uses arrows, print uses bold, engineering uses unit-vector notation. Not knowing the equivalences leads to confusion on every exam and in every textbook. A vector is a single mathematical idea — but it gets dressed in different clothes depending on the context. Think of it like a person's name: "Elizabeth", "Liz", and "Dr. Smith" all refer to the same person. In handwriting you draw a small arrow over the symbol: $\vec{F}$. Arrows are slow to typeset, so printed textbooks use bold: $\mathbf{F}$. In engineering and computation, you write out the components explicitly using unit vectors $\hat{i}$, $\hat{j}$, $\hat{k}$. The magnitude of a vector — its size stripped of direction — is written with vertical bars: $|\vec{A}|$ or sometimes just $A$ (no arrow, no bold). Context always tells you which is meant. The **unit vector** in the direction of $\vec{A}$ is found by dividing $\vec{A}$ by its magnitude: This works because dividing each component by $|\vec{A}|$ shrinks the arrow to length 1 without changing its direction. In 3-D, we add a third component and a third basis vector $\hat{k}$ along the $z$-axis: The basis vectors $\hat{i}$, $\hat{j}$, $\hat{k}$ are special because they are **orthonormal**: mutually perpendicular (orthogonal) and each of unit length (normal). Any vector in 3-D space can be written uniquely as a linear combination of them. The uniqueness of the representation is what makes component form so powerful: there is exactly one set of numbers $(A_x, A_y, A_z)$ for each vector $\vec{A}$ relative to a given orthonormal basis. Orthonormal basis A set of vectors $\{\hat{e}_1, \hat{e}_2, \hat{e}_3\}$ is orthonormal if \hat{e}_i \cdot \hat{e}_j = \delta_{ij} \text{ where } \delta_{ij}=1 \text{ if } i=j \text{ else } 0. Why orthonormal? Any other basis works mathematically but makes arithmetic messy. Orthonormal bases make dot products, magnitudes, and projections clean — which is why $\hat{i},\hat{j},\hat{k}$ are used everywhere in physics. Unit vector A vector of magnitude exactly 1. Written with a "hat": $\hat{u}$. Unit vectors only carry direction — no size. $\hat{i}$ points along $+x$, $\hat{j}$ along $+y$, $\hat{k}$ along $+z$. All notations are the same object $\vec{A}$, $\mathbf{A}$, $(A_x,A_y)$, $A_x\hat{i}+A_y\hat{j}$, and $|\vec{A}|\angle\theta$ all describe the same vector. The notation choice is a matter of context, not meaning. Unit vector in the direction of $\vec{A}$ \hat{A} = \frac{\vec{A}}{|\vec{A}|} = \frac{A_x\hat{i} + A_y\hat{j}}{\sqrt{A_x^2+A_y^2}} 3-D unit-vector notation \vec{A} = A_x\hat{i} + A_y\hat{j} + A_z\hat{k} Standard basis vectors \hat{i} = (1,0,0),\quad \hat{j} = (0,1,0),\quad \hat{k} = (0,0,1). \text{ Each has magnitude 1 and points along one coordinate axis.} Don't confuse $\hat{i}$ with $i = \sqrt{-1}$ In physics, $\hat{i}$ is the unit vector along $+x$. In mathematics, $i$ is the imaginary unit. Context (and the hat) distinguishes them. Click a notation — see the same vector appear five ways Same object, five outfits. Build a unit vector from any arrow Dividing by the magnitude never changes direction, only length. Step through the unit-vector derivation The proof is just one division — but worth seeing geometrically. Writing a vector in every notation \text{A force } \vec{F} \text{ has components } F_x = 3\,N,\; F_y = 4\,N. \text{Write it in (a) component notation, (b) unit-vector notation, (c) magnitude–angle form, (d) as a unit vector.} Any vector can be translated between all four notations. Practice until the conversions are automatic. \text{(a) } \vec{F} = (3,\,4)\,N Component/bracket notation. \text{(b) } \vec{F} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}\,N Unit-vector notation — write each component times its basis vector. \text{(c) } |\vec{F}| = \sqrt{9+16} = 5\,N,\quad \theta = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1° Magnitude–angle form. Both components positive → Quadrant I, no correction needed. \text{(d) } \hat{F} = \frac{\vec{F}}{|\vec{F}|} = \frac{3\hat{i}+4\hat{j}}{5} = 0.6\hat{i}+0.8\hat{j} Divide each component by the magnitude. The result has magnitude 1. Identifying notation in the wild \text{Match each expression to its notation type: } (a)\;\mathbf{v},\; (b)\;|\vec{v}|,\; (c)\;v_x\hat{i}+v_y\hat{j},\; (d)\;(v_x,v_y),\; (e)\;\hat{v}. Notation fluency is a prerequisite for every subsequent topic. If any of these felt slow, drill the pattern viz. \text{(a) } \mathbf{v} \Rightarrow \text{bold notation (vector)} Bold in print = arrow in handwriting. \text{(b) } |\vec{v}| \Rightarrow \text{magnitude (scalar)} Vertical bars strip the direction — result is a positive number. \text{(c) } v_x\hat{i}+v_y\hat{j} \Rightarrow \text{unit-vector notation (vector)} Expanded into basis vectors. \text{(d) } (v_x,v_y) \Rightarrow \text{component notation (vector)} Ordered pair of components. \text{(e) } \hat{v} \Rightarrow \text{unit vector (magnitude = 1)} The hat signals direction only, magnitude = 1. \text{Find the unit vector in the direction of } \vec{A} = (-5,\,12). Compute $|\vec{A}|$ first, then divide each component. \hat{A} = \bigl(-\tfrac{5}{13},\;\tfrac{12}{13}\bigr) |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25+144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 The 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple. \hat{A} = \frac{(-5,\,12)}{13} = \bigl(-\tfrac{5}{13},\;\tfrac{12}{13}\bigr) Divide each component by 13. \text{Verify: } \bigl|\hat{A}\bigr| = \sqrt{(5/13)^2+(12/13)^2} = \sqrt{25/169+144/169} = \sqrt{169/169} = 1\checkmark Always verify the result has magnitude 1. \text{A 3-D vector is given as } \vec{B} = 2\hat{i} - 6\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}. \text{Find } |\vec{B}| \text{ and } \hat{B}. The Pythagorean theorem extends to 3-D: $|\vec{B}| = \sqrt{B_x^2+B_y^2+B_z^2}$. |\vec{B}| = 7,\quad \hat{B} = \tfrac{2}{7}\hat{i} - \tfrac{6}{7}\hat{j} + \tfrac{3}{7}\hat{k} |\vec{B}| = \sqrt{2^2+(-6)^2+3^2} = \sqrt{4+36+9} = \sqrt{49} = 7 3-D Pythagorean theorem. \hat{B} = \frac{2\hat{i}-6\hat{j}+3\hat{k}}{7} = \tfrac{2}{7}\hat{i} - \tfrac{6}{7}\hat{j} + \tfrac{3}{7}\hat{k} Divide each component by 7. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Components and Magnitudes
documents.subtitle: How to pull a vector apart — and put it back together.
documents.tags: components magnitude decomposition Pythagorean theorem trig
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Decomposing a velocity vector \text{A ball is launched at } |\vec{v}| = 20\,m/s \text{ at } \theta = 35° \text{ above the horizontal. Find the horizontal and vertical components.}   $v_x \approx 16.4\,m/s$ (horizontal), $v_y \approx 11.5\,m/s$ (vertical). v_x = |\vec{v}|\cos\theta = 20\cos35° Horizontal = magnitude × cos(angle). v_x = 20 \times 0.8192 \approx 16.4\,m/s $\cos35° \approx 0.8192$. This is the speed the ball moves horizontally. v_y = |\vec{v}|\sin\theta = 20\sin35° Vertical = magnitude × sin(angle). v_y = 20 \times 0.5736 \approx 11.5\,m/s $\sin35° \approx 0.5736$. This is the initial upward speed. \text{Check: }\sqrt{16.4^2 + 11.5^2} = \sqrt{268.96 + 132.25} = \sqrt{401.2} \approx 20.0\,m/s\checkmark Recovering the magnitude confirms correctness. Set magnitude = 20, angle = 35° and read off the components The right triangle makes the result visible. Angle measured from the y-axis \text{A force has magnitude } 50\,N \text{ at } 20° \textit{ from the vertical } (y\text{-axis}). \text{Find } F_x \text{ and } F_y.   $F_x \approx 17.1\,N$, $F_y \approx 47.0\,N$. Always convert non-standard angles before applying the formulas. \theta_{\text{from}\,x} = 90° - 20° = 70° "20° from vertical" means 20° from the $y$-axis. Convert to the standard angle (from $+x$): $90° - 20° = 70°$. F_x = 50\cos70° \approx 50 \times 0.342 = 17.1\,N Use the converted angle. F_y = 50\sin70° \approx 50 \times 0.940 = 47.0\,N Large $y$-component makes sense — the vector is mostly vertical. Component to magnitude and angle — Quadrant III \text{An acceleration vector has components } a_x = -4.0\,m/s^2,\; a_y = -3.0\,m/s^2. \text{Find the magnitude and direction.}   $|\vec{a}| = 5.0\,m/s^2$ at $\theta \approx 217°$ from the positive $x$-axis. |\vec{a}| = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{16+9} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0\,m/s^2 The 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple. \theta_{\text{ref}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36.9° Reference angle using absolute values. a_x < 0\;\text{and}\;a_y < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Quadrant III} \Rightarrow \theta = 180° + 36.9° = 216.9° Both components negative → Quadrant III. Add 180° to the reference angle. Could also be stated as "36.9° south of west" or "$-143.1°$" depending on convention.  \text{Find the components of a displacement of } 15\,m \text{ at } 60°\text{ above the }+x\text{-axis.} $d_x = 15\cos60°$, $d_y = 15\sin60°$. Recall $\cos60° = 0.5$, $\sin60° = \sqrt{3}/2$. d_x = 7.5\,m,\quad d_y = \tfrac{15\sqrt{3}}{2}\,m \approx 13.0\,m  d_x = 15\cos60° = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5\,m Exact value: $\cos60° = 1/2$. d_y = 15\sin60° = 15 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{15\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 12.99\,m Exact value: $\sin60° = \sqrt{3}/2$.  \text{A vector has } A_x = 7.0 \text{ and } A_y = -7.0. \text{ Find } |\vec{A}| \text{ and } \theta. With equal magnitudes and opposite-sign components, what angle do you expect? |\vec{A}| = 7\sqrt{2},\quad \theta = 315°\;(-45°)  |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{7^2 + 7^2} = 7\sqrt{2} \approx 9.9 Both components equal in magnitude. \theta_{\text{ref}} = \arctan(7/7) = \arctan(1) = 45° Reference angle is 45°. A_x > 0,\;A_y < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Quadrant IV} \Rightarrow \theta = 360° - 45° = 315° Or equivalently $-45°$.  \text{A pilot flies at } 200\,km/h \text{ on a bearing of N 30° E (30° east of due north). Find the east and north components of velocity.} Bearing N 30° E means 30° measured from north toward east. North is the $+y$ direction. The angle from the $+x$ (east) axis is $90° - 30° = 60°$. v_{\text{east}} = 100\,km/h,\quad v_{\text{north}} = 100\sqrt{3} \approx 173\,km/h  \theta_{\text{from east}} = 90° - 30° = 60° Convert bearing to standard angle measured from east ($+x$). v_{\text{east}} = 200\cos60° = 200 \times 0.5 = 100\,km/h East is the $x$-component. v_{\text{north}} = 200\sin60° = 200 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 100\sqrt{3} \approx 173\,km/h North is the $y$-component. \text{Check: }\sqrt{100^2+(100\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{10000+30000} = \sqrt{40000} = 200\,km/h\checkmark Magnitude recovered correctly.
documents.proofs: The component formulas are consequences of the definitions of sine and cosine on the unit circle. Here we derive them from first principles so the formulas are no longer "things to memorise" but things you could re-derive on the spot. The unit circle is the bridge Place a unit vector at angle $\theta$. Its tip lands at $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ by definition. Scaling by $|\vec{A}|$ gives $(|\vec{A}|\cos\theta,\; |\vec{A}|\sin\theta)$ — those are the components.
documents.applications: Every projectile problem, every incline problem, every force analysis in physics begins with the same move: split the vector into horizontal and vertical pieces. Components are the fundamental tool of applied physics. Components of a vector For $\vec{A}$ with magnitude $|\vec{A}|$ at angle $\theta$ from $+x$: A_x = |\vec{A}|\cos\theta,\quad A_y = |\vec{A}|\sin\theta. The right-triangle picture Draw $\vec{A}$. Drop a perpendicular to the $x$-axis. The hypotenuse is $|\vec{A}|$, the adjacent leg is $A_x$, the opposite leg is $A_y$. SOH-CAH-TOA does the rest.
documents.content: A ball is kicked at 20 m/s at 35° above the ground. How fast is it moving horizontally? How fast vertically? You cannot answer either question without decomposing the vector. Every projectile problem, every incline problem, every force analysis in physics begins with the same move: split the vector into horizontal and vertical pieces. Components are the fundamental tool of applied physics. Picture walking from one corner of a city block to the diagonally opposite corner. You cannot walk diagonally — the buildings are in the way. Instead you walk some distance east, then some distance north. Those two legs — the "east part" and the "north part" — are the *components* of your displacement. Every vector in 2-D has exactly two components: the amount it reaches along $x$ and the amount it reaches along $y$. The original vector is the hypotenuse; the components are the two legs of a right triangle. To find the components you need exactly one tool: trigonometry. The angle $\theta$ (measured from the positive $x$-axis) and the magnitude $|\vec{A}|$ are all the ingredients you need. The two formulas for components come directly from the definitions of sine and cosine. Here is the complete two-way conversion table: A critical skill is recognising which angle to use. The formulas $A_x = |\vec{A}|\cos\theta$ and $A_y = |\vec{A}|\sin\theta$ assume $\theta$ is measured **counterclockwise from the positive $x$-axis**. If a problem gives you an angle measured from a different reference (e.g. from the $y$-axis, or "below the horizontal"), convert to the standard angle first. When recovering the angle from components, always sketch the vector to confirm the quadrant. A calculator's $\arctan$ only covers $(-90°, 90°)$, so you must manually apply quadrant corrections. The component formulas are consequences of the definitions of sine and cosine on the unit circle. Here we derive them from first principles so the formulas are no longer "things to memorise" but things you could re-derive on the spot. The unit circle is the bridge Place a unit vector at angle $\theta$. Its tip lands at $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ by definition. Scaling by $|\vec{A}|$ gives $(|\vec{A}|\cos\theta,\; |\vec{A}|\sin\theta)$ — those are the components. Components of a vector For $\vec{A}$ with magnitude $|\vec{A}|$ at angle $\theta$ from $+x$: A_x = |\vec{A}|\cos\theta,\quad A_y = |\vec{A}|\sin\theta. The right-triangle picture Draw $\vec{A}$. Drop a perpendicular to the $x$-axis. The hypotenuse is $|\vec{A}|$, the adjacent leg is $A_x$, the opposite leg is $A_y$. SOH-CAH-TOA does the rest. Forward: magnitude + angle → components A_x = |\vec{A}|\cos\theta \qquad A_y = |\vec{A}|\sin\theta Inverse: components → magnitude + angle |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{A_x^2 + A_y^2} \qquad \theta = \operatorname{atan2}(A_y,\, A_x) atan2 — the quadrant-aware arctangent \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) \text{ returns the angle in } (-180°, 180°] \text{ by checking the signs of both arguments. Use this instead of plain } \arctan \text{ whenever components can be negative.} Angle reference point matters If a problem states "30° above the horizontal", $\theta = 30°$ in the standard formulas. If it states "30° from the vertical", $\theta = 60°$ from the horizontal. Always convert to "from the positive $x$-axis, counterclockwise" before plugging in. Drag the vector — watch the right triangle appear Components are just shadows: $A_x$ is the shadow on the $x$-axis, $A_y$ on the $y$-axis. Forward and inverse conversion — two sliders, four numbers Build fluency with both directions of the conversion. Unit circle → component formulas, step by step Every component formula is just $\cos$ or $\sin$ scaled by the magnitude. Decomposing a velocity vector \text{A ball is launched at } |\vec{v}| = 20\,m/s \text{ at } \theta = 35° \text{ above the horizontal. Find the horizontal and vertical components.} $v_x \approx 16.4\,m/s$ (horizontal), $v_y \approx 11.5\,m/s$ (vertical). v_x = |\vec{v}|\cos\theta = 20\cos35° Horizontal = magnitude × cos(angle). v_x = 20 \times 0.8192 \approx 16.4\,m/s $\cos35° \approx 0.8192$. This is the speed the ball moves horizontally. v_y = |\vec{v}|\sin\theta = 20\sin35° Vertical = magnitude × sin(angle). v_y = 20 \times 0.5736 \approx 11.5\,m/s $\sin35° \approx 0.5736$. This is the initial upward speed. \text{Check: }\sqrt{16.4^2 + 11.5^2} = \sqrt{268.96 + 132.25} = \sqrt{401.2} \approx 20.0\,m/s\checkmark Recovering the magnitude confirms correctness. Set magnitude = 20, angle = 35° and read off the components The right triangle makes the result visible. Angle measured from the y-axis \text{A force has magnitude } 50\,N \text{ at } 20° \textit{ from the vertical } (y\text{-axis}). \text{Find } F_x \text{ and } F_y. $F_x \approx 17.1\,N$, $F_y \approx 47.0\,N$. Always convert non-standard angles before applying the formulas. \theta_{\text{from}\,x} = 90° - 20° = 70° "20° from vertical" means 20° from the $y$-axis. Convert to the standard angle (from $+x$): $90° - 20° = 70°$. F_x = 50\cos70° \approx 50 \times 0.342 = 17.1\,N Use the converted angle. F_y = 50\sin70° \approx 50 \times 0.940 = 47.0\,N Large $y$-component makes sense — the vector is mostly vertical. Component to magnitude and angle — Quadrant III \text{An acceleration vector has components } a_x = -4.0\,m/s^2,\; a_y = -3.0\,m/s^2. \text{Find the magnitude and direction.} $|\vec{a}| = 5.0\,m/s^2$ at $\theta \approx 217°$ from the positive $x$-axis. |\vec{a}| = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-3)^2} = \sqrt{16+9} = \sqrt{25} = 5.0\,m/s^2 The 3-4-5 Pythagorean triple. \theta_{\text{ref}} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) \approx 36.9° Reference angle using absolute values. a_x < 0\;\text{and}\;a_y < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Quadrant III} \Rightarrow \theta = 180° + 36.9° = 216.9° Both components negative → Quadrant III. Add 180° to the reference angle. Could also be stated as "36.9° south of west" or "$-143.1°$" depending on convention. \text{Find the components of a displacement of } 15\,m \text{ at } 60°\text{ above the }+x\text{-axis.} $d_x = 15\cos60°$, $d_y = 15\sin60°$. Recall $\cos60° = 0.5$, $\sin60° = \sqrt{3}/2$. d_x = 7.5\,m,\quad d_y = \tfrac{15\sqrt{3}}{2}\,m \approx 13.0\,m d_x = 15\cos60° = 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5\,m Exact value: $\cos60° = 1/2$. d_y = 15\sin60° = 15 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{15\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 12.99\,m Exact value: $\sin60° = \sqrt{3}/2$. \text{A vector has } A_x = 7.0 \text{ and } A_y = -7.0. \text{ Find } |\vec{A}| \text{ and } \theta. With equal magnitudes and opposite-sign components, what angle do you expect? |\vec{A}| = 7\sqrt{2},\quad \theta = 315°\;(-45°) |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{7^2 + 7^2} = 7\sqrt{2} \approx 9.9 Both components equal in magnitude. \theta_{\text{ref}} = \arctan(7/7) = \arctan(1) = 45° Reference angle is 45°. A_x > 0,\;A_y < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Quadrant IV} \Rightarrow \theta = 360° - 45° = 315° Or equivalently $-45°$. \text{A pilot flies at } 200\,km/h \text{ on a bearing of N 30° E (30° east of due north). Find the east and north components of velocity.} Bearing N 30° E means 30° measured from north toward east. North is the $+y$ direction. The angle from the $+x$ (east) axis is $90° - 30° = 60°$. v_{\text{east}} = 100\,km/h,\quad v_{\text{north}} = 100\sqrt{3} \approx 173\,km/h \theta_{\text{from east}} = 90° - 30° = 60° Convert bearing to standard angle measured from east ($+x$). v_{\text{east}} = 200\cos60° = 200 \times 0.5 = 100\,km/h East is the $x$-component. v_{\text{north}} = 200\sin60° = 200 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 100\sqrt{3} \approx 173\,km/h North is the $y$-component. \text{Check: }\sqrt{100^2+(100\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{10000+30000} = \sqrt{40000} = 200\,km/h\checkmark Magnitude recovered correctly. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Adding Vectors Graphically — Parallelogram Method
documents.subtitle: Two arrows into one: the diagonal of the parallelogram they form.
documents.tags: vector addition parallelogram resultant graphical method
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Resultant of two perpendicular forces \text{Force } \vec{A} = 30\,N \text{ east and } \vec{B} = 40\,N \text{ north act on an object. Find } |\vec{R}| \text{ and its direction.}   $\vec{R} = 50\,N$ at $53.1°$ north of east. The parallelogram is a rectangle here because the vectors are perpendicular. R_x = A_x + B_x = 30 + 0 = 30\,N East component: A contributes 30, B contributes 0. R_y = A_y + B_y = 0 + 40 = 40\,N North component: A contributes 0, B contributes 40. |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900+1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\,N 3-4-5 triple scaled by 10. \theta = \arctan(40/30) = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1°\text{ north of east} Angle from east (+x axis). Both components positive → Quadrant I. Set A⃗ east, B⃗ north and see the rectangle Perpendicular vectors form a rectangle, not a general parallelogram. Two forces at 60° — using the parallelogram law of cosines \text{Two forces } |\vec{A}| = 40\,N \text{ and } |\vec{B}| = 30\,N \text{ act at } 60° \text{ to each other. Find } |\vec{R}|.   $|\vec{R}| \approx 60.8\,N$. Note that $40 + 30 = 70\,N$ (max, same direction) and $|40-30| = 10\,N$ (min, opposite). 60.8 N is between these bounds, as expected. |\vec{R}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi The law of cosines applied to the triangle formed inside the parallelogram. φ = 60° is the angle between the two vectors. |\vec{R}|^2 = 40^2 + 30^2 + 2(40)(30)\cos60° Substitute values. |\vec{R}|^2 = 1600 + 900 + 2400 \times 0.5 = 2500 + 1200 = 3700 $\cos60° = 0.5$. |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{3700} \approx 60.8\,N Take the square root.  \text{Two vectors have magnitudes 5 and 12 and are perpendicular. Find the resultant magnitude.} Perpendicular vectors form a right triangle. Use the Pythagorean theorem. |\vec{R}| = 13  |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple.  \text{Forces of 8 N and 8 N act at 120° to each other. Find the resultant magnitude.} Use the law of cosines: $|R|^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\phi$ where $\phi = 120°$. |\vec{R}| = 8\,N  |\vec{R}|^2 = 64 + 64 + 2(8)(8)\cos120° Apply law of cosines. = 128 + 128(-0.5) = 128 - 64 = 64 $\cos120° = -0.5$. |\vec{R}| = 8\,N Two equal forces at 120° give a resultant equal to either force.  \text{Prove that } |\vec{A} + \vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 \text{ if and only if } \vec{A} \perp \vec{B}. Expand $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2$ using components. A perpendicular condition produces a specific dot-product result. The condition $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2$ is equivalent to $\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0$, which means $\vec{A}\perp\vec{B}$.  |\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = (A_x+B_x)^2 + (A_y+B_y)^2 Expand the squared magnitude. = A_x^2 + 2A_xB_x + B_x^2 + A_y^2 + 2A_yB_y + B_y^2 Expand each square. = (A_x^2+A_y^2) + (B_x^2+B_y^2) + 2(A_xB_x+A_yB_y) Regroup into three parts. = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}) Recognise the dot product. \therefore\;|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2+|\vec{B}|^2 \iff \vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0 \iff \vec{A}\perp\vec{B} The Pythagorean theorem for vectors holds exactly when the dot product vanishes — i.e. when the vectors are perpendicular.
documents.proofs: The parallelogram law follows directly from the definition of vector addition in $\mathbb{R}^n$: $(A_x, A_y) + (B_x, B_y) = (A_x+B_x, A_y+B_y)$. The geometric construction is simply a way of visualising this component-wise sum. Vector addition (formal) \vec{A} + \vec{B} = (A_x + B_x)\,\hat{i} + (A_y + B_y)\,\hat{j} Why the diagonal? The four vertices of the parallelogram are $\mathbf{0}$, $\vec{A}$, $\vec{B}$, $\vec{A}+\vec{B}$. The diagonal from $\mathbf{0}$ to $\vec{A}+\vec{B}$ is exactly the component sum — the geometry reflects the algebra.
documents.applications: Every time two forces act simultaneously — a kite in the wind, a ship fighting a current, a joint in a truss — you need to find their combined effect. The parallelogram method is the oldest and most visual way to do that. Resultant vector \vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} — the single vector with the same effect as applying both $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$. Tail-to-tail placement Both vectors must share a common tail before you draw the parallelogram. If tails are not at the same point, translate one arrow. Commutativity \vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{B} + \vec{A}. The parallelogram diagonal is the same regardless of which vector is labelled first.
documents.content: Two forces pull on a boat — one northeast, one northwest. What single force replaces them both? Every time two forces act simultaneously — a kite in the wind, a ship fighting a current, a joint in a truss — you need to find their combined effect. The parallelogram method is the oldest and most visual way to do that. Place two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ so their **tails share the same point**. Draw $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ as two sides of a shape. Complete the parallelogram by copying each vector from the tip of the other. The **diagonal** from the shared tail to the opposite corner is the resultant $\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$. The key insight is that this diagonal simultaneously captures both contributions. It is the single vector that has exactly the same effect as applying $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ together. Vector addition is **commutative**: $\vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{B} + \vec{A}$. The parallelogram makes this obvious — the same diagonal results whether you call the sides $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ or $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{A}$. The parallelogram method is geometric — it does not require any calculation. But it is limited by the precision of your drawing. For exact answers we always convert to components (Lesson 6). The parallelogram tells you *what to expect* before you calculate. A useful bound: the magnitude of the resultant satisfies $\bigl||\vec{A}| - |\vec{B}|\bigr| \le |\vec{R}| \le |\vec{A}| + |\vec{B}|$. The maximum is reached when vectors are parallel (same direction); the minimum when they are anti-parallel (opposite directions). The parallelogram law follows directly from the definition of vector addition in $\mathbb{R}^n$: $(A_x, A_y) + (B_x, B_y) = (A_x+B_x, A_y+B_y)$. The geometric construction is simply a way of visualising this component-wise sum. Vector addition (formal) \vec{A} + \vec{B} = (A_x + B_x)\,\hat{i} + (A_y + B_y)\,\hat{j} Why the diagonal? The four vertices of the parallelogram are $\mathbf{0}$, $\vec{A}$, $\vec{B}$, $\vec{A}+\vec{B}$. The diagonal from $\mathbf{0}$ to $\vec{A}+\vec{B}$ is exactly the component sum — the geometry reflects the algebra. Resultant vector \vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} — the single vector with the same effect as applying both $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$. Tail-to-tail placement Both vectors must share a common tail before you draw the parallelogram. If tails are not at the same point, translate one arrow. Commutativity \vec{A} + \vec{B} = \vec{B} + \vec{A}. The parallelogram diagonal is the same regardless of which vector is labelled first. Magnitude bounds (triangle inequality) \bigl||\vec{A}| - |\vec{B}|\bigr| \;\le\; |\vec{A}+\vec{B}| \;\le\; |\vec{A}| + |\vec{B}| The resultant is usually NOT |A| + |B| |\vec{R}| = |\vec{A}| + |\vec{B}| only when both vectors point in exactly the same direction. For any other angle, the resultant is shorter than this sum. Check with extreme cases Same direction: $|\vec{R}| = |\vec{A}| + |\vec{B}|$ (maximum). Opposite: $|\vec{R}| = \bigl||\vec{A}| - |\vec{B}|\bigr|$ (minimum). Perpendicular: $|\vec{R}| = \sqrt{|\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2}$. Drag both vectors — watch the parallelogram build and the diagonal appear Both arrows share a tail. The diagonal is the sum. Rotate one vector — watch the resultant magnitude change The angle between vectors controls everything about the resultant magnitude. Watch the algebra and geometry sync step by step The diagonal IS the component sum — the geometry is not separate from the algebra. Resultant of two perpendicular forces \text{Force } \vec{A} = 30\,N \text{ east and } \vec{B} = 40\,N \text{ north act on an object. Find } |\vec{R}| \text{ and its direction.} $\vec{R} = 50\,N$ at $53.1°$ north of east. The parallelogram is a rectangle here because the vectors are perpendicular. R_x = A_x + B_x = 30 + 0 = 30\,N East component: A contributes 30, B contributes 0. R_y = A_y + B_y = 0 + 40 = 40\,N North component: A contributes 0, B contributes 40. |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900+1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\,N 3-4-5 triple scaled by 10. \theta = \arctan(40/30) = \arctan(4/3) \approx 53.1°\text{ north of east} Angle from east (+x axis). Both components positive → Quadrant I. Set A⃗ east, B⃗ north and see the rectangle Perpendicular vectors form a rectangle, not a general parallelogram. Two forces at 60° — using the parallelogram law of cosines \text{Two forces } |\vec{A}| = 40\,N \text{ and } |\vec{B}| = 30\,N \text{ act at } 60° \text{ to each other. Find } |\vec{R}|. $|\vec{R}| \approx 60.8\,N$. Note that $40 + 30 = 70\,N$ (max, same direction) and $|40-30| = 10\,N$ (min, opposite). 60.8 N is between these bounds, as expected. |\vec{R}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi The law of cosines applied to the triangle formed inside the parallelogram. φ = 60° is the angle between the two vectors. |\vec{R}|^2 = 40^2 + 30^2 + 2(40)(30)\cos60° Substitute values. |\vec{R}|^2 = 1600 + 900 + 2400 \times 0.5 = 2500 + 1200 = 3700 $\cos60° = 0.5$. |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{3700} \approx 60.8\,N Take the square root. \text{Two vectors have magnitudes 5 and 12 and are perpendicular. Find the resultant magnitude.} Perpendicular vectors form a right triangle. Use the Pythagorean theorem. |\vec{R}| = 13 |\vec{R}| = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple. \text{Forces of 8 N and 8 N act at 120° to each other. Find the resultant magnitude.} Use the law of cosines: $|R|^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2AB\cos\phi$ where $\phi = 120°$. |\vec{R}| = 8\,N |\vec{R}|^2 = 64 + 64 + 2(8)(8)\cos120° Apply law of cosines. = 128 + 128(-0.5) = 128 - 64 = 64 $\cos120° = -0.5$. |\vec{R}| = 8\,N Two equal forces at 120° give a resultant equal to either force. \text{Prove that } |\vec{A} + \vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 \text{ if and only if } \vec{A} \perp \vec{B}. Expand $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2$ using components. A perpendicular condition produces a specific dot-product result. The condition $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2$ is equivalent to $\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0$, which means $\vec{A}\perp\vec{B}$. |\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = (A_x+B_x)^2 + (A_y+B_y)^2 Expand the squared magnitude. = A_x^2 + 2A_xB_x + B_x^2 + A_y^2 + 2A_yB_y + B_y^2 Expand each square. = (A_x^2+A_y^2) + (B_x^2+B_y^2) + 2(A_xB_x+A_yB_y) Regroup into three parts. = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 + 2(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}) Recognise the dot product. \therefore\;|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2+|\vec{B}|^2 \iff \vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0 \iff \vec{A}\perp\vec{B} The Pythagorean theorem for vectors holds exactly when the dot product vanishes — i.e. when the vectors are perpendicular. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Adding Vectors Graphically — Tip-to-Toe Method
documents.subtitle: Chain arrows end-to-end; the shortcut from start to finish is the sum.
documents.tags: vector addition tip-to-toe head-to-tail triangle method polygon method
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Three-vector chain \text{Displacements: } \vec{A}=3\,\text{km east},\; \vec{B}=4\,\text{km north},\; \vec{C}=2\,\text{km at }30°. \text{Find the resultant.}   The resultant displacement is $\approx 6.88$ km at $46.6°$ north of east, regardless of the order you walk the three legs. A_x=3,\;A_y=0 East → positive x. B_x=0,\;B_y=4 North → positive y. C_x=2\cos30°=\sqrt{3}\approx1.73,\;C_y=2\sin30°=1.00 Decompose the angled vector. R_x=3+0+1.73=4.73\,\text{km},\;R_y=0+4+1.00=5.00\,\text{km} Sum components. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{4.73^2+5.00^2}=\sqrt{22.37+25.00}=\sqrt{47.37}\approx6.88\,\text{km} Resultant magnitude. \theta=\arctan(5.00/4.73)\approx46.6°\text{ north of east} Direction from +x axis. Zero resultant — closed polygon \text{Vectors } \vec{A}=(3,0),\;\vec{B}=(0,4),\;\vec{C}=(-3,-4). \text{ Show that } \vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C}=\vec{0}.   When vectors form a closed polygon, their sum is zero. This is the graphical condition for equilibrium. R_x = 3+0+(-3) = 0 x-components sum to zero. R_y = 0+4+(-4) = 0 y-components sum to zero. \vec{R}=(0,0)=\vec{0}\;\checkmark The three vectors form a closed triangle — the tip of C⃗ returns exactly to the tail of A⃗.  \text{A robot moves: 5 m east, then 5 m north, then 5 m west. What is the resultant displacement?} Sum the x-components and y-components separately. \vec{R} = 5\,\text{m north}  R_x = 5 + 0 + (-5) = 0\,\text{m} East and west cancel. R_y = 0 + 5 + 0 = 5\,\text{m} Only the north leg contributes vertically. |\vec{R}| = 5\,\text{m north} The three-step journey is equivalent to a single 5 m step north.  \text{Four forces act on a pin: 10 N east, 8 N at 90°, 6 N at 180°, 4 N at 270°. Find } \vec{R}. 90° = north, 180° = west, 270° = south. |\vec{R}| = 4\sqrt{2}\,N\text{ at }45°\text{ (NE)}  R_x = 10+0+(-6)+0 = 4\,N East=+x, west=−x. R_y = 0+8+0+(-4) = 4\,N North=+y, south=−y. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{16+16}=4\sqrt{2}\approx5.66\,N\text{ at }45° Equal x and y → 45°.
documents.proofs: Vector addition is both **commutative** ($\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{B}+\vec{A}$) and **associative** ($(\vec{A}+\vec{B})+\vec{C}=\vec{A}+(\vec{B}+\vec{C})$). Together these two properties mean the order and grouping of a chain of vectors is irrelevant. Commutativity of vector addition \vec{A}+\vec{B} = \vec{B}+\vec{A} Associativity of vector addition (\vec{A}+\vec{B})+\vec{C} = \vec{A}+(\vec{B}+\vec{C})
documents.applications: Navigation, animation rigs, robot arm kinematics — any time you chain a sequence of moves, you need the tip-to-toe method. It scales to any number of vectors, unlike the parallelogram which only handles two at a time. Tip-to-toe rule Place vectors in a chain, each tail touching the previous tip. The resultant goes from the first tail to the last tip. Order independence $\vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C} = \vec{B}+\vec{A}+\vec{C} = \vec{C}+\vec{A}+\vec{B}$. Rearranging the chain changes the path but not the endpoint. Tip-to-toe ≠ tail-to-tail In the parallelogram method, tails are shared. In tip-to-toe, each tail connects to the previous tip. Mixing these up is the most common graphical error.
documents.content: You walk 3 km east, then 4 km north, then 2 km at 30°. What single displacement takes you from start to finish? Navigation, animation rigs, robot arm kinematics — any time you chain a sequence of moves, you need the tip-to-toe method. It scales to any number of vectors, unlike the parallelogram which only handles two at a time. The **tip-to-toe** (head-to-tail) method: place the tail of $\vec{B}$ at the tip of $\vec{A}$. The resultant $\vec{R}$ is the arrow from the tail of $\vec{A}$ to the tip of $\vec{B}$. It is the "shortcut" that replaces the whole journey. The method generalises immediately to any number of vectors: $\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C} + \cdots$ — chain them in sequence and draw the closing arrow. The order does not matter: the closing arrow is the same regardless of the sequence you chain them in. The key difference from the parallelogram method: vectors are placed **tip-to-tail** (not tail-to-tail). You lose the visual symmetry of the parallelogram but gain the ability to add three or more vectors at once. For $n$ vectors, the resultant is found by summing each set of components independently: This is why component methods (Lesson 6) are the practical tool: the graphical method gives insight, but arithmetic gives precision. Vector addition is both **commutative** ($\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{B}+\vec{A}$) and **associative** ($(\vec{A}+\vec{B})+\vec{C}=\vec{A}+(\vec{B}+\vec{C})$). Together these two properties mean the order and grouping of a chain of vectors is irrelevant. Commutativity of vector addition \vec{A}+\vec{B} = \vec{B}+\vec{A} Associativity of vector addition (\vec{A}+\vec{B})+\vec{C} = \vec{A}+(\vec{B}+\vec{C}) Tip-to-toe rule Place vectors in a chain, each tail touching the previous tip. The resultant goes from the first tail to the last tip. Order independence $\vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C} = \vec{B}+\vec{A}+\vec{C} = \vec{C}+\vec{A}+\vec{B}$. Rearranging the chain changes the path but not the endpoint. Tip-to-toe ≠ tail-to-tail In the parallelogram method, tails are shared. In tip-to-toe, each tail connects to the previous tip. Mixing these up is the most common graphical error. Sum of n vectors \vec{R} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vec{v}_k \implies R_x = \sum_{k=1}^n v_{kx},\quad R_y = \sum_{k=1}^n v_{ky} Closing the polygon If $\vec{R} = \vec{0}$, the tip of the last vector lands exactly on the tail of the first. The vectors form a closed polygon — this is the condition for static equilibrium. Chain up to 4 vectors — drag, reorder, watch the resultant close the loop The resultant is the shortcut. The path doesn't matter, only where you end up. Reorder the same vectors — same resultant every time The algebra guarantees commutativity. The viz makes it viscerally obvious. Commutativity — two chains, same endpoint The proof is visible: both paths share one endpoint. Three-vector chain \text{Displacements: } \vec{A}=3\,\text{km east},\; \vec{B}=4\,\text{km north},\; \vec{C}=2\,\text{km at }30°. \text{Find the resultant.} The resultant displacement is $\approx 6.88$ km at $46.6°$ north of east, regardless of the order you walk the three legs. A_x=3,\;A_y=0 East → positive x. B_x=0,\;B_y=4 North → positive y. C_x=2\cos30°=\sqrt{3}\approx1.73,\;C_y=2\sin30°=1.00 Decompose the angled vector. R_x=3+0+1.73=4.73\,\text{km},\;R_y=0+4+1.00=5.00\,\text{km} Sum components. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{4.73^2+5.00^2}=\sqrt{22.37+25.00}=\sqrt{47.37}\approx6.88\,\text{km} Resultant magnitude. \theta=\arctan(5.00/4.73)\approx46.6°\text{ north of east} Direction from +x axis. Zero resultant — closed polygon \text{Vectors } \vec{A}=(3,0),\;\vec{B}=(0,4),\;\vec{C}=(-3,-4). \text{ Show that } \vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C}=\vec{0}. When vectors form a closed polygon, their sum is zero. This is the graphical condition for equilibrium. R_x = 3+0+(-3) = 0 x-components sum to zero. R_y = 0+4+(-4) = 0 y-components sum to zero. \vec{R}=(0,0)=\vec{0}\;\checkmark The three vectors form a closed triangle — the tip of C⃗ returns exactly to the tail of A⃗. \text{A robot moves: 5 m east, then 5 m north, then 5 m west. What is the resultant displacement?} Sum the x-components and y-components separately. \vec{R} = 5\,\text{m north} R_x = 5 + 0 + (-5) = 0\,\text{m} East and west cancel. R_y = 0 + 5 + 0 = 5\,\text{m} Only the north leg contributes vertically. |\vec{R}| = 5\,\text{m north} The three-step journey is equivalent to a single 5 m step north. \text{Four forces act on a pin: 10 N east, 8 N at 90°, 6 N at 180°, 4 N at 270°. Find } \vec{R}. 90° = north, 180° = west, 270° = south. |\vec{R}| = 4\sqrt{2}\,N\text{ at }45°\text{ (NE)} R_x = 10+0+(-6)+0 = 4\,N East=+x, west=−x. R_y = 0+8+0+(-4) = 4\,N North=+y, south=−y. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{16+16}=4\sqrt{2}\approx5.66\,N\text{ at }45° Equal x and y → 45°. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Adding Vectors Numerically
documents.subtitle: The four-step method that works for any vectors, every time.
documents.tags: vector addition numerical method components resultant systematic method
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Two forces at non-right angles \vec{A}=40\,N\text{ at }30°,\;\vec{B}=25\,N\text{ at }145°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}.   $\vec{R} \approx 37.1\,N$ at $67.6°$ from the positive $x$-axis. A_x=40\cos30°=40(0.8660)=34.64\,N,\;A_y=40\sin30°=20.00\,N Decompose A⃗. B_x=25\cos145°=25(-0.8192)=-20.48\,N,\;B_y=25\sin145°=25(0.5736)=14.34\,N Decompose B⃗. Note: 145° is in Quadrant II → negative x-component. R_x=34.64+(-20.48)=14.16\,N,\;R_y=20.00+14.34=34.34\,N Sum components. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{14.16^2+34.34^2}=\sqrt{200.5+1179.2}=\sqrt{1379.7}\approx37.1\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(34.34/14.16)=\arctan(2.425)\approx67.6° Both components positive → Quadrant I. atan2 agrees. Set the example values and step through Watch each DSMD step produce one number. Subtracting vectors: A⃗ − B⃗ = A⃗ + (−B⃗) \vec{A}=(5,3),\;\vec{B}=(2,-1).\text{ Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}.   Vector subtraction is just addition of the negated vector. $-\vec{B}$ is the same length as $\vec{B}$, pointing the opposite direction. -\vec{B}=(-2,1) Negate B⃗ by flipping all component signs. This reverses its direction. \vec{A}+(-\vec{B})=(5+(-2),\;3+1)=(3,4) Add component-wise. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=\sqrt{9+16}=5 Magnitude of the difference.  \vec{A}=10\,N\text{ at }0°,\;\vec{B}=10\,N\text{ at }90°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}\text{ exactly.} 0° is east, 90° is north. Components are trivial. 10\sqrt{2}\,N\text{ at }45°  R_x=10+0=10,\;R_y=0+10=10 Direct from the angles. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{200}=10\sqrt{2}\approx14.1\,N Pythagorean. \theta=45° Equal components → 45°.  \text{Three forces: }\vec{F}_1=50\,N\text{ at }0°,\;\vec{F}_2=30\,N\text{ at }120°,\;\vec{F}_3=20\,N\text{ at }240°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}. Set up the component table. Use exact trig values: $\cos120°=-\frac{1}{2}$, $\sin120°=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, $\cos240°=-\frac{1}{2}$, $\sin240°=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. |\vec{R}|=10\sqrt{7}\approx26.5\,N\text{ at }19.1°  F_{1x}=50,\;F_{1y}=0 0° → full magnitude in x. F_{2x}=30(-\tfrac{1}{2})=-15,\;F_{2y}=30(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2})=15\sqrt{3} 120° → Quadrant II. F_{3x}=20(-\tfrac{1}{2})=-10,\;F_{3y}=20(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2})=-10\sqrt{3} 240° → Quadrant III. R_x=50-15-10=25\,N,\;R_y=0+15\sqrt{3}-10\sqrt{3}=5\sqrt{3}\approx8.66\,N Sum each column. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{625+75}=\sqrt{700}=10\sqrt{7}\approx26.5\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(5\sqrt{3}/25)=\arctan(\sqrt{3}/5)\approx19.1° Both positive → Quadrant I.  \text{Forces } \vec{A}\text{ at }\alpha\text{ and }\vec{B}\text{ at }-\alpha\text{ have equal magnitudes }F. \text{Show that }|\vec{R}|=2F\cos\alpha\text{ and that }\vec{R}\text{ points along the }x\text{-axis.} By symmetry, the y-components cancel. Compute $R_x$ and use $\cos(-\alpha)=\cos\alpha$. |\vec{R}|=2F\cos\alpha,\text{ directed along }+x.  A_x=F\cos\alpha,\;A_y=F\sin\alpha Decompose A⃗. B_x=F\cos(-\alpha)=F\cos\alpha,\;B_y=F\sin(-\alpha)=-F\sin\alpha Decompose B⃗. Note cosine is even, sine is odd. R_y=F\sin\alpha+(-F\sin\alpha)=0 y-components cancel by symmetry.
