R_x=F\cos\alpha+F\cos\alpha=2F\cos\alpha x-components add. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{(2F\cos\alpha)^2+0^2}=2F\cos\alpha,\;\theta=0° Resultant is purely horizontal with magnitude $2F\cos\alpha$.
documents.proofs: The numerical method is exact — not an approximation — because it is literally the definition of vector addition in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Any error is purely arithmetic, not methodological. The only source of genuine imprecision is rounding during the trigonometric steps. Carry at least 4 significant figures through intermediate calculations and round only at the final answer. Premature rounding Rounding $\cos 37° = 0.8$ (instead of $0.7986$) introduces a 0.17% error that compounds through every step. Always use full calculator precision in intermediate steps. The method is definition, not approximation $\vec{A}+\vec{B} \equiv (A_x+B_x, A_y+B_y)$. Decomposing and summing components is not an approximation of some exact geometric result — it IS the exact geometric result expressed algebraically.
documents.applications: Every real engineering calculation — bridge loads, satellite trajectories, robot forces — requires exact vector addition. The numerical (component) method is the workhorse: decompose, sum, reconstruct. Four steps, always works. The four-step method 1.\;v_{kx}=|\vec{v}_k|\cos\theta_k,\;v_{ky}=|\vec{v}_k|\sin\theta_k\quad2.\;R_x=\sum v_{kx},\;R_y=\sum v_{ky}\quad3.\;|\vec{R}|=\sqrt{R_x^2+R_y^2}\quad4.\;\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(R_y,R_x) Check the quadrant every time atan2 returns the correct quadrant automatically. Plain arctan does not — it only covers $(-90°, 90°)$. If $R_x < 0$, add $180°$ to the plain arctan result.
documents.content: Two forces: 40 N at 30° and 25 N at 145°. Graphical methods give only an estimate. How do you get the exact answer? Every real engineering calculation — bridge loads, satellite trajectories, robot forces — requires exact vector addition. The numerical (component) method is the workhorse: decompose, sum, reconstruct. Four steps, always works. The graphical method shows you *what* the answer looks like. The numerical method tells you *exactly* what it is. The four-step recipe never fails: **Step 1 — Decompose** each vector into $x$- and $y$-components. **Step 2 — Sum** all $x$-components; sum all $y$-components. **Step 3 — Magnitude**: $|\vec{R}| = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$. **Step 4 — Direction**: $\theta = \text{atan2}(R_y, R_x)$, then check the quadrant. The method works because addition is done component-by-component, and components are just numbers — ordinary arithmetic applies. The component table is the practical organiser for multi-vector problems. Set up one row per vector, one column per component, and sum the bottom row. A common exam trap: **sign errors in components**. Always write $v_{kx} = |\vec{v}_k|\cos\theta_k$ where $\theta_k$ is the standard angle (from $+x$, counterclockwise). If a problem gives "30° below the horizontal", convert to $\theta = -30°$ before plugging in. The numerical method is exact — not an approximation — because it is literally the definition of vector addition in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Any error is purely arithmetic, not methodological. The only source of genuine imprecision is rounding during the trigonometric steps. Carry at least 4 significant figures through intermediate calculations and round only at the final answer. Premature rounding Rounding $\cos 37° = 0.8$ (instead of $0.7986$) introduces a 0.17% error that compounds through every step. Always use full calculator precision in intermediate steps. The method is definition, not approximation $\vec{A}+\vec{B} \equiv (A_x+B_x, A_y+B_y)$. Decomposing and summing components is not an approximation of some exact geometric result — it IS the exact geometric result expressed algebraically. The four-step method 1.\;v_{kx}=|\vec{v}_k|\cos\theta_k,\;v_{ky}=|\vec{v}_k|\sin\theta_k\quad2.\;R_x=\sum v_{kx},\;R_y=\sum v_{ky}\quad3.\;|\vec{R}|=\sqrt{R_x^2+R_y^2}\quad4.\;\theta=\operatorname{atan2}(R_y,R_x) Check the quadrant every time atan2 returns the correct quadrant automatically. Plain arctan does not — it only covers $(-90°, 90°)$. If $R_x < 0$, add $180°$ to the plain arctan result. The component table \begin{array}{c|c|c}\text{Vector} & v_x & v_y\\\hline\vec{A} & A_x & A_y\\\vec{B} & B_x & B_y\\\hline\vec{R} & R_x=A_x+B_x & R_y=A_y+B_y\end{array} DSMD — the four-step acronym Decompose → Sum → Magnitude → Direction. Say it before every problem. Step through the four stages for two live vectors Decompose → sum → magnitude → direction. Four steps, exact answer. Live component table — drag vectors, see every cell update The table makes sign errors visible before they propagate. Each proof step lights up the corresponding diagram element Proof and diagram in lockstep. Two forces at non-right angles \vec{A}=40\,N\text{ at }30°,\;\vec{B}=25\,N\text{ at }145°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}. $\vec{R} \approx 37.1\,N$ at $67.6°$ from the positive $x$-axis. A_x=40\cos30°=40(0.8660)=34.64\,N,\;A_y=40\sin30°=20.00\,N Decompose A⃗. B_x=25\cos145°=25(-0.8192)=-20.48\,N,\;B_y=25\sin145°=25(0.5736)=14.34\,N Decompose B⃗. Note: 145° is in Quadrant II → negative x-component. R_x=34.64+(-20.48)=14.16\,N,\;R_y=20.00+14.34=34.34\,N Sum components. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{14.16^2+34.34^2}=\sqrt{200.5+1179.2}=\sqrt{1379.7}\approx37.1\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(34.34/14.16)=\arctan(2.425)\approx67.6° Both components positive → Quadrant I. atan2 agrees. Vector subtraction is just addition of the negated vector. $-\vec{B}$ is the same length as $\vec{B}$, pointing the opposite direction. -\vec{B}=(-2,1) Negate B⃗ by flipping all component signs. This reverses its direction. \vec{A}+(-\vec{B})=(5+(-2),\;3+1)=(3,4) Add component-wise. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=\sqrt{9+16}=5 Magnitude of the difference. \vec{A}=10\,N\text{ at }0°,\;\vec{B}=10\,N\text{ at }90°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}\text{ exactly.} 0° is east, 90° is north. Components are trivial. 10\sqrt{2}\,N\text{ at }45° R_x=10+0=10,\;R_y=0+10=10 Direct from the angles. |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{200}=10\sqrt{2}\approx14.1\,N Pythagorean. \theta=45° Equal components → 45°. \text{Three forces: }\vec{F}_1=50\,N\text{ at }0°,\;\vec{F}_2=30\,N\text{ at }120°,\;\vec{F}_3=20\,N\text{ at }240°.\text{ Find }\vec{R}. Set up the component table. Compute $R_x$ and use $\cos(-\alpha)=\cos\alpha$. |\vec{R}|=2F\cos\alpha,\text{ directed along }+x. A_x=F\cos\alpha,\;A_y=F\sin\alpha Decompose A⃗. documents.assignment: Graphical: A⃗ − B⃗ by flip-and-add \vec{A} = 5\,\text{cm east},\;\vec{B} = 3\,\text{cm north. Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}\text{ graphically.}   $\vec{A}-\vec{B} \approx 5.83$ cm at 31° south of east. -\vec{B} = 3\,\text{cm south} Flip B⃗ → −B⃗ points south. \text{Chain: } \vec{A}\text{ then }-\vec{B} \Rightarrow \text{resultant from start to end} Tip-to-toe of A⃗ and −B⃗. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}| = \sqrt{5^2+3^2} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83\,\text{cm} Magnitude by Pythagoras (perpendicular components). \theta = \arctan(-3/5) \approx -31°\text{ (i.e. 31° south of east)} Direction: east component 5, south component 3. Change in velocity \text{A car changes velocity from }\vec{v}_i=30\,\text{m/s east to }\vec{v}_f=40\,\text{m/s north. Find }\Delta\vec{v}.   $|\Delta\vec{v}| = 50$ m/s at $\approx 127°$ (northwest direction). The speed change was $|40-30|=10$ m/s — but the velocity change was 50 m/s. Very different. -\vec{v}_i = 30\,\text{m/s west} Negate the initial velocity. \Delta\vec{v} = \vec{v}_f + (-\vec{v}_i) = 40\,\text{m/s north} + 30\,\text{m/s west} Add tip-to-toe. |\Delta\vec{v}| = \sqrt{40^2+30^2} = 50\,\text{m/s} 3-4-5 triple scaled by 10. \theta = 180° - \arctan(40/30) \approx 180°-53.1° = 126.9° West component negative → Quadrant II. Angle from +x axis (east).  \vec{A}=(5,2),\;\vec{B}=(2,5).\text{ Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}\text{ and }\vec{B}-\vec{A}.\text{ What is the relationship?} Subtract component-wise. Compare the two results. \vec{A}-\vec{B}=(3,-3),\;\vec{B}-\vec{A}=(-3,3).\text{ They are antiparallel.}  \vec{A}-\vec{B}=(5-2,\;2-5)=(3,-3) Component subtraction. \vec{B}-\vec{A}=(2-5,\;5-2)=(-3,3) Swap order. \vec{B}-\vec{A} = -(\vec{A}-\vec{B}) The two results are negatives of each other — same magnitude, opposite direction.  \text{A projectile has }\vec{v}_i=20\,\text{m/s at }60°\text{ and }\vec{v}_f=20\,\text{m/s at }120°.\text{ Find }|\Delta\vec{v}|. Both speeds are equal (20 m/s). Decompose each, subtract, find magnitude. |\Delta\vec{v}|=20\,\text{m/s, directed west (180°).}  v_{ix}=20\cos60°=10,\;v_{iy}=20\sin60°=10\sqrt{3} Decompose v⃗_i. v_{fx}=20\cos120°=-10,\;v_{fy}=20\sin120°=10\sqrt{3} Decompose v⃗_f. \Delta v_x=-10-10=-20,\;\Delta v_y=10\sqrt{3}-10\sqrt{3}=0 Subtract components. |\Delta\vec{v}|=\sqrt{(-20)^2+0^2}=20\,\text{m/s west} Pure horizontal change — the y-components cancelled.
documents.proofs: Formally, $-\vec{B}$ is the additive inverse of $\vec{B}$ in the vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$: it is the unique vector such that $\vec{B} + (-\vec{B}) = \vec{0}$. The existence of additive inverses is one of the eight axioms defining a vector space. Additive inverse -\vec{B} \text{ is defined by } \vec{B} + (-\vec{B}) = \vec{0}. \text{ In components: } -(B_x, B_y) = (-B_x, -B_y). Why flip gives the inverse Adding $\vec{B}$ and $-\vec{B}$ produces a zero resultant: $B_x + (-B_x) = 0$ and $B_y + (-B_y) = 0$. Geometrically: the two equal-length, opposite arrows cancel exactly.
documents.applications: Acceleration is defined as change in velocity: $\vec{a} = \Delta\vec{v}/\Delta t = (\vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i)/\Delta t$. Every acceleration, every force diagram, every relative velocity problem uses vector subtraction. Getting the direction of $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ wrong is one of the most common physics errors. Negative vector -\vec{B}\text{ has magnitude }|\vec{B}|\text{ and direction opposite to }\vec{B}. In component form: }-\vec{B} = (-B_x,\,-B_y). Vector subtraction \vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B}). Tail-to-tail shortcut Place $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ with tails at the same point. Then $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ is the arrow from the **tip of $\vec{B}$** to the **tip of $\vec{A}$**. $\vec{A} - \vec{B} \ne \vec{B} - \vec{A}$ Subtraction is not commutative. $\vec{B} - \vec{A}$ points in exactly the opposite direction from $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$.
documents.content: A ball moves from velocity 4 m/s east to 3 m/s north. What is the change in velocity — and why is it not simply 1 m/s? Acceleration is defined as change in velocity: $\vec{a} = \Delta\vec{v}/\Delta t = (\vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i)/\Delta t$. Every acceleration, every force diagram, every relative velocity problem uses vector subtraction. Getting the direction of $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ wrong is one of the most common physics errors. Subtraction is not a new operation — it is addition in disguise. $\vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B})$. The negative of a vector $-\vec{B}$ has the **same magnitude** as $\vec{B}$ but points in the **opposite direction**. Graphically, you flip the arrow 180°. The two-step recipe: (1) draw $-\vec{B}$ by reversing $\vec{B}$; (2) add $\vec{A}$ and $-\vec{B}$ using any graphical method. There is a useful shortcut: if $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ share a common tail, then $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ is the arrow **from the tip of $\vec{B}$ to the tip of $\vec{A}$**. This is the "tail-to-tail shortcut" and it is especially useful for finding $\Delta\vec{v}$ in kinematics. The change in velocity $\Delta\vec{v}$ is the most important application of vector subtraction. If an object moves from velocity $\vec{v}_i$ to $\vec{v}_f$, then: The magnitude $|\Delta\vec{v}|$ is **not** $|\vec{v}_f| - |\vec{v}_i|$ (scalar difference). It is the magnitude of the vector difference, computed with the Pythagorean theorem or law of cosines. This distinction is the source of countless errors in kinematics problems. Formally, $-\vec{B}$ is the additive inverse of $\vec{B}$ in the vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$: it is the unique vector such that $\vec{B} + (-\vec{B}) = \vec{0}$. The existence of additive inverses is one of the eight axioms defining a vector space. Additive inverse -\vec{B} \text{ is defined by } \vec{B} + (-\vec{B}) = \vec{0}. \text{ In components: } -(B_x, B_y) = (-B_x, -B_y). Why flip gives the inverse Adding $\vec{B}$ and $-\vec{B}$ produces a zero resultant: $B_x + (-B_x) = 0$ and $B_y + (-B_y) = 0$. Geometrically: the two equal-length, opposite arrows cancel exactly. Negative vector -\vec{B}\text{ has magnitude }|\vec{B}|\text{ and direction opposite to }\vec{B}. In component form: }-\vec{B} = (-B_x,\,-B_y). Vector subtraction \vec{A} - \vec{B} = \vec{A} + (-\vec{B}). Tail-to-tail shortcut Place $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ with tails at the same point. Then $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$ is the arrow from the **tip of $\vec{B}$** to the **tip of $\vec{A}$**. $\vec{A} - \vec{B} \ne \vec{B} - \vec{A}$ Subtraction is not commutative. $\vec{B} - \vec{A}$ points in exactly the opposite direction from $\vec{A} - \vec{B}$. Change in velocity \Delta\vec{v} = \vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i = \vec{v}_f + (-\vec{v}_i) |Δv⃗| ≠ |v⃗_f| − |v⃗_i| The magnitude of the velocity change is not the scalar difference of the speeds. Example: 3 m/s north minus 4 m/s east is not 1 m/s — it is 5 m/s at 143°. Drag A⃗ and B⃗ — see −B⃗, and the two subtraction methods side by side Two methods, one answer. Choose whichever is faster for the situation. Change in velocity: v⃗_f − v⃗_i visualised Δv⃗ points from v⃗_i tip to v⃗_f tip (tail-to-tail shortcut). Each proof step animates on the diagram Both methods are the same vector — step 5 makes that explicit. Graphical: A⃗ − B⃗ by flip-and-add \vec{A} = 5\,\text{cm east},\;\vec{B} = 3\,\text{cm north. Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}\text{ graphically.} $\vec{A}-\vec{B} \approx 5.83$ cm at 31° south of east. -\vec{B} = 3\,\text{cm south} Flip B⃗ → −B⃗ points south. \text{Chain: } \vec{A}\text{ then }-\vec{B} \Rightarrow \text{resultant from start to end} Tip-to-toe of A⃗ and −B⃗. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}| = \sqrt{5^2+3^2} = \sqrt{34} \approx 5.83\,\text{cm} Magnitude by Pythagoras (perpendicular components). \theta = \arctan(-3/5) \approx -31°\text{ (i.e. 31° south of east)} Direction: east component 5, south component 3. Change in velocity \text{A car changes velocity from }\vec{v}_i=30\,\text{m/s east to }\vec{v}_f=40\,\text{m/s north. Find }\Delta\vec{v}. $|\Delta\vec{v}| = 50$ m/s at $\approx 127°$ (northwest direction). The speed change was $|40-30|=10$ m/s — but the velocity change was 50 m/s. Very different. -\vec{v}_i = 30\,\text{m/s west} Negate the initial velocity. \Delta\vec{v} = \vec{v}_f + (-\vec{v}_i) = 40\,\text{m/s north} + 30\,\text{m/s west} Add tip-to-toe. |\Delta\vec{v}| = \sqrt{40^2+30^2} = 50\,\text{m/s} 3-4-5 triple scaled by 10. \theta = 180° - \arctan(40/30) \approx 180°-53.1° = 126.9° West component negative → Quadrant II. Angle from +x axis (east). \vec{A}=(5,2),\;\vec{B}=(2,5).\text{ Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}\text{ and }\vec{B}-\vec{A}.\text{ What is the relationship?} Subtract component-wise. Compare the two results. \vec{A}-\vec{B}=(3,-3),\;\vec{B}-\vec{A}=(-3,3).\text{ They are antiparallel.} \vec{A}-\vec{B}=(5-2,\;2-5)=(3,-3) Component subtraction. \vec{B}-\vec{A}=(2-5,\;5-2)=(-3,3) Swap order. \vec{B}-\vec{A} = -(\vec{A}-\vec{B}) The two results are negatives of each other — same magnitude, opposite direction. \text{A projectile has }\vec{v}_i=20\,\text{m/s at }60°\text{ and }\vec{v}_f=20\,\text{m/s at }120°.\text{ Find }|\Delta\vec{v}|. Both speeds are equal (20 m/s). Decompose each, subtract, find magnitude. |\Delta\vec{v}|=20\,\text{m/s, directed west (180°).} v_{ix}=20\cos60°=10,\;v_{iy}=20\sin60°=10\sqrt{3} Decompose v⃗_i. v_{fx}=20\cos120°=-10,\;v_{fy}=20\sin120°=10\sqrt{3} Decompose v⃗_f. \Delta v_x=-10-10=-20,\;\Delta v_y=10\sqrt{3}-10\sqrt{3}=0 Subtract components. |\Delta\vec{v}|=\sqrt{(-20)^2+0^2}=20\,\text{m/s west} Pure horizontal change — the y-components cancelled. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.assignment: Numerical: A⃗ − B⃗ with non-trivial angles \vec{A}=60\,N\text{ at }20°,\;\vec{B}=45\,N\text{ at }110°.\text{ Find }\vec{A}-\vec{B}.   $\vec{A}-\vec{B} \approx 75.0\,N$ at $-16.9°$ (16.9° below the x-axis). A_x=60\cos20°=56.38,\;A_y=60\sin20°=20.52 Decompose A⃗. B_x=45\cos110°=-15.39,\;B_y=45\sin110°=42.29 Decompose B⃗. cos110° is negative (Q II). -B_x=15.39,\;-B_y=-42.29 Negate B⃗ components (Step 0). R_x=56.38+15.39=71.77\,N,\;R_y=20.52+(-42.29)=-21.77\,N Sum: A⃗ + (−B⃗). |\vec{R}|=\sqrt{71.77^2+21.77^2}=\sqrt{5151.0+473.9}=\sqrt{5624.9}\approx74.99\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(-21.77/71.77)\approx-16.9°\text{ (Quadrant IV)} Rₓ>0, Rᵧ<0 → Q IV. atan2 gives −16.9°. Relative velocity \text{Car A travels at }\vec{v}_A=25\,\text{m/s east. Car B travels at }\vec{v}_B=20\,\text{m/s at }30°. \text{Find the velocity of A relative to B: }\vec{v}_{A\text{ rel }B}=\vec{v}_A-\vec{v}_B.   From B's perspective, A appears to move at 12.6 m/s at 52.5° below the x-axis (southeast). v_{Ax}=25,\;v_{Ay}=0 A moves east. v_{Bx}=20\cos30°=17.32,\;v_{By}=20\sin30°=10.00 Decompose B. R_x=25-17.32=7.68,\;R_y=0-10.00=-10.00 Subtract component-wise. |\vec{v}_{A\text{ rel }B}|=\sqrt{7.68^2+10^2}=\sqrt{59.0+100}=\sqrt{159}\approx12.6\,\text{m/s} Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(-10/7.68)\approx-52.5° Q IV (east component positive, south component negative).  \vec{A}=(8,6),\;\vec{B}=(3,-2).\text{ Find }|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|. Subtract components first, then find magnitude. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=\sqrt{89}\approx9.43  \vec{A}-\vec{B}=(8-3,\;6-(-2))=(5,8) Component subtraction. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=\sqrt{25+64}=\sqrt{89}\approx9.43 Magnitude.  \text{Using the law of cosines formula, find }|\vec{A}-\vec{B}|\text{ where }|\vec{A}|=10,\;|\vec{B}|=8,\;\phi=60°. $|A-B|^2 = |A|^2 + |B|^2 - 2|A||B|\cos\phi$ |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=2\sqrt{21}\approx9.17  |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|^2=100+64-2(10)(8)(0.5)=164-80=84 $\cos60°=0.5$. |\vec{A}-\vec{B}|=\sqrt{84}=2\sqrt{21}\approx9.17 Take the square root.
documents.applications: Relative velocity, net force, change in momentum — all are vector differences. The graphical method gives a rough answer. The numerical method gives the exact one. DSMD with negation handles every case. Numerical subtraction recipe \vec{A}-\vec{B} = \vec{A}+(-\vec{B}). \text{Step 0: negate }\vec{B}.\text{ Steps 1–4: DSMD as usual.} In the component table, just flip signs Replace every $B_x$ with $-B_x$ and $B_y$ with $-B_y$ in the table. Everything else is identical to the addition procedure.

documents.assignment: Three forces on a ring \vec{F}_1=50\,N@0°,\;\vec{F}_2=70\,N@130°,\;\vec{F}_3=40\,N@250°.\text{ Find }\vec{F}_{net}.   Net force ≈ 18.2 N at 118.5°. The ring accelerates in that direction. F_{1x}=50,\;F_{1y}=0 0° → full x. F_{2x}=70\cos130°=-45.0,\;F_{2y}=70\sin130°=53.6 Q II: negative x. F_{3x}=40\cos250°=-13.7,\;F_{3y}=40\sin250°=-37.6 Q III: both negative. F_{net,x}=50-45.0-13.7=-8.7\,N,\;F_{net,y}=0+53.6-37.6=16.0\,N Sum each column. |\vec{F}_{net}|=\sqrt{8.7^2+16^2}=\sqrt{75.7+256}=\sqrt{331.7}\approx18.2\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(16/-8.7)\approx180°-61.5°=118.5° Q II correction: add 180° to arctan result.  \vec{F}_1=30\,N\text{ east, }\vec{F}_2=40\,N\text{ north. Find }|\vec{F}_{net}|. Perpendicular forces — Pythagorean theorem. 50\,N\text{ at }53.1°  |\vec{F}_{net}|=\sqrt{30^2+40^2}=50\,N 3-4-5 triple.  A 5 kg block has forces 20 N east and 15 N north. Find acceleration magnitude. Find F_net first, then a = F_net/m. a=5\,m/s^2  F_{net}=\sqrt{20^2+15^2}=25\,N Net force. a=25/5=5\,m/s^2 Newton 2.
documents.proofs: Newton's second law is a vector equation: $\vec{F}_{net}=m\vec{a}$. Resolving into components gives two independent scalar equations: $F_{net,x}=ma_x$ and $F_{net,y}=ma_y$. These can be solved independently. Component form of Newton 2 \sum F_x = ma_x \qquad \sum F_y = ma_y Why components decouple The x and y directions are independent in Newtonian mechanics. A horizontal net force produces only horizontal acceleration; vertical forces produce only vertical acceleration.
documents.applications: Structural engineering, rocket thrust, biomechanics — every real force problem requires adding vector forces. The net force F⃗_net = ΣF⃗ determines acceleration via Newton's second law. Newton's second law (vector form) \vec{F}_{net} = \sum_i \vec{F}_i = m\vec{a} Equilibrium condition \sum F_x = 0 \quad\text{AND}\quad \sum F_y = 0 Do not add magnitudes F_{net} \ne F_1 + F_2 + \cdots\text{ unless all forces are parallel. Always add components.}
documents.content: Three ropes pull a ring: 50 N at 0°, 70 N at 130°, 40 N at 250°. Will the ring accelerate, and which way? Structural engineering, rocket thrust, biomechanics — every real force problem requires adding vector forces. The net force F⃗_net = ΣF⃗ determines acceleration via Newton's second law. Every force on an object is a vector. Newton's second law $\vec{F}_{net} = m\vec{a}$ requires the **vector sum** of all forces — not the scalar sum of their magnitudes. The procedure is identical to adding any vectors: DSMD. The only new element is the **free body diagram** — a sketch showing all forces acting on the object with their magnitudes and angles. A system is in **equilibrium** ($\vec{a}=0$) if and only if $\vec{F}_{net} = \vec{0}$, i.e. $\sum F_x = 0$ AND $\sum F_y = 0$ simultaneously. The component table for force problems adds one column: the force label (e.g. weight, normal, tension). This keeps bookkeeping clear in multi-force problems. Newton's second law is a vector equation: $\vec{F}_{net}=m\vec{a}$. Resolving into components gives two independent scalar equations: $F_{net,x}=ma_x$ and $F_{net,y}=ma_y$. These can be solved independently. Component form of Newton 2 \sum F_x = ma_x \qquad \sum F_y = ma_y Why components decouple The x and y directions are independent in Newtonian mechanics. A horizontal net force produces only horizontal acceleration; vertical forces produce only vertical acceleration. Newton's second law (vector form) \vec{F}_{net} = \sum_i \vec{F}_i = m\vec{a} Equilibrium condition \sum F_x = 0 \quad\text{AND}\quad \sum F_y = 0 Do not add magnitudes F_{net} \ne F_1 + F_2 + \cdots\text{ unless all forces are parallel. Always add components.} Net force components F_{net,x} = \sum_i F_{ix} = \sum_i |F_i|\cos\theta_i \qquad F_{net,y} = \sum_i F_{iy} = \sum_i |F_i|\sin\theta_i Equilibrium check After computing F_{net,x} and F_{net,y}, check: if both are zero (within rounding), the object is in equilibrium. If not, \vec{a} = \vec{F}_{net}/m. Add up to 4 force vectors — see net force and equilibrium status This is a free body diagram in motion. Live force component table — add forces, check equilibrium The component table is the free body diagram in numeric form. The ring accelerates in that direction. F_{1x}=50,\;F_{1y}=0 0° → full x. F_{2x}=70\cos130°=-45.0,\;F_{2y}=70\sin130°=53.6 Q II: negative x. F_{3x}=40\cos250°=-13.7,\;F_{3y}=40\sin250°=-37.6 Q III: both negative. F_{net,x}=50-45.0-13.7=-8.7\,N,\;F_{net,y}=0+53.6-37.6=16.0\,N Sum each column. |\vec{F}_{net}|=\sqrt{8.7^2+16^2}=\sqrt{75.7+256}=\sqrt{331.7}\approx18.2\,N Magnitude. \theta=\arctan(16/-8.7)\approx180°-61.5°=118.5° Q II correction: add 180° to arctan result. \vec{F}_1=30\,N\text{ east, }\vec{F}_2=40\,N\text{ north. Find }|\vec{F}_{net}|. Perpendicular forces — Pythagorean theorem. 50\,N\text{ at }53.1° |\vec{F}_{net}|=\sqrt{30^2+40^2}=50\,N 3-4-5 triple. A 5 kg block has forces 20 N east and 15 N north. Find acceleration magnitude. Find F_net first, then a = F_net/m. a=5\,m/s^2 F_{net}=\sqrt{20^2+15^2}=25\,N Net force. a=25/5=5\,m/s^2 Newton 2. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Dot Product — Scalar Product

documents.tags: dot product scalar product vector multiplication projection

documents.assignment: Basic calculation |A| = 5, |B| = 8, phi = 60^circ. Find A cdot B.   The dot product is 20. A \cdot B = 5 \times 8 \times \cos 60^\circ Substitute values. A \cdot B = 40 \times 0.5 = 20 Calculate.  Two vectors of magnitude 10 are at 45° to each other. Calculate their dot product. Use the cos formula. 70.7  10 \times 10 \times \cos 45^\circ \approx 70.7 Apply formula.
documents.proofs: The dot product follows the distributive law and is commutative: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$. Properties \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} \qquad \vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} + \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C}
documents.applications: Work is computed as the dot product of force and displacement. Solar panels produce the most power when their orientation vector "dots" perfectly with the sun's rays. Scalar Product \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi Geometric meaning The dot product is the shadow of one vector onto another, scaled by the other's length.
documents.content: How much of a force is actually pulling an object in the direction it is moving? Work is computed as the dot product of force and displacement. Solar panels produce the most power when their orientation vector "dots" perfectly with the sun's rays. The dot product measures how much one vector "goes in the direction of" another. Unlike addition, the result of a dot product is a **scalar (a number)**, not a vector. Geometrically, you can think of it as the length of the projection of $\vec{A}$ onto $\vec{B}$ multiplied by the length of $\vec{B}$ (or vice versa). If two vectors are pointed in the same direction, the dot product is positive and large. If they are perpendicular, the result is zero. If they point in opposite directions, the result is negative. The geometric formula $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi$ connects the magnitudes and the angle $\phi$ between the vectors. This formula is particularly useful when you know the relative orientation but not the components. The dot product follows the distributive law and is commutative: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$. Properties \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} \qquad \vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} + \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C} Scalar Product \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi Geometric meaning The dot product is the shadow of one vector onto another, scaled by the other's length. Dot product range -|\vec{A}||\vec{B}| \le \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} \le |\vec{A}||\vec{B}| Interactive Dot Product Projection is the key to understanding the dot product. Dot Product Geometric Explorer Maximum alignment = maximum dot product. Notation Drill Rigor starts with notation. Basic calculation |A| = 5, |B| = 8, phi = 60^circ. Find A cdot B. The dot product is 20. A \cdot B = 5 \times 8 \times \cos 60^\circ Substitute values. A \cdot B = 40 \times 0.5 = 20 Calculate. Two vectors of magnitude 10 are at 45° to each other. Calculate their dot product. Use the cos formula. 70.7 10 \times 10 \times \cos 45^\circ \approx 70.7 Apply formula. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.assignment: 2D Calculation \vec{A} = (3, 4), \vec{B} = (2, -1). \text{ Find } \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}.   The dot product is 2. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(2) + (4)(-1) Multiply x components and y components. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 6 - 4 = 2 Sum the results.  \text{Find } \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} \text{ for } \vec{A} = (5, 0, 2) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (1, 3, -4). Add up three component pairs. -3  5(1) + 0(3) + 2(-4) = 5 + 0 - 8 = -3 Apply definition.
documents.proofs: We can derive the distributive property using this component form. Distributive property \vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} + \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C}
documents.applications: Computer graphics, physics engines, and data analysis all rely on the component form of the dot product because it requires no trigonometry — just simple multiplication and addition. Component-wise Dot Product \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z Scalar result The sum of scalars is a scalar. Every time you "dot" two vectors, the "vec-ness" is lost into a single number.
documents.content: How do you find the dot product if you only have the components $(A_x, A_y)$ and $(B_x, B_y)$? Computer graphics, physics engines, and data analysis all rely on the component form of the dot product because it requires no trigonometry — just simple multiplication and addition. The component-wise definition of the dot product is exactly the same as the geometric one: $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z$. It works because the basis vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are perpendicular. Since $\hat{i} \cdot \hat{j} = 0$ and $\hat{i} \cdot \hat{i} = 1$, the cross-terms vanish during expansion. This calculation is fast, precise, and scales easily to 3D and beyond. The dot product can also be written in matrix notation as $A^TB$ (the transpose of $A$ multiplied by $B$). This component form is why we say the dot product "sums the contributions" from each shared axis. We can derive the distributive property using this component form. Distributive property \vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} + \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C} Component-wise Dot Product \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z Scalar result The sum of scalars is a scalar. Every time you "dot" two vectors, the "vec-ness" is lost into a single number. Dot product and magnitude \vec{A} \cdot \vec{A} = |\vec{A}|^2 \implies |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{A}} Step-by-Step Dot Product Arithmetic Arithmetic is the bridge to automation. Component/Mag-Angle Calculator The ultimate vector scratchpad. Pattern Recognition Quiz Precision requires practice. 2D Calculation \vec{A} = (3, 4), \vec{B} = (2, -1). \text{ Find } \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}. The dot product is 2. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(2) + (4)(-1) Multiply x components and y components. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 6 - 4 = 2 Sum the results. \text{Find } \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} \text{ for } \vec{A} = (5, 0, 2) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (1, 3, -4). Add up three component pairs. -3 5(1) + 0(3) + 2(-4) = 5 + 0 - 8 = -3 Apply definition. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Dot Product — Orthogonality & Perpendicularity
documents.subtitle: Two vectors are orthogonal if and only if their dot product is zero.
documents.tags: dot product scalar product orthogonality perpendicular vectors perpendicularity
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Check Orthogonality \text{Are } \vec{A} = (3, 4) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (-4, 3) \text{ orthogonal?}   Yes, the dot product is zero, so they are orthogonal. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(-4) + (4)(3) Component dot product. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = -12 + 12 = 0 Calculate sum.  \text{Find } k \text{ such that } \vec{A} = (k, 2) \text{ is orthogonal to } \vec{B} = (3, -6). Set the dot product to zero and solve for k. k = 4  3k + 2(-6) = 0 \implies 3k - 12 = 0 \implies k = 4 Solve for k.
documents.proofs: We can use orthogonality to confirm that the basis vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are independent. Basis Orthogonality \hat{i} \cdot \hat{j} = 0 \quad\hat{j} \cdot \hat{k} = 0 \quad\hat{k} \cdot \hat{i} = 0
documents.applications: Orthogonality is critical in physics: if a force is orthogonal to the direction of motion, it does exactly zero work. Gravity does zero work on a block moving on a level surface. Orthogonality Condition \vec{A} \perp \vec{B} \iff \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0 Perpendicular detection The simplest check for 90 degrees: A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z = 0.
documents.content: How do you check if two vectors are exactly 90 degrees apart without measuring the angle? Orthogonality is critical in physics: if a force is orthogonal to the direction of motion, it does exactly zero work. Gravity does zero work on a block moving on a level surface. Two non-zero vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ are orthogonal if they are perpendicular geometrically. Since $\cos 90^\circ = 0$, the dot product $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos 90^\circ = 0$. The dot product is the most powerful "perpendicularity detector" in mathematics. If the dot product is zero, the vectors are either perpendicular or one of them is zero. This applies regardless of the number of dimensions. Even in 10-dimensional spaces, "zero dot product" means "perpendicular." Finding an orthogonal vector is simple in 2D: if $\vec{A} = (x, y)$, then $\vec{B} = (-y, x)$ and $\vec{B}' = (y, -x)$ are both orthogonal to $\vec{A}$. Checking orthogonality in 3D: $\vec{A} = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ and $\vec{B} = (x_2, y_2, z_2)$ must satisfy $x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 + z_1z_2 = 0$. We can use orthogonality to confirm that the basis vectors $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ are independent. Basis Orthogonality \hat{i} \cdot \hat{j} = 0 \quad\hat{j} \cdot \hat{k} = 0 \quad\hat{k} \cdot \hat{i} = 0 Orthogonality Condition \vec{A} \perp \vec{B} \iff \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 0 Perpendicular detection The simplest check for 90 degrees: A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z = 0. Normal vector \text{The vector } \vec{n} \text{ orthogonal to a surface is called the normal vector.} Interactive Orthogonality Visualiser A zero dot product is the signature of 90 degrees. Dot Product Geometric Explorer Vary the angle to watch for the orthogonality crossing point. Orthogonality Detection Quiz Test your "perpendicularity intuition." Check Orthogonality \text{Are } \vec{A} = (3, 4) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (-4, 3) \text{ orthogonal?} Yes, the dot product is zero, so they are orthogonal. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = (3)(-4) + (4)(3) Component dot product. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = -12 + 12 = 0 Calculate sum. \text{Find } k \text{ such that } \vec{A} = (k, 2) \text{ is orthogonal to } \vec{B} = (3, -6). Set the dot product to zero and solve for k. k = 4 3k + 2(-6) = 0 \implies 3k - 12 = 0 \implies k = 4 Solve for k. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Dot Product — Angle Between Vectors
documents.subtitle: Use the dot product to solve for the geometric angle in any dimension.
documents.tags: dot product scalar product angle between vectors arc cosine
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: 3D Calculation \text{Find the angle between } \vec{A} = (1, 2, -2) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (3, -4, 0).   The angle is approximately 109.5°. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 1(3) + 2(-4) + (-2)(0) = 3 - 8 = -5 Component dot product. |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{1^2+2^2+(-2)^2} = 3,\quad |\vec{B}| = \sqrt{3^2+(-4)^2+0^2} = 5 Find magnitudes. \cos\phi = -5 / (3 \times 5) = -1/3 Divide dot product by product of magnitudes. \phi = \arccos(-1/3) \approx 109.5^\circ Obtuse angle because the dot product was negative.  \text{Find the angle between } \vec{A} = (1, 1) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (1, 0). This is the angle between y=x and the x-axis. 45^\circ  \vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=1, |A|=\sqrt{2}, |B|=1 \implies \cos\phi = 1/\sqrt{2} \implies \phi = 45^\circ Apply definition.
documents.proofs: The magnitude of the dot product is also used to derive the Law of Cosines for triangles. Law of Cosines (Vector Form) |\vec{A} - \vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 - 2\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}
documents.applications: Robotics, game engineering, and data analysis all use this formula to find the "angular distance" between vectors — whether those vectors are robot arms, light rays, or data points. Angle Between Vectors \phi = \arccos\left(\frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|}\right) Magnitude-component bridge The angle is the ratio of the actual dot product to the maximum possible dot product (|A||B|).
documents.content: How do you find the angle between two 3D vectors when you only have their components? Robotics, game engineering, and data analysis all use this formula to find the "angular distance" between vectors — whether those vectors are robot arms, light rays, or data points. The dot product provides two paths to the same number: the geometric path $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = |\vec{A}||\vec{B}|\cos\phi$ and the component path $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_xB_x + A_yB_y$. By equating these two paths, we can solve for the angle: $\cos\phi = \frac{A_xB_x + A_yB_y}{|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|}$. This is the most powerful tool for geometric analysis. It works just as well in 3D: just add the $z$-components to the numerator and use the 3D magnitude formula for the denominator. The range of the angle $\phi$ is $0 \le \phi \le \pi$ ($0^\circ \le \phi \le 180^\circ$). The cosine function is positive for acute angles ($phi < 90^\circ$), zero for orthogonal vectors ($phi = 90^\circ$), and negative for obtuse angles ($phi > 90^\circ$). The magnitude of the dot product is also used to derive the Law of Cosines for triangles. Law of Cosines (Vector Form) |\vec{A} - \vec{B}|^2 = |\vec{A}|^2 + |\vec{B}|^2 - 2\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} Angle Between Vectors \phi = \arccos\left(\frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|}\right) Magnitude-component bridge The angle is the ratio of the actual dot product to the maximum possible dot product (|A||B|). Acute vs Obtuse \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} > 0 \implies \text{Acute angle} \quad \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} < 0 \implies \text{Obtuse angle} Interactive Angle Visualiser The angle is always measured between the vectors, tail-to-tail. 3D Angle Between Vectors Calculator Calculators are the best tool for checking your math. Angle Estimation Pattern Spotter Train your brain for visual estimation. 3D Calculation \text{Find the angle between } \vec{A} = (1, 2, -2) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (3, -4, 0). The angle is approximately 109.5°. \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = 1(3) + 2(-4) + (-2)(0) = 3 - 8 = -5 Component dot product. |\vec{A}| = \sqrt{1^2+2^2+(-2)^2} = 3,\quad |\vec{B}| = \sqrt{3^2+(-4)^2+0^2} = 5 Find magnitudes. \cos\phi = -5 / (3 \times 5) = -1/3 Divide dot product by product of magnitudes. \phi = \arccos(-1/3) \approx 109.5^\circ Obtuse angle because the dot product was negative. \text{Find the angle between } \vec{A} = (1, 1) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (1, 0). This is the angle between y=x and the x-axis. 45^\circ \vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=1, |A|=\sqrt{2}, |B|=1 \implies \cos\phi = 1/\sqrt{2} \implies \phi = 45^\circ Apply definition. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Cross Product — Numerical Calculation
documents.subtitle: Multiply vectors by component in 3D: using the determinant $A_yB_z - A_zB_y$.
documents.tags: cross product vector product determinant component calculation
documents.aliases: vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory
documents.assignment: Basic 3D Calculation \vec{A} = (1, 3, -2), \vec{B} = (4, 0, 5). \text{ Find } \vec{A} \times \vec{B}.   The resultant vector is (15, -13, -12). R_x = (3)(5) - (-2)(0) = 15 Top-left minor. R_y = -((1)(5) - (-2)(4)) = -(5 + 8) = -13 Top-middle minor (negative sign!). R_z = (1)(0) - (3)(4) = -12 Top-right minor.  \text{Find } \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \text{ for } \vec{A} = (1, 0, 0) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (0, 1, 0). i 	imes j = k. (0, 0, 1)  \hat{i} \times \hat{j} = (0)(0) - (0)(1) = 0\hat{i} - 0\hat{j} + 1\hat{k} = \hat{k} Apply definition.
documents.proofs: We can derive the distributive property using this component form. Distributive property \vec{A} \times (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \times \vec{B} + \vec{A} \times \vec{C}
documents.applications: Robotics, animation engines, and aerospace navigation all use the determinant form of the cross product to find orthogonal axes — like the orientation of a drone relative to its flight path. Determinant Form \vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix} Perpendicular detection The cross product formula always produces a vector $(x, y, z)$ such that $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{A} = 0$ and $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$.
documents.content: How do you find the cross product when you only have 3D components like $(1, 3, -2)$ and $(4, 0, 5)$? Robotics, animation engines, and aerospace navigation all use the determinant form of the cross product to find orthogonal axes — like the orientation of a drone relative to its flight path. The cross product exists only in 3D. The result is a vector $(R_x, R_y, R_z)$ that is perpendicular to both $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$. The component form is most easily remembered as the determinant of a $3 \times 3$ matrix with $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ in the top row. The formula for each component is: $R_x = A_yB_z - A_zB_y$, $R_y = -(A_xB_z - A_zB_x)$, $R_z = A_xB_y - A_yB_x$. The cross product is **anti-commutative**: $\vec{A} \times \vec{B} = -(\vec{B} \times \vec{A})$. Swapping the inputs flips the direction of the result. If two vectors are parallel, their cross product is the zero vector $\vec{0}$. This is the exact opposite of the dot product. We can derive the distributive property using this component form. Distributive property \vec{A} \times (\vec{B} + \vec{C}) = \vec{A} \times \vec{B} + \vec{A} \times \vec{C} Determinant Form \vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ A_x & A_y & A_z \\ B_x & B_y & B_z \end{vmatrix} Perpendicular detection The cross product formula always produces a vector $(x, y, z)$ such that $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{A} = 0$ and $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$. Parallel vectors \vec{A} parallel \vec{B} \iff \vec{A} \times \vec{B} = \vec{0} Step-by-Step Determinant Calculator The determinant is the ultimate shorthand for cross product arithmetic. Notation Drill The cross product result is always a vector. Pattern Recognition Quiz Train your brain for determinant expansion. Basic 3D Calculation \vec{A} = (1, 3, -2), \vec{B} = (4, 0, 5). \text{ Find } \vec{A} \times \vec{B}. The resultant vector is (15, -13, -12). R_x = (3)(5) - (-2)(0) = 15 Top-left minor. R_y = -((1)(5) - (-2)(4)) = -(5 + 8) = -13 Top-middle minor (negative sign!). R_z = (1)(0) - (3)(4) = -12 Top-right minor. \text{Find } \vec{A} \times \vec{B} \text{ for } \vec{A} = (1, 0, 0) \text{ and } \vec{B} = (0, 1, 0). i 	imes j = k. (0, 0, 1) \hat{i} \times \hat{j} = (0)(0) - (0)(1) = 0\hat{i} - 0\hat{j} + 1\hat{k} = \hat{k} Apply definition. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

The rotation is perpendicular to both the lever arm and the force. \vec{	au} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 & 0 \end{vmatrix} Apply cross product. \vec{	au} = (2 	imes 0 - 0 	imes 10)\hat{i} + (1 	imes 0 - 0 	imes 0)\hat{j} + (1 	imes 10 - 2 	imes 0)\hat{k} = 10\hat{k} Calculate each component.  \vec{A}=(2,-1,2), \vec{B}=(3,1,-2). \text{ Find } |\vec{A}\times\vec{B}|. documents.content: How do you calculate the area of a slanted parallelogram in 3D space? Aerospace engineering, architecture, and lighting design all use the cross product to find "normal vectors" — the vectors perpendicular to a surface at any given point. The magnitude $|ec{A} 	imes ec{B}| = |ec{A}||ec{B}|sinphi$ is exactly the **area of the parallelogram** formed by $ec{A}$ and $ec{B}$. The direction of the result is exactly the **normal vector** to the plane defined by $ec{A}$ and $ec{B}$. This geometric property makes the cross product essential for physics: if $ec{r}$ is the lever arm and $ec{F}$ is the force, the torque $ec{	au} = ec{r} 	imes ec{F}$ is a vector describing the rotation. The cross product can be used to check if three vectors are coplanar (the triple scalar product $\vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} \times \vec{C}) = 0$). For area: Area of triangle = $\frac{1}{2} |\vec{A} \times \vec{B}|$. We can use cross products to confirm that a coordinate system is "right-handed". Coordinate Handedness \hat{i} \times \hat{j} = \hat{k} \implies \text{Right-handed system.} Torque Vector \vec{	au} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} Geometric mapping The cross product magnitude scales with the area of the surface formed by the vectors. Triple scalar product V = |\vec{A} \cdot (\vec{B} \times \vec{C})| \text{ is the volume of a parallelopiped.} Cross Product Explorer (Area & Torque) Area and torque are the same geometric multiplication. Geometric Pattern Recognition Right-hand rule is the standard of direction. Notation Recall Precision requires practice. Calculate Torque \text{Find the torque vector if } \vec{r} = (1, 2, 0) \text{ and } \vec{F} = (0, 10, 0). The torque vector is (0, 0, 10). The rotation is perpendicular to both the lever arm and the force. \vec{	au} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 10 & 0 \end{vmatrix} Apply cross product. \vec{	au} = (2 	imes 0 - 0 	imes 10)\hat{i} + (1 	imes 0 - 0 	imes 0)\hat{j} + (1 	imes 10 - 2 	imes 0)\hat{k} = 10\hat{k} Calculate each component. \vec{A}=(2,-1,2), \vec{B}=(3,1,-2). \text{ Find } |\vec{A}\times\vec{B}|. Find the resulting vector first, then its magnitude. 11.18 \vec{A}\times\vec{B} = (2-2)\hat{i} + (6+4)\hat{j} + (2+3)\hat{k} = (0, 10, 5) \implies |\vec{R}|\approx11.18 Apply definition. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step real world engineering physics economics biology computer science vector-valued velocity acceleration trajectory

documents.title: Motion in One Dimension — Definitions
documents.subtitle: Position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration: the four quantities that describe all motion.
documents.tags: kinematics position displacement velocity acceleration 1D motion
documents.assignment: Displacement vs distance \text{A runner goes 400 m east then 150 m west. Find (a) distance, (b) displacement.}   Distance 550 m; displacement 250 m east. The runner's final position is 250 m east of start. \text{Distance} = 400 + 150 = 550\,\text{m} Total path length — add all segments regardless of direction. \Delta x = +400 + (-150) = +250\,\text{m east} Displacement — net change, east = positive. Average velocity \text{A car moves from } x_i=20\,\text{m to }x_f=80\,\text{m in }\Delta t=4\,\text{s. Find }\bar{v}.}   $\bar{v} = 15$ m/s in the positive direction. \Delta x = 80 - 20 = 60\,\text{m} Displacement. \bar{v} = \frac{60}{4} = 15\,\text{m/s} Average velocity = displacement ÷ time.  \text{Object moves: +8 m, −3 m, +1 m. Find distance and displacement.} Distance sums absolute values. Displacement sums signed values. \text{Distance}=12\,\text{m},\quad \Delta x = +6\,\text{m}  \text{Distance} = 8+3+1 = 12\,\text{m} Always positive. \Delta x = 8-3+1 = +6\,\text{m} Signs kept.  \text{A ball moves from }x=5\,\text{m to }x=-3\,\text{m in 2 s. Find }\bar{v}\text{ and average speed if path length = 10 m.} Displacement can be negative; speed cannot. \bar{v} = -4\,\text{m/s},\quad \text{avg speed} = 5\,\text{m/s}  \Delta x = -3 - 5 = -8\,\text{m} Displacement is negative (moved in −x direction). \bar{v} = -8/2 = -4\,\text{m/s} Negative velocity means moving in −x direction. \text{avg speed} = 10/2 = 5\,\text{m/s} Speed uses total path length — always positive.
documents.proofs: Formally, position is a function $x(t): \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. Displacement over $[t_1, t_2]$ is $\Delta x = x(t_2) - x(t_1)$. Distance is $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} |\dot{x}(t)|\,dt$ — the arc length of the trajectory. These are equal only when $\dot{x}(t)$ does not change sign on the interval. Instantaneous velocity (calculus) v(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{dx}{dt} Instantaneous acceleration a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} Why derivatives? The limit definition captures behaviour at a single instant. Average velocity over a shrinking interval converges to the instantaneous velocity.
documents.applications: GPS navigation, rocket guidance, and sports biomechanics all depend on tracking displacement — not distance. Kinematics is the language physicists use to describe motion precisely, without asking why the motion happens. Displacement \Delta x = x_f - x_i \quad (\text{vector — has sign}) Average velocity \bar{v} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{x_f - x_i}{t_f - t_i} Average acceleration \bar{a} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{v_f - v_i}{t_f - t_i} Distance ≠ |Displacement| Distance counts every metre travelled. Displacement counts only the net change. They are equal only if the object never reverses direction.
documents.content: A car drives 10 km east, then 4 km west. It travelled 14 km — but its displacement is only 6 km. Why does the difference matter? GPS navigation, rocket guidance, and sports biomechanics all depend on tracking displacement — not distance. Kinematics is the language physicists use to describe motion precisely, without asking why the motion happens. Kinematics asks **what** an object does — not **why**. It describes motion using four fundamental quantities: position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration. **Position** $x$ locates an object on a number line (needs a reference point and a direction for positive). **Displacement** $\Delta x = x_f - x_i$ measures the net change in position — it is a vector (has sign). **Distance** is the total path length — always positive, always $\ge |\Delta x|$. **Average velocity** $\bar{v} = \Delta x / \Delta t$ is displacement per unit time — a vector. **Average speed** $= \text{distance} / \Delta t$ — a scalar, always $\ge |\bar{v}|$. **Acceleration** $a = \Delta v / \Delta t$ is the rate of change of velocity. In 1D, direction is encoded by sign: positive = right (or up), negative = left (or down). The sign convention must be stated at the start of every problem. The five kinematic quantities and their SI units: Formally, position is a function $x(t): \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$. Displacement over $[t_1, t_2]$ is $\Delta x = x(t_2) - x(t_1)$. Distance is $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} |\dot{x}(t)|\,dt$ — the arc length of the trajectory. These are equal only when $\dot{x}(t)$ does not change sign on the interval. Instantaneous velocity (calculus) v(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{dx}{dt} Instantaneous acceleration a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} Why derivatives? The limit definition captures behaviour at a single instant. Average velocity over a shrinking interval converges to the instantaneous velocity. Displacement \Delta x = x_f - x_i \quad (\text{vector — has sign}) Average velocity \bar{v} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{x_f - x_i}{t_f - t_i} Average acceleration \bar{a} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{v_f - v_i}{t_f - t_i} Distance ≠ |Displacement| Distance counts every metre travelled. Displacement counts only the net change. They are equal only if the object never reverses direction. Five kinematic quantities x\;(\text{m}),\quad \Delta x\;(\text{m}),\quad v\;(\text{m/s}),\quad a\;(\text{m/s}^2),\quad t\;(\text{s}) Instantaneous vs average Average velocity = slope of the secant on an x–t graph. Instantaneous velocity = slope of the tangent = dx/dt. Signs encode direction +x: right/up/forward. −x: left/down/backward. Always define your positive direction before writing equations. Drag the object along the number line — watch displacement vs distance diverge Reverse direction once and the gap between distance and |displacement| becomes obvious. Set position vs time — read off all five quantities Average velocity = rise/run on the x–t graph. Secant → tangent as Δt → 0 The slope of the tangent is the instantaneous velocity. Displacement vs distance \text{A runner goes 400 m east then 150 m west. Find (a) distance, (b) displacement.} Distance 550 m; displacement 250 m east. The runner's final position is 250 m east of start. \text{Distance} = 400 + 150 = 550\,\text{m} Total path length — add all segments regardless of direction. \Delta x = +400 + (-150) = +250\,\text{m east} Displacement — net change, east = positive. Average velocity \text{A car moves from } x_i=20\,\text{m to }x_f=80\,\text{m in }\Delta t=4\,\text{s. Find }\bar{v}.} $\bar{v} = 15$ m/s in the positive direction. \Delta x = 80 - 20 = 60\,\text{m} Displacement. \bar{v} = \frac{60}{4} = 15\,\text{m/s} Average velocity = displacement ÷ time. \text{Object moves: +8 m, −3 m, +1 m. Find distance and displacement.} Distance sums absolute values. Displacement sums signed values. \text{Distance}=12\,\text{m},\quad \Delta x = +6\,\text{m} \text{Distance} = 8+3+1 = 12\,\text{m} Always positive. \Delta x = 8-3+1 = +6\,\text{m} Signs kept. \text{A ball moves from }x=5\,\text{m to }x=-3\,\text{m in 2 s. Find }\bar{v}\text{ and average speed if path length = 10 m.} Displacement can be negative; speed cannot. \bar{v} = -4\,\text{m/s},\quad \text{avg speed} = 5\,\text{m/s} \Delta x = -3 - 5 = -8\,\text{m} Displacement is negative (moved in −x direction). \bar{v} = -8/2 = -4\,\text{m/s} Negative velocity means moving in −x direction. \text{avg speed} = 10/2 = 5\,\text{m/s} Speed uses total path length — always positive. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Kinematic Equations
documents.subtitle: The five equations that solve every constant-acceleration problem.
documents.tags: kinematic equations SUVAT constant acceleration equations of motion
documents.assignment: Car accelerates from rest v_0=0,\;a=3\,\text{m/s}^2,\;t=5\,\text{s. Find }\Delta x\text{ and }v.   v = 15 m/s, Δx = 37.5 m. v = 0+3(5) = 15\,\text{m/s} Equation 1. \Delta x = 0(5)+\tfrac{1}{2}(3)(25) = 37.5\,\text{m} Equation 3. Braking distance v_0=30\,\text{m/s},\;v=0,\;a=-6\,\text{m/s}^2\text{. Find }\Delta x.   Δx = 75 m. 0 = 900 + 2(-6)\Delta x Equation 5 — time unknown, not needed. \Delta x = 900/12 = 75\,\text{m} Braking distance.  v_0=10,\;a=2,\;t=3.\text{ Find }v\text{ and }\Delta x. Equations 1 and 3. v=16\,\text{m/s},\;\Delta x=39\,\text{m}  v=16\,\text{m/s},\;\Delta x=39\,\text{m} Direct substitution.  A stone is thrown upward at 20 m/s. Find max height and time to return. At max height, v=0. Use a=−9.8 m/s². h\approx20.4\,\text{m},\;t\approx4.08\,\text{s}  v^2=400-2(9.8)h\implies h\approx20.4\,\text{m} Equation 5. t_{up}=20/9.8\approx2.04\,\text{s},\;t_{total}\approx4.08\,\text{s} Equation 1 then double.
documents.proofs: All five equations follow from two fundamental relations: $v = v_0 + at$ and $\Delta x = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2}at^2$. The other three are algebraic rearrangements. Only two are independent Equations 1 and 3 are the two primary equations. Equations 2, 4, and 5 are derived by combining them. You only ever need to memorise two.
documents.applications: These five equations are the engine of classical mechanics. Every projectile, every braking distance, every rocket launch uses them. The five kinematic equations v = v_0 + at \quad (1)\\\Delta x = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_0+v)t \quad (2)\\\Delta x = v_0t + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 \quad (3)\\\Delta x = vt - \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 \quad (4)\\v^2 = v_0^2 + 2a\Delta x \quad (5) SUVAT s = displacement (Δx), u = initial velocity (v₀), v = final velocity, a = acceleration, t = time. Each equation omits one. Only valid for constant acceleration These equations break down the moment acceleration changes. For variable acceleration, use calculus (integration).
documents.content: A car accelerates from rest at 3 m/s² for 5 s. How far does it travel? You need the kinematic equations. These five equations are the engine of classical mechanics. Every projectile, every braking distance, every rocket launch uses them. When acceleration is **constant**, four quantities are related: displacement $\Delta x$, initial velocity $v_0$, final velocity $v$, acceleration $a$, and time $t$. Any three known quantities let you find the other two. There are five equations, each leaving out one of the five variables. Identify what's missing from the problem, pick the equation that doesn't need it. The equations are derived from the definitions of velocity and acceleration assuming $a = \text{const}$: All five equations follow from two fundamental relations: $v = v_0 + at$ and $\Delta x = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2}at^2$. The other three are algebraic rearrangements. Only two are independent Equations 1 and 3 are the two primary equations. Equations 2, 4, and 5 are derived by combining them. You only ever need to memorise two. The five kinematic equations v = v_0 + at \quad (1)\\\Delta x = \tfrac{1}{2}(v_0+v)t \quad (2)\\\Delta x = v_0t + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 \quad (3)\\\Delta x = vt - \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 \quad (4)\\v^2 = v_0^2 + 2a\Delta x \quad (5) SUVAT s = displacement (Δx), u = initial velocity (v₀), v = final velocity, a = acceleration, t = time. Each equation omits one. Only valid for constant acceleration These equations break down the moment acceleration changes. For variable acceleration, use calculus (integration). Derivation of equation 3 \Delta x = \int_0^t v\,dt = \int_0^t (v_0+at)\,dt = v_0t + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 Derivation of equation 5 v^2 = v_0^2 + 2a\Delta x\text{ — eliminates }t\text{ using eq 1 and 3} Pick a missing variable — the right equation highlights One missing variable → one equation. documents.title: Position-Time Graph Analysis
documents.subtitle: Read motion directly from the shape and slope of an x–t graph.
documents.tags: position graph slope kinematics
documents.assignment: Read average velocity From x(2)=4 m and x(6)=20 m, find average velocity on [2,6].   Average velocity is 4 m/s. \bar{v}=\frac{20-4}{6-2}=4\,\text{m/s} Use secant slope.  Can velocity be zero while position is nonzero? Think about a flat tangent on x–t. Yes. v=0 means local flat slope, not x=0.
documents.proofs: Velocity as slope follows from the derivative limit definition.
documents.applications: Traffic analytics and GPS traces are position-time data first. Interpreting slope correctly is how we infer motion from that data.
documents.content: How can two objects at the same position be moving at different speeds? Traffic analytics and GPS traces are position-time data first. Interpreting slope correctly is how we infer motion from that data. An x–t graph tells you where an object is at each time. The slope of the curve at any point is velocity: steeper positive slope means faster forward motion; negative slope means moving backward. Average velocity over [t1, t2] is the secant slope on x–t. Instantaneous velocity is the tangent slope: v(t)=dx/dt. Velocity as slope follows from the derivative limit definition. Average velocity \bar{v}=\frac{x(t_2)-x(t_1)}{t_2-t_1} Instantaneous velocity v(t)=\frac{dx}{dt} Slope on x–t gives velocity Shape describes motion; slope quantifies it. Scenario explorer Different stories, different graph geometry. Kinematics formula recogniser Graph reading and formulas should agree. Read average velocity From x(2)=4 m and x(6)=20 m, find average velocity on [2,6]. Average velocity is 4 m/s. \bar{v}=\frac{20-4}{6-2}=4\,\text{m/s} Use secant slope. Can velocity be zero while position is nonzero? Think about a flat tangent on x–t. Yes. v=0 means local flat slope, not x=0. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Velocity-Time Graph Analysis
documents.subtitle: On v–t graphs, slope gives acceleration and area gives displacement.
documents.tags: velocity graph area acceleration
documents.assignment: Triangular area v rises linearly 0→12 m/s over 4 s. Find displacement.   Displacement is 24 m. \Delta x=\frac12(4)(12)=24\,\text{m} Area of triangle under v–t.  If v is positive but decreasing, what is sign of a? Look at slope of v–t. Negative acceleration.
documents.proofs: The fundamental theorem of calculus links area accumulation to derivatives.
documents.applications: Braking systems, elevators, and landing maneuvers are usually analyzed in v–t form because area and slope are physically meaningful.
documents.content: Why can an object have positive velocity but negative acceleration? Braking systems, elevators, and landing maneuvers are usually analyzed in v–t form because area and slope are physically meaningful. A point on v–t shows current velocity. The signed area under v–t between two times equals displacement. The slope of v–t gives acceleration. Displacement from v(t) is integral: Δx = ∫ v dt. Acceleration is derivative: a(t)=dv/dt. The fundamental theorem of calculus links area accumulation to derivatives. Displacement from velocity \Delta x=\int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t)\,dt Acceleration a(t)=\frac{dv}{dt} Area under v–t is displacement Area tracks where you end up. Signed-area explorer Direction lives in the sign. Pick equation from knowns Graph interpretation feeds algebraic solving. Triangular area v rises linearly 0→12 m/s over 4 s. Find displacement. Displacement is 24 m. \Delta x=\frac12(4)(12)=24\,\text{m} Area of triangle under v–t. If v is positive but decreasing, what is sign of a? Look at slope of v–t. Negative acceleration. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Acceleration-Time Graph Analysis
documents.subtitle: Use a–t graphs to predict velocity changes over time.
documents.tags: acceleration graph delta v kinematics
documents.assignment: Constant acceleration update v0 = 3 m/s, a = -1.5 m/s² for 4 s. Find v.   Final velocity is -3 m/s. v=3+(-1.5)(4)=-3\,\text{m/s} Area under a–t is -6 m/s.  Can acceleration be zero while velocity is nonzero? Think constant-velocity motion. Yes. Then velocity is constant and nonzero.
documents.proofs: The area rule is a direct integral statement.
documents.applications: Engine control, launch profiles, and motion planning often start from acceleration commands.
documents.content: If acceleration is constant at +2 m/s² for 5 s, how much does velocity change? Engine control, launch profiles, and motion planning often start from acceleration commands. An a–t graph tells you how quickly velocity is changing. Signed area under a–t over a time interval equals Δv. Velocity update from acceleration is v = v0 + ∫a dt. For constant a, this becomes v = v0 + at. The area rule is a direct integral statement. Velocity update v(t)=v_0+\int_0^t a(t')\,dt' Constant acceleration \Delta v = a\Delta t Area under a–t is Δv Acceleration accumulates into velocity. x-v-a linked explorer Consistency check across representations. Equation recall Area and formulas should match numerically. Constant acceleration update v0 = 3 m/s, a = -1.5 m/s² for 4 s. Find v. Final velocity is -3 m/s. v=3+(-1.5)(4)=-3\,\text{m/s} Area under a–t is -6 m/s. Can acceleration be zero while velocity is nonzero? Think constant-velocity motion. Yes. Then velocity is constant and nonzero. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step

documents.title: Linking x(t), v(t), and a(t)
documents.subtitle: Differentiate downward, integrate upward: the three-graph chain.

documents.assignment: From acceleration to position Given a=2, v0=1, x0=0, find v(t), x(t).   v and x follow directly from the chain. v(t)=1+2t Integrate acceleration. x(t)=\int (1+2t)dt=t+t^2 Integrate velocity and apply x0=0.  If x(t) is linear, what must a(t) be? Differentiate twice. a(t)=0 for all t.
documents.proofs: These relationships are equivalent statements from calculus definitions.
documents.applications: Robotics and controls routinely switch between these representations to plan and verify trajectories.
documents.content: How do you move instantly between position, velocity, and acceleration views of the same motion? Robotics and controls routinely switch between these representations to plan and verify trajectories. The three graphs represent one motion state from three mathematical perspectives. Slope links x→v and v→a; area links a→v and v→x. Core chain: v = dx/dt and a = dv/dt. Inverse chain: Δx = ∫v dt and Δv = ∫a dt. These relationships are equivalent statements from calculus definitions. Derivative chain a(t)=\frac{dv}{dt}=\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} Integral chain v(t)=v_0+\int a\,dt,\quad x(t)=x_0+\int v\,dt Derivative chain visual One motion, three synchronized views. Parameter-driven sync A consistency engine for kinematics intuition. Chain-aware formula drill Direction of travel in the chain matters. From acceleration to position Given a=2, v0=1, x0=0, find v(t), x(t). v and x follow directly from the chain. v(t)=1+2t Integrate acceleration. x(t)=\int (1+2t)dt=t+t^2 Integrate velocity and apply x0=0. If x(t) is linear, what must a(t) be? Differentiate twice. a(t)=0 for all t. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change antiderivative accumulation area under curve net area total change

documents.title: Definition of dx/dt
documents.subtitle: The derivative as a limit: from average velocity to instantaneous velocity.
documents.tags: definition-of-dx-dt kinematics 1D motion
documents.aliases: instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change
documents.assignment: Differentiate a position function \text{Given }x(t)=3t^2-2t+1,\text{ find }v(t)\text{ and }v(2).   Instantaneous velocity at 2 s is 10 m/s. v(t)=\frac{dx}{dt}=6t-2 Differentiate term by term. v(2)=6(2)-2=10\,\text{m/s} Evaluate at t=2 s.  \text{If }x(t)=t^3,\text{ estimate }v(1)\text{ using }h=0.1\text{ and compare with exact derivative.} Use [x(1+h)-x(1)]/h then compute x'(t). Estimated 3.31, exact 3.0 (estimate improves as h shrinks).  \frac{x(1.1)-x(1)}{0.1}=\frac{1.331-1}{0.1}=3.31 Forward-difference estimate. x'(t)=3t^2\Rightarrow x'(1)=3 Exact instantaneous velocity.
documents.proofs: Let x(t) be differentiable at t. By definition of derivative, the limit of the difference quotient exists and equals x'(t). Physics interprets x'(t) as instantaneous velocity because it is the unique value that local linear motion approaches over arbitrarily short intervals. Formal statement v(t)=x'(t)=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t}
documents.applications: The limit definition bridges the gap between physics and calculus. This is the foundation of differential calculus applied to motion. Average velocity \bar{v}=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} Instantaneous velocity v(t)=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t}=\frac{dx}{dt} Geometry meaning Secant slope (two points) approaches tangent slope (one point limit).
documents.content: How can velocity be defined at a single instant when velocity requires a time interval to measure? The limit definition bridges the gap between physics and calculus. This is the foundation of differential calculus applied to motion. Average velocity over a time interval is easy: divide displacement by elapsed time. But many physics questions ask for velocity at a single instant. A single instant has no width, so we approximate it with smaller and smaller intervals around that time. As the interval shrinks, secant slope converges to tangent slope. That limiting value is instantaneous velocity. Computationally, you can evaluate the derivative analytically or estimate it numerically with tiny \Delta t. For smooth functions, numerical secant estimates converge rapidly to the exact derivative near the target time. Let x(t) be differentiable at t. By definition of derivative, the limit of the difference quotient exists and equals x'(t). Physics interprets x'(t) as instantaneous velocity because it is the unique value that local linear motion approaches over arbitrarily short intervals. Formal statement v(t)=x'(t)=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t} Average velocity \bar{v}=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} Instantaneous velocity v(t)=\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{x(t+\Delta t)-x(t)}{\Delta t}=\frac{dx}{dt} Geometry meaning Secant slope (two points) approaches tangent slope (one point limit). Difference quotient \frac{x(t+h)-x(t)}{h} Numerical caution If h is too large, approximation error dominates. If h is too tiny in floating-point, round-off can dominate. Secant to tangent transition The limit process turns average rate into instantaneous rate. Numerical convergence table Convergence quality is the evidence for the limit. Proof steps Differentiate a position function \text{Given }x(t)=3t^2-2t+1,\text{ find }v(t)\text{ and }v(2). Instantaneous velocity at 2 s is 10 m/s. v(t)=\frac{dx}{dt}=6t-2 Differentiate term by term. v(2)=6(2)-2=10\,\text{m/s} Evaluate at t=2 s. \text{If }x(t)=t^3,\text{ estimate }v(1)\text{ using }h=0.1\text{ and compare with exact derivative.} Use [x(1+h)-x(1)]/h then compute x'(t). Estimated 3.31, exact 3.0 (estimate improves as h shrinks). \frac{x(1.1)-x(1)}{0.1}=\frac{1.331-1}{0.1}=3.31 Forward-difference estimate. x'(t)=3t^2\Rightarrow x'(1)=3 Exact instantaneous velocity. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step instantaneous rate of change slope dy/dx velocity marginal change

Given v0, v, a, which equation should you use first to get displacement? Choose the equation without t. Use v^2=v_0^2+2a\Delta x.

documents.title: Displacement from Velocity Area
documents.subtitle: Integrate velocity to get net displacement, even with changing speed.
documents.tags: integration velocity area
documents.assignment: Piecewise velocity v=4 m/s for 3 s, then v=-2 m/s for 2 s. Find net displacement.   Net displacement is 8 m. \Delta x_1=4(3)=12\,\text{m} Positive area. \Delta x_2=-2(2)=-4\,\text{m} Negative area. \Delta x_{net}=8\,\text{m} Add signed areas.  Can total distance exceed |net displacement| on the same v–t graph? Compare signed area vs absolute area. Yes. Distance sums absolute area; displacement sums signed area.
documents.proofs: This is a direct application of the fundamental theorem of calculus.
documents.applications: Sensors return sampled velocities; integrating those samples is how systems estimate position in practice.
documents.content: Why is area under the v–t graph the physically correct displacement? Sensors return sampled velocities; integrating those samples is how systems estimate position in practice. Small rectangles under v–t approximate displacement over short intervals. As interval width shrinks, the sum converges to the exact integral. Signed area below axis reduces net displacement, matching directional motion. This is a direct application of the fundamental theorem of calculus. Displacement integral \Delta x=\int_{t_1}^{t_2}v(t)\,dt Riemann sum to exact area Integral as limit of accumulated motion slices. Signed area sandbox Direction-aware accumulation. Piecewise velocity v=4 m/s for 3 s, then v=-2 m/s for 2 s. Find net displacement. Net displacement is 8 m. \Delta x_1=4(3)=12\,\text{m} Positive area. \Delta x_2=-2(2)=-4\,\text{m} Negative area. \Delta x_{net}=8\,\text{m} Add signed areas. documents.title: Free Fall — Basics

documents.assignment: Dropped from rest \text{An object is dropped from rest. Find }v\text{ and }\Delta y\text{ after }3\text{ s (up-positive).}   After 3 s: v=-29.4 m/s and Δy=-44.1 m. v=v_0-gt=0-9.8(3)=-29.4\,\text{m/s} Velocity is downward (negative in up-positive axis). \Delta y=v_0t-\frac12gt^2=0-\frac12(9.8)(9)=-44.1\,\text{m} Object is 44.1 m below release point.  \text{Thrown upward at }19.6\,\text{m/s},\text{ find max height (up-positive).} At max height, v=0. Maximum height is 19.6 m above launch point.  0=v_0^2-2g\Delta y\Rightarrow \Delta y=\frac{v_0^2}{2g}=\frac{(19.6)^2}{19.6}=19.6\,\text{m} Use no-time equation with apex condition.
documents.proofs: From Newtonian mechanics near Earth, gravitational force gives approximately constant acceleration over moderate height changes. With a(t)=constant, free-fall kinematics is a direct specialization of the constant-acceleration calculus derivation. Model statement a(t)=\text{constant}=-g;(\text{or }+g\text{ by axis choice})
documents.applications: Free fall is the purest application of constant-acceleration kinematics. Galileo established that (without air resistance) all objects fall equally. documents.content: A feather and a hammer are dropped on the Moon. Which hits the ground first? Free fall is the purest application of constant-acceleration kinematics. Galileo established that (without air resistance) all objects fall equally. This underpins every projectile calculation. Free fall means gravity is the only significant force (air resistance neglected). Near Earth's surface, acceleration is approximately constant and downward with magnitude g≈9.8 m/s². Mass does not appear in the kinematic equations for ideal free fall, so all objects share the same acceleration in this model. Use the standard constant-acceleration equations with a replaced by ±g according to your axis choice. A reliable workflow is: choose convention, write knowns with sign, choose equation by unknowns, then check physical reasonableness. With a(t)=constant, free-fall kinematics is a direct specialization of the constant-acceleration calculus derivation. Core free-fall set (up-positive) v=v_0-gt,\quad y=y_0+v_0t-\frac12gt^2,\quad v^2=v_0^2-2g(y-y_0) Free-fall motion intuition Same acceleration law, many scenarios. documents.title: Two Objects — Meeting Problems (Part 1)
documents.subtitle: When do two objects at different positions with different velocities meet?

documents.assignment: Catch-up example \text{A starts at }x=0\text{ with }v_A=20\,\text{m/s}.\text{ B starts at }x=100\text{ with }v_B=15\,\text{m/s}.\text{ Find meeting time and position.}   They meet after 20 s at x=400 m. x_A=20t,\quad x_B=100+15t Write position models. 20t=100+15t\Rightarrow 5t=100\Rightarrow t=20\,\text{s} Apply meeting condition. x=20(20)=400\,\text{m} Substitute back to get location.  \text{A starts at }x=0\text{ with }v_A=12\,\text{m/s},\text{ B at }x=30\text{ with }v_B=12\,\text{m/s}.\text{ Do they meet?} Check relative velocity first. No, they never meet.  v_A-v_B=0 No relative closing speed. x_{B0}-x_{A0}=30\neq 0 Initial separation persists forever.
documents.proofs: Define f(t)=x_A(t)-x_B(t). A meeting occurs exactly when f(t)=0. For linear x_A and x_B, f(t) is linear, so there is at most one meeting time unless the functions are identical. Root-finding view f(t)=x_A(t)-x_B(t);\;\text{meeting}\iff f(t)=0
documents.applications: Two-object problems appear in every physics exam. The trick: write x(t) for each object and set them equal. Meeting condition x_A(t)=x_B(t) Common mistake Using different time origins for each object without converting them first.
documents.content: Car A is at x = 0 moving at 20 m/s. Car B is 100 m ahead moving at 15 m/s. When does A catch B? Two-object problems appear in every physics exam. The trick: write x(t) for each object and set them equal. Meeting problems are about position equality, not velocity equality. Write one position model for each object in the same coordinate system and same time origin. Then set the two position functions equal and solve for physically valid time (t≥0). For constant velocity motion, use linear models: x_A=x_{A0}+v_A t and x_B=x_{B0}+v_B t. Equivalent relative-motion form: x_{rel}(t)=x_A-x_B=(x_{A0}-x_{B0})+(v_A-v_B)t. Meeting occurs when x_{rel}=0. Define f(t)=x_A(t)-x_B(t). A meeting occurs exactly when f(t)=0. For linear x_A and x_B, f(t) is linear, so there is at most one meeting time unless the functions are identical. Root-finding view f(t)=x_A(t)-x_B(t);\;\text{meeting}\iff f(t)=0 Meeting condition x_A(t)=x_B(t) Common mistake Using different time origins for each object without converting them first. Catch-up time (constant velocities) t=\frac{x_{B0}-x_{A0}}{v_A-v_B}\quad (v_A>v_B) No meeting case If relative velocity is zero and initial separation is nonzero, they never meet. documents.title: Non-Constant Acceleration
documents.subtitle: When acceleration varies with time, only calculus gives the exact answer.
documents.tags: non-constant-acceleration kinematics 1D motion
documents.assignment: Polynomial acceleration example \text{Given }a(t)=6t,\;v_0=2,\;x_0=0.\text{ Find }v(3)\text{ and }x(3).   At 3 s, velocity is 29 m/s and displacement is 33 m. v(t)=2+\int_0^t6\tau\,d\tau=2+3t^2 Integrate acceleration once. v(3)=2+3(9)=29\,\text{m/s} Evaluate at t=3 s. x(t)=\int_0^t(2+3\tau^2)d\tau=2t+t^3\Rightarrow x(3)=6+27=33\,\text{m} Integrate velocity and evaluate.  \text{If }a(t)=4-2t\text{ and }v_0=1,\text{ find }v(t). Integrate a(t) from 0 to t and add v0. v(t)=1+4t-t^2.  v(t)=1+\int_0^t(4-2\tau)d\tau=1+4t-t^2 Single integration step with initial condition.
documents.proofs: From definitions, a(t)=dv/dt and v(t)=dx/dt. Integrating each differential equation with initial conditions gives unique state trajectories. This framework generalizes constant-acceleration kinematics and is the correct model whenever acceleration is time-varying. Initial-value form \frac{dv}{dt}=a(t),\;v(0)=v_0;\quad \frac{dx}{dt}=v(t),\;x(0)=x_0
documents.applications: The kinematic equations assume constant acceleration. When a varies, integration is the only correct approach — and it gives exact answers without memorising new formulas. Variable-acceleration backbone v(t)=v_0+\int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau,\quad x(t)=x_0+\int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau Domain check SUVAT equations are only valid when a is constant over the interval.
documents.content: a(t) = 6t. What is the velocity at t = 3 s if v₀ = 2 m/s? What is the displacement from t = 0 to t = 3? The kinematic equations assume constant acceleration. When a varies, integration is the only correct approach — and it gives exact answers without memorising new formulas. Constant-acceleration formulas fail when acceleration changes with time. In that case, we accumulate acceleration to get velocity, then accumulate velocity to get position. This is the calculus pipeline: a(t) → integrate → v(t) → integrate → x(t). For polynomial a(t), symbolic integration gives exact formulas for v(t) and x(t). For arbitrary sampled a(t), numerical integration (e.g., Euler/trapezoid) provides practical approximations. From definitions, a(t)=dv/dt and v(t)=dx/dt. Integrating each differential equation with initial conditions gives unique state trajectories. This framework generalizes constant-acceleration kinematics and is the correct model whenever acceleration is time-varying. Initial-value form \frac{dv}{dt}=a(t),\;v(0)=v_0;\quad \frac{dx}{dt}=v(t),\;x(0)=x_0 Variable-acceleration backbone v(t)=v_0+\int_0^t a(\tau)\,d\tau,\quad x(t)=x_0+\int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau Domain check SUVAT equations are only valid when a is constant over the interval. Two-step solve recipe (1)\;v(t)=v_0+\int a(t)dt\quad (2)\;x(t)=x_0+\int v(t)dt Integration chain intuition The shape of a(t) controls everything downstream. Direction-changing acceleration Acceleration is about vector change, not just speed change. Analytic vs numeric explorer Numerics converge to analytic solution with finer steps. Centripetal-acceleration derivation A canonical example of acceleration from changing direction. Proof steps Polynomial acceleration example \text{Given }a(t)=6t,\;v_0=2,\;x_0=0.\text{ Find }v(3)\text{ and }x(3). At 3 s, velocity is 29 m/s and displacement is 33 m. v(t)=2+\int_0^t6\tau\,d\tau=2+3t^2 Integrate acceleration once. v(3)=2+3(9)=29\,\text{m/s} Evaluate at t=3 s. x(t)=\int_0^t(2+3\tau^2)d\tau=2t+t^3\Rightarrow x(3)=6+27=33\,\text{m} Integrate velocity and evaluate. \text{If }a(t)=4-2t\text{ and }v_0=1,\text{ find }v(t). Integrate a(t) from 0 to t and add v0. v(t)=1+4t-t^2. v(t)=1+\int_0^t(4-2\tau)d\tau=1+4t-t^2 Single integration step with initial condition. assignment homework quiz exam practice problems worked example step by step
